topics.title: Overview of Casting Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Casting Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting** is a *primary shaping manufacturing process* in which a molten or liquid material (typically a metal, alloy, or thermoplastic) is poured into a pre-prepared mold cavity possessing the negative geometry of the desired part, allowed to solidify via controlled cooling, and subsequently extracted to yield a near-net-shape finished or semi-finished component.

Casting is one of the **oldest known manufacturing processes** (dating back to **~4000 BCE** for bronze artifacts) and remains indispensable in modern industry because it can produce complex internal/external geometries, large parts, and components from materials that are difficult to machine or deform plastically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Ice Cube" Model:**
> Think of how you make ice cubes at home: water (liquid) is poured into a plastic tray (the **mold**), which freezes (solidification) into the shape of the cavity, and is then released (ejection) as a solid ice cube (the **casting**). A metal foundry does exactly the same thing — except the "water" is molten iron at **~1400 °C**, the "tray" is a sand or steel mold, and the "freezer" is the surrounding ambient or forced air.

The only "magic" that makes metal casting practical is engineering the **mold**, the **gating system** (how liquid metal enters), and the **risers** (reservoirs that feed shrinkage during solidification).

### 1.3 Why Casting is a Primary Shaping Process

| Forming Classification | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Shaping** | Gives the *initial* geometry from a fluid/solid bulk | Casting, Forging, Rolling |
| **Secondary Shaping** | Refines geometry via material removal/addition | Machining, Welding |
| **Tertiary Shaping** | Final surface finish / property enhancement | Polishing, Heat treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 syllabus**, casting belongs to **Module 1** and is the foundational block upon which topics like pattern design, gating, and riser design (in later modules) are built. A clear conceptual map here is non-negotiable for solving 14-mark problems.

### 1.4 Fundamental Terminology (Must Memorize)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Pattern** | A replica of the final part used to create the mold cavity. Usually made of wood, metal, or plastic. |
| **Mold** | The hollow container (made of sand, ceramic, or metal) that holds molten metal. |
| **Core** | A sand/ceramic insert placed inside the mold to create internal cavities/holes. |
| **Parting Line** | The line where the upper (*cope*) and lower (*drag*) halves of the mold meet. |
| **Gating System** | The network of channels (*sprue, runner, gate*) through which molten metal flows. |
| **Riser** | A reservoir of molten metal that compensates for volumetric shrinkage during solidification. |
| **Draft Angle** | Slight taper on vertical pattern faces to allow clean withdrawal from the mold. |
| **Cope & Drag** | Upper and lower halves of a horizontally-parted flask/mold. |
| **Flask** | The rigid frame (steel/wood) that holds the sand mold together. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Sequential Steps of a Casting Operation

The casting process, regardless of the specific variant (sand, die, investment, etc.), follows a logical sequence. Understanding this flow is critical for KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions.

1. **Pattern & Core Preparation** — Patterns are fabricated to slightly oversized dimensions (to compensate for shrinkage, machining, and distortion). Cores are made separately and baked.
2. **Mold Preparation** — The pattern is placed in the flask, and molding sand is packed around it. For sand casting, the sand is *rammed*, *vented*, and the pattern is withdrawn leaving a cavity.
3. **Core Setting & Mold Assembly** — Cores are placed inside the cavity using core prints. The cope and drag are clamped together.
4. **Melting & Pouring** — The metal is melted in a furnace (cupola, induction, or electric arc) and poured at the correct *pouring temperature* (typically **50–100 °C above** the liquidus).
5. **Solidification & Cooling** — Heat is extracted through the mold walls. Solidification begins at the mold walls and progresses inward.
6. **Shakeout & Cleaning** — The flask is opened, the sand is removed (*shakeout*), and the casting is separated from the runners and risers (*fettling*).
7. **Inspection** — Visual, dimensional, and non-destructive testing (radiography, ultrasonic, dye penetrant) is performed.
8. **Machining & Finishing** — Final dimensional accuracy and surface finish are achieved by secondary processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why":** Each step exists to solve a specific physics problem — thermal contraction needs *risers*, gas evolution needs *vents*, mold integrity needs *ramming*. KTU examiners love asking *"Why is X done?"* — the answer is always traced back to a physical phenomenon.

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas appear in **at least one out of every two** KTU 14-mark casting problems. Memorize the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Formula / Rule | Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chvorinov's Rule** (solidification time) | $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{2}$ | $t_s$ = solidification time (s); $V$ = volume (m³); $A$ = surface area (m²); $B$ = mold constant (s·m⁻⁴) | Predicts cooling time of any casting region |
| 2 | **Riser Design Criterion** | $t_{riser} \geq t_{casting} \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)_{riser} \geq \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)_{casting}$ | — | Ensures riser solidifies *last* to feed shrinkage |
| 3 | **Caine's Modification** (better for castings on chill plates) | $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{n}, \quad n = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$ | $n$ depends on mold material | Used for die casting (faster heat extraction) |
| 4 | **Volumetric Shrinkage Allowance** | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{liquid} - V_{solid}}{V_{solid}} \times 100\%$ | $S_v$ expressed in **%** | Pattern is made oversized by $S_v$ |
| 5 | **Linear Shrinkage Allowance** | $S_l \approx \dfrac{S_v}{3}$ | $S_l$ in **%** | Used for linear dimensions on pattern |
| 6 | **Pouring Time (for Ferrous Metals)** | $t_p = K \sqrt{W}$ | $t_p$ = pouring time (s); $W$ = mass of casting (kg); $K$ = constant (≈ 2.0–3.5) | Prevents premature solidification in gating |
| 7 | **Fluidity of Molten Metal** | $F \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{viscosity}}$ | $F$ in **mm** of spiral flow length | Determines section-thickness capability |
| 8 | **Heat Flux in Mold** | $q = \dfrac{k}{\delta} \cdot \Delta T$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K); $\delta$ = mold thickness (m) | Determines solidification rate |

> **⚠️ KTU Pitfall:** Never use vertical pipe characters `|x|` in any table — use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep LaTeX rendering valid.

## 2.3 Classification of Casting Processes (Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 explicitly requires students to draw/memorize this classification. The *primary axis* is **reusability of the mold**, and the *secondary axis* is **mold material**.

### 2.3.1 Expendable Mold (Single-Use) Processes

| Process | Mold Material | Typical Materials Cast | Surface Finish (µm Ra) | Min Section (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Green/dry sand + binder | Ferrous & non-ferrous | 5 – 25 | **3 – 6** |
| **Shell Molding (Croning)** | Resin-bonded sand shell | Steel, iron, Al | 2 – 10 | 2.5 |
| **Investment Casting (Lost Wax)** | Ceramic shell | Super-alloys, Al, Ti | 1.5 – 4 | **0.5 – 1** |
| **Plaster Mold Casting** | Gypsum plaster | Al, Zn, Mg | 1 – 3 | 1.0 |
| **Ceramic Mold Casting** | Refractory ceramic | Tool steels, Ti | 2 – 6 | 0.8 |
| **Lost Foam Casting** | Polystyrene foam (evaporated) | Gray iron, Al | 5 – 15 | 2.5 |

### 2.3.2 Permanent Mold (Reusable) Processes

| Process | Mold Material | Typical Materials Cast | Production Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Die Casting (Hot Chamber)** | Steel die, hydraulic injection | Zn, Mg, low-melt Al | **Very high** (up to 500 shots/hr) |
| **Die Casting (Cold Chamber)** | Steel die, separate ladle | Al, brass, Mg | High (50–100 shots/hr) |
| **Permanent Mold (Gravity)** | Cast iron/steel mold | Al, Mg, Zn | Moderate (5–50/hr) |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Rotating steel mold | Pipes, tubes, rings | Moderate to high |
| **Continuous Casting** | Water-cooled copper die | Steel slabs, billets, blooms | Very high (tonnes/hr) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sand casting is the **workhorse** of foundries worldwide (≈ 60% of all castings by tonnage). Investment casting dominates **aerospace & medical implants** because of superior dimensional accuracy. Die casting is the **king of mass production** for consumer electronics and automotive parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule (Chvorinov, 1940)

> [!NOTE]
> Chvorinov's Rule is empirically derived from the assumption that **solidification is dominated by heat conduction through the mold wall** and that the mold's thermal capacity is much larger than the casting's.

### 3.1.1 Governing Heat-Transfer Equation

Consider a casting of volume $V$ and surface area $A$ losing heat by transient conduction into a semi-infinite mold of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ and conductivity $k$.

The instantaneous rate of heat loss from the casting is given by Newton's law of cooling applied to the mold–metal interface:

$$
q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T(t)
$$

where $h$ is the interfacial heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) and $\Delta T(t) = T_{metal} - T_{mold}$ is the instantaneous temperature difference.

### 3.1.2 Energy Balance on the Casting

The thermal energy released by the casting equals the latent heat of fusion $L$ released during phase change plus sensible cooling. For the purpose of solidification time, we approximate that the dominant resistance is the mold's heat-up:

$$
\rho V L = h A \int_{0}^{t_s} \Delta T(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Assuming the mold acts as a *semi-infinite medium* with constant surface temperature $T_s$, the interfacial temperature $\Delta T$ decays approximately as $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{t}}$ (classical 1-D transient conduction solution).

### 3.1.3 Integration and Final Form

Substituting $\Delta T(t) = \dfrac{\Delta T_0}{\sqrt{t}}$:

$$
\rho V L = h A \Delta T_0 \int_{0}^{t_s} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\tau}} \, d\tau = h A \Delta T_0 \cdot 2 \sqrt{t_s}
$$

Rearranging for $t_s$:

$$
t_s = \left( \dfrac{\rho L}{2 h \Delta T_0} \right)^{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{2}
$$

The grouped terms on the right (except $V/A$) are collected into a single *mold constant* $B$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{2}\,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The $(V/A)$ term is called the **modulus** of the casting. A casting with a *small* modulus (thin, spread-out geometry) cools fast; a casting with a *large* modulus (compact, chunky) cools slowly. **Risers are always designed to have a *larger* modulus than the casting they feed.**

### 3.1.4 Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A steel plate casting has dimensions $200\,\text{mm} \times 100\,\text{mm} \times 25\,\text{mm}$. It is to be fed by a cylindrical riser of diameter $D$ and height $H = 1.5D$. The mold constant $B$ is identical for both casting and riser. Determine the minimum riser diameter $D$ such that the riser solidifies at least **20% slower** than the casting.

**Step 1 — Modulus of the Casting (assume plate is poured in a flat orientation):**

The two large faces contribute most to heat loss. With *six* faces of area $2(200\times100) + 2(200\times25) + 2(100\times25)$:

$$
A_c = 2(200)(100) + 2(200)(25) + 2(100)(25) = 40{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 55{,}000 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

$$
V_c = 200 \times 100 \times 25 = 500{,}000 \;\text{mm}^3
$$

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{55{,}000} \approx 9.09 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Modulus of the Cylindrical Riser (top face is insulated, only side + bottom count):**

$$
V_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 (1.5D) = \dfrac{3\pi}{8} D^3
$$

$$
A_r = \pi D H + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \pi D (1.5D) + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 1.5\pi D^2 + 0.25\pi D^2 = 1.75\pi D^2
$$

$$
M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r} = \dfrac{\frac{3\pi}{8} D^3}{1.75\pi D^2} = \dfrac{3 D}{8 \times 1.75} = \dfrac{3 D}{14} = \dfrac{D}{4.667}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 20% Safety Margin:**

$$
M_r \geq 1.20 \cdot M_c
$$

$$
\dfrac{D}{4.667} \geq 1.20 \times 9.09
$$

$$
D \geq 1.20 \times 9.09 \times 4.667 \approx 50.9 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_{min} \approx 51 \;\text{mm},\quad H = 1.5D \approx 77 \;\text{mm}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the *assumption* (e.g., "top face of riser insulated" or "no superheat contribution"). Marks are deducted in 14-mark problems when assumptions are not explicit.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting a Casting Process

| Selection Criterion | Sand | Investment | Die | Centrifugal | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Volume** | Low–Med | Low–Med | **Very High** | Med–High | **Very High** |
| **Dimensional Accuracy (mm/mm)** | ±1.0 | ±0.1 | ±0.05 | ±0.5 | ±0.2 |
| **Surface Finish (µm Ra)** | 5–25 | 1.5–4 | 1–3 | 5–15 | 3–10 |
| **Min. Section Thickness** | 3–6 mm | 0.5–1 mm | 0.8 mm | 4 mm | 50 mm (slabs) |
| **Max. Size** | **Unlimited** | ~1 m | ~0.5 m | ~3 m dia | Continuous |
| **Tooling Cost** | **Very Low** | Moderate | **Very High** | High | **Very High** |
| **Material Wastage** | High | Low | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Typical Industry** | Heavy machinery | Aerospace | Auto/Electronics | Pipes/Cylinders | Steel mills |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite):**
> - **Prototype / One-off** → Sand casting
> - **Complex, high-accuracy, exotic alloy** → Investment casting
> - **Mass production of non-ferrous** → Die casting
> - **Axisymmetric, hollow parts** → Centrifugal
> - **Constant-cross-section, high-volume stock** → Continuous

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Generic Casting Operation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram captures the **eight-step generic casting process** introduced in Section 2.1. Each node is intentionally alphanumeric-prefixed to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 - Pattern and Core Preparation] --> A2[Step 2 - Mold Preparation]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 - Core Setting and Mold Assembly]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 - Melting of Metal in Furnace]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 - Pouring into Mold Cavity]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 - Solidification and Cooling]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 - Shakeout and Cleaning]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 - Inspection and Machining]
    A8 --> A9[Finished Casting Component]

    B1[Pattern Allowance - Shrinkage] -.-> A1
    B2[Pattern Allowance - Draft] -.-> A1
    B3[Pattern Allowance - Machining] -.-> A1
    B4[Gating System Design] -.-> A5
    B5[Riser Design V over A Modulus] -.-> A6
    B6[Heat Transfer and Solidification] -.-> A6

    classDef stepBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef auxBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9 stepBox
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 auxBox
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Casting Process Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Casting Processes] --> C1[Expendable Mold - Single Use]
    C0 --> C2[Permanent Mold - Reusable]

    C1 --> D1[Sand Casting]
    C1 --> D2[Shell Molding]
    C1 --> D3[Investment Lost Wax]
    C1 --> D4[Plaster Mold]
    C1 --> D5[Lost Foam]

    C2 --> E1[Die Casting]
    E1 --> E2[Hot Chamber]
    E1 --> E3[Cold Chamber]
    C2 --> E4[Permanent Mold Gravity]
    C2 --> E5[Centrifugal Casting]
    C2 --> E6[Continuous Casting]

    D1 --> F1[Green Sand]
    D1 --> F2[Dry Sand]
    D1 --> F3[No Bake or Chemical]

    classDef root fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef exp fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef perm fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class C0 root
    class C1,D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 exp

    class F1,F2,F3 leaf
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Riser and Gate Interaction During Solidification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pouring Cup] --> P2[Sprue]
    P2 --> P3[Runner]
    P3 --> P4[Gate]
    P4 --> P5[Casting Cavity]
    P5 --> P6[Riser Feeds Shrinkage]
    P5 --> P7[Heat Loss to Mold]
    P6 --> P8[Last to Solidify]
    P7 --> P9[Directional Solidification]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Sound Casting Free of Shrinkage Porosity]

    classDef flowBox fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef outcomeBox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8,P9 flowBox
    class P10 outcomeBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams above cover (1) the *process flow*, (2) the *classification taxonomy*, and (3) the *riser–gating functional architecture* — all of which are favorite 14-mark KTU questions under the heading "Explain the casting process with a neat sketch."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Q: Define "casting" and list any four advantages of the casting process.**

**Model Answer:**
Casting is a manufacturing process in which a molten metal is poured into a mold cavity of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then removed to obtain the finished part.

**Four advantages:**
1. Complex and intricate shapes with internal cavities can be produced.
2. Very large parts (several tonnes) can be cast, which are impossible to forge or machine.
3. Wide range of materials — ferrous, non-ferrous, alloys — can be cast.
4. Low tooling cost for small production volumes compared to forging or stamping.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
**Q: Differentiate between a "pattern" and a "core" in foundry practice.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pattern | Core |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Creates the **external** mold cavity | Creates **internal** cavities/holes |
| Material | Wood, metal, plastic | Bonded sand, ceramic |
| Placement | Removed before pouring | Remains *inside* the mold during pouring |
| Reusability | Reusable across many castings | Usually expendable (single use) |
| Baking | Not required | Baked at 200–300 °C for strength |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **steps involved in the sand casting process** with a neat sketch. Discuss the function of the **gating system** and **riser**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Chvorinov's rule** problem: a casting of $V = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$ and surface area $A = 7.5 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^2$ is to be fed by a riser with a modulus $(V/A)$ that is 25% higher. If $B = 180\,\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-4}$, determine (i) the solidification time of the casting, and (ii) the minimum volume of the riser. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a)

**The Eight Steps of Sand Casting:**
1. **Pattern preparation** — Made slightly oversized using allowances (shrinkage ≈ 1–2%, draft ≈ 1°–2°, machining ≈ 2–3 mm).
2. **Mold preparation** — Green sand (sand + clay + water) is packed around the pattern in the *flask* and rammed. Vents are pierced.
3. **Core placement** — Pre-baked cores are set into the drag using core prints.
4. **Mold assembly** — Cope and drag are closed and clamped; weights are applied.
5. **Pouring** — Molten metal is poured at the correct *pouring temperature* through the *sprue → runner → gate* system.
6. **Solidification** — Heat flows outward into the sand; directional solidification is engineered by chills and risers.
7. **Shakeout** — The flask is inverted, sand is shaken off, and the casting is lifted out.
8. **Fettling & inspection** — Runners and risers are cut off (chipped, sawed, or torched); surfaces are cleaned and inspected.

**Function of the Gating System:**
- Controls the *flow rate* of molten metal into the cavity.
- Minimizes turbulence (which causes oxide inclusions and gas entrapment).
- Traps slag and dross via *skimmers* and *strainers* in the runner.

**Function of the Riser:**
- Acts as a *molten reservoir* that feeds the casting as it shrinks during solidification.
- Must solidify *last* — designed with a higher $(V/A)$ modulus than the casting.
- The riser compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** (typically 3–6% for steels).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b)

**Given:**
- Casting volume: $V_c = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$
- Casting surface area: $A_c = 7.5 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^2$
- Mold constant: $B = 180\,\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-4}$
- Riser modulus is 25% higher than casting.

**Step 1 — Compute the casting modulus:**

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{7.5 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.016 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Chvorinov's Rule to the casting:**

$$
t_{c} = B \cdot M_c^{\,2} = 180 \times (0.016)^2 = 180 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_c = 0.0461 \;\text{s}\,}
$$

> (Note: This tiny value is for a thin plate-like example. In real KTU problems with $V$ in cm³ and $B$ in s/cm⁴, the answer will be on the order of *minutes to hours*.)

**Step 3 — Riser modulus (25% higher):**

$$
M_r = 1.25 \times M_c = 1.25 \times 0.016 = 0.020 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute riser solidification time:**

$$
t_r = B \cdot M_r^{\,2} = 180 \times (0.020)^2 = 180 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0720 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — For a *spherical* riser (best modulus per unit volume), $A = (36\pi V^2)^{1/3}$ and $M = V/A$ gives:**

$$
V_r = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3, \quad A_r = 4 \pi R^2, \quad M_r = \dfrac{R}{3}
$$

So:

$$
R = 3 \times M_r = 3 \times 0.020 = 0.060 \;\text{m}
$$

$$
V_r = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (0.060)^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_r \approx 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3 = 905 \;\text{cm}^3\,}
$$

**Verification:** $t_r / t_c = (1.25)^2 = 1.5625 > 1$ ✓ — Riser solidifies **56% slower**, satisfying the design criterion.

---

### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a **classification chart**, describe **any four expendable-mold casting processes** and state two industrial applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical casting of diameter 150 mm and length 400 mm is cast vertically. Calculate the **solidification time** using Chvorinov's rule, given $B = 90\,\text{s/cm}^4$. Compare it with a **side-riser cylindrical** of diameter 100 mm and height 150 mm, and verify whether the riser freezes last. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a)

**Classification Chart (already shown in Section 4.2 Mermaid tree).**

**Four Expendable-Mold Processes:**

| # | Process | Description | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sand Casting** | Molten metal poured into green/dry sand mold packed around a pattern. Most versatile and economical. | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, pipe fittings |
| 2 | **Investment Casting (Lost Wax)** | Wax pattern is coated with ceramic slurry, dewaxed, then fired to form a ceramic shell. Excellent surface finish and accuracy. | Turbine blades, dental implants, jewelry |
| 3 | **Shell Molding (Croning Process)** | A heated metal pattern contacts a resin-sand mixture, forming a thin (5–25 mm) hard shell. Two such shells are glued to form the mold. | Cylinder heads, connecting rods, gear housings |
| 4 | **Lost Foam Casting** | A *polystyrene* foam pattern is placed in unbonded sand; molten metal vaporizes the foam and occupies the cavity. No parting line, no draft needed. | Engine blocks (GM Saturn), manifolds, valve bodies |

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b)

**Given:**
- Casting: cylinder, $D_c = 150\,\text{mm}$, $L_c = 400\,\text{mm}$ (vertical → top face contributes)
- Riser: cylinder, $D_r = 100\,\text{mm}$, $H_r = 150\,\text{mm}$ (side riser → side + bottom contribute)
- $B = 90\,\text{s/cm}^4$

**Step 1 — Modulus of the casting (vertical cylinder, all six faces):**

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(15)^2(40) = 7068.6 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

$$
A_c = \pi D L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \pi(15)(40) + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}(15)^2
$$

$$
A_c = 1884.96 + 353.43 = 2238.4 \;\text{cm}^2
$$

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{7068.6}{2238.4} \approx 3.158 \;\text{cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Solidification time of casting:**

$$
t_c = B \cdot M_c^{\,2} = 90 \times (3.158)^2 = 90 \times 9.97 \approx \boxed{\,897 \;\text{s} \approx 14.95 \;\text{min}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Modulus of the side riser (top face insulated, side + bottom count):**

$$
V_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(10)^2(15) = 1178.1 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

$$
A_r = \pi D H + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \pi(10)(15) + \dfrac{\pi}{4}(10)^2 = 471.24 + 78.54 = 549.78 \;\text{cm}^2
$$

$$
M_r = \dfrac{1178.1}{549.78} \approx 2.143 \;\text{cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Solidification time of riser:**

$$
t_r = 90 \times (2.143)^2 = 90 \times 4.59 \approx 413 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
t_r = 413 \;\text{s} \;<\; t_c = 897 \;\text{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **❌ DESIGN FAILED!** The riser solidifies *before* the casting. This is a classic KTU 14-mark trap. The riser would be ineffective at feeding shrinkage, leading to **centerline porosity / shrinkage cavities** in the casting.

**Step 6 — Required correction:**
The riser must satisfy $M_r \geq M_c$. To freeze *last*, the riser should have a *larger* modulus. Using a riser with $D = 150\,\text{mm}$ and $H = 200\,\text{mm}$:

$$
M_r^{new} = \dfrac{\frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2(20)}{\pi(15)(20) + \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2} = \dfrac{3534.3}{942.5 + 176.7} = \dfrac{3534.3}{1119.2} \approx 3.158 \;\text{cm}
$$

This equals $M_c$, so a *slightly taller* riser (e.g., $H = 230\,\text{mm}$) would satisfy $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in KTU 14-Mark Casting Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting assumptions** (e.g., "riser top face is insulated" or "no superheat"). KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing SI and CGS units (e.g., $V$ in m³ and $B$ in s/cm⁴) is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. **Pick ONE unit system and stick to it.**
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor** (typically 1.2–1.25) when designing a riser. Always state: *"a 20% safety margin is applied."*
> 4. **Confusing $V/A$ with $A/V$** — The modulus is always $V/A$, never $A/V$. Many students flip this.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — Always end a Chvorinov problem with: *"Since $t_r > t_c$, the riser will solidify last ✓."*
> 6. **Mermaid / sketch errors in exam** — Use plain, labeled block diagrams (rectangle = mold, oval = riser, arrows = flow). The Mermaid diagrams in these notes are for *revision only*; in the exam, draw by hand.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Overview of Casting Processes**

- [x] **Definition**: Casting = liquid → mold → solidification → solid part (primary shaping).
- [x] **Eight steps**: Pattern prep → Mold prep → Core setting → Melting → Pouring → Solidification → Shakeout → Inspection.
- [x] **Mold classification**: Expendable (sand, investment, shell, lost foam, plaster) vs. Permanent (die, gravity, centrifugal, continuous).
- [x] **Sand casting** = workhorse (≈ 60% tonnage); **Investment** = precision; **Die** = mass production; **Centrifugal** = pipes/cylinders; **Continuous** = steel slabs.
- [x] **Pattern allowances** (must remember all four): **Shrinkage** + **Draft** + **Machining** + **Distortion**.
- [x] **Chvorinov's Rule**: $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^2$ — the *single most important equation* in foundry design.
- [x] **Riser rule**: Modulus of riser $\geq$ Modulus of casting (always apply a **20% safety factor**).
- [x] **Caine's modification**: $t_s = B (V/A)^n$ with $n = 1.5$–$2.0$ for die casting (faster cooling).
- [x] **Shrinkage sequence**: Liquid contraction → Solidification shrinkage → Solid contraction (only the first two are *fed* by the riser).
- [x] **Selection of casting process** is governed by: Production volume, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, min section thickness, tooling cost, and material.
- [x] **Common exam traps**: Unit mixing, modulus inversion, missing assumptions, no safety factor, missing verification statement.
- [x] **Sand casting is the default** when in doubt; for high-volume non-ferrous → die casting; for exotic alloys & precision → investment casting.

> **End of Module 1 Topic: Overview of Casting Processes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations of Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations of Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Casting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCPET402 - Module 1)**
> *Casting is a primary metal shaping manufacturing process in which a molten or liquid material (typically a metal or alloy) is poured into a prepared cavity of a mould (made of sand, metal, ceramic, or other refractory material), allowed to solidify, and then extracted to obtain a finished or near-net-shape product.*

In simpler engineering terminology, casting is the process of **"pouring molten metal into a shaped hole and letting it harden."** Once the metal cools and solidifies, it takes the exact shape of the cavity, which is then called a **casting**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like making ice-cubes in a refrigerator, but with HOT metal instead of water and a sand/metal mould instead of a plastic tray!**

When you pour water into an ice-tray and freeze it, the ice takes the shape of the tray. **Casting works exactly the same way**, except:
- The liquid is **molten metal** (e.g., molten iron at ~1200 °C, aluminium at ~660 °C).
- The "tray" is a carefully engineered **mould** built to withstand extreme heat.
- After solidification, the metal part is called the **casting**, and the mould is broken (in sand casting) or opened (in die casting) to retrieve the part.

This analogy helps a first-time learner immediately visualize why casting is one of the **oldest (≈ 5000 years old)** and most flexible manufacturing processes in human history.

---

## 1.3 Position of Casting in Manufacturing

Casting belongs to the family of **primary shaping processes** (also called *forming processes*). It converts a **raw material in liquid state** into a **solid geometric product**.

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Primary Shaping** | Casting, Powder Metallurgy, Forging, Rolling |
| **Secondary Shaping** | Machining, Bending, Deep Drawing |
| **Joining** | Welding, Brazing, Soldering |
| **Finishing** | Polishing, Painting, Anodizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Casting is often the **first process taught** in any manufacturing course because it forms the foundation for understanding mould design, gating systems, risers, and metallurgy of solidification — all of which appear in later modules of PCPET402.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis: Advantages & Limitations of Casting

## 2.1 Advantages of Casting

Casting has earned its dominant position in industry because of several inherent benefits. These can be grouped into **Geometrical, Economic, Metallurgical, and Operational** advantages.

### 🟢 A. Geometrical Flexibility

1. **Complex Shapes:** Almost any intricate shape — internal cavities, curved surfaces, undercuts — can be produced. Parts that are impossible or uneconomical by machining/forging can be cast.
2. **Size Range:** From a few grams (jewellery) to hundreds of tons (ship propellers, turbine housings, machine tool beds).
3. **Hollow Sections:** Hollow parts like pipes, pump bodies, valve bodies are cast directly using **cores**.

### 🟢 B. Economic Advantages

1. **Low Cost for Complex Geometry:** For intricate parts, casting is cheaper than machining from a solid block because the material is placed exactly where needed.
2. **Less Material Wastage:** Material utilization can exceed **90 %** (especially in die casting and investment casting).
3. **Low Tooling Cost (Sand Casting):** Wooden or plastic patterns are inexpensive compared to forging dies or machining fixtures.
4. **Mass Production:** Die casting, shell moulding, and investment casting allow **high production rates** at low per-unit cost.

### 🟢 C. Metallurgical Advantages

1. **Wide Material Range:** Ferrous (cast iron, steel), non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Mg, Zn alloys), and even non-metals (plastics, ceramics) can be cast.
2. **Controlled Properties:** By controlling cooling rate, alloy composition, and heat treatment, desired mechanical properties can be obtained.
3. **Directional Solidification:** Engineered using chills and risers to produce **sound castings** free of shrinkage cavities.

### 🟢 D. Operational Advantages

1. **Net-shape / Near-net-shape Production:** Minimal subsequent machining required.
2. **Versatility of Mould Materials:** Sand, plaster, ceramic, graphite, metal — chosen based on application.
3. **Reproducibility:** Once pattern is made, thousands of identical castings can be produced.

---

## 2.2 Limitations of Casting

Despite its versatility, casting has notable drawbacks that engineers must understand when selecting a manufacturing route.

### 🔴 A. Quality Limitations

1. **Porosity and Shrinkage Defects:** Gas porosity, shrinkage cavities, and micro-porosity are common unless properly designed.
2. **Lower Strength Compared to Wrought Products:** Cast structure has coarse grains, dendritic structure, and weaker grain boundaries → lower ductility and impact strength compared to forged parts.
3. **Surface Finish:** Sand castings have a relatively rough surface (typically **Ra 12.5 – 25 µm**), requiring secondary finishing.
4. **Dimensional Accuracy:** Tolerances are generally looser than machining or forging. Typical sand casting tolerance: **± 0.8 mm to ± 1.6 mm**.

### 🔴 B. Process Limitations

1. **High Initial Tooling Cost (Die Casting, Investment Casting):** Steel dies and ceramic shells are expensive; justified only for large volumes.
2. **Long Lead Time for Patterns:** Pattern design and manufacture can take weeks for complex castings.
3. **Labour Intensive:** Sand casting requires skilled moulders; not easily automated for small batches.
4. **Material Limitations:** Metals with very high melting points (e.g., tungsten ~3422 °C) or reactive metals (titanium) require special casting techniques.

### 🔴 C. Safety & Environmental Limitations

1. **High Energy Consumption:** Furnaces require substantial energy to melt metal.
2. **Health Hazards:** Exposure to fumes, silica dust (silicosis risk in sand moulding), and molten metal splashes.
3. **Environmental Concerns:** Disposal of used sand, emissions from foundries.

---

## 2.3 Comparative Summary Table — Advantages vs Limitations

| Aspect | Advantage ✅ | Limitation ❌ |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Shape Complexity** | Almost any complex shape possible | Draft angles & undercuts complicate design |
| **Size** | Few grams to hundreds of tons | Very large castings need special furnaces |
| **Cost** | Cheap for complex parts in bulk | High tooling cost for die/investment casting |
| **Material Range** | Wide (ferrous & non-ferrous) | Reactive/high-melting metals difficult |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Tailorable via alloying/cooling | Lower ductility than forged parts |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | Net-shape capability | Looser tolerances vs. machining |
| **Surface Finish** | Acceptable in many cast types | Rough in sand casting (needs finishing) |
| **Defects** | Defects can be minimised | Porosity, shrinkage, inclusions common |
| **Lead Time** | Mass production possible once pattern ready | Pattern manufacture takes time |
| **Safety/Environment** | Mature, well-studied process | Fumes, dust, high energy use |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Metrics (For Numerical/Analytical Questions)

Although "Advantages and Limitations" is largely descriptive, KTU examiners sometimes test student awareness of **quantitative metrics** that differentiate casting from competing processes.

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Typical Tolerance | ± 0.8 to ± 1.6 mm | ± 0.05 to ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.1 to ± 0.3 mm |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | 12.5 – 25 µm | 0.8 – 1.6 µm | 1.6 – 3.2 µm |
| Min. Section Thickness | ~ 3 mm | ~ 0.6 mm | ~ 0.5 mm |
| Production Rate | Low – Medium | Very High | Low – Medium |
| Tooling Cost | Low | Very High | High |
| Typical Lot Size | 1 – 1000 | 10,000 + | 100 – 5000 |
| Common Alloys | Fe, Al, Cu | Zn, Al, Mg | Steel, super-alloys, Ti |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Engineers in product design (e.g., automotive engine blocks, pump housings, turbine blades) use this comparison table to **select the right casting process** based on required strength, finish, tolerance, and volume.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Analytical Matrix: Casting vs Competing Processes

## 3.1 Why "Advantages" Must Be Understood Analytically

In KTU 2024 scheme examinations, a 14-mark question on "Advantages and Limitations" usually requires the student to:
1. **List** the advantages/limitations clearly.
2. **Justify** each with a *why* (mechanical, metallurgical, or economic reasoning).
3. **Compare** casting with at least one competing process (forging, machining, welding).

This section provides the **analytical reasoning matrix** that justifies each point — the exact depth KTU examiners reward.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Reasoning — Why Each Advantage Exists

| # | Advantage | Underlying Scientific/Economic Reason |
|---|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Complex shapes possible | Liquid metal flows into any cavity geometry — limited only by mould design and fluidity of metal. |
| 2 | Hollow sections possible | Sand cores can be placed inside mould; metal flows around them forming hollows. |
| 3 | Wide size range | Moulds can be made from grams (jewellery investment moulds) to tonnes (large foundry pits). |
| 4 | Low material wastage | Material is added only where needed — no swarf/chips like in machining. Yield = (Casting mass / Melt mass) × 100 %. |
| 5 | Wide material compatibility | Most metals/alloys can be melted and poured (provided melting point is below furnace capability). |
| 6 | High production rate | In die casting, cycle time = 30 seconds to few minutes. |
| 7 | Net/near-net shape | Casting directly approximates final geometry — little post-processing. |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Reasoning — Why Each Limitation Exists

| # | Limitation | Underlying Scientific Reason |
|---|------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Porosity & shrinkage | Liquid → solid involves ~ 3 – 7 % volumetric shrinkage; trapped gases form pores. |
| 2 | Lower strength than forging | Cast structure has dendritic/columnar grains with weak grain boundaries; forging refines grains. |
| 3 | Rough surface | Sand grains imprint on casting surface. |
| 4 | Looser tolerances | Moulds (especially sand) deform, expand, and erode. |
| 5 | High tooling cost (die casting) | Hardened steel dies + cooling channels + ejection systems expensive. |
| 6 | Long pattern lead time | Pattern design, fabrication, and testing require skilled labour and time. |
| 7 | Safety hazards | High temperatures (1000–1600 °C), molten metal splash, dust, fumes. |

---

## 3.4 Casting vs Forging — A Comparative Analysis (KTU favourite)

| Parameter | Casting | Forging |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Process State | Liquid → Solid | Solid → Deformed |
| Grain Structure | Coarse dendritic | Refined, directional |
| Tensile Strength | Lower | Higher (up to 30 % more) |
| Impact Toughness | Lower | Higher |
| Shape Complexity | Very High | Limited (open-die / closed-die) |
| Size Range | Very wide | Wide |
| Cost for Complex Shape | Lower | Higher |
| Tooling Cost | Moderate – High | High |
| Defects | Porosity, shrinkage | Cracks, laps, folds |

### Key Engineering Insight

$$
\text{Strength}_{\text{forged}} > \text{Strength}_{\text{cast}}
$$

$$
\text{Shape Complexity}_{\text{cast}} \gg \text{Shape Complexity}_{\text{forged}}
$$

> **Selection Rule:** If the application demands **high strength + critical load-bearing** (e.g., crankshafts, connecting rods, landing gear) → **Forging**.
> If the application demands **complex geometry + moderate strength** (e.g., engine blocks, pump housings, decorative items) → **Casting**.

---

## 3.5 Casting vs Machining — A Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Casting | Machining |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Starting Material | Molten metal | Solid bar/billet |
| Material Utilization | High (≈ 90 %) | Low (≈ 30 – 50 %, swarf loss) |
| Complexity | Very High | Limited by tool access |
| Tolerance | ± 0.8 mm (sand) | ± 0.01 mm (precision) |
| Surface Finish | Rough | Very Smooth |
| Cost for Complex Geometry | Low | Very High |

---

## 3.6 Decision Matrix — When to Choose Casting?

A step-by-step logical flow used by design engineers:

$$
\boxed{\text{Is the part geometrically complex with internal cavities?}} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Consider Casting}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Are strength/ductility requirements extreme?}} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Consider Forging over Casting}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Is the production volume very high (>10,000 units)?}} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Die Casting or Shell Moulding}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Are tight tolerances and fine surface finish mandatory?}} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Investment Casting or post-machining}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Casting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pattern Design and Fabrication] --> B[Mould Preparation]
    B --> C[Melting of Metal in Furnace]
    C --> D[Pouring of Molten Metal into Mould]
    D --> E[Solidification and Cooling]
    E --> F[Shakeout / Mould Break]
    F --> G[Cleaning and Fettling]
    G --> H[Inspection and Quality Check]
    H --> I[Finished Casting]

    A1[Advantages realised here: shape, size, complexity] -.-> A
    B1[Limitations appear here: defects, tolerances] -.-> B
    D1[Liquid metal flow ability exploited] -.-> D
    E1[Shrinkage and porosity may form] -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Advantages vs Limitations — Visual Balance Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADV [Advantages of Casting]
        A1[Complex Shapes]
        A2[Wide Size Range]
        A3[Hollow Sections via Cores]
        A4[Wide Material Selection]
        A5[High Material Yield]
        A6[Mass Production Capability]
        A7[Net Shape Capability]
    end

    subgraph LIM [Limitations of Casting]
        L1[Porosity and Shrinkage Defects]
        L2[Lower Strength vs Forging]
        L3[Rough Surface Finish]
        L4[Loose Dimensional Tolerance]
        L5[High Tooling Cost for Die Casting]
        L6[Safety and Environmental Hazards]
        L7[Long Pattern Lead Time]
    end

    DECISION{Casting Selection Decision}
    PART[Product Design Requirements]
    PART --> DECISION
    ADV --> DECISION
    LIM --> DECISION
    DECISION --> CHOOSE[Choose Casting Process Variant]
```

## 4.3 Application-Based Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ[Part Requirements] --> Q1{Need Complex Internal Cavities?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> CAST1[Use Sand or Investment Casting]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Production Volume > 10,000?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CAST2[Use Die Casting]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Extremely Tight Tolerance Needed?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> CAST3[Use Investment Casting plus Machining]
    Q3 -- No --> CAST4[Use Sand Casting with Light Finishing]
    CAST1 --> FINAL[Selected Process]
    CAST2 --> FINAL
    CAST3 --> FINAL
    CAST4 --> FINAL
```

## 4.4 Casting vs Alternative Processes — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph START[Raw Material Forms]
        R1[Liquid Metal]
        R2[Solid Bar or Billet]
        R3[Metal Powder]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSES[Shaping Routes]
        P1[Casting]
        P2[Forging]
        P3[Machining]
        P4[Powder Metallurgy]
    end

    R1 --> P1
    R2 --> P2
    R2 --> P3
    R3 --> P4

    P1 --> OUT1[Complex Geometry plus Moderate Strength]
    P2 --> OUT2[High Strength plus Simple Geometry]
    P3 --> OUT3[High Tolerance plus Smooth Finish]
    P4 --> OUT4[Net Shape plus Controlled Porosity]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram (4.1) traces a single casting operation from pattern to finished product. The second (4.2) shows a balanced view of why engineers *choose* and *avoid* casting. The third (4.3) is a real decision-tree used in industry. The fourth (4.4) places casting in the broader manufacturing family.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: List any six advantages of casting process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The casting process offers several advantages:
1. **Complex geometries:** Intricate shapes with internal cavities can be produced directly without joining.
2. **Wide size range:** Castings can be made from a few grams (jewellery) to several hundred tonnes (propellers, machine beds).
3. **Wide material compatibility:** Almost all metals and alloys — ferrous, non-ferrous, and even non-metals — can be cast.
4. **High material yield:** Material utilization exceeds 90 % in many casting methods; little waste compared to machining.
5. **Mass production capability:** Methods like die casting allow rapid, repeatable production of thousands of parts.
6. **Net-shape / near-net-shape production:** Minimal subsequent machining is required, reducing overall cost.

> Each point correctly explained: **0.5 mark × 6 = 3 marks** (as per KTU valuation key).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]

**Q: Mention any three limitations of casting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Porosity and shrinkage defects:** Volumetric contraction during solidification (~ 3 – 7 %) and gas entrapment cause internal voids that reduce strength.
2. **Lower mechanical strength:** Cast structure has coarse dendritic grains; forged or rolled parts show refined grains and superior toughness.
3. **Loose dimensional tolerance and rough surface:** Especially in sand casting, tolerances are ± 0.8 – 1.6 mm and surface roughness is Ra 12.5 – 25 µm, requiring secondary finishing.

> Each valid point with brief justification: **1 mark × 3 = 3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B Module-1 questions carry **14 marks**, with each sub-question typically carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice).

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper Style]

**Q: Explain in detail the various advantages and limitations of the casting process. Compare casting with forging and machining processes.**

#### (a) Advantages of Casting (7 Marks)

Casting is one of the most widely used manufacturing processes due to the following advantages:

1. **Geometrical Flexibility:**
   - Almost any complex shape — including internal cavities, curved profiles, and undercuts — can be produced.
   - Hollow sections can be easily made using **sand cores** without joining operations.

2. **Wide Size Capability:**
   - Castings range from a few grams (investment-cast jewellery) to several hundred tonnes (large turbine housings, ship propellers).

3. **Wide Material Range:**
   - Cast iron, steel, aluminium, copper, brass, magnesium, zinc, and even plastics/ceramics can be cast.

4. **High Material Yield:**
   - Material utilization is typically 80 – 95 %, much higher than machining (which loses material as chips).

5. **Net-Shape Production:**
   - Castings approximate final geometry, reducing or eliminating machining cost.

6. **Mass Production Suitability:**
   - Die casting (cycle time 30 s – 2 min) and shell moulding allow high-volume, low-cost production.

7. **Economic for Complex Parts:**
   - Pattern cost is offset by low per-unit cost in bulk production.

#### (b) Limitations of Casting (7 Marks)

1. **Defects:** Porosity, shrinkage cavities, gas holes, inclusions, and cold shuts are common if process is poorly controlled.
2. **Lower Mechanical Strength:** Coarse dendritic grain structure gives lower ductility and impact toughness compared to forged parts.
3. **Surface Finish:** Sand castings have rough surfaces (Ra 12.5 – 25 µm) requiring fettling, grinding, or shot blasting.
4. **Loose Tolerances:** Sand casting accuracy is ± 0.8 to ± 1.6 mm; precision processes are needed for tighter tolerances.
5. **High Tooling Cost for Die/Investment Casting:** Steel dies and ceramic shells are expensive; only justified for large volumes.
6. **Safety & Environmental Hazards:** Exposure to molten metal, fumes, and silica dust; high energy consumption in furnaces.
7. **Long Pattern Lead Time:** Pattern design and manufacture can take weeks for complex castings.

#### Comparison with Forging and Machining

| Feature | Casting | Forging | Machining |
|---------|---------|---------|-----------|
| Starting state | Liquid | Solid | Solid |
| Strength | Moderate | Highest | Depends on stock |
| Shape complexity | Very high | Low – moderate | Moderate |
| Material wastage | Low | Low | High (chips) |
| Tolerance | ± 0.8 mm | ± 0.2 mm | ± 0.01 mm |

**Conclusion:** Casting is preferred when complex geometry and moderate strength are required; forging is preferred for high-strength critical components; machining is used for tight tolerances.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Valuation Key):**
> - Sub-part (a) listing + explaining 7 advantages with examples: **7 marks**
> - Sub-part (b) listing + explaining 7 limitations + comparison table: **7 marks**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q: Discuss the relative merits and demerits of sand casting, die casting, and investment casting. State typical applications of each.**

#### (a) Sand Casting (7 Marks)

**Merits:**
- Lowest tooling cost (wooden/plastic patterns).
- Suitable for very large castings (up to hundreds of tonnes).
- Wide alloy range — ferrous and non-ferrous.
- Easy to change design.

**Demerits:**
- Rough surface finish (Ra 12.5 – 25 µm).
- Looser tolerance (± 0.8 – 1.6 mm).
- Slower production rate; labour-intensive.
- Higher defect probability (porosity, sand inclusions).

**Applications:** Engine blocks, machine tool beds, large pump housings, railway components.

#### (b) Die Casting and Investment Casting (7 Marks)

**Die Casting — Merits:**
- High dimensional accuracy (± 0.05 mm).
- Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.8 – 1.6 µm).
- Very high production rate (cycle time 30 s – 2 min).
- Good mechanical properties.

**Die Casting — Demerits:**
- Very high die cost (only justified for > 10,000 parts).
- Limited to low-melting alloys (Zn, Al, Mg).
- Porosity may occur from trapped gases.

**Die Casting Applications:** Carburettors, gear boxes, mobile phone housings, automotive fittings.

**Investment Casting — Merits:**
- Excellent surface finish and accuracy.
- Suitable for intricate shapes with thin sections (< 1 mm).
- Wide material range including super-alloys and titanium.
- Minimal machining required.

**Investment Casting — Demerits:**
- High process cost (ceramic shell, wax patterns).
- Slow, multi-step process.
- Limited size range.

**Investment Casting Applications:** Turbine blades, surgical implants, jewellery, firearm components.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting |
|---------|--------------|-------------|--------------------|
| Tooling cost | Low | Very high | High |
| Tolerance | Loose | Tight | Tight |
| Surface finish | Rough | Excellent | Very good |
| Production rate | Low | Very high | Low – medium |
| Material range | Very wide | Limited (low mp) | Very wide |
| Typical size | Any | Small – medium | Small – medium |

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Sub-part (a) Sand casting analysis: **7 marks**
> - Sub-part (b) Die casting + investment casting analysis + table: **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Advantages & Limitations of Casting" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing without justification:** Writing "complex shapes" or "low cost" without explaining *why* — KTU examiners deduct up to 50 % marks per point if no reasoning is given.
> 2. **Mixing up casting types:** Students confuse sand casting, die casting, and investment casting advantages/limitations. Always specify *which casting type* the point refers to.
> 3. **Ignoring quantitative data:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards answers that mention tolerance values, surface roughness (Ra), and temperature ranges. Always add numbers.
> 4. **No real-world examples:** Mentioning at least 2 – 3 engineering examples (engine block, pump housing, turbine blade) earns extra credit.
> 5. **Skipping the comparison table:** A well-labelled comparison table (Casting vs Forging vs Machining) is worth 2 – 3 marks by itself.
> 6. **Wrong use of terminology:** Using "casting" loosely — instead say "sand casting," "die casting," "investment casting," etc.
> 7. **Ignoring safety/environment:** Most students forget to mention **silicosis, fume hazards, energy consumption** — these are valid limitations worth 1 – 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✏️

- **Casting** = liquid metal → mould → solidification → finished part.
- **Biggest advantage:** ability to produce **complex shapes and hollow sections** in a single operation.
- **Biggest limitation:** inherent defects (**porosity, shrinkage**) and **lower mechanical strength** than forged parts.
- **Sand casting:** cheapest, roughest, most versatile.
- **Die casting:** fastest, tightest tolerance, but expensive tooling and limited to low-mp alloys.
- **Investment casting:** best surface finish and accuracy; used for aerospace and jewellery.
- **Selection rule:** Complex shape + moderate strength + moderate volume → **Casting**.
- **Always include:** numerical tolerances (e.g., ± 0.8 mm) and surface roughness (e.g., Ra 12.5 µm) for KTU answers.
- **Mention:** at least 3 real-world examples (engine block, pump housing, turbine blade).
- **Compare** casting with forging (strength) and machining (tolerance) for full marks.
- **Acknowledge:** environmental and safety issues (silicosis, fumes, energy use).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Casting Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Casting Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Casting** is defined as a manufacturing process in which a liquid or molten material (typically a metal or alloy) is poured into a mould cavity of the desired shape, allowed to solidify within the mould, and subsequently extracted to obtain a finished or semi-finished product. The process exploits the fluid behavior of metals in their liquid state and the volumetric contraction that occurs during solidification.

**Classification of Casting Processes** refers to the systematic organization of the various methods of producing castings based on the type of mould used, the method of mould production, the molding material, the pouring mechanism, and the application suitability. The KTU 2024 Scheme module emphasizes this classification as the foundation for selecting an appropriate casting technique for engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *classify* casting processes, distinguish between *expendable* and *permanent* moulds, and identify the specific industrial applications of each sub-process. This is a frequently tested 3-mark and 7-mark question topic.

## 1.2 The Broad Classification Tree

Casting processes are broadly classified into two primary families based on the **reusability of the mould**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Category 1 — Expendable (Non-permanent) Mould Casting**
> The mould is destroyed (broken apart) to retrieve the casting. Used only once. The mould is typically made from sand, plaster, ceramics, or similar materials bonded with a binder. Suited for low-to-medium volume production and large/sized components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Category 2 — Permanent (Non-expendable) Mould Casting**
> The mould is reused for thousands to millions of casting cycles. The mould is made from metal (cast iron, steel, or graphite). Suited for high-volume, repetitive production of smaller components with high dimensional accuracy.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of casting like **baking a cake**:

- The **liquid metal** is your cake batter (a fluid that takes the shape of whatever container holds it).
- The **mould** is your cake tin.
- After cooling, the **solidified metal** is the cake, and you "demould" it.

Now, imagine two scenarios:

1. **Disposable Baking Cup (Paper cup)**: You pour batter in, bake it, and then **tear apart the paper** to release the cake. This is *expendable mould casting* — exactly like sand casting, where the sand mould is broken after each pour.

2. **Reusable Metal Cake Tin**: You pour batter in, bake it, pop out the cake, and **reuse the tin** hundreds of times. This is *permanent mould casting* — like die casting or permanent mould casting, where the metal mould survives for thousands of cycles.

The choice between these two depends on **how many cakes you want to make**, **how complex the shape is**, and **how much accuracy you need**.

## 1.4 Classification Based on Mould Material (Standard KTU Sub-classification)

The following are the principal casting processes classified by mould type:

1. **Sand Casting** — Mould made of silica sand + binder (clay, resin).
2. **Shell Mould Casting** — Mould made of thermosetting resin-bonded sand shell.
3. **Investment (Lost-Wax) Casting** — Mould made of ceramic slurry around a wax pattern.
4. **Plaster Mould Casting** — Mould made of plaster of Paris.
5. **Ceramic Mould Casting** — Mould made of refractory ceramic slurry.
6. **Die Casting** — Permanent metal mould (dies) under high pressure.
7. **Permanent Mould Casting (Gravity Die Casting)** — Permanent metal mould, gravity pour.
8. **Centrifugal Casting** — Mould rotates at high speed; centrifugal force drives the metal.
9. **Continuous Casting** — Molten metal continuously fed and solidified into a long strand.
10. **Squeeze Casting** — Combines casting and forging; metal solidifies under pressure.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard industrial metrics apply to casting classification:

- **Solidification Shrinkage of Cast Iron:** 1.0% to 2.5% (volumetric).
- **Solidification Shrinkage of Steel:** 3.0% to 6.0% (volumetric).
- **Melting Point of Cast Iron:** ~1150 °C to 1300 °C.
- **Melting Point of Pure Aluminum:** **660 °C** (commonly cast in die casting at ~700 °C).
- **Density of Molten Steel:** approximately **7.0 g/cm³** (used in centrifugal casting force calculations).
- **Permissible Dimensional Tolerance in Sand Casting:** ±0.8 mm to ±1.6 mm.
- **Surface Finish (Ra) in Sand Casting:** 12.5 µm to 25 µm.
- **Surface Finish (Ra) in Die Casting:** 0.8 µm to 1.6 µm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decision Logic for Classification

The classification of casting processes is governed by **four pivotal decision drivers**, each of which determines the choice of process:

### Step 1 — Production Volume
- **Low volume (< 100 pieces/year):** Expendable mould (sand, investment, shell).
- **Medium volume (100–10,000 pieces/year):** Shell mould, permanent mould, investment.
- **High volume (> 10,000 pieces/year):** Die casting, centrifugal casting, continuous casting.

### Step 2 — Dimensional Accuracy & Surface Finish
- Tolerances below ±0.1 mm and Ra < 1.6 µm: **Die casting, investment casting**.
- Tolerances ±0.5 mm and Ra 3.2–6.3 µm: **Permanent mould, shell mould**.
- Tolerances ±1.0 mm and Ra > 12.5 µm: **Sand casting**.

### Step 3 — Complexity of Geometry
- Highly intricate, thin-walled, near-net-shape parts: **Investment casting, ceramic mould**.
- Simple to moderately complex: **Sand, permanent mould, die casting**.

### Step 4 — Metallurgical Compatibility
- **Ferrous metals (iron, steel):** Sand, investment, centrifugal, continuous.
- **Non-ferrous metals (Al, Zn, Mg, Cu):** Die casting, permanent mould, centrifugal.
- **Reactive/refractory metals (Ti, superalloys):** Investment, vacuum casting.

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU exams. Memorize the trade-off between volume, accuracy, cost, and metal compatibility.

| Process | Mould Type | Metals Cast | Surface Finish (Ra µm) | Tolerance (mm) | Production Volume | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Expendable (sand) | Ferrous & Non-ferrous | 12.5 – 25 | ±0.8 to ±1.6 | Low to Medium | Low |
| **Shell Mould** | Expendable (resin shell) | Ferrous & Non-ferrous | 3.2 – 6.3 | ±0.25 to ±0.4 | Medium | Medium |
| **Investment Casting** | Expendable (ceramic) | All incl. superalloys | 1.6 – 3.2 | ±0.1 to ±0.3 | Low to Medium | High |
| **Plaster Mould** | Expendable (plaster) | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Zn) | 1.6 – 3.2 | ±0.1 to ±0.3 | Low to Medium | Medium |
| **Die Casting** | Permanent (steel die) | Non-ferrous (Zn, Al, Mg) | 0.8 – 1.6 | ±0.05 to ±0.15 | Very High | High (initial) |
| **Permanent Mould** | Permanent (metal) | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu) | 3.2 – 6.3 | ±0.25 to ±0.4 | Medium to High | Medium |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Permanent (rotating) | Ferrous & Non-ferrous | 3.2 – 6.3 | ±0.4 to ±0.8 | Medium to High | Medium |
| **Continuous Casting** | Permanent (water-cooled) | Steel, Al, Cu | 6.3 – 12.5 | ±1.0 to ±2.0 | Very High (continuous) | High (initial) |
| **Squeeze Casting** | Permanent (pressurized) | Al, Mg | 1.6 – 3.2 | ±0.1 to ±0.3 | Medium to High | High |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The classification of casting processes is not merely academic — it directly informs **process selection in industry**:

- **Automotive Industry:** Cylinder blocks, pistons, and transmission housings are mass-produced via **die casting** (Al alloys) and **sand casting** (iron blocks).
- **Aerospace Industry:** Turbine blades with intricate cooling channels are produced by **investment casting** (lost-wax), as it allows complex internal geometries and uses superalloys.
- **Pipe and Tube Manufacturing:** Large-diameter steel pipes are made by **centrifugal casting** (spinning moulds) and **continuous casting** (slab/bloom casting).
- **Jewelry and Art:** Fine artistic and jewelry castings use **investment casting** due to its ability to reproduce intricate detail.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Massive machine tool beds and housings are made by **sand casting**, as it accommodates large sizes economically.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are most frequently asked in numerical problems related to **centrifugal casting** (GATE & KTU), and **solidification shrinkage** calculations.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force on molten metal | $F_c$ = force, $m$ = mass, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $r$ = radius | N, kg, rad/s, m |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | $N$ = rotational speed | rad/s |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{\omega^2 \cdot r}{g}$ | Gravitational "G-force" multiplier | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Static metallostatic pressure | $P$ = pressure, $\rho$ = density, $h$ = head | Pa, kg/m³, m |
| 5 | $S_v = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Shape factor (chvorinov's rule denominator) | $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area | m |
| 6 | $t_s = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | Chvorinov's rule for solidification time | $t_s$ = time, $K$ = mould constant | s |
| 7 | $\Delta V = \beta \cdot V_0$ | Volumetric shrinkage on solidification | $\beta$ = shrinkage fraction | m³ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Casting Pressure Distribution

In **centrifugal casting**, the molten metal is subjected to an apparent radial pressure distribution that is NOT hydrostatic but rather follows a parabolic distribution. Let us derive this rigorously.

### Setup

Consider a horizontal centrifugal casting mould rotating about its own axis at angular velocity $\omega$. A fluid element at radius $r$ experiences a centrifugal acceleration directed outward.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Fluid Element

Take a thin cylindrical shell of fluid at radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and height $H$ (length of mould). The mass of this element is:

$$
dm = \rho \cdot (2\pi r \cdot dr) \cdot H
$$

The centrifugal force acting on this element is:

$$
dF_c = dm \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = \rho \cdot 2\pi r \cdot H \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot dr
$$

$$
dF_c = 2\pi \rho H \omega^2 r^2 \, dr
$$

### Step 2 — Pressure Gradient

This force is balanced by the pressure difference across the shell:

$$
dP \cdot (2\pi r \cdot H) = 2\pi \rho H \omega^2 r^2 \, dr
$$

Dividing both sides by $2\pi r H$:

$$
dP = \rho \omega^2 r \, dr
$$

### Step 3 — Integration Across the Mould Wall

Integrate from the inner radius $r_i$ to the outer free surface $r_o$, where the pressure is atmospheric ($P = 0$ gauge):

$$
\int_{0}^{P} dP = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \rho \omega^2 r \, dr
$$

$$
P(r) = \frac{\rho \omega^2}{2} \left( r_o^2 - r^2 \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$
P(r) = \frac{\rho \omega^2}{2} \left( r_o^2 - r_i^2 \right) \quad \text{(at inner radius)}
$$

This is the **G-function** or **centrifugal pressure equation**, and is the basis for computing the "G-force multiplier" $G$ used in foundry process control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The pressure in centrifugal casting is NOT linearly proportional to radius (as in static hydrostatic head). It follows a **parabolic** distribution. This is a classic KTU conceptual question.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Centrifugal Casting Pressure

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A centrifugal casting mould of inner radius 100 mm rotates at 600 RPM. The molten cast iron has density 7000 kg/m³. Calculate:
(a) The centrifugal pressure at the inner mould wall.
(b) The equivalent static head of molten metal.
(c) The G-force multiplier.

### Given Data
- $r_i = 0.100$ m (inner radius)
- $r_o \approx 0.110$ m (assume outer free surface)
- $N = 600$ RPM
- $\rho = 7000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step (a): Centrifugal Pressure

Compute angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi \approx 62.83 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Now apply the derived formula:

$$
P = \frac{\rho \omega^2}{2} \left( r_o^2 - r_i^2 \right)
$$

$$
P = \frac{7000 \times (62.83)^2}{2} \left( (0.110)^2 - (0.100)^2 \right)
$$

$$
P = \frac{7000 \times 3947.8}{2} \left( 0.0121 - 0.0100 \right)
$$

$$
P = 3.5 \times 3947.8 \times 0.0021
$$

$$
P \approx 13,817,700 \times 0.0021 \approx 29,017 \text{ Pa} \approx 29.02 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Step (b): Equivalent Static Head

$$
h = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{29,017}{7000 \times 9.81} = \frac{29,017}{68,670} \approx 0.422 \text{ m}
$$

So the centrifugal pressure equals the hydrostatic pressure of a **~422 mm column** of molten cast iron.

### Step (c): G-Force Multiplier

$$
G = \frac{\omega^2 \cdot r}{g} = \frac{(62.83)^2 \times 0.105}{9.81} = \frac{3947.8 \times 0.105}{9.81} \approx 42.26
$$

The casting is effectively subjected to **42.26 times the normal gravitational force**, which is what enables high metal fluidity, fine grain structure, and dense castings.

## 3.3 Shrinkage Allowance Derivation (Sand Casting)

When a casting solidifies, it contracts. The pattern must be oversized by a **shrinkage allowance** to compensate.

$$
L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \times (1 + \alpha)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the linear shrinkage fraction (typically 0.5% to 2% for cast iron).

For volumetric shrinkage during liquid-to-solid transition:

$$
V_{liquid} = V_{solid} (1 + \beta)
$$

where $\beta$ is the volumetric shrinkage (typically 3% to 6% for steel).

**Relation between linear and volumetric shrinkage (for isotropic materials):**

$$
\beta \approx 3\alpha
$$

**Proof:**

$$
V = L^3 \Rightarrow \frac{dV}{V} = 3 \frac{dL}{L} \Rightarrow \beta = 3\alpha
$$

## 3.4 Chvorinov's Rule — Theoretical Foundation

The solidification time of a casting is governed by **Chvorinov's Rule**:

$$
t_s = K \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2
$$

where $V/A$ is the **modulus** of the casting. This non-intuitive square-law relationship arises because the heat transfer is governed by **transient heat conduction** in a semi-infinite medium, and the temperature profile evolves with the square root of time. The derivation from Fourier's law of heat conduction is standard and expected in KTU Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Casting Processes

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **complete hierarchical classification** of casting processes as per KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Casting Processes] --> B[Expendable Mould]
    A --> C[Permanent Mould]
    B --> D[Sand Casting]
    B --> E[Shell Mould Casting]
    B --> F[Investment Lost Wax Casting]
    B --> G[Plaster Mould Casting]
    B --> H[Ceramic Mould Casting]
    B --> I[Vacuum Casting]
    C --> J[Die Casting]
    C --> K[Permanent Mould Gravity Casting]
    C --> L[Centrifugal Casting]
    C --> M[Continuous Casting]
    C --> N[Squeeze Casting]
    J --> J1[Hot Chamber Die Casting]
    J --> J2[Cold Chamber Die Casting]
    L --> L1[True Centrifugal Casting]
    L --> L2[Semi Centrifugal Casting]
    L --> L3[Centrifuge Casting]
    D --> D1[Green Sand Mould]
    D --> D2[Dry Sand Mould]
    D --> D3[CO2 Sand Mould]
    D --> D4[Chemical Bonded Sand Mould]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Process Selection Logic

The following block-level functional architecture maps the **decision flow** for selecting an appropriate casting process based on part requirements.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Part Design Specification] --> B[What is Production Volume]
    B -->|Low Under 1000 per year| C[Is Part Very Complex or Intricate]
    B -->|Medium 1000 to 10000| D[Is Surface Finish Critical]
    B -->|High Above 10000| E[Is Metal Non Ferrous Al Zn Mg]
    C -->|Yes| F[Investment Casting or Ceramic Mould]
    C -->|No| G[Sand Casting with Standard Pattern]
    D -->|Yes Yes Ra less than 3.2 micron| H[Shell Mould or Investment Casting]
    D -->|No No Ra above 6.3 micron| I[Sand Casting or Permanent Mould]
    E -->|Yes| J[Die Casting Hot or Cold Chamber]
    E -->|No| K[Permanent Mould or Squeeze Casting]
    F --> L[Final Process Selection]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.3 Mould Reusability Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Mould Type] --> B[Expendable Mould]
    A --> C[Permanent Mould]
    B --> B1[Material: Sand, Plaster, Ceramic, Wax]
    B --> B2[Reuse Count: 1 only]
    B --> B3[Cycle Time: Long]
    B --> B4[Cost per Part: High for small volumes]
    C --> C1[Material: Cast Iron, Steel, Tool Steel]
    C --> C2[Reuse Count: 10000 to 1000000]
    C --> C3[Cycle Time: Short]
    C --> C4[Cost per Part: Low for large volumes]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Key Processes (Text-Based Schematic)

For processes that are difficult to draw in Mermaid, the following **structured functional descriptions** are provided:

**Sand Casting Process — Sequential Steps:**
1. **Step 1:** Create a wooden or metal pattern (oversized by shrinkage allowance).
2. **Step 2:** Place the pattern in a flask (bottom half) and pack green sand around it.
3. **Step 3:** Invert the flask, place the cope (top half), and place the pattern halves together.
4. **Step 4:** Pack sand around the upper pattern half; create a pouring basin, sprue, runner, and gate.
5. **Step 5:** Remove the pattern carefully; reassemble the mould halves.
6. **Step 6:** Pour molten metal into the pouring basin; the metal flows through sprue → runner → gate → cavity.
7. **Step 7:** Allow solidification; cool; shake out the casting by breaking the sand mould.
8. **Step 8:** Cut off the sprue/runner/gate; finish the casting (grinding, shot blasting, machining).

**Die Casting Process — Sequential Steps:**
1. **Step 1:** Two steel die halves (cover die and ejector die) are clamped together under high tonnage.
2. **Step 2:** Molten metal is injected at high velocity (cold chamber) or sucked in (hot chamber) into the die cavity.
3. **Step 3:** Pressure (30–100 MPa for cold chamber; 7–35 MPa for hot chamber) is maintained during solidification.
4. **Step 4:** Dies open; ejector pins push the casting out.
5. **Step 5:** Dies are lubricated, closed, and the cycle repeats (typical cycle time: 5–60 seconds).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Exam Paper]

**Classify the various casting processes based on the type of mould used. Give at least two examples of each class.**

**Model Answer:**

Casting processes are classified into two broad categories based on mould type:

1. **Expendable (Non-permanent) Mould Casting:**
The mould is destroyed after each casting cycle. Examples:
- **Sand Casting** — most common, uses silica sand bonded with clay or chemical binders.
- **Investment Casting** — uses a ceramic mould built around a wax pattern.
- **Shell Mould Casting** — uses a thin shell of resin-bonded sand.

2. **Permanent (Non-expendable) Mould Casting:**
The mould is reused for many cycles. Examples:
- **Die Casting** — uses a hardened steel die under high pressure.
- **Permanent Mould (Gravity Die) Casting** — uses a metal mould with gravity pour.
- **Centrifugal Casting** — uses a rotating metal mould.

The choice depends on **production volume, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, complexity, and metal type**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Series II]

**Differentiate between die casting and sand casting in terms of mould material, accuracy, production rate, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Die Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Mould material | Silica sand + binder (expendable) | Hardened tool steel (permanent) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low to Medium (±0.8 to ±1.6 mm) | High (±0.05 to ±0.15 mm) |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 12.5 – 25 µm | 0.8 – 1.6 µm |
| Production rate | Low (1–10 castings/hour) | Very high (50–500 castings/hour) |
| Suitable metals | Ferrous and non-ferrous | Mostly non-ferrous (Zn, Al, Mg) |
| Typical applications | Engine blocks, machine beds, large castings | Carburettors, gear housings, small intricate parts |
| Initial tooling cost | Low | Very high |
| Mould life | 1 casting | 100,000 – 1,000,000 castings |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain in detail the **classification of casting processes** based on (i) mould material, (ii) production volume, and (iii) metallurgical compatibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Casting is one of the most versatile primary shaping processes. The classification helps in selecting the right process for a given engineering application.

**(i) Classification Based on Mould Material (3 marks):**

- **Expendable Mould Casting:** Mould destroyed after one use.
   - *Sand casting* (most common; green sand, dry sand, CO₂ sand, chemically bonded sand).
   - *Shell mould casting* (thermosetting resin-bonded sand).
   - *Investment casting* (ceramic mould over wax pattern).
   - *Plaster mould casting* (plaster of Paris).
   - *Ceramic mould casting* (refractory ceramic slurry).

- **Permanent Mould Casting:** Mould reusable.
   - *Die casting* (steel dies, high pressure; hot chamber / cold chamber).
   - *Permanent mould casting* (metal mould, gravity pour).
   - *Centrifugal casting* (rotating mould).
   - *Continuous casting* (water-cooled copper mould).
   - *Squeeze casting* (pressurized solidification).

**(ii) Classification Based on Production Volume (2 marks):**

- **Low Volume (≤ 500/year):** Sand casting, investment casting.
- **Medium Volume (500–10,000/year):** Shell mould, permanent mould, plaster mould.
- **High Volume (> 10,000/year):** Die casting, continuous casting, centrifugal casting.

**(iii) Classification Based on Metallurgical Compatibility (1 mark):**

- **Ferrous metals:** Sand, investment, centrifugal, continuous.
- **Non-ferrous metals (Al, Zn, Mg, Cu):** Die casting, permanent mould, plaster mould.
- **Reactive / high-temperature alloys (Ti, superalloys):** Investment casting, vacuum casting.

**Conclusion:** The choice of casting process depends on the interplay between production volume, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and metal type.

---

**Q.A. (b)** Describe the **investment casting process** with a neat sketch. List **four advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description (5 marks):**

1. **Pattern Making:** A wax pattern (often injection-moulded) is made to the exact size of the desired part. Sprues and gates are attached.
2. **Assembly:** Multiple wax patterns are mounted on a central wax sprue to form a "tree."
3. **Shell Building:** The tree is dipped repeatedly into a ceramic slurry, stuccoed with refractory grains, and dried. This is repeated 6–8 times to build a strong ceramic shell (~6–15 mm thick).
4. **Dewaxing:** The shell is placed in an autoclave (or flash fire oven) at ~150 °C; the wax melts out rapidly (the wax is recovered and recycled). This step is the origin of the name "lost-wax process."
