- **Chaplet material melting point** must be **≥ 50 °C above** the casting metal.

### Pillar 9 — **Mould Material Selection**
**For green sand moulds:**
- Silica sand: **85–95 %**
- Bentonite (clay binder): **4–10 %**
- Water: **2–6 %**
- Coal dust / seacoal: **2–5 %** (improves surface finish)

### Pillar 10 — **Shake-out & Collapsibility**
- Mould material must collapse readily after solidification to **release the casting** without cracking.
- AISI casting steel: requires more refractory moulds than grey cast iron.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Typical Value or Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shrinkage allowance (linear) | $L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \cdot (1 + S)$ | $S = 0.01$ (CI), $0.02$ (Steel) |
| 2 | Volumetric shrinkage | $V_{pattern} = V_{casting} \cdot (1 + 3S)$ | For small $S$ |
| 3 | Draft angle → taper per side | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{D}{H}$ | $\theta = 1^{\circ}\text{–}3^{\circ}$ |
| 4 | Draft extra per side | $\Delta = H \cdot \tan\theta$ | mm |
| 5 | Chvorinov's rule | $t_{s} = B \cdot (V/A)^{2}$ | $t$ in seconds |
| 6 | Modulus of casting | $M_{c} = V_{c} / A_{c}$ | cm |
| 7 | Modulus of riser | $M_{r} = V_{r} / A_{r}$ | cm |
| 8 | Riser modulus criterion | $M_{r} \geq 1.2 \, M_{c}$ | — |
| 9 | Caine's criterion (side riser) | $M_{r} \geq 1.2 \, M_{c}$ (general), $\geq 1.5$ for steel | — |
| 10 | Pouring time (graphite) | $t_{p} = K \sqrt{W}$ | $K$ depends on casting temp |
| 11 | Sprue velocity (Bernoulli) | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s |
| 12 | AFS GFN | $\text{GFN} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot \bar{d}_i)}{\sum m_i}$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Permeability number | $P = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot p \cdot t}$ | 80–120 typical |
| 14 | Core print length | $L_{cp} \geq 1.5 \cdot d_{core}$ | mm |
| 15 | Chaplet melting rule | $T_{m,chaplet} \geq T_{m,metal} + 50^{\circ}C$ | °C |
| 16 | Chaplet area (Wlodawer) | $A_{ch} = \dfrac{W_{core}}{n \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, the absolute-value / modulus expression $M = V/A$ has been written as $\dfrac{V}{A}$ (the modulus) to avoid table-formatting conflicts with the pipe symbol.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine blocks:** Moulds contain complex multi-core assemblies — each cylinder bore, water jacket, and oil gallery is a separate core.
- **Turbine casings (aero-engine):** Use ceramic cores for internal cooling channels; design tolerances ±0.05 mm.
- **Pump housings & valve bodies:** Heavy use of dry sand cores bonded with phenolic resin; cores must withstand high ferro-static pressure.
- **Pipe fittings:** Use horizontally-parted green sand moulds with simple cylindrical cores.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Shrinkage Allowance on Pattern Dimensions

**Problem Context:** A cast iron pattern must produce a casting of nominal length $L = 500$ mm. Grey cast iron has a linear shrinkage rate $S = 1.0\% = 0.01$. What should be the pattern length?

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & L_{casting} = 500 \text{ mm}, \quad S = 0.01 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1: Define shrinkage mathematically.} \quad & S = \dfrac{L_{pattern} - L_{casting}}{L_{casting}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Rearrange for } L_{pattern}. \quad & L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \cdot (1 + S) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substitute values.} \quad & L_{pattern} = 500 \times (1 + 0.01) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute.} \quad & L_{pattern} = 500 \times 1.01 = 505 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \boxed{L_{pattern} = 505 \text{ mm}} \quad \text{(the pattern is 5 mm larger)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** If the pattern were made exactly 500 mm, the casting after cooling would be only **495 mm** (because cast iron shrinks 1%). The pattern is therefore oversize by **5 mm** to compensate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Shrinkage Allowance

**Problem Context:** Same casting, find the volumetric shrinkage $\Delta V$ for a cube of side 100 mm in cast iron.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Initial volume.} \quad & V_{casting} = L^{3} = 100^{3} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3} = 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: For small S, volumetric shrinkage is 3S.} \quad & S_{V} = 3S = 3 \times 0.01 = 0.03 \; (3\%) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Pattern volume required.} \quad & V_{pattern} = V_{casting} \cdot (1 + 3S) = 10^{6} \times 1.03 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute.} \quad & V_{pattern} = 1{,}030{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Extra material needed.} \quad & \Delta V = V_{pattern} - V_{casting} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3} = 30 \text{ cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Draft Angle (Taper) Required for Pattern Withdrawal

**Problem Context:** A pattern has a vertical wall of height $H = 200$ mm. The required draft angle is $\theta = 1.5^{\circ}$. Calculate the extra width added per side.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Convert angle to tangent.} \quad & \tan(1.5^{\circ}) = 0.02619 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Use the geometric relation.} \quad & \Delta = H \cdot \tan\theta \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substitute.} \quad & \Delta = 200 \times 0.02619 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute.} \quad & \Delta = 5.238 \text{ mm per side} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Total increase on width.} \quad & 2\Delta = 10.476 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(on both sides of vertical wall)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Riser Sizing by Chvorinov's Rule (Modulus Method)

**Problem Context:** A steel casting of volume $V_{c} = 8000 \text{ cm}^{3}$ and surface area $A_{c} = 2000 \text{ cm}^{2}$ requires a top riser. Using the modulus criterion $M_{r} \geq 1.2 \, M_{c}$, design a **cylindrical riser** with height-to-diameter ratio $H_{r} = 1.5 \cdot D_{r}$.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Modulus of casting.} \quad & M_{c} = \dfrac{V_{c}}{A_{c}} = \dfrac{8000}{2000} = 4.0 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Required riser modulus.} \quad & M_{r} = 1.2 \times M_{c} = 1.2 \times 4.0 = 4.8 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Cylinder volume and area formulas.} \quad & V_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{r}^{2} H_{r}, \quad A_{r} = \pi D_{r} H_{r} + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{r}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Substitute H = 1.5 D.} \quad & V_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{r}^{2} (1.5 D_{r}) = 0.375 \pi D_{r}^{3} \\[4pt]
& A_{r} = \pi D_{r} (1.5 D_{r}) + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{r}^{2} = 1.5 \pi D_{r}^{2} + 0.25 \pi D_{r}^{2} = 1.75 \pi D_{r}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Modulus expression.} \quad & M_{r} = \dfrac{V_{r}}{A_{r}} = \dfrac{0.375 \pi D_{r}^{3}}{1.75 \pi D_{r}^{2}} = \dfrac{0.375 D_{r}}{1.75} = 0.2143 \, D_{r} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Solve for D.} \quad & 4.8 = 0.2143 \, D_{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{r} = \dfrac{4.8}{0.2143} = 22.4 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: Height.} \quad & H_{r} = 1.5 \times 22.4 = 33.6 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 8: Riser volume.} \quad & V_{r} = 0.375 \pi (22.4)^{3} = 0.375 \pi \times 11239 \approx 13{,}237 \text{ cm}^{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 9: Verify modulus.} \quad & M_{r} = 13237 / (1.75 \pi (22.4)^{2}) = 13237 / 2758 \approx 4.80 \text{ cm} \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Chaplet Sizing (Wlodawer's Equation)

**Problem Context:** A cylindrical core of diameter $d = 150$ mm, length $L = 400$ mm, made of baked oil-sand core with density $\rho_{core} = 1.6 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$, is suspended horizontally in molten cast iron ($\rho_{Fe} = 7.0 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$). Use 4 chaplets, allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 40$ MPa. Find chaplet diameter.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Net buoyant force on core.} \quad & W_{net} = (\rho_{Fe} - \rho_{core}) \cdot V_{core} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Volume of core.} \quad & V_{core} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (15)^{2}(40) = 7069 \text{ cm}^{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Net weight.} \quad & W_{net} = (7.0 - 1.6) \times 7069 = 5.4 \times 7069 = 38{,}173 \text{ g} = 38.17 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Force per chaplet (n = 4).} \quad & F_{ch} = \dfrac{38.17 \times 9.81}{4} = 93.6 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Cross-section of each chaplet.} \quad & A_{ch} = \dfrac{F_{ch}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{93.6}{40 \times 10^{6}} = 2.34 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} = 2.34 \text{ mm}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Diameter.} \quad & d_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{ch}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 2.34}{\pi}} = 1.73 \text{ mm} \approx 2 \text{ mm (standard size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Core Print Length for Stability

**Problem Context:** A horizontal cylindrical core of diameter $d = 100$ mm is to be supported by two core prints. Calculate minimum print length.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Apply the KTU rule.} \quad & L_{cp} \geq 1.5 \times d_{core} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Substitute.} \quad & L_{cp} = 1.5 \times 100 = 150 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Each of the two prints.} \quad & L_{cp,\,each} = 150 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Add 1 mm clearance per side for venting.} \quad & L_{cp,\,final} = 150 + 2 = 152 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.7 Summary — Analytical Decision Matrix

| Design Question | Governing Equation | Typical Boundary Condition | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| How big must the pattern be? | $L_p = L_c (1+S)$ | $S$ from metal table | 2–3 marks |
| How much taper on the pattern? | $\Delta = H \tan\theta$ | $\theta = 1°\text{–}3°$ | 2 marks |
| How big a riser? | $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$ | $H/D = 1.0\text{–}2.0$ | 5–7 marks |
| How many chaplets? | $A_{ch} = W_{net}/(n\sigma_{allow})$ | $n \geq 3$ for stability | 4–5 marks |
| How long a core print? | $L_{cp} \geq 1.5 d$ | Plus 1 mm vent gap | 2 marks |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Mould Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pattern] --> B[Copemould]
    A --> C[Dragmould]
    B --> D[Mould Cavity]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Core Assembly]
    E --> E1[Core Body]
    E --> E2[Core Prints]
    E --> E3[Core Vents]
    E --> E4[Chaplets]
    D --> F[Gating System]
    F --> F1[Pouring Basin]
    F --> F2[Sprue]
    F --> F3[Runner]
    F --> F4[Gate]
    D --> G[Riser System]
    G --> G1[Top Riser]
    G --> G2[Side Riser]
    D --> H[Vent System]
    D --> I[Casting Output]
```

## 4.2 Mould Design Decision Tree — Core vs No-Core

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[New Casting Design] --> K{Internal cavity required?}
    K -- No --> L[Use only Mould Cavity]
    K -- Yes --> M{Cavity shape complex?}
    M -- Simple hole --> N[Use Dry Sand Core]
    M -- Branching / undercuts --> O[Use Green Sand Core with Chaplets]
    M -- Thin walls / high accuracy --> P[Use Shell Core or Resin-Bonded Core]
    N --> Q[Mount on Core Prints]
    O --> R{Weight of core high?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Add Chaplets 3 or more]
    R -- No --> T[Self-supporting print only]
    P --> U[Check collapsibility vs strength]
```

## 4.3 Riser Sizing Logic — Modulus Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Compute Casting Volume Vc] --> W[Compute Casting Surface Area Ac]
    W --> X[Compute Modulus Mc = Vc / Ac]
    X --> Y[Set Mr greater or equal 1.2 Mc]
    Y --> Z[Choose riser geometry H by D ratio]
    Z --> AA[Solve for D from Mr equation]
    AA --> AB[Verify Mr greater than Mc]
    AB -- Pass --> AC[Finalise riser]
    AB -- Fail --> AD[Increase D and recompute]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Sand Mould Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Sand Preparation] --> P2[Mulling with Bentonite and Water]
    P2 --> P3[Pattern Placement on Moulding Box]
    P3 --> P4[Ramming of Drag Half]
    P4 --> P5[Rollover and Ram Cope Half]
    P5 --> P6[Insert Core in Core Prints]
    P6 --> P7[Close Mould and Clamp]
    P7 --> P8[Pour Molten Metal]
    P8 --> P9[Solidification and Cooling]
    P9 --> P10[Shakeout and Cleaning]
    P10 --> P11[Final Casting]
```

## 4.5 Core Design Constraints — Mind-Map Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CORE[Core Design] --> STR[Strength]
    CORE --> PERM[Permeability]
    CORE --> COLL[Collapsibility]
    CORE --> SUPP[Support]
    CORE --> VENT[Venting]
    STR --> STR1[Hot strength greater than metal pressure]
    STR --> STR2[Use resin binder for high strength]
    PERM --> PERM1[Permeability number 80 to 120]
    PERM --> PERM2[Avoid fine grains in core]
    COLL --> COLL1[Use cereal binder for collapse]
    COLL --> COLL2[Prevent hot tearing in casting]
    SUPP --> SUPP1[Core print length greater than 1.5 D]
    SUPP --> SUPP2[Chaplets when weight is high]
    VENT --> VENT1[Vents thinner than 3 mm]
    VENT --> VENT2[Vent at highest gas point]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define mould and core. List any four differences between them.**

**Model Answer (Concise & Exam-Ready):**

| Aspect | Mould | Core |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Function | Forms the **external** surface of the casting | Forms the **internal** surface / cavity |
| 2. Position | Surrounds the casting completely | Placed **inside** the mould cavity |
| 3. Bonding | Green sand (clay + water) typical | Oil-sand or resin-bonded sand typical |
| 4. Strength requirement | Lower (just supports sand mass) | Higher (must resist ferro-static pressure) |
| 5. Collapsibility | Should be moderate | Should collapse readily after solidification |
| 6. Permeability | Standard | Higher, to vent gases through core |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's expected keywords (must appear in answer):** External surface, Internal cavity, Ferro-static pressure, Collapsibility, Permeability.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**What is a draft allowance? Why is a larger draft provided on internal surfaces compared to external surfaces?**

**Model Answer:**

A **draft allowance** is the slight taper provided on the vertical (or near-vertical) faces of a pattern to facilitate its clean withdrawal from the mould without damaging the sand walls.

**Typical values:**
- External surfaces: 1° – 3° (hand moulding), 0.5° – 1° (machine moulding)
- Internal surfaces: 2° – 3° (always greater)

**Reason for larger internal draft:**
Internal sand walls (e.g., around a core print or in a hollow pocket) tend to **sag or collapse inward** under their own weight once the pattern is withdrawn, because they are unsupported from the back. To prevent this sagging and to ensure the mould cavity remains intact, a larger taper (2° – 3°) is provided on internal faces.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **pattern allowances** used in foundry practice. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **cylindrical riser** is to be designed for a steel casting of volume 6000 cm³ and surface area 1800 cm². Using the modulus criterion $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$ and riser geometry $H_r = 1.5 D_r$, determine the **diameter and height** of the riser. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Pattern Allowances (7 Marks):**

A pattern is the replica of the casting used to prepare the mould. It is **not** made to the exact size of the final casting; instead, several **allowances** are built into it:

1. **Shrinkage Allowance (2 marks)**
   - Compensates for contraction of metal during solidification and cooling to room temperature.
   - Pattern is oversized.
   - Typical: Cast Iron = 1.0 %, Steel = 2.0 %, Aluminium = 1.5 %, Brass = 1.5 %.

2. **Draft / Taper Allowance (2 marks)**
   - Slight taper (1° – 3°) on vertical faces for easy pattern withdrawal.
   - External = 1° – 3°, Internal = 2° – 3°.

3. **Machining / Finish Allowance (1 mark)**
   - Extra material (1.5 – 6 mm) on surfaces that will be later machined.

4. **Distortion Allowance (1 mark)**
   - For U-shaped, flat, or slender castings that warp on cooling, the pattern is pre-distorted in the opposite direction.

5. **Shake / Rapping Allowance (1 mark)**
   - Compensates for the small cavity enlargement caused by rapping the pattern during withdrawal. Usually subsumed into other allowances.

### Model Solution — Part A(b) — Riser Sizing (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$V_c = 6000$ cm³, $A_c = 1800$ cm², $H_r = 1.5 D_r$, $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Modulus of casting.} \quad & M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{6000}{1800} = 3.333 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Required riser modulus.} \quad & M_r = 1.2 \times 3.333 = 4.0 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Cylinder geometry.} \quad & V_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_r^{2} \cdot 1.5 D_r = 0.375 \pi D_r^{3} \\[4pt]
& A_r = \pi D_r H_r + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_r^{2} = 1.5 \pi D_r^{2} + 0.25 \pi D_r^{2} = 1.75 \pi D_r^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Modulus expression.} \quad & M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r} = \dfrac{0.375 D_r}{1.75} = 0.2143 D_r \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Solve for } D_r. \quad & 4.0 = 0.2143 D_r \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_r = \dfrac{4.0}{0.2143} = 18.67 \text{ cm} \approx 19 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Height.} \quad & H_r = 1.5 \times 19 = 28.5 \text{ cm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: Verification.} \quad & V_r = 0.375 \pi (19)^{3} = 0.375 \pi \times 6859 = 8078 \text{ cm}^{3} \\[4pt]
& A_r = 1.75 \pi (19)^{2} = 1985 \text{ cm}^{2} \\[4pt]
& M_r = 8078 / 1985 = 4.07 \text{ cm} \geq 4.0 \text{ cm} \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $D_r \approx 19$ cm, $H_r \approx 28.5$ cm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **principles of core design** in detail, highlighting the cardinal rules that govern core strength, permeability, and collapsibility. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical core of **diameter 120 mm and length 350 mm** is suspended in molten cast iron. The core density is 1.6 g/cm³ and molten iron density is 7.0 g/cm³. If **3 chaplets** are used, each with allowable stress 35 MPa, calculate the required chaplet diameter. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution — Part B(a) — Principles of Core Design (7 Marks)

A **core** is a pre-formed sand aggregate placed inside the mould to produce internal cavities. Its design is governed by **seven cardinal rules** (1 mark each for any 5-6):

1. **Strength** — Core must possess adequate **dry strength** to withstand the **ferro-static pressure** of molten metal without deformation or breakage. Resin-bonded or oil-bonded cores are preferred for high-strength applications.

2. **Permeability** — Core must allow **gases** generated during pouring to escape freely, preventing blowholes. Permeability number should be **80–120**. Use coarser sand grains and avoid excessive fines.

3. **Collapsibility** — After solidification, the core must collapse readily to **accommodate the shrinkage** of the casting and prevent **hot tearing**. Use **cereal binders** (e.g., dextrin, starch) or add collapsibility agents.

4. **Core Print Adequacy** — The **core print** must be of sufficient length (≥ 1.5 × core diameter) and proper clearance (1–1.5 mm) to **vent gases** and locate the core precisely.

5. **Venting** — Cores must have **vent holes or wax wire** to provide an escape path for gases. Vents should be small (0.5–3 mm) to prevent metal penetration.

6. **Chaplets / Supports** — Heavy cores must be **supported by chaplets** whose melting point is at least 50 °C above that of the casting metal.

7. **Surface Finish** — The core surface must be **smooth** (where casting surface finish is critical) and properly **coated with a refractory wash** (e.g., zircon or chromite wash) to prevent burn-on.

### Model Solution — Part B(b) — Chaplet Sizing (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Core diameter $d = 120$ mm = 12 cm
- Core length $L = 350$ mm = 35 cm
- $\rho_{core} = 1.6$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.0$ g/cm³
- Number of chaplets $n = 3$
- $\sigma_{allow} = 35$ MPa = 35 N/mm²

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Core volume.} \quad & V_{core} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (12)^{2} (35) = 3958 \text{ cm}^{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Net buoyant weight.} \quad & W_{net} = (\rho_{Fe} - \rho_{core}) \cdot V_{core} \\[4pt]
& W_{net} = (7.0 - 1.6) \times 3958 = 5.4 \times 3958 = 21{,}373 \text{ g} \approx 21.37 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Force per chaplet.} \quad & F_{ch} = \dfrac{W_{net} \cdot g}{n} = \dfrac{21.37 \times 9.81}{3} = 69.88 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Required cross-section area.} \quad & A_{ch} = \dfrac{F_{ch}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{69.88}{35} = 1.997 \text{ mm}^{2} \approx 2.0 \text{ mm}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Chaplet diameter.} \quad & d_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{ch}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 2.0}{\pi}} = \sqrt{2.546} = 1.595 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Round up to standard size.} \quad & d_{ch} \approx 2 \text{ mm (standard)} \\[4pt]
\text{Verification:} \quad & \text{Total chaplet area} = 3 \times 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ mm}^{2} \\[4pt]
& \text{Allowable load} = 6.0 \times 35 = 210 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
& W_{net} \text{ in N} = 21.37 \times 9.81 = 209.6 \text{ N} \approx 210 \text{ N} \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Chaplet diameter ≈ **2 mm**, with 3 chaplets of standard size.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — writing cm and mm interchangeably in the same derivation. Always carry units through and box the final answer with units.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 3** when converting linear shrinkage to volumetric shrinkage.
> 3. **Confusing modulus of casting vs modulus of riser** — a 2-mark penalty is common.
> 4. **Not verifying the riser criterion** at the end of the problem — examiner expects a check: $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$ must be shown.
> 5. **Forgetting to include clearance** in core print length.
> 6. **In Part (a) long-answer questions**, students often miss the "**why**" — for example, why is a larger draft on internal surfaces? Examiners look for the physical reason (sand sagging), not just the numerical value.
> 7. **For Chaplet problems**, the most common error is using the **total core weight** instead of the **net buoyant weight** (molten metal density minus core density). This leads to a 2–3 mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Mould** forms the **external shape**; **Core** forms the **internal cavity**.
- [ ] A pattern is **oversized** to compensate for **shrinkage** (CI = 1 %, Steel = 2 %).
- [ ] **Draft angle** = 1° – 3° (external), 2° – 3° (internal, because sand sags).
- [ ] **Five pattern allowances:** Shrinkage, Draft, Machining, Distortion, Shake.
- [ ] **Parting line** = the boundary between cope and drag; choose the flattest, most horizontal face.
- [ ] **Gating system** = Pouring basin → Sprue → Runner → Gate; unpressurised ratio is **1 : 4 (sprue bottom : gate)**.
- [ ] **Riser design** is based on the **modulus (M = V/A)** criterion: $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c$.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ — riser solidifies last because it has the highest V/A ratio.
- [ ] **Core design** obeys 7 cardinal rules: Strength, Permeability, Collapsibility, Support, Venting, Surface finish, Coating.
- [ ] **Core print length** $\geq 1.5 \times$ core diameter, plus 1 mm vent clearance per side.
- [ ] **Chaplet melting point** must be $\geq T_{m,\,metal} + 50°$C.
- [ ] **Chaplet cross-section:** $A_{ch} = W_{net} / (n \cdot \sigma_{allow})$, with $W_{net} = (\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core}) \cdot V_{core}$.
- [ ] **AFS GFN** is the standard grain-size measure for moulding sand; permeability number 80–120.
- [ ] **Mould hardness** (Brinell) target: 75–90 for green sand.
- [ ] Always **draw labelled diagrams** of moulds in 14-mark answers — they carry 2–3 marks independently.
- [ ] In numerical problems, **show units, show Chvorinov's / modulus equation, verify the criterion at the end.**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "MCP-SPRAD":**
> **M**ould, **C**ore, **P**arting line, **S**hrinkage, **P**ermeability, **R**iser, **A**llowance, **D**raft — the eight golden letters of this KTU Module-1 topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gating Systems: Types and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gating Systems: Types and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gating System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *gating system* is the engineered network of refractory channels (passages) that channels molten metal from the *pouring basin* through the *sprue, sprue well, runner,* and *gate* into the mould cavity of a sand or permanent mould. It controls the **flow rate, fill time, fill pattern, thermal gradient, and pressure regime** of liquid metal during casting.

The term "gating" originates from foundry practice where the *gate* is the final entry point of molten metal into the casting cavity. In the KTU 2024 *Manufacturing Processes* (PCPET402) syllabus, gating systems form a sub-topic under the **Casting** module, particularly within sand casting and gravity die casting.

### 1.1.1 Primary Functions of a Gating System

A properly designed gating system must satisfy **six engineering functions** simultaneously:

1. **Conveyance** — Deliver molten metal from the ladle/pouring basin to the mould cavity with minimum heat loss.
2. **Flow Control** — Maintain the correct *pouring time* to avoid premature solidification.
3. **Velocity Regulation** — Prevent *jetting* and *turbulence* (which cause gas entrapment and oxide inclusions).
4. **Dross/Slag Retention** — Trap oxides, sand particles, and slag in the runner system via *skimmers* and *strainer cores*.
5. **Thermal Gradient Management** — Ensure directional solidification from the casting towards the *riser* (which acts as the feed metal reservoir).
6. **Pressure Stabilization** — Either maximize the back pressure (pressurized system) or maintain gravity-driven flow (unpressurized system).

### 1.1.2 Constituent Elements of a Gating System (in flow order)

The standard sequence of components is:

> **Pouring Cup → Pouring Basin → Sprue (Down-Sprue) → Sprue Well (Splash) → Runner → Gate (Ingate) → Mould Cavity**

Each element has a precise geometric and hydrodynamic purpose, which will be detailed in Section 2.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: "The municipal water-supply network of a city."**

Imagine you are designing the water-pipe network that delivers water from an overhead water tank to 50 houses in a city:

| Casting Element | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Pouring Basin** | The overhead tank where water enters the system | Smooth entry, swirl control, initial reservoir |
| **Sprue (Down-Sprue)** | The vertical pipe falling from the tank to ground level | Converts potential energy to kinetic energy |
| **Sprue Well** | A small impact-absorbing pool at the foot of the pipe | Prevents erosion of pipe base, dissipates energy |
| **Runner** | The horizontal main pipeline at ground level | Distributes water to different branches |
| **Gate (Ingate)** | The individual house connection (small valve) | Final controlled entry into the consumer |
| **Riser** | The auxiliary tank that "tops up" the houses as they drain | Feeds shrinkage during solidification |

Just as a civil engineer calculates the **pipe diameter** to maintain pressure and avoid leaks, a foundry engineer calculates the **choke area** (smallest cross-section in the system) to control metal velocity and avoid mould erosion.

> [!TIP]
> **Mental Hook:** *"If the sprue is the throat, the gate is the mouth — and the runner is the trachea that connects them."* The **choke** is whichever section is narrowest, and it dictates the entire flow rate.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

For all KTU 2024 numerical problems, the following constants are **mandatory** to memorize:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Density of cast iron | $\rho_{CI}$ | **7,100** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of steel | $\rho_{St}$ | **7,850** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of aluminium | $\rho_{Al}$ | **2,700** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of copper | $\rho_{Cu}$ | **8,960** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of bronze | $\rho_{Br}$ | **8,800** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Pouring temperature (CI) | $T_p$ | **1,300 – 1,400** | °C |
| Pouring temperature (steel) | $T_p$ | **1,550 – 1,650** | °C |
| Pouring temperature (Al) | $T_p$ | **680 – 750** | °C |
| Kinematic viscosity (molten Al) | $\nu$ | $\approx 5 \times 10^{-7}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In numerical problems, students often forget that the **choke area** controls the entire flow — not the sprue area or gate area in isolation. Always identify the choke first.

---

## 1.4 Why Gating System Design Matters in KTU Examinations

A 2024 KTU Board examiner typically allocates 1 to 2 questions per semester on this topic because it tests:

- **CO1 / CO2** of Manufacturing Processes: *Understanding casting process parameters.*
- **Bloom Level L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze):** Computation of pouring time, sprue area, and Reynolds number.
- **Bloom Level L5 (Evaluate):** Comparing pressurized vs unpressurized systems for a given casting.

> **Key Takeaway from Section 1:** A gating system is a **flow-control device**, not merely a "passage for metal." Its design dictates casting soundness, surface finish, and mechanical integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Components of a Gating System

### 2.1.1 Pouring Basin (Pouring Cup)
The receiving cavity into which the ladle pours metal. Its **primary functions** are:
- Decelerate the incoming metal stream
- Trap the first slag/dross via a **strainer core** (perforated ceramic disc)
- Provide a steady head of metal to the sprue
- Designed as a **hemispherical** or **conical** shape with a **bottom-pour orifice** that aligns with the sprue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** The pouring basin depth should be **at least 2× the sprue diameter** to ensure the basin remains full throughout pouring.

---

### 2.1.2 Sprue (Down-Sprue)
The **vertical channel** that conducts metal from the pouring basin down into the parting plane of the mould. There are two principal geometries:

| Type | Geometry | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel (Straight) Sprue** | Constant cross-section | Small castings, low-pressure flow |
| **Tapered Sprue** | Top area > Bottom area (1.5 : 1 ratio) | Standard practice — compensates for friction loss and prevents aspiration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aspirator Effect (Back-Suction):** If a *parallel* sprue is used, the falling metal column creates a low-pressure zone at the top, drawing gases INTO the sprue from the mould cavity. This causes **blowholes and misruns**. A *tapered sprue* eliminates this by maintaining continuous full flow.

The taper ratio recommended by the *American Foundrymen's Society (AFS)* is:
$$
A_{top} : A_{bottom} = 1.5 : 1 \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0 : 1
$$

---

### 2.1.3 Sprue Well (Splash Core)
A cylindrical well at the base of the sprue. It serves to:
- Absorb the impact energy of the falling metal
- Reduce metal velocity before it enters the runner
- Trap large oxide inclusions
- It is typically a **core** of the same height as the runner depth.

---

### 2.1.4 Runner
The **horizontal channel** that distributes metal from the sprue to the gates. Two important geometric rules:

- The **runner cross-section** is always rectangular (depth : width = 1 : 2) to maintain high hydraulic diameter and reduce friction.
- The runner is always placed on the **drag (lower half)** of the mould to keep it full.
- For multi-gate systems, the runner may be **tapered** (decreasing area in the direction of flow) to maintain equal velocity to each gate.

---

### 2.1.5 Gate (Ingate)
The **final entry point** of metal into the mould cavity. Three types:

| Gate Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Parting Line Gate** | At the mould parting plane | Most common, easiest to make |
| **Top Gate (Direct Pour)** | Metal falls directly from sprue into cavity | Heavy castings, simple shapes |
| **Bottom Gate** | Metal enters from below | Tall castings, ensures bottom-up filling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Velocity Limit:** Ingate velocity should not exceed **0.5 m/s for cast iron** and **1.5 m/s for aluminium** to avoid mould erosion.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Gating Systems (Two Schemes)

### Scheme A: Based on **Pressure Regime**

#### 1. **Unpressurized (Gravity / Open) Gating System**
- The **sprue base is the choke** (smallest cross-section).
- Atmospheric pressure pushes metal from the pouring basin.
- Velocity at the gate is **maximum** (no back pressure).
- **Risk:** Aspiration, mould erosion, slag ingress.
- **Use:** Ferrous castings where dross is not a major concern.
- **Area Ratio:** $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$

#### 2. **Pressurized Gating System**
- The **gate is the choke** (smallest cross-section).
- The runner, sprue, and pouring basin are kept full of metal.
- The system is **completely filled** with metal under static head.
- Low velocity at the gate — **gentle, non-turbulent flow**.
- **Use:** Non-ferrous castings (aluminium, bronze) that oxidize easily.
- **Area Ratio:** $A_s : A_r : A_g = 4 : 2 : 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Unpressurized has Up-going area (1→2→4); Pressurized has Down-going area (4→2→1)."*

---

### Scheme B: Based on **Gate Position**

| Gating Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top Gating** | Metal enters from top of cavity | Simple, hot metal stays at top, low head | Splash, mould erosion, dross enters cavity |
| **Bottom Gating** | Metal enters from bottom | Bottom-up fill, less oxidation, dross rises to riser | Metal cools during rise, may solidify prematurely |
| **Parting Line Gating** | Metal enters at mould parting | Versatile, easy to machine, balanced flow | Requires mould parting at critical level |
| **Step Gating** | Multiple gates at different heights | Controls fill sequence | Complex mould machining |
| **Y-Gate / Finger Gate** | Multiple fingers from runner | Reduced velocity, multiple fill points | Asymmetric fill possible |

---

## 2.3 Choke Area — The Heart of Gating Design

The **choke area** $A_c$ is the smallest cross-section in the entire gating system. It dictates the metal flow rate (via the continuity equation) and is calculated using:

$$
A_c = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot v \cdot t}
$$

Where:
- $W$ = total mass of metal poured (kg), including casting + risers + gating
- $\rho$ = density of molten metal ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient at the choke ($\approx 0.6$ for sprue, $0.7$ for ingate)
- $v$ = velocity of metal at the choke ($\text{m/s}$)
- $t$ = pouring time (s)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** If the problem specifies a *pressurized* system, the **gate** is the choke. If *unpressurized*, the **sprue base** is the choke. Always state this clearly in the first line of your answer.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU Board Exam problems on gating design use a combination of the formulas below. Memorize the **variable definitions** along with the formula.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = K \cdot \sqrt{W}$ | Empirical pouring time (Chvorinov's rule variant) | $t$ = pour time, $W$ = casting mass, $K$ = constant | s, kg, s/kg$^{0.5}$ |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Velocity at sprue base (Bernoulli, frictionless) | $g = 9.81$, $H$ = sprue height | m/s, m |
| 3 | $v = C_d \sqrt{2gH}$ | Velocity with discharge coefficient | $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.9$ | m/s |
| 4 | $A_s = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot v \cdot t}$ | Required cross-section at choke | All as defined | m² |
| 5 | $Q = A \cdot v = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot t}$ | Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ = volume rate | m³/s |
| 6 | $Re = \frac{v \cdot D_h}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number check (for turbulence) | $D_h$ = hydraulic dia, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_a = \rho g H$ | Static head pressure (Pa) | $H$ = effective head | Pa |
| 8 | $N_{Re} < 20,000$ | Turbulent threshold (avoid) | — | — |
| 9 | $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1:2:4$ | Unpressurized area ratio | — | — |
| 10 | $A_s : A_r : A_g = 4:2:1$ | Pressurized area ratio | — | — |
| 11 | $d_1 = d_2 \cdot \left(\frac{H_1}{H_2}\right)^{1/4}$ | Sprue taper relation | $d_1$, $d_2$ = top, bottom dia | m |
| 12 | $H_{eff} = H - \frac{P^2}{2 \cdot C}$ | Effective head with friction | $P$ = perimeter, $C$ = cross-sectional area | m |
| 13 | $K = \left(\frac{\text{For thin section}}{t \le 6 \text{mm}}\right) 1.0$ | Empirical $K$ value | See KTU table | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall on Tables:** Never write absolute-value bars $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the rendering. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or simply $\lvert x \rvert$ in display mode outside tables.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gating system design is **not merely a textbook problem** — it is the foundation of:

1. **Automotive foundries** (engine blocks, brake drums) — pressurized gating for aluminium alloys.
2. **Pipe and valve castings** — vertical, bottom-gated to avoid cold shuts.
3. **Aerospace titanium castings** — vacuum-assisted pressurized gating.
4. **Sanitary ware (ceramic-replaced by cast iron)** — top-gated with very large pouring basins.
5. **Investment casting** of jewellery — pressurized with fine filters.

> **Key Takeaway from Section 2:** Master the *area ratios* (1:2:4 vs 4:2:1) and the *choke-area formula* — these two alone solve ~70% of KTU Board questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section presents **exhaustive derivations** with no skipped steps. Every algebraic transition is fully expanded, and every substitution is explained.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sprue Exit Velocity from Bernoulli's Equation

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem
For a sprue of height $H$ with metal entering at negligible velocity from the pouring basin, derive the exit velocity at the sprue base.

### 3.1.2 Setup
Consider a sprue filled with molten metal of density $\rho$. Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between Point 1 (top of sprue, free surface) and Point 2 (sprue base, exit):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

### 3.1.3 Assumptions
- $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (both points exposed to atmosphere)
- $v_1 \approx 0$ (large basin, negligible surface velocity)
- $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (height of sprue)
- $h_f = 0$ for the moment (frictionless — will be added later)

### 3.1.4 Simplification
Substituting assumptions into Bernoulli's equation:

$$
\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + H = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + 0 + 0
$$

The atmospheric terms cancel:

$$
H = \frac{v_2^2}{2g}
$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for Velocity
Multiply both sides by $2g$:

$$
2gH = v_2^2
$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{2gH}
$$

### 3.1.6 Including the Discharge Coefficient
In real foundry conditions, viscous and turbulent losses reduce the velocity. We introduce $C_d$:

$$
v_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text row explanation:** $C_d \approx 0.6$ for a sharp-edged sprue entry, $0.85$–$0.95$ for a well-rounded entry. KTU problems often use $C_d = 0.6$ for the sprue and $C_d = 0.7$ for the gate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Sprue Choke Area for a Given Pouring Time

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A casting of mass $W$ (kg) must be filled in time $t$ (s). The metal density is $\rho$ ($\text{kg/m}^3$) and the discharge coefficient at the choke is $C_d$. Derive the required cross-sectional area of the sprue base (choke).

### 3.2.2 Continuity Equation (Volume Conservation)
The mass of metal passing through the choke in time $t$ must equal the mass of the casting plus risers plus the gating system itself. In the simplest form:

$$
W = \rho \cdot Q \cdot t
$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$).

### 3.2.3 Velocity at the Choke
From Section 3.1, the velocity of metal at the choke is:

$$
v = C_d \sqrt{2gH}
$$

### 3.2.4 Flow Rate Expression
Volumetric flow rate = Area $\times$ Velocity:

$$
Q = A_c \cdot v = A_c \cdot C_d \sqrt{2gH}
$$

### 3.2.5 Mass Flow Rate
Multiplying by density:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_c \cdot C_d \sqrt{2gH}
$$

### 3.2.6 Total Mass Delivered in Time $t$
$$
W = \dot{m} \cdot t = \rho \cdot A_c \cdot C_d \sqrt{2gH} \cdot t
$$

### 3.2.7 Solving for Choke Area $A_c$
Rearranging:

$$
A_c = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text row explanation:** This is the *fundamental KTU formula* for sprue design. The denominator represents the mass flow rate per unit area; the numerator is the total mass. Always use SI units: $W$ in kg, $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $t$ in s, $H$ in m, $g$ in $\text{m/s}^2$.

### 3.2.8 Numerical Example

> **Given:** $W = 50$ kg, $t = 30$ s, $H = 0.25$ m, $C_d = 0.6$, $\rho = 7,100$ $\text{kg/m}^3$ (cast iron).

Compute $\sqrt{2gH}$:

$$
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25} = \sqrt{4.905} = 2.2147 \text{ m/s}
$$

Substitute into the formula:

$$
A_c = \frac{50}{7100 \times 0.6 \times 30 \times 2.2147}
$$

Step by step:

- Denominator step 1: $7100 \times 0.6 = 4,260$
- Denominator step 2: $4,260 \times 30 = 127,800$
- Denominator step 3: $127,800 \times 2.2147 \approx 283,038.7$

Final division:

$$
A_c = \frac{50}{283,038.7} \approx 1.766 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Converting to $\text{cm}^2$:

$$
A_c = 1.766 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^4 = 1.766 \text{ cm}^2
$$

> **Conclusion:** The required sprue base cross-section is approximately **1.77 cm²**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sprue Taper Ratio (Compensation for Friction)

### 3.3.1 Problem
As metal falls down a parallel-sided sprue, friction reduces its velocity. To keep the sprue *full* at all points (preventing aspiration), the cross-section must decrease from top to bottom.

### 3.3.2 Energy Balance with Friction
$$
H = \frac{v^2}{2g} + h_f
$$

where $h_f$ is the head loss due to friction. For a sprue of length $L$ and hydraulic diameter $D$:

$$
h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{D \cdot 2g}
$$

with $f$ as the friction factor ($\approx 0.02$ for turbulent flow of molten metal).

### 3.3.3 Condition for Full Sprue
For the sprue to remain full, the velocity must remain constant at every level:

$$
v = C_d \sqrt{2gH} = \text{constant}
$$

This requires the **cross-section to taper** according to:

$$
\frac{A_{top}}{A_{bottom}} = \left(1 + \frac{4fL}{D}\right)^{1/2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text row explanation:** For typical mould conditions, $4fL/D \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$, leading to a taper ratio of $\sqrt{1.5} \approx 1.22$ to $\sqrt{2.0} \approx 1.41$. KTU problems often use the round figure of **1.5 : 1** as the standard AFS taper.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pouring Time Using Chvorinov's Rule Variant

### 3.4.1 Foundation
Chvorinov's rule states that solidification time $t_s$ is proportional to $(V/A)^2$, where $V$ = volume and $A$ = surface area of the casting. Empirically, for pouring time:

$$
t = K \cdot \sqrt{W}
$$

### 3.4.2 Justification
The constant $K$ depends on the **section thickness** of the casting:

| Section Thickness (mm) | $K$ (s/kg$^{0.5}$) |
|---|---|
| $\leq 6$ (thin) | 1.0 |
| 6 to 12 (medium) | 1.5 |
| 12 to 25 (medium-heavy) | 2.0 |
| $\geq 25$ (heavy) | 3.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Text row explanation:** Thin sections need *fast* pouring (small $K$) to prevent premature solidification. Heavy sections can be poured slowly (large $K$) to avoid mould erosion.

### 3.4.3 Worked Example
For a 100 kg casting with 10 mm section thickness, the pouring time is:

$$
t = 1.5 \times \sqrt{100} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Reynolds Number Check (Turbulence Criterion)

### 3.5.1 Definition
$$
Re = \frac{v \cdot D_h}{\nu}
$$

where:
- $v$ = velocity of metal (m/s)
- $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter of the channel (m) = $4A / P$
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of molten metal ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)

### 3.5.2 Threshold for Turbulent Flow
For **laminar flow** (desired in unpressurized systems), $Re < 2,000$. For **turbulent flow** (often necessary for cleanliness), $Re > 20,000$ is generally acceptable if the gating is **pressurized**.

### 3.5.3 Numerical Example
> **Given:** Sprue diameter $d = 25$ mm, $v = 2.21$ m/s, $\nu = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (aluminium).

Compute:

$$
D_h = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad v = 2.21 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{2.21 \times 0.025}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{0.05525}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 110,500
$$

> **Conclusion:** $Re = 1.1 \times 10^5$ — strongly **turbulent flow**. Suitable for pressurized systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Gating System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **KTU board examiner's** favourite 14-mark question framework.

| Casting Parameter | Recommended System | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminium alloy, thin section | **Pressurized, bottom gate** | Prevents oxide dross, fills fast |
| Cast iron, heavy section | **Unpressurized, top or parting** | Simple, robust, slag-tolerant |
| Steel, very heavy section | **Bottom-gated, pressurized with filter** | Avoids slag, prevents mould erosion |
| Bronze, intricate shape | **Pressurized, multiple finger gates** | Multiple fill points reduce velocity |
| Magnesium alloy | **Pressurized with inert gas cover** | Prevents oxidation |

---

## 3.7 Master Equation — The "All-in-One" Gating Equation

Combining the choke-area and pouring-time equations:

$$
A_c = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}
$$

Substituting $t = K\sqrt{W}$:

$$
A_c = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot K\sqrt{W} \cdot \sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{\sqrt{W}}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text row explanation:** The choke area is **proportional to $\sqrt{W}$** — meaning doubling the casting mass increases the required sprue area by only 41% ($\sqrt{2}$), not 100%. This is the reason foundries use *standardized* sprue diameters for a range of casting sizes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Complete Gating System Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of metal from the ladle to the mould cavity, identifying each component and its engineering role.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Ladle / Pouring Source] --> PB[Pouring Basin]
    PB --> SC[Strainer Core Slag Trap]
    SC --> SP1[Top of Sprue Area At]
    SP1 --> SP2[Bottom of Sprue Area Ab 1.5x At]
    SP2 --> SW[Sprue Well Impact Absorber]
    SW --> RN1[Runner Section 1]
    RN1 --> RN2[Runner Section 2 Tapered]
    RN2 --> GT1[Gate 1 Choke Area]
    RN2 --> GT2[Gate 2 Choke Area]
    RN2 --> GT3[Gate 3 Choke Area]
    GT1 --> MC1[Mould Cavity Section A]
    GT2 --> MC2[Mould Cavity Section B]
    GT3 --> MC3[Mould Cavity Section C]
    MC1 --> RS1[Riser Feeds Shrinkage]
    MC2 --> RS2[Riser Feeds Shrinkage]
    MC3 --> RS3[Riser Feeds Shrinkage]

    style L fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style PB fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style SC fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f
    style SP1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style SP2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style SW fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f
    style RN1 fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#7e5109
    style RN2 fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#7e5109
    style GT1 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21
    style GT2 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21
    style GT3 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21
    style MC1 fill:#a3e4d7,stroke:#117864
    style MC2 fill:#a3e4d7,stroke:#117864
    style MC3 fill:#a3e4d7,stroke:#117864
    style RS1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style RS2 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style RS3 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **green** sprue nodes represent the tapered down-sprue; the **red** gate nodes are the **choke** in a pressurized system. The **yellow** risers at the bottom are *not* part of the gating system — they are separate feed reservoirs.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Comparison — Pressurized vs Unpressurized System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNPRESSURIZED[Unpressurized Gravity System]
        U1[Pouring Basin] -->|Full| U2[Sprue Top Area]
        U2 -->|Tapered| U3[Sprue Base CHOKE Smallest]
        U3 -->|Falls freely| U4[Runner Larger]
        U4 -->|Accelerates| U5[Gate Largest Area]
        U5 -->|Turbulent entry| U6[Mould Cavity]
    end

    subgraph PRESSURIZED[Pressurized System]
        P1[Pouring Basin] -->|Stays full| P2[Sprue Top Largest]
        P2 -->|Tapered| P3[Runner Medium]
        P3 -->|Tapered| P4[Gate CHOKE Smallest]
        P4 -->|Gentle entry| P5[Mould Cavity]
    end

    style U3 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21
    style U5 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style P2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style P4 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **inversion of size hierarchy** between the two systems. In the unpressurized system, the smallest area (choke) is the *sprue base*; in the pressurized system, it is the *gate*.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Gate Position Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Top Gating] -->|Direct pour| CAV1[Hot metal at top\nBottom-up risk]
    BOT[Bottom Gating] -->|Indirect| CAV2[Hot metal at bottom\nDross rises to riser]
    PL[Parting Line Gating] -->|At mould joint| CAV3[Balanced fill\nMost common]
    ST[Step Gating] -->|Multiple heights| CAV4[Sequential fill\nComplex mould]
    YG[Y or Finger Gating] -->|Multiple branches| CAV5[Reduced velocity\nMultiple entry points]

    style TOP fill:#fad7a0
    style BOT fill:#aed6f1
    style PL fill:#a9dfbf
    style ST fill:#d7bde2
    style YG fill:#f5b7b1
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Decision Flowchart for Gating System Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Casting Design]) --> Q1{Alloy type?}
    Q1 -->|Ferrous| Q2{Section thickness?}
    Q1 -->|Non-ferrous| Q3{Oxidation tendency?}
    Q2 -->|Thin| U1[Unpressurized Top Gate]
    Q2 -->|Heavy| U2[Unpressurized Parting Line]
    Q3 -->|High Mg or Al| P1[Pressurized Bottom Gate]
    Q3 -->|Moderate| P2[Pressurized Parting Line]
    Q3 -->|Low bronze| P3[Pressurized Y Gate]
    U1 --> CHK1[Check Re less than 200000]
    U2 --> CHK2[Check slag trap]
    P1 --> CHK3[Check filter]
    P2 --> CHK4[Check pour basin full]
    P3 --> CHK5[Check finger uniformity]
    CHK1 --> END([Final Gating Drawing])
    CHK2 --> END
    CHK3 --> END
    CHK4 --> END
    CHK5 --> END
```

> **Engineering use:** This flowchart mirrors the *decision logic* taught in PCPET402 design studios. A KTU examiner may ask students to *draw and justify* such a flowchart for a 14-mark question.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Pressure Distribution Along the Gating System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Top of Sprue\nP = Patm] -->|Metal falls| B[Sprue Mid\nP less than Patm]
    B -->|Friction loss| C[Gate Entry\nP lowest]
    C -->|Static head| D[Mould Cavity\nP = Patm plus rho g h]

    style A fill:#d4efdf
    style B fill:#f9e79f
    style C fill:#f1948a
    style D fill:#aed6f1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **red gate** experiences the **lowest pressure**, which is why the *aspirator effect* is most dangerous at this point. A *pressurized* system maintains high pressure throughout by keeping all sections full.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — End Semester Examination pattern for PCPET402: Part A (3-mark short answer) and Part B (14-mark long answer with internal choice, sub-divided into 7 + 7 marks). The questions below are calibrated to this format.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set 2]**
**"Define a gating system and list its primary functions." (3 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:
A *gating system* is the network of refractory channels that conveys molten metal from the pouring basin to the mould cavity, controlling flow rate, velocity, and thermal gradient during casting. Its **six primary functions** are:
1. Conveyance of molten metal
2. Flow rate control (pouring time)
3. Velocity regulation (anti-turbulence)
4. Dross/slag retention
5. Directional solidification support
6. Pressure regime maintenance (pressurized/unpressurized)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for the functions (any 4 of 6).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series A]**
**"Distinguish between pressurized and unpressurized gating systems. (3 Marks)"**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Unpressurized | Pressurized |
|---|---|---|
| Choke location | Sprue base | Gate |
| Area ratio $A_s : A_r : A_g$ | 1 : 2 : 4 | 4 : 2 : 1 |
| Velocity at gate | High (turbulent) | Low (gentle) |
| Aspiration risk | High | Low |
| Typical application | Ferrous castings | Non-ferrous castings |

> Any 3 valid points fetch full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B allows students to choose ONE of TWO alternatives. Both are presented below with complete model solutions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**

**(a) Explain the various components of a gating system with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A steel casting of mass 200 kg is to be poured in a sand mould. The effective sprue height is 250 mm. Determine: (i) the pouring time, (ii) the velocity at the sprue base, and (iii) the required sprue base area. Take $K = 1.7$ s/kg$^{0.5}$, $C_d = 0.6$, density of steel = 7,850 $\text{kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Components of a Gating System (7 Marks)**

A gating system consists of the following components, in flow order:

1. **Pouring Basin (1 mark):** A refractory-lined cup that receives the ladle stream, decouples the pouring jet, and feeds the sprue at a controlled rate. A *strainer core* traps the first slag.

2. **Sprue (Down-Sprue) (2 marks):** The vertical channel that conveys metal from the basin to the parting plane. Two geometries:
   - *Parallel sprue* — constant area; used only in small castings.
   - *Tapered sprue* — top area 1.5× bottom area; standard AFS practice. The taper prevents the *aspirator effect* (back-suction of gases into the sprue).

3. **Sprue Well (Splash) (1 mark):** A cylindrical pocket at the sprue base that absorbs the impact energy and reduces metal velocity before it enters the runner.

4. **Runner (1.5 marks):** A horizontal channel of rectangular cross-section (depth:width = 1:2) that distributes metal to the gates. The runner is placed on the *drag* (lower mould half) to remain full.

5. **Gate (Ingate) (1 mark):** The final entry point of metal into the mould cavity. It is the **choke** in a pressurized system. The gate is sized to limit velocity below 0.5 m/s (for cast iron) to avoid mould erosion.

6. **Riser (0.5 marks):** Although not strictly part of the gating system, the riser is shown in diagrams as a metal reservoir that feeds shrinkage during solidification.

> A neat labelled diagram (such as the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4) carries 1 additional mark.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 200$ kg
- $H = 250$ mm = 0.25 m
- $K = 1.7$ s/kg$^{0.5}$
- $C_d = 0.6$
- $\rho = 7,850$ $\text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

**Step (i): Pouring Time (1.5 marks)**

Using the empirical formula:
$$
t = K \cdot \sqrt{W}
$$

Substitute values:
$$
t = 1.7 \times \sqrt{200}
$$

Compute the square root:
$$
\sqrt{200} = \sqrt{100 \times 2} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 10 \times 1.4142 = 14.142
$$

Therefore:
$$
t = 1.7 \times 14.142 = 24.04 \text{ s}
$$

**Pouring time ≈ 24 seconds.**

---

**Step (ii): Velocity at Sprue Base (2 marks)**

Using Bernoulli's equation with discharge coefficient:
$$
v = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
$$

Compute $\sqrt{2gH}$:
$$
2gH = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 4.905
$$

$$
\sqrt{4.905} \approx 2.2147 \text{ m/s}
$$

Multiply by $C_d$:
$$
v = 0.6 \times 2.2147 = 1.329 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Velocity ≈ 1.33 m/s.**

---

**Step (iii): Required Sprue Base Area (3.5 marks)**

Using the continuity-based formula:
$$
A_c = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}
$$

From step (ii), $C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = v = 1.329$ m/s. Substituting:
$$
A_c = \frac{200}{7,850 \times 1.329 \times 24.04}
$$

Compute the denominator step by step:
- Step 1: $7,850 \times 1.329 = 10,432.65$
- Step 2: $10,432.65 \times 24.04 = 250,802.0$

Final division:
$$
A_c = \frac{200}{250,802.0} = 7.975 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Converting to $\text{cm}^2$:
$$
A_c = 7.975 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^4 = 7.975 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Required sprue base area ≈ 7.98 cm² (≈ 8 cm²).**

If a circular sprue is used, the diameter is:
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4A_c}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 7.98 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.016 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0319 \text{ m} = 31.9 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A sprue of diameter ≈ **32 mm** (with a 1.5:1 taper) is required.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a pressurized gating system. State its advantages and applications. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A horizontal runner of rectangular cross-section (width 25 mm, depth 12 mm) carries molten aluminium at a velocity of 0.8 m/s. The kinematic viscosity of the melt is $5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. Determine the Reynolds number and comment on the flow regime. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Pressurized Gating System (7 Marks)**

*(Note: A neat sketch is mandatory for full marks — refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Definition (1 mark):** A *pressurized gating system* is one in which the **gate acts as the choke** and the entire system (sprue, runner, pouring basin) is kept completely full of molten metal throughout pouring.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Metal enters the pouring basin, which is kept full.
- The sprue is **tapered** (top area > bottom area) to prevent aspiration.
- The runner is also **tapered** to maintain full flow.
- The **gate is the narrowest** section — it controls the flow rate.
- Because the system is full, **static pressure** exists at every point, ensuring a *gentle, non-turbulent* entry into the mould cavity.

**Area Ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$
A_s : A_r : A_g = 4 : 2 : 1
$$

That is, the sprue top is 4 units, the runner is 2 units, and the gate is 1 unit (the choke).

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Prevents *aspiration* (back-suction of gases) because the system is full.
2. Avoids *jetting* and mould erosion.
3. Suitable for *non-ferrous* alloys that oxidize easily (aluminium, magnesium, bronze).
4. Allows use of *filters* and *strainer cores* to trap inclusions.
5. Reduces turbulence and improves casting soundness.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Aluminium alloy castings (automotive pistons, gearbox housings).
- Magnesium alloy aerospace castings.
- Bronze and brass valve/pipe castings.

---

**Part (b) — Reynolds Number Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Runner width: $w = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Runner depth: $d = 12$ mm = 0.012 m
- Velocity: $v = 0.8$ m/s
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area (1 mark)**
$$
A = w \times d = 0.025 \times 0.012 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Wetted Perimeter (1 mark)**
$$
P = 2(w + d) = 2(0.025 + 0.012) = 2(0.037) = 0.074 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic Diameter (1.5 marks)**
$$
D_h = \frac{4A}{P} = \frac{4 \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{0.074} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.074} = 0.01622 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Reynolds Number (2 marks)**
$$
Re = \frac{v \cdot D_h}{\nu} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.01622}{5 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

Compute numerator:
$$
0.8 \times 0.01622 = 0.012976
$$

Compute denominator (already $5 \times 10^{-7}$):
$$
Re = \frac{0.012976}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 25,952
$$

**Reynolds Number ≈ 25,952.**

**Step 5: Comment on Flow Regime (1.5 marks)**

- Threshold for laminar flow: $Re < 2,000$.
- Threshold for fully turbulent flow: $Re > 4,000$.
- $Re = 25,952$ → **Turbulent flow regime.**

> **Engineering interpretation:** Since the flow is turbulent and the metal is aluminium (oxidation-sensitive), the gating should be designed as a **pressurized** system with a *strainer core* at the pouring basin to trap oxides generated by the turbulence. A Reynolds number above 20,000 is acceptable in pressurized gating because the system remains full and the *static pressure* prevents air ingress.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (1 mark lost):** Students often forget to convert mm to m. Always check units first.
> 2. **Wrong area ratio (2 marks lost):** Confusing pressurized (4:2:1) with unpressurized (1:2:4) reverses the answer.
> 3. **Skipping the Reynolds comment (1 mark lost):** A numerical answer without an engineering interpretation loses 1 mark in KTU 2024 evaluation.
> 4. **Forgetting $C_d$ in velocity (1 mark lost):** Writing $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ instead of $v = C_d\sqrt{2gH}$ is a common slip.
> 5. **Not stating the choke location first (1 mark lost):** Always begin the solution with *"The choke is at the gate/sprue base"* for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — memorize before the KTU ESE exam.**

- 🔑 **Definition:** A gating system is the channel network delivering molten metal from the pouring basin to the mould cavity.
- 🔑 **Six functions:** Conveyance, flow control, velocity regulation, slag retention, thermal management, pressure stabilization.
- 🔑 **Six components in order:** Pouring basin → Strainer → Sprue → Sprue well → Runner → Gate → Cavity.
- 🔑 **Tapered sprue rule:** Top area = 1.5× bottom area (standard AFS).
- 🔑 **Unpressurized area ratio:** $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$ — *sprue is the choke*.
- 🔑 **Pressurized area ratio:** $A_s : A_r : A_g = 4 : 2 : 1$ — *gate is the choke*.
- 🔑 **Core velocity formula:** $v = C_d \sqrt{2gH}$, with $C_d = 0.6$ for sprue, $0.7$ for gate.
- 🔑 **Choke area formula:** $A_c = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$.
- 🔑 **Pouring time formula:** $t = K \sqrt{W}$ — $K$ depends on section thickness (1.0 to 3.0).
- 🔑 **Velocity limits:** $\leq 0.5$ m/s for cast iron, $\leq 1.5$ m/s for aluminium (gate velocity).
- 🔑 **Reynolds number threshold:** $Re < 2,000$ laminar; $Re > 4,000$ turbulent; $20,000 < Re < 200,000$ ideal for pressurized systems.
- 🔑 **Aspirator effect:** Caused by parallel sprue — eliminated by tapering.
- 🔑 **Strainer core location:** At the pouring basin / sprue top.
- 🔑 **Runner placement:** Always on the *drag* (lower mould half).
- 🔑 **Hydraulic diameter formula:** $D_h = 4A/P$.
- 🔑 **Standard densities:** CI = 7,100; Steel = 7,850; Al = 2,700; Cu = 8,960; Bronze = 8,800 kg/m³.
- 🔑 **Common exam traps:** Wrong units (mm vs m), wrong area ratio, missing $C_d$, missing Reynolds comment, not identifying the choke.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** This topic is a *high-weightage, high-scoring* area in Part B. Master the *three formulas* (velocity, choke area, pouring time) and the *two area ratios*, and you can secure 12+ out of 14 marks in any gating design question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Riser

A **riser** (also called a *feeder* or *feed head*) is a reservoir of molten metal that is intentionally designed, placed, and connected to a sand mold casting in order to compensate for the volumetric contraction (shrinkage) that occurs during the **liquid-to-solid phase transformation** and the subsequent cooling of the casting down to ambient temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A riser is an auxiliary mass of molten metal placed in the mold that solidifies *after* the casting itself, so that it can continuously feed liquid metal into the casting cavity to prevent the formation of solidification shrinkage defects such as **shrinkage cavities (macro-porosity)** and **microshrinkage (micro-porosity)**.

### Key Governing Principles

- **Liquid metal density (ρ_liquid):** ≈ 7.2 g/cm³ for cast iron, ≈ 2.4 g/cm³ for aluminum
- **Solid metal density (ρ_solid):** ≈ 7.87 g/cm³ for steel
- **Volumetric shrinkage for most non-ferrous metals:** ~**6.5%**
- **Volumetric shrinkage for cast iron:** ~**1 to 3%** (compensated partly by graphitic expansion)

## 1.2 The Shrinkage Problem – Why Risers Are Needed

When molten metal solidifies, it undergoes three types of volume contractions:

1. **Liquid contraction** (above the liquidus temperature) — the riser can NOT compensate for this.
2. **Solidification contraction** (between liquidus and solidus) — **this is what the riser MUST compensate for.**
3. **Solid-state contraction** (below solidus) — compensated by designing a *shrinkage allowance* in the pattern, NOT by the riser.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "The Water Tower Feeding a Village"**
> Imagine a village (the casting) connected by a pipe to a large elevated water tank (the riser). When the village uses up water (the casting shrinks as it solidifies), water from the tank flows in to keep the village supplied. The tank must be **larger** and **cooler-isolated** so that the village runs out of water *first* and the tank still has water to give. If the tank empties or freezes first, the village runs dry and develops a "crater" (shrinkage cavity). In the same way, a riser must have a **larger volume-to-surface-area ratio (modulus)** so that it solidifies *last*, continuing to feed the casting as it shrinks.

## 1.4 Types of Risers (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Top Riser** | Placed above the casting; gravity feeds metal downward | Simple, common for small castings |
| **Side Riser** | Placed alongside the casting at the parting line | Tall, vertically-cast components |
| **Bottom Riser** | Placed below the casting; pressure-fed | Heavy steel castings |
| **Open Riser** | Exposed to atmosphere; visible solidification shrinkage on top | Plain carbon steels, atmospheric pressure |
| **Blind Riser** | Completely enclosed in the mold | Improved yield, gas-protected melts |
| **Atmospheric Riser** | Connected to atmosphere via a vent/core | Pressure compensation |
| **Pressure Riser (Exothermic Sleeve)** | Internal exothermic reaction; gas-pressure assisted | Premium castings, high yield |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Criterion for Riser Sizing

A riser is functional if and only if it satisfies **two simultaneous conditions**:

### Condition 1: Solidification Time Criterion
$$t_{riser} \geq t_{casting}$$

The riser must remain liquid **longer** than the casting. If the casting solidifies first, no liquid metal is left to feed shrinkage.

### Condition 2: Volume Criterion
$$V_{riser} \geq V_{shrinkage} + V_{casting}\left(\frac{\rho_{solid} - \rho_{liquid}}{\rho_{liquid}}\right)$$

The riser volume must be large enough to contain enough liquid metal to fill the shrinkage void plus any riser that itself undergoes solidification shrinkage.

## 2.2 Chvorinov's Rule – The Heart of Riser Design

Chvorinov's rule is the **most critical equation** in KTU Module 1. It states that the **solidification time** of a casting is proportional to the **square of its volume-to-surface-area ratio (the modulus)**.

### Mathematical Statement
$$t = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

where:

- $t$ = solidification time (seconds or minutes)
- $B$ = **mold constant** (depends on mold material, metal superheat, latent heat of fusion, thermal conductivity)
- $V$ = volume of the casting/riser (cm³ or m³)
- $A$ = surface area through which heat is extracted (cm² or m²)
- $M = \dfrac{V}{A}$ = **modulus** of the casting or riser (cm or m)

### Mold Constant $B$ (Typical Values)

| Mold Material | $B$ (s/cm²) | Application |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Green sand | 4 to 6 | General foundry |
| Dry sand | 9 to 11 | Heavy steel castings |
| Skin-dry | 7 to 8 | Medium castings |
| Metallic (chill) | 2 to 3 | Rapid cooling surfaces |
| Ceramic / insulating sleeve | 14 to 20 | Exothermic riser sleeves |

## 2.3 Derivations of the Modulus for Standard Geometries

The modulus $M = V/A$ must be calculated for both the riser and the casting. Standard values are memorized for the exam.

| Geometry | Volume $V$ | Surface Area $A$ (heat-extracting) | Modulus $M = V/A$ |
|----------|------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| Sphere (radius $R$) | $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | $4\pi R^2$ | $\dfrac{R}{3}$ |
| Cylinder (length $L$, radius $R$), side + 1 end | $\pi R^2 L$ | $2\pi R L + \pi R^2$ | $\dfrac{RL}{2L+R}$ |
| Cylinder, side only (open top & bottom) | $\pi R^2 L$ | $2\pi R L$ | $\dfrac{R}{2}$ |
| Cube (side $a$), all 6 faces | $a^3$ | $6a^2$ | $\dfrac{a}{6}$ |
| Plate (length $L$, width $W$, thickness $T$), 6 faces | $L \cdot W \cdot T$ | $2(LW+WT+LT)$ | $\dfrac{LWT}{2(LW+WT+LT)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb:**
> Among all geometric shapes of a given volume, the **sphere** has the *lowest* surface area, hence the *lowest* surface-to-volume ratio. Wait — correction: the sphere has the *highest* modulus-to-volume ratio, meaning it solidifies **slowest** — perfect for risers. That is why a spherical riser is the **most efficient geometry**.

## 2.4 The Riser Design Procedure (Modulus Method)

The classical step-by-step procedure taught in KTU Module 1:

1. **Step 1 — Estimate casting modulus** $M_c$ using the casting's effective dimensions and subtracting the feeding distance from the riser to the farthest hot spot.
2. **Step 2 — Set the riser modulus** $M_r \geq 1.2 \cdot M_c$ (using a 20% safety factor).
3. **Step 3 — Choose riser geometry** (sphere, cylinder, top-bar). For maximum efficiency, prefer a sphere. A practical sphere's modulus is:
$$M_{sphere} = \frac{R}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = 3 M_r$$
4. **Step 4 — Compute the riser volume** $V_r$ from the chosen geometry.
5. **Step 5 — Check the volume sufficiency criterion** to ensure $V_r$ contains enough feed metal.
6. **Step 6 — Compute the yield** $Y$:
$$Y = \dfrac{W_{casting}}{W_{casting} + W_{riser} + W_{gating\ system}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.5 Caine's Method (Yield-Based Riser Design)

For higher yields, the riser volume can be estimated directly from the casting volume and shrinkage percentage:

$$V_{riser} = \frac{S \cdot V_{casting}}{f - S}$$

where:
- $S$ = volumetric solidification shrinkage (e.g., 0.065 for steels, 0.03 for cast iron)
- $f$ = shrinkage factor in riser = 0.14 to 0.20 (typical riser utilization efficiency)

## 2.6 Niyama Criterion (Shrinkage Porosity Prediction)

A widely-used internal-quality criterion for steel castings. **Low Niyama values indicate a high probability of shrinkage porosity.**

$$N = \frac{G}{\sqrt{\dot{T}}}$$

where:
- $G$ = local thermal gradient (°C/cm)
- $\dot{T}$ = local cooling rate (°C/s)
- Critical Niyama value: **$N_{crit} \approx 1.0$ to $1.5$** (threshold below which shrinkage is likely)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The Niyama criterion is implemented in commercial casting simulation software such as **MAGMA, ProCAST, and FLOW-3D**. Casting engineers design feeder/riser placement to keep $N > N_{crit}$ throughout the casting, ensuring internal soundness — a direct application of the KTU 2024 OBE philosophy of *Design-Simulate-Validate*.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $t = B (V/A)^2$ | Chvorinov's rule | $t$ in s, $V$ in cm³, $A$ in cm² |
| 2 | $M = V/A$ | Modulus | cm or m |
| 3 | $t_r \geq t_c$ | Solidification time criterion | — |
| 4 | $M_r \geq M_c$ | Modulus criterion | — |
| 5 | $M_{sphere} = R/3$ | Sphere modulus | cm |
| 6 | $M_{cyl} = RL/(2L+R)$ | Cylinder modulus (side + 1 end) | cm |
| 7 | $V_r = \dfrac{S V_c}{f - S}$ | Caine's yield-based formula | cm³ |
| 8 | $N = G / \sqrt{\dot{T}}$ | Niyama criterion | °C^{0.5}·cm^{-0.5}·s^{0.5} |
| 9 | $B = \dfrac{\rho L_H}{h \cdot \Delta T \cdot k}$ | Mold constant (derivation) | s/cm² |
| 10 | $Y = \dfrac{W_c}{W_c + W_r + W_g} \times 100\%$ | Casting yield | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula sheet above, the modulus term $\dfrac{V}{A}$ is the **single most important concept** in KTU riser design. If you remember nothing else, remember: **A larger modulus ⇒ slower solidification ⇒ more effective riser.**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule

### Assumptions
1. The casting is approximated as an **infinite slab** (one-dimensional heat flow).
2. The mold has uniform thermal properties.
3. The solid-liquid interface moves inward at a rate governed by **Newton's law of cooling**.
4. Latent heat of fusion $L_H$ dominates the heat extraction (no superheat contribution considered in the basic form).

### Step 1: Newton's Law of Cooling
The rate of heat extracted by the mold per unit area is proportional to the temperature difference between the casting surface and the mold:
$$\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{mold} - T_{surface}) = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where $h$ is the heat-transfer coefficient at the casting–mold interface.

### Step 2: Latent Heat Released by the Solidified Skin
Consider a solidified shell of thickness $x$ on a flat plate. The volume solidified per unit time is $A \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$, releasing latent heat at a rate:
$$\dot{Q} = \rho \cdot L_H \cdot A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Equating the Two Heat-Transfer Rates
$$\rho \cdot L_H \cdot A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Cancel $A$:
$$\rho \cdot L_H \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = h \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate
$$dx = \frac{h \cdot \Delta T}{\rho \cdot L_H} \cdot dt$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ at $t = 0$ to the half-thickness $V/A$ at solidification time $t$:

$$\int_0^{V/A} dx = \frac{h \cdot \Delta T}{\rho \cdot L_H} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\frac{V}{A} = \frac{h \cdot \Delta T}{\rho \cdot L_H} \cdot t$$

### Step 5: Solve for Solidification Time
$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L_H}{h \cdot \Delta T} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)$$

Wait — this gives a linear dependence. To obtain the **empirical $V/A$ squared** relation, we must account for the **resistance of the solid shell** (a refinement). With the shell acting as an additional resistance, $h$ becomes time-dependent and the result becomes:

$$t = \underbrace{\left[\frac{\pi \rho L_H}{4 k_s (T_m - T_0)}\right]^{2}}_{B} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form
$$\boxed{t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}}$$

This matches Chvorinov's empirical observation (1940) and is the foundation of all modulus-based riser design taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Problem (Model 14-Mark Question)

### **Problem Statement**
A cylindrical casting of diameter **D = 20 cm** and length **L = 60 cm** is to be cast in green sand. A top riser of cylindrical shape is to be provided. The mold constant $B = 5$ s/cm². The casting has an end-to-end modulus. Determine:

1. The modulus of the casting.
2. The required riser modulus (using a 20% safety factor).
3. The diameter of a spherical riser that satisfies this modulus.
4. The volume of the riser.
5. The solidification time of the casting.
6. Comment on whether a cylindrical riser of equal height and diameter (with side + 1 end feeding) would be more efficient.

### Given Data
- $D_c = 20$ cm, $L_c = 60$ cm
- $B = 5$ s/cm²
- Safety factor = 1.2

### Part (a) Casting Modulus $M_c$

Treat the cylindrical casting as having **6 effective faces** (top, bottom, side). 
$$V_c = \pi R_c^2 L_c = \pi (10)^2 (60) = 6000\pi \text{ cm}^3$$
$$A_c = 2\pi R_c^2 + 2\pi R_c L_c = 2\pi (10)^2 + 2\pi(10)(60) = 200\pi + 1200\pi = 1400\pi \text{ cm}^2$$
$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{6000\pi}{1400\pi} = 4.286 \text{ cm}$$

### Part (b) Riser Modulus $M_r$
$$M_r = 1.2 \cdot M_c = 1.2 \times 4.286 = 5.143 \text{ cm}$$

### Part (c) Diameter of Spherical Riser
For a sphere, $M_{sphere} = R/3$, so:
$$R = 3 M_r = 3 \times 5.143 = 15.43 \text{ cm}$$
$$D_{sphere} = 30.86 \text{ cm} \approx 30.9 \text{ cm}$$

### Part (d) Volume of Spherical Riser
$$V_r = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (15.43)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \times 3672.4 = 15382 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Part (e) Solidification Time of Casting
$$t_c = B \cdot M_c^2 = 5 \times (4.286)^2 = 5 \times 18.37 = 91.83 \text{ s} \approx 92 \text{ s}$$

### Part (f) Efficiency of a Cylindrical Riser (H = D)
For a cylinder with $H = D = 2R$, side + 1 end feeding:
$$M_{cyl} = \frac{R L}{2L + R} = \frac{R \cdot 2R}{2(2R) + R} = \frac{2R^2}{5R} = \frac{2R}{5}$$

To match $M_r = 5.143$ cm:
$$\frac{2R}{5} = 5.143 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = 12.86 \text{ cm}$$
$$D = 25.7 \text{ cm}, \quad L = 25.7 \text{ cm}$$

Cylinder volume:
$$V_{cyl} = \pi R^2 L = \pi (12.86)^2 (25.7) = 13364 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Comparison Table (Efficiency)

| Riser Type | Volume $V_r$ | Modulus $M_r$ |
|------------|--------------|----------------|
| Spherical | **15382 cm³** | 5.143 cm |
| Cylinder (H = D) | **13364 cm³** | 5.143 cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The cylinder requires *less* volume to achieve the *same* modulus, so a cylinder is more volume-efficient. However, the **sphere** solidifies *most slowly* per unit volume, so the sphere is the *thermally safest* design. In practice, **top risers** are usually **cylinders** (for ease of molding and feeding), and **side risers** are typically **spherical** for maximum feeding distance.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of the Mold Constant $B$

From Section 3.1, Step 5 with shell resistance:
$$B = \left[\frac{\pi \rho L_H}{4 k_s (T_m - T_0)}\right]^2$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of solidifying metal
- $L_H$ = latent heat of fusion
- $k_s$ = thermal conductivity of the solidified shell
- $T_m$ = melting point of the metal
- $T_0$ = initial mold temperature

This explains why $B$ is **metal-and-mold specific** — the KTU examiner frequently tests this.
