# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
Define a **riser** in foundry practice. State the **two essential conditions** that a riser must satisfy to be effective in a sand mold casting.

### **Model Answer:**
A riser is a reservoir of molten metal placed in a sand mold which solidifies after the casting and feeds it to compensate for solidification shrinkage.

The two essential conditions are:
1. **Solidification time criterion:** $t_{riser} \geq t_{casting}$ (i.e., modulus $M_r \geq M_c$).
2. **Volume criterion:** the riser volume must be sufficient to feed the entire shrinkage volume plus its own internal solidification shrinkage (Caine's criterion $V_r \geq \dfrac{S \cdot V_c}{f - S}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Writing only one condition scores a maximum of 1.5 marks. Always mention BOTH conditions with their mathematical forms.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
State **Chvorinov's rule** for the solidification time of a casting. Mention the significance of the **mold constant** $B$ and the **modulus** $M = V/A$.

### **Model Answer:**
Chvorinov's rule: 
$$t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

- **Mold constant $B$:** depends on the mold material (sand, chill, ceramic), latent heat of fusion, superheat, and thermal properties. Higher $B$ (insulating mold) ⇒ longer solidification time.
- **Modulus $M = V/A$:** the **shape factor** governing solidification time. A casting with a higher modulus solidifies more slowly, hence the riser must have a *larger* modulus than the casting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A steel casting of **cylindrical shape**, diameter 15 cm and length 45 cm, is cast horizontally in green sand with a mold constant $B = 4$ s/cm². Calculate the **solidification time** of the casting. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **spherical top riser** for the casting in part (a) using a 20% safety factor. Also calculate the **casting yield** if the gating system mass is 1.5 kg and the riser mass is to be computed. Steel density = 7.2 g/cm³ (liquid) and 7.87 g/cm³ (solid). (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

$$R_c = 7.5 \text{ cm}, \quad L_c = 45 \text{ cm}$$
$$V_c = \pi R_c^2 L_c = \pi (7.5)^2 (45) = 2531.25 \pi = 7952.2 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$A_c = 2\pi R_c^2 + 2\pi R_c L_c = 2\pi(7.5)^2 + 2\pi(7.5)(45) = 112.5\pi + 675\pi = 787.5\pi \text{ cm}^2$$
$$A_c = 2474.0 \text{ cm}^2$$
$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{7952.2}{2474.0} = 3.214 \text{ cm}$$
$$t_c = B \cdot M_c^2 = 4 \times (3.214)^2 = 4 \times 10.33 = 41.32 \text{ s}$$

**Part (b):**

$$M_r = 1.2 \times 3.214 = 3.857 \text{ cm}$$
$$R_r = 3 M_r = 3 \times 3.857 = 11.57 \text{ cm}$$
$$D_r = 23.14 \text{ cm}$$
$$V_r = \frac{4}{3} \pi R_r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (11.57)^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi \times 1548.6 = 6485 \text{ cm}^3$$

Mass of riser (using solid density 7.87 g/cm³):
$$W_r = V_r \cdot \rho_s = 6485 \times 7.87 = 51,037 \text{ g} \approx 51.04 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of casting:
$$W_c = V_c \cdot \rho_s = 7952.2 \times 7.87 = 62,584 \text{ g} \approx 62.58 \text{ kg}$$

Gating mass: $W_g = 1.5$ kg

**Casting yield:**
$$Y = \frac{W_c}{W_c + W_r + W_g} \times 100 = \frac{62.58}{62.58 + 51.04 + 1.5} \times 100 = \frac{62.58}{115.12} \times 100 \approx 54.36\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain **Chvorinov's rule** in detail with the relevant derivation starting from Newton's law of cooling. State clearly the assumption of an infinite slab casting. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **plate casting** has dimensions 30 cm × 20 cm × 5 cm. Calculate (i) the modulus of the casting, (ii) the diameter of a cylindrical riser with $H = 1.5 D$ such that it has 30% higher modulus than the casting. The mold constant $B = 6$ s/cm². (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):** See Section 3.1 above for the detailed derivation. (Examiner will allot 7 marks for the derivation including assumptions, Newton's law, latent heat release, separation of variables, integration, and final form $t = B(V/A)^2$.)

**Part (b):**

(i) Plate casting modulus:
$$L = 30, \quad W = 20, \quad T = 5 \text{ cm}$$
$$V_c = L \cdot W \cdot T = 30 \times 20 \times 5 = 3000 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$A_c = 2(LW + WT + LT) = 2(600 + 100 + 150) = 2 \times 850 = 1700 \text{ cm}^2$$
$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{3000}{1700} = 1.765 \text{ cm}$$

(ii) Riser modulus:
$$M_r = 1.3 \times M_c = 1.3 \times 1.765 = 2.294 \text{ cm}$$

For a cylinder with $H = 1.5 D = 3R$:
$$M_{cyl} = \frac{R L}{2L + R} = \frac{R(3R)}{2(3R) + R} = \frac{3R^2}{7R} = \frac{3R}{7}$$
$$M_r = \frac{3R}{7} = 2.294 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{7 \times 2.294}{3} = 5.353 \text{ cm}$$
$$D = 2R = 10.71 \text{ cm}, \quad H = 1.5 D = 16.06 \text{ cm}$$

Solidification time (if required):
$$t_c = B \cdot M_c^2 = 6 \times (1.765)^2 = 6 \times 3.115 = 18.69 \text{ s}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Students write $M_r = M_c$ instead of $M_r = 1.2 M_c$ — *loses 1 to 2 marks*.
> 2. **Using the wrong surface area:** For a cylinder **with end caps**, the formula is $A = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R L$. Using only the curved surface $2\pi R L$ is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Confusing volume and weight:** Mixing $V$ (cm³) with $W$ (kg) when computing yield — *always convert using density*.
> 4. **Not specifying heat-extracting area:** In a **blind riser**, the *top* is not heat-extracting. The KTU exam specifically deducts marks for including all six faces in $A$ for a blind riser.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** $B$ in s/cm² and dimensions in cm ⇒ modulus in cm. Mixing inches and cm leads to wrong answers — *commonly penalized 1 mark*.
> 6. **Choosing sphere for a long, thin casting:** A sphere may not physically fit; the KTU 2024 paper often asks you to *justify* the geometry choice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Riser Design and Solidification Time Calculation:**

- ✅ **Chvorinov's rule:** $t = B \cdot (V/A)^2$ — the foundation. **Do not forget the square.**
- ✅ **Modulus** $M = V/A$ — geometry-dependent. Sphere has the **highest** $M$ for a given volume.
- ✅ **Sphere modulus:** $M = R/3$. **Cylinder (side + 1 end):** $M = RL/(2L+R)$.
- ✅ **Two conditions** for an effective riser: $t_r \geq t_c$ **AND** $V_r \geq V_{shrinkage}$.
- ✅ **Safety factor of 1.2** on modulus is the KTU standard convention.
- ✅ **Top riser** ⇒ easy to mold, gravity-fed. **Side riser** ⇒ long feeding distance. **Bottom riser** ⇒ high pressure.
- ✅ **Open riser** ⇒ exposed to atmosphere. **Blind riser** ⇒ enclosed, higher yield.
- ✅ **Exothermic sleeves** raise effective $B$ and improve feeding efficiency.
- ✅ **Caine's yield-based equation:** $V_r = S \cdot V_c / (f - S)$; $S \approx 0.065$ for steel, $0.03$ for cast iron.
- ✅ **Niyama criterion** $N = G / \sqrt{\dot{T}}$ predicts shrinkage porosity; $N_{crit} \approx 1.0$–$1.5$.
- ✅ **Casting yield** $Y = W_c / (W_c + W_r + W_g) \times 100\%$. Aim for 60–70% in steel, 80%+ in CI.
- ✅ **Feeding distance** (the maximum distance a riser can feed) is **alloy-specific** — for steel, $L_f \approx 4T$ to $5T$ where $T$ is the section thickness.
- ✅ **Differential cooling:** Insulating the riser (sleeve) increases $B$ and prolongs liquid life.
- ✅ **Directional solidification:** A riser promotes heat flow *away from* the casting — achieved by chilling the casting side and insulating the riser.
- ✅ **Unit check:** $B$ in s/cm² ⇒ dimensions in cm ⇒ $M$ in cm ⇒ $t$ in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:**
> *"Modulus of Riser must be greater than Modulus of Casting. Sphere rules, Cylinder schools, the Cube just fools."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Chills and Chaplets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use of Chills and Chaplets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chills – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHILLS (KTU 2024 Definition):** A chill is a metallic (or sometimes non-metallic) insert embedded in the sand mould wall, or placed within the mould cavity, to increase the rate of heat extraction from a specific region of the molten metal. Chills force that region to solidify faster than the surrounding metal, thereby promoting **directional solidification** from the extremities towards the riser.

**Primary Purpose of Chills:**
- To promote directional solidification.
- To eliminate shrinkage porosity (cavities and piping) in thick sections.
- To refine the grain structure locally (chilled surface gives fine equiaxed/pear-shaped grains).
- To produce a chilled wear-resistant surface on a tough, ductile core (e.g., chilled cast iron rolls).

## 1.2 Chaplets – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHAPLETS (KTU 2024 Definition):** Chaplets are small metallic (steel, cast iron, or brass) supports placed inside the mould cavity to support the **core** against displacement caused by the hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, and metallostatic force of the molten metal, thereby maintaining the core in its correct position.

**Primary Purpose of Chaplets:**
- To hold the core in position against buoyant and metallostatic forces.
- To prevent core shift / core sag.
- The chaplet must be of a material that fuses/welds with the casting metal, so it becomes an integral part of the final casting.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Chill Analogy – The Ice-Cube Tray Effect 🧊:**
> Imagine pouring hot tea into an ice-cube tray. The metal tray walls extract heat very quickly from the regions touching the walls, while the centre remains hot. In casting, a **chill is an "engineered hot-spot remover"** — it acts like a localised heat sink, forcing the molten metal in that zone to freeze first. This is exactly the opposite of what a **riser** does (riser is the last to freeze and feeds liquid metal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Chaplet Analogy – The Floating Cork Analogy 🪙:**
> Drop a cork into a bucket of water — it floats. Similarly, a sand core inside molten metal tends to "float" (buoyancy) and would be pushed out of place. A **chaplet works like an anchor pin** screwed into the core and the mould wall, keeping the cork (core) at exactly the right depth. Just as a nail holding the cork would eventually get covered by the water that freezes, the chaplet gets dissolved/merged into the solidifying casting.

## 1.4 Common Metrics & Units (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solidification time | $t$ | seconds (s) | Time for a casting to solidify |
| Modulus | $M$ | cm or m | Ratio V/A — drives cooling rate |
| Mold constant | $B$ | s/cm² | Material property of mould |
| Chill modulus | $M_{ch}$ | cm or m | Determines chill effectiveness |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Chills Required? (Chvorinov's Rule Background)

The local solidification time at any point in a casting is governed by **Chvorinov's Rule** (a high-yield KTU 2024 topic):

$$t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

where:
- $t$ = local solidification time (s)
- $B$ = mould constant (s/cm²); for sand mould $B \approx 80$–$120$ s/cm²; for metallic mould $B \approx 10$–$30$ s/cm².
- $V$ = volume of the casting (or section being considered) in cm³
- $A$ = surface area of that section in cm²
- $V/A$ is called the **Modulus**, $M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solidification time $\propto$ Modulus².** A section with a **larger modulus** (thicker cross-section, less surface area) takes **more time** to solidify. A chill, being metallic and a strong heat sink, has a very **small modulus** → it solidifies (and freezes adjacent metal) very fast.

## 2.2 Design Rule for Chills (KTU Board Favourite)

For a chill to be effective, its modulus must be **much less** than the modulus of the casting section it is placed in:

$$M_{chill} \leq 0.5 \times M_{casting}$$

If this condition is satisfied, the chill surface solidifies first → **directional solidification is achieved** → shrinkage porosity migrates toward the riser.

## 2.3 Classification of Chills (Comprehensive – KTU 2024)

### (A) Based on Location
1. **External Chills:** Placed on the **outer surface** of the mould (touch the mould face). Examples: Chill plates, chill blocks. They chill the casting surface.
2. **Internal Chills:** Placed **inside the mould cavity**, embedded within the molten metal itself. They become part of the casting. Used when the casting's geometry makes external chills impractical.

### (B) Based on Material
1. **Metallic Chills** (most common): Iron, steel, copper, graphite. High heat capacity and conductivity.
2. **Sand Chills / Soft Chills:** Made of highly refractory sand, bonded with a thin layer of binder. They are slightly better than the surrounding mould but slower than metallic chills. Used when one wishes to chill a region but not too sharply.
3. **Insulating Chills:** Used at the **riser** end to slow solidification (a "reverse-chill" effect).

### (C) Based on Surface Coating
Chills are coated with **refractory wash (chalk, silica flour, zircon paint, soot)** to:
- Prevent fusion of the chill with the casting.
- Avoid water/moisture explosions.
- Control cooling intensity.

## 2.4 Classification of Chaplets (Comprehensive – KTU 2024)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cemented Chaplet** | Two separate pieces (head & leg) joined by a weak cement that melts on contact with liquid metal. | General purpose |
| **Crown / Single-piece Chaplet** | Pre-fabricated integral piece; placed between mould and core. | Mass production |
| **Stem Chaplet** | Has a long stem to support large cores. | Long cores |
| **Side-board Chaplet** | Supports cores where moulding is done on a flat board. | Simple castings |
| **Core Arbor Chaplet** | A wire arbor that supports the core. | Light cores |

> [!NOTE]
> **Chaplet Material Rule:** The melting point of the chaplet should be **slightly lower** than the molten metal. This ensures:
> 1. The chaplet surface **melts/fuses** with the casting (no void at interface).
> 2. Sufficient mechanical strength is retained until the metal solidifies around it.
> 3. For ferrous castings → use steel or cast-iron chaplets. For aluminium/bronze castings → use black-heart or tinned-iron chaplets.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = B \cdot (V/A)^{2}$ | Chvorinov's Rule | Solidification time |
| 2 | $M = V/A$ | Modulus | Cooling rate indicator |
| 3 | $M_{chill} \leq 0.5 \, M_{casting}$ | Chill design rule | Effective directional solidification |
| 4 | $R = t_{riser}/t_{casting} = (M_{riser}/M_{casting})^{2}$ | Riser ratio check | Ensures riser freezes last |
| 5 | $\text{Buoyant Force} = V_{core} \cdot \rho_{metal} \cdot g$ | Upward thrust on core | Chaplet load calculation |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{F_{buoyancy} - W_{core}}{\sigma_{chaplet} \cdot A_{chaplet}}$ | No. of chaplets | Number of chaplets needed |

> [!WARNING]
> In all KTU 2024 problems, **modulus** is the most tested parameter. Always compute $V/A$ separately for each section of a stepped casting and identify the section with the **largest modulus** — that section is the **hot spot** requiring chills or a riser.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chilled Cast Iron Rolls** (paper, steel rolling mills): A grey-iron casting made with chills gives a white-iron (Fe₃C-dominated) hardened surface and a grey-iron tough core — used for rolling-mill rolls.
- **Engine cylinder blocks:** Heavy bosses at the bearing saddles are prone to shrinkage porosity → placed with chills in the foundry.
- **Brackets and pulleys with thin arms and a thick hub:** Chill the hub region; riser placed at hub.
- **Turbine / pump impellers:** Chaplets hold the sand cores of the intricate blade passages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chvorinov's Rule (For KTU 14-Mark Questions)

**Starting assumption (Newton's law of cooling at the mould-metal interface):**
Heat lost from metal $\propto$ Surface area $\times$ Time $\times$ Temperature difference.

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0}) \cdot t$$

where $h$ is the heat transfer coefficient, $A$ the surface area, $T_{m}$ the melting point, $T_{0}$ the initial mould temperature.

**Energy to solidify the metal (latent + superheat):**

$$Q = V \cdot \rho \cdot [L + c_{p} \cdot (T_{pour} - T_{m})]$$

where $L$ is the latent heat of fusion, $\rho$ density, $c_p$ specific heat.

**Equating the two expressions for $Q$:**

$$V \cdot \rho \cdot [L + c_{p}(T_{pour} - T_{m})] = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0}) \cdot t$$

**Solving for $t$:**

$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot [L + c_{p}(T_{pour} - T_{m})]}{h \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)$$

**Note:** Strictly the equation gives $t \propto (V/A)$, not $(V/A)^2$. The empirical **square-power rule** $t = B \cdot (V/A)^{2}$ is observed because:
- During solidification, an insulating "air gap" forms between casting and mould.
- The effective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is **not constant** — it drops with time.
- Combining the linear solution with a time-decaying $h \sim 1/\sqrt{t}$ gives the celebrated $t = B(V/A)^{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final result (Chvorinov's Rule, 1940):**
> $$t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$
> This is the KTU 2024 board-examination form. Always quote $M = V/A$ (modulus) for the section.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condition for a Riser to Work (Riser Ratio)

For a riser to feed liquid metal, it must solidify **after** the casting:

$$t_{riser} > t_{casting}$$

Substituting Chvorinov's rule:

$$B \cdot \left(\frac{V_{r}}{A_{r}}\right)^{2} > B \cdot \left(\frac{V_{c}}{A_{c}}\right)^{2}$$

Since $B$ cancels:

$$\frac{V_{r}}{A_{r}} > \frac{V_{c}}{A_{c}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad M_{riser} > M_{casting}$$

**Practical design rule (KTU 2024):** $M_{riser} \geq 1.2 \times M_{casting}$.

**Riser Ratio (commonly asked in 3-mark questions):**

$$R = \frac{t_{riser}}{t_{casting}} = \left(\frac{M_{riser}}{M_{casting}}\right)^{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> A **riser ratio $R$** of **1.25 to 1.5** is considered ideal in industry.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem [Adapted from KTU Past Year Pattern]:**
A steel casting has a hub of diameter $12$ cm and length $8$ cm attached to a thin plate of size $20 \times 15 \times 2$ cm. Determine:
1. Modulus of the hub and the plate.
2. Identify the hot spot and recommend a chill modulus.

**Step 1 — Hub Modulus (treat as a cylinder):**

$$V_{hub} = \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^{2} \cdot 8 = 2,261.95 \;\text{cm}^{3}$$

$$A_{hub} = \pi \cdot 12 \cdot 8 + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^{2} = 301.59 + 226.19 = 527.78 \;\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$M_{hub} = \frac{V_{hub}}{A_{hub}} = \frac{2261.95}{527.78} \approx 4.29 \;\text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Plate Modulus (slab approximation; only large faces lose heat):**

$$V_{plate} = 20 \times 15 \times 2 = 600 \;\text{cm}^{3}$$

$$A_{plate} \approx 2 \times (20 \times 15) = 600 \;\text{cm}^{2}$$
(small edges neglected; this is the standard **"slab" assumption**)

$$M_{plate} = \frac{V_{plate}}{A_{plate}} = \frac{600}{600} = 1.00 \;\text{cm}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the hot spot:**

$$M_{hub} (4.29) \;\gg\; M_{plate} (1.00) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Hub is the hot spot.}$$

**Step 4 — Recommended chill modulus (design rule):**

$$M_{chill} \leq 0.5 \times M_{hub} = 0.5 \times 4.29 = 2.14 \;\text{cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A cast-iron cylindrical chill of diameter 6 cm and length 6 cm has modulus:
> $V = \pi/4 (6)^2 \cdot 6 = 169.65$ cm³, $A = \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 6 + 2 \cdot \pi/4(6)^2 = 113.10 + 56.55 = 169.65$ cm²,
> $M_{chill} = 169.65/169.65 = 1.00$ cm. This is $\ll 2.14$ cm → **chill is effective.** ✔

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Chills vs. Risers (KTU High-Yield)

| Problem Symptom | Solution Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Isolated thick section (hot spot) in a thin plate | **Chill at hot spot + Riser at heavy end** | Localised solidification control |
| Uniformly thick casting | **Riser only** | No thermal gradient possible with chill |
| Thin casting with thick hub | **External/internal chill at hub + Riser on hub** | Common pattern |
| Surface needs to be wear-resistant (chilled iron) | **Metallic chill on mould face** | Surface hardening by rapid cooling |
| Long, slender core tends to float | **Stem chaplets** | Supports against buoyancy |
| Heavy core (large buoyant force) | **Multiple crown chaplets or cemented chaplets** | Load distribution |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Chaplet Material Selection

| Casting Alloy | Recommended Chaplet Material | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Grey/White cast iron | Steel or cast iron chaplet | Must fuse with iron |
| Steel castings | Low-carbon steel chaplet | Similar melting range |
| Aluminium alloys | Tinned iron / black-heart malleable iron | Fuses at Al pour temperature |
| Copper alloys | Tin-coated iron or copper chaplet | Avoids Fe contamination |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Placement & Effect of Chills in a Mould

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pour Molten Metal] --> B[Mould Cavity]
    B --> C1[Thin Section: Solidifies First]
    B --> C2[Thick Section Hot Spot]
    B --> C3[Riser at Top]
    D[External Chill on Mould Wall] -.->|Rapid Heat Extraction| C2
    E[Internal Chill Embedded in Metal] -.->|Fuses with Casting| C2
    C1 --> F[Directional Solidification Wave]
    C2 --> F
    F --> G[Liquid Feed from Riser]
    G --> H[Complete Casting, No Shrinkage]
    C3 --> H
    I[Refractory Coating on Chill] --> D
    I --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The mould is poured → solidification front begins from the **chill** (fast) and the **mould walls** (medium).
- The front moves inward toward the **riser** (slowest), so the riser remains liquid and feeds shrinkage.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Chaplet Function in Supporting a Core

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MouldAssembly
    A[Drag Lower Mould] --> B[Core Placed on Drag]
    B --> C[Chaplet placed on Core]
    C --> D[Top Box Cope Closed]
    D --> E[Pour Molten Metal]
    end
    E --> F{Hot Metal Enters}
    F --> G[Core Tends to Float Buoyancy]
    G --> H[Chaplet Anchors Core]
    H --> I[Chaplet Fuses with Casting]
    I --> J[Sand Core Held in Position]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Chills (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Chills[Chills] --> L1[Based on Location]
    Chills --> L2[Based on Material]
    Chills --> L3[Based on Coating]
    L1 --> P1[External Chills]
    L1 --> P2[Internal Chills]
    L2 --> Q1[Metallic Chills - Iron, Steel, Cu, Graphite]
    L2 --> Q2[Sand Chills - Refractory Sand]
    L2 --> Q3[Insulating Chills - Riser End]
    L3 --> R1[Chalk Wash]
    L3 --> R2[Silica Flour Wash]
    L3 --> R3[Zircon Paint]
    L3 --> R4[Soot Carbon Coating]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Chaplets (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Chaplets[Chaplets] --> T1[Based on Function]
    Chaplets --> T2[Based on Material]
    T1 --> U1[Cemented Chaplet]
    T1 --> U2[Crown or Single-piece Chaplet]
    T1 --> U3[Stem Chaplet]
    T1 --> U4[Side-board Chaplet]
    T1 --> U5[Core Arbor Chaplet]
    T2 --> V1[Steel - for Iron and Steel Castings]
    T2 --> V2[Cast Iron - for Heavy Iron Castings]
    T2 --> V3[Black-heart Iron - for Aluminium]
    T2 --> V4[Tin-coated Iron - for Copper Alloys]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Process Flow of a Chilled Casting

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Hot Spots] --> S2[Step 2: Compute Modulus M = V/A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select Chill Material and Size]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Verify M_chill less than 0.5 M_cast]
    S4 --> S5{Verification Pass?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Resize Chill, Recompute]
    S6 --> S3
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Step 5: Apply Refractory Coating on Chill]
    S7 --> S8[Step 6: Place Chill in Mould]
    S8 --> S9[Step 7: Assemble Mould and Pour]
    S9 --> S10[Step 8: Allow Directional Solidification]
    S10 --> S11[Step 9: Shakeout and Inspect for Shrinkage]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define a chill. Mention any two functions of chills in foundry practice. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A chill is a metallic insert placed in the sand mould to increase the rate of heat extraction from a particular region of the molten metal so that the region solidifies faster.
**Functions:**
1. Promotes directional solidification, eliminating shrinkage cavities in thick sections.
2. Refines the grain structure at the chilled surface, improving surface hardness and wear resistance (e.g., chilled cast-iron rolls).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is a chaplet? Why is the chaplet material chosen to have a slightly lower melting point than the casting alloy? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A chaplet is a small metallic support placed inside the mould cavity to hold the sand core in position against buoyant and metallostatic forces.
The chaplet material is chosen with a melting point **slightly lower** than the casting alloy so that, on contact with the molten metal, the chaplet surface **fuses/welds** with the casting. This eliminates any void at the interface and makes the chaplet an integral part of the final casting, while still retaining sufficient strength to support the core until the metal solidifies around it.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the classification of chills with neat sketches. Discuss the role of chills in producing chilled cast iron rolls. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical steel casting of diameter $10$ cm and length $12$ cm is to be cast in a green-sand mould with a top riser. Compute the solidification time using Chvorinov's rule (take $B = 80$ s/cm²). If an internal chill of diameter $4$ cm and length $6$ cm is placed in the centre, comment on the effectiveness of the chill. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Classification of Chills (with sketches described in words):**

**1. External Chill (Surface Chill):** Placed on the **inner face of the mould wall** so that its surface is in direct contact with the molten metal.
- *Sketch description:* Rectangular chill plate on the pattern face / drag mould wall; chill face flush with mould cavity.

**2. Internal Chill:** Placed **inside the mould cavity** itself. It is completely surrounded by the molten metal and becomes part of the casting. Used where external chills are inaccessible.

**3. Sand Chill (Soft Chill):** Made of highly refractory sand with a binder; cools more slowly than metallic chills, used where mild chilling is required.

**4. Metallic Chills:** Iron, steel, copper, or graphite blocks. Copper chills give the highest conductivity (used for surface hardening of small regions).

**Chilled Cast Iron Rolls (Industrial Application):**
- Grey-iron melt is poured into a mould whose working surface is fitted with **metallic chills** (cast iron or steel).
- The chill extracts heat rapidly, suppressing carbon precipitation as graphite and producing **white iron (Fe₃C + pearlite)** at the surface.
- The interior cools slowly → **grey iron** (graphite flakes + ferrite/pearlite) forms, giving toughness.
- Result: a **hard, wear-resistant chilled surface (≈ 400–550 BHN)** and a **tough, machinable grey-iron core**.
- Used in rolling mills, paper mills, and crushing rolls.

**Diagrams expected:** A simple labelled diagram showing mould, chills on the periphery, and the gradient of structure from chilled surface (white iron) to core (grey iron).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Given:**
- Casting: cylinder, $D = 10$ cm, $L = 12$ cm
- $B = 80$ s/cm² (green sand)
- Internal chill: cylinder, $d = 4$ cm, $l = 6$ cm

**Step 1 — Volume of casting:**

$$V_{c} = \frac{\pi}{4}(10)^{2} \cdot 12 = \pi \cdot 300 \approx 942.48 \;\text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Surface area of casting (full cylinder, including both end faces):**

$$A_{c} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}D^{2} = \pi(10)(12) + \frac{\pi}{2}(10)^{2} = 376.99 + 157.08 = 534.07 \;\text{cm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Modulus of casting:**

$$M_{c} = \frac{V_{c}}{A_{c}} = \frac{942.48}{534.07} \approx 1.765 \;\text{cm}$$

**Step 4 — Solidification time of casting:**

$$t_{c} = B \cdot M_{c}^{2} = 80 \cdot (1.765)^{2} = 80 \cdot 3.115 \approx 249.2 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Modulus of internal chill:**

$$V_{ch} = \frac{\pi}{4}(4)^{2} \cdot 6 = 75.40 \;\text{cm}^{3}$$

$$A_{ch} = \pi \cdot 4 \cdot 6 + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(4)^{2} = 75.40 + 25.13 = 100.53 \;\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$M_{ch} = \frac{75.40}{100.53} \approx 0.750 \;\text{cm}$$

**Step 6 — Effectiveness check:**

$$\frac{M_{ch}}{M_{c}} = \frac{0.750}{1.765} = 0.425 < 0.50 \;\checkmark$$

**Comment:** Since the chill modulus is less than half the casting modulus, the chill is **effective**. It will solidify the surrounding metal rapidly and promote directional solidification toward the riser.

**Solidification time of chill (for reference):**

$$t_{ch} = 80 \cdot (0.750)^{2} = 80 \cdot 0.5625 = 45 \;\text{s}$$

The chill solidifies in only 45 s, while the casting takes 249 s, confirming that the chill region freezes first.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** What is a chaplet? Explain the classification of chaplets with their applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sand core of volume $800$ cm³ and weight $1.2$ kg is to be supported in an aluminium casting (density $2.7$ g/cm³). Calculate the buoyant force on the core. If a single steel chaplet of cross-section $0.5$ cm² is used, find the stress induced in the chaplet. (Take $g = 9.81$ m/s²). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

A chaplet is a small metallic support placed in the mould cavity to hold the sand core in position against buoyant and metallostatic forces. The chaplet fuses with the molten metal and becomes an integral part of the casting.

**Classification of Chaplets:**

| # | Type | Sketch Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cemented chaplet** | Two parts (head + leg) joined by a heat-softening cement | Most common; general purpose |
| 2 | **Crown (single-piece) chaplet** | A flat-headed pin with a tapered stem | Mass production of simple castings |
| 3 | **Stem chaplet** | Has a long stem rising from the base | Long horizontal cores |
| 4 | **Side-board chaplet** | Flat, attached to the mould board | Small castings, e.g. valve bodies |
| 5 | **Core arbor chaplet** | Wire arbor with hooked end | Light, slender cores |

**Application Selection:**
- **Cemented chaplets** are used when the joint must be tight but easily breakable by heat, allowing the head to align with the core.
- **Crown chaplets** are used for mass production of identical castings (e.g., pipe fittings).
- **Stem chaplets** are used for long cores in engine blocks and pump housings.
- **Side-board chaplets** are used for small cores on flat mould boards (e.g., in bench moulding).
- **Core arbor chaplets** are used for very light, thin cores in non-ferrous castings.

**Diagrams expected:** A simple figure showing each chaplet with its mounting position (on core, on mould, etc.).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Given:**
- Core volume $V_{core} = 800$ cm³ = $800 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ = $8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- Core weight $W_{core} = 1.2$ kg → $W_{core} \cdot g = 1.2 \times 9.81 = 11.77$ N (downward)
- Molten aluminium density $\rho_{Al} = 2.7$ g/cm³ = $2700$ kg/m³
- Chaplet cross-section $A_{chaplet} = 0.5$ cm² = $0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m² = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²

**Step 1 — Buoyant force on core:**

The displaced molten-metal volume is $V_{core}$.

$$F_{B} = V_{core} \cdot \rho_{Al} \cdot g$$

$$F_{B} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 2700 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{B} = 21.19 \;\text{N (upward)}$$

**Step 2 — Net upward force to be supported by chaplet:**

$$F_{net} = F_{B} - W_{core} = 21.19 - 11.77 = 9.42 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Stress in chaplet:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F_{net}}{A_{chaplet}} = \frac{9.42}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 188{,}400 \;\text{Pa} = 0.188 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Comment:** A stress of 0.188 MPa is **very low** compared to the yield strength of mild steel (≈ 250 MPa). One chaplet is more than sufficient for this case. In practice, multiple chaplets are distributed to keep each one's stress low and to avoid local crushing of the sand mould.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (10 Most Frequent Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting the end faces of a cylinder** in area calculation → modulus comes out wrong.
> 2. **Mixing up $M$ and $V/A^{2}$** in Chvorinov's rule. The formula is $t = B (V/A)^{2}$ — squaring applies to the **ratio**, not $V$ alone.
> 3. **External vs. internal chill confusion:** External chills do **not** become part of the casting (they remain in the mould); internal chills **fuse with** the casting.
> 4. **Chaplet material rule reversed:** Some students state "chaplet has *higher* melting point" — this is **wrong**; it should be *equal to or slightly lower*.
> 5. **Neglecting refractory coating** on chills — coating is **mandatory** to avoid moisture explosion and to prevent fusion at the chill-casting interface.
> 6. **Conflating modulus with thickness** — modulus is $V/A$, not the half-thickness, except for a **simple slab** (where they are equal).
> 7. **Ignoring unit consistency** in numerical problems — keep all dimensions in cm (or all in m).
> 8. **Not stating Chvorinov's rule explicitly** in derivation questions — even if the answer is correct, the rule must be named and quoted.
> 9. **Chaplets for cores vs. chills for hot spots** — these are two **different** problems. Marks are lost if the answer is jumbled.
> 10. **Not mentioning the cement in a cemented chaplet** — the cement is what allows the chaplet to align with the core before metal is poured.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision (10 Bullets):**
> 1. **Chill = heat extractor; Riser = heat reservoir (last to freeze).** They are complementary, not substitutes.
> 2. **Chvorinov's rule:** $t = B (V/A)^{2}$ — solidification time $\propto$ **modulus²**.
> 3. **Modulus $M = V/A$** — higher $M$ means slower solidification; **hot spot = section with the largest $M$**.
> 4. **Chill design rule:** $M_{chill} \leq 0.5 \, M_{casting}$.
> 5. **Riser design rule:** $M_{riser} \geq 1.2 \, M_{casting}$.
> 6. **Riser ratio $R = (M_{riser}/M_{casting})^{2}$** should be **1.25 to 1.5** for effective feeding.
> 7. **Chill classification:** External vs. Internal × Metallic vs. Sand vs. Insulating × Coated vs. Uncoated.
> 8. **Chaplet material = lower or equal melting point** than casting alloy → fuses with the casting, no void.
> 9. **Chilled cast iron rolls** = white iron surface (chilled) + grey iron core (slow-cooled).
> 10. **Refractory coating on chills** is essential to prevent chill-casting fusion and moisture explosion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Sand Moulds and Their Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Sand Moulds and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sand Mould?

A **sand mould** is a refractory container made of compacted sand grains bonded with clay or chemical binders, used to shape molten metal into a desired geometry during the **sand casting process**. In KTU 2024 terminology, a sand mould is the negative cavity (the inverse shape) into which molten metal is poured, allowed to solidify, and then removed as a finished or near-net-shape casting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> "A sand mould is a one-time-use disposable tooling made of a refractory sand mixture that provides the negative geometry for producing a casting. It must possess sufficient strength, permeability, refractoriness, and collapsibility to survive pouring, solidification, and shake-out."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Jelly in a Bowl" Intuition

Imagine you want to make a **shape out of jelly (jello)** but you do not have a mould. What do you do?

1. You take a **bowl**, press a small toy (or your hand) into it to make a cavity.
2. You pour warm liquid jelly into the cavity.
3. You let it cool and solidify.
4. You turn the bowl upside down — out pops a jelly shaped exactly like the toy.

A **sand mould is that bowl**. The sand particles act like tiny "building blocks" that hold the shape of the cavity. When molten metal is poured in (just like the warm jelly), it takes the shape of the cavity. After cooling, the sand mould is **broken apart** to release the solidified metal casting — this is called **shake-out**. The mould is destroyed after one use, just like the jelly bowl is not reusable if you tore it apart.

---

## 1.2 The Sand Mould — Cross-Sectional Anatomy

A typical sand mould consists of the following parts (must know for KTU diagrams):

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cope** | The **upper half** of the mould (the "lid"). |
| **Drag** | The **lower half** of the mould (the "base"). |
| **Parting Line** | The horizontal plane where cope and drag meet. |
| **Pattern** | The wooden/metal replica of the final casting (used to form the cavity). |
| **Core** | A separate sand insert placed inside the mould to create internal cavities/holes. |
| **Core Print** | A recess in the mould that holds and locates the core. |
| **Sprue** | The vertical channel through which molten metal enters. |
| **Runner** | The horizontal channel distributing metal from sprue to gate. |
| **Gate** | The final entry point into the mould cavity. |
| **Riser** | A reservoir of extra molten metal that feeds shrinkage during solidification. |
| **Vent** | A thin channel that allows gases and air to escape. |
| **Pouring Basin** | The top cup-shaped reservoir that receives the metal stream. |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Sand Moulds (KTU 2024 High-Priority Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the study of moulds classified by **two criteria**:

### A) Classification by **Type of Sand / Binder System**

#### 1. **Green Sand Mould**
- Made from **moist sand** mixed with clay (typically 4–10% moisture by weight).
- The word *"green"* refers to the **moist condition**, **NOT the colour**.
- Used for **small to medium castings** of ferrous and non-ferrous metals.
- Most widely used mould in industry (~70% of all castings).

#### 2. **Dry Sand Mould**
- The mould cavity is **baked / dried in an oven** at 200–320 °C **before pouring**.
- High strength, low moisture → superior surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- Used for **large castings** and high-melting-point alloys.

#### 3. **Skin-Dried Mould**
- A **hybrid**: only the **inner working surface** (~25 mm depth) is dried with a torch/oven.
- Combines the **strength of dry mould** with the **permeability of green mould**.

#### 4. **Loam Sand Mould**
- Uses a **thick layer of loam (a coarse sand + clay + fibre mixture)** applied over a **brick or steel skeleton**.
- Used for **very large castings** (e.g., heavy machinery beds, ship propellers).

#### 5. **Chemically Bonded (No-Bake) Mould**
- Sand is bonded with **chemical resins** (furan, phenolic, urethane) — no moisture, no baking.
- Cures at room temperature. Used for **high-precision, high-volume production**.

### B) Classification by **Method / Position of Moulding**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Floor Mould** | Made directly on the foundry floor; large castings. |
| **Pit Mould** | Made inside a pit dug in the floor; very large castings. |
| **Bench Mould** | Small moulds made on a wooden/metal bench; small castings. |
| **Machine Mould** | Made using a **moulding machine** (jolt, squeeze, jolt-squeeze). |
| **Stack Mould** | Multiple moulds stacked vertically to pour several at once. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** When asked *"List the types of sand moulds"*, always present the answer as a **two-axis classification** (Type of Sand + Method of Moulding) for full marks. Examiners award partial marks for one-axis answers.

---

## 1.4 Core Properties of Moulding Sand — Quick Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A good moulding sand must satisfy **eight key properties** simultaneously. Memorise these as a checklist — they appear in every KTU Part A question bank.

| # | Property | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Refractoriness** | Can survive high molten-metal temperatures without melting/fusing. |
| 2 | **Strength** | Can hold shape under the weight/pressure of molten metal. |
| 3 | **Permeability** | Allows gases/air to escape through the mould. |
| 4 | **Cohesiveness / Adhesiveness** | Sand grains stick to each other (cohesion) and to the pattern (adhesion). |
| 5 | **Plasticity / Deformability** | Can be moulded into shape without cracking. |
| 6 | **Flowability** | Can flow and pack around the pattern uniformly. |
| 7 | **Collapsibility** | Can collapse easily after solidification to allow free shrinkage. |
| 8 | **Reusability** | Sand can be reclaimed and reused after shake-out. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Properties Matter — The "Why" Behind Each Property

Think of moulding sand like the **concrete used in building a house**. Concrete has to be:
- **Strong** (hold the building up)
- **Porous enough** to let trapped air escape
- **Heat-resistant** if used near furnaces
- **Easy to break down** when you want to demolish the formwork

Moulding sand has **exactly these requirements**, plus a few more.

---

### 2.1.1 Refractoriness (Heat Resistance)

- **Definition:** Ability of the moulding sand to withstand **high temperatures of molten metal** without fusion, sintering, or chemical reaction.
- **Measured by:** **Sintering point** (°C) and **Fusion point** (°C).
- **Typical values:** Pure silica (SiO₂) = **1710 °C** fusion point.
- **Standard:** Mould sand should have a sintering point at least **200 °C higher** than the pouring temperature of the metal.

| Metal | Pouring Temp (°C) | Required Sand Sintering Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminium | 700 | > 900 |
| Brass/Bronze | 950 | > 1150 |
| Cast Iron | 1300 | > 1500 |
| Steel | 1500+ | > 1700 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high refractoriness?** If sand fuses to the casting surface, it creates a **sand-burn-on defect** — a rough, hard-to-machine surface. For steel castings, **chromite sand or zircon sand** (sintering point > 2000 °C) is used instead of silica sand.

---

### 2.1.2 Strength (Green Strength, Dry Strength, Hot Strength)

The strength of moulding sand is **NOT a single number**. It depends on the **condition** of the sand:

| Strength Type | Condition | Typical Value (N/cm²) | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Green Strength** | Moist (as-moulded) state | 5–10 | Holds shape during pouring of green sand moulds. |
| **Dry Strength** | After oven drying | 15–35 | Used for dry sand moulds. |
| **Hot Strength** | At elevated temperature (just after pouring) | 5–15 | Prevents **hot tearing** and mould collapse during solidification. |

**Measured by:** Universal sand strength testing machine — gives **compressive strength**, **shear strength**, and **tensile strength** values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** *"What happens if sand has too much strength?"*
> **Answer:** If strength is too high, the mould **will not collapse** after the casting solidifies. The contracting metal is then **restrained**, leading to **hot tears (cracks)** in the casting. Hence, **a balance** between strength and collapsibility is essential.

---

### 2.1.3 Permeability (Gas Permeability Number)

- **Definition:** Ability of the mould to allow **gases, steam, and air** to escape through the sand mass.
- **Why needed?** When molten metal contacts the mould:
  - Moisture in the sand → steam (rapid expansion ~1700×)
  - Air in the cavity → heated expansion
  - Combustion of binders → gases
- If gases cannot escape → they push back against the metal → **blow holes** and **gas porosity** defects.

**Permeability Number Formula:**

$$P = \frac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot P_{\text{pressure}}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Permeability Number** (dimensionless, AFS standard)
- $Q$ = Volume of air passing through (cm³)
- $H$ = Height of sand specimen (cm)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of specimen (cm²)
- $P_{\text{pressure}}$ = Air pressure applied (cm of water)

> The **AFS Permeability Number** is the standard used in foundry practice. Typical values:

| Sand Type | Permeability Number |
|---|---|
| Fine sand (facing) | 50–100 |
| Medium sand (general) | 100–200 |
| Coarse sand (backing) | 200–400+ |

---

### 2.1.4 Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness & Plasticity

These three properties together define how the sand behaves during **moulding**:

- **Cohesiveness:** Sand grains **stick to each other** (sand-to-sand bonding). Governed by **clay content** and **moisture**.
- **Adhesiveness:** Sand sticks to the **pattern surface** during withdrawal. Excessive adhesion → pattern lifts sand → casting defect.
- **Plasticity:** Sand can be **deformed** (rammed, compacted) without cracking. Without plasticity, the mould would **crumble** during pattern removal.

**Quantitative measure:** Plasticity = ability to retain shape after ramming; tested by **mould hardness tester** (gives a Brinell-like hardness number).

---

### 2.1.5 Flowability

- **Definition:** Ability of sand grains to **flow and pack uniformly** around the pattern, filling every contour.
- High flowability → **sharp, accurate casting** with fine details reproduced.
- **Measured by:** Percentage of sand passing through a standard orifice under vibration.
- **Trade-off:** High flowability ↔ Low strength (because flowability needs rounded grains, which pack loosely).

---

### 2.1.6 Collapsibility

- **Definition:** Ability of the mould to **deform / collapse** under the contraction of the casting during cooling.
- Without collapsibility → casting **cracks** due to restrained contraction.
- **Controlled by:** Reducing clay content, using **dead-burnt clay**, adding **wood flour / sawdust** as a "softener".

---

### 2.1.7 Moisture Content & Clay Content

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | **4–10%** (green sand) | Too high → steam blows; too low → low strength. |
| Clay (bentonite) | **5–12%** | Provides bonding and green strength. |
| Grain Fineness (AFS) | 50–140 | Lower AFS = finer sand. |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table for the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AFS Permeability Number | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}$ | dimensionless | Permeability problems |
| 2 | Green Compression Strength | $\sigma_g = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{specimen}}}$ | N/cm² or kPa | Strength tests |
| 3 | Moisture Content (%) | $\%M = \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | % | Sand preparation |
| 4 | Clay Content (%) | $\%C = \dfrac{W_{\text{clay}}}{W_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Bonding strength |
| 5 | Grain Fineness Number (GFN) | $\text{GFN} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{mass\%} \times \text{AFS sieve no.})}{100}$ | dimensionless | Sieve analysis |
| 6 | Mould Hardness Number | Direct reading from hardness tester | Brinell-like | Quality check |
| 7 | Dry Strength / Green Strength Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_d}{\sigma_g}$ | dimensionless | Mould classification |

> **Exam Hint:** Whenever a numerical problem gives you permeability parameters, **always** plug into the first formula and compute $P$ in AFS units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The properties discussed above directly govern the **casting quality index** in industry. The American Foundry Society (AFS) benchmarks for **defect-free castings** are based on these same parameters:

- A **Tier-1 automotive foundry** must hold permeability within ±5 AFS units of the target for the job.
- The **aerospace investment casting sector** uses chemically bonded moulds with permeability controlled to ±2 AFS units.
- **Foundry automation lines** in India (Bajaj, Tata, Mahindra Foundries) use real-time moisture and compactability sensors — both derived from these properties.

> Understanding these properties is the **gateway** to designing moulds for Industry 4.0 / smart foundries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Permeability Number — Full Derivation

### Statement
The **AFS Permeability Number** $P$ of a sand specimen is defined as the rate of airflow through a standardised sand sample under a standard pressure.

### Setup
Consider a cylindrical sand specimen:
- Diameter $D$ = **5.08 cm** (standard AFS specimen = 2 inches)
- Cross-sectional area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Height of specimen $H$ = **5.08 cm** (standard)
- Air pressure $\Delta P$ = **10 cm of water column**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define flow rate.**

Let $Q$ = volume of air (in cm³) passing through the sand in 1 minute.

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law.**

For laminar flow through a porous medium:

$$Q = k \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot H}$$

where:
- $k$ = permeability of the sand (depends on grain size, shape, packing)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of air
- $H$ = sand thickness

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $k$.**

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 4 — Apply standard AFS test conditions.**

Under standard conditions (room temp, $\mu_{\text{air}}$ fixed, $\Delta P = 10$ cm water, $H = 5.08$ cm), the constants collapse into a single dimensionless number:

$$\boxed{\;P_{\text{AFS}} = \frac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}\;}$$

This is the **AFS Permeability Number**. It is dimensionless because:

$$\frac{[\text{cm}^3] \cdot [\text{cm}]}{[\text{cm}^2] \cdot [\text{cm of water}]} = \text{dimensionless}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

> **[KTU 2024 Exam Style]** A sand specimen of diameter $D = 5.08$ cm and height $H = 5.08$ cm is tested for permeability. In 1 minute, $Q = 2000$ cm³ of air passes through at $\Delta P = 10$ cm of water. Calculate the AFS Permeability Number.

**Solution:**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot (5.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 25.8064}{4} = 20.268 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$P = \frac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P} = \frac{2000 \times 5.08}{20.268 \times 10}$$

$$P = \frac{10160}{202.68}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 50.13 \text{ AFS}}$$

A permeability number of ~50 indicates a **fine sand** (suitable for facing layers).

---

## 3.2 Moisture Content — Full Derivation

### Setup
A sample of moulding sand is weighed:
- Wet weight = $W_w$
- The sample is oven-dried at 110 °C for 1 hour until constant weight.
- Dry weight = $W_d$

### Derivation

$$\text{Water evaporated} = W_w - W_d$$

By definition:

$$\% \text{Moisture} = \frac{\text{Water}}{\text{Dry sand}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\;\%\text{Moisture} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100\;}$$

**Worked Example:**

Wet sand weight = 120 g
Dry sand weight = 110 g

$$\%M = \frac{120 - 110}{110} \times 100 = \frac{10}{110} \times 100 = 9.09\%$$

This is within the **green-sand range (4–10%)** — moulding-ready.

---

## 3.3 Grain Fineness Number (GFN) — Sieve Analysis

The GFN characterises the **average grain size** of moulding sand.

### Setup
A sand sample is shaken through a stack of standard AFS sieves. The mass retained on each sieve is recorded.

### Formula

$$\boxed{\;\text{GFN} = \frac{\sum_i (m_i \cdot S_i)}{\sum_i m_i}\;}$$

where:
- $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$ (grams)
- $S_i$ = AFS sieve number (= sieve mesh × factor)

### Worked Example

| Sieve No. | Mass Retained (g) | Product $m_i \times S_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 0 | 0 |
| 12 | 5 | 60 |
| 20 | 20 | 400 |
| 30 | 30 | 900 |
| 40 | 25 | 1000 |
| 50 | 12 | 600 |
| 70 | 5 | 350 |
| 100 | 2 | 200 |
| PAN | 1 | 150 (approx) |
| **Total** | **100** | **3660** |

$$\text{GFN} = \frac{3660}{100} = 36.6 \approx 37$$

**Interpretation:** GFN = 37 → this is a **medium-coarse sand** (general-purpose backing sand).

| GFN Range | Classification | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| < 50 | Fine sand | Facing layer |
| 50–140 | Medium sand | General moulding |
| > 140 | Coarse sand | Backing / large moulds |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Property Matrix — All Five Mould Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this matrix for KTU 14-mark comparison questions.** Examiners love asking *"Compare green sand and dry sand moulds"*.

| Property | Green Sand | Dry Sand | Skin-Dried | Loam Sand | Chemically Bonded |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture (%)** | 4–10 | < 1 | 0–4 (surface only) | 5–10 | 0 (no water) |
| **Bonding** | Clay + water | Clay (oven-dried) | Clay (partial dry) | Clay + fibre | Resin (no moisture) |
| **Strength** | Moderate | High | High (surface) | Moderate | Very High |
| **Permeability** | High | Moderate | High (interior) | Moderate | Variable |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Rough | Excellent |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | ±1.5 mm | ±0.5 mm | ±0.8 mm | Low | ±0.3 mm |
| **Production Rate** | Very High | Slow (baking) | Slow | Slow | High |
| **Cost** | Low | High (fuel) | Moderate | Low | High (resin) |
| **Typical Casting Size** | Small–Medium | Large | Medium | Very Large | Small–Medium |
| **Typical Application** | General castings | Steel, large iron | Heavy ferrous | Propellers, beds | Mass production |

---

## 3.5 Selection Logic — Which Mould for Which Job?

### Decision Flowchart (textual for now, visual in Section 4)

1. **Casting weight > 1000 kg?** → Use **Loam Sand Mould** (or Pit Mould).
2. **Surface finish critical (e.g., gear blank)?** → Use **Dry Sand Mould** (oven-baked).
3. **High-volume production line?** → Use **Chemically Bonded (No-Bake)** mould.
4. **General-purpose small-medium ferrous casting?** → Use **Green Sand Mould** (default choice).
5. **Need balance between speed and finish?** → Use **Skin-Dried Mould**.

> This decision logic is a **standard interview question** for foundry engineer roles at Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland, etc.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of Sand Moulds

> *Mermaid-validated block diagram showing the complete taxonomy of sand moulds. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the V10 mermaid safeguards.*

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootA["Sand Moulds"]
    RootA --> ClassB["Classification Axis"]
    ClassB --> AxisC1["Axis 1 By Sand or Binder Type"]
    ClassB --> AxisC2["Axis 2 By Moulding Method"]

    AxisC1 --> NodeG["Green Sand Mould"]
    AxisC1 --> NodeD["Dry Sand Mould"]
    AxisC1 --> NodeS["Skin Dried Mould"]
    AxisC1 --> NodeL["Loam Sand Mould"]
    AxisC1 --> NodeCB["Chemically Bonded No Bake Mould"]

    NodeG --> PropG1["Moisture 4 to 10 percent"]
    NodeG --> PropG2["Clay bonded"]
    NodeG --> PropG3["Small to medium castings"]
    NodeG --> PropG4["Use General Foundries"]

    NodeD --> PropD1["Oven baked 200 to 320 C"]
    NodeD --> PropD2["Low moisture"]
    NodeD --> PropD3["Large steel castings"]
    NodeD --> PropD4["Use Heavy Engineering"]

    NodeS --> PropS1["Surface dried only"]
    NodeS --> PropS2["Hybrid mould"]
    NodeS --> PropS3["Better finish than green"]
    NodeS --> PropS4["Use Medium Castings"]

    NodeL --> PropL1["Thick loam layer"]
    NodeL --> PropL2["Brick or steel skeleton"]
    NodeL --> PropL3["Very large castings"]
    NodeL --> PropL4["Use Heavy Machinery Beds"]

    NodeCB --> PropCB1["Resin bonded"]
    PropCB1 --> ResinSub1["Furan Resin"]
    PropCB1 --> ResinSub2["Phenolic Resin"]
    PropCB1 --> ResinSub3["Urethane Resin"]
    NodeCB --> PropCB2["No moisture needed"]
    NodeCB --> PropCB3["Mass production"]
    NodeCB --> PropCB4["Use Automotive Sector"]

    AxisC2 --> MethodF["Floor Mould"]
    AxisC2 --> MethodP["Pit Mould"]
    AxisC2 --> MethodBn["Bench Mould"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A **defect-free casting** is achieved at the **optimum balance** of all five core properties. Pushing any one property to its extreme (e.g., very high strength) will harm another (e.g., collapsibility). This is the **central dogma** of foundry engineering.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Direct, Conceptual)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*"What is meant by 'green sand' in foundry practice? Is the sand actually green in colour?"* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Green sand is a **mixture of silica sand, bentonite clay (5–10%), and moisture (4–10%)** in the as-moulded (un-dried) condition. The term *"green"* refers to the **moist, undried state** of the sand — **NOT the colour**. In fact, green sand is typically **grey, brown, or black** depending on the clay used. It is the most widely used moulding sand because it is cheap, recyclable, and allows high production rates. **Keywords: moist, undried, clay-bonded.**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*"List any four desirable properties of moulding sand and state why each is needed."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Why Needed |
|---|---|
| **Refractoriness** | Prevents sand from fusing to casting under high-temperature molten metal. |
| **Permeability** | Allows steam and gases to escape, preventing blowholes. |
| **Strength** | Holds mould shape against the hydrostatic pressure of molten metal. |
| **Collapsibility** | Allows the mould to deform with the casting during cooling — prevents hot tears. |

*(Any four properties + reasons = full 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Theory

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Classify sand moulds by (i) type of sand and (ii) method of moulding. Explain any **three types** in detail with neat sketches. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *Discuss in detail the **properties of moulding sand** — refractoriness, strength, permeability, cohesiveness, collapsibility — with their typical values and measurement methods.* **[7 Marks]***

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Sand Moulds

**Two-axis classification:**

**Axis 1 — By Type of Sand:**
1. **Green Sand Mould** — moist sand (4–10% water), clay-bonded, used immediately after moulding.
2. **Dry Sand Mould** — oven-dried at 200–320 °C, low moisture, used for steel and large castings.
3. **Skin-Dried Mould** — inner surface dried only, retains permeability of green mould.
4. **Loam Sand Mould** — thick loam layer over brick/steel framework, for very large castings.
5. **Chemically Bonded (No-Bake) Mould** — resin-bonded, no moisture, used in mass production.

**Axis 2 — By Method of Moulding:**
1. **Bench Mould** — small moulds on a workbench.
2. **Floor Mould** — large moulds on foundry floor.
3. **Pit Mould** — mould inside a pit (very large castings).
4. **Machine Mould** — made using jolt / squeeze / jolt-squeeze machines.

**Detailed explanation of three types (typical choice — Green, Dry, Chemically Bonded):**

**1. Green Sand Mould:**
- Composition: Silica sand (85–95%) + Bentonite clay (5–10%) + Water (4–10%).
- Properties: Moderate strength, high permeability, moderate refractoriness.
- Applications: 70% of all castings — small to medium ferrous and non-ferrous.
- Advantages: Inexpensive, reusable, fast moulding.
- Disadvantages: Surface finish not as good as dry sand; moisture can cause defects.

**2. Dry Sand Mould:**
- Composition: Same as green sand but **oven-dried** after moulding.
- Properties: High dry strength (15–35 N/cm²), excellent surface finish, low moisture.
- Applications: Large castings, steel castings, precision castings.
- Advantages: Superior dimensional accuracy, fewer gas defects.
- Disadvantages: Energy cost for baking; slower production.

**3. Chemically Bonded (No-Bake) Mould:**
- Composition: Sand + synthetic resin (furan / phenolic / urethane) + catalyst.
- Properties: Very high strength, no moisture needed, excellent dimensional accuracy.
- Applications: Automotive, aerospace, mass production of small-medium castings.
- Advantages: Fast curing at room temperature, very low scrap rate.
- Disadvantages: Resin cost; some resins emit fumes (regulated by EPA).

#### Part (b) — Properties of Moulding Sand

**1. Refractoriness**
- Ability to withstand high temperatures of molten metal without fusing.
- Measured by **sintering point** and **fusion point**.
- For steel castings, sand should have fusion point **> 1750 °C**.
- Silica sand (SiO₂) fusion point = **1710 °C** — adequate for cast iron.
- For higher temperatures: **chromite sand, zircon sand, magnesite sand** are used.

**2. Strength**
- Resistance of mould sand to **mechanical loading** (ramming, metal pressure, lifting).
- Three types: **Green strength** (moist), **Dry strength** (oven-baked), **Hot strength** (elevated temp).
- Measured using a **Universal Sand Strength Testing Machine** (compressive / shear / tensile).
- Typical values: Green compression 5–10 N/cm²; Dry compression 15–35 N/cm².
- Trade-off: Too high strength → poor collapsibility → hot tears.

**3. Permeability**
- Ability to allow gases, steam, and air to escape.
- Measured as **AFS Permeability Number** $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}$.
- Typical values: Facing sand 50–100; Backing sand 200–400.
- Controlled by **grain size, distribution, and clay content**.
- Low permeability → **blow holes, gas porosity**.

**4. Cohesiveness & Adhesiveness**
- Cohesiveness: Sand grains stick to each other (sand-to-sand).
- Adhesiveness: Sand sticks to the pattern surface.
- Both depend on **clay content and moisture**.
- Excessive adhesion → pattern lifts sand during withdrawal → casting defects.

**5. Collapsibility**
- Ability of mould to **deform after solidification** to allow free shrinkage of the casting.
- Without it → **hot tears, shrinkage cracks**.
- Improved by: reducing clay content, adding wood flour / sawdust / coal dust.
- Tested qualitatively by tapping a dried mould and observing crack propagation.

**6. Flowability, Plasticity, Moisture (supplementary):**
- Flowability = ability to pack uniformly around pattern.
- Plasticity = deformability without cracking.
- Moisture = 4–10% for green sand.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Model]**

**(a)** *Explain the **AFS Permeability Test** in detail with the derivation of the Permeability Number formula. A sand specimen of diameter 5.08 cm and height 5.08 cm allows 2500 cm³ of air to pass in 1 minute at a pressure of 10 cm of water. Calculate the permeability number.* **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *Compare **Green Sand Mould** and **Dry Sand Mould** in a tabular form covering composition, strength, permeability, surface finish, applications, advantages and disadvantages.* **[7 Marks]***

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — AFS Permeability Test

**Definition:** Permeability is the ability of moulding sand to allow gases and air to escape through its pores.

**Apparatus:**
- AFS Standard Permeability Tester
- Cylindrical sand specimen: diameter $D = 5.08$ cm, height $H = 5.08$ cm
- Water manometer for pressure measurement
- Rotameter / soap bubble meter for airflow rate

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare a standard cylindrical sand specimen by ramming.
2. Place it in the permeability tester.
3. Apply a standard air pressure $\Delta P = 10$ cm of water column.
4. Measure the volume of air $Q$ (in cm³) passing through in 1 minute.

**Derivation:**

Applying **Darcy's Law** for laminar flow through a porous medium:

$$Q = k \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot H}$$

Rearranging:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

Under the standard AFS test conditions, the constants $\mu$ and the test dimensions are absorbed into a single dimensionless number:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $D = 5.08$ cm
- $H = 5.08$ cm
- $Q = 2500$ cm³/min
- $\Delta P = 10$ cm of water

Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (5.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25.8064}{4} = 20.268 \text{ cm}^2$$

Step 2 — Plug into formula:

$$P = \frac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P} = \frac{2500 \times 5.08}{20.268 \times 10}$$

$$P = \frac{12700}{202.68}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 62.66 \text{ AFS}}$$

**Interpretation:** Permeability ≈ 63 AFS → **fine-to-medium sand**, suitable for **facing layers** (which require moderate-to-high permeability to vent gases close to the metal surface).

---

#### Part (b) — Green Sand vs Dry Sand Mould (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Green Sand Mould | Dry Sand Mould |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Silica sand + Bentonite clay + **4–10% moisture** | Silica sand + Bentonite clay + **< 1% moisture** (dried) |
| **Moisture Content** | High (4–10%) | Very Low (< 1%) |
| **Bonding State** | Clay + water (activated by moisture) | Clay only (water evaporated by baking) |
| **Strength Type** | Green strength (5–10 N/cm²) | Dry strength (15–35 N/cm²) |
| **Permeability** | High (good gas venting) | Lower (because clay is more concentrated) |
| **Refractoriness** | Moderate | Higher (less moisture to vaporise) |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (Ra 6.3–12.5 μm) | Excellent (Ra 1.6–6.3 μm) |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | ±1.5 mm | ±0.5 mm |
| **Production Speed** | Fast (no baking) | Slow (2–6 hours of oven drying) |
| **Cost** | Low | Higher (fuel cost for oven) |
| **Common Defects** | Blow holes, scabs, rattails | Hot tears, sand burn-on |
| **Casting Size** | Small to medium (< 200 kg) | Large (> 200 kg), steel castings |
| **Typical Applications** | Engine blocks, pump housings, pipe fittings | Turbine housings, heavy machine bases, steel castings |
| **Reusability** | Excellent | Good but requires re-conditioning |
| **Energy Consumption** | Very Low | High (oven heating) |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic (PCPET402 Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "green" with colour:** Many students write *"green sand is green in colour"* — this is **wrong**. "Green" = moist. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 2. **Wrong formula for permeability:** Some students use $P = Q / (A \cdot \Delta P)$ forgetting the height $H$ term. The **full AFS formula includes $H$**. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
>
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write "AFS" or "dimensionless" after the permeability number. Without units, 0.5 marks deducted.
>
> 4. **Listing properties without explaining WHY:** KTU 2024 follows **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education** — examiners expect **"Why is this property needed?"** in your answer. A bare list of properties → only 50% marks.
>
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark questions:** Always include a **labelled cross-section** of a sand mould (cope, drag, sprue, runner, gate, riser, core, vent). Without a diagram, you lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Permeability numerical without showing $A$ calculation:** Always show $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ explicitly. Marks are awarded step-wise.
>
> 7. **Confusing green strength with dry strength:** In the comparison table, students often interchange values. Green strength = moist state (lower); Dry strength = baked state (higher).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

- [x] A **sand mould** is a one-time-use disposable container for shaping molten metal.
- [x] Two classification axes: **(1) Sand type** and **(2) Moulding method**.
- [x] **Green sand** = moist (NOT green colour). 4–10% moisture, clay-bonded.
- [x] **Dry sand** = oven-baked at 200–320 °C. Higher strength, better finish.
- [x] **Skin-dried** = only the inner surface is dried; hybrid mould.
- [x] **Loam sand** = thick loam over brick/steel framework; for very large castings.
- [x] **Chemically bonded (no-bake)** = resin-bonded; mass production; aerospace/auto.
- [x] **Eight core properties:** refractoriness, strength (green/dry/hot), permeability, cohesiveness, plasticity, flowability, collapsibility, reusability.
- [x] **AFS Permeability formula:** $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P}$ — dimensionless.
- [x] **Moisture formula:** $\%M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$.
- [x] **GFN formula:** $\text{GFN} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot S_i)}{\sum m_i}$.
- [x] **Mould parts to memorise:** cope, drag, sprue, runner, gate, riser, vent, core, core print, parting line, pouring basin.
- [x] **Refractoriness rule:** sand fusion point should be **200 °C > pouring temperature**.
- [x] **Strength–Collapsibility trade-off:** too strong → hot tears; too weak → mould collapse.
- [x] **Standard specimen:** diameter 5.08 cm, height 5.08 cm — used in AFS tests.
- [x] **Always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers (cross-section of mould).
- [x] **Always include units** in numerical answers (AFS, N/cm², %, °C).
- [x] **Casting defect linkage:** low permeability → blow holes; low collapsibility → hot tears; low refractoriness → sand burn-on.
