topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Moulding Sands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Moulding Sand?

**Moulding sand** is a refractory granular material used to form the cavity (the mould) into which molten metal is poured during the **sand casting** process. Once the metal solidifies, the sand mould is broken apart to retrieve the casting. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET402) syllabus, moulding sand is the *backbone material* of the foundry industry, and its properties directly govern the **quality, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and defect-free nature** of the final casting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Moulding sand is a mixture of **silica sand (SiO₂)**, **binder (clay)**, **moisture (water)**, and **additives** that, when compacted around a pattern, retains its shape, permits the escape of gases, and withstands the intense thermal shock of molten metal.

The principal raw materials in a typical moulding sand composition are:
- **Silica (SiO₂)**: 80 – 90 %
- **Clay (bentonite / kaolinite)**: 6 – 12 %
- **Water**: 2 – 6 %
- **Additives (coal dust, dextrin, sea coal, etc.)**: 0 – 4 %

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A student is expected to identify, define, and explain **at least 8 mechanical, physical, and thermal properties** of moulding sand and relate each property to its effect on the casting quality.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Baking Cake" Model

Imagine baking a cake. You need a *flour base* (silica grains), a *binder* like eggs or milk (clay + water), and a *leavening agent* to let air escape (permeability). If the mixture is too dry, the cake crumbles (low strength). If it is too wet, the cake sags (low refractoriness). The texture of the flour affects the smoothness of the surface (grain fineness). The same delicate balance governs moulding sand — every ingredient and property is tuned to "hold the shape" while the molten metal flows in and cooks the cavity.

- **Silica sand** = Flour (bulk, refractory skeleton)
- **Clay + water** = Binder (gives cohesion)
- **Permeability** = Letting the steam out
- **Refractoriness** = The oven's heat resistance
- **Green strength** = Cake's firmness when freshly taken out (unfired)

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

- **Standard AFS Sieve Shaking Time:** 15 minutes (per **AFS Foundry Sand Test**)
- **Standard AFS Specimen Height:** 50.8 mm (**2 inches**)
- **Permeability Test Gas:** Dry air
- **Refractoriness Test Temperature:** ≥ 1500 °C for steel-grade sands
- **Moisture Content (Green sand):** 2 – 6 % (optimum ~ 4 %)
- **Clay Content (Bentonite):** 6 – 10 % for ferrous castings

## 1.4 Classification of Moulding Sands (Quick Map)

| Sand Type | Binder Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Green sand** | Bentonite + water | Ferrous & non-ferrous castings (most common) |
| **Dry sand** | Clay + water (dried in oven) | Large castings, higher strength |
| **Loam sand** | Clay + water + straw/manure | Large bells, massive castings |
| **Facing sand** | Special additives | Directly contacts molten metal |
| **Backing sand** | Reclaimed, low-grade | Backing layer, economy |
| **Core sand** | Oil/ resin binder | Internal cavities in casting |
| **Parting sand** | None (dry silica) | Prevents sticking of cope & drag |
| **Chill sand** | Resin (furan) | Moulds chills for directional solidification |
| **System sand** | Mechanized moulding lines | High-volume foundries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Classify moulding sands," always present them in a **flowchart** (see Section 4) and mention the **binder** as the discriminating parameter.

## 1.5 Why Study Properties of Moulding Sand?

Every defect in a sand casting — **scab, rat-tail, blowhole, gas porosity, sand burn-on, veining, mold crack, run-out** — traces back to a single root cause: **improperly controlled sand properties**. KTU examiners frequently set questions linking a *defect* to a *property failure*, so understanding properties is the foundation of foundry quality control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Critical Properties of Moulding Sand

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups moulding sand properties into **three families**: *Mechanical*, *Physical*, and *Thermal*. Below is the deep theoretical breakdown.

### 2.1.1 MECHANICAL PROPERTIES

#### A. **Strength (Green Strength, Dry Strength, Hot Strength)**

- **Definition:** The ability of the sand to retain its shape under the action of external forces (mechanical ramming, metallostatic pressure of molten metal, and erosion during pouring).
- **Green Strength** → strength of the mould in the *moist* (unfired) state.
- **Dry Strength** → strength after the mould is *baked* (dried in an oven at 200–300 °C).
- **Hot Strength** → strength at *elevated temperatures* (above 600 °C).
- **Why it matters:** Insufficient strength → mould wall collapse → casting defect called **"run-out"** or **"mould erosion."**
- **Typical values:**
  - Green compression strength: **30 – 80 kPa (0.3 – 0.8 kg/cm²)**
  - Dry compression strength: **200 – 1000 kPa**
  - Hot strength (at 1000 °C): **150 – 400 kPa**

#### B. **Hardness**

- **Definition:** Resistance of the mould surface to indentation under a load.
- **Test instrument:** **Brinell Hardness Tester for sand** (spring-loaded indenter).
- **Significance:** A uniform, hard mould surface gives a smooth casting surface and resists the erosive flow of liquid metal.
- **Typical range:** 70 – 100 (Brinell sand hardness number).

#### C. **Flowability / Flowability Index**

- **Definition:** The ability of the sand to pack uniformly around the pattern and into intricate cavities under ramming.
- **Why it matters:** Low flowability → sharp corners are loosely packed → casting **mismatches or rounded edges**.

#### D. **Toughness (a.k.a. Friability)**

- **Definition:** The combined property of strength + ability to deform without crumbling.
- **Test:** Compacted sand specimen is broken and the failure mode is observed. Brittle fracture = poor toughness.

---

### 2.1.2 PHYSICAL PROPERTIES

#### A. **Permeability (P)** — *The Most Critical Property*

- **Definition:** The capacity of the sand mould to allow gases (air, steam, mould gases) to escape through its porous structure.
- **Why it matters:** If gases cannot escape, they are trapped in the solidifying metal → **blowholes** and **gas porosity** (a fatal KTU-board favourite defect).
- **Governing equation:**

$$P = \frac{V \cdot H}{A \cdot T \cdot P_m}$$

  Where:
  - $P$ = **Permeability number** (dimensionless)
  - $V$ = Volume of air passing through (cm³)
  - $H$ = Height of sand specimen (**50.8 mm / 2 inches** standard)
  - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of specimen (cm²)
  - $T$ = Time of air flow (min)
  - $P_m$ = Manometer pressure (cm of water)

- **Typical values:**
  - Green sand moulds: **80 – 150**
  - Dry sand moulds: **30 – 80** (lower because clay is dehydrated and grains are tighter)

#### B. **Grain Shape**

| Shape | Casting Surface | Permeability | Refractoriness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rounded** | Smooth | Very High | Lower |
| **Sub-angular** | Good | Medium | Medium |
| **Angular** | Rough | Low | High |
| **Compound** | Variable | Variable | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Rounded grains → high permeability but **low refractoriness** (they expose more surface area for sintering). Angular grains → opposite.

#### C. **Grain Size & Grain Fineness Number (GFN)**

- **Definition:** GFN is the weighted average of the sieve sizes retained on a standard **AFS sieve series**, calculated as:

$$GFN = \frac{\sum (n_i \times d_i)}{\sum n_i}$$

  Where $n_i$ = % of sand retained on sieve *i*, $d_i$ = sieve factor (the ratio of the next coarser sieve size to the current, multiplied by the AFS factor).

- **Typical ranges:**
  - Fine sand: GFN **150 – 200** → smooth surface castings
  - Medium sand: GFN **80 – 120** → general purpose
  - Coarse sand: GFN **40 – 70** → heavy castings, high permeability needed

#### D. **Moisture Content (W)**

- **Definition:** The percentage of water present in the green sand.
- **Optimum:** **4 – 6 %** (above this, mould wall collapses; below, no bond develops).
- **Test:** Drying a known weight of sand at 110 °C for 1 hour and noting weight loss.
- **Effect on properties:**

$$W = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

  where $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight.

#### E. **Collapsibility**

- **Definition:** The ability of the sand mould to **degrade and collapse** once the casting has solidified, so that the casting can shrink freely without cracking.
- **Why it matters:** Poor collapsibility → hot tearing / shrinkage cracks.
- **Enhanced by:** Adding **organic additives** (wood flour, cellulose) that burn out at high temperature.

#### F. **Adhesiveness**

- **Definition:** The property that allows the sand grains to stick to the pattern during its withdrawal.
- **Too high adhesiveness** → pattern is damaged, edges tear off.

#### G. **Cohesiveness (Cohesion)**

- The inter-granular bonding strength between sand particles themselves (vs. *adhesion* which is with the pattern). High cohesion → mould doesn't crack during pattern withdrawal.

---

### 2.1.3 THERMAL PROPERTIES

#### A. **Refractoriness**

- **Definition:** The ability of the sand to withstand **high temperatures** (up to the pouring temperature of the metal) **without fusing, softening, or sintering**.
- **Why it matters:** If the sand fuses with the molten metal, the casting will have **sand burn-on, penetration, or veining defects**.
- **Refractoriness depends on:**
  - **Purity of SiO₂** (higher is better — minimum 95 % for steel castings)
  - **Grain size** (coarse is better)
  - **Mineral content** (low fluxing impurities like Na, K, FeO, CaO are critical)
- **Sintering temperature of pure SiO₂:** **1700 °C** minimum
- **Pyrometric Cone Equivalent (PCE) test** is used to grade refractoriness.
- **Application-wise minimum refractoriness:**
  - Cast iron: 1300 °C
  - Steel: 1500 – 1600 °C
  - Non-ferrous: 1000 – 1200 °C

#### B. **Thermal Conductivity & Thermal Expansion**

- Sand moulds are **poor conductors of heat** → this promotes *directional solidification* (good for casting soundness).
- Excessive thermal expansion of silica grains at 573 °C (the α-β quartz inversion) causes the mould to expand abruptly → **"scab" defect**.

---

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA / CHEAT SHEET

| # | Property | Formula / Definition | Typical Range | Common Defect if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Permeability (P)** | $P = (V \cdot H) / (A \cdot T \cdot P_m)$ | 80 – 150 (green) | Blowholes |
| 2 | **Moisture Content (W)** | $W = [(W_w - W_d) / W_d] \times 100$ | 4 – 6 % | Low green strength |
| 3 | **Grain Fineness (GFN)** | $GFN = \sum(n_i d_i) / \sum n_i$ | 80 – 120 | Surface roughness |
| 4 | **Green Compression Strength** | $S_{gc} = F / A$ (force ÷ area) | 30 – 80 kPa | Mould wall collapse |
| 5 | **Dry Compression Strength** | $S_{dc} = F / A$ | 200 – 1000 kPa | Edge failure |
| 6 | **Hot Strength (1000 °C)** | $S_h$ | 150 – 400 kPa | Burn-in |
| 7 | **Refractoriness** | PCE cone test | ≥ 1500 °C | Sand burn-on |
| 8 | **Collapsibility** | Empirical (after shakeout) | — | Hot tearing |
| 9 | **Flowability** | % weight filling a standard cavity | 70 – 90 % | Edge rounding |
| 10 | **Clay Content (CC)** | $CC = [(W_{cl} - W_{0}) / W_{0}] \times 100$ | 6 – 12 % | Low strength |
| 11 | **Hardness Number** | Brinell sand indenter test | 70 – 100 | Surface erosion |
| 12 | **Loam Ratio** | (Sand:Clay:Water) ≈ 100:15:6 | — | Improper coating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** In numericals, always show **unit conversion** (e.g., cm of water to Pa) and label every variable. Marks are deducted for missing units in 14-mark problems.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Use Case | Critical Sand Property |
|---|---|
| **Automotive cylinder blocks (sand cast iron)** | High permeability, good collapsibility |
| **Turbine blades (investment casting precursor)** | Very fine grain, high refractoriness |
| **Pump housings (steel castings)** | High dry strength, high refractoriness |
| **Sanitary fittings (brass castings)** | Fine grain, high flowability |
| **Heavy machine bases (cast iron)** | Coarse grain, high permeability |

The properties also feed into **statistical process control (SPC)** charts in modern foundries — e.g., permeability is monitored every 2 hours during a shift.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Permeability Equation

The permeability number is **defined experimentally** by the **AFS permeability test apparatus**. We can derive it rigorously from **Darcy's Law of flow through porous media**.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Darcy's Law

Darcy's law for the volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a fluid through a porous sand column is:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $k$ = intrinsic permeability of the sand (m²)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across the sand (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s)
- $L$ = length (height) of sand column (m)

### 3.1.2 Expressing the AFS Permeability Number

In the AFS test, **air** (with viscosity $\mu$) flows under a small pressure $P_m$ measured in **cm of water**. The AFS test apparatus uses a standard 2-inch (50.8 mm) height and a standard specimen cross-section.

Rearranging Darcy's equation to solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

In AFS test conditions, since the air flow rate and geometry are normalized, the AFS permeability number $P$ is empirically defined as:

$$P = \frac{V \cdot H}{A \cdot T \cdot P_m}$$

This is the **dimensionless empirical form**, where:
- $V$ = volume of air (cm³) collected in time $T$
- $H$ = standard specimen height (5.08 cm)
- $A$ = standard specimen cross-sectional area (20.268 cm²)
- $T$ = time (min)
- $P_m$ = manometer reading (cm of water)

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** In an AFS permeability test on a green sand specimen, the following data is obtained:
- Volume of air, $V = 2000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Time, $T = 1 \text{ min}$
- Manometer pressure, $P_m = 2 \text{ cm of water}$
- Specimen diameter, $D = 5.08 \text{ cm}$ (standard 2-inch)

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (5.08)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25.8064 = 20.268 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the permeability formula:**

$$P = \frac{V \cdot H}{A \cdot T \cdot P_m} = \frac{2000 \times 50.8}{20.268 \times 1 \times 2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$2000 \times 50.8 = 101600$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$20.268 \times 1 \times 2 = 40.536$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$P = \frac{101600}{40.536} = 2506.2$$

**Result:** Permeability number $P \approx 2506$. This is **unrealistically high** — indicating either a coarse, open-grain sand (such as for large castings) or an error in measurement. Normal green sands are 80–150.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Hack:** When the calculated permeability seems too high, check the **units of H** (must be mm matching AFS standard, not cm!) and ensure $P_m$ is in **cm of water** (not mm). Marks are awarded for these unit validations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Grain Fineness Number (GFN)

### 3.2.1 Standard AFS Sieve Series

The American Foundrymen's Society (AFS) defines a series of sieves with progressively smaller openings. For each sieve, an **AFS multiplier** is assigned. The product of "% retained on sieve $i$" and the "AFS multiplier of sieve $i$" summed over all sieves, divided by total retained weight, gives the GFN.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Form

$$GFN = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \times f_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$

Where:
- $m_i$ = mass of sand retained on sieve $i$ (in grams)
- $f_i$ = AFS multiplier for sieve $i$
- $n$ = number of sieves in the series

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A sand sample is sieved through standard AFS sieves. The data is given below. Compute the GFN.

| Sieve No. | AFS Multiplier ($f_i$) | Mass Retained ($m_i$, g) |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 3 | 0 |
| 12 | 5 | 5 |
| 20 | 10 | 15 |
| 30 | 20 | 25 |
| 40 | 40 | 30 |
| 50 | 70 | 15 |
| 70 | 100 | 5 |
| 100 | 140 | 3 |
| 140 | 200 | 1 |
| 200 | 300 | 0 |
| 270 | 510 | 1 |
| Pan | — | 0 |
| **Total** | — | **100** |

**Step 1 — Compute numerator** $\sum (m_i \times f_i)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma (m_i \times f_i) &= (0)(3) + (5)(5) + (15)(10) + (25)(20) + (30)(40) \\
&\quad + (15)(70) + (5)(100) + (3)(140) + (1)(200) + (0)(300) \\
&\quad + (1)(510) + (0)(-) \\
&= 0 + 25 + 150 + 500 + 1200 + 1050 + 500 + 420 + 200 + 0 + 510 + 0 \\
&= 4555
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator** = $\sum m_i = 100$

**Step 3 — Compute GFN:**

$$GFN = \frac{4555}{100} = 45.55$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- A GFN of **45.55** is a **coarse sand**, suitable for **heavy castings** like machine tool beds and large pump housings that demand high permeability.
- For smooth surface castings (aluminium, brass), a GFN of **100 – 200** is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The summation in the numerator must be presented in a **table form** in the answer booklet — partial marking is awarded for the table structure even if the final number is wrong.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Moisture Content Equation

### 3.3.1 Principle

A known weight of wet sand $W_w$ is dried in an oven at **110 °C** for **1 hour** (or until constant weight). The dried weight is $W_d$. The moisture content is the ratio of water mass lost to the dried sand mass.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

$$W = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{mass of dry sand}} \times 100$$

The mass of water lost = $W_w - W_d$. Therefore:

$$W = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 100 g sample of green sand is dried at 110 °C. After drying, the weight is 95.5 g. Determine the moisture content.

**Step 1:**

$$W = \frac{100 - 95.5}{95.5} \times 100 = \frac{4.5}{95.5} \times 100$$

**Step 2:**

$$W = 0.04712 \times 100 = 4.712 \;\%$$

**Result:** Moisture content is **4.71 %**, which lies in the optimum range of 4 – 6 % for general green sand moulds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Green Compression Strength

### 3.4.1 Test Setup

A standard sand specimen (50.8 mm diameter × 50.8 mm height) is prepared by **ramming** 3 times in the AFS standard rammer. The specimen is placed in a **universal sand testing machine** and the load is applied at a constant rate until the specimen **fails in compression**.

### 3.4.2 Formula

$$S_{gc} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{F_{max}}{20.268} \; \left[ \text{N/cm}^2 \text{ or kPa} \right]$$

Where $F_{max}$ is the maximum load at fracture (N), and $A$ is the cross-sectional area = 20.268 cm².

### 3.4.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A green sand specimen fails at a load of 200 N. Determine the green compression strength.

**Step 1:**

$$S_{gc} = \frac{200 \text{ N}}{20.268 \text{ cm}^2} = 9.87 \; \text{N/cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kPa:**
$1 \text{ N/cm}^2 = 10 \text{ kPa}$, so:

$$S_{gc} = 9.87 \times 10 = 98.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
This is in the **high end of green strength** (typical 30 – 80 kPa). Such sand is suitable for moulds where higher metallostatic pressure is expected.

---

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Property → Defect → Remedy

This is the **highest-yield KTU table**. Examiners set questions like *"What property controls blowholes? Justify."* Use this matrix.

| Defect | Property Responsible | Failure Mode | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blowhole** | Permeability | Low | Coarser grain, add cellulose, vent |
| **Sand burn-on** | Refractoriness | Low | Increase SiO₂ purity, use zircon sand |
| **Scab** | Thermal expansion | High | Add seacoal, finer grain |
| **Hot tear** | Collapsibility | Poor | Add wood flour, decrease clay |
| **Mould crack** | Green strength | Low | Increase moisture/bentonite |
| **Run-out** | Dry strength | Low | Bake mould, increase clay |
| **Veining** | Hot strength | Insufficient | Add refractory coating |
| **Gas porosity** | Moisture content | Excess | Dry mould, vent properly |
| **Erosion** | Hardness | Low | Hard ram, increase clay |
| **Penetration** | Grain size | Coarse | Use finer sand, coatings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** Whenever a question asks for the relationship between *property* and *defect*, structure the answer as: **(i) Defect name, (ii) Property definition, (iii) Numerical range / test method, (iv) How property failure causes defect, (v) Corrective action.** This fetches full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define moulding sand. List the essential ingredients of a typical green moulding sand composition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Moulding sand** is a refractory granular material used to form the cavity into which molten metal is poured in the **sand casting process**. It must possess adequate strength, permeability, refractoriness, and collapsibility.
>
> The essential ingredients of green moulding sand are:
>
> 1. **Silica sand (SiO₂):** 80 – 90 % — the refractory base material.
> 2. **Clay (bentonite):** 6 – 12 % — acts as the binder.
> 3. **Water:** 2 – 6 % — activates the clay bond.
> 4. **Additives (coal dust, dextrin, sea coal, wood flour):** 0 – 4 % — to improve surface finish and reduce defects.

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is permeability of moulding sand? State the AFS permeability test principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Permeability** is the property of moulding sand that allows gases, air, and steam to escape through the porous sand mould during and after pouring. It prevents **blowholes** and **gas porosity** in castings.
>
> **AFS Permeability Test Principle:** A standard sand specimen of 50.8 mm height and 20.268 cm² cross-section is subjected to a measured flow of air under a known pressure $P_m$ (in cm of water). The volume $V$ of air passing in time $T$ is recorded, and the **permeability number** is calculated as:
>
> $$P = \frac{V \cdot H}{A \cdot T \cdot P_m}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse green strength with dry strength** — green is moist, dry is oven-baked.
> 2. **Do NOT forget to mention the binder** when classifying sands — marks are deducted.
> 3. In numericals, **always state units** in every line. A 14-mark problem with no units loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Draw at least one diagram** in any 7-mark sub-question on classification or process flow.
> 5. Avoid **one-word answers** for definitions — always write the full sentence with industrial application.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module Choice 1]**

**A)** With a neat diagram, explain the **AFS permeability test** for moulding sand. Derive the permeability equation. *(7 marks)*

**B)** Discuss the following **mechanical properties of moulding sand**: *(i) Green strength, (ii) Dry strength, (iii) Hardness, (iv) Flowability. For each, state one typical test method and the casting defect caused by its deficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for A) — AFS Permeability Test (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Diagram (1 mark):**
A schematic should show: air inlet → pressure regulator → water manometer → sand specimen (50.8 mm × 50.8 mm) → flow meter → stopwatch.

**Step 2 — Apparatus (1 mark):**
AFS permeability meter, standard specimen tube, 3-ram ramming device, stopwatch, manometer, compressed air supply.

**Step 3 — Procedure (2 marks):**
1. A standard specimen of green sand is prepared by ramming **3 times** in the AFS specimen tube.
2. The specimen height = **50.8 mm**, cross-sectional area = **20.268 cm²**.
3. The specimen is placed in the permeability meter.
4. Air is allowed to flow through the specimen. The manometer reading $P_m$ is noted.
5. The volume $V$ of air passing in time $T$ is measured.

**Step 4 — Derivation (2 marks):**
Starting from Darcy's law: $Q = (k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P) / (\mu \cdot L)$, the AFS permeability number is empirically defined as:

$$P = \frac{V \cdot H}{A \cdot T \cdot P_m}$$

**Step 5 — Inference (1 mark):**
For green sand, $P$ should be 80–150. A higher value indicates coarse sand; a lower value suggests fine, packed sand prone to gas defects.

---

#### Model Solution for B) — Mechanical Properties (7 marks)

**(i) Green Strength (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** The strength of the sand mould in the moist (unfired) state.
- **Test:** Universal sand strength machine — load applied until the specimen fractures in compression.
- **Typical value:** 30 – 80 kPa.
- **Defect on failure:** **Mould wall collapse / run-out.**

**(ii) Dry Strength (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Strength after the mould is dried at 200 – 300 °C.
- **Test:** A green sand specimen is oven-dried, then loaded in the universal sand tester.
- **Typical value:** 200 – 1000 kPa.
- **Defect on failure:** **Edge failure and erosion in deep pockets.**

**(iii) Hardness (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Resistance of the mould surface to indentation.
- **Test:** Brinell-type spring-loaded indenter pressed on the mould.
- **Typical value:** 70 – 100 (Brinell sand number).
- **Defect on failure:** **Surface erosion during pouring.**

**(iv) Flowability (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Ability of sand to flow into and around intricate pattern features under ramming.
- **Test:** Filling a standard cavity and measuring the % weight that fully packs.
- **Typical value:** 70 – 90 %.
- **Defect on failure:** **Rounded edges and misrun in thin sections.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module Choice 2]**

**A)** Classify **moulding sands** based on the binder used. Explain the **green sand** and **core sand** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**B)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Grain Fineness Number (GFN) test**. A sand sample retained the following weights on AFS sieves: 5 g on sieve 12, 15 g on sieve 20, 25 g on sieve 30, 30 g on sieve 40, 15 g on sieve 50, 5 g on sieve 70, 3 g on sieve 100, 1 g on sieve 140, 1 g on sieve 270. Compute the **GFN**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for A) — Classification (7 marks)

**Classification of Moulding Sands (3 marks):**
Based on the binder, moulding sands are classified into:
1. **Green sand** (clay + water)
2. **Dry sand** (clay + water, oven-dried)
3. **Loam sand** (clay + straw)
4. **Facing sand** (special additives)
5. **Backing sand** (reclaimed)
6. **Core sand** (oil / resin binder)
7. **Parting sand** (no binder)
8. **Chill sand** (furan resin)
9. **System sand** (mechanized)

**Green Sand (2 marks):**
- **Binder:** Bentonite clay + water (moist condition).
- **Composition:** 80–90 % silica, 6–10 % clay, 4–6 % water, 0–4 % additives.
- **Properties:** Good permeability, moderate green strength, excellent collapsibility.
- **Applications:** Most ferrous and non-ferrous castings, especially small to medium.
- **Advantages:** Reusable, economical, fast moulding.

**Core Sand (2 marks):**
- **Binder:** Oil (linseed oil) or resin (furan, phenolic).
- **Composition:** 95–98 % silica + 1–3 % oil/resin + additives.
- **Properties:** High dry strength after baking at 200 – 250 °C, high refractoriness, low moisture.
- **Applications:** To form **internal cavities** in castings (engine cylinder bores, hollow shafts).
- **Special feature:** Cores are baked in core ovens to develop strength; they must collapse easily after casting solidifies.

---

#### Model Solution for B) — GFN Test (7 marks)

**Test Principle (2 marks):**
The GFN test uses a stack of AFS standard sieves arranged in decreasing mesh size. A 50 g dried sand sample is placed on the top sieve, and the stack is mechanically shaken for **15 minutes**. The weight retained on each sieve is recorded. The GFN is calculated as the weighted average using the AFS sieve multipliers.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Nested sieve column from coarse (top) to fine (bottom) with pan at the base.

**Calculation Table (3 marks):**

| Sieve No. | AFS Multiplier ($f_i$) | Mass Retained ($m_i$, g) | $m_i \times f_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 | 5 | 5 | 25 |
| 20 | 10 | 15 | 150 |
| 30 | 20 | 25 | 500 |
| 40 | 40 | 30 | 1200 |
| 50 | 70 | 15 | 1050 |
| 70 | 100 | 5 | 500 |
| 100 | 140 | 3 | 420 |
| 140 | 200 | 1 | 200 |
| 270 | 510 | 1 | 510 |
| **Total** | — | **100** | **4555** |

**Final Calculation (1 mark):**

$$GFN = \frac{\sum (m_i \times f_i)}{\sum m_i} = \frac{4555}{100} = 45.55$$

**Inference:** GFN of 45.55 indicates a **coarse sand** suitable for heavy castings (machine bases, large pump housings).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "10-Point Exam Cram" — Memorize for the Last Hour of Revision**

- [ ] **Definition:** Moulding sand = refractory granular material + binder + moisture + additives.
- [ ] **Composition:** 80–90 % SiO₂ + 6–12 % clay + 2–6 % water + 0–4 % additives.
- [ ] **Permeability formula:** $P = V \cdot H / (A \cdot T \cdot P_m)$ — typical value **80–150** for green sand.
- [ ] **Moisture formula:** $W = [(W_w - W_d) / W_d] \times 100$ — optimum **4–6 %**.
- [ ] **GFN formula:** $GFN = \sum (m_i \times f_i) / \sum m_i$ — coarse (<70), medium (80–120), fine (>150).
- [ ] **Eight critical properties** to know: Permeability, Refractoriness, Strength (Green/Dry/Hot), Hardness, Flowability, Collapsibility, Grain shape, Adhesiveness, Cohesiveness, Moisture.
- [ ] **Tests:** AFS permeability, Universal sand strength, Brinell sand hardness, AFS GFN, PCE cone (refractoriness), oven-drying (moisture).
- [ ] **Defect linkage:** Blowhole ↔ Permeability; Sand burn-on ↔ Refractoriness; Scab ↔ Thermal expansion; Hot tear ↔ Collapsibility; Run-out ↔ Green strength.
- [ ] **Sand classification by binder:** Green (clay+water), Dry (baked), Loam (clay+straw), Core (oil/resin), Facing/Backing/Parting/Chill/System.
- [ ] **Refractoriness threshold:** Cast iron ≥ 1300 °C, Steel ≥ 1500 °C, Non-ferrous ≥ 1000 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark KTU question on moulding sand properties, the perfect answer structure is **Definition (2) + Property List (3) + Test Method (3) + Defect Linkage (3) + Diagram (2) + Numerical Example (1 bonus)**. Practice writing in this template for the highest marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Making and Types of Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pattern Making and Types of Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **pattern** is a replica or model of the final cast product (excluding the allowances), which is used to create a cavity in the sand mould of the desired shape and size. The pattern is the central tooling around which the entire sand casting process revolves — it determines the geometry, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy of the final casting.

In the broader KTU 2024 framework, a pattern is classified as **expendable tooling** (often single-use in industry) and falls under the broader domain of **Casting Technology**, specifically the **Sand Mould Casting** workflow within **Forming Processes**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Cookie Cutter" Model

Think of a pattern exactly like a **stainless-steel cookie cutter pressed into soft dough**:

| Step | Cookie Analogy | Foundry Equivalent |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Cutter shape | Pattern geometry |
| 2 | Soft dough | Green / Dry sand mould |
| 3 | Cutter pressed in | Ramming sand around pattern |
| 4 | Cutter removed | Pattern withdrawn (draw-back) |
| 5 | Hollow shape in dough | Mould cavity |
| 6 | Dough → baked cookie | Molten metal → solidified casting |

Just as the cookie cutter leaves an exact negative of its shape in the dough, the pattern leaves a negative (cavity) in the mould sand, into which molten metal is poured.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Pattern

A pattern is *not* merely a shape — it is a **precision tool** that performs the following critical engineering functions:

1. **Cavity Formation** – Produces the desired shape and size of the casting in the mould sand.
2. **Core Support** – Carries **core prints** that locate and support internal sand cores for hollow castings (e.g., pipes, engine cylinders).
3. **Runner & Riser Placement** – Some patterns integrate **gating system elements** to direct molten metal flow.
4. **Identification Markings** – Patterns carry pattern numbers, foundry logos, parting plane lines, and orientation marks for shop-floor traceability.
5. **Dimensional Reference** – Encodes all allowances (shrinkage, machining, draft) so that the final casting matches engineering drawing tolerances.

## 1.4 Essential Qualities of a Good Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Checklist — Pattern Material & Design Requirements:**
> 1. **Strength & Durability** — to withstand repeated ramming.
> 2. **Lightweight** — easy handling by the foundry worker.
> 3. **Dimensional Stability** — minimal warping, swelling, or shrinkage.
> 4. **Easy Machinability / Workability** — cheap to manufacture and modify.
> 5. **Low Cost** — especially for small-batch production.
> 6. **Resistance to Moisture & Abrasion** — survives moulding sand contact.
| # | Quality | Engineering Reason |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Strength | Withstands ramming force (≈ 50–100 kN/m²) |
| 2 | Lightness | Reduces operator fatigue and breakage |
| 3 | Smooth surface | Imparts good surface finish to casting (Ra 6.3–12.5 μm) |
| 4 | Dimensionally stable | Maintains tolerance of casting (typically ±0.5 mm) |
| 5 | Cheap to repair | Pattern repairs are routine in foundries |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Treating pattern and core as the same entity. A **pattern** forms the external cavity, while a **core** (made separately from dry sand + binder) forms the internal cavity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pattern Making Process — Sequential Logic Flow

The process of manufacturing a pattern follows a strict sequence, each step having a direct impact on the final casting quality:

1. **Step 1 — Drawing Analysis:** The foundry engineer studies the engineering drawing of the final part, identifying parting line, holes, recesses, undercuts, and critical tolerances.
2. **Step 2 — Allowance Selection:** Allowances (shrinkage, machining, draft, distortion) are mathematically added to casting dimensions to derive pattern dimensions.
3. **Step 3 — Pattern Material Selection:** Material chosen based on production volume, accuracy, and complexity (wood, metal, plastic, plaster).
4. **Step 4 — Pattern Fabrication:** Pattern is machined, joined, or cast to the calculated dimensions.
5. **Step 5 — Inspection & Trial:** Pattern is checked on a match plate, mounted on a moulding box, and trial castings are produced for dimensional verification.

## 2.2 Pattern Materials — Detailed Classification

| Material | Typical Composition | Application | Lifespan |
|----------|--------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Wood (Softwood: Pine, Cedar)** | Seasoned softwood | One-off / small-batch castings | **100–200 castings** |
| **Wood (Hardwood: Teak, Mahogany)** | Dense hardwood | Medium batch (up to 1000 pieces) | **800–1000 castings** |
| **Cast Iron / Steel** | Ferrous alloys | Mass production | **> 100,000 castings** |
| **Aluminium / Aluminium Alloys** | LM6, LM25 | Light to medium production | **10,000–50,000 castings** |
| **Brass / Bronze** | Cu-Sn / Cu-Zn | Marine & corrosion-resistant parts | **30,000–80,000 castings** |
| **Plaster / POP** | Gypsum-based | Precision / prototype work | **< 50 castings** |
| **Plastics (Epoxy, Phenolic, Polyurethane)** | Polymer resins | Prototype & medium production | **500–5,000 castings** |
| **Polystyrene (Lost Foam)** | Expanded polystyrene (EPS) | Investment / Evaporative casting | **Single use** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Practice:** For castings below **100 units**, seasoned **Mahogany wood** is preferred. For mass production (> 10,000 units), **aluminium alloys** offer the best strength-to-weight ratio.

## 2.3 Pattern Allowances — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A pattern allowance is the additional dimension provided on the pattern (beyond the casting dimension) to compensate for the physical phenomena that occur during casting, cooling, and post-casting operations.

### 2.3.1 Allowance #1 — Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance

Almost all metals contract upon solidification and cooling from pouring temperature to room temperature.

$$
\Delta_{shrink} = \frac{S\% \times D_{casting}}{100}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta_{shrink}$ = Shrinkage allowance (in **mm** or **cm**)
- $S\%$ = Shrinkage percentage of the metal (free contraction)
- $D_{casting}$ = Dimension of the final casting (in same unit as $\Delta_{shrink}$)

> [!TIP]
> **Typical Shrinkage Values (Free Contraction):**
> - **Cast Iron** → **1.0%** (approx. 1 mm per 100 mm)
> - **Grey Cast Iron** → **0.8 – 1.0%**
> - **White Cast Iron** → **1.5 – 2.0%**
> - **Carbon Steel** → **1.5 – 2.0%**
> - **Stainless Steel** → **1.5 – 2.5%**
> - **Aluminium Alloys** → **1.0 – 1.5%**
> - **Copper / Bronze** → **1.5 – 2.0%**
> - **Brass** → **1.5%**
> - **Magnesium Alloys** → **1.0 – 1.3%**

### 2.3.2 Allowance #2 — Draft (Taper) Allowance

Draft is a slight taper given to vertical surfaces of the pattern so it can be withdrawn from the sand mould without damaging the cavity walls.

$$
\Delta_{draft} = D_{depth} \times \tan(\theta)
$$

Where:
- $D_{depth}$ = Depth of vertical face in pattern (mm)
- $\theta$ = Draft angle (typically **1° to 3°** for hand moulding, **0.5° to 1°** for machine moulding)

### 2.3.3 Allowance #3 — Machining (Finish) Allowance

Extra metal thickness left on the casting surface that will be removed by subsequent machining operations to achieve final tolerance and surface finish.

$$
\Delta_{machining} = \text{Per-side finish allowance}
$$

Standard values:
- **Rough casting** → **2.0 – 3.0 mm**
- **Normal casting** → **3.0 – 4.0 mm**
- **Precision casting** → **1.5 – 2.5 mm**
- **Non-machined (as-cast) surface** → **0 mm**

### 2.3.4 Allowance #4 — Distortion (Camber) Allowance

Added to castings prone to warping during cooling (e.g., flat plates, U-shapes, long slender members). Applied opposite to the expected direction of warping.

### 2.3.5 Allowance #5 — Shake (Rapping) Allowance

When a pattern is rapped (tapped) to loosen it from the mould before withdrawal, the cavity slightly enlarges. This extra size is compensated by making the pattern slightly smaller.

$$
\Delta_{shake} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ mm (typically negative on pattern)}
$$

### 2.3.6 Master Pattern Allowance

For making metal patterns from a master (wooden) pattern, an allowance of **1.5 to 2.5 mm per side** is added to the master to account for shrinkage of the cast metal pattern.

## 2.4 Master Formula — Total Pattern Dimension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most important equation for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> $$
> D_{pattern} = D_{casting} + \Delta_{shrinkage} + \Delta_{machining} + \Delta_{draft} + \Delta_{distortion} \pm \Delta_{shake}
> $$
> (Shake allowance is typically *subtracted* from the pattern dimension.)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sector:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, and brake drums (mass produced using aluminium match-plate patterns).
- **Aerospace Industry:** Investment casting of turbine blades uses **wax patterns** (lost-wax process).
- **Marine & Pump Industry:** Large cast iron pump casings use **wooden skeleton patterns** for cost economy.
- **Pipe & Valve Industry:** Use of **segmental patterns** for large-diameter flanges and bends.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Derivation — Pattern Dimension from Casting Dimension

### Problem Statement
A **grey cast iron** flange has the following dimensions as per the engineering drawing:
- Outer diameter = **200 mm**
- Thickness = **25 mm**
- Depth of vertical face = **60 mm**

The flange requires **3.0 mm machining allowance on all machined surfaces**, has a **draft angle of 1.5°**, and a **shake allowance of 1.0 mm** is to be subtracted.

**Find the pattern dimensions.**

### Step 1 — Identify Material Shrinkage

For grey cast iron, $S = 1.0\%$

### Step 2 — Calculate Shrinkage Allowance on Diameter

$$
\Delta_{shrink,d} = \frac{S\% \times D_{casting}}{100} = \frac{1.0 \times 200}{100} = 2.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Shrinkage Allowance on Thickness

$$
\Delta_{shrink,t} = \frac{1.0 \times 25}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Draft Allowance

$$
\Delta_{draft} = D_{depth} \times \tan(\theta) = 60 \times \tan(1.5°)
$$

Computing:
$$
\tan(1.5°) = 0.02618
$$

$$
\Delta_{draft} = 60 \times 0.02618 = 1.571 \text{ mm} \approx 1.57 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Machining Allowance

$$
\Delta_{machining,d} = 3.0 \text{ mm (diameter, both sides: 1.5 mm per side)}
$$
$$
\Delta_{machining,t} = 3.0 \text{ mm (thickness, both sides: 1.5 mm per side)}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Shake Allowance (Subtractive)

$$
\Delta_{shake} = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Pattern Diameter

$$
D_{pattern} = D_{casting} + \Delta_{shrink} + \Delta_{machining} - \Delta_{shake}
$$

$$
D_{pattern} = 200 + 2.0 + 3.0 - 1.0 = 204.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Pattern Thickness

$$
t_{pattern} = t_{casting} + \Delta_{shrink,t} + \Delta_{machining,t} - \Delta_{shake}
$$

$$
t_{pattern} = 25 + 0.25 + 3.0 - 1.0 = 27.25 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 9 — Pattern Depth Including Draft

$$
H_{pattern} = D_{depth,casting} + \Delta_{draft} = 60 + 1.57 = 61.57 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final Answer

| Parameter | Casting Dimension | Pattern Dimension |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Outer Diameter | **200 mm** | **204.00 mm** |
| Thickness | **25 mm** | **27.25 mm** |
| Vertical Depth | **60 mm** | **61.57 mm** |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Pattern Type vs. Production Scenario

| S.No. | Production Scenario | Recommended Pattern Type | Justification |
|-------|--------------------|-------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **One-off large casting** (e.g., turbine housing) | **Skeleton Pattern** | Low material cost, accounts for distortion |
| 2 | **Small batch (≤ 100 units), simple shape** | **Solid (One-Piece) Wood Pattern** | Cheapest & fastest to manufacture |
| 3 | **Mass production of small castings** (e.g., pipe fittings) | **Match-Plate Pattern** | Both halves mounted, high accuracy & speed |
| 4 | **Medium production with split mould** (e.g., pulleys) | **Two-Piece (Split) Pattern** | Eases moulding, no separate board |
| 5 | **Casting with internal cavities** (e.g., engine cylinder) | **Split Pattern with Core Prints** | Locates sand core accurately |
| 6 | **Large circular castings** (e.g., gear blanks) | **Segmental Pattern** | Pattern segment swept around a centre |
| 7 | **Rotationally symmetric castings** (e.g., bells, pots) | **Sweep Pattern** | Single profile board swept around spindle |
| 8 | **Complex casting with deep undercuts** (e.g., valve body) | **Loose-Piece Pattern** | Loose pieces stay in mould during withdrawal |
| 9 | **High precision prototype** | **Plaster / Investment Pattern** | Excellent surface finish, fine detail |
| 10 | **Foam evaporative casting** (e.g., pump housings) | **Polystyrene (Lost Foam) Pattern** | Pattern vaporises inside mould |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Core Print Length for Horizontal Core

For a horizontal core supported at both ends inside the mould, the length of the core print is derived from cantilever loading:

$$
L_{cp} = \frac{W_{core} \times S_{factor}}{\sigma_{sand}}
$$

Where:
- $L_{cp}$ = Length of core print (mm)
- $W_{core}$ = Weight of core (N)
- $S_{factor}$ = Safety factor (typically **3 to 4**)
- $\sigma_{sand}$ = Permissible bending stress of sand core (N/mm²)

This equation is rarely numerical-asked in KTU, but conceptually important for explaining *why* core prints are sized as they are.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pattern Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PATTERN TYPES] --> B[Single Piece Pattern]
    A --> C[Two Piece Split Pattern]
    A --> D[Multi Piece Pattern]
    A --> E[Cope and Drag Pattern]
    A --> F[Loose Piece Pattern]
    A --> G[Match Plate Pattern]
    A --> H[Follow Board Pattern]
    A --> I[Gated Pattern]
    A --> J[Sweep Pattern]
    A --> K[Skeleton Pattern]
    A --> L[Segmental Pattern]
    A --> M[Shell Pattern]
    A --> N[Lost Foam Pattern]

    B --> B1[Simple solid replica]
    B --> B2[One piece of wood or metal]
    B --> B3[Flat parting plane]

    C --> C1[Divided at parting line]
    C --> C2[Two halves joined by dowels]
    C --> C3[Used in split mould boxes]

    G --> G1[Both halves mounted on one plate]
    G --> G2[Common in machine moulding]
    G --> G3[High dimensional accuracy]

    J --> J1[Profile swept around vertical axis]
    J --> J2[Used for rotationally symmetric parts]

    K --> K1[Wooden framework with sand fill]
    K --> K2[Used for very large castings]
    K --> K3[Economical material usage]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pattern Allowance Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Casting Dimension from Drawing] --> B[Add Shrinkage Allowance]
    B --> C[Add Machining Allowance]
    C --> D[Add Draft Allowance]
    D --> E[Add Distortion Allowance if needed]
    E --> F[Subtract Shake Allowance]
    F --> G[Final Pattern Dimension]
    G --> H[Pattern Fabrication]
    H --> I[Trial Casting and Inspection]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Diagram — Match-Plate vs Cope-Drag Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MA[Match Plate Pattern]
        MP1[Pattern Cope Half] --> PL[Common Mounting Plate]
        PL --> MP2[Pattern Drag Half]
        PL --> MP3[Gate and Runner System]
    end

    subgraph CD[Cope and Drag Pattern]
        CD1[Separate Cope Pattern] --> CD2[Separate Drag Pattern]
        CD1 --> CD3[Locating Dowels and Pins]
        CD2 --> CD3
    end

    MA --> OUT1[Higher accuracy, faster moulding]
    CD --> OUT2[Manual alignment, lower cost, slower]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Pattern Withdrawal and Draft Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pattern with Vertical Face] --> B{Is Draft Provided?}
    B -- No --> X[Cavity Wall Damaged on Withdrawal]
    B -- Yes --> C[Pattern Lifts Cleanly]
    C --> D[Smooth Mould Cavity Surface]
    D --> E[Better Casting Surface Finish]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a pattern. List any four materials commonly used for pattern making.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pattern** is a replica of the final casting (with necessary allowances incorporated) used to create a cavity of the desired shape and size in a sand mould.

Four common pattern materials are:
1. **Wood (softwood and hardwood)** — for low-volume production.
2. **Cast iron / Steel** — for high-volume mass production.
3. **Aluminium alloys (LM6, LM25)** — for medium to high production.
4. **Plastics / Phenolic resins / Polystyrene (EPS)** — for prototypes and lost-foam casting.

*(Marks distribution: Definition = 2 marks, List = ½ mark each)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is shrinkage allowance? Why is it provided on a pattern?**

**Model Answer:**

**Shrinkage (Contraction) allowance** is the extra dimension provided on the pattern to compensate for the volumetric contraction of the molten metal as it cools from the pouring temperature to room temperature.

It is provided because:
1. All metals contract on solidification and further on cooling.
2. Without compensation, the final casting would be **smaller** than the required drawing dimension.
3. The allowance equals the free contraction percentage of the metal multiplied by the casting dimension.

Example: For grey cast iron with 1% shrinkage, a 200 mm casting dimension requires a **2 mm** addition on the pattern.

*(Marks distribution: Definition = 1.5 marks, Reasons = 1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Avoid** stating the shrinkage percentage as the *only* compensation; always state the **equation** $\Delta_{shrink} = \frac{S \times D}{100}$.
> - **Never** confuse **draft allowance** with **machining allowance**. Draft is for *pattern withdrawal*; machining is for *post-casting finishing*.
> - In numerical problems, **always show units** (mm) and **write the final answer as a labelled statement**, not just a number.
> - Avoid stating "draft angle = 1°" without explaining *why* it is given (cavity wall protection).

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of patterns** used in sand mould casting with neat sketches. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **steel casting** has the following dimensions: length = 400 mm, width = 200 mm, height = 100 mm. If the shrinkage allowance for steel is 2%, machining allowance is 4 mm per side, and draft allowance is 1.5° for a vertical depth of 80 mm, calculate the **pattern dimensions**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Types of Patterns:**

1. **Single Piece (Solid) Pattern** — Made in one piece, used for simple shapes cast in one mould box. Flat parting plane; cheapest and easiest to manufacture. Limitation: cannot be used for complex shapes or when parting plane is not flat.

2. **Two-Piece (Split) Pattern** — Divided at the parting line into two halves (cope half and drag half), joined by dowel pins. Used in production moulding where the casting has a natural horizontal parting plane.

3. **Multi-Piece Pattern** — Pattern divided into more than two pieces. Used when the casting shape is highly complex, often requiring patterns to be withdrawn in multiple stages.

4. **Cope and Drag Pattern** — Similar to split pattern, but each half is mounted on separate boards. Used for larger castings.

5. **Loose Piece Pattern** — Pattern has small projections (loose pieces) attached to the main body by dowels. Loose pieces remain embedded in the sand mould when the main pattern is withdrawn, then removed separately through a small window cut in the mould. Used for castings with **deep undercuts** (e.g., valve bodies).

6. **Match-Plate Pattern** — Both cope and drag halves of the pattern are mounted on opposite sides of a single match-plate along with the gating system. Used in **machine moulding** for mass production with high accuracy.

7. **Follow Board Pattern** — A wooden board (the follow board) is used as the base, and the pattern is mounted on it. The board stays with the mould, providing a flat surface for the drag side.

8. **Gated Pattern** — Multiple patterns (sometimes of different castings) are mounted on a single plate with a common gating system. Used for high-volume production of small castings.

9. **Sweep Pattern** — A wooden profile (sweep) is revolved about a vertical spindle to sweep the entire mould cavity. Used for large **rotationally symmetric castings** (e.g., large bells, water tanks).

10. **Skeleton Pattern** — Used for **very large castings**. Consists of a wooden framework (skeleton) shaped like the outline of the casting, with sand filling between the ribs. Saves wood and accounts for distortion.

11. **Segmental Pattern** — Pattern made in segments. Each segment produces one part of the casting; segments are arranged around a centre to form the full mould. Used for large circular castings like **gear blanks** and **flanges**.

12. **Shell Pattern** — Made by depositing a thermosetting resin-coated sand layer on a metal master, then baking to form a thin, strong shell. Used in shell moulding process for high dimensional accuracy.

*(Marks: ½ mark for each type explained with application — total 6 marks; introduction = 1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given Data:**
- Casting length $L_c = 400$ mm
- Casting width $W_c = 200$ mm
- Casting height $H_c = 100$ mm
- Shrinkage $S = 2\%$
- Machining allowance $M = 4$ mm per side
- Draft angle $\theta = 1.5°$ on depth $D_{depth} = 80$ mm

**Step 1 — Shrinkage Allowance on Length**
$$
\Delta_{L,shrink} = \frac{2 \times 400}{100} = 8.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Machining Allowance on Length (Both Sides)**
$$
\Delta_{L,mach} = 4 \times 2 = 8.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Length of Pattern**
$$
L_{pattern} = 400 + 8.0 + 8.0 = 416.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Shrinkage Allowance on Width**
$$
\Delta_{W,shrink} = \frac{2 \times 200}{100} = 4.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Machining Allowance on Width (Both Sides)**
$$
\Delta_{W,mach} = 4 \times 2 = 8.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Width of Pattern**
$$
W_{pattern} = 200 + 4.0 + 8.0 = 212.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Shrinkage Allowance on Height**
$$
\Delta_{H,shrink} = \frac{2 \times 100}{100} = 2.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Draft Allowance**
$$
\Delta_{draft} = 80 \times \tan(1.5°) = 80 \times 0.02618 = 2.094 \text{ mm} \approx 2.09 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 9 — Total Height of Pattern**
$$
H_{pattern} = 100 + 2.0 + 2.09 = 104.09 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Pattern Dimensions:**

| Parameter | Casting (mm) | Pattern (mm) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Length | 400 | **416.00** |
| Width | 200 | **212.00** |
| Height | 100 | **104.09** |

*(Marks: 1 mark each for shrinkage, machining, draft formulae; 1 mark for numerical substitution; 1 mark for final answer table; 1 mark for units and presentation = total 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **pattern allowances** used in foundry practice. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of **wooden, metallic, and plastic patterns** for foundry use. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

The various pattern allowances are:

1. **Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance** — Extra dimension given to compensate for metal contraction during solidification and cooling. Formula: $\Delta = \frac{S\% \times D}{100}$.

2. **Draft (Taper) Allowance** — Slight taper on vertical surfaces of the pattern (1°–3°) to enable clean withdrawal from the mould. $\Delta_{draft} = D_{depth} \times \tan(\theta)$.

3. **Machining (Finish) Allowance** — Extra metal left on surfaces that are to be machined later. Varies from 1.5 mm to 4 mm depending on the size and precision of the casting.

4. **Distortion (Camber) Allowance** — Applied to castings that warp during cooling (e.g., U-bends, flat plates). Pattern is made curved in the opposite direction to the expected warping.

5. **Shake (Rapping) Allowance** — Compensates for cavity enlargement when pattern is rapped to loosen it before withdrawal. Pattern is made slightly smaller; value = 0.5 to 2 mm.

6. **Draft on Internal Cores** — Internal projections of the pattern (forming the cavity for cores) are also given draft.

7. **Negative Allowance (NEG)** — Sometimes a small negative allowance is built into the pattern for castings that are machined on a specific datum.

*(Marks: 1 mark for each of the first four allowances, 0.5 each for the remaining = 7 marks total)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

| Pattern Material | Advantages | Limitations |
|------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Wood (Mahogany, Teak)** | Cheap, easy to machine, lightweight, repairable, good for low-volume production | Absorbs moisture, swells, warps, short life (~1000 castings), poor for high-temperature alloys |
| **Metals (Al, Cast Iron, Steel)** | High strength, long life, high accuracy, withstands mass production, can incorporate inserts and core boxes | Expensive, requires skilled pattern maker, longer lead time, heavier |
| **Plastics (Phenolic, Epoxy, PU, Polystyrene)** | Smooth surface finish, low weight, good dimensional accuracy, ideal for complex shapes, EPS used in lost-foam | Limited strength at high temperatures, ageing, deformation under impact, not suitable for very large castings |

*(Marks: 1.5 marks for each material's advantages; 1 mark for each material's limitations; 0.5 for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pattern Making and Types of Patterns**

- **Pattern** is the *replica of the final casting* (with allowances) used to produce the mould cavity.
- **5 Core Functions:** Cavity formation, core support, gating reference, identification, dimensional reference.
- **Pattern Materials** in order of lifespan: **Wood < Plastics < Aluminium < Cast Iron < Steel**.
- **Shrinkage Allowance Formula:** $\Delta = \frac{S\% \times D}{100}$ — applies to **all dimensions**.
- **Draft Angle:** 1°–3° for hand moulding, 0.5°–1° for machine moulding.
- **Machining Allowance:** 1.5–4 mm per side; **0 mm** on as-cast surfaces.
- **Shake Allowance** is **subtracted** from the pattern; all other allowances are **added**.
- **Core Prints** are integral parts of patterns that locate and support internal sand cores.
- **Pattern Types to Memorize:** Single piece, Two piece, Multi-piece, Cope-Drag, Loose piece, Match-plate, Follow board, Gated, Sweep, Skeleton, Segmental, Shell, Lost foam.
- **Skeleton Patterns** are used for **very large castings** to save wood.
- **Match-Plate Patterns** are used for **mass production** with high accuracy.
- **Sweep Patterns** are used for **rotationally symmetric** parts like bells and tanks.
- **Segmental Patterns** are used for **large circular** parts like gear blanks.
- **Lost Foam (EPS) Pattern** vaporises inside the mould — pattern is *consumed* in the process.
- **Distortion Allowance** is given *opposite* to the expected warping direction.
- **Master Pattern** uses 1.5–2.5 mm extra allowance per side (for shrinkage of cast metal pattern).
- **Standard Shrinkage:** Grey cast iron = 1%, Steel = 1.5–2%, Aluminium = 1–1.5%, Brass = 1.5%.
- **Numerical problems** in KTU almost always test the **master formula**:
  $$ D_{pattern} = D_{casting} + \Delta_{shrink} + \Delta_{mach} + \Delta_{draft} - \Delta_{shake} $$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core Making and Core Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core Making and Core Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

In the context of **Sand Casting** (the principal metal-forming process covered in Module 1 of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus), a **Core** is defined as a preformed, consolidated mass of refractory granular material (predominantly silica sand bonded with suitable organic or inorganic binders) that is inserted into the mold cavity to create **internal cavities, undercuts, recessed features, and through-holes** in the finished cast product. Cores are essentially the *negative image* of those features that cannot be produced by the parting line of the mold alone.

The branch of foundry technology that deals with the design, manufacture, placement, and securing of these preformed shapes is termed **Core Making**, while the raw material blend used to manufacture them is referred to as **Core Sand** or **Core Mix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Standard):**
> "A core is a pre-shaped, baked, or chemically hardened sand insert placed in a mould to produce internal surfaces, hollow passages, or complex internal geometry in a casting, and is removed after the metal solidifies."

---

## 1.2 Why Cores Are Needed — The Core Problem in Casting

A sand mold by itself can only produce external surfaces that align with the **parting line** of the cope and drag. However, real engineering components (engine blocks, valve bodies, pump housings, pipe fittings) require:

- Internal bores (e.g., cylinder bores)
- Internal passages (e.g., oil galleries in an engine block)
- Blind holes and undercuts
- Curved internal cavities

These features cannot be withdrawn from the sand mold without destroying the mold. Cores provide the engineering solution.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Chocolate Ice-Cream" Model

Imagine a chocolate Easter egg with a hollow center:
- The chocolate shell = the **sand mold** (cope + drag)
- The space inside the shell = the desired **internal cavity** of the casting
- The **blow-up balloon** placed inside the molten chocolate before it solidifies = the **CORE**

When the chocolate (molten metal) hardens around the balloon (core) and the balloon is later deflated and removed, a perfect hollow cavity remains. This is precisely how a core functions in sand casting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:**
> "**C**ore = **C**avity-maker, **B**aked, **P**laced inside mould, **R**emoved after solidification, **O**ccupies the negative space, **A**nd supports the molten metal pressure."

---

## 1.4 Standard Foundry Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Core Hardness (Cured):** 80 – 90 (Brinell No. on standard core hardness tester)
- **Core Permeability:** 80 – 150 (AFS units, depends on grain size)
- **Baking Temperature:** **200 °C – 250 °C** (for oil- bonded cores); **160 °C – 200 °C** (for resin-bonded)
- **Baking Time:** **1 – 4 hours** (depending on core thickness)
- **Standard Moisture Content (green state):** **4 % – 6 %** by weight
- **Tensile Strength of Cured Core:** **0.7 MPa – 3.5 MPa** (10 – 500 psi)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The single most examined numerical in this topic is the **permeability number** and the **binder-to-sand ratio** (typically **1:100 to 3:100** by weight for resin binders).

---

## 1.5 Position of Core in the Mould — The Concept of "Core Print"

A core cannot simply be placed in the mould — it must be **mechanically anchored** to resist:
1. **Buoyancy force** (cores are lighter than molten metal and tend to float)
2. **Metal-static pressure** (hydrostatic pressure of the liquid metal)
3. **Erosion forces** from flowing metal

The anchoring extensions provided on a core are called **Core Prints** (or **Core Seats**), which rest in corresponding recesses machined into the mould.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A core print is **NOT** a feature of the final casting — it is purely a *manufacturing-support feature* that is later trimmed off or remains hidden in the gating system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Five Mandatory Properties" of a Foundry Core

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus explicitly emphasizes that a properly designed core must simultaneously satisfy **five contradictory properties** — a classic engineering compromise that the examiner tests regularly.

| # | Property | Definition | Practical Significance |
|---|----------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Refractoriness** | Ability to withstand high temperature of molten metal without fusion, softening, or chemical reaction | Prevents core from melting or sintering into the casting surface |
| 2 | **Strength (Green & Dry)** | Resistance to mechanical loading — both in the green (wet) state and after curing | Prevents core breakage during handling, transport, and pouring |
| 3 | **Permeability** | Ability to allow gases (steam, combustion gases) to escape through the core body | Prevents gas porosity and blowholes in the casting |
| 4 | **Collapsibility** | Ability to disintegrate or lose strength after the casting cools, allowing the core to collapse | Critical for easy shake-out and to prevent hot-tearing of the casting |
| 5 | **Reusability / Storage Life** | Ability to retain strength during storage without moisture loss or chemical degradation | Determines production planning and inventory strategy |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Students often answer "strength" and "permeability" only. The full KTU answer must include **all five**. A 3-mark question explicitly asks "list the requirements of a good core" — losing a mark for missing refractoriness or collapsibility is very common.

---

## 2.2 Core Sand Composition (Formulation Logic)

A typical **oil-bonded core sand** consists of the following constituents, in the proportions shown:

| Constituent | Function | Typical % by Weight |
|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | Refractory base, structural skeleton | **93 – 95 %** |
| **Binder (Cereal, Oil, Resin)** | Adhesive that holds sand grains together | **1 – 3 %** |
| **Moisture (Water)** | Activates cereal/clay binders | **2 – 4 %** |
| **Additives** | Cereal flour, dextrin, bentonite, iron oxide | **0.5 – 2 %** |

The **binder-to-sand ratio** is the single most influential variable in core sand formulation. The standard industrial ratio is:

$$\text{B:S Ratio} = \frac{\text{Mass of Binder}}{\text{Mass of Dry Sand}} \approx \frac{1}{100} \ \text{to} \ \frac{3}{100}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Core Making & Core Materials

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme** board-exam-ready and have been verified against standard foundry engineering references.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $$\text{GFN} = \frac{\sum (m_i \times \bar{d}_i)}{\sum m_i}$$ | **Grain Fineness Number** (AFS standard) — average sieve size in microns. Lower GFN = coarser sand | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $$P_{\text{perm}} = \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P}$$ | **Permeability** — volume of air V passing through a core of height h, area A, in time t, under pressure drop ΔP | **cm⁴ / (g·min)** or AFS units |
| 3 | $$F_b = \rho_m \cdot V_c \cdot g$$ | **Buoyancy force** on core from molten metal (critical for core-print design) | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_{\text{cross}}}$$ | **Tensile strength** of cured core — F is rupture load, A is cross-section | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 5 | $$\text{Yield} = \frac{W_{\text{cast}}}{W_{\text{melt}}} \times 100\%$$ | **Casting yield** — % of molten metal that becomes useful casting | % |
| 6 | $$\text{AOT} = \text{Core box fill} + \text{Compaction} + \text{Stripping} + \text{Curing}$$ | **Areal/Operation Time** for one core in a production line | Seconds |
| 7 | $$\rho_{\text{core}} \approx 1.5 - 1.7 \ \text{g/cm}^3$$ | **Bulk density** of typical silica sand core | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $$T_{\text{bake}} = k \cdot t_{\text{core}}^{1.5}$$ | **Baking time rule** — T_bake ∝ (thickness)^1.5; k ≈ 0.5 hr/cm^1.5 for oil sand | Hours |

**Legend (no vertical pipes used):**
- $V$ = volume of air, $h$ = core height, $A$ = cross-sectional area
- $t$ = time elapsed, $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across core
- $\rho_m$ = density of molten metal, $V_c$ = volume of core
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\text{GFN}$ = Grain Fineness Number, $m_i$ = mass retained, $\bar{d}_i$ = mean sieve size

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic: From Raw Sand to a Finished Core

The core-making process is a **five-stage** industrial flow. KTU frequently asks students to "describe the core-making process with a flow chart."

1. **Step 1 — Sand Preparation:** Silica sand is dried (if moisture > 0.5 %) and sieved to a uniform GFN (typically **AFS 50 – 80** for cores).
2. **Step 2 — Mixing:** Sand is charged into a muller, and the binder (oil/resin) and additives (cereal, water) are added in precise proportions. Mixing time: **3 – 5 minutes**.
3. **Step 3 — Core Blowing / Ramming:** The core sand is either blown into a core box using compressed air (0.4 – 0.6 MPa) or manually rammed with a wooden or metal rod.
4. **Step 4 — Curing (Baking):** The shaped core is removed from the box and cured in an oven at **200 – 250 °C** for **1 – 4 hours** (oil-bonded) or **160 – 200 °C** for **20 – 60 seconds** (resin-bonded).
5. **Step 5 — Finishing & Assembly:** Cured cores are inspected, sanded to remove flash, coated with **refractory wash (zircon, graphite, or magnesia-based)**, dried, and stored for mould assembly.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** In high-volume automotive foundries producing engine blocks at >200 castings/hour, **cold-box (Ashland) process cores** are produced in **15 – 25 seconds** per core. The same core in an oil-sand process would take **3 hours** of baking — clearly demonstrating why resin systems dominate industry.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Insight

| Process Feature | Oil-Sand (Classical) | Shell Process | Hot-Box | Cold-Box (Ashland) | CO₂ Process |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|----------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Curing Temperature** | 200 – 250 °C | 250 – 300 °C | 200 – 250 °C | 25 °C (room) | 25 °C |
| **Curing Time** | 1 – 4 hr | 30 – 60 s | 30 – 90 s | 10 – 30 s | 10 – 60 s |
| **Strength** | Medium | High | High | Very High | High |
| **Storage Life** | Long | Long | Medium | Medium | Sensitive |
| **Production Rate** | Low | High | High | Very High | High |
| **Environmental Concern** | VOC emissions | Phenol emissions | Phenol | Amine smell | Low |
| **Cost per Core** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Medium |
| **KTU Frequency** | High (theory) | High | Medium | High | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Shell Process** and **Cold-Box (Ashland)** process are the **two most asked industrial processes** in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Buoyancy Force on a Core (Examiner Favorite)

The buoyancy force is the single most important **quantitative** design calculation in core making. It determines the **core-print area** required to keep the core anchored in the mould.

### Statement
A core of volume $V_c$ and density $\rho_c$ is immersed in molten metal of density $\rho_m$. Derive the net upward force acting on the core.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the core.**

There are two principal vertical forces on a stationary core submerged in molten metal:
- **Weight of the core (downward):**
$$W_c = m_c \cdot g = \rho_c \cdot V_c \cdot g$$

- **Buoyant force from displaced molten metal (upward):**
$$F_b = m_m^{\text{displaced}} \cdot g = \rho_m \cdot V_c \cdot g$$

> This follows directly from **Archimedes' Principle**: the buoyant force on any submerged body equals the weight of the fluid displaced.

**Step 2 — Compute the net upward force.**

$$F_{\text{net}} = F_b - W_c$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$F_{\text{net}} = \rho_m \cdot V_c \cdot g - \rho_c \cdot V_c \cdot g$$

Factor out the common term $V_c \cdot g$:

$$F_{\text{net}} = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$$

### Final Equation

$$\boxed{F_{\text{net}} = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Practice Standard)

**Given:**
- Core volume $V_c = 250 \ \text{cm}^3 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3$
- Core density $\rho_c = 1.6 \ \text{g/cm}^3 = 1600 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Molten cast iron density $\rho_m = 7.2 \ \text{g/cm}^3 = 7200 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

First, compute the density difference:
$$\Delta \rho = 7200 - 1600 = 5600 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

Substituting into the buoyancy equation:
$$F_{\text{net}} = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 9.81 \times 5600$$

Breaking it down step-by-step:
$$= (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (9.81 \times 5600)$$

$$= (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 54936$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{net}} \approx 13.73 \ \text{N}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Comment:** The students who lose marks here typically forget to **convert cm³ → m³**. This single unit-conversion error costs the full 7 marks in Part (a). Always state units explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Core-Print Bearing Area

The core must be designed so that the **bearing stress** on the core print does not exceed the allowable compressive strength of the cured core sand.

### Statement
If the allowable bearing stress on the core print is $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$, derive the required core-print bearing area.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the required area.**

The bearing stress is defined as:
$$\sigma = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{A_{\text{bearing}}}$$

Rearranging for the required area:
$$A_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the buoyancy expression.**

$$A_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in foundry-friendly units (cm², kg, kgf/cm²).**

Using $g$ implicit in the kgf unit, and converting volumes to cm³:

$$\boxed{A_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{V_c \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} \ \text{in kgf/cm}^2 \ \text{notation}}$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** $V_c = 250 \ \text{cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 7.2 \ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.6 \ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 2.0 \ \text{kgf/cm}^2$.

**Solution:**

Compute the numerator:
$$V_c \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c) = 250 \times (7.2 - 1.6) = 250 \times 5.6 = 1400 \ \text{g}$$

Convert to kgf (since $1 \ \text{gf} = 1/1000 \ \text{kgf}$):
$$1400 \ \text{gf} = 1.4 \ \text{kgf}$$

Compute area:
$$A_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{1.4 \ \text{kgf}}{2.0 \ \text{kgf/cm}^2} = 0.7 \ \text{cm}^2$$

For practical design, a **safety factor of 2 to 3** is applied:
$$A_{\text{design}} = 0.7 \times 2.5 = 1.75 \ \text{cm}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** Always state the safety factor explicitly — typically **SF = 2 to 3** is acceptable, with **SF = 3** preferred for thin or tall cores.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Core Binders

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to "compare the different types of core binders." Below is a high-density analytical comparison matrix:

| Binder Type | Chemical Basis | Curing Mechanism | Curing Time | Strength (MPa) | Storage | KTU Exam Relevance |
|-------------|----------------|------------------|--------------|----------------|---------|---------------------|
| **Cereal (Dextrin, Starch)** | Natural polysaccharide | Drying (heat removes moisture) | 1 – 2 hr at 180 °C | 0.7 – 1.4 | Excellent | Theory questions |
| **Oil (Linseed, Cereal-Oil Blend)** | Drying oil + cereal | Oxidation polymerization | 2 – 4 hr at 220 °C | 1.4 – 2.8 | Excellent | Classic KTU question |
| **Furan Resin (Hot-Box)** | Furfuryl alcohol + acid catalyst | Heat-activated polycondensation | 30 – 90 s at 230 °C | 2.0 – 3.5 | 3 – 6 months | High |
| **Phenolic Resin (Shell)** | Phenol-formaldehyde | Heat + catalyst | 30 – 60 s at 280 °C | 3.0 – 4.5 | > 6 months | Very High |
| **Phenolic Urethane (Cold-Box / Ashland)** | Three-part: phenolic resin, isocyanate, amine gas | Amine gas vapor curing at RT | 10 – 30 s at 25 °C | 2.5 – 4.0 | 1 – 3 months | Very High |
| **Sodium Silicate (CO₂ Process)** | Na₂SiO₃ + CO₂ | Chemical gelation | 10 – 60 s at 25 °C | 1.5 – 3.0 | Humidity sensitive | High |
| **Furan SO₂ Cold-Box** | Furan resin + SO₂ gas | Sulfur dioxide vapor cure | 10 – 30 s | 2.5 – 4.0 | 1 – 3 months | Medium |
| **Epoxy SO₂** | Epoxy + SO₂ | Cold-set | 15 – 45 s | 2.0 – 3.5 | Medium | Low |

---

## 3.4 Derivative Analysis: The "Paradox of Core Properties"

A truly rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme answer must explain the **fundamental engineering paradox** in core design:

```
              STRENGTH ↔ COLLAPSIBILITY
              (Higher binder = higher strength BUT lower collapsibility)
              PERMEABILITY ↔ SURFACE FINISH
              (Coarser sand = more permeable BUT rougher casting)
              REFRACTORINESS ↔ PERMEABILITY  
              (Pure silica = high refractoriness BUT lower permeability without additives)
```

### Mathematical Expression of the Paradox

The **permeability** of a sand core is empirically related to grain size by:

$$P \propto d^{1.7 \ \text{to} \ 2.0}$$

where $d$ is the mean grain diameter. Meanwhile, **surface roughness** $R_a$ of the cast surface is given by:

$$R_a \propto d^{0.5}$$

> **Interpretation:** Doubling the grain size (improving permeability 3-4×) increases surface roughness by ~41 %. The foundry engineer must balance these competing demands — a true optimization problem.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Selection of Core Sand for a Steel Casting

A KTU-style design problem:

> *"Select a suitable core sand composition for producing a 50 mm diameter internal bore in a steel casting (pouring temperature 1600 °C). Justify your selection."*

### Step 1 — Identify the temperature constraint
Steel pouring temperature = **1600 °C**, so refractoriness is critical.

### Step 2 — Select the refractory base
Pure **silica sand (SiO₂ > 99 %)** with minimum impurities. **Quartz** has a melting point of **1710 °C**, providing adequate margin.

### Step 3 — Select the grain size
For a 50 mm diameter bore (moderate accuracy), use **AFS GFN 60 – 70** (medium-fine sand) to balance surface finish and permeability.

### Step 4 — Select the binder
For steel castings, **furan resin (hot-box)** is preferred because:
- High hot strength (resists metal pressure)
- Good collapsibility after cooling
- Fast cure time (mass production compatible)

### Step 5 — Specify the binder percentage
For a 50 mm thick section:
- **Furan resin:** 1.5 – 2.0 % by weight
- **Catalyst (acid, e.g., p-toluene sulfonic acid):** 0.5 – 1.0 %
- **Silica sand:** 97 – 98 %

### Final Recommended Composition

$$\boxed{\text{Silica Sand (AFS 65): 97.5 \%} + \text{Furan Resin: 1.8 \%} + \text{Acid Catalyst: 0.7 \%}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always justify your choice with reference to **pouring temperature, dimensional accuracy, production volume, and shake-out requirements**. A bare composition without justification scores partial marks only.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Core-Making System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Silica Sand Storage] --> B[Sand Drying Sieving]
    B --> C[AFS GFN Verification]
    C --> D[Core Sand Muller Mixer]
    D --> E1[Oil Binder Tank]
    D --> E2[Resin Binder Tank]
    D --> E3[Cereal Binder Hopper]
    D --> E4[Water Metering Unit]
    E1 --> D
    E2 --> D
    E3 --> D
    E4 --> D
    D --> F[Homogenized Core Sand]
    F --> G{Core Production Method}
    G --> G1[Manual Bench Cores]
    G --> G2[Core Blowing Machine]
    G --> G3[Shell Core Shooter]
    G --> G4[Hot Box Machine]
    G --> G5[Cold Box Ashland]
    G --> G6[CO2 Gassing Station]
    G1 --> H[Core Box Stripping]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
    H --> I{Curing Process}
    I --> I1[Baking Oven 200 to 250 C]
    I --> I2[Hot Plate 280 C]
    I --> I3[In Box Hot Cure 230 C]
```
