```mermaid
    I --> I4[Amine Gas Room Temp]
    I --> I5[CO2 Gas Room Temp]
    I1 --> J[Cooling and Inspection]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K[Refractory Wash Coating]
    K --> L[Final Core Storage]
    L --> M[Mold Assembly Line]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each terminal node (G1–G6) represents an alternative core-forming technology, and each curing node (I1–I5) is matched to the corresponding binder chemistry. The diagram maps the **complete industrial value chain** from raw sand to installed core.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Core Inside a Sand Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Drag_Half["DRAG Lower Mould Half"]
        D1[Drag Sand Body] 
        D2[Drag Core Print Recess]
    end

    subgraph Cope_Half["COPE Upper Mould Half"]
        C1[Cope Sand Body]
        C2[Cope Core Print Recess]
    end

    subgraph Core_Assembly["CORE ASSEMBLY"]
        K1[Main Core Body Silica Sand Plus Binder]
        K2[Core Vent Channels]
        K3[Lower Core Print]
        K4[Upper Core Print]
        K5[Refractory Wash Coating]
    end

    subgraph Final_Cast["RESULTING CASTING"]
        R1[Outer Cast Surface]
        R2[Internal Cavity from Core]
        R3[Core Print Marks Removed]
    end

    D2 -. supports .-> K3
    C2 -. supports .-> K4
    K1 --> R2
    K5 --> R1
    K2 --> R1
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flowchart: Cold-Box (Ashland) Process — Industry Standard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Pre-mix Sand with Phenolic Resin Part 1] --> P2[Step 2: Add Isocyanate Part 2 to Sand]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Blow Sand into Core Box at 0.5 MPa]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Close Core Box]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Gaseous Amine TEA or DMEA Purged into Box]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Reaction Forms Urethane Bond in 10 to 30 s]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Purge Excess Amine with Air]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Strip Core from Box]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Core Ready for Use within 60 s]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** The Cold-Box process is the **fastest mass-production core process** in the world, used for >70 % of automotive iron castings. KTU questions on this process are very common in the **Part B (14-mark) section**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: CO₂ Sodium Silicate Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Step 1: Mix Silica Sand with 3 to 5 percent Sodium Silicate Na2SiO3] --> Q2[Step 2: Blow or Ram into Core Box]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Close Box and Pierce Gas Inlet]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Pass CO2 Gas at 0.2 to 0.4 MPa for 10 to 60 s]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Chemical Gelation Reaction Occurs]
    Q5[Step 5: Chemical Gelation Reaction Occurs Na2SiO3 + CO2 → Na2CO3 + SiO2 Gel] --> Q6[Step 6: Core Hardens Almost Instantly]
    Q6 --> Q7[Step 7: Strip Core and Use Immediately]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Mermaid: Curing Methods at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Core Sand] --> Y{Binder Type}
    Y --> Y1[Oily or Cereal]
    Y --> Y2[Thermosetting Resin]
    Y --> Y3[Inorganic Silicate]
    Y1 --> Z1[Heat Curing at 200 to 250 C, 1 to 4 hr]
    Y2 --> Z2{Resin Chemistry}
    Z2 --> Z2a[Novolac Shell: 280 C, 30 to 60 s]
    Z2 --> Z2b[Hot-Box Furan: 230 C, 30 to 90 s]
    Z2 --> Z2c[Cold-Box Urethane: RT, 10 to 30 s amine gas]
    Y3 --> Z3[CO2 Gas Curing at RT, 10 to 60 s]
    Z1 --> W[Finished Core]
    Z2a --> W
    Z2b --> W
    Z2c --> W
    Z3 --> W
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Visual Learners:** Notice how the four parallel branches (Z1, Z2a, Z2b, Z2c, Z3) all converge at the same node "Finished Core." This visually reinforces the concept that **different binder chemistries all achieve the same end goal** — a hardened, dimensionally accurate core.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q: Define a "core" in sand moulding. List any four essential properties that a good core must possess.**

#### Model Answer:

A **core** is a preformed, pre-shaped mass of refractory granular material (typically silica sand bonded with suitable organic or inorganic binders) that is placed inside a sand mould cavity to produce **internal cavities, undercuts, blind holes, or through-bores** in the finished casting. The core occupies space that cannot be moulded by the parting line of the cope and drag, and is removed after the molten metal has solidified.

**Four essential properties of a good core:**

1. **Refractoriness** — The core must withstand the high temperature of molten metal without fusing, sintering, or chemically reacting with the metal.

2. **Adequate Strength (Green & Dry)** — The core must possess sufficient mechanical strength to withstand handling, transportation, and the metal-static pressure during pouring without deformation or breakage.

3. **High Permeability** — The core must allow the escape of gases (steam, entrapped air, combustion products) to prevent gas porosity and blowholes in the casting.

4. **Good Collapsibility** — After the casting has solidified and cooled, the core must disintegrate or collapse readily to allow easy shake-out and to prevent hot-tearing of the casting.

> *(Note: The fifth commonly tested property is "Storage Life / Reusability" — the core should retain its properties during storage.)*

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition of core: 1 mark
- Four properties listed with brief description: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **Q: What is a "core print"? Explain its function in the mould assembly.**

#### Model Answer:

A **core print** (also called a **core seat** or **core bearing**) is a precisely machined extension or projection on the sand core that is designed to **rest in a corresponding recess** machined into the cope and drag halves of the sand mould. It is the **anchoring mechanism** that holds the core in the correct position during mould assembly and metal pouring.

**Functions of a Core Print:**

1. **Positioning:** It locates the core accurately in the mould cavity, ensuring the internal dimensions of the casting are correct.

2. **Support Against Buoyancy:** It resists the **upward buoyant force** exerted by the molten metal on the lighter core.

3. **Resistance to Metal-Static Pressure:** It withstands the lateral and vertical hydrostatic pressure of the molten metal without displacement or core deflection.

4. **Gas Vent Path:** The core print recess often doubles as a gas vent, allowing gases from the core to escape upward into the cope.

> **KTU Note:** A core print is **not visible** in the final casting — it is a manufacturing-support feature only.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Functions (any three): 2 marks

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — KTU ESE Pattern**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

> **Q: With neat sketches, explain the following core-making processes:**
> **(a) Shell core process** (7 marks)
> **(b) Cold-box (Ashland) process** (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Shell Core Process (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The shell process (also called the **Croning process** or **hot shell process**) is a mechanized core-making method in which a **thermosetting phenolic resin-coated sand** is dropped onto a heated metal pattern (typically at **250 – 300 °C**). The heat melts the resin coating, causing the sand grains to bond together into a hard shell, which is then ejected and baked to final cure.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Resin-Coating of Sand:** Dry silica sand is pre-coated with **2 – 4 % novolac phenolic resin** and a small amount of hexamethylenetetramine (hexa) hardener in a hot muller. The coated sand is cooled and stored.

2. **Pattern Heating:** A metal pattern (core box) is heated to **250 – 300 °C**.

3. **Sand Dumping:** The resin-coated sand is dumped onto the heated pattern, often with a dwell time of **15 – 30 seconds**.

4. **Shell Formation:** A shell of bonded sand of thickness **5 – 25 mm** forms on the pattern surface. The thickness is controlled by dump time and pattern temperature.

5. **Inversion and Excess Removal:** The pattern is inverted to dump the un-bonded sand back into the hopper.

6. **Curing with Ejector Pins:** The shell is held on the pattern with ejector pins for another **30 – 60 seconds** for full cure.

7. **Stripping:** The hardened shell is stripped from the pattern using ejector pins.

8. **Assembly:** Two halves (or more) of the shell are joined using adhesive or by hot-plate welding to form a closed core.

**Advantages:**
- High dimensional accuracy (± 0.25 mm)
- Excellent surface finish
- Long storage life of cores
- High strength (3.0 – 4.5 MPa)
- Suitable for mass production

**Disadvantages:**
- High initial pattern cost
- Phenol emissions
- Limited to thin-section cores (thickness < 30 mm economical)

#### Part (b) — Cold-Box (Ashland) Process (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The **Cold-Box process** (developed by Ashland Chemical Company in 1968) is a rapid core-making method that cures sand cores at **room temperature** using a **gaseous tertiary amine catalyst** (typically **triethylamine, TEA**, or **dimethylethylamine, DMEA**) passed through the sand-binder mixture. The process is the industry standard for high-volume iron and steel castings.

**The Three-Part Binder System:**

| Part | Composition | Function |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Part I** | Phenolic resin (alkaline) | Reactive partner |
| **Part II** | Polyisocyanate (MDI-based) | Reactive partner |
| **Part III** | Gaseous tertiary amine (TEA/DMEA) | Catalyst/cure agent |

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Sand Pre-Mixing:** Dry silica sand (AFS 50 – 70) is mixed with **Part I (phenolic resin, 1.5 %)** and **Part II (isocyanate, 1.5 %)** in a continuous muller for 1 – 2 minutes.

2. **Blowing into Core Box:** The sand-binder mix is blown into a sealed core box using compressed air (0.4 – 0.6 MPa).

3. **Amine Gassing:** Gaseous **tertiary amine (TEA vapor)** is purged through the core box for **5 – 15 seconds**. The amine catalyses the polycondensation of phenolic resin and isocyanate, forming a **rigid polyurethane-urea network**.

4. **Air Purge:** Excess amine vapor is purged from the box using clean air (10 – 15 s) — this is critical for **operator safety and environmental compliance**.

5. **Stripping:** The core is stripped from the box — it is **fully cured and ready for immediate use**.

6. **Cure Time:** Total cycle = **15 – 30 seconds per core**.

**Advantages:**
- Extremely fast cure (room temperature)
- Excellent dimensional accuracy
- High strength (2.5 – 4.0 MPa)
- Long bench life of sand mix (1 – 3 hours)
- No ovens required → energy savings

**Disadvantages:**
- Amine vapor is toxic and smelly
- Requires amine scrubber for environmental compliance
- Equipment cost is high

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Cold-Box Process:**
> 1. Students often confuse **"cold-box" with "CO₂ process"** — they are **not the same**. Cold-box uses organic amine + phenolic urethane; CO₂ uses sodium silicate + CO₂ gas.
> 2. Students forget to mention the **three-part binder system** — losing 2 marks.
> 3. Students miss the **air-purge step** — losing 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> **Q: (a) Explain the desirable properties of core sands in detail. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Describe the CO₂ sodium silicate process for core making with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Desirable Properties of Core Sands (7 Marks)

A core sand is a special sand mix used exclusively for making cores — it is generally **finer, drier, and more strongly bonded** than the sand used for the mould body. The following are the **seven** desirable properties of a good core sand:

1. **High Refractoriness:** The sand must withstand the pouring temperature of the molten metal (up to **1600 °C** for steel, **1400 °C** for cast iron) without fusion. This requires **high silica content (SiO₂ > 95 %)** and minimum fluxing impurities (FeO, Na₂O, K₂O).

2. **Adequate Green Strength:** Strength in the unbaked state to allow handling of the green core from the box to the oven. Typical green compressive strength: **0.035 – 0.07 MPa**.

3. **Adequate Dry Strength:** Strength after baking/cure. Typical dry tensile strength: **1.0 – 3.5 MPa**.

4. **High Permeability:** The sand must allow gases to escape. AFS permeability number of **80 – 150** is typical for core sands.

5. **Good Collapsibility:** After the casting has cooled, the core must disintegrate readily to avoid hot-tearing. This is sometimes called "**friability**."

6. **Good Surface Finish:** Fine-grain sand (low GFN) provides smoother casting surfaces. Typically **AFS GFN 60 – 90** for cores.

7. **Long Storage Life:** Cured cores must retain their strength for several days to weeks without degradation. This requires the binder to be chemically stable.

#### Part (b) — CO₂ Sodium Silicate Process (7 Marks)

**Principle:** When **sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃)** is mixed with sand and exposed to **carbon dioxide (CO₂) gas**, a chemical gelation reaction occurs that hardens the sand mass almost instantaneously at room temperature.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \ \text{(silica gel)}$$

The silica gel acts as a hard binder, cementing the sand grains together.

**Procedure:**

1. **Mixing:** Dry silica sand is mixed with **3 – 5 % sodium silicate (water glass)** and a small amount of moisture (2 – 3 %) in a muller for 2 – 3 minutes.

2. **Ramming/Blowing:** The mix is either hand-rammed or blown into a core box.

3. **Gassing:** The core box is closed, and **CO₂ gas at 0.2 – 0.4 MPa pressure** is passed through the sand for **10 – 60 seconds**.

4. **Hardening:** The CO₂ reacts with the sodium silicate to form a hard silica gel, and the core hardens within seconds.

5. **Stripping:** The core is stripped from the box and is immediately ready for use — **no oven required**.

**Diagram (Ascii Schematic):**

```
   CO2 Gas Inlet ───────►  |▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓|  ───────► CO2 Gas Outlet
                            |    CORE BOX      |
                            |  Sand + Na2SiO3  |
                            |  [Hardening Zone]|
                            |__________________|
                                     │
                                     ▼
                              Cured Core Ready
                              for Mould Assembly
```

**Advantages of CO₂ Process:**

- No oven required → low energy cost
- Fast cure (10 – 60 s)
- High strength (1.5 – 3.0 MPa)
- Low capital investment
- No toxic emissions (environmentally clean)

**Disadvantages:**

- Poor collapsibility (silica gel is brittle) → causes hot-tearing in some castings
- Moisture-sensitive — cores can soften in humid storage
- Limited storage life (typically 1 – 2 weeks)
- Difficult shake-out for intricate cores

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — CO₂ Process:**
> 1. Writing the **chemical equation** is **mandatory** in a 7-mark question — students who omit it lose **2 marks**.
> 2. The reaction is **gelation, NOT polymerization** — do not confuse it with resin cures.
> 3. Mention **storage life problem** as a disadvantage — examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Rapid Revision Checklist for "Core Making and Core Materials"**

### A. Core Concepts (Must-Know)
- [ ] **Core Definition:** Preformed sand insert for internal cavities in castings.
- [ ] **Five Mandatory Properties:** Refractoriness, Strength, Permeability, Collapsibility, Storage Life.
- [ ] **Core Print:** Anchoring extension that rests in mould recess.
- [ ] **Buoyancy Force Formula:** $F_{\text{net}} = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$.

### B. Materials (Must-Know)
- [ ] **Base:** Silica sand (SiO₂ > 95 %), AFS GFN 60 – 80.
- [ ] **Binders — Organic:** Cereal, Oil, Furan resin, Phenolic resin, Phenolic urethane.
- [ ] **Binders — Inorganic:** Sodium silicate (water glass).
- [ ] **Additives:** Cereal flour, dextrin, iron oxide, refractory wash (zircon, graphite, magnesia).

### C. Processes (Must-Know)
- [ ] **Oil-Sand Process:** 200 – 250 °C, 1 – 4 hours (classical).
- [ ] **Shell Process:** 250 – 300 °C, 30 – 60 seconds, phenolic resin-coated sand.
- [ ] **Hot-Box Process:** 200 – 250 °C, 30 – 90 seconds, furan resin + acid catalyst.
- [ ] **Cold-Box (Ashland) Process:** Room temperature, 10 – 30 s, phenolic urethane + amine gas (three-part binder).
- [ ] **CO₂ Process:** Room temperature, 10 – 60 s, sodium silicate + CO₂.

### D. Numerical Problem Toolkit
- [ ] Buoyancy force calculation: $F = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$
- [ ] Core-print area: $A = F_{\text{net}} / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ × Safety Factor (2 – 3)
- [ ] Permeability: $P = V \cdot h / (A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P)$
- [ ] Grain Fineness Number (GFN): weighted average sieve size
- [ ] Baking time rule: $T_{\text{bake}} \propto t_{\text{core}}^{1.5}$

### E. Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do **not** confuse the **Cold-Box** process with the **CO₂** process.
- [ ] Always state the **chemical equation** in the CO₂ process question.
- [ ] Always include the **safety factor** in core-print area calculations.
- [ ] Always convert **cm³ to m³** in buoyancy calculations.
- [ ] Do **not** write "permeability and strength" as the only two properties — there are **five**.

### F. Industrial Application (Real-World Linkage)
- [ ] **Engine blocks** → Cold-Box cores (high volume).
- [ ] **Aerospace investment castings** → Ceramic cores (high temp).
- [ ] **Pipe fittings** → Oil-sand cores (low volume, low cost).
- [ ] **Cylinder heads** → Shell cores (thin sections, accuracy).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** If asked to compare two core processes, always structure your answer in the format: **(1) Working Principle, (2) Step-by-Step Procedure, (3) Advantages, (4) Disadvantages, (5) Typical Applications.** This 5-point structure is the KTU board-exam gold standard and guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps in the Sand Casting Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps in the Sand Casting Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Sand Casting** is an expendable-mould, gravity-fed metal forming process in which a molten metal is poured into a one-time-use sand-based mould cavity, allowed to solidify, and subsequently removed to obtain a near-net-shape metallic component. It is classified under the broad family of **foundry processes** and is the most widely used casting method globally, accounting for over **70%** of all cast metal products by mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCPET402 Module 1):**
> Students are expected to describe, sketch, and sequence the **nine fundamental steps** of the sand casting process, identify the materials used (silica sand, binders, parting agents, refractories), and relate each step to its impact on final casting quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (must use exact KTU phrasing):**
> - **Pattern:** A replica of the part to be cast, slightly oversized to account for shrinkage.
> - **Mould:** The cavity formed in compacted sand that defines the external shape of the casting.
> - **Core:** A sand insert placed inside the mould to create internal cavities or hollow features.
> - **Cope & Drag:** The upper and lower halves of the sand mould.
> - **Sprue / Runner / Riser:** Channels that deliver molten metal and feed shrinkage during solidification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a child on the beach building a **sand castle** using a bucket. The child:

1. Takes a **bucket (pattern)**.
2. Presses it into wet sand (creating a **mould cavity**).
3. Removes the bucket.
4. Fills the cavity with something (in casting, this is **molten metal**).
5. Waits for it to set (**solidification**).
6. Digs out the hardened shape (**casting removal**).

Sand casting is the industrial, high-temperature, metallurgical version of this exact process — except the "sand" is carefully engineered with **binders (clay or chemical resins)**, and the "something" is **molten metal at temperatures up to 1500 °C** for steels.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Bold) |
|---|---|
| Pouring temperature of grey cast iron | **1250–1400 °C** |
| Pouring temperature of aluminium alloys | **680–750 °C** |
| Pattern allowance (shrinkage for cast iron) | **1% (10 mm per metre)** |
| Sand grain size (AFS number) | **50–100** |
| Moisture content in green sand | **3–6%** |
| Permeability of moulding sand | **80–200** (AFS units) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Steps of Sand Casting — Structured Logic

The complete process is conventionally divided into **nine sequential steps**. Each step must be understood in terms of three questions: **Why is it done? How is it done? What happens if it is done incorrectly?**

### STEP 1 — Pattern Making

**Why?** A precise replica of the final part is needed. Patterns are made slightly larger to compensate for **shrinkage** (the metal contracts as it cools from solidification temperature to room temperature).

**How?** Patterns are typically manufactured from **wood (fir, mahogany), metals (aluminium, cast iron)**, or **plastics / polymers (epoxy, polystyrene)**. They include **shrinkage allowance, draft allowance, machining allowance, and parting line compensation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Shrinkage allowance (cast iron):** ~1.0%
> - **Shrinkage allowance (steel):** ~2.0%
> - **Shrinkage allowance (aluminium):** ~1.5%
> - **Draft (taper) allowance:** 1°–3° on vertical walls for mould release.

### STEP 2 — Core Making (if internal cavities are required)

**Why?** The external shape of a casting is created by the mould halves (cope and drag), but **internal cavities, holes, or undercuts** cannot be formed by simply compacting sand. A pre-shaped hardened sand insert — called a **core** — is required.

**How?** Cores are made separately by **ramming a sand–binder mixture** (typically silica sand + **linseed oil**, **furan resin**, or **sodium silicate (CO₂ process)**) into a **core box**, then curing the binder to harden it. Cores are supported in the mould cavity by **core prints** (extensions on the pattern that leave recesses in the mould for core placement).

> [!NOTE]
> Cores are baked at **150–250 °C** for **30–120 minutes** depending on binder chemistry. They must be **porous enough to allow gas escape** (else gas porosity defects form in the casting).

### STEP 3 — Mould Preparation (Sand Mould Making)

**Why?** The mould must accurately reproduce the external geometry of the part, withstand the metallostatic pressure of molten metal, and allow gases to escape.

**How?**
1. **Moulding sand preparation:** Silica sand (95%+ SiO₂) is mixed with:
   - **Binder (5–10% bentonite clay for green-sand moulds)**
   - **Moisture (3–6% water for activation of bentonite)**
   - **Additives (coal dust, sea coal) to improve surface finish and prevent sand burn-on**
2. **Moulding box placement:** The **flask** (a two-part steel frame) is placed on a **moulding board**.
3. **Pattern placement:** The pattern is positioned on the board with the parting surface down, dusted with **parting compound (chalk, talc, or silica flour)**.
4. **Sand ramming:** Moulding sand is poured over the pattern and **rammed uniformly** using a **hand rammer or pneumatic rammer** to a uniform hardness (typically **85–95 on a Brinell-type mould hardness tester**).
5. **Slicking and venting:** The mould is vented by piercing small holes with a **vent wire (1–3 mm diameter)** to permit gas escape.

### STEP 4 — Core Placement & Assembly

The baked core is carefully placed into the **core prints** of the drag half. Positioning must be exact; any misalignment causes **core shift defects** and dimensional inaccuracy.

### STEP 5 — Closing (Clamping) the Mould

The **cope (upper flask)** is inverted and placed over the **drag (lower flask)**. The two halves are aligned using **dowel pins (aligning pins)** and **clamped** to prevent **flashing** (metal escape) during pouring. The **gating system (sprue, runner, ingates)** and **riser** are arranged in the cope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gating system** must achieve:
> - Smooth filling (no splashing → no **sand erosion defects**)
> - Adequate temperature control
> - Effective **slag/dross trapping** via strainer cores or skimmers

### STEP 6 — Pouring

**Why?** Liquid metal must be transferred from the furnace (cupola, induction furnace, crucible furnace) into the mould cavity.

**How?** Done manually with a **hand ladle** or automatically with a **pouring ladle/bucket**. The pouring rate must be **controlled** — too slow causes **misruns** (premature freezing), too fast causes **erosion of the mould wall** and **gas evolution**.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard pouring time formula: $t_p = K \cdot \sqrt{W}$ seconds, where $W$ is the casting mass in **kg**, and $K$ is an empirical constant (typically **1.0–1.5** for thin sections, **1.5–2.0** for thick sections).

### STEP 7 — Solidification & Cooling

**Why?** The molten metal must release its **latent heat of fusion** and contract to form a solid casting. The shrinkage that occurs during this phase must be compensated by the **riser (feeder)**, which supplies reserve molten metal to the casting.

**How?** The casting is left in the mould until it has cooled below the **solidus temperature** of the alloy. The cooling time is governed by **Chvorinov's Rule** (see Section 3).

### STEP 8 — Mould Shake-Out (Casting Removal)

**Why?** The mould is expendable and must be broken to retrieve the casting.

**How?** Once the casting has cooled sufficiently (to avoid **thermal shock cracks**), the mould is broken apart by **manual knocking** or by mechanical **shake-out grids / vibrators**. The sand is either **reclaimed** (via magnetic separation, attrition) or **discarded** (in the case of chemically bonded moulds).

### STEP 9 — Cleaning, Fettling & Finishing

The casting is subjected to:
1. **Shot blasting / sand blasting** to remove adhered sand particles.
2. **Gate and riser removal** by **cutting, chipping, sawing**, or **arc-air / plasma cutting**.
3. **Grinding** to remove flash and surface defects.
4. **Heat treatment** (annealing, normalizing) for stress relief.
5. **Inspection** (visual, dimensional, NDT such as radiography, ultrasonic).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | Chvorinov's solidification time | $t_s$ = solidification time (s); $V$ = volume (m³); $A$ = surface area (m²); $B$ = mould constant (s·m⁻⁴) |
| $t_p = K \cdot \sqrt{W}$ | Pouring time (Chesters) | $t_p$ = pouring time (s); $W$ = casting mass (kg); $K$ = empirical constant (s/√kg) |
| $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot S$ | Shrinkage allowance | $\Delta L$ = dimensional increase on pattern; $L_0$ = nominal dimension; $S$ = shrinkage factor |
| $A_{sprue} = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot v \cdot t_p}$ | Sprue cross-sectional area | $A_{sprue}$ (m²); $\rho$ = density of metal (kg/m³); $v$ = pour velocity (m/s) |
| $R_{riser} \ge 1.2 \cdot R_{casting}$ | Modulus rule for riser design | $R = V/A$ (m); riser must solidify last |
| $\text{Permeability} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{fines content}}$ | Sand permeability | Higher fines → lower permeability → more gas defects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use vertical bars inside table cells** — this is a KTU formatting safety rule. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX instead of $\vert$ when needed in inline math contexts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sand casting is the **backbone of heavy engineering manufacturing**. It is used to produce:
- **Automotive engine blocks, cylinder heads, and gearbox housings** (cast iron, aluminium).
- **Pump and valve bodies** (bronze, stainless steel).
- **Railway bogies and rolling stock components**.
- **Turbine housings, wind turbine hubs** (multi-tonne castings).
- **Manhole covers, pipe fittings, agricultural machinery parts**.

Its key advantage is **low tooling cost** and the ability to produce **large, complex geometries** that are uneconomical by forging or machining.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time)

Chvorinov's Rule is the single most important equation in casting design. It governs the **directional solidification principle**.

### Step 1 — Heat Flow Assumption

Consider a small solidification shell advancing inward at velocity $v_s$. The rate of latent heat released per unit area at the solidification front equals the rate of heat conducted into the surrounding mould:

$$
q = \rho \cdot L \cdot v_s = \frac{k_m (T_m - T_0)}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_m t}}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal (kg/m³)
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $v_s$ = velocity of the solidification front (m/s)
- $k_m$ = thermal conductivity of the mould (W/m·K)
- $T_m$ = solidification temperature of metal (K)
- $T_0$ = initial mould temperature (K)
- $\alpha_m$ = thermal diffusivity of mould (m²/s)
- $t$ = time elapsed (s)

### Step 2 — Integrate to Find Total Solidification Time

The solidification front moves a total distance equal to the characteristic half-thickness $D = V/A$ (where $V$ is the casting volume and $A$ is its surface area). Integrating the velocity equation with respect to time:

$$
\int_0^{t_s} dt = \int_0^{V/A} \frac{\rho L \sqrt{\pi \alpha_m t}}{k_m (T_m - T_0)} \, dx
$$

After integration and rearranging:

$$
t_s = \frac{\pi \rho^2 L^2}{4 k_m^2 (T_m - T_0)^2} \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2
$$

### Step 3 — Combine Constants

The entire coefficient is grouped into a single mould constant $B$:

$$
\boxed{t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2}
$$

This is **Chvorinov's Rule** in its final form. The constant $B$ depends on:
- **Mould material** (sand mould $B \approx 600$ s/m², die casting $B \approx 30$ s/m²)
- **Pouring superheat**
- **Alloy thermal properties**

### Step 4 — Logical Interpretation

The ratio $V/A$ is called the **modulus** of the casting. The rule states:

> *"A casting with a higher modulus takes longer to solidify."*

This has a direct engineering consequence: **the riser must have a higher modulus than the casting** so that the casting solidifies first and the riser can feed shrinkage. This is the **directional solidification principle** introduced by KTU Module 1.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A cast iron block has dimensions **0.3 m × 0.2 m × 0.1 m**. Using mould constant $B = 600$ s/m²:

Volume:
$$
V = 0.3 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.006 \text{ m}^3
$$

Surface area:
$$
A = 2(0.3 \times 0.2 + 0.2 \times 0.1 + 0.3 \times 0.1) = 2(0.06 + 0.02 + 0.03) = 0.22 \text{ m}^2
$$

Modulus:
$$
M = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.006}{0.22} \approx 0.02727 \text{ m}
$$

Solidification time:
$$
t_s = 600 \times (0.02727)^2 \approx 600 \times 0.000744 = 0.446 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Result interpretation: ~0.45 s for the casting skin to fully solidify. The riser must be designed with a modulus at least **20% higher** → riser $M_{riser} \geq 0.0327$ m.

## 3.2 Riser Sizing Derivation (Riser Modulus Rule)

A cylindrical riser of diameter $D_r$ and height $H_r$ must satisfy $M_{riser} \geq 1.2 M_{casting}$.

For a cylinder (side surface + top + bottom, ignoring bottom contact with casting if it's covered):

$$
M_{riser} = \frac{\frac{\pi D_r^2}{4} \cdot H_r}{\pi D_r H_r + \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4}}
$$

For an optimal cylinder with $H_r = 1.5 D_r$ (industry rule of thumb), substituting:

$$
M_{riser} = \frac{\frac{\pi D_r^2}{4} \cdot 1.5 D_r}{\pi D_r (1.5 D_r) + \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4}} = \frac{1.5 D_r^3 / 4}{1.5 \pi D_r^2 + \pi D_r^2 / 4}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
M_{riser} = \frac{1.5 D_r}{4 \cdot (1.5 + 0.25)} = \frac{1.5 D_r}{4 \times 1.75} = \frac{1.5 D_r}{7} \approx 0.214 D_r
$$

Therefore the required riser diameter is:

$$
D_r \geq \frac{1.2 \times M_{casting}}{0.214} \approx 5.6 \times M_{casting}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why, in KTU board problems, riser diameters are often specified as **4–6 times** the casting modulus.

## 3.3 Pouring Time Derivation (Bernoulli's Principle Applied to Sprue)

Using **Bernoulli's equation** between the pouring basin (point 1) and the sprue base (point 2):

$$
h_1 = h_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + \text{losses}
$$

For ideal flow (losses neglected), the theoretical pour velocity is:

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

where $h$ is the sprue height. Actual velocity is reduced by a **discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.6–0.9$**:

$$
v_{actual} = C_d \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

Required sprue cross-sectional area to pour mass $W$ in time $t_p$:

$$
A_{sprue} = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot C_d \sqrt{2 g h} \cdot t_p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> To prevent aspiration of gases into the sprue, the sprue is designed with a **tapered (downward-narrowing)** shape, keeping the metal in contact with the sprue walls at all times — this is the **unpressurized gating system** rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flowchart — The Nine Steps of Sand Casting

The diagram below provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the entire sand casting process, mapped from raw materials to finished casting. This is the most efficient way to visualize a sequence-based manufacturing process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Pattern Making] --> B[Step 2: Core Making]
    B --> C[Step 3: Mould Preparation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Core Placement in Drag]
    D --> E[Step 5: Closing the Mould Cope and Drag]
    E --> F[Step 6: Pouring Molten Metal]
    F --> G[Step 7: Solidification and Cooling]
    G --> H[Step 8: Shake Out and Casting Removal]
    H --> I[Step 9: Cleaning Fettling and Inspection]
    I --> J[Finished Casting]

    A1[Wood Metal or Plastic Pattern] -.-> A
    B1[Core Box and Sand Binder Mix] -.-> B
    C1[Silica Sand Bentonite Water Additives] -.-> C
    F1[Furnace Ladle Gating System] -.-> F
    I1[Shot Blast Grinding NDT] -.-> I

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style I fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style J fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mould Assembly Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

The schematic below represents the **assembled mould** with all functional components labelled. This replaces a hand-drawn cross-section in KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASSEMBLED_MOULD
        COP[COPE - Upper Flask]
        DRAG[DRAG - Lower Flask]
        PAT[PATTERN CAVITY - Shape of Final Part]
        CORE[CORE - Internal Cavity Former]
        SPRUE[SPRUE - Vertical Pouring Channel]
        RUN[RUNNER - Horizontal Channel]
        GATE[INGATE - Entry to Cavity]
        RIS[RISER - Feeder for Shrinkage]
        VENT[VENT HOLES - Gas Escape]
    end

    COP --- DRAG
    DRAG --- CORE
    DRAG --- PAT
    COP --- SPRUE
    COP --- RUN
    COP --- GATE
    COP --- RIS
    COP --- VENT
    SPRUE --> RUN
    RUN --> GATE
    GATE --> PAT
    RIS --> PAT
    VENT --> PAT
    PAT --> CORE

    style COP fill:#4682B4,color:#FFF
    style DRAG fill:#4682B4,color:#FFF
    style PAT fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style CORE fill:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style SPRUE fill:#FF4500,color:#FFF
    style RUN fill:#FF4500,color:#FFF
    style GATE fill:#FF4500,color:#FFF
    style RIS fill:#32CD32,color:#000
    style VENT fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Input → Output Matrix

| **Step** | **Inputs** | **Equipment** | **Outputs** | **Quality Risk if Mishandled** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Pattern Making | Wood / metal / plastic | Pattern shop, CNC | Accurate pattern | Dimensional error, shrinkage mismatch |
| 2. Core Making | Sand + binder | Core box, oven | Hardened core | Core collapse, gas porosity |
| 3. Mould Preparation | Sand, clay, water | Moulding box, rammer | Rammed sand mould | Lamination, low permeability |
| 4. Core Placement | Core | Hand tools | Positioned core | Core shift, dimensional error |
| 5. Closing | Cope + drag | Clamps, dowels | Sealed mould | Flash, sand inclusions |
| 6. Pouring | Molten metal | Ladle, furnace | Filled cavity | Misrun, oxidation, cold shut |
| 7. Solidification | Filled mould | Ambient / controlled | Solid casting | Shrinkage porosity, hot tears |
| 8. Shake-out | Solidified casting | Vibrator, hammer | Raw casting | Surface damage, cracks |
| 9. Cleaning | Raw casting | Grinder, blast cabinet | Finished casting | Inclusions, surface defects |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List and briefly explain any six steps involved in the sand casting process.**

**Model Answer:**

The six steps of sand casting are:

1. **Pattern Making:** A wooden or metal replica of the casting is made, with allowances for shrinkage, draft, and machining.
2. **Mould Preparation:** Silica sand mixed with bentonite and water is rammed around the pattern in a flask to form the mould.
3. **Core Placement:** A baked sand core is placed in the drag to form internal cavities.
4. **Closing the Mould:** The cope is placed on the drag, aligned using dowel pins, and clamped.
5. **Pouring:** Molten metal is poured into the sprue at a controlled rate, filling the cavity.
6. **Shake-out & Cleaning:** After solidification, the mould is broken, the casting is removed, gates are cut, and the surface is cleaned by grinding or shot blasting.

*(Examiner tip: Students often skip mentioning "allowances" on the pattern. Always state shrinkage and draft allowance explicitly.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is a moulding flask? Distinguish between green sand mould and dry sand mould.**

**Model Answer:**

A **moulding flask** is a rigid steel (or wooden) frame that contains and supports the moulding sand around the pattern during ramming. It consists of two halves — the **cope (upper)** and the **drag (lower)**.

| **Property** | **Green Sand Mould** | **Dry Sand Mould** |
|---|---|---|
| Binder moisture | 3–6% water with bentonite | Baked at 200–300 °C to remove moisture |
| Strength | Lower | Higher |
| Permeability | Higher | Lower |
| Surface finish | Rougher | Smoother |
| Dimensional accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Applications | Large castings, low precision | Thin sections, intricate castings |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) With neat sketches, describe the nine steps involved in the sand casting process.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A steel casting of dimensions 200 mm × 150 mm × 75 mm is to be made in a sand mould. Calculate the solidification time. Given $B = 600$ s/m² for sand mould.** *(7 marks)*

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the functions of pattern, core, sprue, runner, gate, and riser in a sand casting mould.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A cylindrical aluminium riser of diameter 75 mm and height 100 mm is to be designed for a casting having a modulus of 0.018 m. Check whether the riser is adequate. Given $B_{aluminium-riser} = 150$ s/m², $B_{casting} = 300$ s/m².** *(7 marks)*

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Solution A(a) — Nine Steps of Sand Casting (7 marks)

The nine steps are:

**Step 1: Pattern Making** — A pattern is a replica of the final part, made of wood, metal, or plastic. It is oversized by the **shrinkage allowance** (2% for steel, 1% for cast iron, 1.5% for aluminium) and includes a **draft allowance (1–3°)** for easy withdrawal from the sand. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Core Making** — A core is a separate sand mass bonded with oil or resin, baked in a core box. It creates internal cavities, holes, or undercuts in the casting. Cores are supported by **core prints** in the mould. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Mould Preparation** — Moulding sand (silica + bentonite + 3–6% water + coal dust) is filled into the flask and rammed uniformly around the pattern. A parting compound is dusted to prevent sand sticking to the pattern. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Core Placement** — The baked core is carefully lowered into the core prints in the drag. Vents are pierced to allow gas escape. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 5: Closing the Mould** — The cope (containing the gating system) is placed over the drag, aligned with dowel pins, and clamped. The parting line is sealed to prevent metal escape. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 6: Pouring** — Molten metal is poured into the sprue at the correct rate and temperature. Pouring time $t_p = K \sqrt{W}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 7: Solidification** — The casting cools, releasing latent heat. The riser feeds the shrinkage. Chvorinov's rule applies: $t_s = B(V/A)^2$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 8: Shake-out** — The mould is broken after sufficient cooling. The casting is separated from the sand. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 9: Cleaning & Finishing** — Gates and risers are cut, sand is blasted off, surfaces are ground, and the casting is inspected. *(0.5 mark)*

*(Examiner tip: A neat flowchart sketch is compulsory. Marks are awarded for clear labelling of pattern, mould, core, sprue, runner, gate, riser.)*

### Solution A(b) — Solidification Time Calculation (7 marks)

Given:
- Casting dimensions: $200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} \times 75 \text{ mm}$ $= 0.20 \times 0.15 \times 0.075$ m
- Mould constant: $B = 600$ s/m²

**Step 1: Volume of casting**

$$
V = 0.20 \times 0.15 \times 0.075 = 0.00225 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Surface area of casting**

$$
A = 2(ab + bc + ac) = 2(0.20 \times 0.15 + 0.15 \times 0.075 + 0.20 \times 0.075)
$$

$$
A = 2(0.030 + 0.01125 + 0.015) = 2(0.05625) = 0.1125 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Modulus of casting**

$$
M = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.00225}{0.1125} = 0.020 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Chvorinov's Rule**

$$
t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 = 600 \times (0.020)^2
$$

$$
t_s = 600 \times 0.0004 = 0.24 \text{ s}
$$

**Result:** The solidification time is **0.24 seconds** ≈ **0.25 s**.

*(Examiner tip: Forgetting to square the modulus is the most common error. Also remember to convert mm → m before calculation.)*

### Solution B(a) — Functions of Mould Components (7 marks)

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Pattern** | Replica of the part; gives the external shape to the casting. Includes allowances for shrinkage, draft, and machining. |
| **Core** | Forms internal cavities, holes, and complex internal features that cannot be made by the mould alone. Made of baked sand with binder. |
| **Sprue** | The vertical channel through which molten metal enters the mould from the pouring basin. Must be tapered to prevent aspiration. |
| **Runner** | The horizontal channel that distributes molten metal from the sprue to the gate. Provides uniform flow distribution. |
| **Gate (Ingate)** | The entry point from the runner into the mould cavity. Controls the flow rate and filters slag via strainer cores. |
| **Riser** | An extra reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting during solidification shrinkage, ensuring a sound casting. Designed with a higher modulus than the casting. |

*(1 mark for each component × 6 = 6 marks, 1 mark for neat schematic diagram.)*

### Solution B(b) — Riser Adequacy Check (7 marks)

Given:
- Riser dimensions: diameter $D_r = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, height $H_r = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Casting modulus: $M_c = 0.018$ m
- $B_{riser} = 150$ s/m², $B_{casting} = 300$ s/m²

**Step 1: Riser volume**

$$
V_r = \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4} \cdot H_r = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} \times 0.100
$$

$$
V_r = \frac{\pi \times 0.005625}{4} \times 0.100 = 4.418 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Riser surface area (excluding bottom contact with casting)**

$$
A_r = \pi D_r H_r + \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4} = \pi(0.075)(0.100) + \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_r = 0.02356 + 0.004418 = 0.02798 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Riser modulus**

$$
M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{4.418 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02798} \approx 0.01579 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Solidification time of riser**

$$
t_{s,riser} = B_{riser} \cdot M_r^2 = 150 \times (0.01579)^2 = 150 \times 2.494 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0374 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Solidification time of casting**

$$
t_{s,casting} = B_{casting} \cdot M_c^2 = 300 \times (0.018)^2 = 300 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0972 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6: Comparison**

Since $t_{s,riser} = 0.0374$ s $< t_{s,casting} = 0.0972$ s, the riser solidifies **before** the casting.

> [!WARNING]
> **The riser is NOT adequate.** The riser modulus is **less than** the casting modulus, violating the directional solidification principle. The riser must be redesigned with a larger diameter or height so that $M_r \geq 1.2 M_c = 0.0216$ m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the bottom surface** of the riser from its cooling area (since the bottom is in contact with the casting and does not radiate heat). This is the most common error.
> 2. **Using the wrong B value** — the riser (in the mould) has a different mould constant than the casting, especially if made of insulating sleeve.
> 3. **Not converting mm to m** — the entire calculation must be in SI units.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sand Casting Process (KTU Module 1):**

- ✅ Sand casting is an **expendable mould, gravity-fed** process — the most widely used casting method.
- ✅ The **nine steps** in sequence are: Pattern → Core → Mould → Core placement → Closing → Pouring → Solidification → Shake-out → Cleaning.
- ✅ The **pattern** is oversized by **shrinkage allowance** (1% CI, 2% steel, 1.5% Al) and includes **draft** (1–3°).
- ✅ **Moulding sand** = silica sand (95%+) + bentonite (binder) + water (3–6%) + coal dust (additive).
- ✅ **Cope** = upper flask, **Drag** = lower flask, **Parting line** separates them.
- ✅ **Cores** form internal cavities; they are made separately and baked.
- ✅ The **gating system** = **Sprue** (vertical) + **Runner** (horizontal) + **Gate** (entry to cavity).
- ✅ The **riser** feeds shrinkage and must solidify **last** — its modulus must be ≥ 1.2 × casting modulus.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$ — higher modulus = longer solidification.
- ✅ **Pouring time:** $t_p = K \sqrt{W}$ (Chester's formula).
- ✅ Common defects: **misrun, cold shut, sand inclusion, gas porosity, shrinkage porosity, hot tears, flash, blowholes**.
- ✅ Always convert dimensions to **SI units (metres)** before applying Chvorinov's rule.
- ✅ **Directional solidification** = casting solidifies first, riser solidifies last.
- ✅ Module hardness tested on **Brinell-type mould hardness tester**; typical value **85–95**.
- ✅ Permeability of moulding sand controls **gas escape**; high fines = low permeability = gas defects.
- ✅ Sand reclamation is possible in **green sand systems** via magnetic separation + attrition.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Steps in the Sand Casting Process.**
> For KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations, students are expected to:
> 1. Sequence the steps correctly in 1-mark MCQs.
> 2. Sketch a labelled mould cross-section in 7-mark descriptive questions.
> 3. Solve Chvorinov's rule and riser design problems in 7-mark numerical questions.
> 4. Identify defects and root causes in 3-mark short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sand Casting** is an expendable mold casting process in which molten metal is poured into a sand-based mold cavity, allowed to solidify, and then the sand mold is broken to retrieve the casting. The mold is formed by compacting a mixture of **silica sand (SiO₂)**, **bentonite clay (binder)**, **water (moisture)**, and **additives** around a reusable pattern. Sand casting is classified as a **transient (non-permanent) mold process** because the mold is destroyed after each casting cycle.

The process belongs to the family of **foundry casting processes** and is governed by standards such as **ASTM A957** (for steel and alloy castings) and **IS 1395** (Indian Standard for foundry sands). The minimum draft allowance is typically **1° to 3°**, and achievable tolerances fall within **±0.8 mm to ±2.5 mm** for small castings under standard conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Casting Processes):** Sand casting is the foundational process from which all other casting methods are comparatively evaluated. Students must be able to justify process selection by listing specific advantages (when sand casting is preferred) and disadvantages (when alternative methods like die casting or investment casting are favored).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **clay cupcake** (jelly mold). Here is the analogy:

| Step | Cupcake Analogy | Sand Casting Equivalent |
|------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | You take a plastic cupcake shape (the form) | A wooden/metal pattern is made |
| 2 | You press wet sand/soil around it in a cup | Sand + binder is packed around the pattern in a flask |
| 3 | You gently remove the cupcake shape, leaving a cavity | The pattern is withdrawn, leaving a mold cavity |
| 4 | You pour hot chocolate into the cavity | Molten metal is poured into the cavity |
| 5 | You wait for it to cool, then break the sand to release | The casting is shaken out after solidification |
| 6 | You throw away the broken sand cup | Sand is reclaimed or discarded |

The **key takeaway**: the mold is **destroyed** every time (unlike metal molds in die casting), which gives sand casting its unique cost and flexibility profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter?** The fact that the mold is destroyed each cycle makes sand casting **cheap for one-off or low-volume production** but **slower for mass production** compared to permanent-mold processes.

---

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

- Sand casting accounts for approximately **60% of all metal castings produced globally** by weight.
- Used across automotive, aerospace, pumps, valves, and heavy machinery industries.
- Cost of pattern: typically **5%–15%** of total casting cost.
- The process can cast parts ranging from a few grams (jewelry) to over **200 tonnes** (steel mill housings).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Sand Casting Process

The process flow consists of **eight sequential steps**:

1. **Pattern Making** – A replica of the final part (with allowances for shrinkage, draft, and machining) is fabricated, usually from wood, metal, or plastic.
2. **Mold Preparation** – Green sand (a moist mixture of silica sand, bentonite 4%–10%, water 2%–8%, and additives like coal dust) is rammed around the pattern in a two-part flask (cope and drag).
3. **Pattern Withdrawal** – The pattern is carefully withdrawn, leaving the cavity.
4. **Core Placement** – If internal cavities are required, pre-formed sand cores are positioned in the drag.
5. **Mold Assembly** – The cope is placed over the drag and clamped.
6. **Pouring** – Molten metal at the appropriate superheat temperature is poured into the sprue.
7. **Solidification & Cooling** – The metal solidifies inside the cavity, governed by **Chvorinov's Rule**: $t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2$.
8. **Shake-out, Cleaning & Inspection** – The sand mold is broken, the casting is removed, runners/risers are cut off, and finishing operations are performed.

> **Where:** $t_s$ = solidification time (s), $V$ = volume of casting (m³), $A$ = surface area (m²), $B$ = mold constant (s/m²).

---

## 2.2 Comprehensive Advantages of Sand Casting

### A. Economic Advantages
1. **Low Tooling Cost**: Pattern cost is significantly lower (often **10x cheaper**) than dies for die casting or investment casting.
2. **Low Capital Investment**: A basic sand-casting foundry can be set up with minimal infrastructure; no high-tonnage presses or hydraulic clamps are required.
3. **Recyclability of Sand**: Used sand (reclaimed sand) can be reprocessed and reused, reducing raw material cost.
4. **Suitable for All Production Volumes**: Effective for one-off prototypes, small batches, and medium-volume runs.
5. **Short Lead Time**: Patterns can be fabricated in days, enabling rapid prototyping.

### B. Design Flexibility
6. **Wide Size Range**: From **few grams to over 200 tonnes** — almost no practical size limitation.
7. **Complex Geometry**: Internal cavities, undercuts, and intricate external features can be produced using cores and parting lines.
8. **Wide Material Compatibility**: Ferrous (cast iron, steel), non-ferrous (aluminum, copper, brass, bronze), and even exotic alloys can be cast.
9. **Design Modifications are Easy**: Patterns can be modified quickly without rebuilding expensive tooling.

### C. Technical Advantages
10. **High Strength-to-Weight Capability**: Large castings (e.g., machine tool beds) with excellent rigidity can be produced in one piece.
11. **Can Cast Both Thin and Thick Sections**: Wall thicknesses from **3 mm to several hundred mm** are achievable.
12. **No Limitation on Metallurgical Composition**: Any castable alloy can be melted and poured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Sand casting is the only process that economically produces very large castings (e.g., turbine housings, ship propellers, crusher frames) where the cost of a permanent mold would be prohibitive.

---

## 2.3 Comprehensive Disadvantages of Sand Casting

### A. Quality Limitations
1. **Poor Dimensional Accuracy**: Typical tolerance of **±0.8 mm to ±2.5 mm**, far inferior to die casting (**±0.05 mm**) or investment casting (**±0.1 mm**).
2. **Poor Surface Finish**: Surface roughness typically **Ra = 12.5–25 µm**, requiring extensive post-machining.
3. **Internal Defects**: Susceptible to **blowholes, sand inclusions, porosity, hot tears, and misruns** due to gas evolution and mold interactions.

### B. Production Limitations
4. **Slower Production Rate**: Each cycle requires mold making, pouring, cooling, and shake-out — incompatible with high-volume mass production.
5. **Labor-Intensive**: Manual ramming, pattern withdrawal, and finishing demand skilled operators.
6. **Pattern Wear**: Wooden patterns degrade after 100–200 uses, requiring frequent replacement.

### C. Environmental & Health Concerns
7. **Silica Dust Hazard**: Inhalation of crystalline silica causes **silicosis** (an occupational lung disease).
8. **High Energy Consumption for Sand Reclamation**: Drying and thermal reclamation of used sand is energy-intensive.
9. **Noise and Emissions**: Pouring fumes and shake-out noise contribute to environmental load.

### D. Post-Processing Burden
10. **High Machining Allowance Required**: Castings often require **3–6 mm** stock removal on critical surfaces.
11. **Cleaning & Fettling Required**: Sprues, risers, and gates must be cut off and ground.
12. **Limited Tolerance for Thin Sections**: Sections below **3 mm** are prone to misrun defects.

---

## 2.4 KTU Reference Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Mold Type | Expendable (Sand) | Permanent (Steel) | Expendable (Ceramic) |
| Tolerance | ±0.8 to ±2.5 mm | ±0.05 to ±0.15 mm | ±0.1 to ±0.5 mm |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | 12.5–25 µm | 1–2.5 µm | 1.6–6.3 µm |
| Production Rate | Low (10–50 pcs/hr) | Very High (100–500 pcs/hr) | Medium (5–50 pcs/hr) |
| Tooling Cost | **Low** | Very High | High |
| Material Range | All metals | Non-ferrous only | All metals |
| Min. Wall Thickness | 3 mm | 1 mm | 0.5 mm |
| Max. Casting Weight | Up to 200+ tonnes | Up to 25 kg | Up to 50 kg |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive**: Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings.
- **Aerospace**: Large structural castings, turbine housings.
- **General Engineering**: Pump bodies, valve bodies, gear blanks.
- **Heavy Industry**: Rolling mill housings, crusher frames, marine propellers.
- **Art & Decorative**: Sculptures, ornamental castings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Decision Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Decision Framework: When to Choose Sand Casting

The selection of sand casting over competing processes is governed by a multi-criteria analytical framework. Below is the **systematic evaluation logic** that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

### Step 1: Quantity Assessment

$$
N = \text{Annual Production Volume (parts/year)}
$$

- If $N \leq 1000$ → **Sand casting is preferred** (low tooling cost dominates).
- If $1000 < N \leq 50000$ → **Green-sand or shell-mold casting** is competitive.
- If $N > 50000$ → **Die casting** becomes more economical.

### Step 2: Cost Trade-off Equation

$$
C_{total} = C_{tooling} + N \cdot C_{piece} + C_{finishing}
$$

Where:
- $C_{tooling}$ = one-time pattern/die cost
- $C_{piece}$ = per-piece material + labor + overhead
- $C_{finishing}$ = machining, fettling, inspection

**Logic**: For sand casting, $C_{tooling}$ is small, but $C_{piece}$ and $C_{finishing}$ are large. The breakeven point with die casting is typically reached at $N \approx 5000–10000$ pieces.

### Step 3: Mass / Size Constraint

$$
M_{max} = 200{,}000 \text{ kg (sand)} \quad \gg \quad M_{max} = 25 \text{ kg (die casting)}
$$

**Logic**: For castings above ~50 kg, sand casting is often the **only** economically viable option.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Why Sand Casting Tolerances Are Loose

The dimensional variation in sand casting arises from multiple contributing factors. The total tolerance band can be approximated as:

$$
T_{total} = \pm \sqrt{T_{pattern}^2 + T_{mold}^2 + T_{shrinkage}^2 + T_{core}^2}
$$

Where each term accounts for:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pattern} &= \pm 0.1 \text{ to } \pm 0.3 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(pattern wear, machining error)} \\
T_{mold} &= \pm 0.5 \text{ to } \pm 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(sand shift, parting line mismatch, ramming variability)} \\
T_{shrinkage} &= \pm 0.2 \text{ to } \pm 0.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(thermal contraction, restrained vs. unrestrained)} \\
T_{core} &= \pm 0.3 \text{ to } \pm 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(core placement and bending)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing these using RSS (Root Sum of Squares) for a typical small steel casting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= \pm \sqrt{(0.3)^2 + (1.0)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (0.8)^2} \\
&= \pm \sqrt{0.09 + 1.00 + 0.25 + 0.64} \\
&= \pm \sqrt{1.98} \\
&\approx \pm 1.41 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the KTU textbook range of **±0.8 to ±2.5 mm**, confirming the theoretical basis of the limitation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Advantages vs. Disadvantages at a Glance

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Cost** | Low tooling, low capital | High per-piece cost at volume |
| **Dimensional** | Large size possible | Poor dimensional accuracy |
| **Surface** | Wide material range | Rough surface finish (Ra 12.5–25 µm) |
| **Production** | Small batches viable | Slow cycle time |
| **Design** | Complex shapes with cores | Undercuts limited by draft |
| **Environment** | Sand recyclable | Silica dust → silicosis |
| **Post-Processing** | One-piece large castings | High machining allowance |
| **Labour** | Local foundry possible | Skilled labor required |

---

## 3.4 Defect-Based Limitation Analysis

The following defects are **inherent** to sand casting and must be compensated for in design:

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Number of Defective Castings}}{\text{Total Castings Produced}}
$$

Typical industry defect density for sand casting is **3%–8%**, far higher than die casting (**0.5%–1%**) or investment casting (**1%–2%**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Defects in Sand Casting:**
> - **Blowholes** – trapped gases from sand moisture
> - **Sand Inclusions** – loose sand grains enter the cavity
> - **Hot Tears** – restrained shrinkage cracks
> - **Misruns** – incomplete filling due to low pouring temperature
> - **Porosity** – gas micro-voids in the casting
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sand Casting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Pattern Design and Fabrication] --> B[Step 2: Green Sand Preparation]
    B --> C[Step 3: Mold Ramming in Flask]
    C --> D[Step 4: Pattern Withdrawal]
    D --> E[Step 5: Core Placement if Needed]
    E --> F[Step 6: Mold Assembly Cope on Drag]
    F --> G[Step 7: Molten Metal Pouring]
    G --> H[Step 8: Solidification and Cooling]
    H --> I[Step 9: Shake Out and Casting Removal]
    I --> J[Step 10: Fettling Cleaning and Inspection]
    J --> K[Step 11: Final Machining if Required]
    K --> L[Step 12: Finished Casting Ready for Use]
    L --> M[Used Sand Reclamation and Recycle]
    M -.-> B
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Advantages-Disadvantages Functional Architecture

```mermaid

        A1[Economic Low Tooling Cost]

    end

    end

```

## 4.3 Schematic: Typical Sand Mold Assembly (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid

        P[Pouring Cup Top]
        SP[Sprue Vertical Channel]
        RG[Runner Horizontal Channel]
        GT[Gate Entry to Cavity]
        CV[CASTING CAVITY Negative of Pattern]
        CR[Core Box Internal Cavity Form]
        RS[Riser Feeder for Shrinkage Compensation]
        DG[Drag Bottom Flask]
        CP[Cope Top Flask]
    end

    P --> SP
    SP --> RG
    RG --> GT
    GT --> CV
    CR -. Embedded Inside .-> CV
    RS -. Adjacent to .-> CV
    DG --> CP
```

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
    Q1 -->|1000 to 50000| Q3{Tolerance Required}
    Q1 -->|More than 50000| R1[Consider Die Casting]

    Q2 -->|Less than 50 kg| R2[Sand Casting or Die Casting]
    Q2 -->|More than 50 kg| R3[Sand Casting PREFERRED]

    Q3 -->|Loose plus minus 1 mm| R4[Sand Casting or Shell Mold]
    Q3 -->|Tight plus minus 0.1 mm| R5[Investment or Die Casting]
```
