<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**"List any six advantages of sand casting."**

### Model Answer (6 Points × 0.5 mark each):

1. **Low tooling cost** — Pattern fabrication is cheaper than permanent dies.
2. **Wide material range** — Ferrous, non-ferrous, and exotic alloys can be cast.
3. **Large size capability** — Castings up to 200 tonnes are possible.
4. **Complex geometry** — Internal cavities achievable using sand cores.
5. **Design flexibility** — Patterns can be modified quickly for design changes.
6. **Sand recyclability** — Used sand can be reclaimed and reused, reducing waste.
7. **Low capital investment** — A basic foundry setup is affordable.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**"State the major limitations of sand casting."**

### Model Answer:

1. **Poor dimensional accuracy** — Typical tolerance is ±0.8 to ±2.5 mm.
2. **Poor surface finish** — Surface roughness Ra = 12.5 to 25 µm, requiring machining.
3. **Low production rate** — Slow cycle time unsuitable for mass production.
4. **High defect rate** — Susceptible to blowholes, porosity, sand inclusions, and hot tears.
5. **Labor-intensive** — Requires skilled operators for ramming, pouring, and fettling.
6. **Environmental hazard** — Silica dust causes silicosis; high energy for sand reclamation.

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently lose marks by listing advantages/disadvantages **without grouping them logically** (economic vs. design vs. quality). Always **categorize** your points.
> - Avoid **one-word answers** like *"Low cost"* — instead write *"Low tooling cost compared to die casting"* for full marks.
> - For 14-mark questions, **minimum 8–10 distinct points** with brief explanation are expected on each side.
> - **Do not skip examples** — quoting even one industrial application (e.g., *"engine block"* or *"turbine housing"*) adds marks.
> - Tolerance values, Ra values, and weight limits must be **quoted with units** to earn full technical marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **advantages of sand casting**, grouping them under economic, design flexibility, and material versatility. Give two industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **major disadvantages of sand casting**, emphasizing dimensional accuracy, surface finish, defect formation, and environmental concerns. Compare the achievable tolerance with that of die casting. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A):

**Part (a) — Advantages of Sand Casting (7 marks):**

**1. Economic Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Low tooling cost**: Patterns cost only **5%–15%** of die-casting dies; wooden patterns can be made in days.
- **Low capital investment**: No high-tonnage presses or hydraulic clamps required; small foundries can be set up economically.
- **Sand recyclability**: Used (reclaimed) sand can be reprocessed and reused multiple times.
- **Cost-effective for small batches**: Per-piece cost remains low when production volume is limited.

**2. Design Flexibility Advantages (2.5 marks):**
- **Wide size range**: Castings from a few grams to over **200 tonnes** are feasible.
- **Complex geometry**: Internal cavities, undercuts, and intricate shapes can be produced using **sand cores**.
- **Easy design modifications**: Patterns can be altered quickly without re-tooling.
- **Large one-piece castings**: Eliminates assembly of multiple parts (e.g., machine tool beds, pump housings).

**3. Material Versatility (1.5 marks):**
- Can cast **ferrous metals** (cast iron, carbon steel, alloy steel), **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper, brass, bronze, magnesium), and **exotic alloys**.
- No restriction on metallurgical composition — any castable alloy works.

**Industrial Examples (1 mark):**
- **Automotive engine block** — complex internal water jackets using sand cores.
- **Turbine housing for power generation** — large ferrous casting in a single piece.

---

**Part (b) — Disadvantages of Sand Casting (7 marks):**

**1. Dimensional Accuracy (2 marks):**
- Achievable tolerance is only **±0.8 mm to ±2.5 mm** for small castings.
- Compared to **die casting (±0.05 mm)** and **investment casting (±0.1 mm)**, sand casting is far inferior.
- Causes: sand mold deformation, pattern wear, and uneven ramming.

**2. Surface Finish (1.5 marks):**
- Surface roughness is typically **Ra = 12.5–25 µm**.
- Requires extensive **post-machining** (3–6 mm stock removal).
- Die casting achieves **Ra = 1–2.5 µm**, eliminating much finishing.

**3. Defect Formation (1.5 marks):**
- Common defects: **blowholes, porosity, sand inclusions, hot tears, misruns**.
- Caused by gas evolution from moisture, mold erosion, and restrained shrinkage.
- Industry defect density is **3%–8%**, much higher than die casting (**0.5%–1%**).

**4. Environmental & Health Concerns (1 mark):**
- **Silica dust** from sand handling causes **silicosis**, a serious lung disease.
- High energy consumption for **thermal sand reclamation**.
- Emissions and noise during pouring and shake-out.

**5. Production Limitations (1 mark):**
- Slow cycle time — mold must be made, poured, cooled, and shaken out each cycle.
- Labor-intensive and requires skilled operators.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **sand casting process** with a neat block diagram. List the major **advantages** of sand casting in terms of cost and design flexibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of sand casting** with respect to dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and production rate. Suggest suitable alternatives when high dimensional accuracy and surface finish are required. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B):

**Part (a) — Sand Casting Process and Advantages (7 marks):**

**Sand Casting Process Flow (3.5 marks):**
1. **Pattern making** — Wood/metal replica of the final part is fabricated.
2. **Mold preparation** — Green sand (silica + bentonite + water) is rammed in flask.
3. **Pattern withdrawal** — Pattern is removed, leaving cavity.
4. **Core placement** — Sand cores inserted for internal features.
5. **Mold assembly** — Cope placed over drag and clamped.
6. **Pouring** — Molten metal poured into sprue.
7. **Solidification** — Metal cools following **Chvorinov's Rule**: $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^2$.
8. **Shake-out** — Sand mold broken; casting retrieved.

**Advantages – Cost (1.5 marks):**
- Low pattern cost (5%–15% of die cost).
- Low capital investment in equipment.
- Sand reclamation reduces recurring material cost.

**Advantages – Design Flexibility (2 marks):**
- Castings from grams to 200 tonnes.
- Complex internal cavities via cores.
- Wide alloy range — ferrous, non-ferrous, and exotic alloys.
- Easy design modifications without re-tooling.

---

**Part (b) — Limitations and Alternatives (7 marks):**

**1. Dimensional Accuracy (2 marks):**
- Loose tolerance of **±0.8 to ±2.5 mm** vs. ±0.05 mm for die casting.
- Caused by mold deformation, pattern wear, and shrinkage variability.

**2. Surface Finish (1.5 marks):**
- **Ra = 12.5–25 µm** — requires extensive post-machining.
- Cannot match die casting (**Ra = 1–2.5 µm**) or investment casting (**Ra = 1.6–6.3 µm**).

**3. Production Rate (1.5 marks):**
- Cycle time is slow: each mold is destroyed after one use.
- Not economical for high-volume mass production (>50,000 pieces/year).

**4. Suitable Alternatives (2 marks):**

| Requirement | Recommended Process |
|-------------|---------------------|
| High dimensional accuracy | Die casting (±0.05 mm) |
| Excellent surface finish | Investment casting (Ra 1.6–6.3 µm) |
| High volume + non-ferrous | Die casting |
| Complex geometry + tight tolerance | Investment casting |
| Very large casting (mass > 50 kg) | Sand casting remains the only option |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Sand casting is **versatile and economical for low-volume, large, complex parts**, but for high-volume, tight-tolerance, fine-finish applications, die casting or investment casting must be considered.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sand Casting Advantages & Disadvantages**

- ✔ Sand casting is an **expendable mold process** using green sand (silica + bentonite + water).
- ✔ Achieves casting weights from **grams to 200+ tonnes** — unique advantage.
- ✔ Tolerance band: **±0.8 to ±2.5 mm**; Surface finish: **Ra = 12.5–25 µm**.
- ✔ Pattern cost is only **5%–15%** of die-casting dies.
- ✔ Defect rate is **3%–8%** (much higher than die casting's 0.5%–1%).
- ✔ **Common defects**: blowholes, porosity, sand inclusions, hot tears, misruns.
- ✔ Solidity governed by **Chvorinov's Rule**: $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^2$.
- ✔ **Choose sand casting** when: low volume, large size, complex geometry, low budget.
- ✔ **Avoid sand casting** when: high volume, tight tolerance, fine surface, mass production.
- ✔ **Health hazard**: silica dust → silicosis; sand reclamation is energy-intensive.
- ✔ Best alternative for tight tolerance = **die casting**; for fine finish + complex = **investment casting**.
- ✔ Always **categorize** advantages/disadvantages (economic, design, quality) in exam answers.
- ✔ Always **quote numerical values with units** for full marks in KTU valuation.

> 🎯 **One-Line Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"Sand casting = cheapest tooling, biggest size, widest materials — but loosest tolerance, roughest finish, slowest cycle."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Casting Processes: Investment Casting (Lost Wax Process)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Casting Processes: Investment Casting (Lost Wax Process)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investment Casting (Lost Wax Process)** is a precision metal-forming process in which a disposable **wax pattern** (coated with a refractory ceramic slurry to form a one-piece, disposable mold) is used to produce a casting of exceptional dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and metallurgical integrity. The process is classified by **ASTM**, **BIS (IS 4841)**, and **KTU syllabus (PCPET402 Module 1)** as a "near-net-shape" forming operation belonging to the family of *expendable mold, non-expendable pattern → expendable mold* casting techniques.

The generic term **"Investment"** refers to the act of *surrounding* or *coating* the wax pattern with a refractory investment material (a fine ceramic slurry). The synonym **"Lost Wax"** highlights the sacrificial nature of the wax pattern, which is melted out prior to pouring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Birthday Candle Inside a Chocolate Shell** 🍫🕯️
> 1. You take a candle (the **wax pattern**).
> 2. You dip it repeatedly into melted chocolate (the **ceramic slurry**), letting each layer harden — this is **investing** the pattern.
> 3. You heat the whole assembly. The candle wax melts and drains out — this is **dewaxing** (lost wax!).
> 4. You pour molten metal into the empty chocolate shell.
> 5. You break the chocolate to retrieve a perfectly shaped metal replica of the original candle.

The chocolate shell is a one-time disposable mold (you cannot reuse it) — exactly how investment casting works. The **accuracy** comes from the original wax replica being machined/cast to near-final dimensions, and the **smooth surface** comes from the fine ceramic coating.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

> [!TIP]
> Key Industry-Standard Performance Metrics for Investment Casting:
> - **Dimensional Tolerance:** $\pm$ **0.005 inch/inch** ($\pm 0.13$ mm/mm) as-cast (Source: ASM Metals Handbook Vol. 15)
> - **Surface Roughness:** **1.5 – 6.5 $\mu$m Ra** (60 – 250 $\mu$in Ra)
> - **Minimum Section Thickness:** **0.6 mm (0.025 inch)**
> - **Maximum Casting Weight:** up to **35 kg (75 lbs)** in commercial practice; aerospace super-alloy castings can reach **300+ kg**
> - **Typical Metals Cast:** Aluminium, magnesium, brass, bronze, steels, stainless steels, tool steels, titanium, nickel/cobalt-based super-alloys (e.g., Inconel 718, Nimonic 90).

### 1.4 Classification in Casting Family Tree

| Feature | Investment Casting Property |
|---|---|
| Mold Type | **Expendable (one-time use)** |
| Pattern Type | **Expendable (wax, plastic, frozen mercury)** |
| Molding Material | Refractory ceramic (silica, zircon, alumina) |
| Process Category | **Near-net-shape / Precision casting** |
| Production Volume | Low-to-medium (prototypes to medium batches) |
| Cost Driver | Pattern tooling & labor-intensive shell making |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Sequential Stages of Investment Casting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect you to write these steps **in chronological order** with a labelled diagram. Memorize the seven steps below — this is **the most frequently asked 7-mark question** on this topic.

1. **Pattern Injection (Wax Pattern Making)**
   - Wax (or plastic, frozen Hg) is injected under pressure (typically **0.5 – 1.5 MPa**) into a metallic die (aluminium or steel).
   - Pattern material: **microcrystalline paraffin wax** (most common), often blended with **synthetic resins** (e.g., polystyrene) and **stearic acid** (release agent).
   - Patterns are made slightly **oversize** to compensate for metal shrinkage.

2. **Pattern Assembly (Tree Construction)**
   - Multiple individual wax patterns are **welded** (using a hot spatula) onto a central wax sprue, forming a "**tree**" or "**cluster**".
   - This maximizes the number of castings produced per pour and optimizes metal yield.

3. **Investment (Shell Building)**
   - The wax tree is **dipped** into a ceramic slurry (refractory filler + liquid binder) and then **stuccoed** with coarse ceramic grains.
   - This dip-stucco cycle is repeated **6 – 10 times** to build a strong, layered shell (typically **6 – 15 mm thick**).
   - **Primary coat (face coat):** fine zircon $(\text{ZrSiO}_4)$ or fused silica + silica sol binder → gives surface finish.
   - **Backup coats:** coarser silica, mullite, or chamotte + gypsum/ethyl silicate binder → provides strength.

4. **Dewaxing (Lost-Wax Stage)**
   - The invested shell is placed in an **autoclave** (steam-heated pressure vessel) at **0.3 – 0.5 MPa** and **150 – 175°C** for **5 – 15 minutes**.
   - Sudden heat + pressure causes the wax to expand and **drain out rapidly** (thermal expansion coefficient of wax ≈ $200 \times 10^{-6}$/°C — much higher than ceramic shell ≈ $5 \times 10^{-6}$/°C).
   - Wax is recovered (≈ **90 – 95%** recovery rate) and recycled.

5. **Burnout (Firing)**
   - The dewaxed shell is heated in a **high-temperature furnace** (typically **900 – 1100°C** for 1 – 4 hours).
   - Removes residual wax, decomposes binder, and **sinters** the ceramic — producing a hard, porous, gas-permeable mold.

6. **Pouring**
   - Molten metal is poured into the still-hot shell (or pre-heated to **900 – 1200°C** for super-alloys).
   - Pouring methods: **gravity**, **vacuum**, **centrifugal**, or **counter-gravity (vacuum-assisted)**.
   - Gating follows the **choke / sprue-runnergate ratio principle**: $\sum A_{\text{gate}} : A_{\text{sprue}} = 1 : 1.5$ (unpressurized) or $1:1$ to $1.25$ (pressurized).

7. **Knockout, Cut-off & Finishing**
   - After solidification, the ceramic shell is broken mechanically (vibratory, hammer, or water-jet).
   - Castings are **cut off** from the tree (using bandsaw, abrasive wheel, or plasma).
   - Final operations: **grinding, shot blasting, machining, heat treatment, inspection**.

### 2.2 Pattern & Casting Shrinkage — The Critical Calculation

Investment casters must pre-compensate for **two distinct shrinkages**:

$$S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{pattern}} \times S_{\text{metal}} \times S_{\text{mold}}$$

where each $S$ is expressed as the **shrinkage factor** (linear coefficient).

In standard practice, the die cavity is machined oversize by the cumulative shrinkage allowance:

$$L_{\text{die}} = L_{\text{casting}} \times (1 + \alpha_{\text{total}})$$

**Typical Linear Shrinkage Allowances (for steel / ferrous castings):**

| Stage | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Wax pattern shrinkage (cooling from injection to room temp) | $\alpha_w$ | **0.5 – 1.0 %** |
| Metal shrinkage (cooling from solidus to room temp) | $\alpha_m$ | **1.5 – 2.5 %** (ferrous), **1.0 – 1.5 %** (non-ferrous) |
| Mold (ceramic shell) expansion | $\alpha_s$ | **0.1 – 0.5 %** (negligible for sintered shells) |
| **Total (cumulative) linear allowance** | $\alpha_{\text{total}}$ | **≈ 2.0 – 4.0 %** |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 SCHEME**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cumulative Shrinkage Factor | $L_{\text{die}} = L_{\text{casting}} \times (1 + \alpha_w + \alpha_m - \alpha_s)$ | Linear, dimensionless |
| 2 | Die Cavity Dimension | $D_{\text{die}} = D_{\text{cast}} \cdot (1 + f)$ | $f$ = total fractional shrinkage |
| 3 | Choke Area (sprue) Ratio | $A_{\text{choke}} : A_{\text{sprue}} \approx 1 : 1.5$ | Unpressurized gating |
| 4 | Surface Roughness (Ra) | $R_a \approx 1.5 - 6.5 \, \mu\text{m}$ | As-cast IC surface |
| 5 | Dimensional Tolerance | $\Delta L / L \approx \pm 0.005$ in/in | ASTM Grade 3 – 5 |
| 6 | Chvorinov's Rule (applicable) | $t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | Solidification time |
| 7 | Wax Thermal Expansion | $\alpha_{\text{wax}} \approx 200 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$ | Causes dewaxing burst |
| 8 | Typical Pouring Temperature (steel) | $T_{\text{pour}} \approx T_{\text{liquidus}} + 50^\circ\text{C}$ | Superheat ≈ 50°C |
| 9 | Shell Thickness | $t_{\text{shell}} \approx 6 - 15$ mm | After 6 – 10 dip-stucco cycles |
| 10 | Pouring Time (Chvorinov) | $t_p = K \cdot \sqrt{W}$ | $W$ = pour weight, $K$ = constant |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where do you actually see investment castings?**
> - **Aerospace:** Turbine blades, vanes, combustor liners, after-burner parts (Inconel, Ti-6Al-4V).
> - **Medical:** Orthopedic implants (hip joints, dental crowns — Co-Cr alloys, Ti).
> - **Automotive:** Turbocharger wheels, valve bodies, steering housings.
> - **Defence:** Missile nose cones, gun components.
> - **General Engineering:** Jewellery, art sculpture (bronze statues), gear blanks, pump impellers.
> 
> **Why?** Because the process produces **complex, thin-walled, near-net-shape parts** that would be impossible or uneconomical to machine from solid bar stock.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Shrinkage Allowance for a Die Cavity

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Problem Style):**
> A **brass gear blank** is to be investment cast. The final casting diameter (post-machining reference) is **$\mathbf{80\ mm}$**. The wax pattern material exhibits a linear shrinkage of **$\mathbf{0.7\ \%}$** on cooling, and the brass alloy shrinks by **$\mathbf{1.6\ \%}$** on solidification. The ceramic shell expansion is **$\mathbf{0.2\ \%}$**. Determine the **die cavity diameter** to be machined into the wax injection die.

**Given:**
$$D_{\text{cast}} = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad \alpha_w = 0.7\%, \quad \alpha_m = 1.6\%, \quad \alpha_s = 0.2\%$$

**Step 1 — Convert percentages to fractional shrinkages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_w &= \frac{0.7}{100} = 0.007 \\
f_m &= \frac{1.6}{100} = 0.016 \\
f_s &= \frac{0.2}{100} = 0.002
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the linear scaling equation (cumulative multiplication, but for small shrinkages, fractional addition is acceptable):**

$$
D_{\text{die}} = D_{\text{cast}} \cdot (1 + f_w) \cdot (1 + f_m) \cdot (1 + f_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each factor separately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 + f_w) &= 1.007 \\
(1 + f_m) &= 1.016 \\
(1 + f_s) &= 1.002
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply all three (using exact compounding):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{die}} &= 80 \times (1.007 \times 1.016 \times 1.002) \\
&= 80 \times (1.007 \times 1.016) \times 1.002 \\
&= 80 \times 1.023112 \times 1.002 \\
&= 80 \times 1.025158 \\
&\approx 82.01 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the additive approximation (valid because $f < 5\%$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{die}} &\approx D_{\text{cast}} \cdot (1 + f_w + f_m + f_s) \\
&= 80 \times (1 + 0.007 + 0.016 - 0.002) \\
&= 80 \times 1.021 \\
&= 81.68 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between the two methods:** $\vert 82.01 - 81.68 \vert = 0.33$ mm. For KTU exam purposes, the **additive method** (Step 5) is sufficient and most often used. Always state the assumption: *"Since all shrinkages are small ($<5\%$), linear superposition is valid."*

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Dip-Stucco Coats for a Given Shell Thickness

**Problem:** A wax tree requires a ceramic shell of total thickness **$t_{\text{shell}} = 10$ mm**. If each dip-stucco cycle deposits a wet layer of **$\delta_1 = 0.4$ mm** (primary coat) and the backup coats each add **$\delta_2 = 1.2$ mm**, with **1 primary + $n$ backup coats**, find $n$.

**Given:**
$$t_{\text{shell}} = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad \delta_1 = 0.4 \text{ mm}, \quad \delta_2 = 1.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Equation:**

$$
t_{\text{shell}} = \delta_1 + n \cdot \delta_2
$$

**Solve for $n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot \delta_2 &= t_{\text{shell}} - \delta_1 \\
n &= \frac{t_{\text{shell}} - \delta_1}{\delta_2} = \frac{10 - 0.4}{1.2} = \frac{9.6}{1.2} = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{n = 8 \text{ backup coats (Total coats} = 1 + 8 = 9)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Investment vs. Other Casting Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently ask for comparative tables.** Use the matrix below as your template.

| Parameter | Investment Casting | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Shell Molding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mold type | Expendable | Expendable | Permanent | Expendable |
| Pattern type | **Expendable (wax)** | Expendable (wood/metal) | Permanent (steel die) | Metal (heated) |
| Surface finish ($\mu$m Ra) | **1.5 – 6.5** | 12 – 25 | 1 – 3 | 6 – 12 |
| Dimensional tolerance (mm/mm) | **$\pm 0.005$** | $\pm 0.015$ | $\pm 0.002$ | $\pm 0.005$ |
| Min section thickness (mm) | **0.6** | 3 – 6 | 0.8 | 1.5 |
| Production rate | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| Tooling cost | Medium | Low | Very high | Medium |
| Best for | Complex / super-alloy | Large ferrous parts | High-volume non-ferrous | Medium-batch ductile |
| Cost per piece | **High** | Low | Very low (high vol.) | Medium |

### 3.4 Application of Chvorinov's Rule to Investment Casting

**Problem:** A steel investment casting has a volume $V = 8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$ and surface area $A = 0.06 \, \text{m}^2$. Given mold constant $B = 600 \, \text{s} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$, find the solidification time.

**Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$t_s = B \cdot \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Compute the modulus $V/A$:**

$$
\frac{V}{A} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{0.06} = 0.01333 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Square the modulus:**

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 = (0.01333)^2 = 1.778 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by mold constant $B$:**

$$
t_s = 600 \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1067 \text{ s} \cdot \text{(unit-corrected)} \approx 107 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The Chvorinov constant $B$ in SI has units of $\text{s/m}^2$. Always re-check the units; a $V/A$ ratio in **meters** is needed. Some textbooks use $B$ in $\text{s/mm}^2$ — then $V/A$ must be in **mm**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
