<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Investment Casting. List any four advantages of the process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Definition (1.5 marks):* Investment casting, also known as the **lost-wax process**, is a precision casting process in which a **wax pattern** is coated with a refractory ceramic slurry to form a one-time (expendable) mold. After the wax is melted out, molten metal is poured into the resulting ceramic shell to produce a casting of high dimensional accuracy and surface finish.

*Advantages (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks):*
1. Excellent surface finish (**1.5 – 6.5 $\mu$m Ra**) and tight dimensional tolerance ($\pm 0.13$ mm/mm).
2. Complex shapes with thin walls (down to **0.6 mm**) and undercuts are possible.
3. Wide range of metals can be cast — ferrous, non-ferrous, and super-alloys.
4. Near-net-shape reduces (often eliminates) secondary machining.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the role of the primary (face) coat in the investment casting shell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **primary (face) coat** is the **innermost ceramic layer** in direct contact with the wax pattern surface. Its functions are:
1. It reproduces the **surface texture and micro-detail** of the wax pattern, determining the **as-cast surface roughness** of the final casting (1 mark).
2. It uses **fine refractory powders** (e.g., fused silica, zircon $\text{ZrSiO}_4$, or calcined alumina) and a **high-quality binder** (silica sol or ethyl silicate) to produce a dense, smooth, non-reactive surface (1 mark).
3. It acts as a **barrier against metal-mold reaction** and must be **chemically compatible** with the poured alloy — especially critical for reactive super-alloys (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**With a neat labelled diagram, explain the step-by-step process of investment casting. List its advantages, limitations, and applications.**

**Model Answer (14 marks — Part a: 7 marks):**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A flow chart similar to *Section 4.1* above must be drawn and labelled clearly.

**Step-by-step Explanation (5 marks — split into the seven stages, ~0.7 mark each):**

1. **Wax Pattern Injection:** Molten wax is injected into a metallic die under pressure. Multiple identical patterns may be produced.
2. **Pattern Assembly:** Individual patterns are welded onto a central wax sprue to form a tree.
3. **Investment (Shell Building):** The tree is dipped into ceramic slurry and stuccoed with refractory grains. Cycle is repeated 6 – 10 times.
4. **Dewaxing:** The invested tree is autoclaved at 150 – 175°C, causing the wax to melt and drain out rapidly. Wax is recycled.
5. **Burnout:** The shell is fired at 900 – 1100°C to burn out residual wax and sinter the ceramic.
6. **Pouring:** Molten metal is poured (gravity, vacuum, or centrifugal) into the hot shell.
7. **Knockout & Finishing:** The shell is broken; castings are cut off, ground, blasted, and inspected.

---

**Q3(b).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain any two of the following in detail:**
**(i)** Wax pattern materials and their desirable properties.
**(ii)** Dewaxing operation in investment casting.
**(iii)** Gating system design in investment casting.

**Model Answer (14 marks — Part b: 7 marks):**

**(ii) Dewaxing Operation (3.5 marks) + (iii) Gating System (3.5 marks) — choose any two.**

*Wax Pattern Materials:*
- **Composition:** microcrystalline paraffin wax + natural resins + synthetic polymers (e.g., polystyrene) + fillers (e.g., $\text{CaCO}_3$) + stearic acid (release agent).
- **Desirable properties:** low shrinkage on cooling ($< 1\%$), high strength, low coefficient of thermal expansion, good surface finish capability, easy to inject, recyclable.

*Dewaxing:*
- Carried out in an **autoclave** at **0.3 – 0.5 MPa** and **150 – 175°C** for **5 – 15 min**.
- Rapid heating causes the wax to expand (CTE $\approx 200 \times 10^{-6}/°\text{C}$) more than the shell (CTE $\approx 5 \times 10^{-6}/°\text{C}$), forcing the wax to **burst out** of the shell.
- **Flash dewaxing** (newer method) uses direct radiant heating at $260°C$ for $\approx 20$ s.
- Wax recovery: **90 – 95%**, recycled for economic viability.

*Gating System:*
- Sprue + runner + gate (single or multiple).
- **Unpressurized system:** $A_{\text{gate}} : A_{\text{sprue}} = 1 : 1.5$ — slow, smooth flow.
- **Pressurized system:** $A_{\text{gate}} : A_{\text{sprue}} = 1 : 1$ to $1 : 0.8$ — fast filling, but higher turbulence.
- Sprue must be **tapered** (top-to-bottom area ratio $\approx 1.4 : 1$) to prevent aspiration.

---

#### ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** Describe the **investment casting process** in detail with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A steel casting of nominal diameter **120 mm** is to be produced by investment casting. Pattern wax shrinks by **0.8%**, steel shrinks by **2.0%**, and ceramic shell expands by **0.3%**. Calculate the **die cavity diameter** to be machined. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Given:** $D_{\text{cast}} = 120$ mm, $\alpha_w = 0.8\%$, $\alpha_m = 2.0\%$, $\alpha_s = 0.3\%$

**Conversion to fractions (1 mark):**
$$
f_w = 0.008, \quad f_m = 0.020, \quad f_s = 0.003
$$

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{die}} = D_{\text{cast}} \cdot (1 + f_w + f_m - f_s)
$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**
$$
D_{\text{die}} = 120 \times (1 + 0.008 + 0.020 - 0.003) = 120 \times 1.025
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{die}} = 123.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The die cavity should be machined to **123.0 mm** diameter to account for cumulative shrinkage from wax → metal → cooling, and to deliver a final casting of exactly 120 mm diameter.

---

**Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the **ceramic shell making** process in investment casting. What are the materials used for the primary and backup coats? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare **investment casting** with **sand casting** and **die casting** in terms of tolerance, surface finish, complexity, production rate, and cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

*Part (a):* Discuss **dip-stucco** process; primary coat = fine zircon/silica + silica sol binder; backup coats = chamotte + gypsum/ethyl silicate; drying time 4 – 24 h per coat; 6 – 10 cycles; total shell thickness 6 – 15 mm.

*Part (b):* A 3-column comparative table (refer to *Section 3.3* matrix above). Highlight that **investment casting bridges the gap** between sand casting (low cost, rough) and die casting (high cost, high volume, fine finish) — it offers **fine finish at low-to-medium volumes**, especially for exotic alloys.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the order of operations** — many students list "pouring" before "dewaxing." Always remember: **Dewaxing happens BEFORE burnout**, and burnout happens BEFORE pouring. (Deducts 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention wax recovery** — examiners specifically award a 0.5 mark bonus for noting that wax is recycled (90 – 95%).
> 3. **Using sand-casting terminology in investment casting** — words like "**cope**", "**drag**", "**flask**" are wrong. Use: "**primary coat**", "**backup coat**", "**shell**", "**tree**", "**ceramic slurry**".
> 4. **Not stating the autoclave temperature and pressure** in dewaxing — always quote **150 – 175°C** and **0.3 – 0.5 MPa**.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark long answers — KTU policy requires a labelled diagram; missing it costs **1 – 2 marks**.
> 6. **Sign errors in shrinkage formula** — the ceramic shell **expands** (use $-f_s$ in additive form), it does not shrink. Many students wrongly add it.
> 7. **In numerical problems, not stating units** — write **mm**, not just a number.
> 8. **Confusing face coat and backup coat materials** — face coat is **fine fused silica/zircon**; backup is **coarse chamotte/mullite**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — INVESTMENT CASTING RAPID REVISION SHEET**

- ✅ **Process Name:** Investment Casting = Lost Wax Process (synonymous).
- ✅ **Pattern:** Wax (microcrystalline paraffin + resins + fillers).
- ✅ **Mold:** Expendable ceramic shell (6 – 15 mm thick; 6 – 10 dip-stucco layers).
- ✅ **Primary Coat:** Fine zircon / fused silica + silica-sol binder.
- ✅ **Backup Coat:** Coarse chamotte / silica + gypsum / ethyl silicate binder.
- ✅ **Dewaxing:** Autoclave at 150 – 175°C, 0.3 – 0.5 MPa → wax melts & drains out.
- ✅ **Burnout:** 900 – 1100°C for 1 – 4 h → ceramic sinters.
- ✅ **Pouring:** Gravity, vacuum, centrifugal, or counter-gravity; metal at $T_{\text{liquidus}} + 50°C$.
- ✅ **Tolerance:** $\pm 0.005$ in/in ($\pm 0.13$ mm/mm).
- ✅ **Surface finish:** 1.5 – 6.5 $\mu$m Ra.
- ✅ **Min section thickness:** 0.6 mm.
- ✅ **Total shrinkage allowance:** 2 – 4 % (cumulative).
- ✅ **Wax recovery:** 90 – 95% (recycled).
- ✅ **Shrinkage formula:** $D_{\text{die}} = D_{\text{cast}} \times (1 + \alpha_w + \alpha_m - \alpha_s)$.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ → governs solidification time.
- ✅ **Gating ratios:** $A_{\text{gate}} : A_{\text{sprue}} = 1 : 1.5$ (unpressurized) or $1:1$ to $1.25$ (pressurized).
- ✅ **Top applications:** Aerospace turbine blades, medical implants, jewellery, automotive turbochargers.
- ✅ **Metals cast:** All — including reactive Ti and super-alloys (Inconel, Nimonic).
- ✅ **Process family:** Expendable mold + Expendable pattern.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** High cost, labor-intensive, low production rate, size limitation (~35 kg commercial, larger for aerospace).
- ✅ **KPCOFGS mapping (KTU 2024):** Maps to **CO1 / CO2** of PCPET402 — "Understand casting processes and selection criteria."
- ✅ **Bloom's Level (Revised):** L2 (Understand) for definitions, L3 (Apply) for shrinkage calculations, L4 (Analyze) for process selection.
- ✅ **Examiner favourite question patterns:**
  - 3-mark: *Define investment casting + list 4 advantages*
  - 7-mark: *Explain the step-by-step process with a neat sketch*
  - 7-mark: *Calculate die cavity dimension for given shrinkage values*
  - 14-mark: *Process + numerical + comparison table*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Die Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Die Casting** is a permanent mold casting process in which molten metal is forced under high pressure into a reusable steel mold (called a **die**) to produce precisely dimensioned, sharply defined, and smoothly finished metal parts. The process is characterized by the use of a die machined from high-grade tool steel or other refractory metals, with at least two sections (cover die and ejector die) that form the cavity.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, die casting is classified as a **high-pressure, high-volume, near-net-shape manufacturing process** under the broader umbrella of **expendable and non-expendable mold casting** techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Die casting falls under the category of *Permanent Mold Casting* processes. The two principal variants examined are **Hot-Chamber Die Casting** and **Cold-Chamber Die Casting**, differentiated by the mechanism used to inject molten metal.

### Key Process Attributes

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| Typical Pressure Range | **7 MPa to 350 MPa** (1,000 – 50,000 psi) |
| Production Rate | **200 – 1,000+ shots/hour** |
| Dimensional Tolerance | **±0.05 mm to ±0.10 mm** for small parts |
| Surface Finish | **1 – 2.5 μm Ra** (excellent) |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** (alloy dependent) |
| Typical Alloys | Zinc, Aluminum, Magnesium, Copper, Lead, Tin |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of die casting like using a **high-tech industrial syringe** to inject molten metal into a precision metal mold:

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Waffle Iron & Cake Decorator**
> Imagine a heavy-duty waffle iron (the **die**) that clamps shut. Now imagine instead of pouring batter, you use a powerful industrial **piping bag filled with hot liquid chocolate** (the **injection system** with molten metal) that forces the chocolate into every tiny crevice of the iron under immense pressure. When the iron opens, out pops a perfectly shaped chocolate figure. Replace chocolate with molten aluminum, and the waffle iron with a hardened steel die — that is **die casting**.

The **mold (die) is permanent** (used thousands to millions of times), unlike sand casting where the mold is destroyed after each part. This is the defining feature that makes die casting economical for **mass production** despite high initial tooling costs.

## 1.3 Process Variants — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Variants (KTU Exam Focus)**
> 1. **Hot-Chamber Die Casting** — Injection mechanism is **immersed in molten metal bath**. Best for low-melting-point alloys (Zn, Sn, Pb).
> 2. **Cold-Chamber Die Casting** — Molten metal is **ladled into a cold shot chamber** before each injection. Best for high-melting-point alloys (Al, Mg, Cu).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The die casting process operates on the principle of **forced injection of molten metal under controlled high pressure** into a closed, water-cooled steel die. The sequence of operations is:

1. **Die Closure** — The two die halves (cover die and ejector die) clamp together with immense force (typically **1,800 kN to 35,000 kN** clamping force).
2. **Metal Injection** — Molten metal is forced into the die cavity through a precisely engineered **gate** and **runner** system.
3. **Solidification Under Pressure** — Pressure is maintained during solidification to compensate for shrinkage and prevent porosity.
4. **Die Opening & Ejection** — The die opens and the solidified part is ejected via **ejector pins**.
5. **Trimming** — Excess material (flash, runner, overflow) is removed in a secondary **trimming press** operation.

## 2.2 Detailed Process Analysis — Hot-Chamber (Gooseneck) Die Casting

### Operating Mechanism
- The **injection plunger and gooseneck (shot well) are submerged** in a bath of molten metal.
- As the plunger retracts, molten metal fills the gooseneck chamber.
- The plunger then drives forward hydraulically/pneumatically, sealing the port and forcing metal through the nozzle into the die cavity.

### Why It Works
- **Short metal flow path** → minimal heat loss, lower temperature required.
- **High cycle speed** → ideal for high-volume production.
- **Limitation** — The submerged plunger is in constant contact with molten metal, restricting use to alloys that do **not chemically attack (erode)** the plunger steel.

### Typical Alloys & Temperatures
| Alloy | Melting Point | Die Casting Temp |
|---|---|---|
| Zinc (Zn) | **419 °C** | 420 – 450 °C |
| Tin (Sn) | **232 °C** | 240 – 280 °C |
| Lead (Pb) | **327 °C** | 340 – 380 °C |

## 2.3 Detailed Process Analysis — Cold-Chamber Die Casting

### Operating Mechanism
- Molten metal is **manually or automatically ladled** into an unheated injection chamber (shot sleeve).
- A separate hydraulic plunger then forces the metal through a nozzle into the die cavity.
- The plunger **does not contact the molten bath** continuously.

### Why It Works
- **Prevents attack on the plunger** by aggressive high-temperature alloys.
- **Allows higher injection pressures** (up to **350 MPa**) for better part density.
- **Longer cycle time** due to ladling step.

### Typical Alloys & Temperatures
| Alloy | Melting Point | Die Casting Temp |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminum (Al) | **660 °C** | 670 – 720 °C |
| Magnesium (Mg) | **650 °C** | 660 – 700 °C |
| Copper (Cu) | **1,083 °C** | 1,100 – 1,200 °C |

## 2.4 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Pressure** | Force pushing metal into cavity | 7 – 350 MPa |
| **Injection Velocity** | Speed of metal flow | 10 – 100 m/s |
| **Clamping Force** | Force holding die halves closed | 1,800 – 35,000 kN |
| **Die Temperature** | Pre-heated die surface temp | 180 – 250 °C (Al) |
| **Cooling Time** | Solidification time before ejection | 1 – 30 s |
| **Cycle Time** | Total time per shot | 5 – 60 s |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Die Casting Calculations**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ | Clamping force required to resist injection pressure | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| $Q = A_v \times v_{inj}$ | Volumetric flow rate of molten metal | $Q$ in m³/s |
| $t_{solidify} = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | Chvorinov's rule (solidification time) | $B$ is mold constant |
| $P_{proj} = \rho g h$ | Metallostatic pressure in gooseneck | $P$ in Pa |
| $F_{eject} = \sigma_{yield} \times A_{part}$ | Ejection force needed | $F$ in N |
| $S_{factor} = \frac{L}{t}$ | Solidification safety factor (length/thickness) | Dimensionless |
| $\eta_{process} = \frac{m_{part}}{m_{total}}$ | Material yield (part mass / total mass poured) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Valid Ranges (KTU Exam Emphasis)**
> - $F_{clamp}$ must exceed the *projected area × injection pressure* — this is why the calculation is critical for **die selection**.
> - For Chvorinov's rule, $n \approx 2$ for most casting processes, and $B$ depends on mold material thermal properties.
> - Material yield $\eta_{process}$ typically lies between **50% and 80%** for well-designed die cast parts.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

Die casting serves industries demanding **high-volume, dimensionally accurate, and aesthetically superior** metal components:
- **Automotive** — Engine blocks, transmission housings, brackets (Al alloys)
- **Consumer Electronics** — Laptop chassis, smartphone frames (Zn, Mg alloys)
- **Hardware** — Locks, hinges, fasteners (Zn alloys)
- **Toys & Decorations** — Die-cast model cars (Zn, lead-free alloys)
- **Aerospace & Defense** — Lightweight Mg and Al components

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force Requirement

The clamping force must be sufficient to keep the die halves closed against the **injection pressure acting on the projected area of the cavity and runner system**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the die parting line:**
The molten metal exerts a normal force on the die cavity wall. The total horizontal force tending to open the die is the integration of pressure over the projected area.

**Step 2 — Set up the force balance:**
At static equilibrium (just before the die opens):

$$
F_{clamp} = F_{resist} = F_{injection}
$$

**Step 3 — Express injection force using pressure and area:**

$$
F_{inj} = P_{inj} \times A_{projected}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor (typically 1.1 to 1.25):**

$$
F_{clamp} = k_s \times P_{inj} \times A_{proj}
$$

where $k_s$ is the safety factor (≈ **1.15** for general die casting).

**Step 5 — Rearranged working formula:**

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} = k_s \cdot P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A die casting machine injects molten aluminum at a pressure of **40 MPa**. The projected area of the casting (including runner and overflow) is **450 cm²**. Calculate the minimum clamping force required, assuming a safety factor of **1.15**.

**Given:**
- $P_{inj} = 40 \text{ MPa} = 40 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
- $A_{proj} = 450 \text{ cm}^2 = 450 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.0450 \text{ m}^2$
- $k_s = 1.15$

**Solution:**

$$
F_{clamp} = 1.15 \times (40 \times 10^{6}) \times (0.0450)
$$

**Computing the product:**

$$
40 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0450 = 1.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

**Applying safety factor:**

$$
F_{clamp} = 1.15 \times 1.8 \times 10^{6} = 2.07 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} = 2{,}070{,}000 \text{ N} = 2{,}070 \text{ kN} \approx 211 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Pressure (Pa = N/m²) × Area (m²) = Force (N). Always convert cm² → m² by multiplying by $10^{-4}$, and MPa → Pa by multiplying by $10^{6}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chvorinov's Rule Applied to Die Casting

Chvorinov's rule governs the **solidification time** of a casting, which directly determines cycle time in die casting.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Heat balance during solidification:**

The heat released by the metal as it solidifies must equal the heat absorbed by the die:

$$
\rho \cdot V \cdot L = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{die}) \cdot t
$$

where $L$ is latent heat of fusion, $h$ is heat transfer coefficient, $T_m$ is metal melting temperature, and $T_{die}$ is initial die temperature.

**Step 2 — Solve for solidification time $t$:**

$$
t = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{die})}
$$

**Step 3 — Group mold/metal constants into a single constant $B$:**

$$
t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{m} - T_{die})} \cdot \frac{V}{A}
$$

Defining:

$$
B = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{m} - T_{die})}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical exponent $n \approx 2$ for castings:**

$$
\boxed{t_{solidify} = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}}
$$

### Interpretation for Die Casting
- **V/A ratio** is the **modulus** of the casting. A thin-walled part (low V/A → small modulus) freezes quickly.
- Die casting benefits from **high $h$** (intimate metal-die contact under pressure) and **small V/A**, enabling **fast cycle times**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Flow Rate & Gate Design

The **gate** is the restrictive orifice through which metal enters the cavity. Its cross-sectional area must be sized to deliver metal fast enough to fill the cavity before premature freezing.

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate equation:**

$$
Q = A_{gate} \times v_{inj}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = \rho \times A_{gate} \times v_{inj}
$$

**Step 3 — Required fill time:**
For cavity volume $V_c$:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{V_c}{Q} = \frac{V_c}{A_{gate} \cdot v_{inj}}
$$

**Step 4 — Sizing gate area:**

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{A_{gate} = \frac{V_c}{t_{fill} \cdot v_{inj}}}
$$

### Worked Example

> **Problem:** A die cavity has volume **120 cm³**. The permissible fill time is **0.05 s**, and the recommended injection velocity is **40 m/s**. Calculate the minimum gate area.

**Solution:**

Convert volume: $V_c = 120 \text{ cm}^3 = 120 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$

$$
A_{gate} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.05 \times 40}
$$

$$
A_{gate} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{gate} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 = 60 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.60 \text{ cm}^2}
$$

This area is typically distributed across **multiple gates** to ensure uniform cavity filling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hot vs. Cold Chamber

| Criterion | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber |
|---|---|---|
| Plunger position | Submerged in melt | External to melt |
| Cycle time | **Shorter** (1–5 s) | Longer (5–30 s) |
| Pressure capability | Up to ~35 MPa | Up to ~350 MPa |
| Suitable alloys | Zn, Sn, Pb, low-melting | Al, Mg, Cu, high-melting |
| Plunger wear | High (chemical attack) | Low |
| Cast part size | Smaller (up to ~5 kg) | Larger (up to ~50 kg) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Hot-Chamber Die Casting (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a hot-chamber die casting system** — preferred over attempting a physical drawing in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Melting Furnace] --> A2[Gooseneck Chamber Submerged in Molten Metal]
    A2 --> A3[Plunger Retracts - Metal Fills Gooseneck]
    A3 --> A4[Die Halves Clamp Shut]
    A4 --> A5[Plunger Advances - Forces Metal Through Nozzle]
    A5 --> A6[Metal Enters Die Cavity via Gate and Runner]
    A6 --> A7[Pressure Maintained During Solidification]
    A7 --> A8[Cooling Channels Extract Heat via Water Circulation]
    A8 --> A9[Die Opens - Ejector Pins Push Part Out]
    A9 --> A10[Trimming Press Removes Flash and Runner]
    A10 --> A11[Finished Die Cast Part]
    A9 -. Recycle .-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a **functional stage** in the process chain. The dotted line from ejection back to the melting furnace represents the **recycling of runner, overflow, and sprue material** — a key economic consideration in die casting.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Cold-Chamber Die Casting (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[External Melting Furnace] --> B2[Auto or Manual Ladling of Molten Metal]
    B2 --> B3[Metal Poured into Cold Shot Sleeve]
    B3 --> B4[Hydraulic Plunger Advances]
    B4 --> B5[Plunger Seals Sleeve and Pushes Metal]
    B5 --> B6[Metal Forced Through Nozzle and Gate]
    B6 --> B7[High Pressure Injection into Die Cavity]
    B7 --> B8[Solidification Under Maintained Pressure]
    B8 --> B9[Die Opens and Ejector Pins Release Part]
    B9 --> B10[Trimming Operation]
    B10 --> B11[Final Die Cast Component]
    B9 -. Recycle Sprue .-> B1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Die System Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Die Assembly"
        C1[Cover Die Half - Stationary]
        C2[Ejector Die Half - Movable]
        C3[Cavity - Product Geometry]
        C4[Runner System - Channels]
        C5[Gate - Metal Entry Point]
        C6[Overflow Wells - Trap Air and Debris]
        C7[Cooling Channels - Water Circulation]
        C8[Ejector Pins - Part Removal]
        C9[Vents - Air Escape]
    end
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C3
    C3 --> C6
    C3 --> C9
    C7 -. Heat Extraction .-> C3
    C8 -. Mechanical Push .-> C3
    C1 --- C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **overflow wells** and **vents** are critical — they trap the leading air column and front-end contaminants, ensuring the cavity is filled with clean metal. Their placement is a high-weightage topic in KTU die design questions.

---

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Diagram of Common Die Casting Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Die Casting Defects]
    D0 --> D1[Porosity]
    D0 --> D2[Cold Shut]
    D0 --> D3[Flash]
    D0 --> D4[Soldering]
    D0 --> D5[Cracks]
    D0 --> D6[Dimensional Variation]

    D1 --> D1a[Cause: Insufficient Pressure]
    D1 --> D1b[Cause: Gas Entrapment]
    D1 --> D1c[Cause: High Die Temp]

    D2 --> D2a[Cause: Low Melt Temp]
    D2 --> D2b[Cause: Slow Injection]

    D3 --> D3a[Cause: Insufficient Clamp Force]
    D3 --> D3b[Cause: Excessive Injection Pressure]

    D4 --> D4a[Cause: Alloy Attack on Die Steel]
    D5 --> D5a[Cause: Thermal Stress]
    D6 --> D6a[Cause: Die Wear]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Distinguish between hot-chamber and cold-chamber die casting with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber |
|---|---|---|
| Plunger | Submerged in molten metal | External to melt |
| Ladling | Automatic (from bath) | Manual or auto ladling |
| Alloys used | Low-melting (Zn, Sn, Pb) | High-melting (Al, Mg, Cu) |
| Cycle time | Shorter | Longer |
| Example parts | Zip fasteners, toys | Car cylinder heads, gearbox housings |

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. What is the role of overflow wells and vents in a die casting die?**

**Model Answer:**
Overflow wells and vents serve to:
1. **Trap air and contaminants** — The leading front of the molten metal carries air and oxides; overflows capture this contaminated material, ensuring the final cavity volume is filled with clean metal.
2. **Regulate thermal balance** — They absorb heat, helping control solidification direction.
3. **Improve die life** — Venting prevents back-pressure and reduces thermal stress on the die.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
> **Q3. (a)** Explain the hot-chamber die casting process with a neat functional diagram. List its advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Hot-chamber die casting (also called **gooseneck die casting**) is a high-pressure permanent mold casting process where the injection mechanism is **permanently submerged** in a bath of molten metal.

**Working Principle:**
- A vertical **gooseneck channel** with a plunger dips into the molten metal.
- As the plunger retracts, the channel fills with metal by gravity/suction.
- The die halves close; the plunger advances under hydraulic or pneumatic pressure (**7 – 35 MPa**), sealing the port and forcing metal through the nozzle into the die cavity.
- The metal solidifies under maintained pressure; the die opens and the part is ejected.

**Advantages:**
- High production rate (fast cycles)
- Lower metal temperature requirement → reduced energy consumption
- Better metal quality (less oxidation)
- Lower cost per part at high volumes

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for high-melting-point alloys (e.g., Al, Cu) because they attack the plunger.
- Plunger erosion limits die life.
- Restricted to smaller part sizes (up to ~5 kg).

**Applications:** Zinc hardware, toys, decorative items, automotive sub-components, electronic housings (low-melt alloys).

---

> **(b)** A cold-chamber die casting machine is to be selected for producing an aluminum alloy component. The projected area of the part (including runner and overflow) is **600 cm²**, and the required injection pressure is **60 MPa**. Calculate the minimum clamping force required and recommend a suitable machine capacity. Assume a safety factor of **1.20**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $A_{proj} = 600 \text{ cm}^2 = 600 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.0600 \text{ m}^2$
- $P_{inj} = 60 \text{ MPa} = 60 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
- $k_s = 1.20$

**Step 1 — Apply clamping force equation:**

$$
F_{clamp} = k_s \times P_{inj} \times A_{proj}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
F_{clamp} = 1.20 \times (60 \times 10^{6}) \times (0.0600)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute product:**

$$
60 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0600 = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor:**

$$
F_{clamp} = 1.20 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 4.32 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-force ≈ 9,807 N):**

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{4.32 \times 10^{6}}{9{,}807} \approx 440.5 \text{ tonnes-force}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 4{,}320 \text{ kN} \approx 440 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

**Step 6 — Machine Recommendation:**
A standard die casting machine rated at **5,000 kN (≈ 500 tonnes)** clamping force would be the appropriate selection, providing adequate margin above the calculated minimum.

---

> **Q4. (a)** Compare die casting with sand casting and investment casting under the heads: (i) production rate, (ii) dimensional accuracy, (iii) surface finish, (iv) tooling cost, and (v) typical alloys. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Die Casting | Sand Casting | Investment Casting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Rate | **Very High** (200–1,000/hr) | Low (5–20/hr) | Low to Medium |
| Dimensional Accuracy | **±0.05 – 0.10 mm** | ±1.0 – 2.0 mm | ±0.10 – 0.25 mm |
| Surface Finish | **1 – 2.5 μm Ra** | 12 – 25 μm Ra | 1.6 – 3.2 μm Ra |
| Tooling Cost | **Very High** (steel dies) | Low (wood patterns) | Medium (wax patterns) |
| Typical Alloys | Zn, Al, Mg, Cu | Ferrous & non-ferrous | Steel, superalloys, Ti |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | 0.5 – 1.5 mm | 3 – 6 mm | 0.5 – 1.0 mm |

---

> **(b)** With the help of Chvorinov's rule, explain how solidification time influences cycle time in die casting. A die casting has a volume of **150 cm³** and surface area of **300 cm²**. If $B = 3.0 \text{ s/cm}^2$ and $n = 2$, calculate the solidification time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theoretical Explanation:**
Chvorinov's rule states that solidification time $t$ of a casting is proportional to the square of the **casting modulus** $(V/A)$:

$$
t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n}
$$

In die casting, the modulus $(V/A)$ is intentionally kept small (thin walls, large surface-to-volume ratio) to enable **rapid solidification**. This directly reduces:
- Cycle time → higher production rate
- Die exposure to high temperatures → extended die life
- Energy consumption per part

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 150 \text{ cm}^3$
- $A = 300 \text{ cm}^2$
- $B = 3.0 \text{ s/cm}^2$
- $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Compute modulus $(V/A)$:**

$$
\frac{V}{A} = \frac{150}{300} = 0.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Square the modulus:**

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chvorinov's rule:**

$$
t = 3.0 \times 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{solidify} = 0.75 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**
> - **Unit Consistency:** $B$ must have units compatible with $(V/A)^n$. In the equation $B \cdot (V/A)^n$, if $V/A$ is in cm, $B$ should be in s/cm². Mixing SI and CGS units is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - **Clamping Force Sign:** Always ensure $A_{proj}$ includes the runner and overflow system, not just the part area. Examiner penalizes "part area only" answers.
> - **Plunger vs. Chamber Confusion:** In Q&A, students often confuse *shot sleeve* with *gooseneck*. The gooseneck is specific to hot-chamber; cold-chamber uses a **shot sleeve**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Die Casting**

- **Definition:** Permanent mold casting with high-pressure metal injection into a reusable steel die.
- **Two Variants:** Hot-chamber (submerged plunger; low-melt alloys) & Cold-chamber (external ladling; high-melt alloys).
- **Pressure Range:** 7 – 350 MPa; clamping force 1,800 – 35,000 kN.
- **Cycle Time:** Hot-chamber (1–5 s) < Cold-chamber (5–30 s).
- **Alloys:** Zn (low melt), Al/Mg (mid melt), Cu (high melt).
- **Tolerance:** ±0.05 – 0.10 mm; Surface finish: 1 – 2.5 μm Ra.
- **Clamping Force Formula:** $F_{clamp} = k_s \cdot P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}$ — always include safety factor (≈1.15–1.25).
- **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t = B(V/A)^2$ — governs solidification time and thus cycle time.
- **Gate Area Sizing:** $A_{gate} = V_c / (t_{fill} \cdot v_{inj})$.
- **Die Components:** Cover die, ejector die, cavity, runner, gate, overflow wells, vents, cooling channels, ejector pins.
- **Defects to Remember:** Porosity, cold shut, flash, soldering, cracks, dimensional variation.
- **Advantages:** High production rate, excellent surface finish, tight tolerances, near-net shape.
- **Limitations:** High tooling cost, limited alloy selection, restricted part size, gas porosity issue.
- **Industrial Applications:** Automotive, electronics, hardware, toys, aerospace.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Centrifugal Casting** is a permanent-mould casting process in which **molten metal is poured into a rapidly rotating mould**, and the **centrifugal force** generated by rotation distributes the liquid metal against the inner periphery of the mould cavity, where it solidifies to form a casting of essentially **rotationally symmetric geometry** (typically hollow cylindrical parts, pipes, tubes, bushings, liners, and wheels).

The centrifugal acceleration, $a_c = \omega^2 r$, replaces (and vastly exceeds) gravity as the driving force for metal flow and feeding, which is the defining characteristic of this process per the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus under **PCPET402 – Module 1: Casting Processes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – PCPET402, Module 1):**
> Centrifugal casting is grouped under *special casting techniques* and is tested regularly for its process principle, the **N-factor (mould speed) formula**, types (true, semi, centrifuging), and the role of the **G-factor**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you spin a bucket half-filled with water. The water climbs up the wall and forms a hollow cylindrical shape, with the open centre being the bucket's rotation axis. That is **exactly** what happens in centrifugal casting:

- **Bucket** → Rotating metal mould (chill or sand).
- **Water** → Molten metal (e.g., cast iron, bronze, steel, aluminium).
- **Hollow centre** → The bore of the cast pipe / bushing.
- **Spinning motion** → Provides *artificial gravity* 30–100× stronger than Earth's $g$.

> [!NOTE]
> Because the *gravity itself is "manufactured" by rotation*, parts cast this way have **superior density, fewer gas/shrinkage porosity, and a very fine-grained outer surface** — all formed without any external feeder heads or risers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Earth's gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$
- **G-factor (typical for cast iron):** $G = 60$ to $80$
- **G-factor (typical for aluminium):** $G = 30$ to $50$
- **G-factor (typical for steel):** $G = 80$ to $120$
- **Standard mould speeds:** $100$ to $3000 \, \mathrm{rpm}$ depending on diameter.
- **Useful dimensionless ratio:** the **G-factor** itself, $G = \dfrac{a_c}{g} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$.

> [!TIP]
> The term **G-factor** is the single most exam-relevant metric. It is the *ratio of centrifugal acceleration to gravitational acceleration* and dictates whether the molten metal "sticks" to the mould wall without slumping.

### 1.4 Three Recognised Variants (KTU Classification)

| Variant | Axis Orientation | Typical Product | Mould Axis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Centrifugal Casting** | Horizontal (mostly) | Pipes, tubes, bushings, cylinder liners | Longitudinal |
| **Semi-Centrifugal Casting** | Vertical (mostly) | Wheels, gear blanks, brake drums | Vertical |
| **Centrifuging (Pressure Casting)** | Any | Irregular, non-circular parts (e.g., shaped drain covers) | Vertical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of True Centrifugal Casting

1. **Mould Preparation:** A hollow cylindrical metal mould (called the *chill* or *die*) is mounted horizontally between rollers or a spindle. Its inner surface is coated with a refractory wash (e.g., silica or chalk) to prevent welding of the casting to the mould.
2. **Rotation Initiation:** The mould is spun up to a pre-calculated **mould speed $N$ (rpm)** *before* pouring. This avoids the metal sloshing.
3. **Metal Pouring:** A measured ladle of molten metal at the correct **pouring temperature** (typically $50$–$100^\circ\mathrm{C}$ above liquidus) is introduced through a pouring spout or trough.
4. **Centrifugal Distribution:** Centrifugal force throws the metal radially outward, where it forms a perfectly **concentric hollow cylinder** with a free central bore — the bore diameter is self-regulating.
5. **Solidification:** Solidification proceeds *inward* from the mould wall, which is a directional solidification — *exactly* what foundrymen desire to feed shrinkage.
6. **Spinning Continues Until Solid:** Rotation is maintained until the casting is *fully solid*; otherwise the still-liquid core would slump and deform.
7. **Extraction:** The mould slows, the casting is withdrawn, and the bore is machined if critical dimensions are required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does solidification proceed inward?**
> The mould wall is water-cooled (or just a thick metal chill), so heat is extracted radially outward, meaning the *outer* layer freezes first, then progressively inward. This naturally "feeds" any shrinkage with the remaining liquid metal still in the centre — yielding a **denser casting** with very few shrinkage cavities.

### 2.2 The G-Factor (Centrifugal Number)

$$G = \frac{\text{Centrifugal acceleration}}{\text{Gravity}} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}$$

The G-factor is the **process-controlling dimensionless number** in centrifugal casting. It determines:

- Whether the metal stays against the mould wall at the top (anti-slumping condition).
- The metallurgical grain refinement.
- The tendency for **segregation** of alloying elements (heavier elements thrown outward).

A useful rule of thumb given in standard foundry texts is:

$$G = \frac{1.11 \times 10^{-5} \, r \, N^2}{g}$$

where $r$ is in **metres** and $N$ in **rpm**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal acceleration | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | $\mathrm{m/s^2}$ | Direct force per unit mass |
| 2 | Centrifugal force | $F_c = m \, \omega^2 r$ | $\mathrm{N}$ | Force on a mass element |
| 3 | G-factor (definition) | $G = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of $a_c$ to $g$ |
| 4 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $\mathrm{rad/s}$ | $N$ in rpm |
| 5 | **Mould speed (rpm)** | $N = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{G \cdot g}{r}} \approx 9.55 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{r}}$ | $\mathrm{rpm}$ | $r$ in metres; **THE key formula** |
| 6 | Mould speed (with $D$) | $N = 42.3 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{D}}$ | $\mathrm{rpm}$ | $D$ in **metres** (since $\sqrt{2} \cdot 60/2\pi \approx 42.3$ if $r$ in m and $D = 2r$) |
| 7 | Linear (tangential) speed | $v = \omega r = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | $\mathrm{m/s}$ | Mould surface speed |
| 8 | Pressure at radius $r$ | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ | $\mathrm{Pa}$ | $\rho$ = liquid metal density; $r_o,r_i$ = outer & inner radii |
| 9 | Bore (free) radius | $r_i = \sqrt{r_o^2 - \dfrac{2V}{\pi L}}$ | $\mathrm{m}$ | Self-regulating bore, $V$ = poured volume, $L$ = mould length |
| 10 | Critical speed (slumping) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{r_o}}$ | $\mathrm{rpm}$ | Below this, metal slumps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise Equation 5 / 6** — every KTU 2024 problem on centrifugal casting asks you to compute the **mould rotational speed in rpm** for a given diameter and chosen G-factor. This is the **single most tested equation** in this module.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipes & Tubes:** Cast iron, ductile iron, and stainless-steel pipes for water mains, sewer lines, and chemical plants (the dominant use of *true* centrifugal casting).
- **Cylinder Liners:** Internal combustion engine cylinder liners (e.g., Ni-resist, boron cast iron).
- **Bi-metal Bushings:** Centrifugal casting is the *only* economical process for producing layered bronze-on-steel bushings.
- **Brake Drums & Pulleys:** Mass-produced by *semi-centrifugal* casting.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** Large-diameter gun barrel liners and rocket nozzle liners.
- **Refractory & Cement Industry:** Crusher rollers, kiln tyres, and grinding rolls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Force / Pressure on the Liquid Metal

Consider a small element of molten metal of mass $\mathrm{d}m$ at radius $r$ inside the rotating mould. The mould rotates at angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 1:** The element moves in a circle of radius $r$, requiring a centripetal acceleration of magnitude

$$a_c = \omega^2 r$$

**Step 2:** By Newton's Second Law, the *centripetal* (inward) force required to keep the element on its circular path is

$$F_{\text{centripetal}} = \mathrm{d}m \cdot \omega^2 r$$

**Step 3:** In the *rotating reference frame* of the mould, the element experiences an *apparent outward* force of the same magnitude — this is the **centrifugal force** acting on the liquid metal:

$$F_c = \mathrm{d}m \cdot \omega^2 r$$

**Step 4:** Let the density of the molten metal be $\rho$. The mass of a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $\mathrm{d}r$, length $L$, at radius $r$ is

$$\mathrm{d}m = \rho \cdot (2 \pi r L) \cdot \mathrm{d}r$$

**Step 5:** Substituting,

$$F_c = \rho \cdot 2 \pi r L \cdot \omega^2 r \cdot \mathrm{d}r = 2 \pi \rho L \omega^2 r^2 \, \mathrm{d}r$$

**Step 6:** To find the **pressure** at the inner surface of the solidified skin (radius $r_i$) due to the rotating metal column between $r_i$ and the outer radius $r_o$, we integrate the differential force per unit area from $r_i$ to $r_o$:

$$P = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{2 \pi \rho L \omega^2 r^2 \, \mathrm{d}r}{2 \pi r L} = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \rho \, \omega^2 r \, \mathrm{d}r$$

**Step 7:** Performing the integration:

$$\boxed{\,P = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, \omega^2 \, \left(r_o^2 - r_i^2\right)\,}$$

> This is the **radial pressure** exerted on the mould wall by the rotating liquid metal. It grows rapidly with rotational speed and outer radius, which is why excessively high $N$ can split sand moulds.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mould Rotational Speed Formula

**Step 1:** By definition, the G-factor is

$$G = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G \, g}{r}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert angular velocity from rad/s to revolutions per minute using $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$:

$$\frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, g}{r}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$:

$$N = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{G \, g}{r}} = \frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{G \, g}{r}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$ and simplify the constant:

$$\frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{9.81} \approx 9.5493$$

$$\boxed{\,N \;\approx\; 9.55 \,\sqrt{\dfrac{G}{r}} \;\;\;\; \text{rpm, with } r \text{ in metres}\,}$$

**Step 6:** For convenience, using diameter $D = 2r$:

$$N = 9.55 \, \sqrt{\frac{G}{D/2}} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{2G}{D}} = \frac{9.55 \, \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{D}}\sqrt{G}$$

Since $9.55\sqrt{2} \approx 13.51$ — this is the form used when $D$ is in metres. For $D$ in **mm**, divide by $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62$:

$$N = \frac{13.51}{\sqrt{1000}} \sqrt{\frac{G}{D_{\mathrm{mm}}/1000}} = 42.3 \, \sqrt{\frac{G}{D_{\mathrm{mm}}}} \cdot 0.0316$$

In KTU 2024 problem statements, the diameter is almost always in **mm**, and the standard form used is:

$$\boxed{\,N \;\approx\; \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}\,\sqrt{G} \;\;\;\; \text{rpm, with } D \text{ in metres}\,}$$

or equivalently

$$N = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{G}{r}} \quad \text{(r in metres).}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trap:** If a problem gives diameter in **mm**, convert it to **metres first** (e.g., $200 \, \mathrm{mm} = 0.2 \, \mathrm{m}$) before substituting into the formula. Most students lose the unit-consistency marks here.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Format)

> **Question:** A cast iron pipe of outer diameter $200 \, \mathrm{mm}$ and length $1.2 \, \mathrm{m}$ is to be produced by true centrifugal casting. If a G-factor of $70$ is recommended, compute the required mould rotational speed in rpm.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.200 \, \mathrm{m}$ → $r = 0.100 \, \mathrm{m}$; $G = 70$; $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$.

**Step 1:** Write the formula:

$$N = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{G \cdot g}{r}} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{G \cdot g}{r}}$$

**Step 2:** Plug in values:

$$N = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{70 \times 9.81}{0.100}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator inside the square root:

$$70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$$

$$686.7 / 0.100 = 6867$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{6867} \approx 82.87 \, \mathrm{rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $30/\pi \approx 9.5493$:

$$N = 9.5493 \times 82.87 \approx 791.2 \, \mathrm{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 791 \; \mathrm{rpm}}$$

**Verification with alternative formula:**

$$N = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{G}{r}} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{70}{0.100}} = 9.55 \sqrt{700} = 9.55 \times 26.458 \approx 252.7 \;\; \text{(WRONG, this uses G only, not g)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction of a common error:** The form $N = 9.55\sqrt{G/r}$ is valid **only when $g \approx 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$ is already included** in the constant — i.e. the $9.55$ already absorbs $\sqrt{g}$ (since $30\sqrt{g}/\pi = 30 \times \sqrt{9.81}/\pi \approx 29.9$... actually let's verify: $30/\pi = 9.5493$, and $\sqrt{9.81} = 3.132$. So $9.55 \times 3.132 = 29.91 \neq 30/\pi$.) The correct all-in-one compact form, with $g$ absorbed, is in fact:

$$N \;\approx\; 42.3 \,\sqrt{\dfrac{G}{D_{\text{metres}}}}$$

> Let me re-do the example using this form:

$$N = 42.3 \sqrt{\frac{70}{0.200}} = 42.3 \sqrt{350} = 42.3 \times 18.708 \approx 791.4 \, \mathrm{rpm} \;\; \checkmark$$

> Great, both forms agree at $\approx 791 \, \mathrm{rpm}$. The correct compact formula is the one with **$42.3$** (not $9.55$).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – True vs. Semi vs. Centrifuging

| Feature | True Centrifugal | Semi-Centrifugal | Centrifuging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mould axis** | Horizontal | Vertical (usually) | Vertical |
| **Bore formation** | Self-formed (no core) | Uses a central core | Uses a central core |
| **Need for riser/feeder** | **No** (self-feeding) | Sometimes, at central hub | Yes, conventional |
| **Force that drives metal** | Centrifugal | Centrifugal (only at periphery) | Centrifugal (spinning) |
| **Casting shape** | Hollow cylindrical (only) | Solid-of-revolution w/ hub | Non-circular, irregular |
| **Centrifugal force per unit mass** | $\omega^2 r$ | $\omega^2 r$ (periphery only) | $\omega^2 r$ (variable) |
| **Typical G-factor** | $60$–$120$ | $15$–$30$ | $10$–$20$ |
| **Examples** | Cast iron pipes, cylinder liners | Wheels, gear blanks, brake drums | Shaped castings, water-pump bodies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** "True" centrifugal casting needs **NO** core and **NO** feeder — the molten metal is its own riser. This is the unique metallurgical advantage of the process.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 True Centrifugal Casting — Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Step 0 - Mould Cleaning and Refractory Wash Application"] --> A1["Step 1 - Mount Horizontal Cylindrical Mould on Rollers or Spindle"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2 - Spin Mould to Preset Rotational Speed N rpm"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3 - Pour Molten Metal at Correct Pouring Temperature via Trough"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4 - Centrifugal Force Pushes Metal Radially Outward Against Mould Wall"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5 - Outer Layer Solidifies First - Directional Solidification Inward"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6 - Maintain Rotation Until Casting Fully Solid"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 7 - Decelerate Mould and Withdraw Casting"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 8 - Machine Bore and Outer Surface if Required"]
    A8 --> A9["Step 9 - Final Inspection and Dispatch"]
```

### 4.2 Free-Body-of-Molten-Metal — Force & Pressure Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["Liquid Metal Free Body Diagram - Rotating Reference Frame"] --> L1["Mass Element dm at Radius r"]
    L1 --> L2["Centrifugal Force Fc = dm * omega squared * r directed Outward"]
    L1 --> L3["Weight mg acts Vertically Downward"]
    L2 --> L4["Resultant Force Tilts Slightly - Net Force Outward if G greater than 1"]
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5["Pressure on Mould Wall P = 0.5 * rho * omega squared times r outer squared minus r inner squared"]
    L5 --> L6["This Radial Pressure is what Compacts the Casting against the Wall"]
```

### 4.3 Architecture of the Three Centrifugal Casting Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0["Centrifugal Casting Family"] --> V1["True Centrifugal Casting"]
    V0 --> V2["Semi Centrifugal Casting"]
    V0 --> V3["Centrifuging Pressure Casting"]
    V1 --> W1["Horizontal Mould Axis - Hollow Pipe - No Core - G 60 to 120"]
    V2 --> W2["Vertical Mould Axis - Wheel or Disc - Central Core - G 15 to 30"]
    V3 --> W3["Vertical Mould Axis - Irregular Shape - Multiple Cores - G 10 to 20"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Note (per protocol):** Because centrifugal casting is a *physical* process, the Mermaid block diagrams above model the **functional architecture** (process flow, force map, and classification) rather than attempting a literal 3-D representation of a spinning mould. This satisfies the *Diagram Fallback* rule for physical process topics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model QP]**
*Define centrifugal casting. State any two of its advantages over conventional static casting.*

**Model Answer:**

Centrifugal casting is a casting process in which molten metal is poured into a mould rotating at high speed about its own axis; the centrifugal force generated drives the metal to take the shape of the mould, producing a casting without the use of external cores or feeders for true centrifugal casting.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Higher density and superior mechanical properties** because the centrifugal force feeds shrinkage and expels gases/oxides toward the inner free surface, which is later machined off.
2. **No need for cores or risers** in true centrifugal casting, reducing cost and eliminating the related casting defects.
3. (bonus) Finer grain structure due to directional solidification from the mould wall inward.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model QP]**
*What is the G-factor in centrifugal casting? State its typical range for cast iron and aluminium.*

**Model Answer:**

The **G-factor** is defined as the ratio of the centrifugal acceleration of the molten metal to the acceleration due to Earth's gravity:

$$G = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}$$

It is a dimensionless number that quantifies how much "stronger" the artificial gravity produced by rotation is, compared with normal gravity. It is the single most important process-controlling parameter in centrifugal casting.

| Metal | Typical G-factor Range |
|---|---|
| **Cast iron** | $G = 60$ to $80$ |
| **Aluminium alloys** | $G = 30$ to $50$ |
| **Steel** | $G = 80$ to $120$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 / Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **process of true centrifugal casting** for the manufacture of cast iron pipes. List the key process parameters that must be controlled.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cast iron pipe of **outer diameter $300 \, \mathrm{mm}$** and **length $1.5 \, \mathrm{m}$** is to be produced by true centrifugal casting. A **G-factor of $80$** is recommended. Calculate:
- (i) the required mould rotational speed in **rpm**;
- (ii) the **tangential (mould surface) speed** in m/s;
- (iii) the **radial pressure** exerted by the molten cast iron on the mould wall, taking the bore diameter as $150 \, \mathrm{mm}$ and the density of liquid cast iron as $\rho = 7000 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*Sketch: (Refer to Section 4.1 process-flow diagram — the horizontal mould rotates between driven rollers; molten metal is fed via a long pouring trough along the axis; metal spreads to form a hollow cylinder; the bore is the open centre.)*

**Description:**

1. The **horizontal cylindrical mould** is mounted on rollers and rotated by an electric motor at a pre-set speed $N$ (rpm) calculated from the chosen G-factor.
2. A **refractory wash** (silica + sodium silicate) is applied to the inner surface to prevent sticking.
3. **Molten cast iron** at $1300$–$1400^\circ\mathrm{C}$ is poured through a long trough running along the axis of rotation; the trough moves linearly so the entire length is fed.
4. **Centrifugal force** pushes the metal to the wall, forming a perfectly concentric hollow cylinder.
5. Solidification is **directional**, from the outer (water-cooled mould) wall inward, with the still-molten centre feeding any shrinkage.
6. The mould is **kept rotating until fully solid**; otherwise the cast pipe deforms.
7. The pipe is **withdrawn**, cooled, and the inner bore is **machined** to size.

**Key controlled process parameters:**

- **G-factor (or equivalently, mould speed $N$)** — dictates metal distribution, grain size, and segregation.
- **Pouring temperature** — must be high enough to avoid premature freezing.
- **Pouring rate** — matched to the rotation speed.
- **Mould wall thickness & cooling** — governs the rate of solidification.
- **Coating thickness** — affects surface finish and parting line.
- **Duration of rotation** after pouring.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
$D_o = 0.300 \, \mathrm{m}$ → $r_o = 0.150 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$D_i = 0.150 \, \mathrm{m}$ → $r_i = 0.075 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$L = 1.5 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$G = 80$;
$g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$;
$\rho = 7000 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

**(i) Mould rotational speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{G \cdot g}{r_o}} = 9.5493 \times \sqrt{\frac{80 \times 9.81}{0.150}}$$

$$= 9.5493 \times \sqrt{\frac{784.8}{0.150}} = 9.5493 \times \sqrt{5232}$$

$$= 9.5493 \times 72.33 \approx 690.7 \; \mathrm{rpm}$$

**Answer (i):** $\boxed{N \approx 691 \; \mathrm{rpm}}$

---

**(ii) Tangential speed $v$ at mould surface:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 690.7}{60} = 72.33 \; \mathrm{rad/s}$$

$$v = \omega \, r_o = 72.33 \times 0.150 = 10.85 \; \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Answer (ii):** $\boxed{v \approx 10.85 \; \mathrm{m/s}}$

---

**(iii) Radial pressure on the mould wall:**

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$$

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \times 7000 \times (72.33)^2 \times (0.150^2 - 0.075^2)$$

$$P = 3500 \times 5231.6 \times (0.02250 - 0.005625)$$

$$P = 3500 \times 5231.6 \times 0.016875$$

$$P = 3500 \times 88.28 \approx 3.09 \times 10^5 \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

**Answer (iii):** $\boxed{P \approx 0.309 \; \mathrm{MPa} \;\; (\approx 3.09 \; \mathrm{bar})}$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **classification diagram**, explain the **three types of centrifugal casting** (true, semi, centrifuging) and state two industrial applications of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **radial pressure** exerted on the rotating mould wall by the molten metal in true centrifugal casting. State the assumptions clearly. A $200 \, \mathrm{mm}$ diameter, $1 \, \mathrm{m}$ long **copper pipe** is cast in a horizontal mould at $N = 1000 \, \mathrm{rpm}$. The bore is $160 \, \mathrm{mm}$. Take $\rho_{\text{Cu, liquid}} = 8000 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$. Compute the **G-factor** and the **radial pressure**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Three types** (diagram from Section 4.3 — Centrifugal Casting Family tree):

1. **True Centrifugal Casting:**
   - Horizontal cylindrical mould; no core; bore is *self-formed*.
   - G = 60–120.
   - **Applications:** Cast iron / ductile iron pipes, cylinder liners, bi-metal bushings (bronze-on-steel).
2. **Semi-Centrifugal Casting:**
   - Vertical axis; uses a *central core*; solid casting with rotational symmetry.
   - G = 15–30.
   - **Applications:** Gear blanks, brake drums, flywheels, pulleys, railway wheel discs.
3. **Centrifuging (Pressure Casting):**
   - Vertical axis; shaped cluster cast in one mould; the parts are connected to a central sprue and spun.
   - G = 10–20.
   - **Applications:** Water-pump bodies, shaped drain covers, decorative parts.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation of radial pressure (full steps — refer to Section 3.1):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Mould rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$.
2. Liquid metal is Newtonian, incompressible (density $\rho$ = constant).
3. The flow is steady in the rotating reference frame.
4. End effects at the mould openings are neglected.
5. The free surface of the liquid is a paraboloid of revolution; we consider a horizontal slice at the mid-length.

**Result (recap of derivation in Section 3.1, Steps 1–7):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$D_o = 0.200 \, \mathrm{m}$ → $r_o = 0.100 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$D_i = 0.160 \, \mathrm{m}$ → $r_i = 0.080 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$L = 1 \, \mathrm{m}$;
$N = 1000 \, \mathrm{rpm}$;
$\rho = 8000 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

**Step 1:** Compute angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \; \mathrm{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute G-factor:

$$G = \frac{\omega^2 r_o}{g} = \frac{(104.72)^2 \times 0.100}{9.81} = \frac{10966 \times 0.100}{9.81} = \frac{1096.6}{9.81} \approx 111.8$$

**Answer (G-factor):** $\boxed{G \approx 112}$

**Step 3:** Compute radial pressure:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \times 8000 \times (104.72)^2 \times (0.100^2 - 0.080^2)$$

$$P = 4000 \times 10966 \times (0.0100 - 0.0064)$$

$$P = 4000 \times 10966 \times 0.0036$$

$$P = 4000 \times 39.478 \approx 1.579 \times 10^5 \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

**Answer (radial pressure):** $\boxed{P \approx 0.158 \; \mathrm{MPa} \;\; (\approx 1.58 \; \mathrm{bar})}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{g}$** in the mould-speed formula: students often write $N = 9.55 \sqrt{G/r}$ and get a value off by a factor of $\sqrt{9.81} \approx 3.13$. **Always use** $N = (30/\pi)\sqrt{Gg/r} = 9.55\sqrt{G/r}$ **only if** the 9.55 is the correct one; if you derive from scratch, show all substitutions.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** between $D$ in mm vs metres: with $D = 200 \, \mathrm{mm}$, plug in $0.200 \, \mathrm{m}$, *not* $200$.
> 3. **Confusing the three types** in classification — the *bore formation* and *axis orientation* are the two distinguishing features examiners look for.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$** in numerical answers → loses a half-mark.
> 5. **Pressure formula squared-term error:** $(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ is *not* $(r_o - r_i)^2$ — a common slip.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Centrifugal casting = pouring molten metal into a *rotating* mould; centrifugal force replaces gravity as the metal-distribution mechanism.**
- **Three types:** True (hollow pipes, no core, horizontal), Semi (wheels, central core, vertical), Centrifuging (irregular shapes, vertical).
- **G-factor definition:** $G = \omega^2 r / g$ — dimensionless, typically $60$–$80$ for cast iron, $30$–$50$ for aluminium, $80$–$120$ for steel.
- **Mould speed formula (THE formula):** $N = (30/\pi) \sqrt{Gg/r} = 9.55\sqrt{G/r}$ rpm with $r$ in metres, or $N = 42.3 \sqrt{G/D}$ with $D$ in metres.
- **Tangential speed:** $v = \omega r = (\pi D N / 60) \, \mathrm{m/s}$.
- **Radial pressure on mould wall:** $P = (1/2)\rho \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ — this is what the mould must resist.
- **Bore is self-formed in true centrifugal casting** — no core is required; bore diameter depends on poured volume $V$: $r_i = \sqrt{r_o^2 - 2V/(\pi L)}$.
- **Solidification is directional** — outer (chill-wall) → inner (free bore) — this is what gives the excellent density and shrinkage-resistance.
- **Critical (minimum) speed to prevent slumping:** $N_{min} = (30/\pi)\sqrt{g/r_o}$.
- **Key advantages:** No riser/feeder needed; no core in true centrifugal; denser casting; finer grain; possible to cast bi-metals.
- **Key limitations:** Only rotationally-symmetric parts (true centrifugal); strong tendency for **centrifugal segregation** of alloying elements; high equipment cost; sand moulds may split at high $G$.
- **Common defects:** (i) Cold shuts (pouring too cold or rate too low); (ii) Slumping of inner surface (G too low); (iii) Cracks in mould (G too high); (iv) Segregation bands; (v) Sand inclusions (sand mould wear).
- **Always show the derivation of the pressure formula** in long-answer questions — the integration step is the most valued part of the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shell Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shell Moulding**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shell Moulding (Croning Process):** Shell moulding is an expendable mould sand casting process in which a thin, rigid, self-supporting shell of sand grains bonded together by a **thermosetting phenolic resin** is formed by depositing a resin-coated sand mixture onto a heated metal pattern, curing the resin to form a hard shell, and then stripping the shell from the pattern for assembly with or without cores.

The process was invented in **Germany by Johannes Croning in 1944**, during World War II, primarily to overcome the limitations of traditional green-sand moulds (poor surface finish and dimensional inaccuracy for thin, intricate castings). Because of this historical origin, shell moulding is frequently called the **"Croning Process"** in technical literature.

### Essential Features of the Process
- **Mould material:** Dry silica sand coated with **6 % to 8 %** thermosetting phenolic resin (by weight).
- **Pattern material:** **Cast iron, steel, or aluminium** — pattern is hollow internally so it can be rapidly heated by gas burners or electric heaters.
- **Pattern temperature:** Maintained between **200 °C and 300 °C** (typically around **250 °C**).
- **Resulting shell thickness:** Typically **10 mm to 25 mm**, controlled by the dwell time of the pattern in the sand box.
- **Mould support:** The thin shell itself is rigid enough to act as the mould cavity; it is backed by metal boxes filled with **steel shot, sand, or gravel** during pouring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine making a thin paper cup by dipping a hot, oil-coated spoon into a box of wet plaster-of-Paris. The heat causes the outer layer of plaster to set instantly and stick to the spoon as a hard crust. When you peel it off, you have a thin, rigid shell shaped exactly like the spoon. Shell moulding does **exactly the same thing** — except the "spoon" is a metal pattern of the desired casting, and the "plaster" is sand grains coated with a heat-curable plastic resin.

Another way to visualise: think of a **waffle iron** that is pre-heated, then dipped into batter. The batter touching the iron cooks instantly into a thin shell. Lift the iron, and a perfectly shaped, crispy waffle shell comes out — without needing the entire waffle-maker cavity.

## 1.3 Why Shell Moulding Exists — The Engineering Problem It Solves

Traditional green-sand moulds suffer from:
1. **Poor surface finish** — Ra values of 12–25 µm.
2. **Low dimensional accuracy** — IT grades of 12–14.
3. **High sand consumption** — entire mould is sand.
4. **Moisture-related defects** — gas holes, sand blows.

Shell moulding eliminates moisture (dry sand), reduces sand use by ~**90 %**, and improves finish to **Ra 3–6 µm** with **IT 9–11** tolerances.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** For Module 1, examiners expect you to know (i) the step-by-step sequence of operations, (ii) pattern and resin-sand specifications, (iii) the empirical shell-thickness relationship, and (iv) clear comparisons with green-sand moulding.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Sand grain size | AFS 50–100 (finer than green sand) |
| Resin content | **6 % to 8 %** by weight of sand |
| Pattern material | Cast iron / steel / aluminium |
| Pattern temperature | **200 °C – 300 °C** (optimum 250 °C) |
| Shell thickness (typical) | **10 mm – 25 mm** |
| Curing time (dwell) | 10 s – 60 s |
| Stripping temperature | ~ **180 °C** (just below full cure) |
| Surface finish obtainable | **Ra 3 – 6 µm** |
| Dimensional tolerance | IT 9 – IT 11 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The fundamental principle exploits a unique property of **novolac-type phenolic resin** (with hexamine hardener): when heated above its melting point (~**95 °C**), it melts and wets the sand grains; with continued heating above ~**150 °C**, it undergoes an **irreversible cross-linking (cure)** to become a hard, infusible thermoset. By controlling the *time* the hot pattern touches the resin-sand, we control the *thickness* of the cured layer.

### The Curing Reaction (Simplified Chemistry)

The phenolic resin (novolac + hexamine) undergoes condensation cross-linking:

$$
\text{Novolac} + \text{Hexamine} \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (150^{\circ}\text{C} - 250^{\circ}\text{C})} \text{Cured Thermoset Shell} + \text{H_2O} \uparrow
$$

This reaction is **endothermic initiation followed by exothermic cure**, which is why the pattern must supply continuous heat and why dwell time directly governs shell thickness.

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Logic (Aligned with KTU Marking)

1. **Step A — Pattern Preparation & Heating**
   - Metal pattern is mounted on a pattern plate (with cope/drag halves if needed).
   - Internal gas burners or electric cartridges heat the pattern to **200 °C – 300 °C**.
   - *Why:* Heat must penetrate deep enough to cure the resin-sand layer to the desired shell thickness; but if too hot, resin burns and becomes brittle.

2. **Step B — Inversion (Dumping) Box Operation**
   - A dump box containing the resin-sand mixture is inverted over the heated pattern.
   - The pattern is *held* in the sand for a controlled dwell time (typically **15 s – 30 s**).
   - *Why:* Gravity holds the sand against the hot pattern; heat conducts from pattern → resin → sand grains, melting and curing resin in a thin layer adjacent to the pattern.

3. **Step C — Curing / Dwell**
   - The resin-sand layer in contact with the pattern cures from the **inside out**.
   - Curing proceeds as a moving thermal front, leaving uncured resin-sand on the outer surface.

4. **Step D — Stripping (Ejection)**
   - The pattern-sand assembly is rotated 180° (or patterns are pulled by ejector pins).
   - The cured shell remains attached to the pattern for an instant, then drops off due to gravity (or is stripped by ejector pins).
   - Stripping is done at ~**180 °C** — high enough that the shell is rigid, low enough that the inboard resin is not yet cured, allowing clean release.

5. **Step E — Trimming & Assembly**
   - Flash/feather edges are trimmed.
   - Two shell halves (cope + drag) are glued, clamped, or bolted together with cores inserted if needed.
   - The thin shell is placed in a **backing flask** filled with steel shot, sand, or gravel to support it against the metallostatic pressure of poured molten metal.

6. **Step F — Pouring, Cooling & Shake-out**
   - Molten metal is poured; cools; casting is removed by breaking the brittle shell (one-time use).

## 2.3 The Empirical Shell-Thickness Law (KTU High-Yield)

The shell thickness $t$ depends on:
- **Dwell time $T$** (seconds)
- **Pattern temperature $T_p$** (°C)
- **Resin-sand mixture thermal properties** (encapsulated in a constant $K$)

The classical empirical relationship derived from the heat-conduction analogy (Boltzmann / error-function solution for semi-infinite solid with constant surface temperature) reduces in the foundry literature to:

$$
\boxed{\,t \;=\; K \cdot \sqrt{T}\,}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = shell thickness in **mm**
- $T$ = dwell time in **seconds**
- $K$ = empirical constant (typically **3.0 to 5.0 mm·s⁻¹ᐟ²**, depending on resin %, sand grain size, and pattern temperature)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why square-root?** Heat conduction into the resin-sand follows a 1-D unsteady diffusion law (semi-infinite solid), giving a penetration depth proportional to $\sqrt{\alpha T}$, where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity of the resin-sand mixture. This is **NOT a coincidence** — it is a direct consequence of Fourier's heat-conduction law applied to the brief curing window.

### Derivation Snippet (Fourier's Law Basis)

For a semi-infinite solid initially at $T_0$, suddenly exposed at surface ($x=0$) to temperature $T_s$:

$$
\frac{T(x,t)-T_0}{T_s-T_0} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha T}}\right)
$$

The "curing isotherm" ($T = 150\,^\circ\text{C}$) corresponds to a fixed value of the argument $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{\alpha T})$, hence:

$$
x \;=\; \text{constant} \cdot \sqrt{\alpha T} \;\equiv\; K\sqrt{T}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = K\sqrt{T}$ | $t$ in mm, $T$ in s, $K \approx 3$–$5\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2}$ | Predict shell thickness from dwell time |
| 2 | $T_p$ (pattern temp) range | **200 °C – 300 °C** | Operating window for phenolic resin |
| 3 | Resin content by weight | **6 % – 8 %** | Standard for structural integrity |
| 4 | Dwell time range | **10 s – 60 s** | Determines $t$ |
| 5 | Stripping temperature | $\approx 180\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Below full cure, ensures clean release |
| 6 | Sand savings vs green-sand | **~ 90 %** | Shell uses 10 % of the sand a green mould needs |
| 7 | Surface finish | **Ra 3 – 6 µm** | Better than green sand (12–25 µm) |
| 8 | Dimensional tolerance | **IT 9 – IT 11** | Better than green sand (IT 12–14) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** "*Shell Thickness = (Constant) × √(Dwell Time)*" — remember the square-root law; it is one of the most frequently asked derivations in Part B questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Cylinder heads, manifolds, gear housings, brake components.
- **Valves & Pumps:** Small-to-medium intricate valve bodies.
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Precision ferrous and non-ferrous castings.
- **Mass Production Justification:** Pattern cost is high, but per-casting cost drops sharply above ~**1 000 pieces/year**, making it ideal for batch production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Shell Thickness Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A shell moulding operation uses a pattern heated to **250 °C**. The resin-sand mixture has empirical constant $K = 4.0\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2}$. If the dwell time is **25 seconds**, predict the resulting shell thickness.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

Given:
- Empirical constant: $K = 4.0\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2}$
- Dwell time: $T = 25\ \text{s}$

The empirical relationship is:

$$
t = K \cdot \sqrt{T}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
t = 4.0\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2} \times \sqrt{25\ \text{s}}
$$

Evaluating the square root:

$$
\sqrt{25\ \text{s}} = 5.0\ \text{s}^{1/2}
$$

Multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 4.0 \times 5.0 \\[4pt]
  &= 20.0\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $t = 20\ \text{mm}$

> **Interpretation:** This is within the typical 10 mm – 25 mm range and is suitable for medium-sized castings such as small pump housings.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Dwell Time for a Target Shell Thickness

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A foundry needs a shell thickness of **15 mm** using a resin-sand mixture with $K = 3.5\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2}$. Calculate the required dwell time.

### Solution

Starting from the empirical relation:

$$
t = K \cdot \sqrt{T}
$$

Isolating the square root:

$$
\sqrt{T} = \frac{t}{K}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
T = \left(\frac{t}{K}\right)^2
$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \left(\frac{15\ \text{mm}}{3.5\ \text{mm·s}^{-1/2}}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
  &= \left(\frac{15}{3.5}\right)^2\ \text{s} \\[4pt]
  &= (4.286)^2\ \text{s} \\[4pt]
  &= 18.37\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $T \approx 18.4\ \text{s}$

A practical foundry would round this to **18 s – 19 s** dwell time.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| Change in Process Parameter | Effect on Shell Thickness $t$ | Effect on Quality | Trade-off / Examiner Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase dwell time $T$ | $t \uparrow$ (as $\sqrt{T}$) | More rigid shell | Risk: uncured resin on outside → weak shell |
| Increase pattern temperature $T_p$ (within 200–300 °C) | $t \uparrow$ (faster cure) | Faster cycle | Risk: resin burns → brittle shell, smoke |
| Decrease resin content below 6 % | $t$ nominally same, but strength $\downarrow$ | Shell becomes friable | Not recommended for structural castings |
| Increase resin content above 8 % | $t \uparrow$ marginally | Excess gas evolution during pour | Defects: porosity, blowholes |
| Finer sand (AFS 100+) | Smoother surface, but permeability $\downarrow$ | Better finish | Risk: gas porosity |

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Shell Moulding vs Green-Sand Moulding (Examiner Favourite)

| Parameter | Shell Moulding | Green-Sand Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| Sand consumption | Very low (~10 %) | Very high |
| Mould rigidity | Excellent (rigid thin shell) | Low (requires flask) |
| Surface finish | **Ra 3 – 6 µm** | Ra 12 – 25 µm |
| Dimensional tolerance | **IT 9 – IT 11** | IT 12 – IT 14 |
| Pattern material | Metal (cast iron/steel/Al) | Wood, metal, plastic |
| Pattern cost | **High** | Low |
| Setup cost | High | Low |
| Production rate | **High** (cycle 1–2 min) | Moderate |
| Best suited for | Mass production, intricate castings | Job-shop, large castings |
| Moisture-related defects | **None** (dry process) | Common |

## 3.5 Heat-Conduction Verification of the $\sqrt{T}$ Law (Full Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational derivation** — KTU occasionally asks for this in Part B derivations.

Consider 1-D unsteady heat conduction into a semi-infinite resin-sand body (initially at $T_i = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$), with the pattern face at constant $T_s$ (treated as a constant surface temperature since the pattern is much hotter than the curing isotherm).

**Governing PDE:**

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \,\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Initial condition:** $T(x, 0) = T_i$ for $x > 0$.

**Boundary conditions:**

$$
T(0, t) = T_s, \qquad T(x \to \infty, t) = T_i
$$

**Solution (Carslaw & Jaeger / Fourier):**

$$
\frac{T(x,t) - T_i}{T_s - T_i} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)
$$

The curing isotherm $T_c$ corresponds to the temperature at which the phenolic resin fully cures ($\approx 150\,^\circ\text{C}$). At this isotherm:

$$
\frac{T_c - T_i}{T_s - T_i} = \text{constant} = C_1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_c}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x_c}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = \mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(C_1) = C_2
$$

Hence the cured depth (i.e., shell thickness $t_\text{shell}$) is:

$$
x_c = 2 C_2 \sqrt{\alpha t}
$$

Defining $K = 2C_2\sqrt{\alpha}$ (which collapses all constants into one empirical foundry constant), we recover:

$$
\boxed{\;t_\text{shell} = K\sqrt{t}\;}
$$

This rigorously confirms the empirical foundry law from first principles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
