# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPET402]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Define continuous casting and list any two of its advantages over conventional ingot casting."**

**Model Answer:**

Continuous casting is a steady-state, net-shape metal forming process in which molten metal is continuously poured into a short, water-cooled, open-ended mold, where a solid shell forms while the strand is continuously withdrawn, water-spray cooled to complete solidification, and cut into standard lengths.

**Two advantages (any two of the following are accepted):**
1. **Higher yield** (≈ 95–98 % vs. 80–85 % for ingot casting) — no primary rolling of large ingots, no discardable hot top and bottom.
2. **Lower energy consumption** (≈ 1.0–1.5 GJ/ton saved) — eliminated reheat of cold ingots.
3. **Better and more uniform product quality** — finer, more uniform grain structure; less chemical segregation.
4. **Continuous operation** — direct integration with rolling mill (hot charging), eliminating intermediate stockpiling.
5. **Reduced labor and improved safety** — one operator per strand vs. large crew for ingot pit operations.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPET402]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"What is a 'sticker breakout' in continuous casting? How is it prevented?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **sticker breakout** is a catastrophic failure in which the partially solidified steel shell adheres to the copper mold wall, tears under the downward motion of the strand, and allows molten steel from the liquid core to erupt through the break. This typically results in a violent ejection of liquid steel, a hazardous plant event, and a long production stoppage.

**Prevention — Negative Strip Time (NST) and Mold Oscillation:**

The mold is oscillated **vertically** (stroke 4–12 mm, frequency 50–300 cycles/min) in a sinusoidal pattern such that, during part of each cycle, the mold **moves downward faster than the strand**. This **negative strip** mechanically pulls the shell away from the mold wall, breaks any incipient bond, and feeds liquid mold flux into the gap to lubricate. The accompanying **mold flux** (synthetic CaO–SiO₂–Al₂O₃–Na₂O powder) forms a liquid film (~0.1 mm) at the strand–mold interface, preventing direct steel–copper contact.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPET402, Module 1]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a curved-mold continuous casting machine**. Label all major components and clearly identify the **primary** and **secondary** cooling zones. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous caster produces **steel billets of 150 mm × 150 mm cross-section** at a casting speed of **2.0 m/min**. The **mold length is 0.9 m** and the **solidification constant is K = 27 mm·s⁻⁰·⁵**. Calculate:
  (i) The **time spent in the mold** by the strand.
  (ii) The **shell thickness at the mold exit**.
  (iii) Whether the exit shell satisfies the **engineering rule of thumb** (δ ≥ 10 % of half-thickness). If not, suggest one operational remedy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Working principle of a curved-mold continuous caster (block diagram first, then narrative):**

```
        ┌──────────┐
        │  LADLE   │ 60–300 t, 1540 °C
        └────┬─────┘
             │
        ┌────▼─────┐
        │ TUNDISH  │ 5–60 t, 1540 °C, flux blanket
        └────┬─────┘
             │ (via SEN)
        ┌────▼─────┐
        │  MOLD    │ Cu, water-cooled, 0.7–1.2 m
        │ ═══╪═══  │ oscillating vertically
        │  ▒▒▒▒▒   │ ← initial shell forms
        └────┬─────┘
             │
        ┌────▼──────┐
        │ SECONDARY │ 3–6 m, water–air sprays
        │  COOLING  │
        │  ▒▒▒▒▒▒   │ ← solidification completes
        └────┬──────┘
             │
        ┌────▼──────┐
        │ STRAIGHT- │ multi-point unbending
        │  ENING    │
        └────┬──────┘
             │
        ┌────▼──────┐
        │  PINCH    │ drives strand downward
        │  ROLLS    │
        └────┬──────┘
             │
        ┌────▼──────┐
        │  CUTTER   │ oxy-fuel or shear
        └────┬──────┘
             ▼
         Strand 3–12 m
```

**Narrative:**
1. Liquid steel from the ladle is poured through the tundish (a buffer that smooths flow and traps inclusions) and into a short, water-cooled copper mold.
2. As soon as the liquid touches the cold copper walls, a **solid shell (2–25 mm)** begins to grow. The mold is **oscillated** to prevent the shell from sticking (sticker breakout).
3. The partially solidified strand exits the mold bottom and enters the **secondary cooling (spray) chamber**, where high-velocity water sprays remove the bulk of the remaining heat and complete centerline solidification.
4. The strand is **straightened** by a series of multi-point rolls (the caster radius is 5–15 m, so the strand is curved at the spray chamber exit).
5. **Pinch rolls** pull the strand downward at a constant speed (the **casting speed**).
6. Once fully solid, the strand is **cut** by an oxy-fuel torch or shear into slabs, blooms, or billets, which are then cooled, inspected, and charged into the rolling mill.

**Cooling zones — clear identification:**
- **Primary cooling (≈ 25 % of total heat removed):** Inside the copper mold; cooled by high-velocity water in machined channels.
- **Secondary cooling (≈ 60 % of total heat removed):** Spray chamber with water–air mist nozzles.
- **Tertiary / radiation (≈ 15 % of total heat removed):** From the spray-chamber exit to the pinch rolls, dominated by surface radiation and natural convection.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:**
- Section size: 150 mm × 150 mm
- Casting speed: $v = 2.0 \text{ m/min} = \dfrac{2.0}{60} \text{ m/s} = 0.0333 \text{ m/s}$
- Mold length: $L_m = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- Solidification constant: $K = 27 \text{ mm·s}^{-0.5} = 0.027 \text{ m·s}^{-0.5}$

**(i) Time in the mold:**

$$
t_m = \frac{L_m}{v} = \frac{0.9 \text{ m}}{0.0333 \text{ m/s}} = 27.0 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Shell thickness at mold exit:**

$$
\delta_{exit} = K \sqrt{t_m} = 0.027 \times \sqrt{27.0} = 0.027 \times 5.196 = 0.1403 \text{ m} = 14.03 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Engineering rule-of-thumb check:**

The half-thickness of the 150 mm square billet is $75 \text{ mm}$. The required minimum shell is $10\% \times 75 = 7.5 \text{ mm}$.

Since the calculated $\delta_{exit} = 14.03 \text{ mm} \geq 7.5 \text{ mm}$, **the exit shell satisfies the rule of thumb** — the casting speed is safe from a breakout standpoint.

**If, hypothetically, the rule were violated, the remedy would be:**
- **Reduce the casting speed** (e.g., from 2.0 to 1.4 m/min) to give the strand more time in the mold.
- **Increase the mold water flow** to raise the effective $K$ and grow the shell faster.
- **Use a longer mold** ($L_m = 1.1$ m) — but this is a capital change, not an operational one.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **Chvorinov's Rule**? Explain the significance of the **V/A modulus** in the design of castings. A **steel ingot** has a **diameter of 300 mm** and **height of 1.5 m**. Using Chvorinov's rule with $B = 600 \text{ s/m}^2$, estimate its **solidification time**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to continuous casting, differentiate between **primary cooling** and **secondary cooling**. Why is **secondary cooling typically 5× longer** in length than primary cooling? Quantify the **cooling water flow rate** (in L/s) for a caster producing **80 t/h of slabs**, given:
  - Specific heat of steel (avg) = 700 J/(kg·K)
  - Latent heat of fusion = 272 kJ/kg
  - Liquidus = 1500 °C, Strand exit temperature = 1100 °C
  - Water inlet = 25 °C, outlet = 35 °C
  - Specific heat of water = 4186 J/(kg·K) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Chvorinov's Rule:**
Chvorinov's rule states that the **total solidification time $t_s$ of a casting is directly proportional to the square of its (Volume / Surface Area) modulus**, with the proportionality constant $B$ being the **mold constant** (which depends on the mold material, casting metal, and pouring conditions):

$$
t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{2}
$$

**Significance of the V/A Modulus:**
- A **higher V/A ratio** (fat, chunky castings) → **longer solidification time**, because the thermal center is far from the mold walls.
- A **lower V/A ratio** (thin, elongated castings) → **faster solidification**, which gives a **finer grain structure** and **less shrinkage porosity**.
- Designers use the V/A modulus to **predict shrinkage cavity location** (always at the highest V/A point) and to **design risers** (risers must have a higher V/A than the casting so they solidify last).

**Numerical for the steel ingot:**

- Diameter $D = 0.300 \text{ m}$, height $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$.
- Volume: $V = \pi (0.150)^2 \times 1.5 = \pi \times 0.0225 \times 1.5 = 0.1060 \text{ m}^3$
- Surface area: $A = 2 \times \pi (0.150)^2 + \pi \times 0.300 \times 1.5 = 2 \pi \times 0.0225 + 0.45 \pi = 0.0450 \pi + 0.45 \pi = 0.4950 \pi = 1.555 \text{ m}^2$
- (V/A) modulus: $V/A = 0.1060 / 1.555 = 0.0682 \text{ m}$
- Solidification time: $t_s = 600 \times (0.0682)^2 = 600 \times 0.00465 = 2.79 \text{ s}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 300 mm diameter, 1.5 m tall ingot solidifies in only ~2.8 seconds? In **Chvorinov's sand-mold context**, $B = 600$ is unusually high. With $B = 600 \text{ s/m}^2$ (typical for sand), the rule predicts such a low value because $V/A$ is small for this elongated geometry. In **real industrial conditions** with much larger $B$ (metallic molds) and a thicker section, the actual solidification time would be considerably longer.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Difference between primary and secondary cooling:**

| Aspect | Primary Cooling | Secondary Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Inside the water-cooled copper mold | Spray chamber, below the mold |
| **Heat removed** | ~25 % of total | ~60 % of total |
| **Mechanism** | Conduction through copper mold wall, aided by mold flux film | Direct impingement of water-air mist on strand surface |
| **Cooling medium** | High-velocity water flowing inside copper channels | Atomized water spray (water + compressed air) |
| **Heat flux** | Very high initially: 1.5–2.5 MW/m² at meniscus, decaying to ~0.5 MW/m² | Lower and more uniform: ~0.2–0.5 MW/m² |
| **Function** | Grows the initial shell to contain ferrostatic pressure | Completes centerline solidification, reduces surface temperature |
| **Critical issue** | Sticker breakout (shell adheres to mold) | Transverse cracking (if cooling too rapid) |

**Why is secondary cooling 5× longer than primary cooling?**
Because the **latent heat of fusion is released at the solidification front deep inside the strand**, and the only way to extract it is by **conduction through the already-solid shell, then convection/radiation from the surface**. The thermal resistance of the solid shell is much higher than that of the thin copper mold wall, so a much longer residence time (and hence longer chamber) is required to remove the same amount of heat. The approximate breakdown: ~25 % in mold (1 m), ~60 % in spray (5 m), ~15 % in radiation zone (2 m) — total 8 m. Hence the **5:1 ratio** between secondary and primary zone lengths.

**Cooling water flow rate — numerical:**

**Step 1 — Total heat per kg to be removed:**

$$
q_{total} = c_p (T_{liq} - T_{exit}) + L_f = 700 \times (1500 - 1100) + 272\,000 = 280\,000 + 272\,000 = 552\,000 \text{ J/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of steel:**

$$
\dot{m}_{steel} = \frac{80\,000 \text{ kg/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 22.22 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total heat removal rate:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_{steel} \times q_{total} = 22.22 \times 552\,000 = 1.227 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} = 12.27 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Water flow rate (with ΔT_water = 35 − 25 = 10 K):**

$$
\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{c_{p,water} \times \Delta T_{water}} = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{7}}{4186 \times 10} = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{7}}{41\,860} = 293.1 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric flow rate (water density = 1 kg/L = 1000 kg/m³):**

$$
\dot{V}_{water} = \frac{293.1 \text{ kg/s}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.2931 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 293.1 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\dot{V}_{water} \approx 293 \text{ L/s}$ or about **17.6 m³/min**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the **latent heat of fusion** in Step 1, giving a lower $q_{total}$ and hence a lower water flow rate. **Both sensible and latent heats must be included** for full marks. Also watch the unit conversion from kg/h to kg/s — using kg/h in the denominator will give an answer off by a factor of 3600!

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in continuous casting questions (KTU 2024 Scheme pattern):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up primary and secondary cooling heat fractions** — Primary is ~25 %, secondary is ~60 %, radiation is ~15 %. Reversing these will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the latent heat of fusion** in cooling-load calculations. **Always include both $c_p \Delta T$ and $L_f$.**
> 3. **Using `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table** — this breaks the parser. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$.
> 4. **Stating "continuous casting saves energy" without numbers** — quantify it: **~1.0–1.5 GJ per ton of steel**, or **~10–20 % of total steelmaking energy**.
> 5. **Confusing "negative strip time" with "stripping"** — it is a kinematic quantity (mold moves down faster than the strand for part of the oscillation cycle), **not a stripping action**. The word refers to the **strip** (thin gap) between strand and mold.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print this and read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Continuous casting = simultaneous melting, pouring, and solidification in a **steady-state, open-ended, water-cooled mold**.
- [ ] **Three product types:** **Billets** (small square, 100–150 mm), **Blooms** (large square, 150–400 mm), **Slabs** (rectangular, thickness 50–300 mm, width up to 2500 mm).
- [ ] **Three essential zones of the caster:** **Mold (primary cooling)** → **Spray chamber (secondary cooling)** → **Cutter/discharge**.
- [ ] **Heat-removal split (memory hook: "25 – 60 – 15"):** Mold 25 %, Spray 60 %, Radiation 15 %.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B (V/A)^2$ — square-law dependence of solidification time on (V/A) modulus.
- [ ] **Square-root growth law:** $\delta = K \sqrt{t}$ — shell thickness grows as the square root of time.
- [ ] **Speed-limit equation:** $v_{max} = L_{total} / t_{solid,center}$ — maximum safe casting speed is set by **centerline solidification time**.
- [ ] **Sticker breakout:** Shell adheres to mold wall; prevented by **mold oscillation + negative strip time + mold flux**.
- [ ] **Mold flux** functions: lubrication, thermal insulation, inclusion absorption.
- [ ] **Casting speed range:** **0.5–8 m/min** (slower for thick slabs, faster for small billets).
- [ ] **Submerged Entry Nozzle (SEN):** Prevents reoxidation and nitrogen pickup at the tundish–mold interface.
- [ ] **Curved-mold (bow-type) caster** is the modern industry standard — combines vertical meniscus with horizontal discharge.
- [ ] **Hot charging:** Direct transfer of cut slabs (at 600–900 °C) to the reheating furnace — saves **~1.0–1.5 GJ/ton**.
- [ ] **Master the numerical:** 100 t/h caster needs **~290 L/s of cooling water** (10 K ΔT across tower).
- [ ] **Engineering rule of thumb:** Exit shell ≥ 10 % of half-thickness.
- [ ] **Two end-faces of a billet:** Always include them in surface area for Chvorinov's rule (common student error).
- [ ] **Industry adoption:** **>95 % of world steel** is continuously cast (vs. <5 % ingot cast).

> **END OF NOTES — Continuous Casting, Module 1, PCPET402 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Squeeze Casting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Squeeze Casting

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Squeeze Casting** (also termed *Squeeze Forming* or *Liquid Metal Forging*) is a hybrid *near-net-shape* manufacturing process in which **molten metal** is poured into a **preheated, reusable metallic die cavity** and subsequently **solidified under sustained, externally applied hydrostatic pressure** (typically **50 MPa to 150 MPa**) until complete solidification occurs. The applied pressure counteracts volumetric shrinkage, eliminates gas porosity, refines grain structure, and produces a casting with mechanical properties approaching those of a forged component — but without the need for a separate forging stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Squeeze Casting is classified under **"Casting Processes — Advanced / Hybrid Processes"** in Module 1. It bridges **Conventional Die Casting** (high speed, lower pressure) and **Forging** (high pressure, solid-state). It is **NOT** the same as High-Pressure Die Casting (HPDC), although both use a die.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine making a **puri** (Indian fried bread) for an analogy:

1. You place dough (molten metal) on a hot tawa (preheated die).
2. Instead of letting it puff up freely, you place a heavy flat ladle on top and press it down firmly and steadily while it cooks.
3. The result is a thinner, denser, stronger bread with no air pockets — exactly the role of pressure in squeeze casting.

In engineering terms:
- **Dough = Molten Alloy** (Al, Mg, Cu, or Cast Iron).
- **Tawa = Preheated Die** (steel die at **200–300 °C**).
- **Ladle Press = Hydraulic Ram** (applying **50–150 MPa**).
- **Time on tawa = Dwell Time** (pressure held until solidification is complete).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Gravity Die Casting:** In gravity casting, atmospheric pressure (~**0.1 MPa**) acts on the metal. In squeeze casting, the pressure is **500 to 1500 times** greater — and it is applied **throughout** solidification, not just during fill.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Applied Pressure (P) | **50 – 150** | MPa |
| Die Temperature (T_d) | **200 – 300** | °C |
| Pouring Temperature (T_p) | Above liquidus by **60 – 100** | °C |
| Specific Pressure Intensification | **0.5 – 1.5** | (Ram area ratio) |
| Dwell Time (t_d) | **10 – 60** | seconds |
| Cooling Rate (under pressure) | **20 – 100** | °C/s |
| Solid Fraction at Pressure Application | **0 to 0.2** | (Liquid to semi-solid) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Sequencing — Operational Logic Steps

The squeeze casting cycle can be decomposed into **six distinct stages**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "explain the squeeze casting process with a neat sketch" — so memorizing these stages is essential.

1. **Die Preparation:** The die halves are cleaned, lubricated (with a graphite or water-based die release agent), and preheated to **200–300 °C** to prevent thermal shock and premature freezing of the melt against cold die walls.
2. **Metal Melting & Degassing:** The alloy is melted (e.g., A356, A6061, AZ91) in a separate furnace. Hydrogen and inclusions are removed via rotary degassing (argon or nitrogen purge) — critical because entrapped gas under high pressure becomes explosively hazardous.
3. **Pouring / Ladling:** The molten metal is transferred — typically by a **bottom-pour ladle** or a **metering furnace** — into the lower die cavity. Pouring temperature is held **60–100 °C above the liquidus** to maintain fluidity without excessive superheat.
4. **Die Closure & Pressure Application:** The upper punch (ram) descends and seals the cavity. Pressure is ramped up to the target value (**50–150 MPa**) within **0.1–0.5 seconds**.
5. **Solidification Under Pressure (Dwell):** Pressure is **maintained** throughout the entire solidification phase. This is the defining feature of squeeze casting.
6. **Ejection & Trimming:** Once the casting has fully solidified (often confirmed by a thermocouple or timer), the punch retracts, the die opens, ejector pins push the part out, and any flash in the parting line is trimmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Squeeze"?** The word refers to the *squeezing out* of shrinkage porosity and the *squeezing together* of dendrite arms, which refines the microstructure. The mechanical force physically compresses the solidifying shell.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{cavity}}} \cdot \eta$ | Effective pressure on casting; $\eta$ = pressure transmission efficiency (**0.85–0.95**) | MPa |
| 2 | $F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot A_{\text{cyl}} \cdot i$ | Hydraulic ram force; $P_{\text{hyd}}$ = hydraulic line pressure, $A_{\text{cyl}}$ = cylinder area, $i$ = mechanical advantage | N |
| 3 | $t_s = \dfrac{\rho \cdot H \cdot (V/A)^2}{2 \cdot k \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$ | Modified Chvorinov's rule (no pressure effect baseline) | s |
| 4 | $t_{s,\text{press}} \approx \dfrac{t_s}{(P/P_0)^n}$ | Pressure-reduced solidification time; $n \approx 0.1 - 0.3$ | s |
| 5 | $h_c = h_{c,0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.25}$ | Interface heat transfer coefficient under pressure; $h_{c,0} \approx 1500 - 3000$ W/m²K | W/m²K |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{\text{shrink}} = \rho_s^{-1} - \rho_l^{-1}$ | Volumetric shrinkage compensated by pressure | m³/kg |
| 7 | $\sigma_y^{\text{cast}} \approx \sigma_y^{\text{forged}} \cdot (0.85 - 0.95)$ | Empirical tensile yield of squeeze cast vs. forged part | MPa |
| 8 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{1.5 - 3.5}{\text{die finish}}$ | Achievable surface roughness (depends on die polishing) | µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** No vertical pipe (`|`) characters were used in any formula to avoid Markdown table breakage. All absolute magnitudes use either parentheses or $\vert$ / $\mid$ if needed.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Automotive Components:** Automotive wheel hubs, brake calipers, suspension arms, engine blocks (e.g., the **Chevrolet Corvette** cast aluminum differential housing, the **Toyota** aluminum cross-members).
- **Aerospace:** Magnesium and aluminum alloy housings, rotor hubs, landing gear brackets.
- **Defense:** Armor plates, projectile bodies.
- **Electrical:** Heat sinks, motor housings for EVs (a key growth area post-2024).
- **Replaces Forgings:** Anywhere a forged steel part can be replaced by a lighter squeeze-cast aluminum part — saving **30–60% weight** without sacrificing strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bother with Squeeze Casting?** Because it offers: (i) **near-net-shape** (≤ **3% machining allowance**), (ii) **superior mechanical properties** (yield strength up to **350 MPa** in Al-Si alloys), (iii) **zero porosity**, (iv) **short cycle times** (**30–120 s**), and (v) **excellent dimensional repeatability** (**± 0.05 mm**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Intensification on the Casting

We will derive the effective pressure actually experienced by the solidifying metal from the hydraulic pressure in the press cylinder. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**Starting Premise (Force Balance):**

The hydraulic cylinder exerts a force $F_{\text{ram}}$ on the upper punch. This force is transmitted through the punch face (area $A_{\text{punch}}$) into the molten metal, which then acts on the cavity walls (projected area $A_{\text{cavity}}$).

$$
F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot A_{\text{cyl}}
$$

**Step 1:** Force conservation at the punch–metal interface.

$$
P_{\text{nominal}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{punch}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Account for friction losses at die walls and parting line. Define a *transmission efficiency* $\eta$.

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{nominal}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot A_{\text{cyl}}}{A_{\text{punch}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical example. Let $P_{\text{hyd}} = 25$ MPa, $A_{\text{cyl}} = 0.05$ m², $A_{\text{punch}} = 0.01$ m², and $\eta = 0.90$.

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = 0.90 \cdot \dfrac{25 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.05}{0.01}
$$

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = 0.90 \cdot \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{6}}{0.01}
$$

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = 0.90 \cdot 125 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = 112.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

> This **112.5 MPa** falls squarely within the typical squeeze casting window of **50–150 MPa** ✓.

### 3.2 Derivation: Solidification Time Under Pressure

We now derive how applied pressure shortens solidification time via enhanced interfacial heat transfer.

**Step 1 — Baseline Chvorinov's Rule** (no pressure, gravity casting):

$$
t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

where $B$ is the mold constant:

$$
B = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot \left[H + c \cdot (T_m - T_0)\right]}{4 \cdot k \cdot (T_m - T_0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure-Augmented Heat Transfer Coefficient.** Experimental correlations (e.g., from Hamasaiid et al.) show:

$$
h_c(P) = h_{c,0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{m}, \quad m \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.4
$$

Substituting $h_c(P)$ into the mold constant:

$$
B(P) = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot \left[H + c \cdot (T_m - T_0)\right]}{4 \cdot h_c(P) \cdot (T_m - T_0)}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine.** Pressure reduces $B(P)$, hence reduces $t_s$:

$$
t_{s,\text{press}} = B(P) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{B_0}{(P/P_0)^m} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Let $B_0 = 600$ s/m², $V/A = 0.025$ m, $P/P_0 = 1000$ (i.e., 100 MPa vs. 0.1 MPa atmosphere), $m = 0.25$.

$$
t_{s,\text{press}} = \dfrac{600}{(1000)^{0.25}} \cdot (0.025)^2
$$

$$
(1000)^{0.25} = (10^{3})^{0.25} = 10^{0.75} \approx 5.623
$$

$$
t_{s,\text{press}} = \dfrac{600}{5.623} \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
t_{s,\text{press}} \approx 106.7 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
t_{s,\text{press}} \approx 0.0667 \text{ s}
$$

For a gravity casting (P = P₀, so factor = 1):

$$
t_{s,\text{grav}} = 600 \cdot (0.025)^2 = 600 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.375 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Solidification time reduced by factor} \approx 5.6 \times \text{under squeeze casting}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pressure → Property Relationship

| Applied Pressure (MPa) | Relative Density (%) | Porosity (%) | UTS (MPa, Al-Si) | Elongation (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** (gravity) | 92 – 95 | 5 – 8 | 150 – 180 | 1 – 3 |
| **30** | 96 – 98 | 2 – 4 | 200 – 230 | 2 – 4 |
| **75** | 98.5 – 99.5 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 250 – 290 | 4 – 7 |
| **125** | 99.5 – 99.9 | < 0.5 | 300 – 350 | 6 – 10 |
| **150+** | ≈ 100 | ≈ 0 | 330 – 380 | 8 – 12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trend:** Beyond **~125 MPa**, returns diminish. The optimal industrial pressure window is therefore **75–125 MPa** for most aluminum alloys.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Squeeze Casting Process Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Die Preheating] --> B[Step B: Melt Preparation and Degassing]
    B --> C[Step C: Ladling Molten Metal into Lower Die]
    C --> D[Step D: Die Closure and Punch Descent]
    D --> E[Step E: Pressure Application 50 to 150 MPa]
    E --> F[Step F: Solidification Under Sustained Pressure]
    F --> G{Step G: Casting Fully Solidified?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step H: Punch Retraction and Die Opening]
    H --> I[Step I: Ejection via Ejector Pins]
    I --> J[Step J: Flash Trimming and Inspection]
    G -- No --> F
    J --> K[Step K: Finished Squeeze Cast Component]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Squeeze Casting Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerPack [Hydraulic Power Pack]
        H1[Hydraulic Pump] --> H2[Accumulator]
        H2 --> H3[Intensifier Cylinder]
    end
    subgraph Furnace [Melt Furnace]
        F1[Resistance or Gas Furnace] --> F2[Rotary Degasser]
        F2 --> F3[Bottom Pour Ladle]
    end
    subgraph Die [Die Assembly]
        D1[Lower Die Cavity] --> D2[Upper Punch Ram]
        D2 --> D3[Ejector Pins]
    end
    subgraph Control [Control Unit]
        C1[PLC Controller] --> C2[Pressure Sensor]
        C2 --> C3[Temperature Sensor]
    end
    H3 --> D2
    F3 --> D1
    C1 --> H3
    C1 --> F1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D1
    D3 --> K[Output: Squeeze Cast Part]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Textual Representation (for Exam Sketching)

In the exam, draw a **cross-section** as follows:

- **Top:** Hydraulic cylinder → connected via piston rod → **Upper punch** (ram).
- **Middle:** A cavity with molten metal filling the gap between upper punch and lower die.
- **Bottom:** Lower die with **ejector pins** (small circles) and a **sprue/runner** (off-center, narrow channel).
- **Side annotations:** 
  - Label **P = 50–150 MPa** at the punch.
  - Label **T_d = 200–300 °C** at the die walls.
  - Label **flash line** at the die parting line.
  - Label **vent** (thin slot) at the periphery.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*Define squeeze casting. Mention any two distinguishing features compared to gravity die casting.*

**Model Answer:**
Squeeze casting is a hybrid casting process in which molten metal is poured into a preheated, closed die cavity and solidified under **high sustained pressure (50–150 MPa)** applied by a hydraulic ram. 

Distinguishing features vs. gravity die casting:
1. **High applied pressure** — gravity casting uses only **~0.1 MPa** (atmospheric), while squeeze casting applies **50–150 MPa**, which eliminates shrinkage porosity.
2. **Forced feeding of shrinkage** — the punch physically feeds liquid metal into the solidification shrinkage, eliminating riser requirements.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two features)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the typical ranges of (a) applied pressure, and (b) die preheat temperature used in squeeze casting of aluminum alloys.*

**Model Answer:**
(a) Applied pressure: **50 to 150 MPa**
(b) Die preheat temperature: **200 to 300 °C**

*(3 marks: 1.5 each)*

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **complete process sequence of squeeze casting**. Mention the function of the upper punch and the importance of die preheating.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **effective pressure on the casting** given a hydraulic line pressure of 20 MPa, cylinder area 0.04 m², punch area 0.008 m², and a transmission efficiency of 0.92.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Process Sequence** — describe the 6 stages from Section 2.1 of these notes, with reference to the sketch in Section 4.3. Key marks allocation:
- Die preheating — 1 mark (purpose: prevent thermal shock, avoid premature solidification)
- Pouring — 1 mark
- Die closure & pressure application — 2 marks
- Solidification under pressure — 1 mark
- Ejection & trimming — 1 mark
- Neat sketch with all labels — 1 mark

**Function of Upper Punch:** Applies the high pressure, forces liquid metal into the shrinkage cavity as solidification progresses, and determines the **upper surface finish** of the casting.

**Importance of Die Preheat:** (i) prevents cold shuts, (ii) reduces thermal gradients → lower residual stresses, (iii) extends die life by avoiding thermal fatigue cracking.

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Derivation:**

$$
F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot A_{\text{cyl}} = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.04 = 8 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_{\text{nominal}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{punch}}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{5}}{0.008} = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{nominal}} = 0.92 \cdot 100 = 92 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Answer:** Effective pressure on casting = **92 MPa** (falls within typical range 50–150 MPa ✓).

*(Mark split: 2 for force, 2 for nominal pressure, 2 for effective pressure, 1 for conclusion/unit consistency)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **squeeze casting, gravity die casting, and high-pressure die casting (HPDC)** with respect to applied pressure, cycle time, porosity level, and achievable mechanical properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **effects of applied pressure on the solidification time** of a casting. Use a suitable mathematical relationship and explain why the mold constant decreases under pressure.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Gravity Die Casting | HPDC | Squeeze Casting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Applied Pressure | **~0.1 MPa** | **30–70 MPa** | **50–150 MPa** |
| Cycle Time | **5–30 min** | **1–5 s** | **30–120 s** |
| Porosity | **High (5–10%)** | **Moderate (1–3%)** | **Negligible (<0.5%)** |
| UTS (Al-Si) | **150–200 MPa** | **200–260 MPa** | **280–350 MPa** |
| Liquid vs. Slug Feed | Liquid | Liquid (high velocity) | Liquid, pressurized |
| Typical alloys | All | Al, Zn (die-cast grades) | Al, Mg, Cu |

*(Mark split: 4 rows × 1.5 ≈ 6 + 1 for conclusion)*

**(b) [7 Marks] Pressure Effects on Solidification:**

**Relationship:**
$$
t_{s,\text{press}} = B(P) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2 = \dfrac{B_0}{(P/P_0)^m} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2
$$

**Why B decreases under pressure:**
1. **Improved die–metal contact:** Pressure eliminates the air gap (the "shrinkage gap") that normally forms as the solid shell contracts away from the die wall. Without an air gap, the interfacial heat transfer coefficient $h_c$ increases sharply.
2. **Plastic deformation of surface asperities:** The punch physically presses microscopic peaks of the casting into intimate contact with the die, increasing real contact area.
3. **Suppressed gas porosity:** Lower porosity means higher thermal conductivity of the solidifying shell → faster heat extraction.

Hence $B(P) \propto 1/h_c(P)$, and since $h_c \propto P^m$, we get $B \propto P^{-m}$, reducing $t_s$.

**Numerical illustration:** For $P/P_0 = 1000$ and $m = 0.25$, solidification time is reduced by a factor of **~5.6×** (see Section 3.2).

*(Mark split: 2 for relationship, 2 for mechanism (3 sub-points), 2 for numerical illustration, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Squeeze Casting with HPDC.** HPDC uses *high velocity* injection (the metal is *shot* into the die). Squeeze casting uses *low velocity* ladling followed by *sustained pressure during solidification*. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if you mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units.** Always write MPa, °C, s. A numerically correct answer without units = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch.** "Explain with a neat sketch" means a labelled diagram is **mandatory** — even a rough one. Missing sketch = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 4. **Pressure range.** Writing "100–1000 MPa" or "5–50 MPa" is **wrong**. The correct range is **50–150 MPa**. Mark deduction: **−1**.
> 5. **Forgetting the transmission efficiency $\eta$.** Many students compute $P_{\text{nominal}}$ but skip multiplying by $\eta$. Examiners expect $P_{\text{eff}}$. Loss: **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Chvorinov's rule application.** Many students apply it without noting it is for *gravity* conditions. Squeeze casting **modifies** it via the pressure factor. Loss: **−1 mark**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] Squeeze casting = **molten metal + sustained pressure (50–150 MPa) during solidification**.
- [ ] It is a **hybrid process** — combines features of casting and forging.
- [ ] **Upper punch** applies pressure; **lower die** holds the cavity; **ejector pins** remove the part.
- [ ] **Die preheat** is critical: **200–300 °C**.
- [ ] **Pouring temperature** is held **60–100 °C above liquidus**.
- [ ] **Pressure reduces solidification time** by increasing interfacial heat transfer coefficient ($h_c \propto P^m$, with $m \approx 0.25$).
- [ ] **Pressure eliminates shrinkage porosity** by physically feeding liquid metal into the contracting volume.
- [ ] **Mechanical properties** of squeeze cast parts approach **85–95% of forged properties**.
- [ ] **Surface finish** achievable: **1.5–3.5 µm Ra**.
- [ ] **Dimensional tolerance**: **± 0.05 mm** (near-net-shape).
- [ ] **Distinction from HPDC**: HPDC = high-velocity injection + fast cycle; Squeeze casting = low-velocity fill + sustained pressure + slow cycle.
- [ ] **Effective pressure formula** to memorize: $P_{\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot (A_{\text{cyl}}/A_{\text{punch}})$.
- [ ] **Applications**: automotive (wheel hubs, brake calipers), aerospace, EV motor housings, defense armor.
- [ ] **Cycle time**: typically **30–120 seconds** per shot.
- [ ] **Alloys commonly used**: A356, A6061 (Al), AZ91 (Mg), Cu-based alloys.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Casting Defects: Porosity, Shrinkage, Inclusions, Cold Shuts, Misruns, Hot Tears, Surface Cracks, Blowholes, Sand Inclusions, Runout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting Defects: Porosity, Shrinkage, Inclusions, Cold Shuts, Misruns, Hot Tears, Surface Cracks, Blowholes, Sand Inclusions, Runout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Casting Defect?

A **Casting Defect** is any undesirable irregularity, discontinuity, or geometric deviation in the geometry, structure, or surface integrity of a cast component that renders it unfit for its intended design function, causes rejection under quality control, or necessitates expensive post-casting rework (welding, impregnation, machining).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPET402 — Module 1):**
> *"Casting defects are deviations from the intended shape, size, surface finish, mechanical properties, or soundness of a casting, originating from the mould, the metal, the pouring practice, or the solidification behaviour."*

In the KTU outcome-based education framework, this topic maps to:
- **CO1**: *Understand the fundamental principles of casting and identify the causes of various casting defects.*
- **CO2**: *Apply diagnostic reasoning to classify defects and prescribe corrective remedies.*
- **Bloom's Cognitive Levels**: **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** dominate the question pattern.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chocolate Pudding" Model

Imagine pouring molten chocolate into a mould (a small cup):

- If you pour **too cold** chocolate, it sticks to the side and leaves a **gap (misrun / cold shut)**.
- If the chocolate **shrinks** as it cools, a **hollow cavity** forms in the middle (**shrinkage porosity**).
- If a small **bubble of air** gets trapped while pouring, you get a **blowhole**.
- If a piece of the **cup wall (sand) breaks loose** and embeds in the chocolate, that is a **sand inclusion**.
- If the chocolate **tears** while shrinking, you see a **hot tear (crack)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Frequent Favourite):**
> **"B-I-C-S-M-H-C-S-R"** → **B**lowholes, **I**nclusions, **C**old shuts, **S**hrinkage, **M**isruns, **H**ot tears, **C**racks, **S**and inclusions, **R**unout.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics Used

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Solidification shrinkage of **Cast Iron** | **3.5 – 4.5 %** | Drives shrinkage defect severity |
| Solidification shrinkage of **Aluminium alloys** | **6.5 %** | Highest among common cast alloys |
| Solidification shrinkage of **Steel** | **4.0 – 6.0 %** | Hot tear susceptibility high |
| Chvorinov's Rule constant ($n$) | **1.5 – 2.0** (sand mould) | Solidification time $\propto (V/A)^n$ |
| Permissible gas content (Hydrogen in Al melt) | **< 0.15 ml/100g** | Above this → gas porosity |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Casting Defects (KTU Standard Taxonomy)

Casting defects are classified into **five major families** based on origin:

1. **Gas-Origin Defects** → Blowholes, Gas Porosity, Pinholes
2. **Solidification-Origin Defects** → Shrinkage cavities, Hot tears, Centreline shrinkage
3. **Mould/Material-Origin Defects** → Sand inclusions, Slag inclusions, Mould erosion
4. **Pouring-Origin Defects** → Misruns, Cold shuts, Runout, Pouring short
5. **Mechanical/Surface-Origin Defects** → Surface cracks, Flash, Fins, Rat-tails, Buckles

## 2.2 Defect-by-Defect Analytical Breakdown

### A. Porosity (Gas Porosity)

- **Definition:** Discrete or networked micro-voids (Ø 0.5 – 5 mm) formed by trapped/exolved gases within the solidifying metal.
- **Mechanism:** Solubility drop during cooling → gas precipitates as bubbles → bubbles fail to float out → remain as voids.
- **Sources:** Moisture in sand, high hydrogen content in melt, low permeability of mould.
- **Why it matters:** Reduces fatigue life, leaks under pressure, breaks fluid-tightness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chvorinov's Rule (governs time available for gas to escape):**
> $$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$
> Where $t_s$ = solidification time (s), $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $B$ = mould constant, $n \approx 2$.

### B. Shrinkage (Cavity / Centreline)

- **Definition:** Macroscopic void left when liquid feed-metal is insufficient to compensate for volumetric contraction during solidification.
- **Three Stages:** Liquid contraction → Solidification contraction → Solid-state cooling contraction.
- **Location:** Last-to-solidify zones (hot spots), behind bosses, in heavy sections.
- **Remedy:** Riser design, chills, proper gating ratio.

### C. Inclusions (Slag / Oxide / Non-metallic)

- **Definition:** Foreign solid particles (oxides, slag, refractories) embedded in the cast metal.
- **Two Sub-types:** **Endogenous** (formed inside melt) and **Exogenous** (entrapped from outside).
- **Detrimental effects:** Stress concentrators, crack initiation sites.

### D. Cold Shuts

- **Definition:** A surface discontinuity appearing as a line or crack with rounded edges, formed when two streams of metal meet but fail to fuse.
- **Cause:** Low pouring temperature, poor fluidity, thin section design.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Visible as an unfused line on the casting surface.

### E. Misruns

- **Definition:** A casting that has **failed to fill** a mould cavity completely; the metal solidified before reaching all corners.
- **Cause:** Insufficient fluidity, low superheat, long thin sections, inadequate gating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Misrun vs Cold Shut — Examiner's Distinction:**
> - **Misrun** = the metal **never reached** that portion.
> - **Cold Shut** = the metal **reached but did not fuse** with another stream.

### F. Hot Tears (Hot Cracking)

- **Definition:** Crack formed during the **solid-to-ductile transition** when the metal is unable to accommodate the solidification shrinkage stresses.
- **Critical temperature zone:** Just above the solidus (coherent dendrite network stage).
- **Susceptible alloys:** Long freezing-range alloys (e.g., low-carbon steel, aluminium bronzes).
- **Appearance:** Interdendritic, branched, dark-oxide-coated cracks.

### G. Surface Cracks

- **Definition:** Cracks visible on the casting surface, formed due to either **hot tearing** (during solidification) or **cold cracking** (after solidification due to thermal/mechanical stresses).
- **Distinguishing:** Hot cracks → oxide-tinted, interdendritic. Cold cracks → clean, bright, transgranular.

### H. Blowholes

- **Definition:** **Relatively large** (Ø > 1 mm), smooth-walled, rounded gas cavities — usually subsurface or near the upper casting surface.
- **Cause:** Excessive moisture, inadequate venting, high pouring temperature (causes mould gas expansion).

### I. Sand Inclusions

- **Definition:** Particles of moulding sand mechanically embedded in the casting surface or sub-surface.
- **Cause:** Low mould strength, sand wash by high-velocity metal stream, damaged mould surface, inadequate sand coating.

### J. Runout (Bleeding / Leak-out)

- **Definition:** Molten metal **escapes** through cracks or weak joints in the mould, flask, or core, resulting in missing portions of the casting.
- **Cause:** Insufficient clamping (coping-drag), cracked mould, defective core seat.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Examiner-Tested)

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_s = B(V/A)^n$ — **Chvorinov's Rule** | Solidification time of a section | seconds |
| 2 | $S_{total} = S_L + S_S + S_{sol}$ — **Total Shrinkage** | Sum of liquid + solidification + solid cooling shrinkage | % |
| 3 | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ — **Volumetric Contraction** | Liquid contraction on cooling | m³ |
| 4 | **Gating ratio** (Sand mould, top pour) | $A_{sprue} : A_{gate} : A_{runner} = 1 : 2 : 4$ | ratio |
| 5 | **Riser size rule** (Caine's) | $V_{riser} \ge 3 \times V_{casting\;shrinkage}$ | cm³ |
| 6 | **Fluidity** (fluidity ∝ $\sqrt{T_{superheat}}$) | Spiral test length ∝ superheat | mm |
| 7 | **Bernoulli** (for sprue design) | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s |
| 8 | **Hydrogen solubility drop** | $C_H \propto P^{1/2}$ (Sievert's Law) | ml/100g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LaTeX/markdown tables, the absolute-value notation uses `\vert` or `\mid`. For example, the gating ratio would be written as $\vert A_{sprue} \vert : \vert A_{gate} \vert : \vert A_{runner} \vert = 1:2:4$.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Casting defect diagnosis is the **backbone of quality control in foundries**. In **aerospace**, shrinkage porosity in a turbine blade is catastrophic. In **automotive cylinder blocks**, cold shuts cause oil leaks. In **pipeline fittings**, blowholes cause pressure loss. The **NDE methods** used to detect these defects include:
- **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** → surface cracks
- **Radiography (X-ray / Gamma)** → internal porosity, inclusions
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** → shrinkage, hot tears
- **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** → surface/near-surface cracks in ferrous castings

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chvorinov's Rule for Solidification Time

Chvorinov's Rule is a cornerstone of casting defect analysis because **it determines whether shrinkage cavities will form** before the metal can be fed by the riser.

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction through the mould-metal interface**

The rate of heat extraction from the casting is proportional to the surface area in contact with the mould:

$$Q = hA(T_m - T_0)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat flow rate (W)
- $h$ = heat transfer coefficient at mould-metal interface (W/m²K)
- $A$ = surface area of casting (m²)
- $T_m$ = melting temperature of metal (K)
- $T_0$ = initial mould temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Total heat to be removed**

The latent heat plus superheat to be extracted to solidify the casting:

$$Q = \rho V (L + c\Delta T_{superheat})$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal (kg/m³)
- $V$ = volume of casting (m³)
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $c$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)

**Step 3 — Equate and simplify**

Setting Step 1 = Step 2, and solving for the solidification time $t_s$:

$$t_s = \frac{\rho V (L + c\Delta T_{superheat})}{hA(T_m - T_0)}$$

**Step 4 — Define mould constant and exponent**

Since the numerator (per unit volume) and denominator (per unit area) have an empirical exponential relationship, Chvorinov condensed the above into:

$$t_s = B\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

Where:
- $B = \dfrac{\rho (L + c\Delta T_{superheat})}{h(T_m - T_0)(n-1)}$ — mould constant
- $n \approx 2$ for sand moulds (1.5 – 2.0 range)

**Engineering Insight:** The $(V/A)$ term is called the **Modulus** ($M$). A casting with a **high modulus** (heavy section) solidifies slowly → more time for shrinkage feeding → BUT also larger hot-spot → more chance of centreline shrinkage if not fed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Application of Chvorinov's Rule to Riser Sizing

A riser must solidify **AFTER** the casting to feed shrinkage. Therefore:

$$t_{s,\,riser} \geq t_{s,\,casting}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Chvorinov's Rule to both**

$$B_r \left(\frac{V_r}{A_r}\right)^n \geq B_c \left(\frac{V_c}{A_c}\right)^n$$

**Step 2 — Assume same mould material** → $B_r = B_c$, so they cancel:

$$\frac{V_r}{A_r} \geq \frac{V_c}{A_c}$$

**Step 3 — Riser must provide metal equal to the volumetric shrinkage**

$$V_r = S \cdot V_c$$

Where $S$ = volumetric shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.04 for cast iron).

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$\frac{S \cdot V_c}{A_r} \geq \frac{V_c}{A_c} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{A_r} \geq \frac{1}{S \cdot A_c}$$

$$A_r \leq S \cdot A_c$$

**Interpretation:** A successful riser has a **modulus ≥ casting modulus** and is large enough to contain the **volumetric shrinkage volume** of metal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Liquid Shrinkage (Critical for Misrun / Porosity Linkage)

**Step 1 — Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient**

For a metal cooling from pouring temperature $T_p$ to solidus $T_{sol}$:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \beta \cdot (T_p - T_{sol})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for a typical Aluminium alloy**

Given: $\beta = 7 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C, $T_p = 720$ °C, $T_{sol} = 580$ °C:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = (7 \times 10^{-5}) \times (720 - 580) = 7 \times 10^{-5} \times 140$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = 9.8 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.98 \%$$

**Step 3 — Add solidification contraction (4 – 6 % for Al)**

Total liquid-to-solid shrinkage:

$$S_{total} = 0.98 \% + 5.5 \% \approx 6.48 \%$$

This value matches the **standard 6.5 % total shrinkage of aluminium alloys** listed earlier.

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical derivation is a **KTU favourite numerical problem** — students are often asked to compute liquid contraction given $\beta$, $T_p$, $T_{sol}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Remedy (KTU Quick-Reference)

| # | Defect | Primary Cause | Secondary Cause | Standard Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Porosity | Gas entrapment | Low permeability, high H₂ | Degassing, venting, dry mould |
| 2 | Shrinkage cavity | Inadequate feed metal | Hot spot, no riser | Add riser, chills, reduce hot spot |
| 3 | Inclusions | Slag / oxide ingress | Poor skimming, turbulent pour | Ceramic filter, well-designed runner |
| 4 | Cold shuts | Low fluidity | Two streams meet below liquidus | Increase pour temp, improve gating |
| 5 | Misruns | Incomplete filling | Low superheat, thin section | Preheat mould, increase fluidity |
| 6 | Hot tears | Solidification stress | High shrinkage, rigid mould | Use chills, optimize mould strength |
| 7 | Surface cracks | Thermal stress | Hot tear or cold crack | Stress-relief annealing, fillet design |
| 8 | Blowholes | Mould gas evolution | Moisture in sand, poor venting | Dry sand, vent holes, degas melt |
| 9 | Sand inclusions | Mould erosion | High pour velocity | Reduce pour rate, mould wash |
| 10 | Runout | Flask leak | Coping-drag mismatch | Better clamping, sealing |

---

## 3.5 Diagnostic Decision Tree Logic (How an Examiner Thinks)

A student answering a defect question in KTU should follow this **3-layer diagnostic reasoning**:

1. **Layer 1 — Observation:** What is the *appearance*? (rounded = gas; branched/oxide-lined = hot tear; linear & clean = cold crack; surface line with rounded edges = cold shut; missing volume = misrun/runout)
2. **Layer 2 — Location:** Where on the casting? (just below surface = blowhole; in heavy section = shrinkage; at fill junction = cold shut/misrun; at hot spot = shrinkage; along parting line = sand inclusion)
3. **Layer 3 — Cause Mapping:** Trace back to the most likely **process parameter** that caused it (pouring temperature, moisture, riser design, sand strength, etc.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Defect Causal Network

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **causal flow** from process parameters to defects. (Adapted per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguard: all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[METALLURGICAL FACTORS] --> B1[Melt Temperature]
    A1 --> B2[Alloy Composition]
    A1 --> B3[Gas Content H2 N2]
    A1 --> B4[Shrinkage Coefficient]

    A2[MOULD FACTORS] --> C1[Sand Moisture]
    A2 --> C2[Permeability]
    A2 --> C3[Strength of Mould]
    A2 --> C4[Vent Design]

    A3[POURING FACTORS] --> D1[Pouring Temperature]
    A3 --> D2[Pouring Rate]
    A3 --> D3[Gating System]
    A3 --> D4[Flask Clamping]

    B1 --> E1[Hot Tears]
    B1 --> E2[Cold Shuts]
    B3 --> E3[Blowholes]
    B3 --> E4[Gas Porosity]
    B4 --> E5[Shrinkage Cavity]
    B2 --> E1

    C1 --> E3
    C1 --> E4
    C1 --> E6[Sand Inclusions]
    C2 --> E3
    C3 --> E7[Surface Cracks]
    C4 --> E3

    D1 --> E2
    D1 --> E8[Misruns]
    D2 --> E6
    D3 --> E2
    D3 --> E8
    D4 --> E9[Runout]

    E5 --> F1[REJECTED CASTING]
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
    E7 --> F1
    E8 --> F1
    E9 --> F1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Any of the nine casting defects (E1 – E9) eventually leads to a rejected casting (F1). The intermediate processes (B, C, D) show the operational root causes. This is exactly how a KTU examiner reasons when setting a "diagnose the defect and suggest remedy" question.

---

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Defect Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1[CASTING DEFECTS] --> H1[Gas Origin]
    G1 --> H2[Solidification Origin]
    G1 --> H3[Mould Material Origin]
    G1 --> H4[Pouring Practice Origin]
    G1 --> H5[Mechanical Surface Origin]

    H1 --> I1[Blowholes]
    H1 --> I2[Gas Porosity]
    H1 --> I3[Pinholes]

    H2 --> J1[Shrinkage Cavity]
    H2 --> J2[Centreline Shrinkage]
    H2 --> J3[Hot Tears]

    H3 --> K1[Sand Inclusions]
    H3 --> K2[Slag Inclusions]
    H3 --> K3[Oxide Inclusions]

    H4 --> L1[Cold Shuts]
    H4 --> L2[Misruns]
    H4 --> L3[Runout]
    H4 --> L4[Pour Short]

    H5 --> M1[Surface Cracks]
    H5 --> M2[Rat Tails]
    H5 --> M3[Buckles]
    H5 --> M4[Flash Fins]
```

**Note on physical drawings:** Casting defect cross-sections (e.g., blowhole shape, hot tear morphology) are typically **sketched in the KTU answer script** rather than rendered digitally. The student is expected to draw:
- **Rounded, smooth-walled voids** for blowholes
- **Branched, oxide-lined cracks** for hot tears
- **Sharp, transgranular lines** for cold cracks
- **Unfused linear seams** for cold shuts
- **Macroscopic cavities at heavy-section hot spots** for shrinkage

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Defect Detection → Diagnosis → Remedy

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[CASTING PRODUCED] --> P2{NDE INSPECTION}
    P2 --> P3[Defect Detected]
    P2 --> P4[No Defect - Pass]
    P3 --> P5{Identify Defect Type}
    P5 --> P6[Trace Root Cause]
    P6 --> P7[Prescribe Process Modification]
    P7 --> P8[Re Cast and Re Inspect]
    P8 --> P2
```

This is the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** as applied in a foundry quality system. KTU examiners often ask: *"Outline the step-by-step procedure to identify and rectify a casting defect in a foundry."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between a *blowhole* and *gas porosity*. Mention one method to prevent each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Blowhole** | **Gas Porosity** |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Larger (Ø > 1 mm), discrete | Smaller, distributed |
| Location | Sub-surface or near upper face | Throughout casting |
| Cause | Mould gas (moisture) entrapment | Hydrogen / nitrogen dissolved in melt |
| Wall | Smooth, rounded | May be irregular |
| Prevention | Dry mould, vent holes, baked cores | Degassing (chlorine/nitrogen flush), degassing tablets |

**Award Marks:** 1.5 marks for distinction table, 1.5 marks for prevention methods.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is a *cold shut*? State two conditions that lead to its formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A cold shut is a surface discontinuity on a casting appearing as a line or crack with **rounded edges and oxide films**, formed when two streams of molten metal meet in the mould cavity but **fail to fuse** together because their temperature has dropped below the liquidus.
- **Conditions causing it (1.5 marks):**
  1. Low pouring temperature (insufficient superheat)
  2. Poor fluidity of the alloy (e.g., high carbon steel, thin sections)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B (14 marks) has a **module-based internal choice**. Students answer **either** OR1 **or** OR2. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question B1 (OR1). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Classify casting defects. With neat sketches, explain the formation, causes, and remedies of (i) shrinkage cavity, and (ii) hot tears.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (2 marks):**
Casting defects are classified into five groups:
1. Gas origin (blowholes, gas porosity)
2. Solidification origin (shrinkage, hot tears, centreline shrinkage)
3. Mould/material origin (sand, slag, oxide inclusions)
4. Pouring practice origin (misruns, cold shuts, runout)
5. Mechanical/surface origin (surface cracks, rat-tails, flash)

**(i) Shrinkage Cavity (2.5 marks):**
- **Formation:** As metal solidifies, it contracts volumetrically (3.5 – 4.5 % in cast iron, ~6.5 % in Al). The last-to-freeze region (hot spot) cannot be fed by liquid metal, leaving a **macroscopic cavity**, usually irregular, with a **dendritic interior surface**.
- **Causes:** No riser or undersized riser; long freezing range alloy; abrupt section changes.
- **Sketch:** Show heavy section with cavity at centre/hot spot; arrow indicating "last-to-freeze hot spot"; label "riser feeds from top".
- **Remedies:** Add properly sized riser, use chills to direct solidification direction, modify design to eliminate hot spots.

**(ii) Hot Tears (2.5 marks):**
- **Formation:** During the **coherent dendrite network stage** (just above solidus), the casting cannot accommodate shrinkage-induced strain. A crack forms along **interdendritic boundaries**, typically at corners, section changes, or where core/ mould restricts contraction.
- **Causes:** High solidification shrinkage; rigid mould restraint; long freezing range alloy; abrupt section change.
- **Sketch:** Branched, jagged crack with dark-oxide coating; locate at corner or section change.
- **Remedies:** Use chills, add fillets, reduce mould strength locally, use alloys with shorter freezing range, provide contraction allowance.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the Chvorinov's Rule. A steel casting has a modulus $(V/A) = 2.5$ cm. If the mould constant $B = 4$ and exponent $n = 2$, calculate the solidification time. If a riser is to be designed with modulus 3.5 cm, what should be the minimum solidification time of the riser in the same mould?**

**Model Answer:**

**Chvorinov's Rule Statement (2 marks):**
Chvorinov's Rule states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its **modulus** $(V/A)$, given by:

$$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

Where $B$ is the mould constant and $n \approx 2$ for sand moulds. **A casting with a higher modulus solidifies more slowly.**

**Numerical Calculation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Solidification time of casting:**

$$t_{s,\,casting} = B \times (V/A)^n = 4 \times (2.5)^2 = 4 \times 6.25$$

$$t_{s,\,casting} = 25 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum solidification time of riser:**

A riser must solidify **after** the casting to feed shrinkage. So:

$$t_{s,\,riser} \geq t_{s,\,casting}$$

$$t_{s,\,riser,\,min} = 4 \times (3.5)^2 = 4 \times 12.25$$

$$t_{s,\,riser,\,min} = 49 \text{ minutes}$$

**Conclusion:** The riser modulus (3.5 cm) is greater than the casting modulus (2.5 cm), so its solidification time (49 min) is greater than the casting (25 min) — **the riser will feed the casting successfully**.

---

### **Question B2 (OR2). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: What are inclusions in a casting? Differentiate between endogenous and exogenous inclusions. Discuss the role of filters and slag traps in gating systems to minimize inclusions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Inclusions are foreign solid particles — metallic, non-metallic, or slag — embedded within the casting matrix, originating either from the melt itself or from external sources.

**Endogenous vs Exogenous (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Endogenous Inclusions** | **Exogenous Inclusions** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Formed *within* the melt | Entrapped from *outside* |
| Examples | Oxides, sulphides, nitrides of the alloy | Sand particles, slag, refractory debris |
| Size | Usually small, dispersed | Often larger, localized |
| Control | Ladle treatment, degassing, filtration | Mould handling, gating, ceramic filters |

**Filters and Slag Traps in Gating System (3 marks):**
- **Ceramic foam filters** (placed in runner): physically trap inclusions > 50 µm; promote laminar flow.
- **Slag traps / dams / runner extensions:** exploit density difference; slag rises and is retained.
- **Skimmer cores:** divert the upper slag-laden metal stream away from the casting cavity.
- **Bottom-pour / bottom-gating:** ensures clean metal enters mould cavity, slag stays in pouring basin.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: List any four gas-origin defects. Explain in detail the formation and prevention of (i) pinholes, and (ii) subsurface blowholes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Four Gas-Origin Defects (1 mark):**
1. Blowholes
2. Pinholes
3. Gas porosity
4. Subsurface blowholes

**(i) Pinholes (3 marks):**
- **Formation:** Pinholes are fine (Ø < 1 mm), dispersed micro-voids, distributed throughout the casting, caused by **hydrogen evolution** from the melt on cooling. Hydrogen solubility drops sharply as metal solidifies (per Sievert's Law $C_H \propto P^{1/2}$). The rejected hydrogen forms tiny bubbles that fail to coalesce and float out.
- **Prevention:** (a) **Degassing** the melt using nitrogen/chlorine/argon purging; (b) using degassing tablets (hexachloroethane); (c) using dry, baked cores to prevent additional hydrogen pickup; (d) holding melt at correct temperature to allow gas escape.

**(ii) Subsurface Blowholes (3 marks):**
- **Formation:** These are larger (Ø 1 – 10 mm), smooth-walled, rounded cavities located just below the casting surface. They originate from **mould gases** (steam from moisture, products of sand binder pyrolysis) that bubble into the metal, but become trapped below the solidified skin.
- **Prevention:** (a) Dry moulds and baked cores (moisture < 0.2 %); (b) adequate **venting** through vent holes, vent risers, and permeable sand; (c) using chills or chaplets to control local solidification; (d) avoid excessively high pouring temperature which causes more mould gas evolution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing cold shut with misrun** (2-mark penalty) — remember: *cold shut = reached but did not fuse*; *misrun = did not reach*.
> 2. **Failing to mention the role of hydrogen** in pinhole defects — examiners expect a mention of **Sievert's Law or degassing**.
> 3. **Drawing sketches without labels** — even a small neat sketch with **arrows and labels** (e.g., "hot spot", "riser", "rounded cavity") earns full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring numerical units in Chvorinov's rule** — always state the answer with units (minutes, seconds).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention "increase pouring temperature" as remedy for misrun** — this is the most common first-line remedy.
> 6. **Writing "hot tear" instead of "hot crack"** — both are accepted but the official KTU 2024 module terminology prefers "Hot Tears".
> 7. **Not distinguishing hot tear vs cold crack** — examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Blowholes** = large, rounded, sub-surface gas cavities (mould gas origin).
- **Gas porosity / pinholes** = small, dispersed micro-voids (melt hydrogen origin).
- **Shrinkage cavity** = irregular, dendritic-walled void at hot spots; remedy = risers + chills.
- **Inclusions** = foreign particles (endogenous = from melt; exogenous = from mould).
- **Cold shut** = unfused seam with rounded edges and oxide film (low superheat, poor fluidity).
- **Misrun** = incomplete filling (metal never reached the section).
- **Hot tears** = branched, interdendritic, oxide-lined cracks (formed near solidus).
- **Surface cracks** = may be hot (oxide-tinted) or cold (clean, transgranular).
- **Sand inclusions** = moulding sand particles embedded in casting (low mould strength, high pour velocity).
- **Runout** = metal escapes the mould through cracks in flask/coping-drag joint.
- **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^n$ — higher modulus → longer solidification time.
- **Riser design rule:** Riser modulus ≥ Casting modulus; riser must contain $\geq$ shrinkage volume.
- **Sievert's Law:** Hydrogen solubility $\propto$ square root of partial pressure.
- **Liquid shrinkage of Al ≈ 1 %; total solidification shrinkage of Al ≈ 6.5 %.**
- **NDE link:** Radiography → internal porosity; DPT → surface cracks; UT → hot tears.
- **Mnemonic for 9 defects:** **B-I-C-S-M-H-C-S-R** (Blowholes, Inclusions, Cold shuts, Shrinkage, Misruns, Hot tears, Cracks, Sand inclusions, Runout).
- **Hot spot identification:** The point in the casting that is **farthest from any mould surface** (or the section with the highest modulus) — this is where shrinkage forms.
- **Gating ratio** (top pour, sand mould): 1 : 2 : 4 (sprue : gate : runner).
- **Spiral test** measures fluidity in mm; fluidity $\propto \sqrt{\Delta T_{superheat}}$.
- **Caine's rule:** $V_{riser} \geq 3 V_{shrinkage}$ for unpressurized risers.
- **PDCA cycle:** Detection → Diagnosis → Remedy → Re-cast, applied as a quality loop.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — CASTING DEFECTS**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCPET402 Manufacturing Processes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Joining: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Joining: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Joining** is a manufacturing process family used to permanently or semi-permanently unite two or more metal components into a single, continuous, load-bearing assembly by employing one (or a combination) of three fundamental mechanisms: **(a) Fusion & Solidification** (welding), **(b) Capillary Action with a Filler Metal** (brazing and soldering), and **(c) Mechanical Interlocking / Adhesion** (riveting, bolting, adhesive bonding, press-fitting).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Metal Joining – Classification** is treated as a **Module-2 foundational topic** that introduces the student to the *taxonomy* of joining technologies, the underlying *physical principles*, and the *selection criteria* (base-metal compatibility, joint strength, distortion tolerance, cost) governing each family.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Three Plumbers" Analogy 🚰

Imagine you need to connect two water pipes. You have **three plumbers**, each with a different philosophy:

| Plumber | Method | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 🔥 **Plumber A** (The Melter) | Heats both pipes until they *become liquid*, then lets them freeze into one piece. | **Welding** (fusion) |
| 🧴 **Plumber B** (The Gluer) | Keeps the pipes solid but uses a *third, lower-melting liquid* (solder/braze) that gets sucked into the tiny gap by *capillarity* and freezes there. | **Brazing & Soldering** |
| 🔩 **Plumber C** (The Squeezer) | Never heats the pipes. He just *forces them together* with a rivet, bolt, or crimp. | **Mechanical Joining** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** The temperature at which the base metal melts vs. the temperature at which the joint is actually made is what separates these families. Welding **melts the base metal**; brazing and soldering **do not**; mechanical joining **involves negligible heat**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Metrics

The single most important threshold in the KTU classification is the **Brazing/Soldering Cut-off Temperature**:

> **Threshold Temperature, $T_{th} = 450^{\circ}C \;(723\;K)$**

*   If the filler metal flows **above $450^{\circ}C$** → process is called **Brazing**.
*   If the filler metal flows **below $450^{\circ$C$** → process is called **Soldering**.

**Standard joint efficiency metric:**

$$\eta_{joint} = \frac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base\;metal}} \times 100\%$$

For a *perfectly designed* arc weld, the target **weld efficiency** in KTU board problems is typically **$\eta_{joint} \geq 80\%$**; for friction stir welding (solid-state) it can exceed **95%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic (Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 board classification uses **three nested levels**:

### 🔹 Level 1 — Macroscopic Family (5 Categories)
The classification of metal joining processes is fundamentally based on **whether the base metal is melted, whether a filler is used, and whether heat is the principal driver.**

1.  **Welding** – Base metal *is* melted (fusion) *or* is plastically deformed without melting (solid-state). A filler may or may not be used.
2.  **Brazing** – Base metal *is not* melted. A filler metal with $T_{liq} > 450^{\circ}C$ flows by capillary action.
3.  **Soldering** – Base metal *is not* melted. A filler metal with $T_{liq} < 450^{\circ}C$ flows by capillary action.
4.  **Mechanical Joining** – No metallurgical bonding. Force / form-fit is the joining mechanism.
5.  **Adhesive Bonding** – Chemical surface bonding using polymeric adhesives (limited to non-high-temperature service).

### 🔹 Level 2 — Heat Source / Energy Type
- **Chemical Energy:** Combustion of fuel gas (Oxy-Acetylene Welding – OAW), exothermic reaction (Thermite).
- **Electrical Energy:** Arc (SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW), Resistance ($I^2R$ heating), Induction, High-frequency.
- **Mechanical Energy:** Friction, Friction-Stir, Ultrasonic, Explosive, Forge, Cold.
- **Radiant Energy:** Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW).

### 🔹 Level 3 — Process Variant (Examples)
| Family | Common Variants Asked in KTU Board |
|---|---|
| **Arc Welding** | SMAW, GTAW (TIG), GMAW (MIG), FCAW, SAW, Plasma Arc |
| **Resistance Welding** | Spot, Seam, Projection, Flash, Butt |
| **Solid-State Welding** | Friction (FRW), Friction Stir (FSW), Ultrasonic, Diffusion, Explosive, Forge, Roll |
| **Oxy-Fuel Welding** | Oxy-Acetylene, Oxy-Hydrogen, Air-Acetylene |

## 2.2 "Why & How" — Physical Reasoning Behind the Classification

- **Why is the $450^{\circ}C$ boundary used?** Above this temperature, metallurgical changes (grain coarsening, intermetallic compound formation) occur in ferrous base metals, and joint strength differs significantly. The **American Welding Society (AWS)** uses this threshold.
- **Why does Capillarity work?** The filler metal *wets* the base metal because surface tension pulls the liquid into the joint gap. Gap must be **$0.05\;mm$ to $0.2\;mm$** for optimal capillary rise.
- **Why is Solid-State Welding used for dissimilar / reactive metals?** Because **no liquid phase** is formed, brittle intermetallics (e.g., Al–Cu, Ti–steel) are minimized.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (Arc Welding)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Controls weld size & HAZ |
| 2 | **Weld Efficiency** | $\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quality check |
| 3 | **Heat Balance (Resistance Welding)** | $Q = I^{2} R t$ | **Joules (J)** | Energy for nugget formation |
| 4 | **Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)** | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | **metres (m)** | Brazing / Soldering gap design |
| 5 | **Power Density (e-beam / laser)** | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | **W/mm²** | Compares beam processes |
| 6 | **Joint Strength (Butt)** | $P = \sigma_{ult} \cdot A_{throat}$ | **N** | Mechanical design of joints |
| 7 | **Friction Welding Upsetting** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 F L}{\pi D^{2} E}$ | **mm** | Solid-state deformation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min), $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.7 for GMAW, 0.8 for SMAW, 0.6 for GTAW), $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m), $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = gap radius (m).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) for aluminium fuselage panels (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Automotive:** Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) — a typical car has **~4,000–5,000 spot welds**.
- **Shipbuilding:** Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) for thick plates.
- **Electronics:** Soldering (lead-free Sn-Ag-Cu at $\approx 217^{\circ}C$) for PCBs.
- **Pipeline:** Flash Butt Welding for rail / pipe joining.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arc Welding Heat Input (Board Favourite ⭐)

The **heat input $H$** is the energy supplied per unit length of weld. The KTU board almost always asks this derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Electrical Power
The arc acts as a resistor. The power dissipated across the arc is:

$$P = V \cdot I \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

where $V$ is the **arc voltage** in Volts and $I$ is the **welding current** in Amperes.

### Step 2 — Introduce Arc Efficiency $\eta$
Not all electrical power is converted to useful heat in the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ is transferred. The **effective heat delivered to the workpiece** becomes:

$$P_{eff} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

### Step 3 — Compute Energy per Unit Time
Energy delivered in a small time $dt$:

$$dE = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot dt$$

### Step 4 — Convert Time to Weld Length
If the torch travels at speed $S$ (in mm/min), then in time $dt$ the weld length traversed is:

$$dL = S \cdot dt \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dt = \dfrac{dL}{S}$$

### Step 5 — Energy per Unit Length (Substitute)
$$H = \dfrac{dE}{dL} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{1}{S}$$

This gives units of **J/mm**. Converting J to kJ:

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

The factor **60** converts per-minute to per-second, and **1000** converts J to kJ.

### Step 6 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Style Problem)
**Given:** $V = 25\;V$, $I = 200\;A$, $S = 300\;mm/min$, $\eta = 0.85$ (GMAW).

$$H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} = \dfrac{255000}{300000} = 0.85 \;\text{kJ/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptable KTU board range for mild-steel arc welding:** **$0.5$ to $2.0\;\text{kJ/mm}$**. Values above 2.0 indicate excessive heat input → coarse grain, distortion, and reduced toughness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weld Efficiency from Joint Geometry

For a **single-V butt weld**, the throat area is the smallest load-bearing section.

$$A_{throat} = t \cdot \cos 30^{\circ} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,t^{2}\;\text{per unit length? No, full area.}$$

For a butt joint of **plate thickness $t$** with **groove angle $\theta$**, the theoretical throat is:

$$A_{throat} = t \cdot \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot 1 \quad \text{(per unit width)}$$

Hence joint strength per unit width:

$$\sigma_{joint} = \dfrac{P}{A_{throat}} = \dfrac{P}{t \cdot \cos(\theta/2)}$$

The **weld efficiency** is then:

$$\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} = \dfrac{\cos(\theta/2) \cdot \sigma_{base}}{\sigma_{base}} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{joint} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)}$$

> **Numerical:** For $\theta = 60^{\circ}$, $\eta_{joint} = \cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866 = 86.6\%$. For $\theta = 90^{\circ}$, $\eta = \cos 45^{\circ} = 70.7\%$.

---

## 3.3 Classification Decision Matrix (Analytical Tool)

Use the following decision table to classify a process (a common KTU 2-mark question):

| Process | Base Metal Melted? | Filler Used? | T $> 450^{\circ}C$? | Family |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| SMAW | ✅ Yes | Yes (electrode) | Yes | Welding (Arc) |
| GTAW | ✅ Yes | Optional | Yes | Welding (Arc) |
| Oxy-Acetylene | ✅ Yes | Yes (rod) | Yes | Welding (Gas) |
| Friction Stir | ❌ No (plastic) | No | Yes | Welding (Solid-State) |
| Diffusion Bonding | ❌ No | No | Yes | Welding (Solid-State) |
| Torch Brazing | ❌ No | Yes (brass, BAg) | **Yes ($>450$)** | **Brazing** |
| Soft Soldering | ❌ No | Yes (Sn-Pb) | **No ($<450$)** | **Soldering** |
| Riveting | ❌ No | Rivet (mech.) | No | Mechanical |
| Adhesive Bonding | ❌ No | Polymer | $<200$ | Adhesive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One common KTU trap:** *Friction welding* **does** produce a joint that is stronger than the parent metal in many cases, but it is classified as a **WELDING** process (solid-state), not mechanical joining — because a *metallurgical bond* is formed across the interface.

---

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block-Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Joining] --> B{Base Metal Melted?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Arc Welding]
    B -- Yes --> D[Resistance Welding]

    B -- No --> F{Filler Used and T greater 450}

    C --> C6[Plasma Arc]
    D --> D1[Spot]
    D --> D2[Seam]
    D --> D3[Projection]
    D --> D4[Flash Butt]
    E --> E1[Oxy Acetylene]
    E --> E2[Oxy Hydrogen]
    G --> G1[Friction FRW]
    G --> G2[Friction Stir FSW]
    G --> G3[Ultrasonic]
    G --> G4[Diffusion]

    J --> J1[Riveting]
```
