
## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Filler-Flow vs Fusion-Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF[Supply and Energy]
        EP[Electrical Power] --> ARC[Arc Source]
        GAS[Fuel Gas O2 C2H2] --> FLM[Flame]
        MECH[Mechanical Motion] --> FRIC[Friction Drive]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Process Family]
        ARC --> ARCW[Arc Welding]
        FLM --> OAW[Oxy Fuel Welding]
        FRIC --> SSW[Solid State Welding]
    end
    subgraph JOINT[Final Joint Type]
        ARCW --> FUS[Fusion Joint]
        OAW --> FUS
        SSW --> DSS[Diffusion or Plastic Joint]
    end
    FUS --> OUT[Continuous Metallurgical Bond]
    DSS --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The energy source (left block) determines the process family (middle), which in turn determines the *bonding mechanism* (right). The KTU board often asks you to "reverse-map" a given process back to its energy source and bonding type.

## 4.3 Capillary vs Fusion — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Joining of Two Plates] --> Q1{Temperature Reached}
    Q1 -- Above Base Metal Melting Point --> WF[Fusion Zone Forms Both Plates Melt]
    Q1 -- Below Base Metal Melting Point --> WC[Base Metal Stays Solid]
    WC --> Q2{Filler Melting Point}
    Q2 -- Above 450 C --> WB[Brazing Filler Wets and Flows by Capillarity]
    Q2 -- Below 450 C --> WS[Soldering Filler Wets and Flows by Capillarity]
    WF --> OUT1[Welded Joint]
    WB --> OUT2[Brazed Joint]
    WS --> OUT3[Soldered Joint]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Classify metal joining processes with a neat flowchart. Differentiate between brazing and soldering using the temperature criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Metal joining processes are classified into five major families based on whether the base metal is melted, whether a filler is used, and the joining temperature:

1.  **Welding** – base metal melted (fusion) or plastically deformed (solid-state).
2.  **Brazing** – base metal *not* melted; filler melts **above $450^{\circ}C$**.
3.  **Soldering** – base metal *not* melted; filler melts **below $450^{\circ$C$**.
4.  **Mechanical Joining** – rivets, bolts, press-fits.
5.  **Adhesive Bonding** – polymeric adhesives.

The **$450^{\circ}C$** cut-off is the **American Welding Society (AWS) criterion**: above it the joint is *brazed*; below it the joint is *soldered*. Soldered joints are weaker and used in electronics; brazed joints are used in plumbing, automotive, and aerospace.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**What is the difference between arc welding and resistance welding? Name two variants of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Arc Welding | Resistance Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Electric arc across a gap | $I^{2}R$ heating at the interface |
| **Heat Generation** | Continuous electrical discharge | Resistance to current flow |
| **Application** | Heavy fabrication, shipbuilding | Thin sheets, automotive body |
| **Filler** | Usually required (electrode) | Usually not required |

- **Arc Variants:** SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding), GTAW / TIG.
- **Resistance Variants:** Spot welding, Seam welding.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting:**
> • Do **not** confuse *brazing* and *soldering*; the $450^{\circ}C$ cut-off is mandatory.
> • Always state **units** in numerical answers; **$-0.5$ mark** is deducted per missing unit.
> • When asked to "classify", use a **flowchart or table** — pure paragraphs lose 1–2 marks.
> • For "differentiate" questions, a **comparison table** is preferred over prose.
> • For heat-input problems, **carry forward the formula** with all variables defined.

---

### **📌 Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify metal joining processes in detail. Explain the differences between **fusion welding** and **solid-state welding** with at least **three examples** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A GMAW process is carried out with arc voltage **$V = 28\;V$**, current **$I = 250\;A$**, travel speed **$S = 400\;mm/min$**, and arc efficiency **$\eta = 0.85$**. Calculate the **heat input in kJ/mm** and comment on whether the heat input is within the recommended range for mild steel. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 Marks)

**Classification of Metal Joining Processes (3 Marks):**
Metal joining processes are classified into:
1.  **Welding** (with and without fusion of base metal)
2.  **Brazing** (filler $> 450^{\circ}C$)
3.  **Soldering** (filler $< 450^{\circ}C$)
4.  **Mechanical Joining** (riveting, bolting, press-fit)
5.  **Adhesive Bonding** (epoxy, acrylics)

**Difference between Fusion and Solid-State Welding (4 Marks):**

| S.No. | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---|---|---|
| 1. | Base metal **melts** at the joint. | Base metal **does not melt**; only plastically deforms. |
| 2. | A **fusion zone** and **HAZ** (Heat Affected Zone) are formed. | No fusion zone; no HAZ. |
| 3. | Metallurgical changes (grain growth, residual stress) occur. | Minimal metallurgical disturbance. |
| 4. | Suitable for **similar** metals and many dissimilar pairs. | Excellent for **dissimilar / reactive** metals (Al–Cu, Ti–steel). |
| 5. | Examples: **SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, SAW, Oxy-Acetylene**. | Examples: **Friction Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Bonding, Explosive Welding**. |

**Examples (3 required for each — 1.5 Marks each):**
- *Fusion:* SMAW, GTAW, Oxy-Acetylene.
- *Solid-State:* Friction, Friction Stir, Diffusion Bonding.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V = 28\;V$, $I = 250\;A$, $S = 400\;mm/min$, $\eta = 0.85$.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Substitution (2 Marks):**

$$H = \frac{0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$H = \frac{357000}{400000} = 0.8925 \;\text{kJ/mm}$$

**Comment (2 Marks):**
The heat input **$H = 0.8925\;\text{kJ/mm}$** lies within the recommended range of **$0.5$ to $2.0\;\text{kJ/mm}$** for mild-steel arc welding. The weld is therefore acceptable; we can expect a sound weld with adequate penetration and no excessive HAZ coarsening.

---

### **📌 Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **principle of resistance spot welding**. State its **two main applications** and **two limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **brazing and soldering** in a **tabular form** with at least **six points**. Justify why **$450^{\circ}C$** is chosen as the demarcation temperature. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 Marks)

**Principle of Resistance Spot Welding (5 Marks):**
Resistance spot welding (RSW) is a **fusion welding** process in which two (or more) sheets of metal are joined by the heat generated from the **resistance to the flow of electric current** through the workpieces held between two **water-cooled copper electrodes**.

**Heat Generated:**

$$Q = I^{2} R t$$

where $Q$ = heat (J), $I$ = current (A), $R$ = interface resistance ($\Omega$), $t$ = time (s).

**Sequence:**
1.  Sheets are clamped between **two copper electrodes**.
2.  High current (**$5,000$ to $20,000\;A$**) is passed for a short time (**$0.1$ to $0.5\;s$**).
3.  Resistance at the faying surface is highest → **localized heating → melting → nugget formation**.
4.  Current is cut; electrodes hold the joint until the **nugget solidifies** under pressure.

**Applications (1 Mark):**
- Automotive body-in-white assembly (car panels).
- Sheet-metal fabrication (cabinets, white goods).

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Restricted to **thin sheets** (usually $< 3\;mm$).
- Requires **high capital investment** for the transformer.
- Difficult to inspect nugget quality non-destructively.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 Marks)

**Tabular Difference (6 × 1 Mark = 6 Marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Brazing | Soldering |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. | **Filler melting temperature** | **Above $450^{\circ}C$** | **Below $450^{\circ$C$** |
| 2. | **Filler strength** | High (e.g., brass, silver alloys) | Low (e.g., Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu) |
| 3. | **Joint strength** | High (up to $\sim 70\%$ of base metal) | Low (typically $< 30\%$ of base metal) |
| 4. | **Typical filler examples** | Copper, brass, BAg, BCuP | Tin-lead, lead-free solders |
| 5. | **Heat source** | Torch, induction, furnace | Soldering iron, hot plate |
| 6. | **Applications** | Plumbing, automotive radiators, aerospace | Electronics PCB, jewellery, cable joints |
| 7. | **Joint type** | Capillary, stronger | Capillary, weaker |
| 8. | **Inspection** | Dye-penetrant, X-ray | Visual, continuity test |

**Why $450^{\circ}C$ is the Demarcation (1 Mark):**
The **American Welding Society (AWS)** adopted **$450^{\circ}C$** (about **$840^{\circ}F$**) because **above this temperature**, metallurgical changes begin to occur in ferrous base metals, and the strength of the filler metals used is significantly higher. It is a **standardized international convention** that allows unambiguous classification, even though the underlying metallurgy is a continuum.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> 📌 **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Five families** of metal joining: Welding, Brazing, Soldering, Mechanical, Adhesive.
- [ ] **Brazing vs Soldering** cut-off: **$T_{th} = 450^{\circ}C$** (AWS standard).
- [ ] Welding can be **fusion** (base metal melts) or **solid-state** (no melting).
- [ ] Solid-state welding is best for **dissimilar / reactive metals**.
- [ ] Arc welding heat input formula: $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm**.
- [ ] Acceptable arc-welding heat input for mild steel: **$0.5$ to $2.0\;\text{kJ/mm}$**.
- [ ] Resistance spot welding heat: $Q = I^{2} R t$ (J).
- [ ] Friction welding is **welding** (metallurgical bond), *not* mechanical joining.
- [ ] Capillary action requires a joint gap of **$0.05$ to $0.20\;mm$**.
- [ ] Brazing filler examples: brass, BAg (silver brazing). Solder filler examples: Sn-Pb, SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu).
- [ ] Friction stir welding (FSW) → aerospace (Boeing, Airbus).
- [ ] A typical car has **~4,000–5,000 spot welds**.
- [ ] Weld efficiency for a $60^{\circ}$ single-V butt: **$\eta = \cos 30^{\circ} = 86.6\%$**.
- [ ] Always mention **units** and **sign convention** in numerical answers.
- [ ] For "classify" questions — **always use a flowchart or table**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Of all the topics in **Module 2 (Metal Joining)**, the **Classification flowchart** and the **Heat Input derivation** are the two most frequently asked questions. Memorize the table in Section 2.4 and the formula in Section 3.1 — together they are worth **10–14 marks** in every KTU end-semester paper.

topics.title: Welding Heat Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding Heat Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Welding Heat Source** is a localized, high-intensity energy delivery system used to generate sufficient thermal energy at the joint interface to melt, fuse, and metallurgically bond the parent metals (with or without filler material). The heat source must provide a **power density** (energy per unit area per unit time) high enough to overcome the thermal conductivity losses and raise the workpiece temperature beyond the **melting point** of the base metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Welding heat sources are classified based on the **form of energy** (chemical, electrical, mechanical, or radiative) and the **mode of heat generation**. The 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes the relationship between **power density (W/mm²)**, **heat input (J/mm)**, and the resulting **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** characteristics.

The governing thermodynamic principle is the **Heat Balance Equation**:

$$P_{in} \times \eta = P_{melt} + P_{loss} + P_{base}$$

Where $\eta$ (eta) is the **thermal efficiency** of the process (a key KTU numerical), and the right side accounts for useful melting, conduction/radiation losses, and heating of the surrounding base metal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Focused Sunlight vs. Campfire" Model**
> Imagine you want to melt a sheet of butter. Two approaches:
>
> 1. **Campfire (Low Power Density):** A large, diffuse flame touching the butter. The butter melts slowly, but a wide surrounding area gets warm — much of the heat is wasted on the surroundings.
> 2. **Magnifying Glass (High Power Density):** A focused beam of sunlight hits a tiny spot. The butter at that spot melts almost instantly, but the surrounding region remains cool.
>
> **Welding heat sources operate on the same principle.** An **oxy-acetylene flame** is like the campfire (~10²–10³ W/mm²), while a **laser or electron beam** is like the magnifying glass (~10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²). Higher power density = **deeper penetration, narrower HAZ, faster travel speed**, and **less distortion**.

## 1.3 Classification Overview (KTU Mandate)

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies welding heat sources into **four primary families**:

| Sl. | Energy Family | Representative Processes | Typical Power Density (W/mm²) |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | **Chemical Combustion** | Oxy-fuel gas welding, Thermit welding | 10² – 10³ |
| 2 | **Electrical Resistance** | Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt welding | 10² – 10⁴ |
| 3 | **Electric Arc** | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, PAW | 10³ – 10⁴ |
| 4 | **High-Energy Density Beams** | LBW, EBW | 10⁶ – 10⁷ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU High-Yield):** The **thermal efficiency ($\eta$)** values commonly used in KTU numerical problems are:
> - **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW):** $\eta = 0.70 - 0.85$
> - **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG):** $\eta = 0.65 - 0.85$
> - **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG):** $\eta = 0.40 - 0.60$
> - **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW):** $\eta = 0.85 - 0.95$
> - **Oxy-Acetylene Welding:** $\eta = 0.40 - 0.50$
> - **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** $\eta = 0.30 - 0.40$ (electrical-to-thermal)
> - **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** $\eta = 0.65 - 0.80$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Heat Generation in Welding

Every welding heat source, regardless of family, must satisfy **three fundamental requirements**:

1. **Sufficient Power Density** — To exceed thermal diffusion losses from the weld pool into the base metal.
2. **Controllability** — To maintain a stable molten pool without excessive spatter, porosity, or burn-through.
3. **Localized Energy Deposition** — To minimize the **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** and reduce residual stress/distortion.

### 2.1.1 Chemical Combustion Heat Sources

In **oxy-fuel welding (OFW)**, the heat is generated by the exothermic reaction between a fuel gas (typically **acetylene C₂H₂**) and oxygen. The primary combustion reaction is:

$$C_2H_2 + \frac{5}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

- **Flame Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene:** ~**3,200 °C** (highest among practical fuel gases).
- **Acetylene** is preferred over other fuels (hydrogen, propane, natural gas) because it produces a **neutral/reducing flame** that prevents oxidation of the weld metal.

**Step-by-step logic of OFW operation:**
- Acetylene and oxygen are mixed in a **welding torch** with a **neutral flame ratio** of **1:1 to 1:1.2** (acetylene:oxygen by volume).
- The **inner cone** (bright white) is the hottest zone where combustion is most intense.
- The **outer envelope** (pale blue) is a protective reducing atmosphere that shields the weld pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermit Welding** uses a fundamentally different chemical principle: the **aluminothermic reaction** between aluminum powder and iron oxide.
>
> $$Fe_2O_3 + 2Al \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3 + \text{Heat (} \approx 850 \text{ kJ/mol)}$$
>
> This reaction reaches ~**2,500 °C** and is used for **in-situ rail welding, repair of large steel castings**, and field joining of heavy sections without external power.

### 2.1.2 Electrical Resistance Heat Sources

In **resistance welding**, heat is generated by **Joule heating ($I^2R$)** as current passes through the interface between two workpieces held under pressure.

$$Q = I^2 \times R \times t \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

Where:
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $R$ = contact resistance between the parts + bulk resistance (Ohms)
- $t$ = time of current flow (seconds)

**Key KTU Point:** The heat is generated **internally** at the faying interface — not from an external flame or arc. This makes resistance welding **self-limiting** and ideal for sheet metal joining.

### 2.1.3 Electric Arc Heat Sources

The **electric arc** is a sustained electrical discharge through a **partially ionized gas (plasma)** between two electrodes. The arc column temperatures can reach **6,000–20,000 °C**, making it the most common industrial heat source.

The **arc is sustained** by **thermionic emission** from the cathode and **field emission** at high currents. The voltage-current characteristic is **falling (negative resistance)**, which is why a **constant-current power source** (drooper or inverter) is mandatory.

**Arc welding processes (KTU-mandatory classification):**
- **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) — consumable electrode, manual
- **GMAW** (Gas Metal Arc Welding / MIG) — continuous wire, inert gas shield
- **GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding / TIG) — non-consumable W electrode, inert gas
- **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) — granular flux submerges the arc
- **PAW** (Plasma Arc Welding) — constricted arc through a copper nozzle
- ** FCAW** (Flux-Cored Arc Welding) — tubular electrode with flux core

### 2.1.4 High-Energy Density Beam Heat Sources

These are the most advanced heat sources with **power densities of 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²**:

- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** Photons from a CO₂ or Nd:YAG/fiber laser are focused to a small spot, vaporizing the metal to create a **keyhole**.
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** Accelerated electrons (in a vacuum) strike the workpiece, converting **kinetic energy to heat** upon impact. Achieves the deepest penetrations (up to **200 mm in a single pass** in steel).

$$P_{beam} = V \times I \quad (\text{electrical input power})$$

$$P_{delivered} = \eta_{coupling} \times P_{beam}$$

For LBW, $\eta_{coupling}$ depends on **reflectivity** of the metal surface (e.g., aluminum reflects ~90% of CO₂ laser light, requiring high power or fiber lasers at 1.06 µm).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA REFERENCE — Memorize for KTU Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | **Heat Input** (arc processes) | J/mm |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P}{v}$ | Heat Input using power $P = VI$ | J/mm |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{0.06 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | Heat Input when $S$ = travel speed in **mm/s** | kJ/mm (factor 0.06 = 60 s/1000) |
| 4 | $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S_{ipm}}$ | Heat Input when speed is in **inches/min (ipm)** | J/inch |
| 5 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | **Power Density** (focused energy) | W/mm² |
| 6 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | **Joule Heat** (resistance welding) | J |
| 7 | $E_{fusion} = m \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)$ | **Energy to melt** a mass $m$ | J |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{A_{bead} \cdot \rho \cdot E_{fusion}}$ | Travel speed for a given bead area | mm/s |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | **Fourier heat conduction** (losses) | W |
| 10 | $\text{HAZ width} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}}$ | Empirical HAZ growth | mm |

**Key variable definitions (for KTU):**
- $\eta$ = thermal efficiency (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $V$ = arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $v$ or $S$ = travel speed (mm/s or mm/min)
- $H$ = heat input (J/mm or kJ/mm)
- $P_d$ = power density (W/mm²)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap (Common KTU Mistake):** If $V$ is in Volts, $I$ in Amperes, and $v$ in **mm/minute**, then $H$ is in **J/mm**. If $v$ is in **mm/second**, the result is in J/mm only if you use the **0.06 factor**:
>
> $$H \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v \text{ (mm/s)}}$$
>
> Always **check units first** before plugging in numbers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** LBW and EBW for turbine blades and fuselage panels (minimum HAZ preserves fatigue life).
- **Automotive:** Resistance spot welding (RSW) for body-in-white assembly (~4,000–5,000 spots per car).
- **Shipbuilding:** SAW for thick plate butt joints (single-pass deep penetration, high deposition rate).
- **Pipeline construction:** GMAW/SAW for girth welds with strict heat input limits to preserve toughness.
- **Railways:** Thermit welding for continuous welded rail (CWR) on-site.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Input Equation from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** The electrical power delivered to an arc is the product of arc voltage and current:

$$P_{electrical} = V \times I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{J/s}]$$

**Step 1 — Account for Thermal Efficiency:**
Not all electrical energy is converted to useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ (eta) represents the **process thermal efficiency**, accounting for losses to spatter, radiation, fume, and electrode heating. The useful heat delivered to the workpiece per unit time is:

$$P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Energy per Unit Length:**
If the arc travels along the joint at a constant speed $v$ (mm/s), then in time $dt$ the arc covers a length $dx = v \cdot dt$. The useful heat deposited per unit length of weld is the heat input $H$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{P_{useful}}{v} \\
  &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm/s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Numerical Form (KTU Standard):**
For KTU board exam numericals, the speed is often given in **mm/minute** but the result required is in **kJ/mm**. We convert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \text{ (kJ/mm)} &= \frac{\eta \times V \text{ (V)} \times I \text{ (A)}}{v \text{ (mm/min)}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} \times \frac{1 \text{ kJ}}{1000 \text{ J}} \\
  &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot v} \times 1000 \\
  &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{60 \cdot v} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(in kJ/mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently, using the **mm/s** form:

$$
H \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v \text{ (mm/s)}}
$$

> The factor **0.06 = 60 s/min ÷ 1000 J/kJ** reconciles the unit mismatch.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Required to Melt a Unit Volume of Metal

To determine whether a heat source can sustain a particular weld bead size, we calculate the **minimum energy** required to bring a unit mass of base metal from ambient temperature to fully molten state.

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat (Temperature Rise to Melting Point):**
The energy to heat 1 kg of metal from $T_0$ (ambient, ~25 °C) to $T_m$ (melting point) is:

$$Q_{sensible} = c_p \times (T_m - T_0) \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

For **mild steel:** $c_p \approx 670$ J/(kg·K), $T_m \approx 1530$ °C, so $Q_{sensible} \approx 670 \times 1505 \approx 1.01 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

**Step 2 — Latent Heat of Fusion (Phase Change):**
To convert the solid at $T_m$ to liquid at $T_m$ requires the latent heat of fusion $L_f$:

$$Q_{latent} = L_f \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

For mild steel, $L_f \approx 2.47 \times 10^5$ J/kg.

**Step 3 — Total Energy per Unit Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fusion} &= Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} \\
  &= c_p \times (T_m - T_0) + L_f
\end{aligned}
$$

For **mild steel:** $E_{fusion} \approx 1.01 \times 10^6 + 0.247 \times 10^6 \approx 1.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

**Step 4 — Energy per Unit Volume (using density $\rho$):**

$$E_{fusion, vol} = \rho \times E_{fusion} \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$

For mild steel ($\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m³): $E_{fusion, vol} \approx 7850 \times 1.26 \times 10^6 \approx 9.89 \times 10^9$ J/m³ $\approx$ **9.89 J/mm³**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Travel Speed for a Required Bead Area

**Given:** A weld bead of cross-sectional area $A_{bead}$ (mm²) must be deposited in a single pass using a heat source with power $P$ and efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Energy per unit length required to form the bead:**

$$E_{req} = A_{bead} \times \rho \times E_{fusion} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 2 — Energy per unit length supplied by the source:**

$$E_{supplied} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{per second, divide by speed}]$$

**Step 3 — Equating supplied to required for steady-state travel:**

$$
\frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} = A_{bead} \cdot \rho \cdot E_{fusion}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for travel speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{A_{bead} \cdot \rho \cdot E_{fusion}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{kg/mm}^3 \cdot \text{J/kg}} = \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A **GMAW** process is used to weld 6 mm thick mild steel plate. The arc voltage is **28 V**, current is **220 A**, and the travel speed is **8 mm/s**. Given $\eta = 0.80$ and mild steel properties ($c_p = 670$ J/kg·K, $L_f = 2.47 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $T_m = 1530$ °C, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³), calculate:
> (a) Heat input in kJ/mm
> (b) Power density if the arc spot diameter is 4 mm
> (c) Maximum possible travel speed for a 5 mm² bead cross-section

### Solution:

**(a) Heat Input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \text{ (kJ/mm)} &= \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 0.80 \times 28 \times 220}{8} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 0.80 \times 6160}{8} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 4928}{8} \\
  &= \frac{295.68}{8} \\
  &= 36.96 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \text{(≈ 1.0 kJ/mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Power Density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= V \times I = 28 \times 220 = 6160 \text{ W} \\
A_{spot} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 4^2 = 12.566 \text{ mm}^2 \\
P_d &= \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{6160}{12.566} \approx 490 \text{ W/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Maximum Travel Speed for 5 mm² Bead:**

First, compute $E_{fusion}$ for mild steel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fusion} &= c_p \times (T_m - T_0) + L_f \\
  &= 670 \times (1530 - 25) + 2.47 \times 10^5 \\
  &= 670 \times 1505 + 247000 \\
  &= 1{,}008{,}350 + 247{,}000 \\
  &= 1{,}255{,}350 \text{ J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the travel speed formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{A_{bead} \cdot \rho \cdot E_{fusion}} \\
  &= \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 220}{5 \times (7850 \times 10^{-9}) \times 1{,}255{,}350} \quad \text{[converting ρ to kg/mm³]} \\
  &= \frac{4928}{5 \times 9.854 \times 10^{-6} \times 1{,}255{,}350} \\
  &= \frac{4928}{5 \times 12.371} \\
  &= \frac{4928}{61.86} \\
  &\approx 79.7 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** This is the *thermodynamic maximum*; in practice, factors like arc stability, bead shape factor (typically 0.6–0.8 of the theoretical), and preheat reduce the actual speed to ~**40–60 mm/s**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Heat Source

| Selection Criterion | Best Heat Source | KTU Justification |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Thinnest sheets (< 0.5 mm) | **GTAW (TIG)** or **LBW** | Lowest heat input, precise control |
| Thick plates (> 25 mm) | **SAW** or **EBW** | Deep penetration, high deposition |
| Field repair, no electricity | **Oxy-acetylene / Thermit** | Self-contained fuel |
| Mass production sheet metal | **Resistance spot welding** | Fast (1–2 s/weld), automatable |
| Reactive metals (Ti, Al) | **GTAW** or **PAW** | Inert shielding prevents oxidation |
| Nuclear / aerospace critical | **EBW** (in vacuum) | No atmospheric contamination |
| Maximum travel speed | **LBW** (keyhole mode) | 10–100× faster than arc processes |
