# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**"Define welding heat source. List any four types of welding heat sources with one example each."**

**Model Answer:**

A **welding heat source** is a localized source of high-intensity energy used to generate sufficient thermal energy to melt and fuse the parent materials at the joint interface.

Four types of welding heat sources:

1. **Chemical combustion heat source** — e.g., Oxy-acetylene gas welding (flame temp ~3,200 °C).
2. **Electrical resistance heat source** — e.g., Resistance spot welding (Joule heating $I^2R$).
3. **Electric arc heat source** — e.g., Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) (arc temp 6,000–7,000 °C).
4. **High-energy density beam** — e.g., Laser Beam Welding (power density 10⁶ W/mm²).

> **Mark Distribution (3 M):** Definition 1M + 4 types × 0.5M = 1 + 2 = **3M**.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
**"Differentiate between power density and heat input in arc welding processes."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Power Density | Heat Input |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Definition** | Energy delivered per unit area of the arc spot | Total energy supplied per unit length of weld |
| **Formula** | $P_d = \dfrac{V \times I}{A_{spot}}$ | $H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{v}$ |
| **Units** | W/mm² | J/mm |
| **Governs** | Penetration depth, HAZ width, weld pool shape | Overall thermal cycle, distortion, residual stress |
| **Typical values** | SMAW: ~1000 W/mm²; LBW: 10⁶ W/mm² | SMAW: 1–3 kJ/mm; LBW: 0.05–0.2 kJ/mm |

> **Mark Distribution (3 M):** Definitions 1M + Formulae 1M + Distinguishing points 1M = **3M**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse heat input with power density** — they have different units and physical meanings.
> 2. **Always include the thermal efficiency $\eta$** in the heat input formula. Omitting $\eta$ leads to a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit conversions are critical.** Show the conversion factor (0.06 or 60) explicitly in numericals.
> 4. **State the assumption** of bead shape factor (typically 0.6–0.8) when computing maximum travel speeds; otherwise, the answer is "thermodynamically theoretical."

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of welding heat sources** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **chemical combustion** and **electrical resistance** heat sources in detail, highlighting their operating principles, typical parameters, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **submerged arc welding (SAW)** operation is carried out on a 10 mm thick low-carbon steel plate. The welding parameters are: arc voltage = **32 V**, welding current = **450 A**, and travel speed = **5 mm/s**. Given the thermal efficiency of SAW is **0.90**:
  (i) Calculate the **heat input** in kJ/mm.
  (ii) If the arc spot diameter is **6 mm**, determine the **power density** at the workpiece.
  (iii) Comment on the suitability of this heat input for a **6 mm thick plate** of mild steel (typical heat input limit ~1.5 kJ/mm). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Classification & Detailed Discussion (7 M):**

**Classification (1.5 M — with block diagram):**

The four families are: **(i) Chemical combustion**, **(ii) Electrical resistance**, **(iii) Electric arc**, and **(iv) High-energy density beams**. (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)

**Chemical Combustion Heat Sources (2.5 M):**
- **Principle:** Exothermic oxidation of a fuel gas in the presence of oxygen releases heat. The maximum flame temperature is achieved with **acetylene (C₂H₂)** because of its high flame propagation velocity and endothermic dissociation energy.
- **Oxy-acetylene reaction:** $C_2H_2 + \frac{5}{2}O_2 \to 2CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Heat (1,300 kJ/mol)}$
- **Flame types:** Neutral (1:1), Carburizing (excess C₂H₂), Oxidizing (excess O₂). Only **neutral flame** is used for steel welding.
- **Thermal efficiency:** 0.40–0.50 (low due to convective/radiative losses from the diffuse flame).
- **Applications:** Thin sheet welding, repair work, brazing, soldering, pipe joint in field where electricity is unavailable.

**Electrical Resistance Heat Sources (3 M):**
- **Principle:** Joule heating $Q = I^2 R t$ generated at the faying interface when high current (kA range) passes through two clamped workpieces under electrode pressure.
- **Heat generation zones:** (i) at the electrode-workpiece interface, (ii) at the faying interface, (iii) in the bulk of the workpiece. The faying interface resistance is the **largest** because of surface oxide roughness.
- **Processes:**
  - **Spot welding** (RSW): Single localized nugget, used in automotive sheet metal.
  - **Seam welding:** Continuous overlapping spots via roller electrodes, used for tank seams.
  - **Projection welding**: Embossed projections localize current, used for nut-to-plate welding.
  - **Butt / Flash welding**: End-to-end joining of bars, rods, tubes, rails.
- **Parameters:** Current 5–20 kA, time 0.1–2 s, electrode force 2–8 kN.
- **Advantages:** Fast, automatable, no filler metal, narrow HAZ, self-limiting (when nugget grows, resistance drops, heat generation ceases).
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to lap or butt joints; requires heavy machinery for high force; not suitable for thick sections (> 6 mm per pass).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 M):**

**(i) Heat Input in kJ/mm (3 M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \text{ (kJ/mm)} &= \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 0.90 \times 32 \times 450}{5} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 0.90 \times 14400}{5} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 12960}{5} \\
  &= \frac{777.6}{5} \\
  &= 155.52 / 1000 \text{ (recheck unit)} \\
  &= \frac{0.06 \times 12960}{5} = \frac{777.6}{5} = 155.52 \text{ ...} \text{(recheck)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Re-evaluation (corrected):**
- $V \times I = 32 \times 450 = 14{,}400$ W
- $\eta \times V \times I = 0.90 \times 14400 = 12{,}960$ W
- $H$ (J/s ÷ mm/s) at $v = 5$ mm/s: $H = 12{,}960 / 5 = 2{,}592$ J/mm
- Convert to kJ/mm: $H = 2.592$ kJ/mm

**Easier method (using the 0.06 form):**

$$
H = \frac{0.06 \times 0.90 \times 32 \times 450}{5} = \frac{777.6}{5} = 155.52 \text{ kJ/mm ??}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction Alert:** The factor 0.06 already converts W to **Watts × 60 s/min / 1000 J/kJ = 0.06 kJ/(W·min)**. But we used $v$ in mm/s, not mm/min. Let me redo:

The standard form is:

$$
H \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \times 60}{v \text{ (mm/min)} \times 1000}
$$

If $v$ is given in **mm/s**, multiply by 60 to get mm/min:

$$
H = \frac{0.90 \times 32 \times 450 \times 60}{(5 \times 60) \times 1000} = \frac{777600}{300000} = 2.592 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

> **Alternative — direct J/mm:**
>
> $$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} = \frac{0.90 \times 32 \times 450}{5} = \frac{12960}{5} = 2592 \text{ J/mm} = 2.592 \text{ kJ/mm}$$
>
> **The factor 0.06 is ONLY used when $v$ is in mm/s and you want kJ/mm but the formula is structured as**:
> $$H \text{(kJ/mm)} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \times 60}{v \text{ (mm/min)} \times 1000}$$
>
> Substituting $v$ (mm/s): $H = \frac{0.06 \times \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \text{ (mm/s)}}$ but the result is in **kJ/m**, not kJ/mm. **Verify with your instructor's preferred form.**

**Final Answer: $H = 2.592$ kJ/mm** (i.e., 2,592 J/mm).

**(ii) Power Density (2 M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= V \times I = 32 \times 450 = 14{,}400 \text{ W} \\
A_{spot} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6^2 = 28.27 \text{ mm}^2 \\
P_d &= \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{14400}{28.27} \approx 509.4 \text{ W/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** For a true process power density, multiply by $\eta$: $P_{d, useful} = 0.90 \times 509.4 = 458.5$ W/mm².

**(iii) Suitability Comment (2 M):**

The computed heat input of **2.592 kJ/mm** is **excessive** for a 6 mm thick plate (typical limit ~1.5 kJ/mm for mild steel to avoid grain coarsening in the HAZ and to maintain impact toughness). For the given parameters, this heat input is more appropriate for **plate thicknesses of 10–16 mm** in a single pass, which matches the actual 10 mm plate. The result indicates **acceptable heat input for the 10 mm plate** but the comment in the question is slightly misleading — for a **6 mm plate**, the same parameters would cause **excessive penetration, distortion, and possibly burn-through**; parameters should be reduced (lower current or higher travel speed).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe the **electric arc** as a welding heat source. With the help of a voltage-current characteristic curve, explain the difference between **constant-current (CC)** and **constant-voltage (CV)** power sources. State one typical application for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **gas tungsten arc welding (GTAW/TIG)** process is to be used for joining **2 mm thick stainless steel (SS 304)** sheet. Calculate:
  (i) The **heat input** if $V = 18$ V, $I = 120$ A, $v = 4$ mm/s, and $\eta = 0.55$.
  (ii) The **mass of metal melted per second** if the bead cross-sectional area is **4 mm²** and SS 304 density is **8.0 g/cm³ = 8 × 10⁻³ kg/cm³**.
  (iii) The **theoretical energy required per second** to melt this mass, given $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·K), $T_m = 1450$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $L_f = 2.6 \times 10^5$ J/kg. Compare it with the supplied energy and comment on the **process efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Electric Arc & Power Source Characteristics (7 M):**

**The Electric Arc (2 M):**
An **electric arc** is a self-sustaining, high-temperature electrical discharge through a partially ionized gas (plasma) maintained between two electrodes — the **cathode** (negative) and **anode** (positive). In welding, the arc is sustained by:
- **Thermionic emission** of electrons from the hot cathode (in non-consumable systems like GTAW).
- **Field emission** at high current densities (in consumable electrode processes like GMAW/SMAW).

The arc consists of three zones: **(i) Cathode drop region** (~10 V, intense heat), **(ii) Arc column/plasma** (nearly neutral, low voltage gradient), and **(iii) Anode drop region** (~10 V, lower temperature than cathode for steel). The total arc voltage ranges from **15–40 V** and the temperature from **6,000–20,000 K** in the plasma column.

**V-I Characteristics & Power Source Types (4 M):**

The arc exhibits a **negative (falling) V-I characteristic** — as current increases, voltage drops slightly. This is unstable, so external regulation is required.

```
   V (Voltage) ^
              |   CC Source
              |    /--___
              |   /      ----__
              |  /            ----__  CV Source
              | /                  --____
              |/_________________________>  I (Current)
```

- **Constant-Current (CC) Power Source:** Maintains current nearly constant despite arc length variations. Has a **steeply drooping V-I curve**. Used for **manual processes (SMAW, GTAW)** where the operator cannot maintain perfectly constant arc length. Typical: transformer-reactor (saturable reactor) or engine-driven generator.
- **Constant-Voltage (CV) Power Source:** Maintains voltage nearly constant. Has a **flat V-I curve**. Used for **semi-automatic/automatic GMAW (MIG/MAG) and FCAW** where the wire feed speed self-regulates the current. Typical: rectifier with feedback control.

**Applications (1 M):**
- **CC power source → SMAW** (manual stick welding of structural steel).
- **CV power source → GMAW/MIG** (robotic welding of automotive bodies, sheet metal).

---

**(b) GTAW Numerical Solution (7 M):**

**(i) Heat Input (2 M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \text{ (J/mm)} &= \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v} \\
  &= \frac{0.55 \times 18 \times 120}{4} \\
  &= \frac{0.55 \times 2160}{4} \\
  &= \frac{1188}{4} \\
  &= 297 \text{ J/mm} \approx 0.297 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is a very low heat input — ideal for thin 2 mm SS 304 sheet** to prevent warping and sensitization (chromium carbide precipitation in the 450–850 °C range).

**(ii) Mass of Metal Melted per Second (2 M):**

The volume melted per second equals bead area × travel speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= A_{bead} \times v = 4 \text{ mm}^2 \times 4 \text{ mm/s} = 16 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \\
  &= 16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(since 1 cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3)\\
\dot{m} &= \rho \times \dot{V} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cm}^3 \times 16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \\
  &= 128 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} \\
  &= 1.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical Energy Required & Efficiency (3 M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fusion} &= c_p \times (T_m - T_0) + L_f \\
  &= 500 \times (1450 - 25) + 2.6 \times 10^5 \\
  &= 500 \times 1425 + 260000 \\
  &= 712500 + 260000 \\
  &= 972500 \text{ J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy required per second to melt $\dot{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{theoretical} &= \dot{m} \times E_{fusion} \\
  &= 1.28 \times 10^{-4} \times 972500 \\
  &= 124.48 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy supplied per second (= useful power delivered):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{supplied} &= \eta \times V \times I = 0.55 \times 18 \times 120 = 1188 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Actual melting efficiency (ratio of theoretical to supplied):**

$$
\eta_{melt} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{theoretical}}{\dot{Q}_{supplied}} = \frac{124.48}{1188} = 0.1048 = 10.48\%
$$

> **Comment:** Only ~**10.5%** of the useful arc power is used to actually melt the base metal. The remaining ~89.5% is dissipated as:
> - **Conduction** into the cold base metal surrounding the weld pool (~50–60%).
> - **Radiation and convection** from the arc column and weld pool surface (~15–20%).
> - **Heating of the tungsten electrode and shielding gas** (~10%).
>
> This is consistent with the KTU concept that **the actual melting efficiency of an arc process is typically 10–25%** of the electrical input, and the rest is lost to the surroundings. The textbook value of $\eta = 0.55$ for GTAW is the **arc-to-workpiece heat transfer efficiency** (not the melting efficiency).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Scheme**

### 1. Core Definition
- A **welding heat source** is a localized, high-intensity energy delivery system that generates sufficient thermal energy to melt and fuse base metals.
- The four families: **Chemical combustion, Electrical resistance, Electric arc, High-energy density beams**.

### 2. Power Density Hierarchy (Memorize This Order)
$$\text{OFW} < \text{SMAW} < \text{GTAW} < \text{PAW} < \text{LBW} < \text{EBW}$$
$$\sim 10^2 < 10^3 < 10^3\text{–}10^4 < 10^4 < 10^6 < 10^7 \quad \text{W/mm}^2$$

### 3. Master Formula
$$\boxed{H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}}$$
- Add factor **0.06** only when converting units explicitly to **kJ/mm with v in mm/s**.
- Add factor **60/1000** when $v$ is in **mm/min** and result is in **kJ/mm**.

### 4. Thermal Efficiency ($\eta$) — Quick Recall
- OFW: 0.40–0.50 | SMAW: 0.70–0.85 | GMAW: 0.65–0.85
- GTAW: 0.40–0.60 | SAW: 0.85–0.95 | LBW: 0.30–0.40 | EBW: 0.65–0.80

### 5. Arc Welding Process Selection
- **CC source** → SMAW, GTAW (manual/stiff control)
- **CV source** → GMAW, FCAW, SAW (self-regulating wire feed)

### 6. Resistance Welding — Joule's Law
$$Q = I^2 R t$$
- Heat generated **internally** at the faying interface.
- **Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt, Flash** are the KTU-mandated variants.

### 7. Common Numerical Traps
- **Speed units:** mm/s vs. mm/min — always verify.
- **Result units:** J/mm vs. kJ/mm — check the question carefully.
- **Spot area:** Use $A = \pi d^2/4$ (circle) for the arc spot.
- **Bead shape factor:** Theoretical max speed assumes 100% of melted metal forms the bead — reality is 60–80%.

### 8. High-Energy Density Beam Essentials
- **LBW** uses photon energy — works in air, fiber lasers preferred for metals.
- **EBW** uses kinetic energy of electrons — **requires vacuum** (otherwise electrons scatter in air).
- Both create a **keyhole** (vapor cavity) for deep penetration.

### 9. Oxy-Acetylene Flame
- **Neutral flame** = 1:1 to 1:1.2 (C₂H₂:O₂) — used for steel.
- **Carburizing** = excess C₂H₂ — used for hardfacing, carbide deposition.
- **Oxidizing** = excess O₂ — rarely used, causes metal oxidation.

### 10. Typical Heat Input Limits (Industrial Practice)
- **Mild steel:** ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm to avoid HAZ grain coarsening.
- **Stainless steel (SS 304):** ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm to prevent sensitization.
- **HSLA steels:** ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm to preserve toughness.
- **Aluminum alloys:** ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm to avoid HAZ softening.

> **End of Module 2 — Welding Heat Sources Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | Course: PCPET402 Manufacturing Processes*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fusion and Non-Fusion Welding Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fusion and Non-Fusion Welding Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Welding — The KTU 2024 Definition

According to the **AWS (American Welding Society)** and adopted by the KTU 2024 Manufacturing Processes syllabus, **Welding** is defined as:

> *“A localized coalescence of materials, produced by heating to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal, to produce a metallurgical bond between the parts.”*

In simpler words, **welding is the process of permanently joining two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastics) by causing atomic-level fusion or bonding at the joint interface**, so that the resulting assembly behaves as a single continuous piece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPET402 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (a) classify welding processes as fusion vs solid-state, (b) explain the operating principle of at least 3 fusion and 2 non-fusion processes, and (c) compute heat input, deposition rate, and efficiency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gluing" Metaphor

Think of welding like **gluing two pieces of wood** — but instead of chemical glue, we either:

1. **Melt a small region of the wood itself** (and sometimes add a wooden stick) until the wood fibers literally flow into each other, then let it cool → this is **Fusion Welding** (the "hot glue gun" of metals).
2. **Press the two wood pieces together so hard** that their fibers interlock mechanically at room temperature, with or without gentle heat to help → this is **Non-Fusion / Solid-State Welding** (like the "cold welding" pressure technique).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Distinction:**
> - **Fusion Welding** → Base metal is melted (reaches liquidus). A weld pool forms and solidifies.
> - **Non-Fusion Welding** → Base metal stays essentially in the **solid state**. Bonding occurs by diffusion, plastic deformation, or mechanical interlocking — *no melting of the base metal*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Melting point of mild steel | $T_m$ | **≈ 1500 °C** |
| Acetylene flame temperature | $T_{flame}$ | **≈ 3200 °C** |
| Arc temperature (TIG/GMAW) | $T_{arc}$ | **6000 – 20,000 °C** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Heat of fusion of iron | $L_f$ | **247 kJ/kg** |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Welding Processes

**2.1.1 Fusion (Fusion-type) Welding Processes**
These processes melt the **base metal** (and often a filler metal) to form a weld pool. Bonding occurs upon **solidification**.

**2.1.2 Non-Fusion (Solid-State) Welding Processes**
These processes join materials **without melting the base metal**. Bonding occurs by:
- Plastic deformation
- Atomic diffusion
- Mechanical interlocking (friction stir)

### 2.2 Detailed Process Breakdown — Fusion Welding

**A. Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW / Gas Welding)**
- **Fuel gas:** Acetylene ($C_2H_2$)
- **Combustion supporter:** Oxygen ($O_2$)
- **Flame temperature:** ~3200 °C (Neutral flame)
- **Heat source:** Chemical combustion
- **Filler:** Optional filler rod added manually
- **Weld pool size:** Large, slow cooling
- **Applications:** Thin sheets, repair work, plumbing, low-melting non-ferrous metals

**B. Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW — "Stick" welding)**
- **Heat source:** Electric arc between a consumable electrode and base metal
- **Electrode:** Coated (flux-coated) consumable stick
- **Shielding:** Flux coating decomposes to form $CO_2$ + slag vapour blanket
- **Current type:** AC or DC
- **Applications:** Heavy structural work, pipelines, shipbuilding, field repairs

**C. Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG)**
- **Heat source:** Continuous arc between continuously-fed **wire electrode** and workpiece
- **Shielding gas:** Argon, $CO_2$, or Ar/$CO_2$ mix
- **Wire:** Solid (most common) or metal-cored
- **Mode of metal transfer:** Short-circuit / Globular / Spray / Pulsed spray
- **Applications:** Automotive, fabrication, robotics

**D. Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)**
- **Electrode:** Non-consumable Tungsten (W) electrode
- **Shielding gas:** Inert (Argon or Helium)
- **Filler:** Added separately by hand if required
- **Advantages:** Highest quality, no spatter, precise control
- **Applications:** Aerospace, stainless steel, aluminium, exotic alloys

**E. Resistance Welding (Spot, Seam, Projection)**
- **Heat source:** Electrical resistance heating ($H = I^2 R t$)
- **Pressure:** Mechanical forge force applied simultaneously
- **No filler metal, no shielding gas, no arc**
- **Applications:** Automotive body sheets, wire mesh, sheet metal joining

**F. Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)**
- **Arc is buried** under a thick layer of granular flux
- **No visible arc, no spatter, no UV radiation**
- **Applications:** Heavy plates, pressure vessels, ship hulls

**G. Thermit Welding**
- **Heat source:** Exothermic chemical reaction of thermit mixture ($Fe_2O_3 + 2Al → 2Fe + Al_2O_3$)
- **Temperature:** ~2500 °C — molten steel flows into joint
- **Applications:** Rail welding (in-situ), large forging repair

**H. Electroslag Welding (ESW)**
- **Heat source:** Resistance heating of molten slag bath
- **Used for:** Very thick plates (>25 mm) in vertical position
- **Applications:** Heavy machinery, large diameter pipes

**I. Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**
- **Constricted arc** through a copper nozzle → very high energy density
- **Higher temperature and penetration** than TIG
- **Applications:** Aerospace, thin-wall tubes

**J. Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**
- **Heat source:** Focused coherent laser beam ($CO_2$ or Nd:YAG or fibre laser)
- **Energy density:** Very high → deep penetration, narrow HAZ
- **Applications:** Automotive (tailor-welded blanks), electronics, medical devices

**K. Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**
- **Heat source:** Accelerated electron beam in vacuum
- **Energy density:** Extremely high → keyhole mode welding
- **Applications:** Aerospace turbine blades, nuclear components, dissimilar metals

### 2.3 Detailed Process Breakdown — Non-Fusion Welding

**A. Friction Welding (FW) — Inertia & Direct Drive**
- One part is rotated against another under axial pressure
- **Frictional heat** softens the interface → plastic deformation → forging bond
- **No arc, no filler, no shielding gas**
- **Applications:** Drill bits, valves, drive shafts, dissimilar metals (steel-aluminium)

**B. Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**
- A **non-consumable rotating tool** with a pin & shoulder plunges into the joint
- Tool **stirs** the material plastically without melting
- Bond forms in the **solid state** through dynamic recrystallization
- **Applications:** Aluminium ship decks, aerospace panels, railway cars

**C. Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**
- High-frequency vibration (20–40 kHz) under moderate pressure
- Heat generated by **surface friction and shear**
- **Applications:** Thin foils, electrical terminals, battery tabs, plastics

**D. Diffusion Bonding (Diffusion Welding)**
- Joints held under pressure at elevated temperature (typically 0.5–0.8 $T_m$)
- Atomic diffusion across the interface forms the bond
- **No macroscopic deformation, no liquid phase**
- **Applications:** Titanium honeycomb, aerospace sandwich structures, nuclear fuel elements

**E. Explosion Welding (EXW)**
- Controlled detonation drives one plate (flyer) into another (target) at very high velocity
- **Bond forms by jetting of surface oxides and atomic bonding**
- **Applications:** Cladding of corrosion-resistant plates (SS on CS, Ti on steel)

**F. Cold Pressure Welding**
- Severe plastic deformation at **room temperature** breaks surface oxides
- Clean metal-to-metal contact under high pressure → bond
- **Applications:** Electrical conductors, aluminium wire splices

**G. Roll Bonding (Roll Welding)**
- Sheets passed through rolling mill under high pressure
- Bond forms by plastic deformation + diffusion
- **Applications:** Clad plates, bimetallic strips

**H. Forge Welding (Historical)**
- Oldest known welding method — heating in forge + hammering
- Mostly obsolete; replaced by modern fusion methods

**I. Hot Pressure Welding**
- Combination of elevated temperature (below melting) + pressure
- Used for powder metallurgy and ceramic-metal bonding

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Input (H)** is the single most important quantity KTU examiners test. Memorize this carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input (arc welding) | **kJ/mm** |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | Heat Input with efficiency $\eta$ | **J/mm** |
| 3 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Heat generated (resistance welding) | **Joules** |
| 4 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power | **Watts** |
| 5 | $R_d = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{A}$ | Deposition rate (SMAW) | **kg/h** |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Heat to workpiece}}{\text{Total heat generated}}$ | Process thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $v_{trav} = \dfrac{S}{t}$ | Travel speed of electrode | **mm/s** |
| 8 | $T_{arc} \approx 6000 - 20000 \ ^\circ C$ | Arc temperature | °C |
| 9 | $T_{flame} \approx 3200 \ ^\circ C$ | Oxy-acetylene flame | °C |
| 10 | $P_{electrode} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m}$ | Specific electrode consumption | **kJ/kg** |
| 11 | $A_{weld} = \dfrac{\text{Wire feed rate}}{\text{Travel speed}}$ | Cross-section area of weld | **mm²** |
| 12 | $W_{H} = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000 S}$ | Heat input (simplified KTU form) | **kJ/mm** |

> **Where:**
> - $V$ = Arc voltage (**Volts**)
> - $I$ = Welding current (**Amperes**)
> - $S$ = Travel speed (**mm/min**)
> - $t$ = Time (**seconds**)
> - $\eta$ = Process efficiency (decimal)
> - $R$ = Electrical resistance (**Ohms**)

### 2.5 Typical Process Efficiencies ($\eta$) — Examiner Favourite

| Process | Thermal Efficiency $\eta$ |
|---|---|
| Oxy-Acetylene Welding | **0.40 – 0.50** |
| SMAW (Stick) | **0.65 – 0.85** |
| GMAW (MIG) | **0.65 – 0.85** |
| GTAW (TIG) | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| SAW | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| PAW | **0.55 – 0.70** |
| LBW | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| EBW | **0.70 – 0.80** |
| Resistance Spot Welding | **0.80 – 0.90** |
| Friction Welding | **~ 1.00 (very efficient)** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding & bridges** → SMAW + SAW for thick sections
- **Aerospace** → EBW, GTAW, FSW for high-strength lightweight joints
- **Automotive mass production** → Resistance spot welding (4000+ spots per car body)
- **Pipeline laying** → SMAW + Cellulosic electrodes (E6010, E7010)
- **Railway tracks** → Thermit welding for in-situ joint restoration
- **Battery manufacturing** → Ultrasonic welding for cell tabs
- **Aluminium ship hulls** → Friction stir welding (no porosity, no HAZ cracking)

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Formula (Arc Welding)

The **heat input** is the amount of heat energy delivered per unit length of weld. KTU examiners love this derivation.

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of electrical power.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2:** Energy delivered over time $t$ (in seconds) is:

$$E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** Of this total energy, only a fraction $\eta$ reaches the workpiece (the rest is lost as radiation, spatter, fumes, etc.).

$$E_{weld} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Heat input is defined as energy per unit length of weld. If the travel speed in mm/min is $S$, then the time to traverse a length $L$ (mm) is:

$$t = \dfrac{L \cdot 60}{S}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the time into the energy expression, then divide by $L$ to get heat per unit length:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot L \cdot 60}{S \cdot L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}$$

**Step 6:** When $V$ is in volts, $I$ in amperes, and $S$ in mm/min, the resulting units are J/mm. To convert to **kJ/mm** (KTU preferred unit), divide by 1000:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

> This is the **canonical KTU heat input equation**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resistance Welding Heat Generation

**Step 1:** Electrical power dissipated in a resistance is given by Joule's law:

$$P = I^2 R$$

**Step 2:** Total heat generated during time $t$ is:

$$H = P \cdot t = I^2 R t$$

**Step 3:** For **resistance spot welding** specifically, the contact resistance $R_c$ between two sheets + bulk resistance of the sheets combined gives the total resistance $R$. The heat generated at the faying surface is:

$$H = I^2 (R_{c} + 2R_{b}) t$$

**Step 4:** Power density (heat per unit area of nugget) is critical:

$$q = \dfrac{I^2 R t}{A_{nugget}} \quad [\text{J/mm}^2]$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deposition Rate for SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)

**Step 1:** The mass of metal deposited per second is determined by the wire feed rate $W$ (kg/min):

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{W}{60} \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

**Step 2:** The deposition rate in kg/h is:

$$R_d = W = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{wire}}{1} \quad \text{(since 1 minute = 1/60 hour)}$$

**Step 3:** In terms of welding current (empirical relation for mild steel, SAW):

$$R_d \approx 0.0024 \cdot I \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$

(for a 4 mm wire, single arc, typical SAW)

**Step 4:** Travel speed is related to deposition rate and weld cross-section by:

$$S = \dfrac{R_d \cdot 10^6}{A_{weld} \cdot \rho \cdot 60} \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

where $\rho$ = density of weld metal (≈ 7850 kg/m³ for steel), $A_{weld}$ in mm².

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Input Calculation

**Problem:** A SMAW operation is performed at $V = 25 \text{ V}$, $I = 200 \text{ A}$, $S = 300 \text{ mm/min}$. Take process efficiency $\eta = 0.75$. Calculate the heat input.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**
$V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 300$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.75$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat input formula:**

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$H = \dfrac{0.75 \cdot 25 \cdot 200 \cdot 60}{300 \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.75 \cdot 25 = 18.75$$
$$18.75 \cdot 200 = 3750$$
$$3750 \cdot 60 = 225{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator:**

$$300 \cdot 1000 = 300{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$H = \dfrac{225{,}000}{300{,}000} = 0.75 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

> **Answer:** $H = 0.75 \text{ kJ/mm}$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fusion vs Non-Fusion Welding

| Criterion | Fusion Welding | Non-Fusion Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Base metal state | Molten (liquidus reached) | Solid (below solidus) |
| Heat-affected zone (HAZ) | Large, distinct | Minimal or none |
| Solidification defects possible | Yes (porosity, cracks) | No |
| Dissimilar metal joining | Difficult | Often excellent |
| Filler metal | Often required | Usually not required |
| Thermal distortion | High | Low |
| Equipment cost | Variable | Often high (specialized) |
| Examples | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, LBW, EBW | FSW, FW, USW, Diffusion, EXW |
| Typical KTU mark weightage | 60–70 % | 30–40 % |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Welding Processes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["WELDING PROCESSES"] --> B["FUSION WELDING"]
    A --> C["NON FUSION WELDING"]
    B --> B1["Oxy Acetylene Gas Welding"]
    B --> B2["Arc Welding SMAW"]
    B --> B3["Arc Welding GMAW MIG"]
    B --> B4["Arc Welding GTAW TIG"]
    B --> B5["Submerged Arc Welding SAW"]
    B --> B6["Plasma Arc Welding PAW"]
    B --> B7["Laser Beam Welding LBW"]
    B --> B8["Electron Beam Welding EBW"]
    B --> B9["Resistance Welding"]
    B --> B10["Thermit Welding"]
    B --> B11["Electroslag Welding ESW"]
    C --> C1["Friction Welding FW"]
    C --> C2["Friction Stir Welding FSW"]
    C --> C3["Ultrasonic Welding USW"]
    C --> C4["Diffusion Bonding"]
    C --> C5["Explosion Welding EXW"]
    C --> C6["Cold Pressure Welding"]
    C --> C7["Roll Bonding"]
    C --> C8["Forge Welding"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Welding Power Source AC or DC"] --> P2["Electrode Holder"]
    P2 --> P3["Consumable Flux Coated Electrode"]
    P3 --> P4["Electric Arc Strikes Between Tip and Workpiece"]
    P4 --> P5["Base Metal Melts to Form Weld Pool"]
    P5 --> P6["Electode Tip Melts and Adds Filler"]
    P4 --> P7["Flux Decomposes to Form Shielding Gas CO2"]
    P7 --> P8["Slag Layer Forms on Top of Weld"]
    P5 --> P9["Weld Pool Solidifies into Joint"]
    P8 --> P10["Slag is Chipped Off After Cooling"]
    P9 --> P11["Final Weld Bead"]
    P10 --> P11
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Resistance Spot Welding Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["Sheets Clamped Between Copper Electrodes"] --> Q2["Force F Applied via Pneumatic or Hydraulic Cylinder"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Welding Current I Switched On for Time t"]
    Q3 --> Q4["I squared R Heat Generated at Faying Surface"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Local Melting Forms a Nugget"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Current Switched Off"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Hold Time Applied Under Pressure"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Nugget Solidifies under Forge Force"]
    Q8 --> Q9["Electrodes Retract Weld Spot Complete"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Friction Stir Welding Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1["Two Plates Butted Together"] --> R2["Rotating Tool with Pin and Shoulder Plunges In"]
    R2 --> R3["Frictional Heat Softens Material Below Melting Point"]
    R3 --> R4["Shoulder Forges and Stirs Plasticized Material"]
    R4 --> R5["Pin Mixes Material Across the Joint Line"]
    R5 --> R6["Solid State Bond Forms Behind Tool"]
    R6 --> R7["Tool Retracts Leaving Recess and Flash"]
    R7 --> R8["Recrystallized Fine Grained Weld Zone"]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Block — Heat Source Energy Density Spectrum

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Oxy Acetylene 10 to 100 W per mm2"] --> S2["SMAW 50 to 200 W per mm2"]
    S2 --> S3["GTAW TIG 200 to 1000 W per mm2"]
    S3 --> S4["PAW 1000 to 5000 W per mm2"]
    S4 --> S5["LBW 10 to 100 kW per mm2"]
    S5 --> S6["EBW 100 to 1000 kW per mm2"]
```

### 4.6 Process Selection Logic Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["Choose Welding Process"] --> T2{"Material Type"}
    T2 --> T3["Carbon Steel"] & T4["Aluminium Alloy"] & T5["Stainless Steel"] & T6["Titanium or Exotic"]
    T3 --> T7["SMAW GMAW SAW"]
    T4 --> T8["GTAW FSW GMAW"]
    T5 --> T9["GTAW PAW LBW"]
    T6 --> T10["EBW LBW Diffusion"]
    T1 --> T11{"Thickness"}
    T11 --> T12["Thin under 3 mm"] & T13["Medium 3 to 25 mm"] & T14["Thick above 25 mm"]
    T12 --> T15["GTAW LBW USW"]
    T13 --> T16["SMAW GMAW GTAW"]
    T14 --> T17["SAW ESW EBW"]
    T1 --> T18{"Production Volume"}
    T18 --> T19["Low"] & T20["High"]
    T19 --> T21["SMAW GTAW"]
    T20 --> T22["Resistance Spot GMAW Robotic"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Differentiate between fusion welding and non-fusion welding with two examples of each.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fusion Welding | Non-Fusion Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Base metal state during joining | **Molten** (reaches liquidus temperature) | **Solid** (stays below solidus) |
| Filler metal | Usually required (SMAW, GMAW) | Usually **not required** |
| HAZ | Large and distinct | Minimal or absent |
| Examples | **SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, Oxy-acetylene** | **Friction welding, Friction stir welding, Ultrasonic welding, Diffusion bonding** |

> *[Examiner Note: Defining the term "fusion" and "non-fusion" with 1 example each fetches full 3 marks.]*

---

**Q2. What is heat input in arc welding? Write the formula and state the significance of controlling it.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Heat input is the amount of heat energy delivered per unit length of the weld. The **formula** is:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

where $\eta$ = process efficiency, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $S$ = travel speed.

**Significance:**
- Controls **weld bead size** and **penetration**
- Excessive heat input → **wider HAZ**, **grain coarsening**, **distortion**, **lower toughness**
- Insufficient heat input → **incomplete fusion**, **porosity**, **lack of penetration**
- Critical parameter for **low-alloy and high-strength steels**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Format]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Fusion Welding Focus**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process. State the functions of the **flux coating** and mention **two advantages** and **two limitations** of SMAW. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description:**
In SMAW ("stick welding"), an electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The intense heat of the arc (~6000 °C) melts both the **electrode tip** (which acts as filler metal) and a small region of the **base metal**, forming a molten **weld pool**. The flux coating decomposes to:
- Generate a shielding gas blanket ($CO_2$, $H_2$)
- Form a molten **slag** layer that floats on top of the weld pool
- Add deoxidizers and alloying elements to the weld

**Setup Components:**
- AC or DC welding power source
- Electrode holder (stinger)
- Earth clamp + cable
- Flux-coated consumable electrode
- Personal protective equipment (helmet, gloves, apron)

**Functions of Flux Coating (4 key points):**
1. **Shielding** – produces a gas cloud ($CO_2$, $H_2$) that protects the molten pool from atmospheric $O_2$ and $N_2$
2. **Slag formation** – a liquid slag floats on the pool, slowing cooling and preventing oxidation
3. **Deoxidation** – flux contains deoxidizers (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si) that scavenge oxygen
4. **Alloying** – adds alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo) to the weld deposit

**Advantages (any 2):**
- Simple, portable, low-cost equipment
- Works in outdoor/windy conditions (flux provides its own shielding)
- Versatile — can join most metals, all positions

**Limitations (any 2):**
- Limited to short weld lengths (electrode length ~ 350 mm)
- Slag must be chipped off between passes (low productivity)
- Lower deposition rate than GMAW/SAW
- Frequent electrode changing interrupts the process

---

**(b)** Describe the **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)** process. Why is it preferred for welding **stainless steel and aluminium**? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description:**
In GTAW (TIG), a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** is used. An arc forms between the tungsten tip and the workpiece. The arc and weld pool are protected by an **inert shielding gas** (Argon or Helium) flowing through the nozzle of the torch. If filler metal is needed, it is **added separately** by the welder's other hand (cold filler rod technique).

**Schematic (described):**
Power source → TIG torch → Tungsten electrode → Argon gas inlet → Shielding nozzle → Arc → Workpiece (clamped on bench with earth return)

**Key Features:**
- **Constant current (CC)** power source
- DCEN polarity (electrode negative) for most metals; AC for aluminium
- Foot pedal / current control for precise heat management
- No spatter, no slag, no smoke

**Why preferred for stainless steel and aluminium:**

1. **Stainless Steel (SS):**
   - Inert gas (Ar) prevents **chromium carbide precipitation** and sensitization
   - No flux → no **slag inclusions** in the weld
   - Precise heat control → preserves **corrosion resistance**
   - Clean bead appearance → no post-weld pickling required for cosmetic welds

2. **Aluminium:**
   - AC current provides **cathodic cleaning** action (removes tough $Al_2O_3$ film)
   - Argon shielding prevents **porosity** (Hydrogen rejection)
   - Tungsten electrode does not contaminate the soft aluminium pool
   - Low heat input capability → reduces **distortion** in thin sheets

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Non-Fusion Welding Focus**

**(a)** Explain the **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** process with a schematic. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of FSW. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description:**
Friction Stir Welding is a **solid-state** joining process developed at **TWI (The Welding Institute), UK in 1991**. A non-consumable rotating tool consisting of a **pin (probe)** and a **shoulder** is plunged into the joint line between two butted plates.

**Mechanism:**
- The **shoulder** generates frictional heat by rubbing against the top surface
- The **pin** stirs the plasticized material from the **advancing side (AS)** to the **retreating side (RS)**
- The material is **forged** behind the tool as it traverses
- Bond forms by **dynamic recrystallization** — no melting occurs
- Temperature typically reaches **0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$** of the base metal

**Key Tool Design Points:**
- Pin length ≈ plate thickness
- Pin shape: cylindrical, threaded, or fluted
- Shoulder diameter > pin diameter (typically 3×)

**Schematic Description:**
Butted plates → Clamped on rigid backing plate → Rotating tool with shoulder & pin → Tool plunges at joint → Traverses along joint line → Solid-state weld bead forms behind tool → Exit hole left at end of weld.

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. **No melting** → no porosity, no solidification cracks
2. **Excellent mechanical properties** (often stronger than parent metal)
3. **Low distortion** compared to fusion welding
4. **No shielding gas, no filler metal** required
5. **Environmentally clean** — no fumes, no UV radiation, no spatter
6. Ideal for **aluminium alloys** (used in ship hulls, aerospace, railway)

**Limitations (any 2):**
1. Requires **rigid backing** and high clamping force → limited to simple geometries
2. Leaves an **exit hole** at the end of the weld
3. Slower than fusion processes for thin materials
4. Tool wear is significant for high-temperature materials (steel, titanium)

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** process. Derive the expression for heat generated during the process and state the effect of **welding current** and **weld time** on nugget formation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description:**
Resistance Spot Welding is a process in which two or more sheets of metal are joined by the heat obtained from the **resistance to electric current flow** through the sheets, with **mechanical pressure** applied simultaneously through copper alloy electrodes.

**Process Steps:**
1. Sheets placed between **two copper alloy electrodes**
2. **Electrode force (F)** applied (typically 2–8 kN)
3. **Welding current (I)** passed (typically 5–20 kA) for a short time (~0.1–0.5 s)
4. Heat = $I^2 R t$ generated at the **faying surface** (interface) due to high contact resistance
5. A small **molten nugget** forms at the interface
6. Current switched off, **hold time** applied → nugget solidifies under pressure
7. Electrodes retract; **weld spot** is formed

**Derivation of Heat Generated:**

**Step 1:** Heat generated is the electrical energy dissipated in the resistance path.

**Step 2:** For a constant current I flowing through resistance R for time t, the energy (heat) is:

$$H = I^2 R t$$

**Step 3:** The total resistance in the path is the sum of:
- $R_c$ = contact resistance between sheets
- $2 R_b$ = bulk resistance of the two sheets

$$R_{total} = R_c + 2 R_b$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the heat generated is:

$$H = I^2 (R_c + 2 R_b) t \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Effect of Welding Current (I):**
- Heat is proportional to $I^2$ → small increase in current gives **large increase in heat**
- Too low I → **small or no nugget** (cold weld)
- Too high I → **expulsion** (molten metal ejected), porosity

**Effect of Weld Time (t):**
- Heat is proportional to t → longer time = more heat input
- Too short → insufficient melting
- Too long → excessive indentation, electrode sticking, burn-through

> **Optimum** $I$ and $t$ are chosen to give a **nugget diameter ≥ $5\sqrt{t}$ mm** (where $t$ is sheet thickness in mm) as a rule of thumb.

**Applications:** Automotive body-in-white (4000+ spots per car), refrigerator cabinets, wire mesh.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Formula units mismatch** — Heat input MUST be in kJ/mm (not J/mm). Forgetting to divide by 1000 is a 1-mark error in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency (η)** — Some students put $\eta = 0.75$ as 75 in the formula. Always use **decimal form** (0 < $\eta$ < 1).
> 3. **Wrong polarity notation** — In TIG for aluminium, it is **AC with high-frequency start**, not DC. Examiners penalize if you say "DC for everything".
> 4. **TIG ≠ MIG confusion** — TIG uses **non-consumable tungsten**, MIG uses **consumable wire**. Mixing them loses definition marks.
> 5. **FSW exit hole** — Many students forget to mention that FSW leaves an exit hole at the end of the weld. Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for this.
> 6. **Heat input without η** — In KTU 2024 questions, $\eta$ is **always** given. Marks are deducted if you omit it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Fusion Welding (Melt the base metal):**
> - **OAW** — Acetylene + O₂ flame, ~3200 °C, low efficiency, thin sheets
> - **SMAW** — Flux-coated stick, AC/DC, field work, slag removal needed
> - **GMAW (MIG)** — Wire + shielding gas, high deposition, automotive
> - **GTAW (TIG)** — Tungsten + Ar, no filler, high quality, aerospace/SS/Al
> - **SAW** — Arc buried in flux, no spatter, heavy plates
> - **PAW** — Constricted arc, deeper penetration than TIG
> - **LBW** — Laser, deep penetration, narrow HAZ, tailor-welded blanks
> - **EBW** — Electron beam in vacuum, very deep keyhole welds, aerospace
> - **Resistance** — $I^2 R t$ heat, no arc, automotive sheet joining
> - **Thermit** — Exothermic Fe₂O₃ + Al reaction, rail welding
> - **ESW** — Molten slag bath resistance heating, very thick vertical plates

> **Non-Fusion Welding (Solid-state bonding):**
> - **FW (Friction)** — Rotating + axial pressure, dissimilar metals
> - **FSW (Friction Stir)** — Rotating tool stirs material, no melting, Al alloys
> - **USW (Ultrasonic)** — 20-40 kHz vibration, thin foils, battery tabs
> - **Diffusion Bonding** — 0.5-0.8 $T_m$ + pressure, titanium honeycomb
> - **EXW (Explosion)** — Detonation-driven cladding, SS on CS plates
> - **Cold Pressure** — Severe room-temp deformation, Al wire splices
> - **Roll Bonding** — Rolling mill cladding, bimetallic strips

> **Critical Formulae (Must memorize):**

> $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

> $$H = I^2 R t \quad [\text{J}] \quad \text{(Resistance welding)}$$

> - Heat input ↑ → wider HAZ, more distortion, more grain coarsening
> - Heat input ↓ → incomplete fusion, lack of penetration, porosity

> **Efficiencies (memory aid):**
> - Highest efficiency → **SAW (0.85–0.95)**
> - Lowest efficiency → **OAW (0.40–0.50)**
> - Friction welding → near unity (~ 1.0)

> **Process Selection Mantra:**
> - **Thick + Steel + Heavy** → SAW, ESW, EBW
> - **Thin + Aluminium + Aerospace** → GTAW, FSW, LBW
> - **High Volume + Sheet Steel + Auto** → Resistance spot, GMAW
> - **Outdoor + Repair** → SMAW
> - **Stainless / Sanitary** → GTAW
> - **Rails** → Thermit
> - **Battery / Electronics** → Ultrasonic
> - **Cladding / Bimetallic** → Explosion, Roll bonding

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gas Welding # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas Welding** is a **fusion welding process** in which the joint surfaces of the two workpieces (parent metals) are melted and joined together by directing a high-temperature flame produced by the combustion of a suitable fuel gas with oxygen. The heat source used is a **gas flame** — not an electric arc — and a filler rod (welding rod) is generally added manually to the weld pool to strengthen the joint. In the context of the KTU PCPET402 syllabus, the term *Gas Welding* overwhelmingly refers to **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** — the most widely used form of gas welding in engineering workshops — because acetylene (C₂H₂) is the only common fuel gas that, when burned with pure oxygen, produces a flame hot enough (**~3200 °C**) to melt steel and most engineering metals. The relevant combustion reaction is: $$ 2 \, \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5 \, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \, \text{CO}_2 + 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blowtorch" Mental Model Imagine you are lighting a small bonfire with a match — the match alone is too weak, but if you feed pure oxygen into the fire through a small hose, the flame suddenly becomes fierce, focused, and white-hot. That is essentially what an oxy-acetylene torch does: - **Acetylene** plays the role of the *fuel* (the wood/logs). - **Oxygen (pure)** plays the role of the *intensifier* (the air being pumped in), but instead of normal air (which is only ~21% O₂), the torch uses 100% O₂ to reach extreme temperatures. - The **blowpipe / torch** is just a controlled "blowtorch" with two supply lines, a mixing chamber, and a tip (nozzle) that shapes and directs the flame. A *neutral flame* in OAW is roughly equivalent to a perfectly balanced campfire — neither fuel-rich (sooty, lazy) nor oxygen-rich (hissing, aggressive). This balance is the heart of producing a sound weld. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU Module-2 Map) | Sub-Topic | Syllabus Weight | |---|---| | Principles of gas welding (combustion chemistry) | Foundational | | Oxy-acetylene equipment (cylinders, regulators, hoses, torch) | High | | Types of flames (Neutral, Carburizing, Oxidizing) | **Very High** | | Welding techniques (Forehand / Backhand) | **Very High** | | Filler rods and fluxes | Medium | | Advantages, limitations, applications | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU board questions from this module almost always include either a labeled sketch of the three flame types or a comparison table. Memorizing the flame structures (inner cone, outer envelope, feather) is non-negotiable. ## 1.4 Why Gas Welding Still Matters in 2024+ Even with MIG/TIG/Laser dominating heavy industry, OAW remains the **go-to process for repair, maintenance, and field work** — pipe-line repairs, sheet-metal work, plumbing, brazing, and heating of large steel parts before bending. The reason is *portability* (no electricity needed) and *versatility* (can braze, solder, weld, and cut with the same equipment). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of the Oxy-Acetylene Flame When acetylene burns in **pure oxygen**, the combustion is split into two distinct zones: ### Step 1 — Primary Combustion (Inner Cone / "Feather") Acetylene first dissociates into Carbon and Hydrogen in the presence of limited oxygen. The Hydrogen portion burns first, releasing part of the energy. The remaining carbon is dispersed as incandescent particles — this is the **bright luminous zone** (the *feather*) that gives a carburizing flame its characteristic shape. $$ 2 \, \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2 \, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4 \, \text{CO} + 2 \, \text{H}_2 $$ ### Step 2 — Secondary Combustion (Outer Envelope) Carbon monoxide and the leftover hydrogen now meet the surrounding atmospheric oxygen (drawn in from the air around the torch) and burn to completion as **CO₂ and H₂O**, releasing the bulk of the heat in a **pale blue outer envelope**. $$ 4 \, \text{CO} + 2 \, \text{H}_2 + 5 \, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4 \, \text{CO}_2 + 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **inner cone** of every oxy-acetylene flame is the **hottest usable region** of the flame (≈ 3200 °C for neutral). Welders always hold the inner cone **1–3 mm above the workpiece** — never pressed into the metal — to avoid contaminating the weld pool with soot (in carburizing) or burning the metal (in oxidizing). ## 2.2 The Three Flame Types (KTU Most-Frequently-Asked Topic) The flame type is controlled by adjusting the **Oxygen-to-Acetylene ratio** at the torch's mixing chamber via the regulator and the torch valves. ### 2.2.1 Neutral Flame - **Volume ratio** $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1 : 1$ - Both gases are consumed exactly — neither excess remains. - **Appearance:** Sharp, well-defined **bright white inner cone** surrounded by a **faint blue outer envelope**. No "feather" is visible. - **Temperature:** ~ 3200 °C at the tip of the inner cone. - **Best for:** Welding **mild steel, cast iron, stainless steel, copper, brass, aluminium** — i.e., the *default* flame for general workshop welding. ### 2.2.2 Carburizing (Reducing) Flame - **Volume ratio** $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 0.8 \text{ to } 0.9 : 1$ (oxygen-deficient). - Excess acetylene remains; carbon migrates into the weld pool. - **Appearance:** A long, **feathery white plume ("acetylene feather")** extends beyond the inner cone. - **Temperature:** ~ 3000 °C (slightly cooler than neutral). - **Best for:** Welding **high-carbon steel, hard-surfacing (hardfacing), Stellite work, low-alloy steels** — adds carbon to compensate for decarburization. > [!WARNING] > Excessive carbon pickup in a carburizing flame makes the weld **hard and brittle** (martensite formation). Always use a carburizing flame only when deliberately adding carbon. ### 2.2.3 Oxidizing Flame - **Volume ratio** $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1.1 \text{ to } 1.5 : 1$ (oxygen-rich). - Excess oxygen attacks the molten metal — it oxidizes iron, manganese, silicon, etc. - **Appearance:** A **short, sharp, pointed inner cone**, **purple-hued**, with a hissing sound. No feather; outer envelope is short and harsh. - **Temperature:** ~ 3300 °C (hottest of the three). - **Best for:** Welding **brass, bronze, and copper-zinc alloys** (these alloys form protective oxide films that need active oxidation) and for **fusion welding of low-carbon steel sheet**. > [!CAUTION] > Oxidizing flame is **NOT** used for high-carbon or alloy steels because the dissolved oxygen causes porosity and brittleness in the weld. ## 2.3 Oxy-Acetylene Equipment — Component-Level Analysis | Component | Function | KTU Exam Notes | |---|---|---| | **Oxygen cylinder** | Stores O₂ at ~ 13.8 MPa (2000 psi), black body. | Right-hand thread (to avoid accidental mixing with fuel). | | **Acetylene cylinder** | Stores C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone at ~ 1.7 MPa (250 psi), maroon body. | **Left-hand thread** on regulator nut. Never use above 1.5 m³/h flow. | | **Pressure regulator (O₂ & C₂H₂)** | Reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure (typically 0.07–0.35 MPa). | Two gauges: cylinder pressure + working pressure. | | **Hoses** | Twin rubber hoses (red = fuel, green/black = O₂ — Indian/ISO standard or red/blue US). | Burst pressure > 5× working. Replace at first crack. | | **Flashback arrestors** | Prevent flame travelling back into the hose/cylinder. | **Mandatory** in modern KTU-lab practice. | | **Torch / Blowpipe** | Mixes gases and delivers flame. | Has O₂ valve, fuel valve, mixing chamber, interchangeable nozzle/tip. | | **Welding tip / nozzle** | Sizes the flame for plate thickness. | Tip size chosen so that **1 mm of plate ≈ 100 L/h of acetylene** (rule of thumb). | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol / Relation | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | Acetylene combustion | $2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Total enthalpy released ≈ 1300 kJ/mol | | Neutral flame ratio | $V_{\text{O}_2} / V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 1$ | Default for most steels | | Carburizing ratio | $V_{\text{O}_2} / V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \in [0.8,\, 0.9]$ | Carbon pickup | | Oxidizing ratio | $V_{\text{O}_2} / V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \in [1.1,\, 1.5]$ | Brass / bronze welding | | Acetylene flow rule | $Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \approx 100 \times t$ L/h | $t$ = plate thickness in mm | | Specific heat of steel | $c_p \approx 0.486$ kJ/(kg·K) | For heat-input calculations | | Heat input (general) | $H = \eta \cdot P / v$ | $\eta$ = efficiency, $P$ = power, $v$ = travel speed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The acetylene-flow rule of thumb (100 L/h per mm) is used in workshops to size the **nozzle/tip selection** quickly without manual calculation. A 6 mm plate needs a tip rated for ~ 600 L/h of acetylene. ## 2.5 Welding Techniques — Forehand vs. Backhand ### 2.5.1 Forehand Welding (Leftward / Push Technique) - Torch moves **left to right** (for a right-handed operator). - Filler rod is held **ahead** of the torch flame; both move together. - **Flame angle:** ~ 45° to the plate. - **Best for:** Thin plates (≤ 3 mm), low melting-point metals, sheet metal. ### 2.5.2 Backhand Welding (Rightward / Pull Technique) - Torch moves **right to left**. - Filler rod is held **behind** the torch flame. - **Flame angle:** ~ 60–70° to the plate. - **Best for:** Thick plates (> 3 mm), heavy-duty work, deeper penetration. > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners often ask: *"State any four differences between forehand and backhand welding."* — Memorize the table in Section 3. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Acetylene Flow Requirement vs. Plate Thickness A common KTU numerical-type question asks to determine the **acetylene flow rate** and **tip size** for a plate of given thickness. The derivation below makes the rule of thumb explicit. ### Statement For a steel plate of thickness $t$ mm, the required acetylene volume flow rate is empirically $$ Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \approx 100 \cdot t \quad \text{(L/h)} $$ ### Derivation (from energy balance) **Step 1 — Energy needed to melt a unit length of plate.** Consider 1 m length of plate, width $w$ (m), thickness $t$ (m). Mass to be melted per metre length: $$ m = \rho \cdot w \cdot t $$ For steel, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **Step 2 — Heat absorbed (sensible + latent).** $$ Q_{\text{absorbed}} = m \left[ c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right] $$ With $c_p = 0.486$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_m = 1500$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $L_f = 272$ kJ/kg. $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{absorbed}} &= 7850 \cdot w \cdot t \cdot \left[ 0.486 \cdot (1500 - 25) + 272 \right] \quad \text{(kJ/m)} \\ &= 7850 \cdot w \cdot t \cdot \left[ 716.85 + 272 \right] \\ &= 7850 \cdot w \cdot t \cdot 988.85 \quad \text{(kJ/m)} \\ &\approx 7.76 \times 10^6 \cdot w \cdot t \quad \text{(kJ/m)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Heat delivered per metre of travel by the flame.** The calorific value of acetylene ≈ **55 MJ/m³** (heat of combustion per cubic metre at STP). Flame efficiency in OAW is typically $\eta \approx 0.5$ (the rest is lost to convection/radiation). Travel speed $v$ is taken as ~ 0.10 m/min (a typical workshop value). $$ Q_{\text{delivered per metre}} = \eta \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \cdot Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \cdot \frac{1}{v \cdot 60} $$ **Step 4 — Equate absorbed to delivered and solve for $Q$.** After simplifying (cancelling $w$ and $t$ contributions), the empirical rule emerges: $$ Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \approx 100 \cdot t \quad \text{L/h per mm of thickness} $$ > [!NOTE] > This rule is the **KTU-expected short-cut** in numerical problems. The full derivation is rarely asked, but the rule is mandatory in **marking schemes**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Flame Selection for 5 mm Mild Steel **Problem:** A 5 mm thick mild-steel plate is to be welded by OAW. Determine (a) the type of flame, (b) the acetylene flow rate, (c) the appropriate tip size, and (d) the recommended welding technique. **Solution:** **(a) Flame type — Neutral flame** (default for mild steel, O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1). **(b) Acetylene flow:** $$ Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 100 \times t = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ L/h} $$ **(c) Tip size:** Select a tip rated for **500 L/h** of acetylene. Most torch tip tables list a **No. 3 or No. 4 tip** for this range. **(d) Technique:** **Forehand (leftward)**, since 5 mm is on the borderline — forehand gives better control for moderate thicknesses; backhand is used for > 6 mm with U-groove preparation. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forehand vs. Backhand Welding | Parameter | Forehand (Leftward) | Backhand (Rightward) | |---|---|---| | Direction of travel | Left → Right | Right → Left | | Filler rod position | Ahead of torch | Behind torch | | Torch angle to plate | ~ 45° | ~ 60–70° | | Heat concentration | Distributed over a larger area | Concentrated on the pool | | Penetration | Shallow → moderate | Deep | | Suitable thickness | Up to 3 mm (sheet) | > 3 mm (heavy plate) | | Weld pool visibility | Better (flame ahead reveals pool) | Pool partly hidden by flame | | Heat input to base metal | Lower (less distortion) | Higher (more distortion) | | Speed | Faster | Slower | | Skill level required | Beginner-friendly | Skilled welder | | Typical application | Sheet-metal work, repairs, light fabrication | Pressure vessels, heavy structural work | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flame Types vs. Metals | Metal | Recommended Flame | Reason | |---|---|---| | Mild steel | Neutral | Avoids oxidation & carbon pickup | | Stainless steel | Neutral (slight excess acetylene) | Preserves Cr content (no Cr-oxide) | | Cast iron | Neutral (with preheating 250 °C) | Avoids hardening, prevents cracking | | High-carbon steel | Carburizing | Replaces lost carbon | | Copper | Neutral (with flux) | Higher thermal conductivity requires more heat | | Brass / Bronze | **Oxidizing** | Zn and Sn oxide films are volatile; need to blow off | | Aluminium | Neutral (with special flux) | Al₂O₃ layer must be fluxed away | | Lead | Neutral | Low melting point, no flux needed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why oxidizing for brass?** Zinc oxide and tin oxide are **volatile** at welding temperatures — they form bubbles that are blown off by the excess oxygen stream. Using a neutral flame on brass traps these gases, causing porosity. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Oxy-Acetylene Welding Equipment Train The figure below maps the **gas-flow path** from cylinder to weld pool, with all the safety and regulation devices in the correct order. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYL["Gas Source Stage"] OX["OXYGEN CYLINDER
Black body, 13.8 MPa"] AC["ACETYLENE CYLINDER
Maroon body, 1.7 MPa"] end subgraph REG["Pressure Regulation Stage"] R1["O2 REGULATOR
Two gauges

Two gauges

Checks flame propagation"] F2["C2H2 FLASHBACK ARRESTOR
Checks flame propagation"] end subgraph HOS["Hose Stage"] H1["O2 HOSE
Green / Black

Needle type"] V2["FUEL VALVE
