Neutral flame
Low heat input"] Q1 -- "3 to 6 mm" --> T2["Use FOREHAND or BACKHAND
Neutral flame
Single V-groove if needed"] Q1 -- "More than 6 mm" --> T3["Use BACKHAND technique
Neutral flame
U or V groove preparation
Multi-pass"] M1{"Which base metal?"} M1 -- "Mild steel" --> F1["Neutral flame"] M1 -- "Brass or Bronze" --> F2["Oxidizing flame"] M1 -- "High carbon steel" --> F3["Carburizing flame"] M1 -- "Aluminium" --> F4["Neutral flame plus special flux"] style T1 fill:#d1e7dd,color:#000000 style T2 fill:#fff3cd,color:#000000 style T3 fill:#f8d7da,color:#000000 style F1 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000000 style F2 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000000 style F3 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000000 style F4 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** Always label the **inner cone**, **outer envelope**, and (for carburizing) the **acetylene feather** clearly. Examiners award marks specifically for correctly identifying the feather as a unique feature of a **carburizing flame**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the three types of oxy-acetylene flames and state the O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio for each.** **Model Answer:** The three types of flames and their volume ratios are: 1. **Neutral Flame** — $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1 : 1$. Used for general mild-steel welding. 2. **Carburizing (Reducing) Flame** — $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 0.8 - 0.9 : 1$. Excess acetylene produces a feather; used for high-carbon steel. 3. **Oxidizing Flame** — $\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1.1 - 1.5 : 1$. Excess oxygen; used for brass and bronze. **(3 marks: 1 mark per flame + ratio)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is a neutral flame? Why is it preferred for welding mild steel?** **Model Answer:** A **neutral flame** is the oxy-acetylene flame in which the oxygen and acetylene are mixed in the exact stoichiometric ratio of **1 : 1** by volume, so that both gases are completely consumed in the combustion reaction. It is recognized by a **sharp, well-defined bright white inner cone** surrounded by a faint blue outer envelope, with **no acetylene feather**. It is preferred for welding mild steel because: 1. It provides a balanced, **non-oxidizing and non-carburizing** atmosphere, so the chemistry of the weld pool is not altered. 2. It gives the **maximum usable temperature (~ 3200 °C)** at the tip of the inner cone, sufficient to melt mild steel. 3. It produces **clean, ductile, porosity-free welds**, with no carbon pickup (which would cause brittleness) and no oxygen pickup (which would cause porosity). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model] **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the three types of flames used in oxy-acetylene welding. State the applications of each flame. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **forehand** and **backhand** techniques of gas welding. State any **four differences** between them. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (a) The three flames produced by varying the **O₂ : C₂H₂ volume ratio** in an oxy-acetylene torch are: **(i) Neutral Flame** — Ratio **1 : 1** - Combustion is **stoichiometric**; no excess of either gas. - **Appearance:** A sharp, well-defined **white inner cone** with a faint blue outer envelope. **No feather**. - **Temperature:** ~ 3200 °C. - **Applications:** Welding of **mild steel, low-carbon steel, cast iron, stainless steel, copper, and aluminium** (the most common workshop flame). **(ii) Carburizing Flame** — Ratio **0.8 – 0.9 : 1** - **Excess acetylene** → unburned carbon migrates into the molten weld pool. - **Appearance:** A long, **white feathery plume (acetylene feather)** extending from the inner cone; outer envelope is bright and ragged. - **Temperature:** ~ 3000 °C. - **Applications:** Welding of **high-carbon steel, hard-surfacing (hardfacing), Stellite overlays**, and any application needing additional carbon in the weld. **(iii) Oxidizing Flame** — Ratio **1.1 – 1.5 : 1** - **Excess oxygen** → oxidizes the base metal actively. - **Appearance:** A short, **pointed, slightly purple inner cone**, a hissing harsh sound, and a very short outer envelope. - **Temperature:** ~ 3300 °C. - **Applications:** Welding of **brass, bronze, and copper-zinc alloys**; also fusion welding of thin low-carbon steel sheet. > *(Sketch expected: separate labeled diagrams showing inner cone, feather/outer envelope for each type. 3 marks for diagrams + 4 marks for explanation.)* --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (b) **Forehand (Leftward / Push) Technique:** The welder moves the torch **from left to right**, with the **filler rod held ahead of the torch flame**. The flame is directed at an angle of about **45°** to the workpiece. The technique is used for **thin plates (≤ 3 mm)** because the heat is spread over a larger area, the weld pool is clearly visible, and travel speed is fast. **Backhand (Rightward / Pull) Technique:** The welder moves the torch **from right to left**, with the **filler rod held behind the torch flame**. The flame is held more steeply, at **60°–70°** to the plate. The technique concentrates heat on the weld pool, producing **deeper penetration** for **thick plates (> 3 mm)**. | S.No. | Parameter | Forehand | Backhand | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Travel direction | Left → Right | Right → Left | | 2 | Filler rod position | Ahead of torch | Behind torch | | 3 | Torch angle | 45° | 60°–70° | | 4 | Penetration | Shallow | Deep | | 5 | Plate thickness | ≤ 3 mm | > 3 mm | | 6 | Speed | Fast | Slow | | 7 | Distortion | Low | Higher | | 8 | Skill required | Basic | Advanced | > *(7 marks: 3 marks for explanations + 4 marks for the difference table)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model] **(a)** Draw a labeled diagram of the **oxy-acetylene welding setup** and explain the functions of its main components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of gas welding? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (a) A labeled oxy-acetylene welding setup consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 diagram): 1. **Oxygen Cylinder** — Black-colored seamless steel cylinder storing O₂ at ~ 13.8 MPa; fitted with a right-hand threaded valve. 2. **Acetylene Cylinder** — Maroon-colored cylinder storing C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone at ~ 1.7 MPa; fitted with a left-hand threaded valve for safety. 3. **Pressure Regulators (O₂ & C₂H₂)** — Two-stage regulators that reduce the cylinder pressure to a steady working pressure (~ 0.07–0.35 MPa). Each has a high-pressure gauge (cylinder) and a low-pressure gauge (working). 4. **Flashback Arrestors** — Fitted to both gas lines; they prevent the flame from travelling back into the hose and cylinder, an essential safety feature. 5. **Hoses** — Twin flexible rubber hoses (one for O₂, one for C₂H₂), color-coded (O₂ = black/green, C₂H₂ = red). 6. **Welding Torch (Blowpipe)** — Has separate needle valves for O₂ and C₂H₂, an internal mixing chamber, and a tip/nozzle holder. The two gases mix here and burn at the tip. 7. **Welding Tip / Nozzle** — Interchangeable; sizes are chosen based on plate thickness (≈ 100 L/h of acetylene per mm of thickness). 8. **Filler Rod** — Manually fed into the weld pool by the welder to add material to the joint. > *(7 marks: 3 marks for the diagram + 4 marks for component functions)* --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (b) **Advantages of Gas Welding:** 1. **Portable** — Requires no electricity; ideal for field work and remote sites. 2. **Versatile** — The same equipment can be used for welding, brazing, soldering, and cutting. 3. **Low capital cost** — Equipment is cheaper than arc-welding machines. 4. **Independent of power supply** — Useful in locations without electricity. 5. **Easy heat control** — Flame can be adjusted precisely by changing gas flow. 6. **Suitable for thin sections** — Better for thin sheets than arc welding. 7. **Less distortion** in thin plates due to lower heat input. **Limitations of Gas Welding:** 1. **Slow process** — Lower productivity than arc welding. 2. **Limited to thinner sections** — Not economical for plates above ~ 25 mm. 3. **Flame temperatures are lower** than arc temperatures; difficult for high-melting metals like tungsten. 4. **Larger HAZ** (heat-affected zone) compared to arc welding. 5. **Safety hazards** — Acetylene is flammable and explosive above 2 bar; oxygen supports combustion vigorously. 6. **Skilled operator** required for consistent weld quality. 7. **Not suitable for reactive metals** (titanium, zirconium) without inert shielding. **Applications:** 1. **Maintenance and repair** workshops (pipe fittings, machinery parts). 2. **Sheet-metal work** in automobile bodies, ducting, and HVAC. 3. **Plumbing and pipe-line work** in the field. 4. **Brazing and soldering** (joining dissimilar metals). 5. **Joining of ferrous and non-ferrous metals** in small-scale fabrication. 6. **Hardfacing and surfacing** of worn parts. 7. **Heating applications** — flame hardening, preheating before bending, shrinking operations. > *(7 marks: 2 marks for advantages + 2 marks for limitations + 3 marks for applications)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Flame types questions (7-mark type):** Students often confuse the position of the **acetylene feather** — it appears *between* the inner cone and the outer envelope, **not** outside the outer envelope. Drawing it wrongly costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Equipment diagram questions:** Forgetting to label the **flashback arrestor** or confusing the **thread directions** (O₂ right-hand, C₂H₂ left-hand) loses a full mark. Always mention them explicitly in the answer. > 3. **Forehand vs. Backhand tables:** Examiners *do not* accept vague answers like *"forehand is for thin plates"*. You must include **quantitative values** (45° vs. 60°, ≤ 3 mm vs. > 3 mm, L→R vs. R→L). > 4. **Application questions:** Do **not** write *"used in industries"* — name **specific industries/processes** (e.g., *"hardfacing of excavator bucket teeth"*). > 5. **Flame selection for brass/bronze:** A *very common* error is to write "neutral flame for brass" — the **correct** answer is **oxidizing flame**, because Zn and Sn oxides must be volatilized off. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Gas welding** = fusion welding using a **gas flame** (chiefly **oxy-acetylene**, OAW). - [x] **Combustion equation:** $2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$. - [x] **Maximum flame temperature:** ~ **3200 °C** (neutral flame, tip of inner cone). - [x] **Three flame types** — Neutral (1:1), Carburizing (0.85:1, with **feather**), Oxidizing (1.3:1, **hissing**, no feather). - [x] **Default flame** for mild steel is **neutral**. - [x] **Carburizing** → high-carbon steel / hardfacing. - [x] **Oxidizing** → brass, bronze (volatilizes Zn/Sn oxides). - [x] **Equipment chain:** Cylinder → Regulator → Flashback Arrestor → Hose → Torch (valves + mixing chamber) → Nozzle. - [x] **O₂ cylinder** = black, right-hand thread; **C₂H₂ cylinder** = maroon, left-hand thread. - [x] **Flow rule of thumb:** $Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 100 \times t$ L/h, where $t$ = plate thickness in mm. - [x] **Forehand** (leftward, 45°, ≤ 3 mm, fast) vs. **Backhand** (rightward, 60–70°, > 3 mm, deep penetration). - [x] **Weld pool is held ~ 1–3 mm away from the inner cone tip** — never pressed into the pool. - [x] **Advantages** = portable, no electricity, versatile, low cost, low distortion on thin plates. - [x] **Limitations** = slow, limited to thin sections, fire/explosion hazard, lower temperature than arc. - [x] **Applications** = maintenance, repair, plumbing, sheet-metal, brazing, hardfacing, flame heating. - [x] **Always** include **flashback arrestors** in any modern exam diagram. - [x] **Always** state the **volume ratio**, the **inner-cone appearance**, and the **application** together when describing a flame. - [x] **KTU favorite one-liner:** *"A neutral flame has a sharp white inner cone and no feather"* — memorize verbatim. - [x] **KTU favorite trap:** *"Oxidizing flame is hottest"* → **TRUE**, but **NOT used for high-carbon steel** because of porosity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question on gas welding, allocate **at least 3 marks worth of content to a labeled diagram**. A text-only answer — even if technically perfect — rarely crosses 11/14 because KTU valuation keys explicitly award marks for "neat sketch with all parts labeled".

topics.title: Electric Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electric Arc Welding (EAW)** is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for the coalescence of metals is produced by an **electric arc** struck between an **electrode** and the **workpiece** (or between two electrodes). The arc produces temperatures in the range of **6000 °C to 8000 °C**, which is more than sufficient to melt virtually all engineering metals and alloys. The molten metal from the electrode (or filler rod) and the parent workpiece fuse together to form a solid metallurgical joint upon cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** An *electric arc* is a sustained, self-sustaining electrical discharge of high current density and low voltage (typically 10 V – 50 V) occurring across a gap in a circuit, producing intense heat and brilliant light.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning in Your Workshop"

Think of **lightning** during a thunderstorm. The sky builds up huge electrical potential, and electricity jumps across an air gap with a blinding flash and intense heat. **Electric Arc Welding is essentially "controlled, miniature lightning"** channelled between a metal electrode and the workpiece.

- ⚡ **The cloud (high voltage source)** = Your welding transformer/inverter.
- ⚡ **The lightning bolt** = The arc (a path of ionized gas/plasma).
- ⚡ **The ground struck** = The workpiece (which melts locally).
- ⚡ **The intense heat** = The fusion zone.

> [!NOTE]
> The air gap is normally a perfect insulator, but once ionized (by touching the electrode to start the arc), the air becomes a **plasma column** of free electrons and ions that can carry massive current — this is what we call the "arc."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- **Arc Temperature:** 6000 °C – 8000 °C (≈ 6000 K – 8000 K)
- **Arc Voltage:** 10 V – 50 V (open-circuit voltage up to 80 V for safety)
- **Welding Current:** 50 A – 500 A (typical industrial range)
- **Power Source Type:** Constant Current (CC) or Constant Voltage (CV)
- **Electrodes:** Consumable (e.g., E6013) or Non-consumable (e.g., Tungsten Inert Gas)
- **Polarity:** DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative) or DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)
- **Heat Constant:** **0.24** (calories per watt-second — derived from Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat: 1 cal = 4.186 J)

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot for KTU Board Exams

| Arc Welding Type | Electrode | Shielding | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc) | Consumable coated | Flux coating | Most commonly asked |
| **GMAW** (Gas Metal Arc / MIG) | Consumable wire | Inert/active gas | High industrial use |
| **GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc / TIG) | Non-consumable W | Inert gas (Ar/He) | Precision welds |
| **SAW** (Submerged Arc) | Consumable | Granular flux bed | High deposition |
| **PAW** (Plasma Arc) | Non-consumable W | Plasma gas + shielding | Micro-precision |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Arc Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Initiation (Arc Striking)**
- The welder brings the electrode tip into momentary **touch contact** with the workpiece, completing the circuit.
- This contact point experiences a **short-circuit current spike** (very high current through a tiny contact area → localized resistance heating).
- The heat vaporizes the metal at the contact point, releasing free electrons and metal vapour.

**Step 2: Ionization**
- The free electrons are accelerated by the **electric field** between the positively charged anode and negatively charged cathode.
- These high-velocity electrons collide with gas atoms, knocking off more electrons → **cascade ionization**.
- The air gap transforms into a **plasma column** of positive ions, electrons, and neutral atoms.

**Step 3: Arc Sustenance**
- The arc is self-sustaining because the **thermonionic emission** of electrons from the hot cathode and the **field emission** due to the voltage gradient keep feeding the plasma.
- The arc temperature stabilizes in the range **6000 K – 8000 K**.

**Step 4: Heat Transfer to Workpiece**
- Heat is transferred to the workpiece by three mechanisms:
  1. **Conduction** through the anode/cathode spot (~70% of total heat).
  2. **Convection** via the plasma jet (~25%).
  3. **Radiation** from the luminous arc column (~5%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners love to ask *"How is an arc struck and sustained?"* — the answer must include **ionization, thermionic emission, and field emission** as three pillars of arc sustenance.

## 2.2 Heat Generation, Distribution, and Efficiency

### ⚙️ The Core Physics: Joule's Heating Law

The fundamental heat generated by the arc is governed by **Joule's Law of Heating**:

$$H = 0.24 \times V \times I \times t$$

where:
- $H$ = Heat generated in **calories (cal)**
- $V$ = Arc voltage in **volts (V)**
- $I$ = Welding current in **amperes (A)**
- $t$ = Time duration in **seconds (s)**
- **0.24** is Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat (since $1 \text{ W·s} = 1 \text{ J} = 0.239 \text{ cal}$)

### ⚙️ The "Lost" Heat — Where Does the Heat Go?

Not all generated heat goes into melting the workpiece. It is distributed as follows:

| Heat Component | Destination | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| $H_w$ | Melting the workpiece (base metal) | 60% – 85% |
| $H_f$ | Melting the filler electrode | 5% – 15% |
| $H_r$ | Radiative + convective losses to surroundings | 10% – 30% |

### ⚙️ Arc Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$\eta_{arc} = \frac{H_w + H_f}{H_{total}} = \frac{\text{Useful heat for melting}}{\text{Total heat generated}}$$

Typical **arc efficiencies** for KTU reference:

| Process | Arc Efficiency |
|---|---|
| **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) | **0.85 – 0.95** (highest) |
| **GMAW** (MIG/MAG) | 0.65 – 0.85 |
| **GTAW** (TIG) | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| **SMAW** (Manual Arc) | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| **PAW** (Plasma) | 0.50 – 0.70 |

## 2.3 Power Input and Specific Heat Input — The Most Asked Formulas in KTU

### 🔥 Total Power Input
$$P_{in} = V \times I \quad (\text{in Watts})$$

### 🔥 Heat Input per Unit Length (The "Killer Formula" for KTU Numericals)
$$H_{input} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

where:
- $V$ = Arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = Welding current (A)
- $S$ = Travel speed of the electrode (mm/min)
- **60** = conversion from seconds to minutes
- **1000** = conversion from Joules to kilojoules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** This is the single most important formula for solving numerical problems in Manufacturing Processes. If you memorize this one formula, you can solve ~70% of the welding numericals asked in KTU board exams.

### 🔥 Actual Heat Available for Welding
$$H_{available} = \eta_{arc} \times H_{input} = \frac{\eta_{arc} \times V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Generated | $H = 0.24 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ | cal | Total heat produced |
| 2 | Power Input | $P = V \cdot I$ | W (or kW) | Electrical power consumed |
| 3 | Heat Input per Unit Length | $H_{in} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Thermal energy per mm weld |
| 4 | Useful Heat for Welding | $H_{useful} = \eta_{arc} \cdot H_{in}$ | kJ/mm | Actual melting heat |
| 5 | Deposition Rate | $R = \dfrac{I \times \text{melting factor}}{1000}$ | kg/hr | Filler metal deposited |
| 6 | Melting Rate (Paul's Eq.) | $M = k \cdot I$ | g/min | Electrode consumption |
| 7 | Arc Efficiency | $\eta_{arc} = \dfrac{H_w + H_f}{H_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Process comparison |
| 8 | Heat Balance | $H_{total} = H_w + H_f + H_{loss}$ | cal | Energy audit |

> [!NOTE]
> **No Pipes in Tables:** All absolute values and "such that" conditions have been written using $\vert$ (LaTeX absolute value) instead of the markdown pipe `|` character to avoid breaking the table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Electric Arc Welding |
|---|---|
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull plates, structural frames (SAW + GMAW) |
| **Pipeline Construction** | Long-seam welds on oil/gas pipes (SAW) |
| **Automotive** | Robotic MIG welding of car body panels |
| **Aerospace** | TIG welding of thin titanium/aluminium alloys |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Heavy-section welded boilers (SMAW + SAW) |
| **Construction** | On-site structural steel welding (SMAW) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Generated in the Arc (From First Principles)

**Starting Premise:** Current $I$ flows through the arc (a resistive plasma) of voltage drop $V$ for time $t$.

### Step 1: Electrical Energy Dissipated
The electrical work done in the arc is governed by the fundamental relation:

$$W = V \times I \times t$$

where $W$ is work in **Joules (J)**, $V$ in **volts**, $I$ in **amperes**, and $t$ in **seconds**.

### Step 2: Conversion to Heat (Mechanical Equivalent of Heat)
By **Joule's Law**, the electrical work is fully converted into heat energy. To express this heat in calories, we divide by the mechanical equivalent of heat $J = 4.186 \text{ J/cal}$:

$$H = \frac{W}{J} = \frac{V \times I \times t}{4.186}$$

### Step 3: Introducing the Numerical Constant
Since $\dfrac{1}{4.186} \approx 0.24$, we obtain the familiar KTU formula:

$$H = 0.24 \times V \times I \times t \quad \text{(in calories)}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Electrical energy (Joules) $\xrightarrow{\div 4.186}$ Thermal energy (calories) — the 0.24 factor is the inverse of Joule's mechanical equivalent.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input per Unit Length (Step-by-Step)

**Starting Premise:** We want the heat input normalized to the weld length, since welds are made by moving the arc at a travel speed $S$ along the joint.

### Step 1: Total Heat Input per Unit Time (Power Equivalent)
From Joule's law, the rate of heat generation (heat per second) is:

$$H_{rate} = 0.24 \times V \times I \quad \left[\frac{\text{cal}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

### Step 2: Convert to Heat per Minute (KTU Convention)
Since $S$ is usually given in mm/min, we multiply by 60 to get heat per minute:

$$H_{min} = 0.24 \times V \times I \times 60 \quad \left[\frac{\text{cal}}{\text{min}}\right]$$

### Step 3: Normalize by Travel Speed
The heat per millimetre of weld = heat per minute ÷ travel speed in mm/min:

$$H_{per \, mm} = \frac{0.24 \times V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad \left[\frac{\text{cal}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

### Step 4: Convert Calories to Kilojoules
Since $1 \text{ cal} = 4.186 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ} \approx \dfrac{1}{239} \text{ kJ}$, dividing by 1000 in the denominator:

$$H_{input} = \frac{0.24 \times V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 4170}$$

In KTU board exams, the simplified form is used:

$$H_{input} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

> **Note on the constant:** The 0.24 is absorbed into the unit conversion when working in kJ/mm directly from V·I·60/(S·1000). Always verify with the marking scheme which form is expected.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In a manual arc welding operation, the voltage is 25 V, current is 200 A, and the travel speed is 6 mm/s. Calculate:
1. The power input.
2. The heat input per unit length.
3. The actual heat available for melting (assume $\eta_{arc} = 0.75$).

### Step 1: Power Input
$$P = V \times I = 25 \times 200 = 5000 \text{ W} = 5 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Heat Input per Unit Length
First, convert travel speed: $S = 6 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 360 \text{ mm/min}$.

$$H_{input} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = \frac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{360 \times 1000}$$

$$H_{input} = \frac{300000}{360000} = 0.833 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Step 3: Actual Heat Available for Melting
$$H_{useful} = \eta_{arc} \times H_{input} = 0.75 \times 0.833 = 0.625 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Patterns

| Type of Question | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat calculation | $V, I, t$ | $H$ in cal | $H = 0.24 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ |
| Power & cost | $V, I$, cost/kWh | Cost/weld | $P = VI$, Energy = $P \times t$ |
| Heat input per length | $V, I, S$ | $H_{in}$ in kJ/mm | $H_{in} = \dfrac{VI \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ |
| Melting rate | $I$, melting factor | Deposition rate | $M = k \cdot I$ |
| Arc efficiency | $H_{useful}, H_{total}$ | $\eta$ | $\eta = H_{useful}/H_{total}$ |
| Deposition time | Mass, density, cross-section | Time | $t = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Deposition rate}}$ |

## 3.5 Heat Balance Equation — Full Energy Audit

$$H_{total} = H_{workpiece} + H_{electrode} + H_{radiation} + H_{convection} + H_{conduction}_{to\ clamps}$$

Or in compact form:

$$H_{total} = H_{useful\ fusion} + H_{losses}$$

Multiplying by $\eta$:

$$\eta \cdot H_{total} = H_{useful\ fusion}$$

This is the **energy conservation principle** that all welding process calculations are based on.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
