# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, PCPET402]**
> 
> **Define arc production. List any three methods used for arc initiation in welding processes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Arc Production** is the process of generating a **sustained electric discharge** between the electrode and the workpiece through a gaseous medium, producing sufficient heat (**6000–20000 °C**) to melt and fuse the base metals.

**Three methods of arc initiation:**

1. **Scratch (Sliding) Start:** The electrode is dragged across the workpiece surface, creating a momentary short circuit that generates localized heating, leading to ionization and arc establishment. Commonly used in **SMAW**.

2. **Lift (Touch) Start:** The electrode touches the workpiece briefly, then is lifted vertically by 1–3 mm to create a gap. The hot cathode surface emits electrons, ionizing the gap. Used in **GTAW** to avoid tungsten contamination.

3. **High-Frequency Start:** A high-voltage, high-frequency spark (**~100 kV, 1 MHz**) is superimposed on the open circuit voltage, ionizing the gap without physical contact. Used in **GTAW and PAW** for clean, non-contact arc initiation (especially for aluminum and magnesium).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PCPET402]**
> 
> **Explain the three regions of an electric arc with their typical voltage drops.**

**Model Answer:**

The electric arc consists of **three distinct regions**:

| Region | Location | Voltage Drop | Key Phenomenon |
|--------|----------|--------------|----------------|
| **Cathode Drop Region** | Adjacent to negative electrode | **8–12 V** | High electric field extracts electrons; cathode spot forms; intense ionization |
| **Anode Drop Region** | Adjacent to positive electrode | **2–10 V** | Electrons accelerated from plasma strike anode; anode heating occurs |
| **Positive Column** | Bulk of the arc | **~0.5 V/mm** | Uniform potential gradient; quasi-neutral plasma; main heat source |

The **sum of these drops** equals the total arc voltage (typically **15–30 V** in practice).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PCPET402, Model Question Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of electric arc production** in arc welding. Discuss the role of thermionic and field emission. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A GMAW process operates with the following arc constants: $a = 16$ V, $b = 3$ V/mm, $c = 30$ V·A, $d = 6$ V·A/mm. The arc length is **3 mm** and welding current is **250 A**. Calculate:
> - (i) Arc voltage
> - (ii) Arc power
> - (iii) Heat input per unit length if travel speed is **6 mm/s** and arc efficiency is **0.80**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):

**Mechanism of Electric Arc Production:**

**Step 1 — Application of Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):**
The welding power source applies an OCV of **50–100 V** across the electrode-workpiece gap. The gap initially contains **non-ionized air** which acts as an insulator.

**Step 2 — Cathode Electron Emission (Two Mechanisms):**

**(i) Thermionic Emission:**
When the electrode contacts the workpiece (in scratch start), a small **hot spot** forms. Electrons in the metal gain thermal energy exceeding the **work function (Φ)** and are emitted from the cathode surface.

$$
j_{thermionic} = A \cdot T^2 \cdot e^{-\Phi / kT}
$$

Where $A$ is Richardson's constant, $T$ is temperature, $\Phi$ is work function, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant.

**(ii) Field Emission:**
The high electric field at the sharp cathode tip (**~10⁹ V/m**) causes electrons to be extracted by **quantum tunneling** through the surface potential barrier, even at relatively low temperatures. The combined effect (Schottky effect) makes field emission dominant in **TIG welding** with sharp tungsten electrodes.

**Step 3 — Ionization Avalanche:**
The emitted seed electrons are accelerated by the electric field toward the anode. They collide with neutral shielding gas atoms, causing **ionization** when their kinetic energy exceeds the **ionization energy** of the gas. Each ionization event produces **two electrons**, leading to a **Townsend Avalanche**.

**Step 4 — Plasma Formation and Arc Stabilization:**
The gas in the gap becomes a **conductive plasma** (mixture of ions, electrons, and neutrals). The voltage drops from OCV to **arc voltage (15–30 V)**. The arc is now **self-sustaining** as long as:
- Current flow continues
- Arc length is maintained within critical limits
- Shielding gas protects the molten pool

**Step 5 — Heat Transfer:**
Heat is transferred to the workpiece by:
- **Conduction** (through anode spot)
- **Convection** (plasma jet impingement)
- **Radiation** (from arc plasma)

---

### Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):

**Given:**
- $a = 16$ V, $b = 3$ V/mm, $c = 30$ V·A, $d = 6$ V·A/mm
- $l = 3$ mm, $I = 250$ A, $v = 6$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$

**(i) Arc Voltage:**

$$
V_{arc} = a + b \cdot l + \frac{c + d \cdot l}{I}
$$

$$
V_{arc} = 16 + (3 \times 3) + \frac{30 + (6 \times 3)}{250}
$$

$$
V_{arc} = 16 + 9 + \frac{48}{250}
$$

$$
V_{arc} = 16 + 9 + 0.192
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{arc} = 25.192 \text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Arc Power:**

$$
P_{arc} = V_{arc} \times I
$$

$$
P_{arc} = 25.192 \times 250
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{arc} = 6298 \text{ W} \approx 6.3 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(iii) Heat Input per Unit Length:**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V_{arc} \cdot I}{v}
$$

$$
H = \frac{0.80 \times 25.192 \times 250}{6}
$$

$$
H = \frac{5038.4}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 839.73 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCPET402]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the **voltage-current characteristic of an electric arc** used in welding. Discuss the significance of the constant current and constant voltage power sources. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **arc blow phenomenon** in DC arc welding. State the methods to control it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):

**Voltage-Current (V-I) Characteristic:**

The welding arc exhibits a **non-linear, falling V-I characteristic** in its normal operating range. As current increases, the arc voltage **decreases** — this is the **negative resistance** behavior of the plasma column. The complete curve has three regions:

**Region 1 (Low Current, < 50 A):**
- The curve has a **positive slope** — voltage rises with current.
- The arc is **unstable** and easily extinguishes.
- This region corresponds to **short-circuit transfer** in GMAW.

**Region 2 (Normal Operating, 50–500 A):**
- The curve has a **negative slope** — voltage decreases as current increases.
- The arc is **stable** and used for most welding operations.
- This is the **globular, spray, and pulsed transfer** region.

**Region 3 (High Current, > 500 A):**
- The curve becomes **positive** again.
- Used in **submerged arc welding** and **electroslag welding**.

**Mathematical Representation — Ayrton-Hershey Equation:**

$$
V_{arc} = a + bl + \frac{c + dl}{I}
$$

**Significance of Power Source Types:**

| Power Source Type | Characteristic | V-I Behavior | Application |
|-------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Constant Current (CC)** | Steep drooping V-I curve | Voltage drops sharply with arc length change | SMAW, GTAW (manual) |
| **Constant Voltage (CV)** | Flat V-I curve | Current adjusts to maintain voltage | GMAW, FCAW (mechanized) |

**Why it matters:**
- In **CC mode**, the power source maintains **stable current** even if arc length varies — ideal for **manual welding** where the operator cannot hold a perfectly constant gap.
- In **CV mode**, the power source maintains **constant voltage** — current automatically adjusts, providing **self-adjusting arc** for **mechanized high-speed welding**.

---

### Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):

**Arc Blow Phenomenon:**

**Arc blow** is the **unwanted deflection of the welding arc** from its intended path caused by **uneven magnetic fields** around the arc, particularly in **DC welding** of ferromagnetic materials (steel) and near the **end of a weld** or **near edges**.

**Causes:**

1. **Magnetic Field Interaction:** The welding current creates a **magnetic field** around the conductor. If this field is **uneven** (due to asymmetry in the joint or workpiece geometry), it exerts a **Lorentz force** on the ionized arc plasma, deflecting it.

$$
F = I \cdot (L \times B)
$$

2. **Residual Magnetism:** Ferromagnetic workpieces may have **residual magnetization** from prior operations (e.g., magnetic particle inspection), which distorts the arc.

3. **Earth Return Path:** When the **earth cable is on one side** of the workpiece, the return current creates an asymmetric magnetic field.

4. **End of Weld Effect:** As the arc approaches the **end of the workpiece**, the magnetic field becomes **asymmetric** (more metal on one side), causing the arc to blow back toward the weld pool.

**Effects of Arc Blow:**
- **Porosity** and **incomplete fusion** due to wandering arc
- **Spatter** and **irregular bead**
- **Undercut** and **poor penetration**
- **Difficulty in striking and maintaining the arc**

**Control Methods:**

1. **Use AC welding** — the alternating current periodically reverses, **canceling the magnetic field** effects.
2. **Shorten the arc length** — reduces the magnetic field effect.
3. **Move the earth connection** closer to the arc, **symmetrically** placed.
4. **Reduce welding current** to lower the magnetic field strength.
5. **Use a "run-off tab"** at the end of the weld to provide a symmetric magnetic path.
6. **Degauss the workpiece** (demagnetize) before welding.
7. **Use backstep welding technique** — strike the arc ahead and weld backward.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Heat input $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ requires $v$ in **mm/s** for $H$ in **J/mm**. Mixing up units (e.g., using m/min instead of mm/s) leads to **wrong answer and 0 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the three-region breakdown of the arc:** Many students write only "the arc has three regions" without specifying **voltage drops** and **physical phenomena**. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this vague answer.
> 
> 3. **Confusing thermionic and field emission:** Thermionic emission requires **heat**; field emission requires **high electric field**. Students often interchange them. Remember: **Thermionic = Heat-based, Field = Voltage-based**.
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) in arc production definition:** KTU examiners expect OCV in the answer. If you only say "arc is an electric discharge," you lose **1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect arc blow direction explanation:** Arc blow is a **Lorentz force effect**, not "electric field effect." Writing "electric field deflects the arc" loses **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Arc Production (Module 2)**

- **Electric Arc Definition:** A self-sustaining electrical discharge through ionized gas with **low voltage (15–30 V)** and **high current (50–500 A)** at **6000–20000 °C** core temperature.

- **Three Arc Regions:** Cathode drop (8–12 V) → Positive column (~0.5 V/mm) → Anode drop (2–10 V).

- **Arc Initiation Methods:** Scratch, Lift, High-Frequency, Pilot Arc — each with distinct mechanisms for cathode electron emission.

- **Electron Emission Mechanisms:**
  - **Thermionic:** Heat-driven; dominant in **consumable electrode** welding.
  - **Field Emission:** Voltage-driven; dominant in **TIG** with sharp tungsten.

- **Ayrton-Hershey Equation (MUST memorize):**
  $$
  V_{arc} = a + bl + \frac{c + dl}{I}
  $$

- **Heat Input Formula (MUST memorize for numericals):**
  $$
  H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm when } v \text{ is in mm/s]}
  $$

- **V-I Characteristic:** Falling (negative slope) curve in normal operating range; **CC** sources for manual, **CV** sources for mechanized.

- **Arc Efficiency (η):** Typically **0.80–0.85** for most processes; **0.70** for GTAW; up to **0.95** for SAW.

- **Arc Blow:** Magnetic field-induced arc deflection in **DC welding**; controlled by **AC welding, earth placement, degaussing**.

- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Must exceed arc voltage (typically 50–100 V) to allow ionization initiation.

- **Plasma Quasi-Neutrality:** $n_e \approx n_i$ — equal densities of electrons and ions, defining a stable plasma.

- **Ionization Energies to Remember:** Ar = **15.76 eV**, He = **24.59 eV**, Tungsten work function = **4.5 eV**.

- **KTU Exam Tip:** Numerical problems on **heat input** appear in **>80% of past papers**; practice at least 3 problems from the Ayrton-Hershey equation.

- **Common Mistake:** Confusing "arc voltage" (15–30 V, during operation) with "open circuit voltage" (50–100 V, before striking). They are **two different quantities**.

- **Industrial Application Link:** Arc production is the **core enabler** of SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, PAW, and **electroslag welding** — all covered in subsequent modules.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Arc Production | PCPET402 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Arc Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arc Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **electric arc** in the context of manufacturing (specifically arc welding) is defined as a sustained, high-temperature electrical discharge that occurs between two electrodes (or an electrode and the workpiece) when a high current density ionizes the gaseous medium in the gap, producing a self-sustaining plasma column.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET402 – Module 2):**
> The welding arc is a sustained electrical discharge across a gap in a circuit, producing intense heat and light, characterized by specific voltage–current (V–I) relationships, heat distribution zones (anode, cathode, and plasma column), and power densities. The behavior of the arc is governed by its **static characteristics**, **dynamic characteristics**, and **arc stability parameters**.

The **arc characteristics** refer to the measurable and predictable properties of this discharge, including:

1. **Static V–I Characteristics** – Relationship between arc voltage and arc current at a fixed arc length.
2. **Dynamic V–I Characteristics** – Behavior during rapid changes in current or arc length.
3. **Heat Distribution** – Proportion of total heat liberated at the anode, cathode, and plasma column.
4. **Power Density** – Heat liberated per unit area of the arc cross-section (typically **W/mm²**).
5. **Arc Efficiency** – Ratio of heat actually absorbed by the workpiece to the total heat generated.
6. **Arc Stability** – Ability of the arc to maintain a steady state under varying conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **two magnets trying to hold hands across a gap**: the air between them resists connection, but if you push enough electrical "pressure" (voltage), the resistance breaks down and a "spark bridge" (plasma) forms. Once the bridge is created, current flows easily — so much so that you have to *reduce* the pressure (voltage) to control the flow (current).

- **High voltage** is like a high-pressure water hose — it *initiates* the arc (breaks the dielectric strength of air, ~**3 kV/mm**).
- **High current** is the *volume* of water flowing — it determines the *heat* generated ($I^2R$).
- **Arc length** is the *distance* between the hose and the target — longer distance means more voltage drop across the plasma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The plasma column is electrically conductive because the gas molecules are ionized into electrons and positive ions. The temperature of the arc column can reach **5,000 °C to 30,000 °C**, far exceeding the melting point of any metal. This extreme temperature is what makes the arc an ideal tool for fusion welding, cutting, and surfacing.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Welding Arc) |
|---|---|
| Arc Temperature (Core) | **5,000 – 30,000 °C** |
| Voltage (Open Circuit) | **40 – 80 V (GMAW)**, **60 – 90 V (SMAW)** |
| Current Range | **30 – 600 A** |
| Arc Gap (Length) | **1 – 6 mm** |
| Dielectric strength of air | **~3 kV/mm** |
| Plasma conductivity | **~ 25 – 100 S/m** |
| Power density | **10,000 – 100,000 W/mm²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 emphasises the *self-adjusting* and *self-stabilizing* nature of welding arcs and how this affects power source selection (Constant Current vs. Constant Voltage).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of the Welding Arc

The welding arc is a **self-sustaining DC or AC electrical discharge** maintained by thermionic emission at the cathode and thermal ionization in the plasma column. The arc region is divided into three distinct thermal zones:

### 2.1.1 The Three Thermal Zones

| Zone | Location | Heat Generation | Heat Transfer Mechanism | Typical Share of Total Heat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cathode Region** | Negative electrode (electrode tip) | Up to **38%** of total heat | Thermionic emission of electrons + collision | 30 – 38 % |
| **Anode Region** | Positive electrode (workpiece) | Up to **42%** of total heat | Electron impact / bombardment | 40 – 42 % |
| **Plasma Column** | Arc gap (between electrodes) | ~ 20% of total heat | Radiation + convection of ionized gas | ~ 20 % |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Direct Current Electrode Negative (DCEN)** mode, the workpiece is the **anode** and receives more heat than the electrode. In **Direct Current Electrode Positive (DCEP)**, the electrode is the anode and receives more heat — useful for **deep penetration** since the workpiece (cathode) gets concentrated heating at a smaller spot.

### 2.1.2 Arc Initiation (Striking)

The arc is initiated by one of two methods:

1. **Scratch / Strike Method:** Electrode is dragged across the workpiece, momentarily creating a short circuit. When lifted, the high current density ionizes the air gap.
2. **High-Frequency / Pilot Arc Method:** A high-voltage, high-frequency spark (typically **2000 – 3000 V at 100 – 200 kHz**) breaks down the air gap without contact (used in **TIG / GTAW** and **Plasma welding**).

## 2.2 Arc Initiation Parameters

For arc initiation, three conditions must be met:

$$
V_{\text{open}} \geq V_{\text{breakdown}} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{electrode}} \geq T_{\text{emission}} \quad ; \quad I_{\text{arc}} \geq I_{\text{min}}
$$

- $V_{\text{open}}$ — Open-circuit voltage of the power source.
- $V_{\text{breakdown}}$ — Dielectric breakdown voltage of the gap.
- $T_{\text{emission}}$ — Minimum temperature for thermionic emission (typically **2400 K for tungsten**).
- $I_{\text{min}}$ — Minimum current to sustain ionization (typically **5 – 10 A**).

## 2.3 Voltage–Current (V–I) Relationship — Ayrton's Equation

The most fundamental equation governing the welding arc was proposed by **Hertha Ayrton (1902)**. It expresses the static arc voltage as a function of current and arc length:

$$
V_{\text{arc}} = a + b L + \frac{c + d L}{I_{\text{arc}}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{arc}}$ = Arc voltage in **Volts**
- $I_{\text{arc}}$ = Arc current in **Amperes**
- $L$ = Arc length in **mm**
- $a, b, c, d$ = Empirical constants (depend on electrode, gas, and material)

For most practical welding conditions, the simplified form is used:

$$
V_{\text{arc}} = a + bL + \frac{c}{I_{\text{arc}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - $a$ — Cathode and anode fall voltage (constant, ~10 – 20 V).
> - $bL$ — Voltage drop across the plasma column (proportional to length).
> - $c/I$ — Inverse current term (decreases as current rises — explains the **drooping** V–I curve).

## 2.4 Static V–I Characteristic Curves

The static V–I characteristic shows how arc voltage changes with current for a **fixed arc length**. The shape depends on the arc stabilization mechanism:

### 2.4.1 Self-Stabilizing Arc (Flat / Rising Characteristic)
- **Voltage rises** with current (positive slope).
- The arc has a natural tendency to maintain constant energy.
- Used in **TIG (GTAW)** with high-frequency stabilization.
- If current increases → voltage increases → power input is damped → arc self-corrects.

### 2.4.2 Self-Adjusting Arc (Drooping Characteristic)
- **Voltage drops** as current increases (negative slope).
- The arc is naturally unstable — small current increases cause large power surges.
- Requires a **Constant Current (CC)** power source (e.g., transformer-rectifier, generator).
- Common in **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** and **TIG**.

### 2.4.3 Flat Characteristic
- **Voltage remains nearly constant** with changing current.
- Requires a **Constant Voltage (CV)** power source.
- Common in **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** and **FCAW** — the wire feed rate becomes the current control variable.

## 2.5 Dynamic V–I Characteristics

The **dynamic characteristic** describes the transient behavior of the arc voltage when current changes rapidly (such as during short-circuit transfer in GMAW or metal transfer in pulsed MIG).

For a sinusoidal current $I(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{\text{arc}}(t) = a + bL + \frac{c}{I_0 \sin(\omega t)}
$$

The dynamic curve lies **above** the static curve during current rise and **below** it during current fall, forming a hysteresis loop. This is critical for understanding metal transfer modes.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = a + bL + c/I$ | **Ayrton's equation** — arc voltage as a function of current and length. |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | **Arc Power (Watts)** — total heat liberation rate. |
| 3 | $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ | **Heat input to workpiece (Joules)** — $t$ = time in seconds, $\eta$ = arc efficiency. |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | **Heat input per unit length (J/mm)** — $v$ = travel speed in mm/s. |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_{\text{arc}}}$ | **Power density (W/mm²)** — $A_{\text{arc}}$ = arc cross-sectional area. |
| 6 | $R_{\text{arc}} = V/I$ | **Arc resistance** (dynamic). |
| 7 | $V_{\text{anode}} + V_{\text{cathode}} + V_{\text{column}} = V_{\text{arc}}$ | **Voltage partition** across arc regions. |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{\text{workpiece}}}{H_{\text{arc}}}$ | **Arc Efficiency** (typically 0.6 – 0.9). |
| 9 | $f_{\text{arc}} = 50\text{ Hz}$ (AC) | **AC arc re-ignition frequency** — must re-strike every half cycle. |
| 10 | $V_{\text{re-ignition}} \approx 1.5 \cdot V_{\text{sustain}}$ | **Re-ignition voltage** for AC arcs. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat input per unit length ($E$)** is the most important parameter for the designer — it directly controls weld bead width, depth of fusion, and HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) size. Most welding codes (AWS, ASME) limit $E$ to avoid excessive distortion or metallurgical damage.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding arc characteristics is essential for:

1. **Power Source Design:** Choosing CC vs. CV based on process.
2. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):** Setting voltage, current, and travel speed.
3. **Automation:** Seam tracking and adaptive control use dynamic V–I feedback.
4. **Defect Prevention:** Voltage drop indicates loss of shielding gas or arc blow.
5. **Process Selection:** Pulsed MIG uses the rising portion of the V–I curve to control droplet transfer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input to Workpiece

### Step 1: Define Total Heat Generated in the Arc

The arc converts electrical energy into thermal energy at a rate equal to the arc power:

$$
P = V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}}
$$

This is the total rate of heat liberation (in Watts) within the arc column and electrodes.

### Step 2: Apply the Arc Efficiency Factor

Not all the heat reaches the workpiece. Some heat is lost to the electrode, radiation, convection, and spatter. Define the **arc efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Heat absorbed by workpiece}}{\text{Total heat generated}}
$$

Typical values:
- **SMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.75$ – $0.85$
- **GMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.70$ – $0.85$
- **GTAW:** $\eta \approx 0.40$ – $0.60$ (very efficient but lower heat concentration)

### Step 3: Heat Absorbed Over Time

For a welding operation lasting time $t$ seconds:

$$
H_{\text{workpiece}} = \eta \cdot P \cdot t = \eta \cdot V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}} \cdot t
$$

The result is in **Joules (J)**.

### Step 4: Convert to Heat Input per Unit Length

In practice, welds are characterized by **heat input per unit length** (J/mm), which is independent of bead length. With travel speed $v$ in mm/s:

$$
E = \frac{H_{\text{workpiece}}}{L_{\text{weld}}} = \frac{\eta \cdot V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}} \cdot t}{v \cdot t}
$$

Cancelling $t$:

$$
E = \frac{\eta \cdot V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}}}{v}
$$

This is the most widely used parameter in welding codes (e.g., AWS D1.1 limits $E \leq 2.0$ kJ/mm for many carbon steel welds).

## 3.2 Derivation of Arc Resistance from V–I Curve

### Step 1: Apply Ohm's Law (Apparent Form)

For the arc as an element:

$$
R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{V_{\text{arc}}}{I_{\text{arc}}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Ayrton's Equation

$$
R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{a + bL + c/I_{\text{arc}}}{I_{\text{arc}}} = \frac{a}{I_{\text{arc}}} + \frac{bL}{I_{\text{arc}}} + \frac{c}{I_{\text{arc}}^2}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

- $a/I_{\text{arc}}$ — Electrode fall resistance (dominant at low current).
- $bL/I_{\text{arc}}$ — Plasma column resistance (scales with arc length).
- $c/I_{\text{arc}}^2$ — Variable ionization term (dominant at very low current).

As $I_{\text{arc}} \to \infty$, the arc behaves as a near-constant resistance element with $R_{\text{arc}} \to 0$ asymptotically.

## 3.3 Partition of Heat: Anode, Cathode, Column

The total arc voltage is the sum of voltage drops:

$$
V_{\text{arc}} = V_{\text{cathode}} + V_{\text{column}} + V_{\text{anode}}
$$

Using the **Ayrton constants**, the cathode fall is approximately equal to $a$ (the constant term, ~10 – 20 V), the column drop is $bL$ (linear in length, ~ 0.5 – 2 V/mm), and the anode fall is often grouped with the cathode fall.

### Heat Generated in Each Region

Assuming the arc behaves as a near-constant-resistance plasma:

$$
H_{\text{cathode}} = \eta_{\text{cathode}} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

$$
H_{\text{anode}} = \eta_{\text{anode}} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

$$
H_{\text{column}} = (1 - \eta_{\text{cathode}} - \eta_{\text{anode}}) \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

For a steel electrode in argon:
- $\eta_{\text{cathode}} \approx 0.36$
- $\eta_{\text{anode}} \approx 0.42$
- $\eta_{\text{column}} \approx 0.22$

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Heat Input Calculation

**Problem:** A GMAW weld is made on a 6 mm mild steel plate using the following parameters:
- $V_{\text{arc}} = 24$ V
- $I_{\text{arc}} = 200$ A
- Travel speed $v = 5$ mm/s
- Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

**Find:** (a) Heat input per unit length, (b) Total heat absorbed by the workpiece in a 30 cm weld.

### Solution

**(a) Heat input per unit length:**

$$
E = \frac{\eta \cdot V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}}}{v}
$$

$$
E = \frac{0.80 \times 24 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ A}}{5 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{3840 \text{ W}}{5 \text{ mm/s}} = 768 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**(b) Total heat absorbed for 300 mm weld:**

$$
H_{\text{workpiece}} = E \times L_{\text{weld}} = 768 \text{ J/mm} \times 300 \text{ mm} = 230{,}400 \text{ J} = 230.4 \text{ kJ}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative V–I Behavior

| Process | Open-Circuit Voltage | Arc Voltage | Current Range (A) | Power Source | V–I Slope |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** | 60 – 90 V | 20 – 30 V | 30 – 400 | Constant Current | Drooping |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | 60 – 80 V | 10 – 25 V | 10 – 400 | Constant Current | Drooping / Flat |
| **GMAW (MIG)** | 18 – 40 V | 18 – 32 V | 50 – 600 | Constant Voltage | Flat |
| **FCAW** | 24 – 50 V | 22 – 38 V | 100 – 600 | Constant Voltage | Flat |
| **SAW** | 30 – 50 V | 25 – 35 V | 200 – 1000 | Constant Current / Voltage | Drooping / Flat |
| **PAW (Plasma)** | 80 – 120 V | 25 – 40 V | 50 – 400 | Constant Current | Drooping |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Welding Arc Block Architecture — Power Source Interaction

The following block diagram illustrates how a welding power source interacts with the arc to maintain stable operation. The arc is treated as a variable-resistance element whose V–I behavior determines the feedback to the power source controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Three Phase AC Mains Supply] --> A2[Step Down Transformer]
    A2 --> A3[Rectifier Bridge]
    A3 --> A4[DC Link Filter Inductor]
    A4 --> A5[Inverter Switching Stage PWM Control]
    A5 --> A6[Output Terminals Positive and Negative]
    A6 --> A7[Welding Arc Gap Plasma Column]
    A7 --> A8[Workpiece Anode Region]
    A7 --> A9[Electrode Cathode Region]
    A8 --> A10[Arc Voltage Feedback Sensor]
    A9 --> A11[Arc Current Feedback Sensor]
    A10 --> A12[Controller Microprocessor Based]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13{Power Source Type}
    A13 --> A14[Constant Current Mode]
    A13 --> A15[Constant Voltage Mode]
    A14 --> A16[Adjusts Voltage to Hold Current]
    A15 --> A17[Adjusts Current to Hold Voltage]
    A16 --> A5
    A17 --> A5
    A8 --> A18[Heat Transfer to Base Metal]
    A9 --> A19[Heat Transfer to Electrode]
    A18 --> A20[Welded Joint Formation]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Arc Striking and Stabilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Open Circuit Voltage Applied] --> B2[High Frequency or Scratch Initiator]
    B2 --> B3[Air Gap Ionization Plasma Formation]
    B3 --> B4{Arc Established}
    B4 --> B5[Current Rises to Setpoint]
    B5 --> B6[Arc Stabilizes at Equilibrium]
    B6 --> B7[Heat Generated in Column]
    B7 --> B8[Heat Transfer Anode Cathode]
    B8 --> B9[Metal Melting and Fusion]
    B9 --> B10[Welded Pool Solidifies]
    B10 --> B11{Arc Length Drift Detected}
    B11 --> B12[Increase Length Higher Voltage]
    B11 --> B13[Decrease Length Lower Voltage]
    B12 --> B14[Feedback to Power Source]
    B13 --> B14
    B14 --> B6
```

## 4.3 Static V–I Characteristic Family (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Arc Length L fixed parameter] --> C2[Self Stabilizing Arc]
    C1 --> C3[Self Adjusting Drooping Arc]
    C1 --> C4[Flat Characteristic Arc]
    C2 --> C5[Voltage Rises with Current Used in TIG with High Frequency]
    C3 --> C6[Voltage Drops with Current Used in SMAW with Constant Current Source]
    C4 --> C7[Voltage Nearly Constant Used in GMAW with Constant Voltage Source]
    C5 --> C8[Operating Point Intersection with Power Source Curve]
    C6 --> C8
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9[Stable Welding Operation]
```

## 4.4 Voltage Partition Across Arc Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Total Arc Voltage Varc] --> D2[VCathode Fall 10 to 20 V]
    D1 --> D3[VColumn Drop b times L 0.5 to 2 V per mm]
    D1 --> D4[VAnode Fall 5 to 10 V]
    D2 --> D5[Heat to Electrode 30 to 38 percent]
    D3 --> D6[Heat Radiated in Plasma 20 percent]
    D4 --> D7[Heat to Workpiece 40 to 42 percent]
    D5 --> D8[Total Energy Balance]
    D6 --> D8
    D7 --> D8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define welding arc. List the three thermal zones of an arc with their approximate heat distribution percentages.**

**Model Answer:**

A welding arc is a sustained, high-temperature electrical discharge through an ionized gas (plasma) between two electrodes or between an electrode and the workpiece, used as a source of heat for fusion welding.

The three thermal zones and their heat share are:

| Zone | Heat Share |
|---|---|
| Cathode region | **30 – 38 %** |
| Anode region (workpiece) | **40 – 42 %** |
| Plasma column | **~ 20 %** |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Ayrton's equation for arc voltage. What do the constants represent?**

**Model Answer:**

Ayrton's equation expresses arc voltage as a function of arc current and arc length:

$$
V_{\text{arc}} = a + bL + \frac{c}{I_{\text{arc}}}
$$

- $a$ = Cathode and anode fall voltage (constant, ~ 10 – 20 V).
- $b$ = Voltage gradient per unit arc length in plasma column (~ 0.5 – 2 V/mm).
- $c$ = Constant representing non-linear plasma behavior at low current.
- $L$ = Arc length in mm; $I_{\text{arc}}$ = Current in A.

**(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **static V–I characteristics** of a welding arc with neat sketches. Compare the **self-stabilizing, self-adjusting, and flat** characteristic curves. Discuss the role of each in selecting a power source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GMAW welding operation is carried out with the following parameters: Voltage = 28 V, Current = 250 A, Travel speed = 6 mm/s, Arc efficiency = 0.82. Calculate: (i) Heat input per unit length of weld, (ii) Total heat input for a 500 mm long weld, (iii) Power density if the arc cross-section is approximately circular with 4 mm diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Static V–I Characteristics:**

The static V–I characteristic is a plot of arc voltage versus arc current at a **fixed arc length**. It indicates the inherent stability and behavior of the arc for a given power source.

**Type 1 — Drooping (Self-Adjusting) Characteristic:**
- Voltage **decreases** as current increases.
- The slope $dV/dI < 0$.
- The arc has a natural tendency to draw uncontrolled current if voltage is held constant.
- **Power source required:** Constant Current (CC) — a small change in voltage produces a small change in current, providing stability.
- **Processes using this:** SMAW, GTAW, SAW, PAW.

**Type 2 — Flat (Rising) Characteristic:**
- Voltage remains nearly **constant** (or slightly rises) with current.
- Slope $dV/dI \approx 0$ (or positive).
- The arc requires a **Constant Voltage (CV)** power source to be stable.
- **Processes using this:** GMAW (MIG/MAG), FCAW.

**Type 3 — Self-Stabilizing Characteristic:**
- Voltage **rises** with current (positive slope $dV/dI > 0$).
- The arc naturally regulates itself — increased current → increased voltage → increased arc length (and vice versa).
- **Processes using this:** TIG with high-frequency stabilization, Plasma welding.

> **Why power source matters:** The operating point of the arc is the intersection of the arc V–I curve and the power source V–I curve. For stability, the curves should intersect at a steep angle (large slope difference). Drooping source + Drooping arc is **unstable** (they run parallel). Drooping source + Flat or Rising arc is **stable**.

**(7 marks — 3 for diagrams, 2 for explanation, 2 for power source link)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 28$ V
- $I = 250$ A
- $v = 6$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.82$
- Arc diameter $d = 4$ mm
- Weld length $L_{\text{weld}} = 500$ mm

**(i) Heat input per unit length:**

$$
E = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} = \frac{0.82 \times 28 \times 250}{6}
$$

$$
E = \frac{5740}{6} = 956.67 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**(ii) Total heat input for 500 mm weld:**

$$
H = E \times L_{\text{weld}} = 956.67 \times 500 = 478{,}333.3 \text{ J} \approx 478.3 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(iii) Power density:**

Arc area:

$$
A_{\text{arc}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4^2}{4} = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Arc power:

$$
P = V \cdot I = 28 \times 250 = 7000 \text{ W}
$$

Power density:

$$
q = \frac{P}{A_{\text{arc}}} = \frac{7000}{12.566} \approx 557 \text{ W/mm}^2
$$

**(7 marks — 2 for each sub-part, 1 for final summary)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of Ayrton's equation, derive an expression for **arc resistance** and explain how it varies with arc current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **heat distribution** in a welding arc between the cathode, anode, and plasma column. Discuss how this affects **weld penetration** in DCEN and DCEP polarity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Derivation of Arc Resistance:**

**Step 1 — Ohm's Law:**
The arc resistance is defined as:

$$
R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{V_{\text{arc}}}{I_{\text{arc}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Ayrton's equation:**

$$
R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{a + bL + c/I}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand:**

$$
R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{a}{I} + \frac{bL}{I} + \frac{c}{I^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Behaviour at limits:**

- As $I \to \infty$, $R_{\text{arc}} \to 0$ (the arc becomes a near-perfect conductor).
- As $I \to I_{\text{min}}$ (low current), $R_{\text{arc}} \to \infty$ (the arc extinguishes).

**Step 5 — Graphical interpretation:**

The $R_{\text{arc}}$ versus $I$ curve is hyperbolic — sharply falling at low current and asymptotic at high current. This explains why **arcs are easier to maintain at high current** and why **striking requires higher voltage** (to overcome the high initial resistance of the cold gas gap).

**(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for limits, 2 for graphical interpretation)**

---

**(b) Heat Distribution and Polarity Effect:**

**Heat generated in each region:**

| Region | Mechanism | % of Total Heat |
|---|---|---|
| Anode | Electron bombardment + condensation | **42 %** |
| Cathode | Thermionic emission + ion impact | **38 %** |
| Plasma column | Radiation + ionization | **20 %** |

**Effect of Polarity:**

**DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative) — Workpiece = Anode:**
- The workpiece receives the **higher heat** (~ 42 %) at a concentrated point.
- The electron stream is **focused** due to magnetic pinch effect, producing **deep, narrow penetration**.
- **Applications:** TIG welding of stainless steel, aluminium — where deep fusion is needed.
- Heat balance: Electrode (cathode) stays cooler — useful when using **tungsten** (low melting point risk).

**DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive) — Workpiece = Cathode:**
- The electrode receives **42 %** of the heat, the workpiece only ~ 38 %.
- Heat at workpiece is **dispersed** over a larger area, producing **shallow, wide penetration**.
- **Applications:** SMAW (manual stick) — to prevent electrode overheating and improve filler metal transfer.
- The workpiece cathode is heated by **positive ion bombardment**, which is more diffuse.

**Penetration Rule of Thumb:**

$$
\text{DCEN penetration} \approx 1.5 \times \text{DCEP penetration}
$$

(roughly, for the same current and arc length)

**(7 marks — 2 for heat table, 2 for DCEN, 2 for DCEP, 1 for comparison rule)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors in Heat Input (E):** Forgetting to convert travel speed from **mm/min to mm/s** (divide by 60) when the speed is given in mm/min. This is the single most common mistake and costs **2–3 marks** per question.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency definitions:** $\eta$ is the **arc efficiency**, not the **power source efficiency**. The arc efficiency accounts for radiation, convection, and electrode losses — the power source efficiency is typically 0.6 – 0.9 also but they are NOT the same parameter.
> 3. **Forgetting arc length dependence:** The Ayrton equation has $bL$ as the **column voltage**. Students often forget this term, leading to wrong values when arc length is given.
> 4. **Polarity confusion:** "DCEN" means **Electrode is Negative** (workpiece is anode, gets more heat). Many students reverse this in a hurry.
> 5. **Power density without arc area:** If the arc diameter or area is not given, the question may be implying a **standard assumption** — do not leave this part blank; state the assumption clearly (e.g., "assuming circular cross-section").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Arc Characteristics:**

- ⚡ **Welding arc = self-sustaining plasma discharge** with temperatures of **5,000 – 30,000 °C**.
- ⚡ **Three zones** — Cathode (~38 %), Plasma column (~20 %), Anode (~42 %).
- ⚡ **Ayrton's equation** — $V = a + bL + c/I$ — fundamental relation for static V–I.
- ⚡ **Static V–I types** — Drooping (SMAW/TIG with CC source), Flat (GMAW with CV source), Rising (Plasma/TIG self-stabilized).
- ⚡ **Dynamic V–I** — Shows hysteresis during rapid current changes; critical for short-circuit transfer and pulsed MIG.
- ⚡ **Heat input per unit length** — $E = \eta V I / v$ — the most important design parameter; limited by AWS codes (typically $\leq 2.0$ kJ/mm for carbon steel).
- ⚡ **Arc efficiency $\eta$** — 0.4 (TIG) to 0.85 (SMAW) — represents the fraction of arc heat reaching the workpiece.
- ⚡ **DCEN = deep penetration** (workpiece = anode, gets 42 % heat). **DCEP = shallow penetration** (electrode gets 42 % heat).
- ⚡ **Power density** — $q = P / A_{\text{arc}}$ — typically 10⁴ to 10⁵ W/mm², far higher than oxy-fuel flames.
- ⚡ **Arc resistance** — Hyperbolic fall with current; $R \to 0$ at high current, $R \to \infty$ at low current.
- ⚡ **AC arc** — Re-ignition every half cycle (~ 50 Hz); re-ignition voltage is ~ 1.5× the sustain voltage.
- ⚡ **Power source selection rule** — Intersection of arc V–I curve and source V–I curve determines operating point; maximum angle between curves = maximum stability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metal Transfer** is the fundamental physical phenomenon in **arc welding** (particularly in **Gas Metal Arc Welding - GMAW/MIG/MAG** and **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding - GTAW/TIG**) wherein molten metal droplets are detached from the tip of the consumable electrode (or filler wire) and propelled across the electric arc gap into the molten weld pool formed on the parent metal (workpiece). The mode, frequency, and size of droplet detachment critically govern weld bead geometry, penetration profile, spatter intensity, arc stability, and overall joint integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Metal transfer is a core topic under **Arc Welding Processes** in Module 2. Students must be able to classify the modes of transfer, identify the controlling welding parameters for each mode, and explain the influence of shielding gas composition and current type (DC vs AC, Constant Current vs Pulsed Current) on the transfer mode.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **water tap (faucet)** over a sink. As you slowly open the tap, water drips out in **large, irregular blobs** (this is **Globular Transfer**). If you open the tap further, water flows in a **continuous, smooth, fine stream** (this is **Spray Transfer**). Now imagine you are using a **medical syringe** filled with water — you push the plunger gently, the water tip touches the ground (a "short circuit"), then retracts — this is **Short-Circuit / Dip Transfer**.

In arc welding, the "tap" is the melting electrode tip, the "water" is the molten metal, and the "sink" is the weld pool. The flow rate is controlled by **welding current**, **voltage**, **shielding gas**, and **wire feed speed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted:** The **Transition Current** is the threshold current value above which **Globular Transfer** changes to **Spray Transfer**. For a **1.6 mm mild steel electrode in Argon**, the transition current is approximately **280–300 A**.

### Key Governing Physical Quantities (in Bold):
- **Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Voltage (V)** — measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** — measured in **m/min**
- **Shielding Gas Composition** — typically **Argon (Ar), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Helium (He), or mixtures**
- **Droplet Detachment Frequency** — measured in **droplets/second**
- **Deposition Rate** — measured in **kg/hour**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Metal Transfer Modes

The four principal modes of metal transfer in **GMAW** (sorted by increasing current magnitude) are:

1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (Dip Transfer)**
2. **Globular Transfer**
3. **Spray Transfer** (including Rotating / Projected / Streaming variants)
4. **Pulsed-Spray Transfer**

## 2.2 Mode 1 — Short-Circuit (Dip) Transfer

**Operating Window:**
- Current: **50 – 150 A** (low)
- Voltage: **15 – 22 V** (low)
- Wire Diameter: **0.6 – 1.2 mm**
- Shielding Gas: **CO₂ or Ar + 20–25% CO₂**

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- The wire advances and **physically contacts** the weld pool — this creates a **short circuit**.
- The short-circuit current surges rapidly (because resistance is now almost zero across a shorted arc).
- The intense **resistive (I²R) heating** at the contact point melts the wire, forming a **liquid metal bridge**.
- The **surface tension** of the molten bridge (combined with **electromagnetic pinch force**) necks down and **shears the bridge** — the droplet detaches.
- The arc reignites, and the cycle repeats at a frequency of **20–200 times per second** (so it sounds like a "buzz-saw" or "frying bacon" noise).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **Low heat input** → ideal for **thin sheets (< 3 mm)**, out-of-position welding (vertical, overhead), and **bridging gaps**.
- **Drawback:** Mild spatter and shallow penetration.

## 2.3 Mode 2 — Globular Transfer

**Operating Window:**
- Current: **150 – 250 A** (medium)
- Voltage: **22 – 28 V**
- Shielding Gas: **Pure CO₂** (or Ar/CO₂ with high CO₂ content)
- Droplet Size: **Larger than electrode diameter** (e.g., 2–3× the wire diameter)

**Mechanism:**
- The wire melts and a **large globule** (often 1.5–2× the wire diameter) hangs from the tip, held by **surface tension** and **gravity**.
- **Gravity** overcomes surface tension at the precise moment the globule detaches.
- The droplet falls irregularly — **not projected**, but **dripped**. Hence the term *globular* (large ball-like drops).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **High deposition rate** with deep penetration.
- **Drawback:** Severe spatter, irregular bead, **limited to flat (1G/1F) and horizontal (2F) positions** because gravity pulls the globule.

## 2.4 Mode 3 — Spray Transfer

**Operating Window:**
- Current: **Above Transition Current** (typically **> 280 A for 1.6 mm wire in Ar**)
- Voltage: **25 – 35 V**
- Shielding Gas: **Argon-rich (≥ 80% Ar)**, often with **2–5% O₂ or 5–25% CO₂**

**Mechanism:**
- Once current crosses the **transition current**, the **electromagnetic pinch force** becomes strong enough to detach the droplet *before* gravity dominates.
- Droplets are **smaller than the wire diameter**, detached in a **coaxial axial spray** (often at frequencies of **> 250 droplets/second**).
- Variants: **Streaming Spray** (axially projected), **Rotating Spray** (droplets orbit the axis), **Projected Spray** (intermediate mode).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **Spatter-free**, smooth, high-quality weld bead.
- Excellent for **thick plates**, high productivity, **flat and horizontal positions only** (high heat input).

## 2.5 Mode 4 — Pulsed-Spray Transfer

**Operating Window:**
- **Background current** (low, like short-circuit level) — sustains the arc.
- **Pulse current** (high, like spray level) — detaches one droplet per pulse.
- Frequency: **50–500 pulses/second**
- Each pulse is engineered to detach **exactly one droplet** (synchronicity rule).

**Mechanism:**
- Combines the **low heat input of short-circuit** with the **spatter-free, controlled spray behaviour** of spray transfer.
- During each pulse, a droplet is detached; during background current, arc is maintained without metal transfer.

**Engineering Utility:**
- **All-position welding** with **spray-quality bead**.
- Used for **aluminium, stainless steel, and thin-section high-quality welds**.

## 2.6 Forces Acting on the Pendant Droplet (The "Why" Behind Detachment)

| Force | Direction | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravity (W)** | Downward | Promotes detachment in globular mode |
| **Surface Tension (σ)** | Upward (resists deformation) | Holds droplet to wire tip |
| **Electromagnetic / Pinch Force (Fₑ)** | Axial, downward | Squeezes the droplet neck → primary cause of detachment in spray mode |
| **Plasma Jet / Aerodynamic Drag (Fₚ)** | Downward | Strikes the droplet, accelerating it across the arc |
| **Lorentz Force / Marangoni Flow** | Variable | Affects droplet trajectory and weld pool convection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Rule:** Droplet detaches when **Fₑ + Fₚ + W > σ** (downward forces overcome the upward surface-tension retaining force).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **Heat Input** per unit length | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $R_{d} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ | **Deposition Rate** from wire feed speed | kg/hr |
| 3 | $F_{e} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot I^{2}}{4\pi} \left[ \ln \dfrac{r_{tip}}{r_{neck}} \right]$ | **Electromagnetic Pinch Force** (Ampere's force law) | N |
| 4 | $f_{d} = \dfrac{v_{wfs}}{L_{d}}$ | **Droplet Detachment Frequency** ≈ WFS / droplet length | droplets/s |
| 5 | $I_{t} \approx K \cdot d^{n}$ | **Transition Current** (K, n are gas and material constants) | A |
| 6 | $P_{d} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$ | **Power deposited** in arc (η ≈ 0.7–0.85) | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{consumed}} \times 100\%$ | **Metal Deposition Efficiency** | % |

**Where:**
- $V$ = Arc Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $S$ = Travel Speed (mm/min)
- $d$ = Wire diameter (m), $v$ = Wire feed speed (m/s), $\rho$ = Wire density (kg/m³)
- $r_{tip}$, $r_{neck}$ = Radii of electrode tip and droplet neck (m)
- $L_{d}$ = Length of droplet (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by welding engineers to **qualify WPS (Welding Procedure Specifications)** as per **ASME Section IX** and **ISO 15614**, ensuring weld quality in pressure vessels, pipelines (API 1104), and structural fabrications (IS 800 / AWS D1.1).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deposition Rate from Wire Feed Speed

**Problem Setup:** A mild-steel welding wire of diameter $d = 1.2$ mm is fed at a wire feed speed of $v = 5$ m/min. Density of steel $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the theoretical **Deposition Rate** in kg/hour.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the wire:**

The wire is cylindrical, so its cross-sectional area is:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

Substituting $d = 1.2 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{4}$$

$$A = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of wire consumed per second:**

Volume per second = Cross-sectional area × Wire feed speed (converted to m/s):

$$v_{sec} = \dfrac{5 \text{ m/min}}{60} = 0.0833 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{V} = A \cdot v_{sec} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0833 = 9.425 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate (kg/s):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 7850 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\dot{m} = 7.398 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{hr} = 7.398 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{hr} \approx 2.66 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For a 1.2 mm mild steel wire fed at 5 m/min, the theoretical maximum deposition rate is about **2.66 kg/hr**, assuming 100% transfer efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A GMAW weld is made with $V = 28$ V, $I = 220$ A, and travel speed $S = 350$ mm/min. Compute the **Heat Input** in kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — Write the heat input formula:**

$$H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$H = \dfrac{28 \cdot 220 \cdot 60}{350 \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator:**

$$28 \times 220 = 6160$$

$$6160 \times 60 = 369{,}600$$

**Step 4 — Simplify denominator:**

$$350 \times 1000 = 350{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$H = \dfrac{369{,}600}{350{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 1.056 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A heat input of ~1.05 kJ/mm is in the **medium range** — suitable for structural steel welding without excessive HAZ softening or distortion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Droplet Frequency of Transfer

**Problem Setup:** For a wire of diameter 1.0 mm, WFS = 4 m/min. Each droplet length $L_d$ equals one wire diameter. Find the **droplet detachment frequency**.

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$f_{d} = \dfrac{v_{wfs}}{L_{d}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert WFS to mm/s:**

$$v_{wfs} = \dfrac{4 \text{ m/min} \times 1000 \text{ mm/m}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 66.67 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Determine droplet length:**

Assuming $L_d = d = 1.0$ mm.

**Step 4 — Compute frequency:**

$$f_{d} = \dfrac{66.67 \text{ mm/s}}{1.0 \text{ mm}} = 66.67 \text{ droplets/s}$$

$$\boxed{f_{d} \approx 66.7 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A detachment frequency of ~67 Hz indicates **Globular Transfer** (typically 20–150 Hz). Spray transfer would require $f_d > 250$ Hz.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Transfer Modes

| Parameter | Short-Circuit | Globular | Spray | Pulsed-Spray |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Range (A)** | 50 – 150 | 150 – 250 | 250 – 500+ | 50 – 250 (avg) |
| **Voltage (V)** | 15 – 22 | 22 – 28 | 25 – 35 | 18 – 28 |
| **Droplet Size** | ≈ wire dia | 1.5 – 3× wire dia | < wire dia | ≈ wire dia |
| **Detachment Freq. (Hz)** | 20 – 200 | 20 – 150 | > 250 | 50 – 500 |
| **Shielding Gas** | CO₂, Ar/CO₂ | CO₂, Ar/CO₂ | Ar-rich (80%+) | Ar/He-rich |
| **Spatter Level** | Moderate | High | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Heat Input** | Low | High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **Welding Positions** | All (1G–4F) | Flat, Horizontal | Flat, Horizontal | All (1G–6G) |
| **Penetration** | Shallow | Deep | Deep | Controlled |
| **Typical Use** | Thin sheet, gap bridging | Thick plate, high deposition | Thick plate, productivity | Aluminium, stainless, thin sections |
| **Sound Signature** | Buzz / sizzle | Pop / hiss | Smooth hiss | Smooth hiss |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Metal Transfer Modes (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ARC WELDING METAL TRANSFER] --> B1[GMAW MIG MAG]
    A0 --> B2[GTAW TIG]
    A0 --> B3[SAW SUBMERGED ARC]
    A0 --> B4[PAW PLASMA ARC]

    B1 --> C1[SHORT CIRCUIT DIP]
    B1 --> C2[GLOBULAR]
    B1 --> C3[SPRAY]
    B1 --> C4[PULSED SPRAY]

    C1 --> D1[LOW CURRENT BELOW 150A]
    C1 --> D2[CO2 SHIELDING]
    C1 --> D3[ALL POSITION]
    C1 --> D4[BUZZ SOUND]

    C2 --> E1[MEDIUM CURRENT 150 TO 250A]
    C2 --> E2[LARGE GLOBULES]
    C2 --> E3[FLAT ONLY]
    C2 --> E4[HIGH SPATTER]

    C3 --> F1[HIGH CURRENT ABOVE 280A]
    C3 --> F2[ARGON RICH GAS]
    C3 --> F3[SMOOTH SPRAY AXIAL]
    C3 --> F4[FLAT AND HORIZONTAL]

    C4 --> G1[PULSED DC CURRENT]
    C4 --> G2[ONE DROP PER PULSE]
    C4 --> G3[ALL POSITION]
    C4 --> G4[LOW HEAT HIGH QUALITY]

    B2 --> H1[TRANSFER OCCURS BY GRAVITY OR MAGNETIC FIELD]
    B3 --> I1[NO VISIBLE ARC TRANSFER OCCURS UNDER FLUX]
    B4 --> J1[KEYHOLE MODE OR CONDUCTION MODE TRANSFER]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Short-Circuit Transfer Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[START OF CYCLE] --> S1[WIRE ADVANCES TOWARDS WELD POOL]
    S1 --> S2[WIRE TOUCHES POOL SHORT CIRCUIT FORMS]
    S2 --> S3[CURRENT SURGES I SQUARED R HEATING]
    S3 --> S4[LIQUID BRIDGE NECKS DOWN]
    S4 --> S5[PINCH AND SURFACE TENSION SHEAR BRIDGE]
    S5 --> S6[DROPLET DETACHES INTO POOL]
    S6 --> S7[ARC REIGNITES]
    S7 --> S1

    S2 -.Arc voltage drops to 0.-> S2A[VOLTAGE MONITOR]
    S7 -.Arc voltage rises back.-> S7A[VOLTAGE MONITOR]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Metal Transfer Physics

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[POWER SOURCE CONSTANT VOLTAGE] --> P2[WIRE FEED DRIVE ROLLER]
    P2 --> P3[CONTACT TIP]
    P3 --> P4[ELECTRODE WIRE MELTING ZONE]
    P4 --> P5[PENDANT DROPLET]

    P5 --> F1[GRAVITY FORCE]
    P5 --> F2[SURFACE TENSION FORCE]
    P5 --> F3[ELECTROMAGNETIC PINCH FORCE]
    P5 --> F4[PLASMA STREAMING FORCE]

    F1 --> F5[NET DETACHMENT FORCE]
    F2 --> F5
    F3 --> F5
    F4 --> F5

    F5 --> P6[DROPLET TRANSFER ACROSS ARC]
    P6 --> P7[WELD POOL ON WORKPIECE]
    P7 --> P8[SOLIDIFIED WELD BEAD]

    P1 --> GAS1[SHIELDING GAS SUPPLY ARGON OR CO2]
    GAS1 --> NOZ[NOZZLE GAS CUP]
    NOZ --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows in Mermaid represent the *direction of influence* or *flow of material/energy*, not physical movement. Each block performs a specific function in the metal transfer chain.
