# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Metal Transfer. List the various modes of metal transfer in GMAW.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Metal transfer is the process by which molten metal droplets are detached from the tip of a consumable electrode and transferred across the arc into the weld pool during arc welding.

The four principal modes of metal transfer in **GMAW** are:
1. **Short-circuit (Dip) Transfer** — low current, low heat input
2. **Globular Transfer** — medium current, large droplets, spatter-prone
3. **Spray Transfer** — high current, spatter-free axial droplet stream
4. **Pulsed-Spray Transfer** — controlled one-droplet-per-pulse mode

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing and briefly stating characteristics)*

---

### Question 2: What is "Transition Current" in GMAW? Mention its significance.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Transition Current** is the threshold value of welding current above which the mode of metal transfer changes from **Globular Transfer** to **Spray Transfer**.

**Significance:**
- It marks the onset of the **stable, spatter-free, axial spray** of fine droplets.
- It is **dependent on** the electrode diameter, material, and shielding gas composition.
- For example, for a **1.6 mm steel wire in pure Argon**, the transition current is approximately **280–300 A**.
- Operating below this current yields large, irregular globules and spatter; operating above yields smooth, fine-droplet spray.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for significance with example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: (a) Explain Short-Circuit Transfer Mode in GMAW with a neat sketch. (7 marks) 
###          (b) Compare Spray Transfer and Pulsed-Spray Transfer modes. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Short-Circuit Transfer

**Definition (1 mark):** Short-circuit transfer is a GMAW mode in which the molten wire physically contacts the weld pool, forming a short circuit that melts the wire, and detaches the droplet by **surface tension + pinch force necking**.

**Operating Parameters (2 marks):**
- Current: 50 – 150 A
- Voltage: 15 – 22 V
- Wire diameter: 0.6 – 1.2 mm
- Shielding gas: CO₂ or Ar + 20–25% CO₂

**Stages of the cycle (4 marks):**
1. **Arc phase** — wire advances, arc length maintained, wire melts at tip.
2. **Short-circuit** — wire dips into weld pool; arc voltage → 0; current surges.
3. **I²R heating** — intense resistive heating at the contact; liquid metal bridge forms.
4. **Pinch-off** — electromagnetic + surface tension shears the bridge; droplet transfers.
5. **Arc reignition** — voltage jumps back; cycle repeats at 20–200 Hz.

**Advantages:** Low heat input, all-position welding, suitable for thin sheets.
**Disadvantages:** Spatter, shallow penetration, not for thick plates.

*(Sketch should show: electrode tip, arc, pendant droplet, short circuit point, weld pool, necking, detachment — drawn as in the Mermaid functional diagram of Section 4.2.)*

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Comparison: Spray vs Pulsed-Spray

| Feature | Spray Transfer | Pulsed-Spray Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| **Current type** | Steady DC | Pulsed DC (background + peak) |
| **Average Heat Input** | High | Low to medium |
| **Spatter** | Very low | Very low |
| **Droplet Control** | Continuous stream | One droplet per pulse |
| **Position capability** | Flat + horizontal | All positions |
| **Deposition Rate** | High | Moderate (controllable) |
| **Equipment cost** | Conventional CV power | Pulsed-wave power source |
| **Best for** | Thick plate, high productivity | Aluminium, stainless, thin sections |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Pulsed-spray is preferred when **all-position capability** and **low heat input** are required; conventional spray is best for **maximum productivity** in flat-position thick-plate welding.

---

### Question B: (a) With neat sketches, explain the various forces acting on a pendant molten droplet during GMAW. (7 marks) 
###          (b) Derive the expression for the deposition rate in GMAW from the wire feed speed. A welding wire of 1.2 mm diameter is fed at 4 m/min. If the density of the wire material is 7850 kg/m³, find the deposition rate. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Forces on Pendant Droplet

A pendant molten droplet at the electrode tip is acted upon by **four principal forces**:

1. **Gravity (W)** — acts downward, magnitude $= m \cdot g = \rho \cdot V_d \cdot g$, promotes detachment.
2. **Surface Tension (σ)** — acts upward, holds droplet to tip via the necked liquid column; opposes detachment.
3. **Electromagnetic Pinch Force (Fₑ)** — caused by current divergence in the droplet; per **Ampère's force law**:
$$F_{e} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot I^{2}}{4\pi} \left[ \ln \dfrac{r_{tip}}{r_{neck}} \right]$$
4. **Plasma Jet / Aerodynamic Drag (Fₚ)** — the high-velocity plasma stream from the arc strikes the droplet and pushes it across.

**Detachment Criterion (1 mark):** Detachment occurs when:
$$F_{e} + F_{p} + W > \sigma$$

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A diagram showing the electrode, arc, pendant droplet, the downward vectors ($W$, $F_e$, $F_p$), and the upward surface-tension vector at the neck region.

**Engineering significance (1 mark):** In **short-circuit mode**, surface tension dominates; in **spray mode**, electromagnetic force dominates.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Deposition Rate Derivation

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let:
- $d$ = wire diameter (m)
- $v$ = wire feed speed (m/s)
- $\rho$ = density of wire material (kg/m³)

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

Volume fed per second:
$$\dot{V} = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v$$

Mass per second:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho$$

Per hour (× 3600):
$$R_{d} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot 3600 \quad \text{(kg/hr)}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $d = 1.2 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $v = 4 \text{ m/min} = \dfrac{4}{60} = 0.0667 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

**Step 1:** $A = \dfrac{\pi (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$

**Step 2:** $\dot{V} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0667 = 7.54 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$

**Step 3:** $\dot{m} = 7850 \times 7.54 \times 10^{-8} = 5.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$

**Step 4:** $R_{d} = 5.92 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 2.13 \text{ kg/hr}$

$$\boxed{R_{d} \approx 2.13 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "transition current" with "droplet frequency"** — they are NOT the same. Transition current is a current threshold; droplet frequency is a rate of detachment.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversions** — wire feed speed in m/min must be converted to m/s, and deposition rate is asked in kg/**hr** (not kg/s). Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 3. **Mislabelling forces** — the electromagnetic pinch force acts *downward* (it squeezes the droplet off the tip), not upward. Surface tension acts *upward* (it holds the droplet).
> 4. **Skipping the "sketch"** — for 7-mark questions, a labelled diagram is **mandatory** and carries at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring shielding gas** — many answers list transfer modes without specifying that **shielding gas composition governs which mode is achievable**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Metal transfer = detachment and transfer of molten droplets from electrode to weld pool across the arc.
- **Four modes in GMAW:** Short-Circuit → Globular → Spray → Pulsed-Spray (in order of increasing current, complexity, and cost).
- **Short-Circuit (Dip):** Low current (50–150 A), low voltage (15–22 V), uses CO₂, all positions, but produces spatter; detachment via short circuit + I²R necking.
- **Globular:** Medium current (150–250 A), large droplets (1.5–3× wire dia), gravity-driven detachment, pure CO₂ preferred, **flat position only**, heavy spatter.
- **Spray:** Above **transition current** (~280 A for 1.6 mm wire in Ar), spatter-free, axial fine droplets, Argon-rich gas, **flat + horizontal** only, very high heat input.
- **Pulsed-Spray:** Uses a pulsed current waveform (background + peak); **one droplet per pulse**; combines low heat input with spray quality; **all-position** capability.
- **Key Forces on droplet:** Gravity ↓, Surface Tension ↑, Electromagnetic Pinch ↓, Plasma Jet ↓.
- **Detachment criterion:** $\sum F_{\text{down}} > \sum F_{\text{up}}$ (i.e., $W + F_e + F_p > \sigma$).
- **Heat Input formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm — must memorise.
- **Deposition Rate formula:** $R_d = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot 3600$ kg/hr — must memorise and practise unit conversions.
- **Transition Current** depends on wire diameter, material, and shielding gas — operating above it switches to spray mode.
- **Pulsed-spray** = the modern industrial standard for aluminium, stainless steel, and thin-section high-quality work.
- **Sound signatures** during welding can be used by experienced welders to *identify* the transfer mode (buzz = short-circuit, pop = globular, hiss = spray).
- **Shielding Gas Rule:** Argon-rich → spray mode; CO₂-rich → globular/short-circuit mode; mixtures allow intermediate behaviour.
- **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** is the **primary control knob** in GMAW — it sets the current via the constant-voltage power source.
- **Practical tip:** In KTU exam, always draw a **labelled diagram** for any 7-mark question on a transfer mode; it secures partial marks even if the written explanation is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arc Blow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arc Blow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Arc Blow** is a welding defect phenomenon defined as the *unwanted deflection or wandering of the welding arc from its intended path* during the arc welding process. This deviation is primarily caused by the interaction of **magnetic fields** generated by the welding current itself with ferromagnetic workpieces, or by **thermal gradients** and **mechanical gas flow disturbances** in the arc region.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, arc blow is classified under *welding arc stability disturbances* and is considered a critical process parameter because it directly degrades weld bead geometry, fusion characteristics, and overall joint integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Reference Phrasing):**
> *"Arc blow is the deflection of the welding arc from its normal axis caused by magnetic forces arising from the interaction of the welding current with itself or with magnetic materials, resulting in erratic arc behaviour, spatter, porosity, and lack of fusion."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose, and a strong side wind keeps pushing the water stream away from the plants you want to water. The water still flows, but it does not reach the intended target accurately.

In arc welding:
- The **water stream** = the welding arc (a column of ionized plasma).
- The **side wind** = invisible magnetic forces around the workpiece.
- The **plants** = the intended weld joint / fusion zone.

Just as you would have to constantly re-aim the hose, the welder must compensate for arc blow by adjusting the electrode angle. If the magnetic "wind" is too strong, the arc becomes uncontrollable, just as a hose in a gale becomes useless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Observation:**
> Arc blow is most commonly observed in **DC (Direct Current) welding** of ferromagnetic materials (e.g., low-carbon steel, cast iron) and in long weld seams. It is rarely a problem in AC welding or in non-magnetic materials like aluminum and austenitic stainless steel.

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Constants

The following parameters govern the severity of arc blow:

- **Welding Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** — measured in **Tesla (T)**
- **Magnetic Field Strength (H)** — measured in **Ampere-turns per meter (At/m)**
- **Permeability of Free Space (μ₀)** = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- **Relative Permeability of Ferromagnetic Steel (μᵣ)** ≈ **100 to 5000**
- **Distance from Current Path (r)** — measured in **meters (m)**
- **Arc Length** — measured in **millimeters (mm)**
- **Workpiece Thickness (t)** — measured in **mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Why Direct Current (DC) is the main culprit:**
> In **AC (Alternating Current)** welding, the current direction reverses **50 to 120 times per second**, so the magnetic field also reverses, and the arc is pulled in alternating directions so rapidly that it appears stable. In **DC**, the magnetic field has a fixed polarity, producing a steady deflecting force — this is why DC welding is highly susceptible to arc blow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Arc Blow (Structured Logic)

Arc blow is broadly classified into **three categories** based on the dominant causal mechanism:

#### A. Magnetic Arc Blow
- **Cause:** Uneven distribution of magnetic flux in the workpiece due to:
  - The arc current itself generates a magnetic field.
  - Ferromagnetic material (steel) has a high relative permeability, concentrating flux lines.
  - Discontinuities in the workpiece (corners, edges, end of plate, tack welds) cause flux line distortion.
- **Mechanism:** Lorentz Force **F = B × I × L** acts on the arc plasma, deflecting it toward the region of lower magnetic reluctance.

#### B. Thermal Arc Blow
- **Cause:** Non-uniform heating of the workpiece creates localized temperature gradients.
- **Mechanism:** Heated regions have lower permeability (permeability drops sharply above the Curie temperature of ~770°C for steel). This creates a permeability gradient that distorts the magnetic field.

#### C. Mechanical (Aerodynamic) Arc Blow
- **Cause:** Unbalanced shielding gas flow, drafts, or flux gases escaping unevenly from the weld pool.
- **Mechanism:** Gas jet pressure physically pushes the arc column sideways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** *Magnetic arc blow is the most frequently examined type.* In Part B questions, expect at least one 7-mark sub-question on the magnetic causes and one 7-mark sub-question on prevention methods.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Magnetic Arc Blow Develops

1. **Current Flow Initiation** — Welding current enters the workpiece at the arc attachment point and exits through the ground clamp.
2. **Magnetic Field Generation** — By **Biot-Savart Law** and **Ampere's Circuital Law**, the current creates a concentric magnetic field around the conductor.
3. **Flux Distortion at Discontinuities** — When the current path reaches a corner, end, or change in cross-section, the magnetic flux lines are compressed on one side and rarefied on the other.
4. **Force on the Arc** — The arc, being a current-carrying conductor itself, experiences a force **F = BIL** that pushes it away from the region of high flux density.
5. **Arc Deflection Visible** — The arc visibly drifts, often toward the nearest edge or away from the ground clamp.
6. **Weld Defect Formation** — Porosity, spatter, undercut, lack of fusion, and uneven bead shape result.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$ | **Ampere's Circuital Law** (Maxwell's equation for magnetostatics) | $B$ = T, $I$ = A, $l$ = m |
| 2 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$ | Magnetic flux density around a straight current-carrying conductor | $r$ = distance from conductor (m) |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r}$ | Magnetic field strength (in air, non-magnetic medium) | $H$ = At/m |
| 4 | $F = B I L$ | Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field | $F$ = N, $L$ = length of conductor (m) |
| 5 | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I^2 L}{2 \pi r}$ | Resultant force causing arc blow (combined eqs. 2 and 4) | Force is proportional to $I^2$ |
| 6 | $\mu_r = \dfrac{B}{B_0}$ | Definition of relative permeability | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{Curie} \approx 770^\circ C$ | Curie temperature of iron (above this, $\mu_r \to 1$) | Temperature in °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:**
> The force causing arc blow is proportional to the **square of the welding current** ($I^2$). This is why doubling the current **quadruples** the arc blow force — a key reason why high-current DC welding is severely affected.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding arc blow is essential in:
- **Pressure vessel fabrication** (long circumferential seams).
- **Shipbuilding** (large steel plate welding).
- **Pipeline welding** (longitudinal and girth welds).
- **Heavy structural welding** (bridges, offshore platforms).
- **Nuclear and defense component manufacturing** (zero-defect requirements).

In industries where **zero-defect welds** are mandated (e.g., ASME Section IX, AWS D1.1), uncontrolled arc blow is grounds for weld rejection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Current-Carrying Conductor (Basis of Arc Blow)

This derivation establishes **why** a magnetic field is created around the welding current path, which is the root cause of magnetic arc blow.

**Starting Point — Ampere's Circuital Law:**

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Symmetry Assumption:**

Consider a long, straight conductor carrying current $I$. By cylindrical symmetry, the magnetic field $B$ at a distance $r$ from the conductor axis is constant in magnitude on any circular path of radius $r$ centered on the conductor.

Therefore, we can pull $B$ out of the integral:

$$B \oint dl = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Closed Line Integral:**

The integral $\oint dl$ over a circle of radius $r$ is simply the circumference of the circle:

$$\oint dl = 2 \pi r$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Solve for B:**

$$B \cdot (2 \pi r) = \mu_0 I$$

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for a Ferromagnetic Workpiece:**

Inside or near a ferromagnetic material (relative permeability $\mu_r$), the magnetic flux density is enhanced:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** For a typical mild steel workpiece with $\mu_r = 500$, the flux density is **500 times stronger** than it would be in air — this is the fundamental reason arc blow is severe on steel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Lorentz Force Acting on the Welding Arc (Quantitative Arc Blow Force)

The welding arc is essentially a flexible, current-carrying conductor (a column of ionized gas). When placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force.

**Starting Point — Lorentz Force Equation:**

$$F = B I L \sin\theta$$

**Step 1 — Identify Geometry:**

For arc blow, the magnetic field $B$ is perpendicular to the arc current direction, so $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$F = B I L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Expression for B from Derivation 1:**

Replace $B$ with $\dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$:

$$F = \left( \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r} \right) \cdot I \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I^2 L}{2 \pi r}$$

**Step 4 — Analyze the Resulting Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &\propto I^2 \quad \text{(quadratic dependence on current)} \\
F &\propto \mu_r \quad \text{(proportional to relative permeability)} \\
F &\propto L \quad \text{(proportional to arc length)} \\
F &\propto \dfrac{1}{r} \quad \text{(inversely proportional to distance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Insight:**
> This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU examinations. Students are expected to:
> 1. State Ampere's Law clearly.
> 2. Apply the cylindrical symmetry argument.
> 3. Substitute the permeability term.
> 4. Combine with the Lorentz force equation.
> 5. Discuss the engineering implications of the final result.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Effect of Welding Process on Arc Blow)

| Welding Process | Current Type | Arc Blow Susceptibility | Reason |
|-----------------|--------------|------------------------|--------|
| **SMAW (Stick)** | DC | **High** | Ferromagnetic workpiece + DC + long arc |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | DC | **Moderate** | Continuous wire feed, but stable arc |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | DC | **High** | Stable arc easily disturbed; precise process |
| **SAW (Submerged Arc)** | AC / DC | **Low (DC) / Very Low (AC)** | Flux covers arc; submerged environment |
| **AC Welding (any)** | AC | **Very Low** | Alternating flux cancels out |
| **Plasma / Laser** | — | **Negligible** | Different physical mechanism |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A DC arc welding operation on a mild steel plate ($\mu_r = 400$) carries a current of 250 A. The arc length is 4 mm, and the arc is located 25 mm from a magnetic flux concentration zone. Calculate the deflecting force on the arc.

**Given:**
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- $\mu_r = 400$
- $I = 250 \text{ A}$
- $L = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$
- $r = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Magnetic Flux Density B:**

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 400 \times 250}{2 \pi \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 400 \times 250}{2 \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{0.04}{0.05} \\
&= 0.8 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Deflecting Force F:**

$$F = B I L$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 0.8 \times 250 \times 0.004 \\
F &= 0.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A force of 0.8 N on a flexible plasma column is **substantial** — it is enough to deflect a 4 mm arc by several millimeters, producing visible drift, spatter, and porosity. This is why practical arc blow prevention is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Arc Blow Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Welding Current Initiated] --> B[Current Flows Through Workpiece]
    B --> C[Magnetic Field Generated Around Conductor]
    C --> D{Encounter Discontinuity}
    D --> E[Corner Edge Plate End Tack Weld]
    D --> F[Change in Cross Section]
    E --> G[Magnetic Flux Distortion]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Uneven Flux Density on Two Sides of Arc]
    H --> I[Lorentz Force Acts on Arc Plasma]
    I --> J[Arc Deflection from Intended Path]
    J --> K[Defect Formation]
    K --> L1[Porosity]
    K --> L2[Spatter and Undercut]
    K --> L3[Lack of Fusion]
    K --> L4[Erratic Bead Shape]
    J --> M[Prevention Strategies]
    M --> N1[Reduce Welding Current]
    M --> N2[Reposition Ground Clamp]
    M --> N3[Use AC Instead of DC]
    M --> N4[Shorten Arc Length]
    M --> N5[Use Start and Run Off Tabs]
    M --> N6[Use Backstep Technique]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Arc Blow Prevention Methodology

```mermaid

    Q --> R1[Technique Based]

    R1 --> S1[Backstep Welding Sequence]
    R1 --> S2[Symmetric Ground Placement]
    R1 --> S3[Use of Run Off Tabs]

    R2 --> T2[Pulsed Current Mode]
    R2 --> T3[Ceramic Backing Strips]
    R3 --> U1[Reduce Current and Voltage]
    R3 --> U2[Reduce Travel Speed]
    R3 --> U3[Change Electrode Angle]
    S1 --> V[Stable Arc Restored]
    S2 --> V
    S3 --> V
    T1 --> V
    T2 --> V
    T3 --> V
    U1 --> V
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Magnetic Field Distribution Around Welding Current

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WORKPIECE[Ferromagnetic Workpiece Steel Plate]
        direction TB
        W1[Edge or Corner Discontinuity]
        W2[High Flux Density Region]
        W3[Low Flux Density Region]
    end
    subgraph ARCREGION[Arc Plasma Region]
        direction TB
        A1[Arc Current I]
        A2[Arc Length L]
        A3[Arc Attachment Point]
    end
    subgraph FORCEVEC[Force Vector Analysis]
        direction TB
        F1[Lorentz Force F]
        F2[Force Direction Toward Low Flux Region]
        F3[Magnitude Proportional to I Squared]
    end
    A1 --> F1
    A3 --> F1
    W2 --> F2
    W3 --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    W1 --> W2
    W1 --> W3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These block diagrams illustrate the *functional flow* of how arc blow develops and the *intervention points* for prevention. In a typical KTU exam, students may be asked to draw or describe such a causal flow as part of a 7-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define arc blow. Mention any two of its causes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Arc blow is the deflection of the welding arc from its intended path caused by magnetic forces, thermal gradients, or aerodynamic disturbances, leading to erratic arc behaviour and weld defects.

**Two Causes:**
1. **Magnetic field distortion** due to uneven magnetic flux distribution in ferromagnetic workpieces, especially near corners, plate ends, and tack welds.
2. **Direct Current (DC) flow** which produces a steady magnetic field, as opposed to AC which alternates and self-corrects.

> [!TIP]
> **Word Limit Target:** Keep Part A answers between **80 and 120 words** for full 3-mark credit. Use bullet points for causes; avoid long paragraphs.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Ampere's Circuital Law and write the expression for magnetic flux density around a straight current-carrying conductor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ampere's Circuital Law:** The line integral of magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ around a closed loop is equal to $\mu_0$ times the total current enclosed by the loop.

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

**Magnetic Flux Density around a straight conductor:**

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

Where:
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (T)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $r$ = radial distance from conductor (m)
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the medium

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered for full credit. The internal choice means you answer *either* Question A *or* Question B, not both.

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of magnetic arc blow in DC arc welding. With the help of Ampere's Law, derive the expression for the magnetic flux density around a straight current-carrying conductor and show how it leads to a deflecting force on the arc.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss any seven practical methods to prevent or minimize arc blow during DC welding of ferromagnetic materials.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Introduction to Magnetic Arc Blow (2 marks):**
Magnetic arc blow is a phenomenon in which the welding arc is deflected from its intended axis due to magnetic forces arising from the interaction of the welding current with the ferromagnetic workpiece. It is most commonly observed in **DC welding** of steel components at corners, plate ends, and near tack welds where magnetic flux lines become distorted.

**Derivation of B around a Conductor (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** State Ampere's Circuital Law:
$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

**Step 2:** Apply cylindrical symmetry — for a long straight conductor, $B$ is constant on a circle of radius $r$:
$$B \cdot 2\pi r = \mu_0 I$$

**Step 3:** Solve for B in a ferromagnetic medium:
$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

**Connection to Arc Deflection (2 marks):**
The arc itself carries current $I$ and has length $L$. Subjected to the magnetic field $B$ at right angles, the arc experiences a Lorentz force:
$$F = BIL = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I^2 L}{2 \pi r}$$

This force is proportional to $I^2$ and $\mu_r$, which explains why arc blow is severe at high DC currents and on ferromagnetic materials. The arc is pushed toward the region of lower magnetic flux density, causing the visible drift.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Seven Practical Methods to Prevent Arc Blow (1 mark each):**

1. **Reduce Welding Current** — Since $F \propto I^2$, lowering the current drastically reduces the deflecting force.
2. **Reposition the Ground Clamp** — Place the ground clamp at the start of the weld and move it progressively, or place it symmetrically to balance the magnetic field.
3. **Use AC Instead of DC** — Alternating current reverses polarity 50–120 times per second, so the deflecting force changes direction rapidly and the arc remains stable.
4. **Shorten Arc Length** — A shorter arc has less length $L$ over which the deflecting force can act.
5. **Use Start and Run-Off Tabs** — These metal extensions at the start and end of the weld allow the arc to stabilize outside the actual joint, avoiding the high-flux edge regions.
6. **Adopt Backstep Welding Technique** — Weld in short segments moving *backward* from the joint start, allowing the heat and magnetic field to be more uniformly distributed.
7. **Use Pulsed Current or Inverter-Based Power Sources** — Modern inverter welders provide pulsed output that disrupts the steady magnetic field buildup.

**Concluding Statement:** By combining these methods, arc blow can be practically eliminated in most industrial welding applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write Ampere's Law without showing the symmetry argument. Always state *why* $B$ can be taken outside the integral.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Many students forget to mention **$\mu_r$** in the final $B$ equation for a ferromagnetic medium. This costs **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In part (b), students list only 3–4 methods. KTU requires **at least six to seven** distinct methods for full 7 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing arc blow with other defects like porosity or spatter — they are *consequences* of arc blow, not methods of prevention.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different types of arc blow. Explain magnetic arc blow in detail with a neat diagram showing the deflection of the arc near a plate corner.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DC arc welding operation uses 300 A current on a steel plate with $\mu_r = 500$. The arc length is 5 mm and the arc is 20 mm from a magnetic flux concentration zone. Calculate (i) the magnetic flux density $B$ at the arc location, and (ii) the deflecting force on the arc. Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Classification of Arc Blow (2 marks):**

| Type | Primary Cause |
|------|---------------|
| Magnetic Arc Blow | Distortion of magnetic flux lines in ferromagnetic material |
| Thermal Arc Blow | Temperature gradients causing permeability variations |
| Mechanical (Aerodynamic) Arc Blow | Unbalanced gas flow / drafts disturbing the arc |

**Detailed Explanation of Magnetic Arc Blow (4 marks):**

Magnetic arc blow is the most common and severe form. It occurs in DC welding when the magnetic flux generated by the welding current is unevenly distributed around the arc. Causes include:

- **Discontinuities in the workpiece** — corners, edges, ends of plates, tack welds.
- **High permeability of steel** — concentrates flux on one side, creating imbalance.
- **Long current path** — increases the total magnetic flux, amplifying the effect.

The arc, being a flexible current-carrying conductor, is pushed by the Lorentz force toward the side of lower flux density, deflecting it from the intended weld path.

**Diagram Description (1 mark):**
A diagram should show:
- A steel plate with a corner.
- The arc (shown as a curved line) being deflected away from the corner.
- Concentric magnetic field lines around the current path.
- An arrow indicating the deflecting force.

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Tip:** Use a top-view schematic with the plate corner at the top-left, the arc moving from left to right, and the deflection curving toward the open side. Label all key regions (high flux, low flux, force direction).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $I = 300 \text{ A}$
- $\mu_r = 500$
- $L = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- $r = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

**(i) Magnetic Flux Density B (3 marks):**

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times 300}{2 \pi \times 0.020} \\
&= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 150000}{0.04 \pi} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 150000 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{0.04} \\
B &= 1.5 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Deflecting Force F (3 marks):**

$$F = B I L$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 1.5 \times 300 \times 0.005 \\
&= 1.5 \times 1.5 \\
F &= 2.25 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A force of 2.25 N on the arc is significant and will cause visible arc deflection, leading to weld defects. This problem demonstrates the practical severity of arc blow in DC welding of ferromagnetic materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert mm to m. **Always** write "$L = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$" to show unit awareness (1 mark is often reserved for unit conversion).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing $\mu_0$ as $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ but then **forgetting to include $\mu_r$** in the numerator.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using the wrong formula (e.g., $F = B^2 I L$ instead of $F = BIL$).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In part (a), the diagram often lacks a label for the **force direction arrow** — this costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Definition** — Arc blow = deflection of welding arc from intended path due to magnetic, thermal, or aerodynamic forces.
- [x] **Most Common Type** — Magnetic arc blow in **DC welding** of **ferromagnetic** materials (steel).
- [x] **Root Cause** — Distortion of magnetic flux lines at corners, edges, and tack welds.
- [x] **Ampere's Circuital Law** — $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$
- [x] **Magnetic Flux Density Formula** — $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$
- [x] **Deflecting Force Formula** — $F = B I L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I^2 L}{2 \pi r}$
- [x] **Key Insight** — $F \propto I^2$, so high current = severe arc blow.
- [x] **Curie Temperature** — Above ~770°C, steel loses its ferromagnetism ($\mu_r \to 1$); this is why heated regions can locally reduce arc blow.
- [x] **AC vs DC** — AC is far less susceptible due to reversing flux.
- [x] **Seven Prevention Methods** — Reduce current, reposition ground, use AC, shorten arc, use start/run-off tabs, backstep technique, use pulsed/inverter power.
- [x] **Consequences of Arc Blow** — Porosity, spatter, undercut, lack of fusion, irregular bead, poor weld appearance.
- [x] **Unit Conversions** — Always convert mm to m before substituting into formulas.
- [x] **Engineering Standards** — Arc blow is a quality-critical issue in **ASME Section IX** and **AWS D1.1** codes.
- [x] **Most Likely KTU Question Type** — 7-mark derivation question (Ampere's Law + Lorentz Force) + 7-mark prevention/comparison question.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> 1. For Part A, memorize the *definition* and *two causes* in crisp bullet form.
> 2. For Part B, practice the **derivation chain** (Ampere's Law → $B$ → Lorentz Force) at least 3 times before the exam.
> 3. Always **draw a diagram** wherever the question allows — KTU examiners award **1 to 2 marks** specifically for neat, labelled schematics.
> 4. In numerical problems, **show all unit conversions** — this is a free 1-mark booster.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding Electrode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Electrode (KTU 2024 Definition):** A welding electrode is a metallic filler or non-filler component that is used to conduct current, sustain the arc, and supply filler metal to the weld joint. It is the consumable or non-consumable metal wire/rod through which welding current is delivered to the workpiece, enabling the fusion of base metals.

In arc welding, the electrode performs **three simultaneous functions**:
1. **Current Conduction** — It completes the electrical circuit between the welding machine and the workpiece.
2. **Arc Maintenance** — It sustains a stable, ionized electric arc at its tip.
3. **Filler Metal Supply** — For consumable electrodes, it melts and deposits filler metal into the weld pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The classification, designation, coating, and selection of welding electrodes form a **core competency** under CO2 (Identify appropriate manufacturing processes). Every B.Tech Mechanical/Production student must be able to **decode an AWS electrode designation** like a board examiner expects.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are using a **candle to join two pieces of wax together**. The candle flame melts the wax, and the dripping wax (filler) flows into the gap, sealing it. The candle wick conducts the heat, while the wax itself becomes part of the joint.

A **welding electrode behaves exactly like this candle**:
- The **metal core wire** is like the **wax** — it melts and becomes the filler.
- The **flux coating** is like the **candle wick** — it stabilizes the flame (arc) and protects the molten pool from atmospheric contamination.
- The **electric current** is the match that lights it.

If you use a **TIG tungsten electrode**, think of it as a **non-melting matchstick** — it only strikes the arc but never becomes part of the weld (just like a match head lights the candle but does not become the wax).

## 1.3 Broad Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Fundamental Classes:**
> - **Consumable Electrode** — Melts and becomes part of the weld deposit (e.g., E6013, E7018 stick electrodes, ER70S-6 MIG wire).
> - **Non-Consumable Electrode** — Does not melt; only sustains the arc (e.g., Tungsten electrode in TIG/GTAW).
> 
> **Bare vs Coated:** Bare electrodes have no coating (rare, used in special applications). Coated electrodes (covered electrodes) have a flux layer extruded onto the core wire.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants for Welding Electrodes

| Parameter | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel Core Resistivity | $\mathbf{1.7 \times 10^{-7} \, \Omega \cdot m}$ | Resistance heating calculation |
| Tungsten Melting Point | $\mathbf{3422 \, °C}$ | Non-consumable electrode (TIG) |
| Iron Melting Point | $\mathbf{1538 \, °C}$ | Steel electrode core |
| Arc Temperature (typical) | $\mathbf{6000 - 20000 \, °C}$ | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Welding Electrodes

Welding electrodes are classified based on **four primary criteria**:

### (A) Based on Consumability
- **Consumable Electrodes:** Used in SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding), GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding), FCAW (Flux Cored Arc Welding), SAW (Submerged Arc Welding). The electrode melts and contributes filler metal.
- **Non-Consumable Electrodes:** Used in GTAW/TIG (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) and Plasma Arc Welding. Tungsten (W) or Tungsten alloys (W+ThO₂, W+La₂O₃, W+CeO₂) are used.

### (B) Based on Coating
- **Bare Electrodes:** No coating — used in atomic hydrogen welding and some resistance welding applications.
- **Coated (Covered) Electrodes:** Have a flux coating applied by extrusion. The coating performs arc stabilization, deoxidation, slag formation, and alloy addition.

### (C) Based on Coating Type (AWES/AWS Classification)
- **Cellulosic (e.g., E6010):** High hydrogen, deep penetration, DC+ polarity, used for pipe welding.
- **Rutile (e.g., E6012, E6013):** Titanium dioxide coating, smooth arc, AC/DC, all-position welding.
- **Iron Powder (e.g., E7024):** High deposition rate, horizontal fillet welds.
- **Basic / Low Hydrogen (e.g., E7018):** Low moisture absorption, high toughness, used in critical structures.

### (D) Based on Current Compatibility
- **DC Electrodes:** Work only on Direct Current (e.g., E6010).
- **AC/DC Electrodes:** Work on both Alternating and Direct Current (e.g., E6013).

## 2.2 AWS (American Welding Society) Electrode Designation System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AWS A5.1 designation for carbon steel electrodes is the most exam-critical topic under KTU Module 2.** You must decode it letter-by-letter.

**Example:** `E 7018`

| Position | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **E** | Electrode |
| 2 | **70** | Minimum tensile strength in **ksi** (70,000 psi = 483 MPa) |
| 3 | **1** | Position capability (1 = All positions, 2 = Flat & Horizontal only) |
| 4 | **8** | Coating type and current type (8 = Low hydrogen, DC+ or AC) |

### Full Digit-4 Decoding Table

| Digit | Type of Coating | Current Type | Penetration |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Cellulosic (high cellulose) | DCEP | Deep |
| 1 | Cellulosic (high cellulose) | AC or DCEP | Deep |
| 2 | Rutile (TiO₂) | AC or DCEN | Medium |
| 3 | Rutile (TiO₂) | AC or DCEP | Medium-light |
| 4 | Iron powder, rutile | AC or DCEN | Light |
| 5 | Low hydrogen (basic) | DCEP | Medium |
| 6 | Low hydrogen (basic) | AC or DCEP | Medium |
| 7 | Iron powder, low hydrogen | AC or DCEP | Medium |
| 8 | Low hydrogen (basic), iron powder | AC or DCEP | Medium |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in KTU ESE, university supplementary exams, and GATE (Mechanical).**

| Formula | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | $\text{J/mm}$ or $\text{kJ/mm}$ | Controls weld quality, HAZ, distortion |
| **Deposition Rate** | $R_d = \dfrac{W_d}{t}$ | $\text{kg/h}$ | Productivity metric |
| **Deposition Efficiency** | $\eta_d = \dfrac{W_d}{W_e} \times 100\%$ | % | Electrode utilization |
| **Travel Speed** | $v = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{H}$ | $\text{mm/s}$ or $\text{mm/min}$ | Weld procedure planning |
| **Electrode Current Density** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_e}$ | $\text{A/mm}^2$ | Current selection |
| **Arc Force (approx.)** | $F_a = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{8\pi}$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Theoretical arc pressure |
| **Power Input** | $P = V \cdot I$ | $\text{Watts}$ | Welding machine rating |

Where:
- $\eta$ = Arc efficiency (SMAW ≈ 0.75–0.85, GMAW ≈ 0.65–0.75, SAW ≈ 0.90–0.99)
- $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = Welding current (Amperes)
- $v$ = Travel speed (mm/s or mm/min)
- $W_d$ = Weight of metal deposited
- $W_e$ = Weight of electrode consumed
- $A_e$ = Cross-sectional area of electrode core

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The electrode selection system directly impacts:
- **Weld mechanical properties** (tensile strength, impact toughness).
- **Weld metal chemistry** (C, Mn, Si, S, P pickup).
- **Process productivity** (deposition rate in kg/h).
- **Defect prevention** (porosity, cracking, slag inclusion).
- **Cost optimization** in structural, automotive, and pressure-vessel fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Note:** The **E7018 electrode** is the industry standard for structural steel (bridges, buildings, ships) because its **low-hydrogen coating** prevents hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC) in thick sections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Formula

The **Heat Input (H)** is the most frequently asked KTU formula. Let's derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Power Generated by the Arc
The welding arc is essentially an electrical load. The power dissipated in the arc is the product of arc voltage and welding current.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

Where $P$ = arc power in **Watts**, $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts**, $I$ = welding current in **Amperes**.

### Step 2 — Account for Arc Efficiency
Not all electrical power is converted into useful heat at the weld pool. A fraction $\eta$ (eta) is transferred to the workpiece; the rest is lost by radiation, spatter, and convection. Therefore, the **effective heat delivered** is:

$$P_{eff} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

> **Logic:** $\eta$ is dimensionless. For SMAW, $\eta \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$. This is because the flux coating and slag blanket absorb some heat.

### Step 3 — Convert Power to Heat per Unit Length
Heat input is defined as the **energy delivered per unit length of weld**. If the electrode travels at velocity $v$, then the time taken to traverse a unit length is:

$$t = \frac{1}{v}$$

Thus, the heat per unit length is:

$$H = P_{eff} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{v}$$

### Step 4 — Final Heat Input Equation

$$\boxed{H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Higher current, higher voltage, slower travel speed, and higher arc efficiency all increase heat input. Excessive heat input causes **coarse grains, wide HAZ, and distortion**. Insufficient heat input causes **lack of fusion and porosity**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (Model Q):** A welder uses an E7018 electrode (SMAW) on a 10 mm thick mild steel plate. Arc voltage = 25 V, current = 200 A, travel speed = 5 mm/s, arc efficiency = 0.80. Calculate the **Heat Input** in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Substitute values into the heat input equation.

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

### Step 2 — Insert the numerical values.

$$H = \frac{0.80 \times 25 \, \text{V} \times 200 \, \text{A}}{5 \, \text{mm/s}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply the numerator.

$$H = \frac{0.80 \times 5000}{5} = \frac{4000}{5}$$

### Step 4 — Final division.

$$H = 800 \, \text{J/mm} = \frac{800}{1000} \, \text{kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{H = 0.80 \, \text{kJ/mm}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a typical heat input for SMAW on mild steel. For reference, the recommended range for E7018 on 10 mm plate is **0.6 – 1.2 kJ/mm**. Our value lies within the safe band.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Deposition Efficiency

Deposition efficiency tells us how much of the consumed electrode actually becomes weld metal.

### Step 1 — Define the variables.

$$\eta_d = \frac{\text{Weight of metal deposited in the weld}}{\text{Weight of electrode consumed}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 2 — In equation form.

$$\eta_d = \frac{W_d}{W_e} \times 100\%$$

### Step 3 — Typical values (Table for KTU exams):

| Electrode Type | Deposition Efficiency |
|---|---|
| E6010 (Cellulosic) | ~60 – 65% |
| E6013 (Rutile) | ~65 – 70% |
| E7018 (Low Hydrogen) | ~70 – 75% |
| E7024 (Iron Powder) | ~75 – 80% |
| SAW wire + flux | ~95 – 99% |
| MIG wire (GMAW) | ~90 – 95% |

> **Engineering Insight:** The remaining 20–40% is lost as **spatter, stub loss (electrode stub left in holder), and slag**. Iron powder electrodes have higher efficiency because iron powder in the coating adds extra metal to the weld.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Electrode vs. Process

| Selection Criterion | Best Electrode Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| All-position welding | E6013 or E7018 | Coating supports vertical/overhead |
| Structural steel bridge | E7018 | Low hydrogen prevents HIC |
| Pipe welding (X-ray quality) | E6010 | Deep penetration, cellulose coating |
| High deposition, flat only | E7024 | Iron powder boosts rate |
| Stainless steel welding | E308L-16 | Matches chemistry, low carbon |
| Cast iron repair | ENi-CI (Nickel) | Compatible with cast iron |
| Thin sheet (<3 mm) | E6012 or E6013 | Low current, low penetration |
| Critical low-temperature service | E7018 | Charpy impact toughness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
> **Define a welding electrode. List the four AWS classification digits with their meaning for an E6013 electrode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A welding electrode is a metallic rod or wire that conducts current, sustains the arc, and supplies filler metal (in consumable types) to the weld joint.

For **E6013**:
- **E** → Electrode
- **60** → Minimum tensile strength = 60 ksi (≈ 415 MPa)
- **1** → All-position welding capability
- **3** → Rutile (TiO₂) coating, AC or DCEP, medium-light penetration

> **Examiner's Key:** The student **must write all four digit meanings** — partial marks are awarded for each correct digit. Do not skip the "1" or "3".

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** 
> **Differentiate between consumable and non-consumable welding electrodes with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Consumable | Non-Consumable |
|---|---|---|
| Melting behavior | Melts and becomes filler | Does not melt |
| Filler contribution | Yes | No (separate filler rod may be used) |
| Example | E7018 (SMAW), ER70S-6 (MIG) | Tungsten W-2%ThO₂ (TIG), Tungsten (Plasma) |
| Process | SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW | GTAW, PAW |
| Composition | Usually matches base metal | High melting point (W, W+ThO₂) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** 
> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the classification of welding electrodes based on consumability. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the AWS designation system for mild steel covered electrodes. Decode the electrode **E7024** completely. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

Welding electrodes are classified into two broad categories based on consumability:

**(i) Consumable Electrodes:**
These electrodes melt during the welding process and contribute filler metal to the weld joint. The molten electrode material fuses with the molten base metal to form the weld bead.
- **Examples:** Stick electrodes in SMAW (e.g., E6013), solid wires in GMAW/MIG (e.g., ER70S-6), flux-cored wires in FCAW, and wire + flux combination in SAW.
- **Function:** Acts as both the current carrier and the filler metal source.

**(ii) Non-Consumable Electrodes:**
These electrodes do not melt during welding. They only sustain the electric arc between the electrode tip and the workpiece. A separate filler rod (if required) is manually fed into the weld pool.
- **Examples:** Tungsten electrodes in GTAW/TIG (Pure W, W+2%ThO₂, W+La₂O₃, W+CeO₂) and Plasma Arc Welding.
- **Function:** Acts only as a cathode/anode to sustain the arc. Tungsten has an extremely high melting point (3422 °C), so it remains solid.

**Sketch** (Neatly drawn in the answer sheet):
```
   Consumable Electrode (SMAW)       Non-Consumable Electrode (TIG)
   ┌──────────┐                       ┌──────────┐
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ Coated                 │          │ Bare Tungsten
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │                       │          │
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │                       │          │
   └────┬─────┘                       └────┬─────┘
        │  ←— Melts                       │  ←— Does NOT melt
        ▼                                  ▼
   ~~~~~~~~~ Arc ~~~~~~~~~         ~~~~~~~~~ Arc ~~~~~~~~~
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ Base Metal    ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ Base Metal
```

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

The **AWS A5.1 designation system** classifies mild steel covered electrodes using a **4-digit alphanumeric code**.

**Format:** `E XX YY`

**Decoding each position:**

| Position | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **E** | The letter E always denotes **Electrode**. |
| **XX (first 2 digits)** | Minimum **ultimate tensile strength** of the deposited weld metal, measured in **ksi** (kilo-pounds per square inch). |
| **Y (third digit)** | **Position** in which the electrode can be used: **1** = all positions (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead), **2** = flat and horizontal only. |
| **Y (fourth digit)** | Identifies the **type of coating**, **current type** (AC/DC, DCEP, DCEN), and **penetration depth**. |

**Decoding E7024:**

| Digit | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| E | E | Electrode |
| 70 | 70 | Minimum tensile strength = 70 ksi ≈ 483 MPa |
| 2 | 2 | Flat and horizontal positions only |
| 4 | 4 | Iron powder + rutile coating, AC or DCEN, light penetration, **high deposition rate** |

**Conclusion:** E7024 is a **high-deposition rutile-iron powder electrode** used for **flat and horizontal fillet welds** in heavy structural fabrication where productivity matters more than positional versatility.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** 
> **(a)** List and explain the **functions of the flux coating** on a shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) electrode. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A welder uses SMAW with E6013 on a 12 mm mild steel plate. Arc voltage = 24 V, current = 180 A, travel speed = 4 mm/s, and arc efficiency = 0.75. Calculate the **heat input in kJ/mm** and comment on its suitability. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The flux coating on a covered electrode performs **six critical functions**:

1. **Arc Stabilization:** The coating contains **ionization-promoting elements** (e.g., potassium, sodium, titanium oxides) that lower the arc voltage requirement and maintain a smooth, stable arc. This allows the electrode to operate on AC supply without arc blow.

2. **Slag Formation:** The flux melts and forms a **liquid slag blanket** that floats on top of the molten weld pool. This slag **solidifies** after the weld, protecting the cooling bead from atmospheric contamination and slowing the cooling rate (improving mechanical properties).

3. **Gas Shielding:** Decomposition of the coating releases **CO, CO₂, and H₂ gases**, which envelop the weld pool and displace atmospheric N₂ and O₂. This prevents **porosity** and **nitrogen embrittlement**.

4. **Deoxidation and Cleaning:** The flux contains **deoxidizers** (Ferro-manganese, Ferro-silicon, Ferro-titanium) that react with oxides and impurities in the molten pool, forming slag that floats to the surface.

5. **Alloy Addition:** The coating can supply **alloying elements** (Mn, Si, Cr, Ni) to the weld pool to achieve desired mechanical properties (strength, toughness, corrosion resistance).

6. **Weld Bead Appearance:** The slag smooths the bead surface and produces a uniform, aesthetic profile — important for radiographic quality and visual inspection.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Arc voltage, $V = 24 \, \text{V}$
- Welding current, $I = 180 \, \text{A}$
- Travel speed, $v = 4 \, \text{mm/s}$
- Arc efficiency, $\eta = 0.75$

**Formula:**
$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**
$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 24 \times 180}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator:**
$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 4320}{4} = \frac{3240}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Final calculation:**
$$H = 810 \, \text{J/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kJ/mm:**
$$H = \frac{810}{1000} = 0.81 \, \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Comment on Suitability:**
The recommended heat input for **12 mm mild steel using E6013** is typically in the range of **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. Our calculated value of **0.81 kJ/mm** lies **within the acceptable range**, indicating that the selected parameters are suitable for producing a sound weld without excessive HAZ softening or distortion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Many students forget to convert $\text{mm/s}$ to $\text{mm/min}$ when the formula is given in $\text{kJ/mm}$. Always check the **time unit consistency**.
> 2. **Missing η:** Students often omit the arc efficiency $\eta$, losing 1 full mark. Always write: $H = (\eta \cdot V \cdot I) / v$.
> 3. **Decoding Trap:** In Part B (a), writing **E** as "E = Electrode" but skipping the **third and fourth digit meaning** costs 2 marks. Always write **all four** digit meanings explicitly.
> 4. **Heat Input Range:** Examiners love to ask "comment on suitability" — students who do not provide a numeric range comparison **lose 2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Welding Electrode:**

- ✅ A welding electrode is **consumable** (melts) or **non-consumable** (e.g., tungsten).
- ✅ **AWS A5.1 designation:** `E XX YY` → Electrode, Tensile in ksi, Position, Coating/Current.
- ✅ **E7018** = 70 ksi tensile, all-position, low-hydrogen iron powder, AC/DCEP.
- ✅ **E6010** = 60 ksi tensile, all-position, cellulosic, DCEP, deep penetration — used for pipe welding.
- ✅ **E6013** = 60 ksi tensile, all-position, rutile, AC/DCEP, medium-light penetration — general purpose.
- ✅ **E7024** = 70 ksi tensile, flat/horizontal only, iron powder + rutile, high deposition rate.
- ✅ **Tungsten electrode colors:** Pure W = Green, W+2%ThO₂ = Red/Yellow, W+La₂O₃ = Blue, W+CeO₂ = Grey.
- ✅ **Flux coating functions (mnemonic — "SAD GAS"):**
  - **S**lag formation
  - **A**rc stabilization
  - **D**eoxidation
  - **G**as shielding
  - **A**lloy addition
  - **S**mooth bead
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = (\eta \cdot V \cdot I) / v$ — measured in J/mm or kJ/mm.
- ✅ **Typical arc efficiency:** SMAW ≈ 0.75–0.85, GMAW ≈ 0.65–0.75, SAW ≈ 0.90–0.99.
- ✅ **Iron powder electrodes** (E7024, E7018) have the highest **deposition efficiency** (~75–80%).
- ✅ **Low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018)** must be **stored in rod ovens at 120–150 °C** to prevent moisture pickup and hydrogen-induced cracking.
- ✅ **Electrode polarity:** DCEP (Electrode Positive) → deeper penetration. DCEN (Electrode Negative) → shallow penetration, faster melt-off.
- ✅ **Coated electrode parts (anatomy):** Core wire, flux coating, grip end (bare), contact tip, arc tip.

> **Final KTU Board Tip:** The **AWS designation decoding** (4-digit code) is worth **at least 4–6 marks** in any Part B question on welding electrodes. Practice decoding at least **E6010, E6011, E6012, E6013, E7014, E7018, and E7024** before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consumable and Non-Consumable Arc Welding, SMAW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consumable and Non-Consumable Arc Welding, SMAW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Arc Welding — The Foundational Definition

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is achieved by heating the workpiece to the melting temperature using the **intense heat generated by an electric arc** struck between an electrode and the workpiece (or between two electrodes). The arc acts as a sustained, high-temperature electrical discharge (typically **6,000 °C to 20,000 °C** at the anode/cathode spots) through a path of ionized gas (plasma) known as the **arc column**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Arc welding processes are classified based on **electrode behaviour** (consumable vs non-consumable), **shielding mechanism** (flux, gas, slag), and **arc transfer mode** (short-circuit, globular, spray, pulsed). For the 2024 scheme, students MUST clearly differentiate between these classification criteria in Part A (3-mark) questions.

---

### 1.2 The Two Master Families of Arc Welding

| Family | Core Idea | Typical Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumable Electrode Arc Welding** | The electrode itself melts and becomes part of the weld deposit (filler metal). | SMAW, GMAW (MIG/MAG), FCAW, SAW, Electrogas (EGW) |
| **Non-Consumable Electrode Arc Welding** | The electrode remains solid; filler metal (if any) is fed separately from a rod. | **GTAW (TIG)**, **PAW (Plasma Arc Welding)**, Atomic Hydrogen Welding (AHW) |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Matchstick vs. Candle" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Arc Welding:**
> Imagine striking a match. The flame is hot, but you do not consume the match head while it burns (it is the *flame* that does the work) — this is roughly how a **non-consumable** electrode behaves. The tungsten electrode carries the arc but does not melt; a separate filler rod is hand-fed into the puddle (like dipping a candle wick into a flame).
>
> Now, picture a **candle** whose wick itself is wax. As the wick burns, the wax melts and *adds* material to the molten pool. This is exactly how a **consumable** electrode works in SMAW/GMAW — the electrode's tip melts, droplets transfer across the arc, and they become the filler metal of the joint.

---

### 1.4 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — Formal KTU Definition

**Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)**, popularly known as **Manual Metal Arc (MMA) welding** or **"stick welding,"** is a *consumable-electrode, manual arc welding process* in which the heat for welding is produced by an electric arc between a **flux-covered metal electrode** and the workpiece. The flux coating disintegrates during welding to evolve a **shielding gas** and form a **molten slag** that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination, while the molten electrode tip provides the filler metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Shielded"?** The word *shielded* in SMAW refers to the triple protection provided by the flux: (1) gaseous shielding, (2) slag coverage, and (3) deoxidising/cleaning chemical action. This distinguishes SMAW from bare-electrode welding which has no such protection.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Arc Temperature:** **6,000 °C – 20,000 °C** (anode spot ~ 6,000 °C; cathode spot ~ 2,500 °C; arc column centre ~ 20,000 °C).
- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** **60 V – 80 V AC** (for SMAW); **50 V – 80 V DC**.
- **Arc Voltage during welding:** **18 V – 35 V** (typically **20 V – 25 V** for SMAW).
- **Welding Current Range:** **30 A – 600 A** depending on electrode diameter (Ø) and joint.
- **Standard Electrode Length:** **250 mm, 350 mm, 450 mm** (AWS A5.1).
- **Standard Electrode Diameter:** **2.0 mm, 2.5 mm, 3.15 mm, 4.0 mm, 5.0 mm, 6.3 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of an Electric Arc — Step-by-Step Logic

An **electric arc** is a self-sustaining electrical discharge through a partially ionised gas (plasma). Its establishment in arc welding follows a precise sequence:

1. **Hot Start (Touch-Start Method):**
   The electrode is momentarily *short-circuited* to the workpiece, allowing a large surge current to flow. Contact resistance at the touching point causes intense localised heating (I²R heating), which raises the temperature to the electrode's melting point.

2. **Arc Initiation (Arc Stretching):**
   The welder withdraws the electrode 2–4 mm. The molten bridge of metal at the contact stretches into a thin filament, then ruptures, releasing electrons by **thermionic emission**. This ignites the arc.

3. **Arc Stabilisation:**
   Once the gap reaches 2–4 mm, the discharge is maintained by ionisation of the shielding gas column. Three distinct zones form:
   - **Cathode zone** (electron emitter)
   - **Arc column / plasma column** (neutral, charge-balanced conductor; T ≈ 6,000–20,000 °C)
   - **Anode zone** (heat sink; ~ 60 % of arc heat dissipated here for DCEN-positive electrode)

4. **Heat Dissipation Modes:**
   - **Conduction** into the base metal
   - **Convection** via shielding gas
   - **Radiation** from the arc plasma

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** The question "Explain the mechanism of arc establishment" or "Differentiate between arc initiation in AC and DC welding" is a **favourite 7-mark Part B question**. Always mention *thermionic emission*, *arc column*, and the role of *ionisation potential* of the shielding medium.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Arc Welding Processes (Logic Tree)

**Why classify?** KTU examiners test this directly. The classification is hierarchical and criterion-based.

**Criterion 1: Electrode behaviour**
- **Consumable:** Electrode melts and adds filler metal → SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW.
- **Non-consumable:** Electrode remains solid → GTAW, PAW, AHW.

**Criterion 2: Shielding medium**
- **Flux shielded** (slag + gas) → SMAW, SAW.
- **Inert gas shielded** (Argon, Helium) → GTAW, PAW.
- **Active gas shielded** (CO₂, Ar+CO₂) → GMAW (MAG).
- **Self-shielded** (no external gas) → Self-shielded FCAW.

**Criterion 3: Mode of metal transfer** (consumable processes)
- **Short-circuit transfer** (low voltage, dip transfer) — GMAW.
- **Globular transfer** (large, irregular droplets) — CO₂ GMAW.
- **Spray transfer** (fine, axial droplets) — Argon-rich GMAW at high current.
- **Pulsed transfer** (controlled droplet per pulse).
- **Slag-protected metal transfer** — SMAW (covered electrode droplets pass through gas + slag envelope).

**Criterion 4: Automation level**
- **Manual:** SMAW, GTAW.
- **Semi-automatic:** GMAW, FCAW.
- **Fully automatic:** SAW, Orbital TIG, Robotic GMAW.

---

### 2.3 Comparison Table: Consumable vs Non-Consumable Arc Welding

| Parameter | Consumable Electrode | Non-Consumable Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode material | Mild steel, low-alloy, stainless, etc. (matches base metal) | **Tungsten (W)** or carbon (older AHW) |
| Filler metal source | Electrode itself | Separate filler rod (if needed) |
| Arc temperature (typical) | 6,000 – 10,000 °C (depends on current) | 10,000 – 20,000 °C (concentrated arc) |
| Shielding | Flux decomposition / external gas | Inert gas (Ar/He) — must be external |
| Process complexity | Lower | Higher (requires gas, HF TIG unit, foot pedal) |
| Deposition rate | **High** (especially SAW, GMAW) | **Lower** |
| Weld bead quality | Good (depends on skill) | **Excellent** — clean, spatter-free |
| Typical processes | SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW | GTAW, PAW, AHW |
| Outdoor usability | Yes (SMAW, FCAW-SS) | Poor (wind blows away shielding gas) |
| Cost | Lowest (SMAW) to moderate | High (TIG/PTA equipment) |

---

### 2.4 Non-Consumable Arc Welding — Quick Brief (GTAW/TIG and PAW)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG):**
> - Electrode: 2 % Thoriated Tungsten (red tip) or Ceriated / Lanthanated.
> - Shielding gas: **Pure Argon (99.99 %)** or Ar + He.
> - Arc is struck by **HF (high-frequency) spark** or *lift arc* method (no contact needed).
> - Polarity: **DCEN (Electrode Negative)** is the standard — concentrates ~ 70 % heat at the workpiece (anode).
> - Used for stainless steel, aluminium, magnesium, titanium, exotic alloys, and root passes of critical pipes.

> **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW):**
> - GTAW evolved: tungsten electrode is enclosed in a copper nozzle. **Orifice gas** (Argon) is constricted through a small hole, forming a high-velocity, high-energy **constricted plasma column** (T > 20,000 °C).
> - **Key advantages over TIG:** Higher energy density, deeper penetration, less sensitive to arc length variation (no "wandering arc"), can weld thicker sections without edge preparation, and supports *keyhole* mode for full-penetration square-butt welds.
> - Two modes: **Melt-in mode** (similar to TIG) and **Keyhole mode** (penetrates through the plate).

---

### 2.5 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — Deep Dive

#### 2.5.1 Principle of Operation
The flux-coated stick electrode is clamped in an **electrode holder** connected to one terminal of a welding power source. The **workpiece** is connected to the other terminal via a **ground clamp**. The arc is established between the electrode tip and the workpiece. Three simultaneous actions occur:

1. **Electric arc** generates the heat (≈ 6,000 °C at electrode tip).
2. **Electrode tip melts** and metal droplets transfer across the arc into the weld pool — *this is the filler metal*.
3. **Flux coating decomposes** to:
   - Evolve a **shielding gas envelope** (typically CO₂ from cellulose or limestone decomposition).
   - Form a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, protecting it from O₂ and N₂.
   - Add **deoxidising / de-nitrifying elements** (Si, Mn, Ti) to refine the weld metal.
   - Provide **arc-stabilising compounds** (potassium / sodium silicates, limestone).

#### 2.5.2 SMAW Equipment Block Diagram

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Welding Power Source** (Transformer / Rectifier / Inverter / Generator) | Delivers controlled V & I; constant-current (CC) characteristic preferred |
| 2 | **Electrode Holder** (stinger) | Mechanically grips electrode; conducts current |
| 3 | **Electrode (Stick)** | Filler + flux combined in one consumable |
| 4 | **Work Cable + Ground Clamp** | Completes the electrical circuit via the workpiece |
| 5 | **Welding Helmet / Hand Shield** | Manual protection from UV/IR radiation |
| 6 | **Chipping Hammer & Wire Brush** | Slag removal between passes |

#### 2.5.3 Polarity in SMAW — DCEP, DCEN, AC

| Polarity | Connection | Heat at Electrode | Heat at Plate | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP** (Direct Current Electrode Positive) | Electrode = + (anode), Plate = − (cathode) | ⅓ (≈ 40 %) | ⅔ (≈ 60 %) | **Most common for SMAW**; deep penetration; high deposition; rutile/iron-powder electrodes |
| **DCEN** (Direct Current Electrode Negative) | Electrode = −, Plate = + | ⅔ (≈ 60 %) | ⅓ (≈ 40 %) | Cellulosic electrodes (E6010) for pipe welding; high electrode melting rate |
| **AC** (Alternating Current) | Alternates 50/60 Hz | — | — | **Used for aluminium / magnesium** in GTAW; also for some SMAW electrodes (e.g., E6013 on AC) to reduce arc blow |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "DCEP" — the *electrode* is the *positive* terminal. Memorise: **DC(EP)** = *Electrode Positive* = the **E** is **P**ositive.

#### 2.5.4 AWS Electrode Classification (A5.1 — Carbon Steel)

**E XXXX** format:
- **E** = Electrode
- **First two digits (XX)** = **Minimum tensile strength** in **ksi** (thousands of psi) of the deposited weld metal.
  - **60** = 60,000 psi (≈ **410 MPa**)
  - **70** = 70,000 psi (≈ **490 MPa**)
  - **80** = 80,000 psi (≈ **550 MPa**)
  - **110** = 110,000 psi (≈ **760 MPa**)
- **Third digit** = **Welding position** capability:
  - **1** = All positions (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead)
  - **2** = Flat & horizontal only
- **Fourth digit (suffix)** = **Type of coating, current, and impact toughness**:
  - **0** = Cellulosic (DCEP only) — e.g., **E6010**
  - **1** = Cellulosic + potassium (DCEP / AC) — e.g., **E6011**
  - **2** = Titania / Rutile (smooth arc) — e.g., **E6012**
  - **3** = Titania / Potassium (all position, AC/DCEP) — e.g., **E6013** (the *most common* general-purpose electrode)
  - **4, 5, 6, 7, 8** = Iron powder, low hydrogen, etc.
  - **Suffix "−1"** = Impact tested at **−46 °C** (e.g., **E7018-1**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples to memorise for KTU:**
> - **E6013** — Mild-steel general purpose; all positions; AC or DCEP.
> - **E7018** — Low-hydrogen basic-coated; all positions; DCEP only; high-toughness.
> - **E6010** — Cellulosic, deep penetration; DCEP only; used for pipeline root passes.

#### 2.5.5 Slag and Its Functions
1. Shields weld pool from atmospheric O₂ and N₂.
2. Slows the cooling rate → improves mechanical properties.
3. Deoxidises and desulphurises the weld metal.
4. Shapes the weld bead (ripple pattern).
5. Adds alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo) to the weld.
6. Indicates the welding technique (uniform, easily detachable slag = good practice).

#### 2.5.6 The Phenomenon of **Arc Blow**
A magnetic disturbance in the arc caused by **uneven magnetic fields** around the welding circuit (especially in DC welding of ferromagnetic materials).
- **Causes:** Earth magnetism, current flow in workpiece, residual magnetism, poor ground location.
- **Consequence:** Arc deflects sideways → uneven penetration, porosity, spatter, undercut.
- **Remedies:**
  - Use **AC** (no net magnetic field).
  - Place ground clamp as close as possible to the arc.
  - Reduce current.
  - Weld towards a heavy tack weld or a thick section.
  - Wrap the workpiece cable around the part to balance flux.

#### 2.5.7 Advantages and Limitations of SMAW

| **Advantages** | **Limitations** |
|---|---|
| Lowest-cost equipment (portable AC transformer) | Low **deposition rate** (≈ 1–3 kg/h) |
| Portable; works outdoors (no shielding gas cylinder) | Slag must be chipped off between passes |
| All-position capability (with E6013, E7018) | Not suitable for reactive metals (Al, Ti, Mg) |
| Welds cast iron, mild steel, low-alloy, stainless | Fumes contain Mn, Cr, F, NOₓ — health hazard |
| Wide thickness range (1.5 mm to unlimited) | Weld quality depends heavily on welder's skill |
| Simple to learn; widely used in field/repair | Frequent electrode changes reduce arc time |
| Works on dirty/rusty surfaces (with cellulosic) | Limited to short weld lengths per electrode |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are MUST-KNOW for numerical problems in Part B (14 marks).**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **Heat Input** (per unit length) | **kJ/mm** | V = arc voltage (V), I = current (A), S = travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | $H = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ | Heat input using arc efficiency η (when S in m/min) | **kJ/m** | η = arc efficiency (fraction) |
| 3 | $D_{rate} = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot N}{4 \cdot t}$ | Deposition rate (multiple electrodes in time t) | **kg/h** | d = electrode Ø (m), L = length (m), ρ = density (kg/m³), N = no. of electrodes, t = time (h) |
| 4 | $R_{melt} = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot v_{e}}{4}$ | Electrode melting rate (volume basis) | **m³/s** | d = electrode Ø, vₑ = electrode feed rate |
| 5 | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | **Watts (W)** | V = arc voltage, I = current |
| 6 | $E_{total} = \eta \cdot P_{arc} \cdot t$ | Total heat energy transferred to plate | **Joules (J)** | t = welding time (s) |
| 7 | $F = \mu \cdot I$ | Lorentz force on metal droplet in GMAW (theory) | **N** | μ = permeability, I = current |
| 8 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by weld (sensible heat) | **J** | m = mass, c = specific heat, ΔT = temperature rise |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{60 \cdot E}{L}$ | Productivity | **m/h** | E = number of electrodes, L = bead length |

> **Arc Efficiency η (typical values — to memorise):**
> - **SMAW:** η ≈ **0.70 – 0.85** (commonly **0.80**)
> - **GMAW (CO₂):** η ≈ **0.80 – 0.85**
> - **GTAW:** η ≈ **0.50 – 0.70**
> - **SAW:** η ≈ **0.90 – 0.99**
> - **PAW:** η ≈ **0.60 – 0.70**
> - **Oxy-fuel (for comparison):** η ≈ **0.40 – 0.50**

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SMAW** is the **workhorse of construction sites, shipyards, pipelines, and field repair** because of portability and no-shielding-gas requirement.
- **GTAW** dominates **aerospace, nuclear, food-grade stainless, and root-pass welding** of pressure vessels and boiler tubes.
- **SAW** is the standard for **thick-plate fabrication** in pressure vessels, ship hulls, and structural steel because of its very high deposition rate and deep penetration.
- **PAW** is used in **aerospace thin-wall tubes** and **auto parts** (keyhole mode allows square-butt welds without edge prep).
