# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW). Mention any two advantages of GMAW over Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW).**

**Model Answer:**

GMAW is an arc welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating with an electric arc established between a **continuous, consumable solid wire electrode** and the workpiece, with **shielding obtained from an externally supplied gas**.

**Two advantages over SMAW:**
1. **Higher productivity:** Continuous wire feeding eliminates the frequent stops needed to change stick electrodes in SMAW, yielding 3–4× higher deposition rate.
2. **Cleaner welds:** GMAW produces a slag-free weld bead (with solid wire), eliminating the need for deslagging between passes and reducing post-weld cleaning time.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List the four modes of metal transfer in GMAW. Which mode is most suitable for welding thin sheets and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The four modes of metal transfer in GMAW are:
1. **Short-circuit (Dip) transfer**
2. **Globular transfer**
3. **Axial Spray transfer**
4. **Pulsed Spray transfer**

**Short-circuit (Dip) transfer** is most suitable for thin sheets because:
- It operates at **low current (< 200 A)** and **low voltage (17–22 V)**, producing **low heat input**.
- Low heat input prevents **burn-through** of thin material.
- It is suitable for **all welding positions** (overhead, vertical, horizontal) due to the small, controllable weld pool.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ➤ QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) process with a neat block diagram of the equipment setup. Describe the functions of the **constant voltage power source** and **wire feed unit**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **four modes of metal transfer** in GMAW with sketches. For each mode, state the typical current range, shielding gas, and one application.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**GMAW Process Explanation:**

Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW), also known as **MIG/MAG welding**, is an arc welding process in which a continuous solid wire electrode is fed automatically through a welding gun while an electric arc is maintained between the wire tip and the workpiece. The arc melts both the electrode wire (which becomes filler metal) and the base metal. Simultaneously, an externally supplied shielding gas (inert like Ar, or active like CO₂) flows through the gun nozzle to protect the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Block Diagram of Equipment:**

```
[Shielding Gas Cylinder] → [Regulator/Flowmeter] → [Gas Hose] → [Welding Gun]
                                                                          ↓
[Spool of Wire] → [Wire Feed Unit] → [Conduit] → [Contact Tip in Gun]      ↓
                                                                          ↓
                                                  [Electric Arc]           ↓
                                                  [Molten Pool] ←──────────┘
                                                                          ↓
[Constant Voltage Power Source (DCEP)] ←── [Ground Cable] ←── [Workpiece Clamp]
```

**Functions of Constant Voltage Power Source:**
1. Delivers a **stable, regulated DC voltage** (typically 18–40 V) to the arc, irrespective of small load fluctuations.
2. Provides **DCEP polarity** (electrode positive) which concentrates ~70% of the heat on the workpiece, giving deep penetration.
3. Enables **self-regulation of arc length**: if arc length shortens, voltage drops momentarily → CV source increases current → excess wire burns back → arc length is restored. This automatic feedback control is the hallmark of GMAW.

**Functions of Wire Feed Unit:**
1. **Continuously drives** the consumable wire from the spool through the gun conduit at a preset **wire feed speed (WFS)** in m/min.
2. Maintains **constant wire feed rate** for stable arc current.
3. The **drive rolls** (knurled steel) grip the wire without slipping or deforming it; tension is adjustable.
4. Modern wire feeders include **digital speed displays** and **synergic control** where voltage is auto-adjusted to match WFS.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

| Mode | Current Range | Voltage | Shielding Gas | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit (Dip)** | 50–200 A | 17–22 V | Ar/CO₂ mix | Wire touches pool; pinch-off at ~100 Hz | Thin sheets, all positions |
| **Globular** | 150–300 A | 22–28 V | CO₂ (100%) | Large drops fall by gravity | Thick steel, flat only |
| **Axial Spray** | 250–500 A | 26–35 V | Ar-rich (98% Ar + 2% O₂) | Fine droplets stream axially | Thick sections, high speed |
| **Pulsed Spray** | Pulsed: peak 300–500 A; background 50–100 A | 22–28 V | Ar/He mix | Pulsed peak detaches one drop per pulse | All-position, precise control |

**Detailed Mechanisms:**

- **Short-Circuit:** The wire makes contact (short circuit) → current surges due to short → electromagnetic pinch force necks and detaches the droplet → arc re-strikes → cycle repeats 60–200 times/sec. Produces a **characteristic "frying" sound**.

- **Globular:** Droplets grow larger than wire diameter (1.5–2× d) before detaching. The transfer is **non-axial and gravity-assisted**, hence restricted to flat position. High spatter due to random droplet paths.

- **Axial Spray:** Once current exceeds the **transition current** ($I_t = 50 + 20d^2$), the electromagnetic pinch force detaches small droplets (smaller than wire diameter) in a steady axial stream at thousands per second. **Stainless and spatter-free** weld; flat position only due to large fluid pool.

- **Pulsed Spray:** A **background current** maintains the arc; a **pulsed peak current** (above $I_t$, ~2 ms duration) detaches one droplet per pulse. Lower average heat than continuous spray → enables overhead/vertical welding on thicker plates.

---

### ➤ QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of shielding gases** in GMAW. Compare the use of **100% Argon, 100% CO₂, and Ar + 25% CO₂ mixture**, highlighting bead profile, spatter level, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A GMAW operation uses the following parameters: $V = 30$ V, $I = 280$ A, travel speed = $5$ mm/s, wire diameter = $1.2$ mm, wire feed speed = $7$ m/min, arc efficiency = $0.85$, and density of steel = $7850$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) Heat input per unit length in kJ/mm, (ii) Deposition rate in kg/h. State two practical reasons why **constant-voltage polarity DCEP** is preferred over DCEN for GMAW.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Role of Shielding Gases in GMAW:**

Shielding gases serve **three primary roles**:
1. **Exclude atmospheric gases** (O₂, N₂, H₂) from the arc and weld pool, preventing porosity, embrittlement, and oxidation.
2. **Stabilize the arc** by providing ionizable medium with appropriate ionization potential.
3. **Influence weld metallurgy and bead profile** by their active or inert chemical behaviour.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | **100% Argon (Ar)** | **100% CO₂** | **Ar + 25% CO₂ (Corgon)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classification | Inert | Active | Semi-active |
| Bead Profile | Convex, "humped", poor wetting on steel | Flat to concave, good wetting | Smooth, slightly convex, good wetting |
| Spatter Level | Very low | **High** | Low to moderate |
| Arc Stability | Very stable (smooth hum) | Harsh, unstable | Stable, crisp |
| Penetration | Deep on aluminium, **poor on steel** | **Deep on steel** | Good on steel |
| Cost | High (Ar) | Low | Moderate |
| Typical Application | Aluminium, copper, stainless steel | Carbon steel, structural fabrication | **Mild & low-alloy steel (most common)** |

**Why Ar + 25% CO₂ is the most used:**
It balances the **deep penetration of CO₂** with the **arc stability and low spatter of Argon** — making it the workhorse shielding gas for general mild steel fabrication in industries.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
$V = 30$ V, $I = 280$ A, $S = 5$ mm/s, $d = 1.2$ mm, $WFS = 7$ m/min, $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

---

**(i) Heat input per unit length (kJ/mm):**

First, convert travel speed to mm/min:

$$S = 5 \ \text{mm/s} \times 60 = 300 \ \text{mm/min}$$

Apply the heat input formula:

$$H = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

Substitute values:

$$H = \frac{0.85 \times 30 \times 280 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$0.85 \times 30 = 25.5$$

$$25.5 \times 280 = 7140$$

$$7140 \times 60 = 428{,}400$$

Denominator:

$$300 \times 1000 = 300{,}000$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,H = \frac{428{,}400}{300{,}000} = 1.428 \ \text{kJ/mm}\,}$$

---

**(ii) Deposition rate in kg/h:**

First, convert wire diameter to metres:

$$d = 1.2 \ \text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$$

Cross-sectional area of wire:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

Volume of wire fed per minute:

$$V_{wire/min} = A \times WFS = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 7 = 7.917 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Mass of wire per minute:

$$m = V_{wire/min} \times \rho = 7.917 \times 10^{-6} \times 7850 = 0.06215 \ \text{kg/min}$$

Apply deposition efficiency:

$$R_d = 0.85 \times 0.06215 = 0.05283 \ \text{kg/min}$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$R_d = 0.05283 \times 60 = 3.17 \ \text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_d \approx 3.17 \ \text{kg/h}\,}$$

---

**Two practical reasons why DCEP (Electrode Positive) is preferred over DCEN in GMAW:**

1. **Deep penetration:** In DCEP, electrons flow from the workpiece (cathode) to the electrode (anode). Since ~70% of arc heat is generated at the anode side, the workpiece (anode in DCEP) receives maximum heating → deep penetration into the base metal. DCEN concentrates heat on the wire tip, causing excessive wire melting with poor workpiece fusion.

2. **Stable arc and reduced spatter:** DCEP produces a stable, focused arc with good cathodic cleaning action on the workpiece surface (oxide layer removal). DCEN produces a wandering, unstable arc with excessive spatter and poor weld bead shape on steel.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GMAW Questions — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60 in the heat input formula** → Many students write $H = V \times I / S$ directly. Remember: when $S$ is in mm/min and you want $H$ in J/mm, you **must multiply by 60** to convert minutes to seconds. This alone can cost you 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Conflating MIG and MAG** → Examiners check whether you correctly identify inert (Ar, He) vs active (CO₂, O₂) gases. Writing *"MIG uses CO₂"* is a definite mark-deduction.
> 
> 3. **Saying "GMAW uses constant current"** → **WRONG.** GMAW uses a **constant voltage (CV)** power source. Mixing this up with SMAW (which uses CC) is a classic blunder.
> 
> 4. **Wrong polarity** → Always state **DCEP** for GMAW on steel; using DCEN is incorrect for general-purpose work.
> 
> 5. **Units of deposition rate** → Be consistent: if the question asks kg/h, don't give kg/min or g/s without conversion.
> 
> 6. **No block diagram in long answers** → In 14-mark Part B questions, **always draw a labelled block diagram** of the equipment setup. It fetches at least 2–3 marks on its own.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the self-regulating arc concept** → If the question mentions "constant voltage," the examiner expects an explanation of the self-regulating feedback loop. Skipping this is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **GMAW** = Gas Metal Arc Welding = continuous consumable solid wire + external gas shielding.
- ✅ Other names: **MIG** (inert gas), **MAG** (active gas).
- ✅ **Power source:** Constant Voltage (CV), **DCEP polarity**.
- ✅ **Electrode:** Continuous solid consumable wire (e.g., ER70S-6 for mild steel).
- ✅ **Shielding gases:** Ar (inert, for non-ferrous), CO₂ (active, for steel), Ar + CO₂ mix (workhorse for steel).
- ✅ **Four metal transfer modes:** Short-circuit (low heat, all-position), Globular (CO₂, flat only), Spray (high heat, flat only), Pulsed Spray (controlled, all-position).
- ✅ **Self-regulating arc:** CV source + WFS gives automatic arc-length stability.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = \eta V I \times 60 / (S \times 1000)$ in kJ/mm.
- ✅ **Deposition rate:** $R_d = \eta \times (\pi d^2 / 4) \times WFS \times \rho$.
- ✅ **Transition current:** $I_t = 50 + 20 d^2$ Amps (for spray transfer onset).
- ✅ **Penetration is governed by current; bead width by voltage.**
- ✅ **GMAW deposition efficiency: 90–95%** (one of the highest).
- ✅ **Cannot be used outdoors** (wind disturbs shielding gas).
- ✅ **Industry applications:** Automotive, shipbuilding, pressure vessels, structural steel, pipelines.
- ✅ **Compared to SMAW:** Higher productivity, cleaner weld, easier automation, higher initial cost.
- ✅ **Compared to GTAW:** Faster, lower skill, slightly lower precision.
- ✅ **Compared to SAW:** Less deposition rate, but **all-position** capability and no flux handling.
- ✅ **Standard wire diameter range:** 0.6 mm to 1.6 mm; **shielding gas flow:** 10–20 L/min.
- ✅ **Arc temperature:** ~6000–8000 °C.

> 🎯 **One-Line Mantra for Exam Hall:** *"GMAW = Continuous Wire + External Gas + Constant Voltage + DCEP + Self-Regulating Arc."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Resistance Welding (RW)** is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for coalescence is produced by the resistance offered to the flow of an electric current through the workpieces held under pressure. The heat generated at the interface is governed by **Joule's Law of Heating**, while forge pressure is simultaneously applied to forge the softened metal into a continuous monolithic joint.

According to the **American Welding Society (AWS)** and the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCPET402, resistance welding is classified as a *solid-state / fusion hybrid pressure welding process* under the broader umbrella of *electric resistance welding (ERW)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Resistance welding is covered as a non-conventional fusion-coalescence process where heat generation, pressure application, and metallurgical bonding occur in a fraction of a second (typically **0.04 s to 4 s**), making it the most dominant sheet-metal joining process in the automotive and white-goods industry.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Resistance Welding

For the joint to form successfully, **three simultaneous conditions** must be satisfied:

1. **Adequate Heat Generation** — sufficient to bring the interface to a plastic / molten state.
2. **Forging Pressure** — applied to displace the molten/softened metal and create metallurgical bonding.
3. **Controlled Cooling** — to develop a fine-grained, ductile weld nugget.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toaster-Sandwich Model"

Imagine a pair of bread slices stacked together. You place a heated iron plate on top (the **electrodes**) and press it down (**forging force**). Heat flows from the plate into the bread, and where the two slices touch, a thin layer becomes soft and merges with the layer below it. The pressing squashes the softened interface into a single fused layer.

**Mapping the Analogy to Real Welding:**

| Analogy Element | Real Resistance Welding Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Heated iron plate | Water-cooled **copper electrodes** |
| Pressing the iron down | **Electrode force / forge pressure** |
| Heat conducted into bread | **I²R Joule heating** through the metal |
| Fusion of two bread slices | Formation of the **weld nugget** |
| Time iron is held in place | **Weld time (t)** in seconds/cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is resistance welding so widely used?**
> Because it is **self-regulating** to a large extent, **operator-skill-independent**, easily automated, produces **no fumes or spatter**, and gives repeatable quality — making it ideal for mass-production lines (e.g., a single car body has approximately **4,000 – 5,000 resistance spot welds**).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Magnitude of current (I) | **5,000 – 20,000 A** (low voltage, high amperage) |
| Open-circuit voltage | **2 – 12 V** |
| Weld time (t) | **0.04 s – 4 s** (in 50 Hz supply → 2 to 200 cycles) |
| Electrode force (F) | **1.5 – 10 kN** (depending on sheet thickness) |
| Electrode material | Class I, II, III **RWMA copper alloys** |
| Cooling | Water-cooled copper electrodes |
| Joint efficiency | **85 – 100 %** of parent metal |
| Heat generation efficiency (η) | **50 – 80 %** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Physics — Joule's Law of Heating

When an electric current **I** (in amperes) flows through a resistance **R** (in ohms) for a time **t** (in seconds), the heat generated **H** (in joules) is:

$$H = I^{2} R t$$

This single equation is the **heart of resistance welding**. Everything else in the process — electrode design, timing schedules, weld schedules, lobe diagrams — is built around it.

### 2.2 The Three Resistances in Series

The total resistance **R_total** encountered by the current as it travels from one electrode to the other is **NOT just the resistance of the workpiece**. It is the sum of **five individual resistances**:

$$R_{total} = 2R_{e} + 2R_{c} + R_{w}$$

Where:
- $R_{e}$ = Resistance of each **electrode** (copper)
- $R_{c}$ = Resistance at each **electrode–workpiece contact** interface
- $R_{w}$ = Resistance of the **workpiece** itself (sum of bulk resistance and the faying-surface contact resistance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> At the start of the weld cycle, the bulk of heat is generated at the **faying surface contact resistance** ($R_c$ faying interface) because the two sheets are cold, the contact area is small, and therefore the resistance is maximum. As the metal heats and softens, this resistance **collapses**, and heat then shifts toward the bulk of the workpiece. This dynamic is what creates the characteristic **doughnut / nugget shape**.

### 2.3 The Heat Balance Equation (Energy Audit)

The total heat generated is partitioned as follows:

$$H_{total} = I^{2} R t = H_{melt} + H_{loss}$$

Expanding further:

$$I^{2} R t = (m \, C_{p} \, \Delta T + m \, L_{f}) + H_{rad} + H_{cond} + H_{elec}$$

Where:
- $m \, C_{p} \, \Delta T$ = Sensible heat to raise the metal from ambient to melting point
- $m \, L_{f}$ = Latent heat of fusion (actually **NOT** fully required in spot welding because forge pressure expels molten metal — a common exam trap!)
- $H_{rad}$, $H_{cond}$ = Radiative and conductive losses to electrodes and surroundings
- $H_{elec}$ = Heat lost by conduction into the water-cooled electrodes (the **biggest** loss term)

### 2.4 Process Efficiency

The **thermal efficiency** of resistance welding is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{H_{useful}}{H_{total}} = \frac{I^{2}R_{useful}\,t}{I^{2}R_{total}\,t} = \frac{R_{useful}}{R_{total}}$$

Typical efficiencies:
- **Spot welding:** η ≈ **50 – 80 %**
- **Projection welding:** η ≈ **70 – 85 %**
- **Flash welding:** η ≈ **90 – 95 %** (highest, because arc heat is concentrated)

### 2.5 Classification of Resistance Welding Processes (KTU-Mandatory)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following six sub-processes:

| # | Process | Heat Source / Mode | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spot Welding (RSW)** | Localized point heat | Sheet metal lap joints (car bodies, cabinets) |
| 2 | **Seam Welding (RSEW)** | Continuous rolling electrodes | Fuel tanks, radiators, drums |
| 3 | **Projection Welding (RPW)** | Heat concentrated at pre-formed projections | Nuts to plates, cross-wire welding |
| 4 | **Butt Welding (RBW)** | Direct resistance through butted ends | Rods, bars, wires, rails |
| 5 | **Flash Welding (RFW)** | Arc + resistance (with flashing) | Automotive axles, bandsaw blades |
| 6 | **Percussion Welding** | Rapid capacitor discharge arc | Studs to plates, dissimilar metals |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = I^{2} R t$ | Joule's heat generated | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $R_{total} = 2R_{e} + 2R_{c} + R_{w}$ | Sum of all resistances in current path | Ohms (Ω) |
| 3 | $P = F/A$ | Electrode pressure / forging pressure | MPa or N/mm² |
| 4 | $J = I/A_{nugget}$ | Current density at nugget | A/mm² |
| 5 | $\eta = R_{useful} / R_{total}$ | Process thermal efficiency | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | $Q = I^{2}R$ | Rate of heat generation | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance of a conductor of length L, area A | Ω |
| 8 | $d_{nugget} = k \sqrt{t}$ | Empirical nugget diameter growth | mm |
| 9 | $F_{min} = 0.8 \, \sigma_y \, A_{nugget}$ | Minimum forge force to expel molten metal | N |
| 10 | $H_{req} = \pi \, r^{2} \, t_{s} \, \rho \, (C_p \Delta T + L_f)$ | Heat required to melt a nugget of radius *r* | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The $H = I^{2}Rt$ equation is not just academic. It is embedded in every **weld controller / timer** in industry. The technician inputs current (kA), time (cycles), and electrode force (kN); the inbuilt microprocessor uses this equation to compute and regulate the actual heat delivered — a direct industrial application of KTU Module 2 theory.

### 2.7 Heat Generation Distribution in Spot Welding — The Doughnut Effect

The current density at the electrode-workpiece interface is maximum at the **edge of the electrode contact circle**, because current lines converge there. This results in **higher heat generation at the periphery** and a **cooler center**, producing the characteristic **annular (doughnut-shaped) weld nugget** in resistance spot welding — a frequently tested KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Required to Form a Weld Nugget

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Problem):**
Two sheets, each of thickness **t_s**, are to be joined by spot welding. Derive an expression for the **minimum heat required** to form a cylindrical weld nugget of radius **r**, given:
- Specific heat $C_p$
- Latent heat of fusion $L_f$
- Density of workpiece $\rho$
- Melting temperature $T_m$
- Ambient temperature $T_0$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Nugget:**
Model the weld nugget as a cylinder of radius **r** and height equal to the sum of two sheet thicknesses, $2t_s$.

$$V_{nugget} = \pi r^{2} (2t_s)$$

**Step 2 — Mass of the Nugget:**
Multiply volume by density to obtain mass.

$$m_{nugget} = \rho \cdot V_{nugget} = 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho$$

**Step 3 — Heat to Raise Temperature to Melting Point (Sensible Heat):**

$$H_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)$$

**Step 4 — Heat to Convert Solid to Liquid (Latent Heat):**

$$H_{latent} = m \cdot L_f = 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, L_f$$

**Step 5 — Total Useful Heat Required:**

$$\boxed{H_{required} = 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, \bigl[\,C_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \,\bigr]}$$

**Step 6 — Practical Observation:**
In actual spot welding, the molten metal is **expelled from the nugget center** by the forging pressure. Therefore, in practice, the **full latent heat is NOT required**. The empirical correction gives an effective heat:

$$H_{effective} \approx 0.8 \cdot 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, \bigl[\,C_p (T_m - T_0) + 0.5 L_f \,\bigr]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This derivation is the foundation of all **weld-lobe diagrams** in industry. The heat available ($I^2Rt$) is set equal to this $H_{required}$ — and the resulting inequality gives the operating envelope of current vs. time for a given electrode force.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Current, Time, and Electrode Force (The Weld-Lobe Foundation)

**Problem Setup:**
Given that the heat required is $H_{req}$ and the heat available is $H_{avail} = I^{2} R_{total} \, t$, and assuming that electrode force **F** is proportional to sheet thickness **t_s** (Rule of thumb: $F \approx 4 \, t_s$ in kN, where $t_s$ is in mm), derive the relationship between **I, t, and F**.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Setting Heat Available = Heat Required (with efficiency factor):**

$$\eta \cdot I^{2} R_{total} \, t = 2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, \bigl[\,C_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \,\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Current Required:**

$$I = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi r^{2} t_s \rho \, \bigl[\,C_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \,\bigr]}{\eta \, R_{total} \, t}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that for a given electrode geometry and material, $R_{total}$ is approximately constant during the weld:**

$$I \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{t}}$$

This means: **to achieve the same nugget size, a doubling of weld time allows a current reduction by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$**.

**Step 4 — Empirical Forge Force Correlation (AWS / RWMA Standard):**

$$F_{electrode} \approx 4 \cdot t_s \quad \text{(kN, for mild steel)}$$

For stainless steel and high-strength alloys, the multiplier increases to 6 – 8.

$$\boxed{I \cdot \sqrt{t} = k \cdot t_s^{0.75}}$$

This is the famous **heat-input parameter** used by weld-control engineers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrode Pressure and the Concept of Contact Resistance Collapse

**Step 1 — Resistance of the Faying Surface Contact:**

For two nominally flat metal surfaces in contact, the actual contact occurs at a few **asperities (microscopic peaks)**. The contact resistance is given by Holm's equation:

$$R_{c} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 a}$$

where *a* is the effective radius of the contact spot, and $\rho$ is the resistivity.

**Step 2 — Relationship Between Pressure and Asperity Deformation:**
The number and area of asperity contacts increase with force F. Empirically:

$$a \propto \sqrt{F}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$R_{c} \propto \dfrac{\rho}{2 \sqrt{F}} = \dfrac{k}{\sqrt{F}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{c} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Consequence:**
Higher electrode force → lower contact resistance → lower heat concentration at faying surface → heat shifts deeper into bulk → weld nugget grows inward.

This is the principle behind **post-heat / forge cycles** in multi-pulse welding schedules.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Resistance Welding Variants (KTU Exam Ready)

| Parameter | Spot Welding | Seam Welding | Projection Welding | Flash Welding | Butt Welding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current (kA) | 5 – 20 | 5 – 15 | 8 – 25 | 20 – 200 | 5 – 50 |
| Weld time (s) | 0.1 – 2 | Continuous | 0.2 – 1.5 | 5 – 30 | 1 – 10 |
| Electrode force (kN) | 1.5 – 10 | 2 – 8 | 3 – 20 | 50 – 500 | 20 – 300 |
| Efficiency η | 50 – 80 % | 60 – 80 % | 70 – 85 % | 90 – 95 % | 80 – 90 % |
| Heat source | Resistance | Resistance | Resistance (at projections) | Arc + resistance | Resistance |
| Joint type | Lap | Lap | Lap | Butt | Butt |
| Typical use | Auto bodies | Tanks | Nut-to-plate | Axles | Wire/rod |
| Special feature | Single nugget | Continuous seam | Multiple nuggets | Material loss (flash) | Upset forging |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Problem Style)

**Question:**
Two mild-steel sheets, each **1.5 mm thick**, are to be joined by spot welding. The current used is **8000 A** and the total resistance at the interface is **120 μΩ**. Weld time is **0.2 s**. Electrode force is **4 kN**, electrode tip diameter is **6 mm**.

Calculate:
1. Heat generated per weld
2. Power developed
3. Current density at the electrode tip
4. Electrode pressure in MPa

**Solution:**

**(1) Heat generated:**

$$H = I^{2} R t = (8000)^{2} \times (120 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2$$

$$H = 64{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00012 \times 0.2$$

$$H = 1536 \text{ J}$$

**(2) Power developed:**

$$P = I^{2} R = (8000)^{2} \times 120 \times 10^{-6} = 7680 \text{ W} \approx 7.68 \text{ kW}$$

**(3) Current density at electrode tip:**
Electrode tip area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (6)^{2} = 28.27 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$J = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{8000}{28.27} = 283 \text{ A/mm}^{2}$$

**(4) Electrode pressure:**

$$p = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{4000}{28.27} = 141.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Validity check:** All values lie within the standard RWMA Class II envelope — joint is expected to be sound.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resistance Spot Welding — Process Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Power Supply 50 Hz AC] --> B[Step 2: Step-Down Welding Transformer]
    B --> C[Step 3: Secondary Current 5000 to 20000 A]
    C --> D[Step 4: Upper Copper Electrode]
    D --> E[Step 5: Upper Workpiece Sheet]
    E --> F[Step 6: Faying Surface Contact Resistance]
    F --> G[Step 7: Lower Workpiece Sheet]
    G --> H[Step 8: Lower Copper Electrode]
    H --> I[Step 9: Water Cooling Circuit]
    F --> J[Step 10: Weld Nugget Formation]
    J --> K[Step 11: Forge Pressure Application]
    K --> L[Step 12: Forged Weld Nugget with Cast Structure]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Generation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        A1[Mains 415 V 50 Hz] --> A2[Contactor SCR or IGBT]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL STAGE"]
        A2 --> B1[Weld Timer Microprocessor]
        B1 --> B2[Squeeze Time Setting]
        B1 --> B3[Weld Time Setting]
        B1 --> B4[Hold Time Setting]
        B1 --> B5[Off Time Setting]
    end
    subgraph POWER["POWER STAGE"]
        B3 --> C1[Welding Transformer 415 V to 4 to 12 V]
        C1 --> C2[Secondary Busbars Heavy Copper]
    end
    subgraph WELDING["WELDING STATION"]
        C2 --> D1[Upper Electrode Class II Cu Alloy]
        D1 --> D2[Workpiece Lap Joint]
        D2 --> D3[Lower Electrode Class II Cu Alloy]
        D3 --> E1[Weld Nugget Forms]
    end
    subgraph COOLING["COOLING AND FEEDBACK"]
        D1 --> F1[Water Inlet 15 to 25 C]
        D3 --> F1
        F1 --> F2[Water Outlet Temp Monitored]
        E1 --> G1[Load Cell Force Feedback]
        E1 --> G2[Current Shunt or Rogowski Coil]
    end
    G1 --> B1
    G2 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Selection of Resistance Welding Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Joint to be made] --> Q1{Geometry lap or butt}
    Q1 -->|Lap joint| Q2{Single point or continuous}
    Q2 -->|Single point| Q3{Need multiple points simultaneously}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SP[Projection Welding]
    Q3 -->|No| RSW[Spot Welding]
    Q2 -->|Continuous seam| SEAM[Seam Welding]
    Q1 -->|Butt joint| Q4{Cross section type}
    Q4 -->|Small wire or rod| RBW[Butt Welding]
    Q4 -->|Large section with flash material loss acceptable| RFW[Flash Welding]
    Q4 -->|Capacitor discharge required for dissimilar metals| PCW[Percussion Welding]
```

### 4.4 Weld Lobe Diagram — Conceptual Map (KTU Hot Topic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOBE["WELD LOBE DIAGRAM I versus t"]
        A1[High I short t leads to Expulsion]
        A2[Optimum Region forms Sound Nugget]
        A3[Low I short t leads to Cold Weld or Stick]
        A4[Optimum I long t also Sound Nugget]
        A5[Very low I long t leads to Excessive Heat Loss and Cold Weld]
        A1 --> A2
        A2 --> A3
        A3 --> A4
        A4 --> A5
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the Lobe:** For a **fixed electrode force**, the operating window (current vs. time) that produces an acceptable weld is enclosed within an upper **expulsion limit curve** and a lower **cold-weld limit curve**. Increasing force shifts both curves upward, expanding the process window — this is why high-quality weld schedules always specify force as the primary variable.

### 4.5 Sequence of Operations in a Spot Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Squeeze Time ts 0.05 to 0.5 s] --> S2[Weld Time tw 0.04 to 2 s]
    S2 --> S3[Hold Time th 0.05 to 1 s]
    S3 --> S4[Off Time toff 0 s continuous]
    S4 --> S5[Next Weld Pulse if Multi Pulse]
```

- **Squeeze Time** – delay between electrode contact and current flow; lets the forging force stabilize.
- **Weld Time** – duration of current flow (heat generation).
- **Hold Time** – current OFF, pressure maintained; allows nugget to solidify under pressure.
- **Off Time** – cooling interval before next cycle (only in multi-pulse schedules).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

*State Joule's Law of Heating as applied to resistance welding. List any four process parameters that influence the quality of a resistance spot weld.*

**Model Answer:**

Joule's Law states that the heat generated when an electric current flows through a resistance is:

$$H = I^{2} R t$$

where *H* = heat in joules, *I* = current in amperes, *R* = total resistance in ohms, *t* = time of current flow in seconds.

**Four process parameters influencing spot-weld quality:**

1. **Welding current (I)** — controls heat input; too low → cold weld, too high → expulsion/splatter.
2. **Weld time (t)** — determines the duration of heat generation; influences nugget growth.
3. **Electrode force (F)** — controls contact resistance and forge pressure; affects nugget density.
4. **Electrode material and tip geometry** — affects current density distribution and heat removal.

(Mentioning surface condition, sheet thickness, and cooling rate can also fetch full marks.)

---

**Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

*Compare resistance spot welding and resistance seam welding based on (i) electrode type, (ii) joint continuity, (iii) current flow pattern, and (iv) typical application.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Spot Welding | Seam Welding |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Electrode type | Stationary pointed/tapered copper electrodes | **Roll electrodes** that rotate continuously |
| (ii) Joint continuity | **Discrete, individual nuggets** at each weld point | **Continuous liquid-tight seam** |
| (iii) Current flow | Single pulse of current per weld | **Intermittent (roll-spot) or continuous** pulsed current |
| (iv) Typical application | Car body panels, cabinets, frames | Fuel tanks, milk cans, radiators, drums |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question B(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-Mod-2-Prob-7]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **resistance spot welding process**. Discuss the formation of the **weld nugget** and explain why it has a **doughnut / annular shape** in low-carbon steel.

**(b)** Two sheets of **low-carbon steel**, each **1.2 mm thick**, are spot-welded using a current of **7,500 A** for **0.25 s**. The total resistance measured at the interface is **150 μΩ**. Calculate:
- (i) Total heat generated per weld
- (ii) Power developed
- (iii) If the electrode tip diameter is **5 mm**, calculate the current density and electrode pressure when the electrode force is **3.5 kN**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Description and Nugget Formation:**

In resistance spot welding (RSW), two or more overlapping sheets are clamped between a pair of water-cooled copper electrodes. A low-voltage, high-amperage current is passed through the assembly for a fraction of a second. Heat generated by **I²R losses** at the sheet-to-sheet interface raises the local metal temperature above its melting point, forming a molten pool called the **weld nugget**. Simultaneous forging pressure from the electrodes expels surface contaminants and oxide films, and forges the nugget into a solid, dense, fine-grained joint upon cooling.

**Why the doughnut shape?**

At the moment of current application, the **contact resistance at the faying surface is highest at the periphery of the electrode contact area**, because current lines converge there. Heat generation rate is proportional to $I^{2}R$, and since current density is greatest at the rim, more heat is liberated at the periphery. The centre of the contact zone is comparatively cooler due to:
- Direct heat conduction into the bulk electrode
- Lower local current density
- Greater heat-sink effect from the surrounding metal

Consequently, melting begins at the **annular periphery**, and as the cycle progresses, the molten ring grows **inward and downward**, ultimately producing a characteristic **ring-shaped (doughnut) nugget** — visible upon metallographic cross-sectioning.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total heat generated per weld:**

$$H = I^{2} R t = (7500)^{2} \times (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25$$

$$H = 56{,}250{,}000 \times 0.00015 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{H = 2109.4 \text{ J} \approx 2.11 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(ii) Power developed:**

$$P = I^{2} R = (7500)^{2} \times 150 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P = 56{,}250{,}000 \times 0.00015$$

$$\boxed{P = 8437.5 \text{ W} \approx 8.44 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Current density and electrode pressure:**

Electrode tip area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (5)^{2} = 19.63 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Current density:

$$J = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{7500}{19.63} = 382.0 \text{ A/mm}^{2}$$

Electrode pressure:

$$p = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{3500}{19.63} = 178.3 \text{ MPa}$$

All values are within the RWMA Class II envelope — joint expected to be **sound**.

---

**Question B(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **flash welding** and **upset butt welding** with respect to:
- (i) Heat source
- (ii) Material wastage
- (iii) Joint quality and inclusions
- (iv) Typical industrial applications

**(b)** In a **flash welding** operation on two **mild-steel bars of 25 mm diameter**, the current is **30 kA** and the flashing time is **8 s** at an average resistance of **75 μΩ**. Calculate the **heat generated during flashing**, and the **energy per unit volume** of the metal being joined.

Take bar density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, melting point considerations not required.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis:**

| Parameter | Flash Welding | Upset Butt Welding |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Heat source | **Arc + resistance** during flashing; followed by resistance upset | **Resistance heating only** |
| (ii) Material wastage | Significant (10–20 % material lost as flash/spatter) | **Minimal to none** |
| (iii) Joint quality | **Excellent** — flash action expels oxides and impurities | **Lower** — inclusions may remain trapped |
| (iv) Typical applications | Automotive axles, band-saw blades, drill bits | Wire, small-diameter rod, chain links |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Heat generated during flashing:

$$H = I^{2} R t = (30{,}000)^{2} \times (75 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8$$

$$H = 900{,}000{,}000 \times 0.000075 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{H = 540{,}000 \text{ J} = 540 \text{ kJ}}$$

Volume of two bars (per unit length, but here we take a length equivalent to the deformation zone — typically the **flash zone length** is given; otherwise, assume a 25 mm diameter × 50 mm affected length as a typical assumption, but since length is not specified in the problem, the answer for **H is 540 kJ** and the energy per unit volume cannot be computed without L).

**Refined Approach (if affected length is given, e.g., L = 50 mm):**

$$V = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} L = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} (0.05)$$

$$V = 4.91 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$\text{Energy per unit volume} = \dfrac{540{,}000}{4.91 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.10 \times 10^{10} \text{ J/m}^{3}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Resistance Welding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in formulas** — Writing $H = I^2Rt$ without stating J, A, Ω, s loses 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Not drawing the doughnut-shaped nugget** with proper labels in sketches — examiners explicitly award **2 marks** for a clean, labeled figure.
> 3. **Confusing forge pressure with electrode contact pressure** — The forge pressure is on the **weld nugget**, not on the electrode sheet.
> 4. **In numericals, not converting μΩ to Ω** — A classic trap. Students write $H = 7500^2 \times 150 \times 0.25$ and get astronomical numbers.
> 5. **Omitting the efficiency factor η** in the heat balance — full credit requires writing $\eta I^2Rt = H_{useful}$.
> 6. **Wrong classification of flash vs. butt welding** — Flash uses **arc + resistance**; butt uses **resistance only**. Mixing these up is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Skipping the reasoning behind the doughnut shape** — examiners want both the **physics** (current density at periphery) and the **thermal** (heat sinking at centre) explanation.
> 8. **Not mentioning RWMA electrode class** in process descriptions — a **1-mark loss** if omitted.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Resistance welding is governed by **Joule's Law**: $H = I^{2}Rt$.
- ✅ The total resistance is **five-fold**: $R_{total} = 2R_e + 2R_c + R_w$.
- ✅ Process efficiency $\eta$ ranges from **50 % (spot)** to **95 % (flash welding)**.
- ✅ Electrode material is **RWMA-classified copper alloy** (Class I, II, or III).
- ✅ Typical current range: **5 – 20 kA**; voltage: **2 – 12 V**; time: **0.04 – 4 s**.
- ✅ **Doughnut-shaped nugget** is due to non-uniform current density at the electrode contact.
- ✅ Six sub-processes: **Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt, Flash, Percussion** — must be memorised with distinguishing features.
- ✅ **Flash welding** has the **highest efficiency** and is used for **large cross-section butt joints**.
- ✅ **Seam welding** uses **roll electrodes** for **continuous liquid-tight joints**.
- ✅ **Projection welding** is preferred when **multiple welds** are needed at the **same station**.
- ✅ Electrode force for mild steel: **$F \approx 4 t_s$ kN** (with $t_s$ in mm).
- ✅ **Weld lobe diagram** defines the operating window of **current vs. time** for a given force.
- ✅ The cycle consists of **squeeze → weld → hold → off** (multi-pulse).
- ✅ Heat balance must consider **sensible + latent + conduction + radiation** losses.
- ✅ **Stainless steel and high-strength alloys require higher force** (multiplier 6 – 8) and use **RWMA Class III electrodes** (higher conductivity + hardness).
- ✅ In industry, **a single car body contains ~4000 – 5000 spot welds** — highlighting the industrial relevance of this process.
- ✅ Always convert **μΩ → Ω**, **mm² → m²**, and **mm → m** correctly in numericals to avoid the unit trap.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: RESISTANCE WELDING**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Slag Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electro Slag Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ELECTRO SLAG WELDING (ESW)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard)**
> **Electro Slag Welding (ESW)** is a *non-arc*, vertical-up, fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by the heat generated due to the **resistance offered by a molten conductive slag pool** to the passage of a heavy electric current passing between a continuously fed **consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The molten slag — held between the base metal and water-cooled copper dams — melts the edges of the joint and the filler electrode, producing a weld in a **single vertical pass** suitable for thick sections typically from **25 mm to 450 mm**.

The process was developed in **1952** at the **E.O. Paton Institute of Electric Welding, Kiev, USSR (Ukraine)**, and was commercialised in the West in the 1960s. It is governed by standards such as **AWS C5.5/A5.5** and **ISO 14171**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **"The Underground Furnace" Analogy**
> Imagine you want to melt a deep, vertical crack in a thick ice wall from the **bottom upward** using heat. Instead of an open flame (which would dissipate), you pour a **layer of electrically conductive salt water (slag)** into the crack and run electricity through it. The water resists the current, gets hot, melts the ice above it, and as the ice melts, more water rises, and the molten puddle moves upward — leaving behind solid, frozen (welded) metal behind it.
>
> In ESW, the **conductive molten slag** is the "salt water," the **electric current** is the energy source, and the **molten metal pool** trapped below the slag is what freezes into a solid weld bead as the assembly moves up. There is **no arc** in steady state — only **Joule (resistance) heating**.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Identity of ESW (Why it is NOT Arc Welding)

| Property | Conventional Arc Welding (e.g., SAW) | Electro Slag Welding (ESW) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Sustained electric arc | Resistance (Joule) heating of slag |
| Shielding | Flux + gas blanket | Molten slag pool (conductive) |
| Position | All positions (with variation) | **Vertical-up only** |
| Thickness | Multi-pass for thick plate | **Single pass up to 450 mm** |
| Slag state | Granular powder | Fully molten liquid bath |

> [!TIP] **Key KTU Buzzword to Memorise**
> *"In ESW, the welding heat is generated by the **I²R** loss in the molten slag, not by an arc."* — This single sentence carries **2 marks** in any Part A question.

### 1.4 Standard Physical / Process Metrics

- **Welding Current Range:** **400 A – 900 A** (DC, electrode positive)
- **Welding Voltage Range:** **35 V – 55 V**
- **Slag Pool Temperature:** Approximately **1900 °C – 2300 °C** (well above melting point of steel, 1538 °C)
- **Molten Metal Pool Temperature:** Approximately **1700 °C – 1800 °C**
- **Heat Input:** **80 kJ/mm to 200 kJ/mm** (very high — causes coarse grain)
- **Deposition Rate:** **5 kg/hr to 25 kg/hr** (much higher than SAW)
- **Travel Speed:** **10 mm/min to 30 mm/min** (very slow)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The process is initiated in three distinct phases, each critical for KTU exam answers:

### Phase I: Starting (Arc Initiation)
1. A small amount of **starting flux (slag-making powder)** is placed at the root gap between the two thick plates to be joined.
2. The **consumable electrode wire** is lowered until it touches the backing plate (or a starting block).
3. An **arc is struck momentarily** — this is the **only arc** in the process.
4. The arc heat melts the starting flux, which then becomes a **conductive molten slag pool** of about **20 mm to 40 mm depth**.

### Phase II: Steady-State Welding (Resistance Heating)
1. As the slag becomes deep enough, the arc can no longer be sustained — it is **extinguished**.
2. From this moment, heat is generated purely by **I²R (Joule) heating** of the molten slag.
3. The current flows: **Electrode (+) → Molten Slag → Molten Metal Pool → Workpiece (-)**.
4. The intense slag heat **melts the filler wire continuously** as it is fed in.
5. The slag heat also **melts the edges of the base plates** (parent metal fusion).
6. The weld pool sits at the **bottom of the slag bath**, surrounded and protected by molten slag.
7. **Water-cooled copper dams (shoes)** slide upward on rails, mechanically containing the molten pool and slag, preventing run-out.
8. The electrode is fed **automatically at a constant rate** matched to its melting rate.
9. The entire head (electrode, feed rolls, slag pool) travels **vertically upward** at the set welding speed.

### Phase III: Solidification & Termination
1. As the head moves up, the **bottom of the weld pool freezes** into a solid weld bead.
2. The directional upward solidification produces a **columnar dendritic grain structure** aligned with the heat flow.
3. At the top, when the weld approaches completion, the slag pool is **gradually reduced** (current is tapered off) to fill the crater.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

> [!NOTE] **Cause-Effect Analysis (KTU Favourite)**

- **Why water-cooled copper dams?** — Copper has very high thermal conductivity (~400 W/m·K), so it rapidly extracts heat from the molten pool's edges, **preventing run-out** of molten metal and slag while acting as a mould.
- **Why no arc in steady state?** — A submerged arc would produce spatter, porosity, and unstable heat input. The resistance-heating mode gives **smooth, uniform, deep penetration**.
- **Why vertical-up only?** — Gravity holds the molten metal and slag in place against the dams. If the weld were horizontal or downward, the pool would simply fall out.
- **Why high heat input?** — Thick plates (≥25 mm) require **deep penetration** and the slag must remain molten throughout the long weld time; high current ensures this.
- **Why single pass?** — A single pass eliminates the **reheating and tempering effects** of multi-pass welds, but produces a **coarse-grained HAZ** — a known drawback.

## 2.3 Process Variables and Their Effects

| Variable | Typical Range | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|---|
| Welding Current (I) | 400 – 900 A | ↑ Penetration, ↑ deposition, ↑ heat input |
| Welding Voltage (V) | 35 – 55 V | ↑ Slag pool depth, ↑ heat in slag |
| Electrode Diameter (d) | 2.4 – 3.2 mm | ↑ Current capacity, ↑ deposition |
| Travel Speed (v) | 10 – 30 mm/min | ↓ Heat input, ↑ columnar grain refinement |
| Slag Depth (h_s) | 20 – 50 mm | ↑ Voltage drop, ↑ thermal reserve |
| Stand-off (electrode tip to pool) | 25 – 50 mm | Stability of process |
| Wire Feed Rate (W_f) | 100 – 400 mm/min | Matches current via melting rate |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Numerical/Descriptive Questions**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | Heat input | kJ/mm | Predicts HAZ grain size, distortion |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Welding power | W (or kW) | Sizing transformer/rectifier |
| 3 | $H_{s} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{h_{s}} \cdot k$ | Slag pool heat density | W/mm³ | Ensures slag stays molten |
| 4 | $Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Deposition rate | kg/hr | Productivity / cost estimation |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_{s} \cdot L}{A_{s}}$ | Slag resistance (Ohm's law) | Ohms | Determines V needed for given I |
| 6 | $t_{weld} = \dfrac{L_{plate}}{v}$ | Total welding time | minutes | Cycle time / production planning |
| 7 | $E_{melt} = m \cdot [c_{p}\Delta T + L_{f}]$ | Energy to melt metal | Joules | Heat balance check |
| 8 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{slag}$ | Voltage drop across slag | Volts | Slag acts as a resistor |
| 9 | $R_{r} = \dfrac{\text{Heat to melt}}{\text{Heat input}} \cdot 100$ | Thermal efficiency (%) | % | Comparison with SAW (~70%), ESW (~80%) |
| 10 | $P_{f} = \dfrac{m_{f}}{t}$ | Filler wire consumption rate | kg/hr | Material costing |

> **Where:**
> - $H$ = Heat input (kJ/mm), $\eta$ = Process efficiency (0.80–0.85 for ESW)
> - $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $v$ = Travel speed (mm/min)
> - $\rho$ = Density of filler (kg/mm³), $A$ = Cross-section of wire (mm²)
> - $\rho_{s}$ = Resistivity of molten slag (~0.4 Ω·cm), $L$ = Slag path length
> - $L_{plate}$ = Plate length to be welded, $m_f$ = filler mass used
> - $c_{p}$ = Specific heat, $L_{f}$ = Latent heat of fusion

## 2.5 Slag Chemistry (Flux Composition)

> [!TIP] **KTU 2-Mark Question: "Name the constituents of ESW flux."**

| Component | Typical % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CaF₂ (Calcium Fluoride / Fluorspar)** | **60 – 80 %** | Primary slag former, low melting point, removes S and P |
| **CaO (Lime)** | 10 – 20 % | Basicity, deoxidation |
| **Al₂O₃ (Alumina)** | 5 – 15 % | Increases slag viscosity, controls fluidity |
| **SiO₂ (Silica)** | 0 – 5 % | Fluxing agent (kept low to avoid Si pickup) |
| **MgO, MnO, FeO** | Trace | Minor metallurgical effects |

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Mistake**
> ESW flux is **fully melted and re-used** (it floats on the weld pool). Do NOT confuse it with SAW granular flux which is consumed. ESW flux is essentially a **recirculating molten salt bath**, not a consumable powder.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding** — hull plates, bulkheads (up to 100 mm)
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** — low-alloy steel rings
- **Wind turbine tower sections** — circumferential joints
- **Boiler drums and headers** — petrochemical industry
- **Heavy forgings and castings** — repair of large shafts
- **Bridge girders** — thick plate splicing
- **Hydraulic press cylinders** — repair welding

> The process is also extended to **Electroslag Surfacing (ESS)**, **Electroslag Cladding**, and **Electroslag Remelting (ESR)** for high-purity ingots — a related metallurgical process.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Input Equation (Most-Asked KTU Derivation)

The electrical energy supplied per unit length of weld is the primary design equation for any arc or resistance welding process.

**Step 1 — Define electrical power delivered to the welding zone:**

For a DC welding power source, the electrical power consumed at the weld is the product of arc/load voltage and current.

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

where $P$ is in watts, $V$ in volts, and $I$ in amperes.

**Step 2 — Account for process thermal efficiency:**

Not all electrical energy reaches the weld pool; some is lost to radiation, conduction through dams, convection in slag, and electrode extension. This is captured by a thermal efficiency $\eta$ (also called the heat transfer factor).

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

For **ESW, $\eta \approx 0.80$ to 0.85**; for SAW, $\eta \approx 0.70$.

**Step 3 — Convert power (W or J/s) to energy per unit weld length (kJ/mm):**

If the head travels at speed $v$ (mm/min), the time taken to weld 1 mm is $t = 1/v$ minutes $= 60/v$ seconds.

The heat input per millimetre of weld is:

$$
H = \dfrac{\text{Useful power} \times \text{time per mm}}{\text{length unit}}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot (60/v)}{1 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \ \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why divide by 1000?** — Watts × seconds = Joules; 1 kJ = 1000 J. The constant 60 converts minutes to seconds.

**Step 4 — Interpret for ESW (Plug Typical Values):**

Take $V = 45$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 20$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$
H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 45 \times 600}{20 \times 1000} = \dfrac{22950}{20000} = 1.1475 \ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

Converting to J/mm:

$$
H = 1147.5 \ \text{J/mm} \approx 1.15 \ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

This is **5–10× higher** than a typical arc welding process, which is why the HAZ of ESW welds is large and coarse-grained.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deposition Rate from Wire Feed

**Step 1 — Mass per unit length of filler wire:**

For a cylindrical wire of diameter $d$ (mm) and density $\rho$ (kg/mm³):

$$
m' = \rho \cdot A = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \quad \text{kg/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear feed rate (W_f) to mass rate:**

If the wire is fed at $W_f$ mm/min, the mass deposition per minute is:

$$
\dot{m} = m' \cdot W_f = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W_f \quad \text{kg/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/hr:**

$$
Q = \dot{m} \times 60 = 60 \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W_f \quad \text{kg/hr}
$$

For steel, $\rho \approx 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³, $d = 3.2$ mm, $W_f = 200$ mm/min:

$$
Q = 60 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{\pi \times 10.24}{4} \times 200
$$

$$
Q = 60 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.04 \times 200 = 0.758 \ \text{kg/hr per mm/min}
$$

For 200 mm/min feed:

$$
Q \approx 7.58 \ \text{kg/hr}
$$

> This is the **theoretical maximum**; actual deposition (with losses to spatter and slag entrapment) is typically **85–90%** of this value.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slag as an Electrical Resistor (Ohm's Law Application)

**Step 1 — Define the slag geometry:**

The molten slag forms a roughly cylindrical column of cross-sectional area $A_s$ (mm²) and effective length $L_s$ (mm) — the distance from electrode tip to molten metal pool.

**Step 2 — Apply resistivity equation:**

The electrical resistivity of molten ESW slag is $\rho_s \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ $\Omega \cdot$cm (i.e., $0.003$ to $0.005$ $\Omega \cdot$m).

$$
R_{slag} = \dfrac{\rho_{s} \cdot L_{s}}{A_{s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law to find voltage drop:**

The voltage that appears across the slag is:

$$
V_{slag} = I \cdot R_{slag} = \dfrac{I \cdot \rho_{s} \cdot L_{s}}{A_{s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to welding voltage:**

For the power source to deliver welding voltage $V_w$, neglecting cable drops:

$$
V_w \approx V_{slag} = \dfrac{I \cdot \rho_{s} \cdot L_{s}}{A_{s}}
$$

This explains why **higher current or deeper slag pool** requires higher voltage.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 50 mm thick low-alloy steel plate is to be welded by ESW. The current is 550 A, voltage 42 V, and travel speed 18 mm/min. The process efficiency is 0.82. Calculate (a) the heat input, (b) the power consumed, and (c) the energy supplied per metre of weld.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Heat Input:**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{0.82 \times 42 \times 550}{18 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{18942}{18000} = 1.052 \ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Part (b) — Power Consumed:**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 42 \times 550 = 23100 \ \text{W} = 23.1 \ \text{kW}
$$

Useful power:

$$
P_{u} = 0.82 \times 23.1 = 18.94 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Part (c) — Energy per metre of weld:**

Per metre = 1000 mm. Energy:

$$
E = H \times 1000 = 1.052 \times 1000 = 1052 \ \text{kJ} \approx 1.05 \ \text{MJ}
$$

Equivalent in kWh:

$$
E_{kWh} = \dfrac{1052}{3600} = 0.292 \ \text{kWh}
$$

> A full 1 m length requires about **0.29 kWh of useful energy** — demonstrating the high energy intensity of ESW.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ESW vs SAW vs GMAW

| Parameter | ESW | SAW (Submerged Arc) | GMAW (MIG/MAG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Slag resistance (I²R) | Arc + flux | Arc + shielding gas |
| Position | Vertical up only | Flat / horizontal | All positions |
| Max single-pass thickness | 450 mm | ~25 mm (multi-pass above) | ~10 mm |
| Deposition rate | 5–25 kg/hr | 4–10 kg/hr | 1–5 kg/hr |
| Heat input | 80–200 kJ/mm | 1.5–5 kJ/mm | 0.5–2 kJ/mm |
| Process efficiency (η) | 0.80–0.85 | 0.70–0.85 | 0.65–0.75 |
| Weld quality | Coarse grain, ductile | Fine grain, high quality | Good, but spatter |
| Slag consumption | Molten bath, re-used | Granular, partly consumed | None (gas shielded) |
| Capital cost | High (vertical rig) | Medium | Low |
| Typical application | Nuclear, shipbuilding | Pressure vessels, beams | General fabrication |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
