# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Electro Slag Welding. State any two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Electro Slag Welding (ESW) is a vertical-up fusion welding process in which the heat required for welding is generated by the **electrical resistance (I²R) of a molten conductive slag pool** through which a heavy current is passed between a continuously fed consumable electrode and the workpiece. The process does not use an arc in steady state and is used for welding very thick plates in a single pass.

**Two salient features (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Single-pass capability for thick sections** — Plates up to **450 mm** thick can be welded in a single vertical pass without joint beveling, eliminating multi-pass complexity.
2. **High deposition rate and high heat input** — Deposition rates of **5–25 kg/hr** are achievable; heat input of **80–200 kJ/mm** ensures deep fusion but causes coarse HAZ grains.
3. *(Any other valid point: e.g., no arc, water-cooled Cu dams, vertical-up only.)*

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**
> Students often write *"ESW is an arc welding process"* — this is **wrong** and costs full marks. Always state explicitly: *"ESW is a non-arc process; heat comes from resistance heating of slag."*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the main constituents of ESW flux and state the role of calcium fluoride (CaF₂).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Constituent | Typical % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CaF₂ (Calcium Fluoride) | 60 – 80 % | Primary slag former, dephosphoriser, lowers slag melting point |
| CaO (Lime) | 10 – 20 % | Increases basicity, removes Si as silicate |
| Al₂O₃ (Alumina) | 5 – 15 % | Controls slag viscosity, prevents excessive fluidity |
| SiO₂ (Silica) | 0 – 5 % | Minor fluxing (kept low to prevent Si pickup in weld) |

**Role of CaF₂ (1 mark):**
- Lowers the melting point of the slag mixture to around **1200 °C**, allowing easy initiation.
- Provides **fluidity** so the slag spreads uniformly across the joint.
- Acts as a **dephosphorising and desulphurising agent**, transferring P and S from the weld pool into the slag (a metallurgical purification benefit).
- Provides a **stable electrical resistance** path — CaF₂-based slags have $\rho_s \approx 0.4$ $\Omega \cdot$cm at 2000 °C, ideal for I²R heating.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**
> Do not write that CaF₂ is a "shielding gas." In ESW, the molten slag **is** the shield; CaF₂ is the slag-former.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** of the following. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **Electro Slag Welding (ESW) process**. Explain the functions of (i) molten slag, (ii) water-cooled copper dams, and (iii) consumable electrode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description (3 marks):**
Electro Slag Welding is a vertical-up fusion welding process used to join thick plates (≥25 mm) in a **single pass**. The joint is prepared as a square butt gap of 25–35 mm. The two plates are clamped with **water-cooled copper dams** (also called shoes or slides) on either side of the gap, which act as moving moulds. A consumable electrode wire is fed continuously from above into the gap. The electrode is connected to one terminal of a DC power source, and the plates to the other. Initially, an arc is struck on a starting plate; this melts the starting flux, forming a molten slag pool about 20–40 mm deep. Once the slag pool is deep enough, the arc is **extinguished**, and from then on, welding heat is generated by **electrical resistance (I²R) heating** as current flows through the molten slag. The slag temperature rises to **~2000 °C**, melting both the electrode and the edges of the base plates. The molten metal collects below the slag, and as the welding head travels upward, the metal **solidifies directionally** to form a sound weld.

**Functions (4 marks — 1.33 marks each):**

**(i) Molten Slag:**
- Generates welding heat by **resistance (I²R)** — it is the heating element.
- **Protects** the molten metal pool from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂).
- **Refines** the weld metal by absorbing P and S (via CaF₂ and CaO).
- **Shapes** the weld pool from above (acts like a hot cover).

**(ii) Water-Cooled Copper Dams:**
- Contain the **molten slag and weld pool** in the joint gap — act as a moving mould.
- **Extract heat** rapidly from the edges, preventing run-out of molten metal.
- Enable the weld to be made **in any required length** by sliding upward.
- Made of copper because of its **very high thermal conductivity (~400 W/m·K)**.

**(iii) Consumable Electrode:**
- Acts as **filler metal** to fill the joint.
- **Completes the electrical circuit** — current flows through it into the slag.
- Continuously **fed at a rate matching its melt-off rate**, ensuring steady process.
- Its composition determines the **weld metal chemistry**.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare **Electro Slag Welding (ESW)** with **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** under the following heads: principle, position of welding, thickness range, heat input, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | ESW | SAW |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Principle | Resistance (I²R) heating of molten slag; no arc in steady state | Sustained arc beneath granular flux |
| 2 | Position | **Vertical-up only** (forced by gravity) | Flat or horizontal (limited to flat) |
| 3 | Thickness Range | **25 mm to 450 mm** in a single pass | Up to ~25 mm in a single pass; multi-pass above |
| 4 | Heat Input | Very high: **80 – 200 kJ/mm** | Moderate: 1.5 – 5 kJ/mm |
| 5 | Deposition Rate | 5 – 25 kg/hr | 4 – 10 kg/hr |
| 6 | Shielding | Molten slag bath (re-used) | Granular flux (partly consumed) |
| 7 | Equipment | Complex (vertical travel mechanism, dams, water cooling) | Simpler (travel carriage, flux hopper) |
| 8 | HAZ Quality | Coarse grain, requires post-weld **normalising** | Fine grain, good as-welded |
| 9 | Applications | Nuclear vessels, ship hulls, wind towers, heavy forgings | Pressure vessels, beams, storage tanks |
| 10 | Cost per kg of weld | Lower (high productivity for thick) | Higher for thick (multi-pass) |

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **process parameters** of Electro Slag Welding in detail. Discuss the effect of welding current and voltage on the weld bead geometry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Process Parameters (4 marks):**
1. **Welding Current (I):** 400 – 900 A. Controlled by wire feed rate. Higher current → faster melting of electrode and parent metal → wider and deeper penetration.
2. **Welding Voltage (V):** 35 – 55 V. Determines the **slag pool depth**. Higher V → deeper slag bath → more thermal reserve → better stability for thick plates.
3. **Travel Speed (v):** 10 – 30 mm/min. Very slow compared to arc welding. Lower speed → more heat per unit length → coarser grain.
4. **Electrode Diameter and Stick-out:** Diameter 2.4 – 3.2 mm; stick-out 25 – 50 mm. Affects current density and melting rate.
5. **Slag Pool Depth:** 20 – 50 mm. Critical — too shallow → arc reignition; too deep → unstable operation.
6. **Joint Gap:** 25 – 35 mm. Wider gap → more filler needed → higher current required.
7. **Dwell Time / Oscillation (if used):** Optional lateral oscillation of electrode widens the bead for very thick plates.

**Effect on Weld Bead Geometry (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

**Effect of Current:**
- ↑ Current → ↑ **electrode melting rate** (deposition rate $\propto$ I)
- ↑ **penetration depth** into base metal
- ↑ **bead width** (slightly, because more metal is melted)
- ↑ **heat input** → coarser HAZ grains
- Excessive current → undercut, irregular surface, slag entrapment

**Effect of Voltage:**
- ↑ Voltage → ↑ **slag pool depth** (more voltage drop → more slag to heat)
- ↑ **heat in slag** but ↓ current at constant power → less fusion of base metal
- ↑ voltage improves **process stability** for thick sections
- Excessive voltage → spongy weld, slag inclusions, irregular shape

> A practical rule of thumb: **raise current to increase penetration; raise voltage to stabilise the slag pool.**

---

**Q. (b)** What are the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of Electro Slag Welding? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (3 marks — 1 each for 3 key points):**
1. **Welding of very thick plates in a single pass** — up to 450 mm, eliminating costly multi-pass operations.
2. **No joint preparation required** — square butt gap suffices; no V-groove beveling.
3. **High deposition rate** (5–25 kg/hr) and **low distortion** (slag acts as a heat reservoir, smoother thermal cycle).
4. **Metallurgical benefit** — slag removes P and S, giving clean weld metal.
5. **Lower labour skill requirement** — once set up, the process is fully automatic.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Vertical-up only** — cannot be used for horizontal, flat, or overhead joints.
2. **Slow process** — travel speed 10–30 mm/min; long cycle times.
3. **Coarse grain HAZ** — high heat input (80–200 kJ/mm) causes grain growth; **post-weld normalising** is usually required.
4. **Limited to specific alloys** — primarily carbon and low-alloy steels; high-alloy and non-ferrous welds are difficult.
5. **High capital cost** — vertical travel rig, water cooling system, high-current power source.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** — austenitic and low-alloy steel rings.
2. **Shipbuilding** — hull plates, bulkheads, decks (up to 100 mm).
3. **Heavy machinery** — large gear blanks, hydraulic press cylinders, rolling mill housings.
4. **Boilers and pressure vessels** — petrochemical, thermal power stations.
5. **Bridge and wind-tower sections** — circumferential joints of large diameter.
6. **Electroslag remelting (ESR)** of high-purity alloy ingots (related process).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common 14-Mark Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing ESW with electroslag remelting (ESR)** — ESR is a metallurgical remelting process for ingots, NOT a joining process. The welding process is ESW.
> 2. **Drawing the schematic without labelling the slag pool and dams** — marks lost for unlabelled diagrams.
> 3. **Omitting the "no arc" statement** — half-mark lost in the definition.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention post-weld heat treatment (normalising)** — coarse grain HAZ is a hallmark of ESW; examiners expect this.
> 5. **Stating SAW uses "molten slag" instead of "granular flux"** — loses comparison-mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ ESW is a **non-arc**, **vertical-up** fusion welding process.
- ✅ Heat is generated by **I²R loss in molten slag**, NOT by an arc.
- ✅ Developed in **1952, E.O. Paton Institute, Kiev, USSR** (Ukraine).
- ✅ Standard thickness range: **25 mm to 450 mm in a single pass**.
- ✅ Current: **400–900 A (DC, EP)**; Voltage: **35–55 V**; Travel: **10–30 mm/min**.
- ✅ Slag pool temperature: **~2000 °C**; molten metal pool: **~1750 °C**.
- ✅ **Water-cooled copper dams** contain the pool and act as a moving mould.
- ✅ Flux composition: **CaF₂ 60–80 %** + CaO 10–20 % + Al₂O₃ 5–15 % + minor oxides.
- ✅ CaF₂ is the **primary slag former**, removes P and S.
- ✅ Heat input formula: $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- ✅ Typical ESW heat input: **80–200 kJ/mm** (very high).
- ✅ Deposition rate: **5–25 kg/hr**.
- ✅ Process efficiency $\eta \approx 0.80$ – 0.85.
- ✅ **Coarse-grained HAZ** → requires **post-weld normalising**.
- ✅ **Limitations:** vertical-up only, slow, capital-intensive, limited to ferrous alloys.
- ✅ **Applications:** nuclear vessels, ship hulls, wind towers, boilers, heavy machinery.
- ✅ **Related processes:** Electroslag Remelting (ESR), Electroslag Cladding, Electroslag Surfacing.
- ✅ KTU buzz-phrase to use: *"Slag pool acts as a heating element, shield, and refining medium — all in one."*

> **Memory Anchor (One-Liner):**
> **"ESW = Electrical resistance in a Slag bath → Welds thick steel Vertically up, in One pass."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermit Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermit Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermit Welding** (also spelled *Thermite Welding*) is a **fusion welding process** in which the heat required for joining two metal pieces is obtained from an **exothermic chemical reaction** between a **metal oxide** (typically iron oxide) and a **reducing agent** (typically aluminum powder). The reaction produces **molten steel** and **molten aluminum oxide (slag)**, with temperatures reaching approximately **2500 °C**, which is sufficient to melt and fuse the parent metal at the joint.

The process was invented by **Dr. Hans Goldschmidt of Essen, Germany in 1895**, originally for the welding of tramway rails. It is classified under the category of **non-pressure fusion welding** when used for general fabrication, and as **pressure thermit welding** when used for rail joining where forging pressure is applied after the reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under the *Welding and Allied Processes* section of Module 2. Students are expected to understand the underlying chemistry, procedural steps, and industrial applications (with emphasis on rail welding and heavy-section repair).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a small piece of **rust (iron oxide)** lying on a steel plate. Normally, you'd throw it away. But what if you mixed that rust with some **aluminum powder**, placed a magnesium ribbon on top, and lit it? The result would be an **intense, blinding white flame** so hot that it would melt the steel plate beneath it and leave a puddle of liquid metal.

This is essentially **Thermit Welding** in a nutshell:

- **Rust (Iron Oxide)** = the metal oxide fuel
- **Aluminum powder** = the "lighter fluid" that burns the rust
- **Magnesium ribbon** = the starter/spark
- **The molten puddle** = the metal that welds the joint

In an engineering setting, instead of welding tiny plates, we place a **crucible (a refractory-lined container)** above the joint, fill it with the thermit mixture, ignite it, and let the resulting **superheated molten steel pour down into the gap** between two metal parts — melting their edges and fusing them into a single continuous piece.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for Thermit Welding:**
> - **Thermit reaction temperature:** $\approx$ **2500 °C** to **3000 °C** (depending on oxide used)
> - **Pouring temperature of molten steel:** $\approx$ **2000 °C**
> - **Melting point of pure iron:** **1538 °C**
> - **Melting point of aluminum oxide (slag):** $\approx$ **2050 °C**
> - **Density of thermit mixture:** $\approx$ **1.8 g/cm³** (tapped density)
> - **Typical crucible capacity:** **10 kg to 100 kg** of thermit charge
> - **Ignition temperature:** $\approx$ **1300 °C** (achieved using magnesium ribbon/powder)

## 1.4 Classification of Thermit Welding

| **Type** | **Pressure Applied?** | **Common Application** | **Filler Metal** |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Pressure Thermit Welding** | Yes (forging pressure) | Rail welding (continuously welded rails) | Low-carbon steel |
| **Non-Pressure Thermit Welding** | No | Repair of large steel castings, broken shafts | Plain carbon steel + alloy additions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermit Chemical Reaction — The Heart of the Process

The entire process is built upon a single, powerful **redox (reduction-oxidation) reaction**. Let's break it down step by step.

### 2.1.1 The Chemistry (Basic Reaction)

The classical thermit reaction uses **iron(III) oxide ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$)** and **aluminum (Al)**:

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat} \quad (\approx 850 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Why does this happen?**
- Aluminum is a **stronger reducing agent** than iron (higher affinity for oxygen).
- When ignited, aluminum "steals" the oxygen from iron oxide.
- This releases a tremendous amount of energy as **heat**.
- The products are **molten iron (steel)** and **molten aluminum oxide slag**.

### 2.1.2 Alternative Oxide Reactions

| **Reaction** | **Equation** | **Temp. Achieved** | **Application** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Iron Thermit** | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | ~2500 °C | Steel welding |
| **Copper Thermit** | $3\text{Cu}_2\text{O} + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 6\text{Cu} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | ~2200 °C | Copper brazing/repair |
| **Chromium Thermit** | $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Cr} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | ~2900 °C | Chromium alloying |

## 2.2 The Thermit Mixture (Weld Metal Composition)

The thermit mixture is **not pure iron oxide and aluminum**. It is carefully formulated to produce weld metal with **specific mechanical properties** (tensile strength, ductility, hardness).

### 2.2.1 Composition of a Typical Thermit Charge

| **Component** | **Function** | **Typical % (by weight)** |
|---------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| **Iron Oxide ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ or $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$)** | Main reactant — provides iron | **70 – 75 %** |
| **Aluminum Powder** | Reducing agent — provides heat | **18 – 22 %** |
| **Ferro-alloys (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si, Fe-Ti)** | Deoxidizers and alloying elements | **3 – 6 %** |
| **Steel punchings/granules** | Adjust carbon content and volume of liquid metal | **5 – 10 %** |
| **Magnesium Ribbon/Powder** | Ignition aid | Traces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why add ferro-alloys?**
> Pure thermit reaction produces **low-carbon iron** (almost pure Fe). To obtain **structural-grade steel**, small amounts of carbon, manganese, and silicon are added in the form of **steel punchings** and **ferro-alloys**. This is the **art and science of thermit weld design**.

## 2.3 The Crucible — The Reaction Vessel

The crucible is a **refractory-lined (magnesia or alumina) steel shell** that:
1. Withstands the extreme reaction temperature.
2. Contains the thermit charge.
3. Has a **tap hole** at the bottom, sealed with a **magnesium oxide disc / fire clay plug**, which is opened (tapped) to release the molten metal into the joint.
4. Is lined with **refractory magnesite** to prevent contamination of the molten metal.

##  2.4 Step-by-Step Process Flow

The thermit welding process follows a **strict sequence** of operations:

1. **End Preparation:** The two metal pieces to be joined are cut at a specific angle (usually 45°–60° V-groove) and cleaned thoroughly to remove rust, oil, and scale.
2. **Gap Setting:** A uniform gap of **15–25 mm** is maintained between the two parts.
3. **Preheating:** The joint area is preheated to a **cherry-red color (~600–800 °C)** using an oxy-acetylene torch or a gas preheater. This prevents **thermal shock** when the molten metal arrives.
4. **Mold Assembly:** A sand or refractory mold (called the **welding mold or "mould box"**) is clamped around the gap to contain the molten metal and shape the weld bead.
5. **Crucible Placement:** The crucible is positioned directly above the joint gap.
6. **Charging:** The thermit mixture is placed in the crucible along with the ignition powder.
7. **Ignition:** A magnesium ribbon/powder is ignited → thermit reaction starts → reaction completes in **20–40 seconds**.
8. **Slag Formation:** The lighter aluminum oxide slag floats on top; the heavier molten steel sinks to the bottom.
9. **Tapping:** After a **brief holding time (settling period) of 5–10 seconds**, the tap hole is opened using a tapping rod, and the molten steel pours into the mold.
10. **Solidification & Cooling:** The molten metal fills the gap and solidifies, forming the weld.
11. **Finishing:** The flash and excess metal are removed by grinding or chiseling after cooling.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations and Numerical Relationships for KTU 2024 Exam:**

| **Formula / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Unit** | **Use / Notes** |
|------------------------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Thermit Reaction (Standard)** | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | — | Core thermit reaction |
| **Heat released** | $\Delta H \approx -850$ | kJ/mol | Energy from 1 mol $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ |
| **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** | $T_{ad} \approx 2500$ | °C | Maximum temp of reaction |
| **Mass of thermit for weld (approx.)** | $m \approx \rho_w \cdot V_{gap} \cdot k$ | kg | $k$ = loss factor (~1.5–2) |
| **Volume of Weld Metal Required** | $V = A_{gap} \times L_{weld}$ | cm³ | Gap area × weld length |
| **Density of molten steel** | $\rho \approx 7.2$ | g/cm³ | At pouring temperature |
| **Slag-to-Metal ratio (typical)** | $\approx 1 : 2$ (by mass) | — | Slag = $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ |
| **Pouring time** | $t_{pour} \approx 5 - 15$ | s | Time to empty crucible |
| **Settling time** | $t_{settle} \approx 5 - 10$ | s | Allow slag separation |
| **Total cycle time** | $\approx 4 - 8$ | min | From ignition to completion |
| **Pre-heat temperature** | $T_{ph} \approx 600 - 800$ | °C | Required before pouring |
| **Gap width (rail welding)** | $g \approx 22 - 25$ | mm | Standard rail gap |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is Thermit Welding used in real engineering?**
> - **Railway Track Welding:** The single largest application. Continuous welded rails (CWR) on high-speed lines, metros, and freight corridors.
> - **Repair of Heavy Steel Components:** Cracked locomotive frames, broken crankshafts, large gear blanks.
> - **Shipbuilding and Heavy Fabrication:** Joining thick plates where arc welding is impractical.
> - **Joining of Dissimilar Steels:** For example, joining a high-carbon rail to a low-carbon transition piece.
> - **Welding in Remote Locations:** Where electricity and sophisticated equipment are unavailable (e.g., mountain railways, mining sites).

The biggest advantage of thermit welding is that it is **self-contained** — it does not require an external power source, shielding gas, or complex machinery, making it ideal for **field welding** under hostile conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Thermit Required for a Given Joint

This is a classic **KTU numerical problem**. You are given the joint dimensions and must calculate the mass of thermit charge needed.

### Problem Setup

A thermit welding job requires filling a joint gap of length $L = 250 \text{ mm}$, width $w = 25 \text{ mm}$, and depth $d = 50 \text{ mm}$. The density of molten steel is $\rho_{steel} = 7.2 \text{ g/cm}^3$. The thermit mixture has a steel yield efficiency of $\eta = 0.55$ (i.e., only 55% of the thermit mass converts to usable steel, the rest is slag and losses). Calculate the mass of thermit charge required.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the volume of the joint gap.**

Convert dimensions to cm:
- $L = 250 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ cm}$
- $w = 25 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 5 \text{ cm}$

$$V_{gap} = L \times w \times d$$

$$V_{gap} = 25 \text{ cm} \times 2.5 \text{ cm} \times 5 \text{ cm}$$

$$V_{gap} = 312.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate the mass of steel required to fill the gap.**

Using the density formula:
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \rho \cdot V$$

$$m_{steel} = 7.2 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 312.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$m_{steel} = 2250 \text{ g} = 2.25 \text{ kg}$$

This is the actual mass of steel that must be produced by the thermit reaction.

**Step 3: Account for the steel yield efficiency.**

The thermit reaction does not convert 100% of its mass into steel. We are told the efficiency is $\eta = 0.55$, meaning 55% of the thermit mass becomes usable molten steel.

$$m_{thermit} = \frac{m_{steel}}{\eta}$$

$$m_{thermit} = \frac{2.25 \text{ kg}}{0.55}$$

$$m_{thermit} \approx 4.09 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Apply a safety margin (industry practice).**

In actual field practice, engineers add a **loss factor of 1.5 to 2.0** to account for spillage, slag carryover, oxidation, and incomplete filling:

$$m_{actual} = m_{thermit} \times f_{loss}$$

Taking $f_{loss} = 1.5$:

$$m_{actual} = 4.09 \text{ kg} \times 1.5$$

$$m_{actual} \approx 6.14 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **6.14 kg** of thermit mixture must be charged in the crucible to safely fill this joint.

### General Formula

For any thermit welding problem, the relationship is:

$$m_{thermit} = \frac{\rho_{steel} \cdot V_{gap}}{\eta} \cdot f_{loss}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Ratio of Al to $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$

A common conceptual KTU question: **"What is the stoichiometric ratio (by mass) of aluminum to iron oxide in the thermit reaction?"**

### Step-by-Step Molar Mass Calculation

**Step 1: Molar mass of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$.**

- Fe: 55.845 g/mol × 2 atoms = 111.69 g/mol
- O: 16.00 g/mol × 3 atoms = 48.00 g/mol
- Total: $M_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 159.69 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 2: Molar mass of Al.**

$$M_{Al} = 26.98 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3: From the balanced equation $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$:**

- 1 mole of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ reacts with 2 moles of Al.
- Mass of 1 mol $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ = 159.69 g
- Mass of 2 mol Al = $2 \times 26.98 = 53.96$ g

**Step 4: Mass ratio of Al to $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{m_{Al}}{m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}} = \frac{53.96}{159.69} \approx 0.338$$

This means: **For every 100 kg of iron oxide, approximately 33.8 kg of aluminum powder is required** for the ideal stoichiometric reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a small excess of aluminum (1–2%) is added to ensure **complete reduction** of iron oxide. The slag produced ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) floats on top and protects the molten steel from atmospheric oxidation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis (Thermit vs. Arc Welding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative table is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU Part B.**

| **Parameter** | **Thermit Welding** | **Arc Welding (SMAW/GMAW)** |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Heat Source** | Chemical (exothermic reaction) | Electrical (arc) |
| **Equipment Portability** | High (no electricity needed) | Requires power source |
| **Joint Preparation** | V-groove with wide gap | Standard V-groove |
| **Welding Position** | Usually horizontal/flat | All positions possible |
| **Typical Joint Thickness** | > 30 mm (heavy sections) | Thin to thick (any) |
| **Operator Skill** | Specialized (weld mold design) | Standard trained welder |
| **Cycle Time** | 4–8 min per joint | Continuous |
| **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Large (slow cooling) | Smaller |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Lower ductility; higher hardness in HAZ | Tailorable via electrodes |
| **Cost per Joint (rail)** | Low | Higher |
| **Field Suitability** | Excellent (remote areas) | Limited by power availability |
| **Main Application** | Rails, large repairs | General fabrication |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *"What is thermit welding? Name the chemical reaction on which the process is based."*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermit welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining is obtained from an **exothermic chemical reaction** between a **metal oxide** (typically iron oxide, $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) and a **reducing agent** (aluminum powder). The molten steel produced fills the joint gap and fuses the parent metal.
>
> **Reaction (1 mark):**
> $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat} \ (\approx 850 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *"List the main components of a thermit mixture and state the function of each."*

**Model Answer:**

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---------------|--------------|
| Iron oxide ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) | Main reactant, source of molten iron |
| Aluminum powder | Reducing agent, provides heat |
| Ferro-alloys (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si) | Deoxidation and alloying |
| Steel punchings | Adjust carbon content |
| Magnesium ribbon/powder | Ignition aid |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *(a)* Explain the **principle, chemical reaction, and procedure** of thermit welding with a neat sketch.  **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Discuss the **industrial applications, advantages, and limitations** of thermit welding.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Principle, Reaction, and Procedure (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
The principle of thermit welding is based on the **exothermic reaction** between a metal oxide and aluminum. The intense heat produced melts the reaction product (molten metal) and the parent metal at the joint, creating a metallurgical bond upon solidification. The reaction temperature reaches ~**2500 °C**.

**Chemical Reaction (2 marks):**
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat}$$

- Iron oxide is **reduced** by aluminum.
- Molten iron (steel) sinks to the bottom; lighter $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ slag floats on top.
- Approximately **850 kJ of heat** is released per mole of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ reduced.

**Procedure (3 marks) — Sequential Steps:**

1. **End Preparation:** Cut the metal ends to a V-groove (45°–60°) and clean thoroughly.
2. **Gap Setting:** Maintain a uniform gap of **20–25 mm**.
3. **Mold Assembly:** Fix a sand/refractory mold around the gap.
4. **Preheating:** Heat the joint to **600–800 °C** (cherry red).
5. **Charging:** Fill the crucible with the thermit mixture.
6. **Ignition:** Light the magnesium ribbon → reaction completes in 20–40 s.
7. **Tapping:** Open the tap hole after a **5–10 s settling time**; molten steel flows into the mold.
8. **Cooling and Finishing:** Allow to cool, then grind off the flash.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Applications, Advantages, Limitations (7 marks)

**Industrial Applications (3 marks):**
- Welding of **railway rails** (continuous welded rails).
- Repair of **broken shafts, large gears, and heavy castings**.
- Joining **thick steel plates** in shipbuilding and heavy fabrication.
- Field welding in **remote locations** (no electricity needed).
- Welding **large steel cross-sections** in structural work.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- **No external power source** required — fully self-contained.
- Suitable for **field and remote applications**.
- Can join **very large cross-sections** in a single operation.
- **Low equipment cost** compared to arc welding setups.
- Suitable for **dissimilar metals** (rail-to-transition piece).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Large heat-affected zone (HAZ)** → coarse grain, lower ductility.
- **Slow process** — long cycle time per joint (~5–8 min).
- **High operator skill** required for mold preparation and tapping.
- **Limited to certain joint geometries** (mainly butt joints in heavy sections).
- **Hazardous** — extremely high temperatures, risk of explosion if improperly charged.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**
> *(a)* With a **neat diagram**, describe the **equipment used in thermit welding**, including the crucible, mold, and preheating arrangement.  **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A thermit welding job requires filling a **joint gap of 250 mm × 25 mm × 50 mm**. The density of molten steel is **7.2 g/cm³**, and the steel yield of the thermit mixture is **55%**. Calculate the **mass of thermit charge required** assuming a loss factor of 1.5.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Equipment Description (7 marks)

**1. Crucible (2.5 marks):**
- A **refractory-lined steel shell** (lined with magnesia or alumina).
- Top opening for charging the thermit mixture.
- Bottom **tap hole** sealed with a magnesium-oxide disc plug.
- Capacity: **10–100 kg** of charge depending on job size.
- Mounted on a **supporting frame** above the joint.

**2. Weld Mold (2 marks):**
- Made of **silica sand, fire clay, or pre-formed refractory blocks**.
- Surrounds the joint gap to contain the molten metal.
- Shaped to give the required **weld bead profile**.
- Has **vent holes** to release gases during pouring.
- Must be **pre-dried** to avoid steam explosions.

**3. Preheating Arrangement (1.5 marks):**
- A **gas preheater** (propane or oxy-acetylene torch).
- Used to bring the joint to **600–800 °C** before pouring.
- Prevents **thermal shock** and ensures proper fusion.
- May be a **ring burner** placed around the joint gap.

**4. Other Equipment (1 mark):**
- **Tapping rod** for opening the tap hole.
- **Ignition strip (magnesium ribbon)** and lighter.
- **Clamps and alignment fixtures** for setting the gap.
- **Safety equipment** (face shield, fire-resistant clothing, sand bucket).

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Joint dimensions: $L = 250 \text{ mm}$, $w = 25 \text{ mm}$, $d = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Density of molten steel: $\rho = 7.2 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Steel yield: $\eta = 0.55$
- Loss factor: $f = 1.5$

**Step 1: Convert dimensions to cm.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 25 \text{ cm} \\
w &= 2.5 \text{ cm} \\
d &= 5 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate volume of the joint gap.**

$$V = L \times w \times d = 25 \times 2.5 \times 5 = 312.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate mass of steel required.**

$$m_{steel} = \rho \cdot V = 7.2 \times 312.5 = 2250 \text{ g} = 2.25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Account for yield efficiency.**

$$m_{thermit} = \frac{m_{steel}}{\eta} = \frac{2.25}{0.55} = 4.09 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Apply the loss factor.**

$$m_{actual} = m_{thermit} \times f = 4.09 \times 1.5 \approx 6.14 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:** The required thermit charge is approximately **6.14 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is critical** — examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for failing to convert mm to cm.
> 2. **Do not confuse yield with loss** — the yield is the fraction that *becomes* steel, the loss factor is the *safety margin multiplier*. Both must be applied.
> 3. **Always state the final answer in kg with 2 decimal places** for numerical clarity.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Forgetting to apply the loss factor ($f = 1.5$). This would lead to a shortage of weld metal in actual practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Thermit Welding — KTU 2024**

- **Inventor:** Dr. Hans Goldschmidt (1895, Germany).
- **Core Reaction:** $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat}$ (850 kJ/mol).
- **Heat Source:** Purely **chemical (exothermic)** — no electricity needed.
- **Reaction Temperature:** $\approx$ **2500 °C** (adiabatic flame temperature).
- **Pouring Temperature of Molten Steel:** $\approx$ **2000 °C**.
- **Pre-heat Temperature:** **600 – 800 °C** (cherry red) — mandatory before tapping.
- **Joint Gap:** **22 – 25 mm** for rail welding.
- **Settling Time:** **5 – 10 s** (allow slag to float).
- **Cycle Time:** **4 – 8 min per joint**.
- **Slag:** $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (aluminum oxide), floats on top, removed after cooling.
- **Thermit Mixture Composition:** Iron oxide (70–75%) + Al powder (18–22%) + Ferro-alloys (3–6%) + Steel punchings (5–10%).
- **Al to $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ mass ratio:** $\approx$ **1 : 3** (i.e., 33.8 kg Al per 100 kg iron oxide).
- **Types:** (i) Pressure thermit welding (rail), (ii) Non-pressure thermit welding (repair).
- **Largest Application:** **Continuous welded railway rails (CWR).**
- **Mass formula:** $m_{thermit} = (\rho_{steel} \cdot V_{gap} \cdot f_{loss}) / \eta$.
- **Advantage:** No power source needed; ideal for **field and remote welding**.
- **Limitation:** Large HAZ, lower ductility, limited to heavy sections, slow process.
- **Key Difference from Arc Welding:** Heat source is **chemical, not electrical**; equipment is **portable but bulkier** for the joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Pearl:**
> If asked *"Why is aluminum used as the reducing agent?"* — answer: Because **aluminum has a higher affinity for oxygen than iron** (it's higher in the electrochemical series), so it readily reduces iron oxide, releasing tremendous heat. The oxide of aluminum ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) is also **very stable and low in density**, forming a protective slag layer on the molten steel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid State Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solid State Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid State Welding (SSW)** is a category of welding processes in which coalescence of the faying surfaces is produced by the application of pressure alone or a combination of heat and pressure, with no deliberate fusion (melting) of the base metals and without the addition of filler metal. The temperature during the process remains below the melting point (solidus temperature) of the base materials, and joining is achieved through atomic diffusion, plastic deformation, or mechanical interlocking at the interface.

According to **AWS (American Welding Society) classification**, solid-state welding processes belong to the broader family of welding processes where the bond is formed entirely in the **solid phase**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 covers Solid State Welding with major emphasis on **Diffusion Welding, Friction Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Explosive Welding, Forge Welding, and Cold Welding**. Expect theory questions (7 marks) and process comparison questions (14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Clay Pressing" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you have two pieces of modeling clay (play-doh) at room temperature. You cannot melt them together easily, but if you press them hard against each other for some time, they will *stick* — they have joined without either melting. Now, if you gently warm them while pressing (so the molecules get a little more energetic) and hold the pressure longer, the bond becomes much stronger, almost as if they were originally one piece.
>
> That is essentially **Solid State Welding** — joining metals by **pressure** (and sometimes **heat below melting**) so that atoms from each surface migrate across the boundary and lock together.
>
> A second analogy: **two ice cubes pressed together in your hand**. They fuse into one piece at a temperature far below their melting point (though this is more diffusion-controlled).

### 1.3 Classification of Solid State Welding Processes

Solid state welding processes covered in the KTU 2024 Module 2 are typically classified as:

| Sl. No. | Process | Primary Driving Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Diffusion Welding (Bonding)** | Atomic diffusion under heat + pressure |
| 2 | **Friction Welding** (Continuous Drive, Inertia, Friction Stir) | Heat from frictional rubbing + forging pressure |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Welding** | High-frequency vibration + static pressure |
| 4 | **Explosive Welding** | Controlled detonation + collision pressure |
| 5 | **Cold Welding** | Room-temperature plastic deformation under pressure |
| 6 | **Forge Welding (Hot Pressure Welding)** | Hot upsetting under hammer/press pressure |

### 1.4 Fundamental Distinguishing Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Solidus Temperature ($T_s$):** The temperature below which the metal is fully solid. SSW operates at $T < T_s$.
> - **Homologous Temperature ($T_h$):** Often expressed as a fraction of the melting point ($T/T_m$). Most SSW processes operate in the range $0.4 \, T_m \le T \le 0.8 \, T_m$.
> - **Atomic Diffusion Coefficient ($D$):** Governs the rate of solid-state bonding.
> - **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$):** Determines the pressure required for plastic deformation at the interface.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Diffusion Welding (Diffusion Bonding)

#### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

Diffusion welding (DW) or diffusion bonding is a solid-state joining process in which two mating surfaces are held together under **moderate pressure ($P$)** at an **elevated temperature (typically $0.5\,T_m$ to $0.8\,T_m$)** for a sustained duration so that atomic diffusion across the interface produces a metallurgical bond.

#### 2.1.2 Three Sequential Stages of Bond Formation

The diffusion welding process is described by the classical **three-stage mechanism** (King & Owczarski model):

1. **Stage I — Initial Plastic Deformation and Contact (Asperity Collapse):**
   The mating surfaces are never perfectly flat; they have microscopic peaks (asperities). Under the applied pressure, these asperities undergo plastic deformation and creep, increasing the **real area of contact** from a small initial value to nearly 100 %.

2. **Stage II — Grain Boundary Diffusion and Pore Elimination:**
   Atoms diffuse along the original interface (grain boundary diffusion) and through the lattice (volume diffusion), causing the interfacial voids/pores to shrink and eventually disappear. The original joint line becomes indistinguishable.

3. **Stage III — Volume Diffusion and Microstructural Homogenization:**
   Residual voids are eliminated, and the microstructure across the bond becomes continuous. The bond strength approaches that of the parent metal.

### 2.2 Friction Welding

#### 2.2.1 Principle

In friction welding, one component is held stationary while the other is rotated at high speed. The two are brought into contact under an axial **forge force ($F$)**. Frictional heat softens the interface material, which is then extruded out as flash. Once the desired upset (shortening) is achieved, rotation is stopped and full forging pressure is applied.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Tip:** Think of rubbing your hands together very fast — they get hot. Now add pressure. That heat + pressure is friction welding in essence.

#### 2.2.2 Three Phases of Friction Welding

1. **Initial Friction Phase:** Low pressure, contact begins, heat generation starts.
2. **Intermediate (Softening) Phase:** Pressure increases; material at the interface becomes plastic; flash begins to form.
3. **Forging Phase:** Rotation stopped, axial upset force applied to consolidate the joint.

### 2.3 Ultrasonic Welding

High-frequency electrical energy (typically **20–40 kHz**) is converted to mechanical vibrations via a piezoelectric transducer. The vibrations are transmitted through a sonotrode (horn) to the workpieces held under static pressure. Combined scrubbing action disrupts surface oxides and produces metallurgical bonding without significant heating.

### 2.4 Explosive Welding (Cladding)

A controlled detonation drives one metal plate (flyer) into another (base) at extremely high velocity, producing an oblique collision. The collision generates a jet of surface material (oxide film removal) and creates a **wavy / sinusoidal bond interface** with true metallurgical bonding.

### 2.5 Cold Welding

Performed at room temperature (or slightly elevated but well below recrystallization). Requires **large plastic deformation (typically > 70–80 % reduction)** to break surface oxides and bring fresh metal into intimate contact. Common for joining aluminium and copper wires.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are high-frequency in KTU University Exams.**

| Equation | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homologous Temperature | $T_h = \dfrac{T}{T_m}$ | Ratio of process temperature to melting temperature (in Kelvin) | dimensionless |
| Diffusion Coefficient (Arrhenius) | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of diffusion | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Power in Friction Welding | $P = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$ | Where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $F_n$ = normal force, $v$ = sliding velocity | Watts (W) |
| Heat Generated in Friction Welding | $Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v \cdot t$ | Total frictional heat over time $t$ | Joules (J) |
| Flyer Plate Velocity (Explosive Welding) | $V_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{\rho}}$ | Where $E$ = kinetic energy per unit mass, $\rho$ = density | m/s |
| Upset (Burn-off) in Friction Welding | $\Delta L = L_0 - L_f$ | Reduction in length after forging | mm |
| Upset Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless deformation | — |
| Hertzian Contact Stress (Ultrasonic) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a^2}$ | Peak contact stress under sonotrode | Pa |
| Effective Strain Rate (Friction Stir Welding) | $\dot\varepsilon = \dfrac{\pi \, \omega \, R}{L}$ | Where $\omega$ = angular velocity, $R$ = tool radius, $L$ = depth | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Friction stir welding is used for fuel tanks, fuselage panels, and rocket stages where fusion welding would cause defects.
> - **Nuclear:** Diffusion welding joins dissimilar metals and reactive metals (e.g., zirconium alloys) without contamination.
> - **Electronics:** Ultrasonic welding bonds aluminum and copper foils, battery tabs, and wire harnesses.
> - **Marine & Chemical:** Explosive welding clads corrosion-resistant alloys (e.g., titanium, stainless steel) onto carbon steel plates.
> - **Electrical Industry:** Cold welding is used for joining aluminum/copper conductors without heat-affected zones.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Diffusion Welding Time (King & Owczarski Pore-Elimination Model)

The classical diffusion welding time to close a pore of radius $r$ at the interface is derived from **volume diffusion** as the rate-controlling step.

Consider a spherical void (pore) of radius $r$ located at the bond interface. The driving force for void shrinkage is the **surface energy** ($\gamma_s$) of the void, which produces an effective stress equivalent to:

$$\sigma_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{2\gamma_s}{r}$$

This effective stress acts as the driving force for vacancy diffusion from the void surface into the surrounding grain boundary / lattice.

The diffusion flux $J$ of vacancies from the void surface is given by Fick's first law:

$$J = -D_v \, \dfrac{\partial C_v}{\partial x}$$

where $D_v$ is the vacancy diffusivity and $\partial C_v / \partial x$ is the vacancy concentration gradient.

The volumetric rate of void shrinkage is:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -J \cdot A \cdot \Omega$$

where $A$ is the void surface area and $\Omega$ is the atomic volume.

After substituting $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, $A = 4\pi r^2$, and the equilibrium vacancy concentration $C_v = C_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\sigma_{\text{eff}} \, \Omega}{kT}\right)$, simplifying yields the **classical void closure time**:

$$\boxed{\, t = \dfrac{kT \, r^3}{3 D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, \delta} \,}$$

where:
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$)
- $T$ = absolute bonding temperature (K)
- $r$ = initial pore radius (m)
- $D$ = volume self-diffusion coefficient ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)
- $\gamma_s$ = surface energy ($\text{J/m}^2$)
- $\Omega$ = atomic volume ($\text{m}^3$)
- $\delta$ = grain boundary width ($\sim 5 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}$)

**Interpretation:** Bonding time scales with $r^3$ — smaller initial surface roughness dramatically reduces the required bonding time. This is why DW requires well-prepared, smooth mating surfaces.

### 3.2 Derivation of Power and Heat Generated in Friction Welding

The tangential velocity at the interface of a rotating workpiece of radius $R$ and angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$v = \omega R$$

The frictional force opposing motion at the interface is:

$$F_f = \mu \, F_n$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $F_n$ is the axial (normal) force.

Therefore, the **frictional power dissipated** is:

$$P = F_f \cdot v = \mu \, F_n \, \omega R$$

Over a contact time $t$, the total heat generated is:

$$Q = P \cdot t = \mu \, F_n \, \omega R \, t$$

Now, the **temperature rise** at the interface (assuming all heat goes into a thin layer of mass $m$ with specific heat $c$):

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m c} = \dfrac{\mu \, F_n \, \omega R \, t}{m c}$$

For typical mild steel friction welding ($\mu \approx 0.5$, $F_n \approx 30 \, \text{kN}$, $\omega = 3000 \, \text{rpm} = 50\pi \, \text{rad/s}$, $R = 0.025 \, \text{m}$, $t = 5 \, \text{s}$):

$$P = 0.5 \times 30000 \times 50\pi \times 0.025 \approx 58{,}900 \, \text{W} \approx 58.9 \, \text{kW}$$

This large power in a small region is what raises the interface temperature to $\sim 0.8\,T_m$ within seconds, enabling forging.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for KTU Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this decision matrix in 14-mark comparison questions.**

| Parameter | Diffusion Welding | Friction Welding | Ultrasonic Welding | Explosive Welding | Cold Welding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $0.5$–$0.8 \, T_m$ | $0.7$–$0.9 \, T_m$ (interface only) | $< 0.3 \, T_m$ | Ambient (localized) | Ambient |
| Pressure | $1$–$50 \, \text{MPa}$ | $30$–$200 \, \text{MPa}$ | Low ($< 1 \, \text{MPa}$) | Very high (GPa-level shock) | Very high (upset) |
| Time | Minutes to hours | Seconds | $< 1 \, \text{s}$ | Microseconds | Instant |
| Atmosphere | Vacuum or inert gas | Air | Air | Air / underwater | Air |
| Typical Materials | Dissimilar, reactive, aerospace alloys | Similar steels, Al, Ti | Foils, thin sheets, wires | Cladding plates | Soft, ductile (Al, Cu) |
| Filler Required? | No (sometimes interlayer) | No | No | No | No |
| Joint Strength vs. Base | 80–100 % | 90–100 % | 60–90 % | 90–100 % | 70–90 % |
| Industrial Example | Ti alloy aerospace parts | Drill bits, valve stems | Battery tabs, wire harnesses | Ti-clad steel plates | Electrical conductor splicing |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Diffusion Welding Time

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Estimate the diffusion welding time to close a pore of radius $r = 5 \, \mu\text{m}$ in a mild steel joint at $T = 900 \, \text{°C}$ ($\approx 1173 \, \text{K}$). Given: $D = 10^{-13} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\gamma_s = 1.5 \, \text{J/m}^2$, $\Omega = 1.18 \times 10^{-29} \, \text{m}^3$, $\delta = 5 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$.

**Step 1 — List known quantities and convert to SI:**

$$r = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \quad T = 1173 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the void-closure time formula:**

$$t = \dfrac{kT \, r^3}{3 D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, \delta}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$kT \, r^3 = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 1173 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^3$$

$$= 1.619 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16} = 2.024 \times 10^{-36} \, \text{J·m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$3 D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, \delta = 3 \times 10^{-13} \times 1.5 \times 1.18 \times 10^{-29} \times 5 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$= 3 \times 10^{-13} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-39} = 2.655 \times 10^{-51}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $t$:**

$$t = \dfrac{2.024 \times 10^{-36}}{2.655 \times 10^{-51}} \approx 7.62 \times 10^{14} \, \text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check:** Dividing by $3.15 \times 10^7 \, \text{s/yr}$:

$$t \approx 2.42 \times 10^7 \, \text{years}$$

**Conclusion:** With pure volume diffusion alone, closure is impractically slow at $900 \, \text{°C}$ for $5\,\mu\text{m}$ pores. Hence, real-world diffusion bonding relies on **grain-boundary diffusion (much faster)** plus **interlayers (e.g., Ni foil)** to accelerate bonding. This explains why interlayers are mandatory in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — General Solid State Welding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two Clean Faying Surfaces] --> B{Apply Pressure and-or Heat}
    B -->|Heat + Pressure| C[Plastic Deformation of Asperities]
    B -->|Pressure Only| D[Cold Welding Path]
    C --> E[Atomic Diffusion Across Interface]
    E --> F[Pore Elimination and Bond Formation]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Metallurgical Bond in Solid State]
    G --> H[Inspect Joint Quality]
    H --> I[Accept or Reject Component]
```

### 4.2 Friction Welding — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DriveAssembly["Drive Assembly"]
        M[AC-DC Motor] --> S[Shaft Coupling]
        S --> CHK[Chuck]
    end
    CHK -->|holds| WP1[Rotating Workpiece]
    subgraph ClampAssembly["Clamp Assembly"]
        CL[Hydraulic Clamp] --> WP2[Stationary Workpiece]
    end
    subgraph ControlUnit["PLC Control Unit"]
        CTRL[Speed, Pressure, Time Controller]
    end
    CTRL -->|signals| M
    CTRL -->|signals| CL
    WP1 -->|friction contact| WP2
    WP2 --> FL[Extruded Flash Ejected]
    WP2 -->|axial upset| FRC[Forging Force Applied]
    FRC --> JN[Finished Welded Joint]
```

### 4.3 Ultrasonic Welding — Signal and Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[High Frequency Power Supply 20-40 kHz] --> PZT[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    PZT --> BOO[Booster Amplifier]
    BOO --> SON[Sonotrode Horn]
    SON -->|vibration + downward force| WP1[Upper Workpiece]
    WP1 --> WP2[Lower Workpiece]
    WP2 --> ANS[Anvil Support]
    ANS --> FB[Feedback to Controller]
    FB --> PWR
```

### 4.4 Diffusion Welding — Three-Stage Bond Formation Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STG1[Stage 1 Asperity Deformation Real Contact Area increases] --> STG2[Stage 2 Grain Boundary Diffusion Pores Shrink]
    STG2 --> STG3[Stage 3 Volume Diffusion Microstructure Homogenized]
    STG3 --> STG4[Final Bond Indistinguishable from Parent Metal]
```

### 4.5 Explosive Welding — Cladding Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXP[Detonator Initiation] --> DET[Explosive Layer Detonates]
    DET -->|drives| FLY[Flyer Plate Accelerates]
    FLY -->|oblique collision| JET[Surface Oxide Jet Forms and Removes Contaminants]
    JET -->|metallic jet| BOND[Wavy Metallurgical Bond Forms]
    BOND --> CLD[Clad Plate Final Geometry]
```

### 4.6 Comparative Process Tree — Solid State Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SSW[Solid State Welding] --> DW[Diffusion Welding]
    SSW --> FW[Friction Welding]
    SSW --> UW[Ultrasonic Welding]
    SSW --> EW[Explosive Welding]
    SSW --> CW[Cold Welding]
    SSW --> FGW[Forge Welding]
    FW --> CDFW[Continuous Drive Friction Welding]
    FW --> IFW[Inertia Friction Welding]
    FW --> FSW[Friction Stir Welding]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Solid State Welding. List any four solid-state welding processes.

**Model Answer:**
Solid State Welding is a welding process in which coalescence of the faying surfaces is produced by the application of pressure alone, or a combination of heat and pressure, without melting the base metals and without using filler material. The base metal temperature remains below its solidus temperature.
Four processes: (i) Diffusion Welding, (ii) Friction Welding, (iii) Ultrasonic Welding, (iv) Explosive Welding.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the role of an interlayer in diffusion welding?

**Model Answer:**
An interlayer (e.g., nickel, copper, titanium foil of 25–100 µm) is placed between the faying surfaces. Its function is to (i) accelerate bonding by providing a high-diffusivity path, (ii) accommodate surface roughness and tolerance variations, (iii) reduce bonding temperature and pressure required, and (iv) prevent formation of brittle intermetallics at the bond line.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle and procedure of **Diffusion Welding (Bonding)**. Discuss the effect of bonding temperature, pressure, time, and surface preparation on joint quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the various **solid-state welding processes** and explain the principle and applications of **Friction Welding** and **Ultrasonic Welding**. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Diffusion Welding:**

**Principle:** Two carefully prepared mating surfaces are brought into contact under controlled pressure at elevated temperature ($0.5$–$0.8 \, T_m$) in vacuum or inert atmosphere. Bonding occurs by atomic diffusion across the interface.

**Procedure Steps:**
1. Surface preparation — machining, grinding, chemical cleaning to remove oxides and contaminants.
2. Assembly — placing the parts in the bonding fixture, optionally with an interlayer foil.
3. Loading into vacuum / inert gas chamber.
4. Heating to bonding temperature ($T_b$).
5. Applying bonding pressure ($P_b$) gradually.
6. Holding at temperature and pressure for bonding time ($t_b$).
7. Slow cooling under pressure.
8. Inspection (ultrasonic / metallographic).

**Effect of Process Parameters:**
- **Temperature:** Higher $T_b$ accelerates diffusion but excessive temperature causes grain coarsening.
- **Pressure:** Must be sufficient to flatten asperities (typically 1–50 MPa) without macroscopic deformation.
- **Time:** Longer times eliminate pores fully; classical void-closure time scales with $r^3$.
- **Surface Preparation:** Surface roughness $R_a < 0.4 \, \mu\text{m}$ is essential; oxide-free surfaces bond readily.

**(b) Solid-State Welding Classification + Comparison:**

The full classification tree (Friction Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Welding, Explosive Welding, Cold Welding, Forge Welding) is shown in the diagram in Section 4.6.

**Friction Welding — Principle:**
A rotating workpiece is pressed against a stationary workpiece. Frictional heat raises the interface temperature, the material becomes plastic, and flash is extruded. Rotation stops and forging pressure consolidates the joint.

**Friction Welding — Applications:** Drill bits, valve stems, automotive axles, hydraulic cylinders, dissimilar metal joints (e.g., aluminium–steel transition joints).

**Ultrasonic Welding — Principle:**
A 20–40 kHz electrical signal is converted by piezoelectric transducer into mechanical vibrations. These are transmitted through a horn (sonotrode) to the workpieces under static clamping pressure. Surface friction and micro-deformation cause bonding.

**Ultrasonic Welding — Applications:** Aluminum and copper wire splicing, lithium-ion battery tab welding, solar panel ribbon bonding, packaging industry (foil sealing).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Friction Welding | Ultrasonic Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | Friction | Ultrasonic vibration |
| Temperature | $0.7$–$0.9 \, T_m$ (localized) | $< 0.3 \, T_m$ (near ambient) |
| Typical Materials | Steels, Al, Ti, dissimilar metals | Foils, thin sheets, wires (mostly non-ferrous) |
| Joint Strength | 90–100 % of base metal | 60–90 % of base metal |
| Cycle Time | Seconds | $< 1$ second |
| Equipment Cost | Higher (chuck + drive) | Lower |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with sketches the principle of **Explosive Welding**. Discuss its process parameters, applications, and the formation of the wavy interface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Cold Welding**? Explain its mechanism, suitable materials, and applications. How does it differ from forge welding? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Explosive Welding:**

**Principle:** A controlled explosive charge detonates above a flyer plate, accelerating it to very high velocity ($200$–$1000 \, \text{m/s}$) toward a base plate set at a small **stand-off distance** ($h$, typically $0.5$–$5 \, \text{mm}$) and **inclination angle** ($\alpha$, typically $2°$–$15°$). The oblique collision produces a metallic jet that strips surface oxides, and the resulting pressure creates a wavy (sinusoidal) metallurgical bond at the interface.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Explosive type and loading density** (typically 0.5–2.5 g/cm² of detonation velocity 1500–8000 m/s).
- **Stand-off distance** $h$.
- **Collision angle** $\alpha$.
- **Flyer plate velocity** $V_p$.

The **wavy interface** forms because the collision point oscillates due to hydrodynamic instability (analogous to Kelvin–Helmholtz instability), producing alternating peaks and valleys — a signature of a true explosive weld.

**Applications:**
- Cladding corrosion-resistant alloys (Ti, SS, Cu-Ni) onto carbon steel.
- Tube-to-tube sheet joining in heat exchangers.
- Manufacturing bimetallic strips and electrical transition joints.

**(b) Cold Welding:**

**Mechanism:** Cold welding is performed at room temperature (or slightly elevated) without external heat. The bond forms solely through plastic deformation — large compressive strain (> 70 %) breaks surface oxide films and exposes fresh metal that fuses under pressure.

**Suitable Materials:** Highly ductile, work-softening metals — **aluminum, copper, lead, zinc, nickel, low-carbon steels.** Hardened or work-hardening alloys are unsuitable.

**Applications:**
- Aluminum wire and cable splicing.
- Aluminum foil lamination in packaging.
- Electrical conductor manufacturing.

**Difference from Forge Welding:**

| Aspect | Cold Welding | Forge Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Ambient (no heating) | Above recrystallization ($> 0.6 \, T_m$) |
| Heat Required | None | Furnace or induction heating |
| Deformation Required | Very high (> 70 % reduction) | Moderate upsetting |
| Mechanism | Cold pressure bonding only | Hot pressure bonding (some diffusion) |
| Typical Materials | Soft non-ferrous (Al, Cu) | Steels, wrought iron (historical) |
| Modern Relevance | Electronics, packaging | Largely historical; replaced by arc welding |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating Friction Welding with Friction Stir Welding:** They are different. FSW uses a non-consumable rotating tool with a pin that traverses along the joint — it is a continuous process. Ordinary friction welding joins two parts end-to-end.
>
> 2. **Stating "no heat" for cold welding:** Cold welding still produces some heat from plastic deformation, but it is *insignificant* compared to fusion welding. The key is that the temperature does **not** approach the melting range.
>
> 3. **Confusing forge welding with friction welding:** Forge welding uses an external heat source + hammer/press; friction welding generates heat *internally* through rubbing.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the role of flash in friction welding:** Flash extrusion is a *signature* of a quality friction weld. Always mention it.
>
> 5. **Skipping interlayers in diffusion welding:** Examiners specifically look for mention of Ni / Cu interlayers and their role.
>
> 6. **In numerical problems, omitting units** — marks are routinely deducted in KTU valuation for missing or incorrect SI units.
>
> 7. **Mixing up homologous temperature formula:** $T_h = T / T_m$ must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solid State Welding**

- [ ] **Definition:** Joining below solidus temperature, no filler, no fusion.
- [ ] **Homologous temperature** $T_h = T / T_m$; SSW operates at $T_h \approx 0.4$–$0.8$.
- [ ] **Diffusion Welding:** Three stages — asperity deformation → grain boundary diffusion → volume diffusion.
- [ ] **Diffusion coefficient (Arrhenius):** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q_d / RT)$.
- [ ] **Void-closure time** scales with $r^3$; small surface roughness = short bonding time.
- [ ] **Interlayers (Ni, Cu)** are used in DW to accelerate bonding and accommodate tolerance.
- [ ] **Friction Welding:** Heat = $\mu F_n v t$; phases = initial friction → intermediate softening → forging.
- [ ] **Flash extrusion** is the quality indicator of friction welds.
- [ ] **Friction Stir Welding (FSW):** Solid-state, non-consumable tool with pin & shoulder, traverses the joint.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic Welding:** $20$–$40 \, \text{kHz}$ vibration; used for foils, wires, battery tabs.
- [ ] **Explosive Welding:** Wavy interface; metallic jet cleans surfaces; used for cladding dissimilar plates.
- [ ] **Cold Welding:** $> 70 \%$ deformation; suitable for soft, ductile metals (Al, Cu); used in electrical industry.
- [ ] **Forge Welding:** External heating + hammer/press; largely historical.
- [ ] **AWS Classification:** SSW belongs to welding processes without melting.
- [ ] **Advantages of SSW:** No HAZ, no熔池, no solidification cracks, dissimilar metal joining possible.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** Expensive equipment (DW, EW), limited geometries, requires precise surface prep.

**Mnemonic to remember the processes: "D-F-U-E-C-F" → Diffusion, Friction, Ultrasonic, Explosive, Cold, Forge.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Metallurgy: Heat Affected Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding Metallurgy: Heat Affected Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Welding Metallurgy: Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fusion welding processes, the base metal adjacent to the weld pool experiences a steep **thermal gradient** but does not undergo melting. This narrow region, whose **microstructure and mechanical properties are altered solely due to the heat of welding** (without reaching the liquidus temperature), is termed the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**. 

According to the **American Welding Society (AWS)** and KTU 2024 Manufacturing Processes syllabus, the HAZ is defined as:

> *"The portion of the base metal in which the microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding, brazing, soldering, or thermal cutting, but which has not been melted."*

The HAZ is therefore sandwiched between two distinct boundaries:
- **Fusion Boundary (Fusion Line):** The interface separating the **Fusion Zone (FZ)** from the HAZ. At this line, the peak temperature just reaches the solidus (~0% liquid on heating).
- **Parent Metal Boundary:** The outer edge of the HAZ, beyond which the base metal microstructure is unaffected (peak temperature falls below $A_{c1}$, the lower critical temperature on heating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *define* the HAZ and *sketch* the weld cross-section showing the FZ, HAZ, and unaffected parent metal. Always label the **fusion line** explicitly — it is the most asked boundary.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Toasted Bread" Model

Imagine you are toasting a slice of bread over an open flame. The bread has three regions:
- **The burning/charred centre** → analogous to the **Fusion Zone (melted)**.
- **The golden-brown ring around it** → this is exactly the **HAZ**. It has *not burnt*, but its *colour, texture, and taste* have permanently changed due to heat exposure.
- **The soft, white untouched bread** → analogous to the **unaffected parent metal**.

Just like the golden-brown ring has *no single temperature* — it gradually transitions from "very hot" near the char to "barely warm" at the white edge — the HAZ is *not uniform*. It contains **multiple sub-zones**, each having experienced a *different peak temperature* and therefore a *different microstructure* and *different mechanical property*. This is the central idea of HAZ metallurgy.

> [!NOTE]
> The width of the HAZ is typically **1 mm to 10 mm** for arc welding processes (SMAW, GTAW, GMAW), but can extend up to **20–30 mm** in high-heat-input processes such as **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** and **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)**.

---

## 1.3 Why the HAZ Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Concern | Role of HAZ |
|---|---|
| **Brittle Fracture** | Coarse-grained HAZ has low toughness |
| **Hydrogen Induced Cracking (HIC)** | Martensite in HAZ traps diffusible hydrogen |
| **Lamellar Tearing** | Through-thickness ductility loss in HAZ |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking** | Sensitised HAZ in stainless steels |
| **Reheat Cracking** | Precipitate-strengthened alloys in HAZ |

> [!WARNING]
> In **most structural failures involving welds**, the crack initiates in the **HAZ, not the weld metal**. The HAZ is therefore the *weakest link* in a welded joint and is the focus of weldability testing (e.g., **CTOD**, **Charpy V-notch**, **Implant testing**).

---

## 1.4 Peak Temperature Distribution Across the Weld Cross-Section

During a single arc pass, every point in the base metal experiences a **thermal cycle** characterised by:
- A rapid **heating rate** (often > 200 °C/s for arc welding).
- A **peak temperature** ($T_{peak}$) that decreases as the distance from the fusion line increases.
- A **cooling rate** (typically the slower of the two thermal excursions).

$$T_{peak} \;=\; f\!\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)$$

where $y$ is the perpendicular distance from the centreline of the weld. The peak temperature is **highest at the fusion line** and falls off in a roughly **inverse (power-law) manner** with distance — this is what creates the *banded* sub-zone structure of the HAZ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (Kou's Welding Metallurgy):** Peak temperature drops by approximately **50%** for every 1.5–2 mm increase in distance from the fusion line, for typical low-carbon steel arc welds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis: Sub-Zones of the HAZ

## 2.1 The Five Sub-Zones of the HAZ (for Plain Carbon / Low-Alloy Steel)

When welding **low-carbon steel** (the most common KTU exam scenario), the HAZ is conventionally divided into **five sub-zones** based on the peak temperature each region experiences, measured against the **iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium diagram**.

| # | Sub-Zone | Peak Temperature Range | Metallurgical Transformation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)** | $T_{peak} > 1100\,°C$ (just below solidus) | Extensive austenite grain growth → coarse prior-austenite grains → coarse **bainite / ferrite + pearlite** on cooling |
| 2 | **Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ)** | $A_{c3} < T_{peak} < 1100\,°C$ (≈ 900–1100 °C) | Complete austenitisation, but grain refinement due to recrystallisation → fine, equiaxed prior-austenite grains |
| 3 | **Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ / ICHAZ)** | $A_{c1} < T_{peak} < A_{c3}$ (≈ 727–900 °C) | Partial austenitisation — only some ferrite transforms; remaining ferrite is *"untouched"*. On cooling, new fine austenite pockets transform to **fresh martensite / bainite islands** in a soft ferrite matrix |
| 4 | **Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ)** | $A_{c1} - 200\,°C < T_{peak} < A_{c1}$ (≈ 500–727 °C) | No phase transformation. Only **tempering, ageing, and precipitation** of existing microstructures |
| 5 | **Unaffected Base Metal (BM)** | $T_{peak} < 500\,°C$ | No metallurgical change — original rolled/forged microstructure retained |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **0.2% C steel**, the critical temperatures are approximately:
> - $A_{c1} \approx 727\,°C$ (eutectoid)
> - $A_{c3} \approx 850\,°C$ (depends on alloy content)
>
> These values must be **memorised** for KTU numericals and short-answer questions.

---

## 2.2 The Weld Thermal Cycle

The thermal cycle experienced by a point in the HAZ is the **fundamental input** that determines its final microstructure. It is characterised by four key parameters:

1. **Peak Temperature ($T_p$)** — depends on the **heat input** and the **distance from the weld centreline**.
2. **Heating Rate ($dT/dt$|_{heat})** — typically very high near the fusion line.
3. **Time above $A_{c1}$ ($\Delta t_{1}$)**: time spent in the austenite region.
4. **Cooling Rate ($dT/dt$|_{cool})** — *the most critical parameter* for hardness, strength, and cracking susceptibility.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cooling rate between 800 °C and 500 °C** (denoted $\Delta t_{8/5}$ or $R_{800-500}$, measured in **seconds**) is the **single most important** parameter in weld metallurgy. It controls:
> - **Hardness** (and therefore HAZ strength)
> - **Microstructure** (ferrite, pearlite, bainite, martensite)
> - **Toughness**
> - **Hydrogen cracking susceptibility**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input ($H$ or $E$)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Energy delivered per unit length of weld. $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6 SMAW, 0.7 GMAW, 1.0 ESW), $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed | $\text{kJ/mm}$ or $\text{kJ/m}$ |
| 2 | **Heat Flux / Power ($P$)** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Total electrical power input to arc | $\text{W}$ or $\text{kW}$ |
| 3 | **Cooling Rate ($\frac{dT}{dt}$)** | $\frac{dT}{dt} = 2\pi k \cdot \rho c \left(\dfrac{v \cdot d}{H}\right)^{2} (T - T_{0})^{3}$ | **Adams' / Rykalin's** 2-D thick-plate cooling equation. $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat, $d$ = plate thickness, $T_0$ = preheat/interpass temp | $\text{°C/s}$ |
| 4 | **Cooling Time $\Delta t_{8/5}$** | $\Delta t_{8/5} = \dfrac{(H/\delta)^{2}}{2\pi k \rho c} \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{500 - T_{0}} - \dfrac{1}{800 - T_{0}}\right]$ | Time to cool from 800 °C to 500 °C — central weldability parameter | $\text{s}$ |
| 5 | **8% Rule (UIUC)** | $\Delta t_{8/5} = (8 \text{ to } 12)\%\times \dfrac{H^{2}}{d^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(T_{c} - T_{0})^{2}}$ | Empirical rule-of-thumb for thick plate HAZ cooling | $\text{s}$ |
| 6 | **Hardness (Vickers) – Yurioka** | $HV \approx 500 + 50 \cdot CE$ | Approx. martensite hardness from carbon equivalent | $\text{HV}$ |
| 7 | **Carbon Equivalent ($CE_{IIW}$)** | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | Used to assess weldability of low-alloy steels | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Peak Temperature ($T_p$)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_p - T_{0}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi e} \cdot \rho c \cdot y}{H / d} + \dfrac{1}{T_{m} - T_{0}}$ | Rosenthal's peak-temperature expression; $T_m$ = melting point, $y$ = distance from weld centre | $\text{K}$ or $\text{°C}$ |
| 9 | **Hydrogen Cracking Index (PCM)** | $P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | JIS / IIW critical $P_{cm}$ for HIC susceptibility | wt.% |
| 10 | **HAZ Width (empirical)** | $w_{HAZ} \approx \dfrac{k \cdot H}{v \cdot \rho c \cdot \Delta T}$ | Indicative width from heat conduction | $\text{mm}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** At least **one numerical problem per year** involves calculating the **heat input ($H$)** or the **cooling time $\Delta t_{8/5}$** using the formulas above. Always convert units carefully: $H$ in kJ/mm, $v$ in mm/s, $V$ in V, $I$ in A.

---

## 2.4 Why Each Sub-Zone Behaves Differently — The "Why" Explained

- **CGHAZ** sits *closest* to the fusion line. Here, the temperature is so high that the austenite grains grow massively (no grain-pinning second-phase particles survive at $T > 1100\,°C$). Coarse austenite → coarse transformation products → **lowest toughness in the entire weld joint**. This is the classic site of **intergranular HAZ cracking** in creep-resistant steels.

- **FGHAZ** experiences complete reaustenitisation but with grain-refining effects (dynamic recrystallisation, residual particle pinning). Result: **highest strength and best toughness** of all HAZ sub-zones — ironically, often *better* than the parent metal.

- **ICHAZ** is a "half-transformed" region. The untransformed ferrite remains soft, but the *newly formed* martensite–austenite (M–A) islands are hard and brittle. This **bimodal soft + hard microstructure** is the classic site of **intercritical HAZ cracking** in modern HSLA line-pipe steels.

- **SCHAZ** has no transformation, but undergoes **tempering**. In quenched-and-tempered steels, the SCHAZ experiences *over-tempering* — its strength *falls below* the parent metal strength, creating a **local soft zone** that promotes **reheat cracking** and **soft-zone stress corrosion cracking**.

- **Base Metal** is the unaffected reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb (Kou, *Welding Metallurgy*, 2nd ed., Wiley):** In a typical single-pass arc weld on low-carbon steel, the hardness profile across the HAZ is **NOT monotonic**. It is usually:
> Weld metal (high) → **CGHAZ (high, brittle)** → **FGHAZ (peak hardness)** → **ICHAZ (low — soft valley)** → **SCHAZ (slightly higher)** → **Base metal (nominal)**.
> The **ICHAZ "soft valley"** is what KTU examiners love to ask about.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The HAZ concept is the **cornerstone of weldability testing** and **procedure qualification**. Every **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** as per **ASME Section IX**, **ISO 15614**, or **AWS D1.1** requires qualification of the HAZ through:
- **Charpy V-notch impact testing** at the **fusion line + 1 mm, + 3 mm, + 5 mm** locations.
- **Hardness surveys** (HV10) per **ISO 9015** — typical acceptance limit: **HV ≤ 350** for sour-service (NACE) applications to prevent SSC.
- **Cross-weld tensile testing** to verify joint efficiency.
