$$A_{throat} = 0.707 \times 8 \times 100 = 565.6 \; \text{mm}^2$$

Compute the load capacity:

$$P = 565.6 \times 110 = 62{,}216 \; \text{N} \approx 62.2 \; \text{kN}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Strength of Fillet Weld Subjected to Combined Direct Shear and Bending Moment

This is a **frequently tested 14-mark KTU question pattern**.

**Problem Setup:** A bracket is welded to a column using **two vertical fillet welds** (one on each side of the bracket). A load **P** acts at a distance **e** (eccentricity) from the weld line, producing:
- A **direct vertical shear force** = P (transferred equally to each weld).
- A **bending moment** = M = P × e (producing additional shear stress in the welds).

**Step 1 — Direct shear stress in each weld.**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{P}{2 \times (0.707 \cdot s \cdot L)}$$

**Step 2 — Bending (secondary) shear stress in each weld.**
The polar moment of the weld group about its centroidal axis:

$$J = 2 \times \left[ \frac{(0.707 \cdot s \cdot L)^3}{12} + (0.707 \cdot s \cdot L) \cdot \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 \right]$$

The bending stress at the extreme fibre (distance **r = b/2** from the centroid):

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r}{J} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot (b/2)}{J}$$

**Step 3 — Resultant shear stress (vector addition).**
For a KTU board-level problem, the resultant is:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2 + 2 \cdot \tau_1 \cdot \tau_2 \cdot \cos\theta}$$

For welds on opposite sides, **θ = 0°** (the direct shear and bending shear are in the same direction at the critical point), giving:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2$$

**Step 4 — Equate to allowable stress and solve for s.**

$$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Given: P = 50 kN, e = 300 mm, b = 200 mm (distance between welds), L = 150 mm (weld length), τ_allow = 110 N/mm². Find the required leg size **s**.

Direct shear (per weld):

$$\tau_1 = \frac{50{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 150} = \frac{50{,}000}{212.1 \cdot s} = \frac{235.7}{s}$$

Bending moment: M = P × e = 50,000 × 300 = 15,000,000 N·mm.
Bending shear stress (extreme fibre, r = b/2 = 100 mm):

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r}{J} = \frac{15{,}000{,}000 \times 100}{J}$$

For two parallel welds, the approximate J:

$$J \approx 2 \times 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 = 2 \times 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 150 \cdot 100^2 = 2{,}121{,}000 \cdot s$$

So:

$$\tau_2 = \frac{15{,}000{,}000 \times 100}{2{,}121{,}000 \cdot s} = \frac{707.2}{s}$$

Resultant:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2 = \frac{235.7 + 707.2}{s} = \frac{942.9}{s}$$

Set equal to allowable:

$$\frac{942.9}{s} \leq 110$$

$$s \geq \frac{942.9}{110} = 8.57 \; \text{mm}$$

**Adopt s = 10 mm** (next standard size).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Joint Type vs. Design Parameter

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this matrix:** Each row is a KTU-typical design scenario. The columns give the critical formula, the failure mode, and the recommended joint type. Use this as a **decision-flowchart substitute** during exam revision.

| Loading Scenario | Critical Stress | Governing Formula | Recommended Joint | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure axial tension | Tensile | $P = s \cdot L \cdot \sigma_t$ | Butt (V-groove) | Plate tearing at HAZ |
| Pure axial shear | Shear | $P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau$ | Fillet (lap) | Throat shear |
| Bending (eccentric) | Combined | $\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2$ | Double fillet (tee) | Throat shear at extreme fibre |
| Torsion (twisting) | Combined | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2}$ | Closed weld loop | Combined throat shear |
| Fatigue (cyclic) | Variable | Modified Goodman / Soderberg | Butt preferred over fillet | Crack initiation at toe |
| Pressure (vessel) | Hoop + longitudinal | $\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}, \; \sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t}$ | Full-penetration butt | Leakage / rupture |
| Impact / Shock | Dynamic | Higher safety factor (2–3) | Butt with reinforcement | Brittle fracture |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Welded Joints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WELDED JOINT DESIGN SYSTEM"] --> B["JOINT GEOMETRY"]
    A --> C["WELD TYPE"]
    A --> D["LOADING CONDITION"]
    A --> E["DESIGN VERIFICATION"]

    B --> B1["BUTT JOINT"]
    B --> B2["LAP JOINT"]
    B --> B3["TEE JOINT"]
    B --> B4["CORNER JOINT"]
    B --> B5["EDGE JOINT"]

    C --> C1["FILLET WELD"]
    C --> C2["GROOVE WELD"]
    C --> C3["PLUG AND SLOT"]
    C --> C4["SPOT AND SEAM"]

    D --> D1["STATIC LOAD"]
    D --> D2["FATIGUE LOAD"]
    D --> D3["ECCENTRIC LOAD"]
    D --> D4["TORSIONAL LOAD"]

    E --> E1["THROAT THICKNESS CHECK"]
    E --> E2["SHEAR STRESS CHECK"]
    E --> E3["FATIGUE STRENGTH CHECK"]
    E --> E4["DEFLECTION CHECK"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fillet Weld Geometry and Stress Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["APPLIED LOAD P"] --> S1["DIRECT SHEAR STRESS TAU1"]
    P1 --> S2["ECCENTRIC MOMENT M"]

    S1 --> SUM["STRESS SUPERPOSITION AT CRITICAL POINT"]
    S2 --> SUM

    SUM --> TMAX["MAXIMUM THROAT SHEAR STRESS"]

    TMAX --> CHK{"TAU MAX LESS THAN ALLOWABLE"}

    CHK -- YES --> PASS["DESIGN ACCEPTED - ADOPT STANDARD SIZE"]
    CHK -- NO --> FAIL["REVISE S OR L - ITERATE"]

    PASS --> OUT["WELD LEG SIZE S, LENGTH L, EFFICIENCY ETA"]
    FAIL --> SUM
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Design Decision Process for a Welded Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: KNOWN LOAD AND PLATE THICKNESS"] --> S1["SELECT JOINT TYPE BASED ON GEOMETRY"]
    S1 --> S2["SELECT WELD TYPE FILLET OR GROOVE"]
    S2 --> S3["ASSUME INITIAL WELD SIZE S"]
    S3 --> S4["COMPUTE THROAT T EQUAL 0.707 S"]
    S4 --> S5["COMPUTE THROAT AREA 0.707 S L"]
    S5 --> S6["COMPUTE PRIMARY SHEAR STRESS"]
    S6 --> S7["COMPUTE SECONDARY STRESS BENDING OR TORSION"]
    S7 --> S8["SUPERPOSE TO GET TAU MAX"]
    S8 --> S9{"TAU MAX LESS THAN TAU ALLOW"}
    S9 -- YES --> S10["FINALIZE S AND L - DRAW WELD SYMBOL"]
    S9 -- NO --> S11["INCREASE S OR L AND REPEAT"]
    S11 --> S4
    S10 --> S12["END"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Weld Symbol Architecture (AWS A2.4 Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WS["WELD SYMBOL ON DRAWING"] --> AR["ARROW LINE POINTS TO JOINT"]
    WS --> RL["REFERENCE LINE HORIZONTAL"]
    WS --> SYM["WELD SYMBOL GROOVE FILLET PLUG"]
    WS --> DIM["DIMENSIONS SIZE LENGTH PITCH"]
    WS --> SUP["SUPPLEMENTARY TAIL INFORMATION"]
    WS --> SID["SIDE INDICATOR ARROW OTHER"]
    WS --> CON["CONTOUR SYMBOL FLUSH CONVEX"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note for Students:**
> - The **arrow side** of the reference line = welds on the side of the arrow.
> - The **other side** (below reference line) = welds on the far side.
> - A symbol placed **above** the reference line = weld on the **other** side of the joint.
> - A symbol placed **below** the reference line = weld on the **arrow** side of the joint.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a fillet weld. With a neat sketch, derive the relationship between throat thickness and leg size of an equal-leg fillet weld.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle (typically 90°). It is the most common weld type used in lap, tee, and corner joints.

For an **equal-leg 90° fillet weld** of leg size **s**, the cross-section is an isosceles right triangle. The hypotenuse (face of the weld) has length **s√2**. The throat thickness **t** is the perpendicular from the root to the hypotenuse, which equals half the hypotenuse:

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{s \sqrt{2}}{2} \\
t &= 0.707 \, s
\end{aligned}$$

A neat sketch shows the two legs (each = s) perpendicular to each other, the throat (t) drawn perpendicular to the hypotenuse from the right-angle corner, and the hypotenuse inclined at 45°.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for sketch + 1 for derivation)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a butt joint and a lap joint. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Member Position** | Edges lie in the same plane | Members overlap each other |
| **Weld Type Used** | Groove welds (square, V, U) | Fillet welds (along edges of overlap) |
| **Load Transfer** | Direct tension/compression along the axis | Shear across the weld throat |
| **Edge Preparation** | Required for thickness > 6 mm | Not required |
| **Strength Efficiency** | Higher (nearly 100% if full penetration) | Lower (stress concentration at overlap ends) |
| **Example** | Two plates joined edge-to-edge in a pressure vessel | Two sheets joined by spot welds in a car door panel |

**(3 marks: comparison table with at least 4 distinguishing points)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**Two mild steel plates, each 12 mm thick, are joined by a double-V butt weld. The joint is subjected to a static tensile load of 200 kN. The ultimate tensile strength of the weld metal is 410 N/mm² and the allowable shear stress is 110 N/mm². Determine:**
**(a)** Whether the joint is safe in tension if the plate width is 150 mm.
**(b)** The efficiency of the joint compared to the parent plate (assume plate UTS = 410 N/mm²).

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Tensile Safety Check (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the joint geometry.**
A double-V butt weld has full penetration, so the throat thickness = plate thickness = 12 mm.

**Step 2 — Compute the effective cross-sectional area of the weld.**

$$A_{weld} = t_{plate} \times b = 12 \times 150 = 1800 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the induced tensile stress in the weld.**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_{weld}} = \frac{200{,}000}{1800} = 111.11 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Check against allowable stress.**
For a butt weld in tension, the allowable stress is typically the **allowable tensile stress** = UTS / factor of safety. For static load with FOS = 1.5 (typical for KTU problems), the allowable is 410 / 1.5 = **273 N/mm²**, OR for a simpler KTU approach, use the given allowable shear = 110 N/mm² and the tension is taken as 0.9 × UTS = **369 N/mm²**.

**Using the conservative KTU approach:**

$$\sigma_t = 111.11 \; \text{N/mm}^2 < 273 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{SAFE in tension}$$

**Conclusion (7 marks):** The joint is **safe** under the given tensile load with a factor of safety of approximately **2.46** (273 / 111.11).

#### Part (b) — Joint Efficiency (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Strength of the parent plate (unwelded).**

$$P_{plate} = \sigma_{UTS} \times A_{plate} = 410 \times (12 \times 150) = 410 \times 1800 = 738{,}000 \; \text{N} = 738 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Strength of the welded joint (using allowable stress).**

$$P_{joint} = \sigma_{allow} \times A_{weld} = 273 \times 1800 = 491{,}400 \; \text{N} = 491.4 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} \times 100 = \frac{491.4}{738} \times 100 = 66.6 \; \%$$

**Alternative KTU approach using allowable shear:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tau_{allow}}{\sigma_{UTS}} \times 100 = \frac{110}{410} \times 100 = 26.8 \; \%$$

(Both answers are accepted depending on the convention adopted; the **66.6%** approach is the more rigorous and commonly expected KTU answer).

**Conclusion (7 marks):** The joint efficiency is approximately **66.6%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**A bracket carrying a vertical load of 60 kN is welded to a column flange using two parallel vertical fillet welds of length 180 mm each. The distance between the two welds is 200 mm. The load acts at an eccentricity of 300 mm from the weld line. The allowable shear stress in the weld is 110 N/mm². Determine the required weld leg size.**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Direct Shear Stress Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the loading components.**
- **Vertical force** on the weld group: P = 60,000 N.
- **Bending moment** about the centroid of the weld group: M = P × e = 60,000 × 300 = 18,000,000 N·mm.

**Step 2 — Assume weld leg size s and compute the throat area per weld.**

$$A_{throat} = 0.707 \times s \times 180 \; \text{mm}^2 \; \text{per weld}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the direct shear stress per weld.**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{P}{2 \times A_{throat}} = \frac{60{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times s \times 180} = \frac{60{,}000}{254.5 \, s} = \frac{235.7}{s} \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Part (a) answer:** $\tau_1 = \dfrac{235.7}{s}$ N/mm².

#### Part (b) — Bending Shear Stress and Final Size (7 marks)

**Step 4 — Compute the polar moment of the weld group.**
For two parallel fillet welds of length L, the polar moment is:

$$J = 2 \times A_{throat} \times \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 = 2 \times (0.707 \cdot s \cdot 180) \times (100)^2 = 2{,}545{,}200 \, s \; \text{mm}^4$$

(Ignoring the Ixx of each individual weld, as is standard in KTU problems.)

**Step 5 — Compute the bending shear stress at the extreme fibre (r = 100 mm).**

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r}{J} = \frac{18{,}000{,}000 \times 100}{2{,}545{,}200 \, s} = \frac{707.2}{s} \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute the resultant maximum shear stress.**
Since the direct shear and bending shear act in the **same direction** at the top and bottom extreme fibres:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2 = \frac{235.7 + 707.2}{s} = \frac{942.9}{s} \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 7 — Equate to allowable and solve for s.**

$$\frac{942.9}{s} \leq 110 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \geq \frac{942.9}{110} = 8.57 \; \text{mm}$$

**Part (b) answer:** Adopt the next standard weld size — **s = 10 mm** (or 8 mm if 8.57 is rounded down with FOS consideration, but **10 mm is the KTU-board-preferred answer**).

**Final Conclusion:** Required weld leg size = **10 mm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor:** Using leg size (s) as the throat (t) is the **#1 mistake** in KTU fillet weld problems. The throat is always **0.707 × s** for a 90° equal-leg fillet.
> 2. **Wrong stress direction in combined loading:** In an eccentrically loaded weld group, the direct shear and the bending shear at the **critical extreme fibre** (top or bottom) are in the **same direction** — they must be **added**, not vector-combined with cos(180°). At the **midpoint**, they are perpendicular and would use the Pythagorean sum.
> 3. **Ignoring the effective length:** A 2s deduction for crater defects is required if the problem says "assume continuous weld" but specifies small lengths. Many students forget this.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing **kN with mm²** without converting to N (×1000) is a frequent arithmetic trap.
> 5. **Confusing butt and fillet weld strength formulas:** A butt weld is designed for **tension** ($P = s \cdot L \cdot \sigma_t$), while a fillet weld is designed for **shear** ($P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau$). Using one formula in place of the other is a guaranteed 7-mark loss.
> 6. **Not standardizing the weld size:** The answer should always be rounded **up** to the next standard size (3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm) — never below.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it to your study wall before the KTU ESE.**

- **Five fundamental joint types:** Butt, Lap, Tee, Corner, Edge — know one real-world example of each.
- **Fillet weld throat:** Always **t = 0.707 × s** for an equal-leg 90° fillet weld. This is the most-tested relation in Module 2.
- **Butt weld throat = plate thickness** when full penetration is achieved.
- **Fillet weld strength (shear):** $P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau$.
- **Butt weld strength (tension):** $P = t_{plate} \cdot b \cdot \sigma_t$.
- **Joint efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} \times 100$ — never exceed 100%.
- **Combined loading rule:** Direct shear and bending shear are **added** at the extreme fibre (same direction) and **Pythagorean-summed** at the centroid (perpendicular).
- **Standard weld sizes (mm):** 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 22, 25.
- **Minimum weld size** depends on plate thickness (e.g., 3 mm for ≤ 10 mm plate, 4–5 mm for 10–20 mm plate, per IS 816).
- **Effective length:** Deduct **2 × weld size** for end craters if L is small.
- **Weld symbol rule:** Symbol **above** the reference line = weld on the **other** side; symbol **below** = weld on the **arrow** side.
- **Fatigue design:** Butt welds > fillet welds in fatigue resistance (stress concentration factor 1.0 vs 2.7).
- **Plug and slot welds:** Used only when lap joints are inaccessible for fillet welding; diameter ≥ (t + 8 mm) for plug welds.
- **Spot welds:** Pitch ≥ 3 × diameter of nugget; edge distance ≥ 2 × diameter.
- **Allowable stresses (typical KTU values):** $\tau_{allow}$ = 110 N/mm² for E6013 welds on mild steel; $\sigma_{allow, tension}$ ≈ 0.6 × UTS.

> **Final Exam Mantra for Module 2 — Joint Design:** *"Throat is 0.707, the rest is geometry; combine stresses vectorially, and always round the weld size up, not down."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residual Stresses and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Residual Stresses and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Residual Stress (KTU Board Definition):**
> Residual stresses are *self-equilibrating internal stresses* that remain locked within a manufactured component **after** all external processing loads (mechanical, thermal, or chemical) have been removed. They exist in the absence of any applied external force and are caused by non-uniform plastic deformation, phase transformations, or thermal gradients introduced during manufacturing processes such as welding, casting, machining, forming, and heat treatment.

In a manufactured component, the total stress state can be conceptually written as:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{applied} + \sigma_{residual}$$

When the component is in service (no external load), $\sigma_{applied} = 0$, and only the residual stresses remain active within the material microstructure.

### 1.2 Types of Residual Stresses (KTU Classification)

| Class | Scale / Nature | Source | Common Manufacturing Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macro (Type I)** | Component-scale, varies over large distances (mm to m) | Non-uniform plastic strain across the body | Welding shrinkage, forging, rolling, machining |
| **Micro (Type II)** | Grain-scale, varies between grains (~µm) | Anisotropic plastic flow in different grains | Cold working, phase transformations |
| **Sub-micro (Type III)** | Atomic-scale, within a few atomic spacings (~nm) | Lattice defects, dislocations, vacancies | Severe plastic deformation, shot peening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** KTU questions predominantly focus on **Type I (Macro) Residual Stresses** because they directly cause measurable distortion and dimensional inaccuracy in manufactured parts.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sun-Baked Wooden Plank:**
> Imagine a long wooden plank left in the sun. The *top surface* heats up, dries, and *wants* to shrink. But the *bottom surface* stays cool, moist, and resists shrinking. Because the two layers are glued together by the wood fibers, neither can fully move. The plank ends up **bowed (warped)**, with the top fibers under *tension* (being stretched by the wet bottom) and the bottom fibers under *compression* (being squeezed by the dry top).
>
> Even when the sun goes down and both layers reach room temperature, the plank **remains bowed forever**. The stresses inside it are *self-balancing* — no external force holds the bow, but the wood "remembers" its unequal shrinkage.
>
> This is exactly what happens in a welded joint, a cast plate, or a machined forging. *Unequal heating/cooling* or *unequal plastic flow* leaves permanent internal stresses and visible distortion — even when the part is unloaded.

### 1.4 Why KTU Cares About This Topic

In manufacturing, residual stresses are a **silent quality killer**:

- They reduce the **fatigue life** of components (especially under cyclic loading).
- They combine algebraically with service loads, potentially pushing a part past its yield point unexpectedly.
- They cause **distortion** — loss of dimensional accuracy and geometric form.
- They promote **stress-corrosion cracking** in aggressive environments.
- They affect **machining stability** (causing part movement during finish cuts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The **Residual Stress Magnitude** is typically expressed in **MPa (N/mm²)** or **psi**. In critical aerospace and nuclear components, residual stresses above **±100 MPa** are flagged for mandatory stress-relief treatment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin Mechanisms of Residual Stress in Manufacturing

Manufacturing processes generate residual stresses through **three fundamental mechanisms**:

#### Mechanism A — Thermal Gradients (Non-Uniform Heating/Cooling)

This is the dominant cause in **welding, casting, and heat treatment**.

When a localized region is heated:
1. The hot zone expands, but is constrained by the surrounding cooler material → **compressive thermal stress** in the hot zone.
2. When the source is removed, the hot zone wants to **shrink** on cooling.
3. The surrounding cold material resists this shrinkage → **tensile residual stress** develops in the previously hot region.
4. To maintain overall equilibrium, balancing **compressive stresses** appear in the surrounding material.

#### Mechanism B — Mechanical Plastic Deformation (Inhomogeneous Yielding)

This is the dominant cause in **machining, forging, rolling, bending, and shot peening**.

When a surface layer is plastically deformed:
1. The deformed surface layer wants to elongate (or compress) relative to the bulk.
2. The bulk material elastically constrains this → a locked-in stress field remains.
3. **Example:** In shot peening, the peened surface is plastically stretched → on elastic spring-back, it is left under **compressive residual stress** (highly beneficial for fatigue).

#### Mechanism C — Metallurgical Phase Transformations

Common in **heat treatment of steels** (e.g., quenching).

1. During quenching, the surface cools faster than the core.
2. The surface undergoes martensitic transformation (a hard, denser phase) before the core.
3. As the core later transforms, its volume expansion is restrained by the already-hard surface → **tensile residual stresses** form at the surface (unfavourable).

### 2.2 The Fundamental Equilibrium Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Equilibration Principle:**
> For any free body (no external load), the residual stress field **must** satisfy both force and moment equilibrium:

$$\int_A \sigma_{res}(x,y,z) \, dA = 0$$

$$\int_A \sigma_{res}(x,y,z) \cdot y \, dA = 0$$

$$\int_A \sigma_{res}(x,y,z) \cdot x \, dA = 0$$

This is why residual stresses are *tensile in some zones* and *compressive in others* — they balance out across the cross-section.

### 2.3 The Bi-Metal Strip Model (Classical Analytical Foundation)

The standard KTU/board analytical model treats a welded or bimetallic strip as **two bars of different materials (or different temperatures) rigidly bonded along the interface**.

Let:
- $E_1, E_2$ = Young's moduli of the two layers
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = coefficients of thermal expansion
- $\Delta T$ = uniform temperature change experienced
- $t_1, t_2$ = thicknesses of the two layers
- $L$ = original length

If the two layers were free, the free thermal strains would be:
$$\varepsilon_{th,1} = \alpha_1 \Delta T \quad , \quad \varepsilon_{th,2} = \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

But because they are bonded, a **compatibility force P** per unit width develops at the interface such that the total elongations become equal:

$$\Delta L_1 = \Delta L_2$$

$$\alpha_1 \Delta T \cdot L + \frac{P L}{E_1 t_1} = \alpha_2 \Delta T \cdot L + \frac{-P L}{E_2 t_2}$$

(Here the force is tensile in one layer and compressive in the other.)

Solving for the interface force per unit width:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}}$$

The corresponding residual stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{t_1} = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T \, E_1 \, t_2}{t_1 t_2 \left(\dfrac{1}{t_1} + \dfrac{1}{t_2}\right) \cdot t_1}$$

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T \, E_1 \, t_2}{t_1 + t_2} \quad , \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \, E_2 \, t_1}{t_1 + t_2}$$

### 2.4 Welding Residual Stress — The Classical Picture

In a single-pass butt weld on a plate, after cooling to room temperature, the **transverse residual stress distribution** looks like this:

| Zone | Longitudinal Residual Stress (along weld axis) | Transverse Residual Stress |
|---|---|---|
| **Weld metal (fusion zone)** | **Tensile**, often approaching yield ($\approx \sigma_y$) | Tensile at centre, compressive at edges |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Tensile, decreasing with distance | Variable |
| **Base metal (far from weld)** | Low / near zero | Compressive |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why weld metal is in tension:** During cooling, the weld metal tries to shrink ~0.1% but is clamped by the cold surrounding base metal. This clamping produces longitudinal tensile stresses equal to the *yield strength at room temperature* — a key KTU board insight.

### 2.5 Distortion — The Visible Outcome of Residual Stress

Distortion is the *macroscopic shape change* that occurs when residual stresses cause the part to relieve itself upon release of constraints.

**Types of Welding Distortion (KTU High-Yield):**

| # | Type | Mechanism | Visual Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transverse Shrinkage** | Weld bead shortens on cooling | Plate ends move closer together |
| 2 | **Longitudinal Shrinkage** | Weld contracts along its length | Plate overall shortens |
| 3 | **Angular (Out-of-Plane) Distortion** | Non-uniform shrinkage across plate thickness | Plate bends into a shallow "V" along weld line |
| 4 | **Buckling Distortion** | Compressive longitudinal stresses → plate instability | Wave/lobe pattern on thin plates |
| 5 | **Rotational Distortion** | Eccentric weld placement | Plate rotates around the weld |
| 6 | **Longitudinal Bending** | Asymmetric heating across thickness | Plate bends like a "C" along weld |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{applied} + \sigma_{residual}$ | Stress superposition principle |
| 2 | $\int \sigma_{res} \, dA = 0$ | Force equilibrium of residual stresses |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}}$ | Interface force in bi-material strip |
| 4 | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{(\alpha_2-\alpha_1)\Delta T \, E_1 t_2}{t_1 + t_2}$ | Residual stress in layer 1 |
| 5 | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{(\alpha_1-\alpha_2)\Delta T \, E_2 t_1}{t_1 + t_2}$ | Residual stress in layer 2 |
| 6 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal expansion |
| 7 | $\sigma_{res} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{plastic}$ (locked-in) | Stress from constrained plastic strain |
| 8 | $\delta_{shrinkage} = \dfrac{0.2 \cdot I_a \cdot V}{t^2}$ (empirical, weld distortion) | Empirical weld transverse shrinkage |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Shot peening introduces compressive residual stress on turbine blades and landing gear, multiplying fatigue life by 5–10×.
- **Pressure Vessels & Boilers:** Welded pressure vessels must undergo **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** at 595–705 °C to drop tensile residual stresses below ~25% of yield.
- **Automotive:** Stamped panels are designed with compensation strategies (springback prediction) to counteract bending residual stresses.
- **Civil Engineering:** Pre-stressed concrete uses *intentional* residual stresses to counter service loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Residual Stress in a Constrained Bar Cooled From $T_0$ to $T_f$

**Problem Setup:**
A uniform steel bar of length $L$, cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ is heated uniformly to temperature $T_0$. It is then rigidly clamped between two immovable walls and allowed to cool to $T_f$. Find the residual stress that develops in the bar.

**Given:**
- Length $L$ (m)
- Cross-sectional area $A$ (mm²)
- Young's modulus $E$ (MPa)
- Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ (per °C)
- Initial temperature $T_0$ (°C)
- Final temperature $T_f$ (°C)
- Temperature drop $\Delta T = T_0 - T_f$ (°C)

**Step 1 — Identify the constraint condition.**

The bar is clamped between rigid walls, so the total change in length must be zero:

$$\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_{thermal} + \Delta L_{mechanical} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Write the free thermal expansion (if unclamped).**

If the walls were not present, the bar would freely contract by:

$$\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Write the mechanical deformation caused by the reaction force.**

To prevent the thermal contraction, the walls exert a **tensile force $P$** on the bar. This force stretches the bar by:

$$\Delta L_{mechanical} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constraint equation.**

$$\Delta L_{thermal} + \Delta L_{mechanical} = 0$$

$$\alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T + \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the reaction force $P$.**

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = - \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

$$P = - \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A \cdot E$$

The negative sign indicates the force is opposite to the assumed direction; physically, the bar is in **tension** because the walls pull it back as it tries to shrink.

**Step 6 — Calculate the residual stress.**

$$\sigma_{residual} = \frac{P}{A} = - \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot E$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{residual} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_0 - T_f)}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical illustration.**

For a steel bar: $E = 200 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\Delta T = 500$ °C:

$$\sigma_{residual} = (200 \times 10^3) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 500$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 200{,}000 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 500$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 200{,}000 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 1{,}200 \text{ MPa}$$

This value **exceeds the yield strength of mild steel (~250 MPa)**, demonstrating that in practice the bar would yield locally and the *actual* residual stress would be capped at $\sigma_y$. This is the basis for the famous KTU board conclusion:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real clamped systems, residual stress cannot exceed the yield strength. The locked-in stress is therefore:

$$\sigma_{residual,\, actual} = \min\left[ E \alpha \Delta T , \; \sigma_y \right]$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Welding Distortion — Transverse Shrinkage on a Butt-Welded Plate

**Problem Setup:**
Two plates of width $W$, thickness $t$, and length $L$ are butt-welded with a single V-groove pass. The weld groove cross-sectional area is $A_w$. Determine the transverse shrinkage $\delta_T$ at the weld joint.

**Given:**
- Plate width $W = 500$ mm
- Plate thickness $t = 10$ mm
- Weld groove area $A_w = 60$ mm² (cross-section of deposited metal)
- Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Effective temperature drop of weld metal $\Delta T_{eff} = 800$ °C

**Step 1 — Compute the free thermal contraction of the weld bead.**

The weld metal, if unconstrained, would contract by:

$$\Delta L_{free} = \alpha \cdot L_{weld} \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$$

For a unit-length weld ($L_{weld} = 1$ mm):

$$\Delta L_{free} = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1 \times 800 = 9.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the classical empirical formula (Kristin & Roy formula) used in KTU board answers.**

The transverse shrinkage per unit length of weld is given by:

$$\delta_T = \frac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}{t + A_w / W}$$

For $A_w \ll t \cdot W$, this simplifies to:

$$\delta_T \approx \frac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$\delta_T = \frac{60 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 800}{10}$$

$$\delta_T = \frac{60 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10}$$

$$\delta_T = \frac{0.576}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_T \approx 0.0576 \text{ mm per mm of weld}}$$

For a weld of total length equal to the plate width (500 mm), the **total transverse shrinkage** is:

$$\Delta_{total} = \delta_T \times W = 0.0576 \times 500 = 28.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

A 28.8 mm shrinkage across a 500 mm plate is enormous and clearly visible — this is why multi-pass welding on thick plates uses **strong-backs**, **tack welds**, and **backstep sequences** to control distortion.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Causes vs. Effects vs. Remedies

| Process | Dominant Residual Stress Cause | Resulting Distortion | Standard Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding** | Thermal gradient + shrinkage | Transverse/longitudinal shrinkage, angular distortion, buckling | Preheating, backstep sequence, strong-backs, PWHT |
| **Casting** | Non-uniform cooling, phase change | Warping, hot tearing | Chill placement, riser design, controlled cooling |
| **Machining** | Non-uniform plastic deformation in cut layer | Part warpage after unclamping | Stress-relief before finish cut, symmetric machining |
| **Forging / Rolling** | Heterogeneous plastic flow | Barrel-shape, springback | Redressing, die-crown compensation |
| **Heat Treatment (Quenching)** | Martensite formation, thermal gradient | Quench cracks, distortion | Interrupted quench, austempering |
| **Shot Peening** | Surface plastic stretching | None (intentional compressive layer) | Used *as* a remedy for fatigue |
| **Bending / Forming** | Elastic springback | Part opens up after load release | Over-bending, stretch forming, bottoming dies |

### 3.4 Equilibrium Self-Balancing Proof

Consider a residual stress distribution $\sigma_{res}(x)$ across the cross-section of a free plate. By Newton's third law and force equilibrium:

$$\int_{-W/2}^{W/2} \sigma_{res}(x) \, dx = 0$$

This means the **area under the tensile portion** of the stress distribution must equal the **area under the compressive portion**. The figure in Section 4 illustrates this.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Residual Stress Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturing Process Input] --> B[Energy Deposition]
    B --> C[Thermal Gradient Field]
    B --> D[Mechanical Plastic Strain Field]
    B --> E[Metallurgical Phase Change Field]
    C --> F[Non-uniform Expansion Contraction]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Constraint by Surrounding Material]
    G --> H[Locked in Elastic Strain Energy]
    H --> I[Residual Stress Field Type I II III]
    I --> J[Self Equilibrating Distribution]
    I --> K[Macroscopic Distortion]
    I --> L[Fatigue and Crack Risk]
    J --> M[Stress Relief Operations]
    K --> N[Dimensional Correction]
    L --> O[Service Failure]
    M --> P[Controlled Acceptable Part]
    N --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Six Types of Welding Distortion (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Welded Plate Assembly] --> W1[Transverse Shrinkage]
    W --> W2[Longitudinal Shrinkage]
    W --> W3[Angular Out of Plane Distortion]
    W --> W4[Buckling Distortion on Thin Plates]
    W --> W5[Rotational Distortion]
    W --> W6[Longitudinal Bending C Shape]
    W1 --> R1[Reduce Weld Size Preheating Backstep]
    W2 --> R2[Balanced Welding Sequence]
    W3 --> R3[Symmetric Groove Strong Back]
    W4 --> R4[Stiffeners Lower Heat Input]
    W5 --> R5[Balanced Fixture Setup]
    W6 --> R6[Even Heat Distribution Preheat]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description — Transverse Residual Stress Profile in a Butt-Weld

A textual schematic of the residual stress variation across a butt-welded plate (transverse to weld axis), reading from far-field base metal → HAZ → weld metal → HAZ → far-field base metal:

```
   compressive        tensile (peak)       tensile       compressive
   (base metal)        (HAZ → weld)        (weld centre)  (HAZ → weld)   (base metal)
       ___                 __              ____              __            ___
      |   |               |  |            |    |            |  |          |   |
  ____|   |_______________|  |____________|    |____________|  |__________|   |____
                                                                  
   |←———————————— plate width W ————————————→|
   |←→| HAZ |←————→| Weld |←————→| HAZ |←→|
```

Key reading:
- **Far-field base metal:** $\sigma_{res} \approx 0$
- **HAZ:** moderate tensile stress
- **Weld centre:** peak tensile stress $\approx \sigma_y$ of weld metal
- **Plate equilibrium:** integral of curve over width = 0

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Process-Mechanism-Remedy Triad

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CauseGeneration
        A1[Heating] --> B1[Unequal Thermal Expansion]
        A2[Forcing] --> B2[Plastic Strain Mismatch]
        A3[Phase Change] --> B3[Volume Change Mismatch]
    end
    subgraph ResultManifestation
        B1 --> C1[Tensile at Hot Region]
        B2 --> C2[Compressive at Stretched Surface]
        B3 --> C3[Tensile in Hardened Skin]
    end
    subgraph ControlRemedy
        C1 --> D1[PWHT Preheating Backstep]
        C2 --> D2[Strategic Peening Stretch Forming]
        C3 --> D3[Interrupted Quench Austempering]
    end
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Relief Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Identify Process Source] --> Q{Distortion Tolerance Critical}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Apply Stress Relief]
    Q -->|No| T[Accept As Is]
    R --> R1{Process Type}
    R1 -->|Welded| R2[Post Weld Heat Treatment 595 to 705 C]
    R1 -->|Machined| R3[Vibration Stress Relief or Thermal]
    R1 -->|Cast| R4[Normalizing Annealing]
    R1 -->|Surface Sensitive| R5[Shot Peening Laser Shock Peening]
    R2 --> V[Verify with X ray Diffraction or Hole Drilling]
    R3 --> V
    R4 --> V
    R5 --> V
    V --> W[Certified Low Stress Component]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define residual stress. How is it classified at different length scales?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Residual stress is the internal stress that remains locked within a material **after** all external processing loads and thermal influences have been removed. It is self-equilibrating (no external force balances it) and arises from non-uniform plastic deformation, thermal gradients, or phase transformations.

Classification (Friedel classification):
1. **Type I — Macro residual stresses:** Vary over distances comparable to the component size (mm to m). Caused by macroscopic plastic flow and thermal gradients. Example: welding shrinkage.
2. **Type II — Micro residual stresses:** Vary between grains over micron distances. Caused by anisotropic plastic flow in different crystal orientations.
3. **Type III — Sub-micro residual stresses:** Vary over atomic distances. Caused by point defects, dislocations, and vacancies in the lattice.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for classification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*State two mechanisms by which welding produces residual stresses.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Thermal Shrinkage Mechanism:** During welding, a localised region is heated to very high temperature and tries to expand. The surrounding cold base metal restrains this expansion, creating compressive stresses. Upon cooling, the weld metal shrinks but is again restrained by the base metal, leaving **tensile residual stresses** in the weld zone.

2. **Phase Transformation Mechanism (in steels):** Rapid cooling causes martensitic transformation in the weld and HAZ, producing a volume change that is non-uniform across the thickness. The differential volume change creates additional residual stresses — typically tensile in the surface and compressive in the core.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for the two mechanisms.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **origin and distribution of residual stresses in a single-pass butt-welded plate**. Sketch the typical longitudinal and transverse residual stress profiles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel bar of length 2 m and cross-section 25 mm × 25 mm is heated to a uniform temperature of 400 °C and then rigidly clamped between two fixed walls. It is allowed to cool to 30 °C. Calculate the residual stress developed. Given: $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

When a butt weld is made on a plate and allowed to cool freely to room temperature, residual stresses develop because the weld bead and HAZ cannot shrink as much as they would if unconstrained.

**Origin:**
- During heating, the weld zone tries to expand but is restrained by the cold surrounding base metal.
- Upon cooling, the solidified weld bead and HAZ try to contract longitudinally and transversely.
- The surrounding base metal resists this contraction, locking in tensile residual stresses in and near the weld.

**Longitudinal residual stress distribution** (along weld axis):
- Far from weld: $\sigma_{long} \approx 0$
- In HAZ and weld: tensile, reaching a **plateau equal to the yield stress $\sigma_y$** of the weld metal at room temperature.
- The tensile region is balanced by small compressive stresses beyond.

**Transverse residual stress distribution** (perpendicular to weld axis):
- At the centre of the plate: high tensile
- Near the edges: compressive
- Again, integrated area = 0 (equilibrium).

A textual sketch:

```
Longitudinal profile (along weld axis):
   σ_y ┤        ┌──────────┐
       │        │          │
       │        │ weld/HAZ │         ← tensile plateau = σ_y
       │________│          │__________
       base    HAZ        weld        HAZ        base

Transverse profile (across plate width):
   σ_t,max ┤              ╱╲
           │             ╱  ╲
           │            ╱    ╲
   ────────┼───────────╱──────╲────────────
   -σ_c    │        edge║║centre
           │
           edge (compressive)   centre (tensile)
```

(7 marks: 2 for origin, 3 for longitudinal profile explanation, 2 for transverse profile explanation.)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 2000$ mm
- Cross-section $A = 25 \times 25 = 625$ mm²
- $T_0 = 400$ °C
- $T_f = 30$ °C
- $\Delta T = 400 - 30 = 370$ °C
- $E = 200 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C

**Step 1 — Apply the constrained cooling equation:**

$$\sigma_{residual} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\sigma_{residual} = (200 \times 10^3) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 370$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 200{,}000 \times 12 \times 370 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 200{,}000 \times 4{,}440 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\sigma_{residual} = 888 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with yield strength.**

For mild steel, $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa.

Since $888 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$, the bar would **yield locally** during cooling. The actual locked-in residual stress equals the yield strength (in the elastic-perfectly-plastic idealisation):

$$\boxed{\sigma_{residual, actual} \approx \sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa (tensile)}}$$

**Comment:** This demonstrates the critical engineering reality that residual stresses in real structures cannot exceed the material's yield strength. The calculated elastic value (888 MPa) is an *upper bound*; in practice, plastic flow relaxes the stress to the yield limit.

(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for substitution, 2 for numerical answer, 1 for comment.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **six major types of welding distortion**. Give one control measure for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bimetallic strip consists of a 2 mm thick aluminium layer ($\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E_1 = 70$ GPa) bonded to a 3 mm thick steel layer ($\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E_2 = 200$ GPa). The bonded strip is stress-free at 20 °C and is then heated uniformly to 220 °C. Determine the interface force per unit width and the residual stresses in each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**The six types of welding distortion:**

1. **Transverse Shrinkage:** The welded plates shorten in the direction *perpendicular* to the weld axis as the weld bead contracts on cooling.
   *Control:* Use minimum weld metal volume; backstep welding sequence.

2. **Longitudinal Shrinkage:** The welded assembly shortens along the *weld direction*.
   *Control:* Preset the parts with extra length before welding (welding allowance).

3. **Angular Distortion (Out-of-Plane):** Non-uniform shrinkage through the plate thickness causes the two plates to rotate out of plane, forming a shallow V or angle.
   *Control:* Use double-V or U grooves; balanced welding on both sides; strong-backs.

4. **Buckling Distortion (Wave/Lobe):** On thin plates, longitudinal compressive residual stresses cause the plate to buckle out-of-plane into a wavy pattern.
   *Control:* Use intermittent welds, lower heat input, add stiffeners.

5. **Rotational Distortion:** Occurs when welds are placed asymmetrically, causing the plate to rotate as it cools.
   *Control:* Use balanced symmetric weld placement and fixtures.

6. **Longitudinal Bending (C-Distortion):** When heat is applied asymmetrically through the thickness, the plate bends longitudinally into a C-shape.
   *Control:* Use balanced welding from both sides; preheat to reduce thermal gradient.

(7 marks: 6 × 1 mark each for one-line description + control, 1 mark for neat sketches.)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $t_1 = 2$ mm (Aluminium), $\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $E_1 = 70 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $t_2 = 3$ mm (Steel), $\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $E_2 = 200 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\Delta T = 220 - 20 = 200$ °C

**Step 1 — Compatibility equation.**

For the bimetallic strip to remain bonded and flat, the total elongations of the two layers must be equal:

$$\alpha_1 \Delta T + \frac{P}{E_1 t_1} = \alpha_2 \Delta T + \frac{-P}{E_2 t_2}$$

(Here $P$ is the interface force per unit width; positive $P$ puts layer 1 in tension and layer 2 in compression.)

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\frac{P}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{P}{E_2 t_2} = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T$$

$$P \left[ \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right] = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{1}{E_1 t_1} = \frac{1}{70 \times 10^3 \times 2} = \frac{1}{140{,}000} = 7.143 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per (MPa·mm)}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_2 t_2} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^3 \times 3} = \frac{1}{600{,}000} = 1.667 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per (MPa·mm)}$$

Sum:
$$\frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} = 8.810 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per (MPa·mm)}$$

RHS:
$$(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T = (12 - 23) \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = -11 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = -2.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P$.**

$$P = \frac{-2.2 \times 10^{-3}}{8.810 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P = -249.7 \text{ N/mm}$$

The negative sign means layer 1 (aluminium) ends up in **compression** and layer 2 (steel) in **tension** — because aluminium expanded more but was held back by the steel.

$$\boxed{P \approx -250 \text{ N/mm per unit width}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute residual stresses in each layer.**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{t_1} = \frac{-250}{2} = -125 \text{ MPa (compressive in aluminium)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{-P}{t_2} = \frac{+250}{3} = +83.3 \text{ MPa (tensile in steel)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify equilibrium check.**

Force in layer 1 = $\sigma_1 \cdot t_1 = -125 \times 2 = -250$ N/mm
Force in layer 2 = $\sigma_2 \cdot t_2 = +83.3 \times 3 = +250$ N/mm
Sum = 0 ✓ (Self-equilibrating confirmed.)

(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for calculation of $P$, 2 for residual stress computation, 1 for equilibrium verification.)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Residual Stress Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the yield-strength cap:** Students compute $E \alpha \Delta T$ and write down e.g. 888 MPa without checking against yield. **You lose 1–2 marks** if you don't comment that the actual stress is capped at $\sigma_y$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in bi-metal strip:** Many students get the direction of $P$ wrong. Always state explicitly which layer is in tension and which in compression based on which had higher thermal expansion.
> 3. **Skipping the equilibrium check:** KTU examiners *expect* you to verify $\sum F = 0$. Leaving this out costs 1 mark minimum.
> 4. **Confusing transverse vs. longitudinal:** "Transverse" = perpendicular to weld axis; "Longitudinal" = parallel to weld axis. Examiners will deduct for swapped definitions.
> 5. **Not stating "self-equilibrating":** Whenever you define residual stress, *always* include the phrase "self-equilibrating internal stress" — examiners explicitly look for it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Residual Stresses and Distortion":**
> - Residual stress = self-equilibrating internal stress present *without* any external load.
> - Three types: Macro (Type I), Micro (Type II), Sub-micro (Type III) — KTU mostly asks about Type I.
> - Three origins: **thermal gradient**, **plastic strain mismatch**, **phase transformation**.
> - In welded plates, longitudinal residual stress in the weld zone reaches **yield strength** at room temperature (most important KTU fact).
> - **Six types of welding distortion:** transverse shrinkage, longitudinal shrinkage, angular, buckling, rotational, longitudinal bending — know all six and one control measure each.
> - Key formula: $\sigma_{res} = E \alpha \Delta T$ (constrained uniform cooling).
> - Bi-metal strip force: $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}}$ — derive the formula, do not just quote.
> - Always verify $\sum F = 0$ across the cross-section (equilibrium).
> - Always comment on whether the calculated elastic stress exceeds yield.
> - Remedies: **PWHT** (595–705 °C), preheating, backstep welding, shot peening, vibration stress relief, annealing.
> - Shot peening is the *only* method that creates **beneficial compressive residual stress** to improve fatigue life.
> - Self-equilibration principle + free thermal expansion are the two pillars of every KTU derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defects in Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of fusion welding processes, a **weld defect** is defined as an *unacceptable discontinuity or imperfection* in the weld metal, heat-affected zone (HAZ), or parent metal whose presence deviates from the intended metallurgical, mechanical, or geometric specifications outlined in design codes (e.g., IS 9595, AWS D1.1, ASME Section IX).

Mathematically, a defect can be quantified as any deviation exceeding permissible tolerance limits:

$$\Delta_{\text{defect}} = \vert X_{\text{actual}} - X_{\text{specified}} \vert > \epsilon_{\text{permissible}}$$

where:
- $X_{\text{actual}}$ = measured geometric or metallurgical property
- $X_{\text{specified}}$ = design-specified value
- $\epsilon_{\text{permissible}}$ = code-allowed tolerance (function of joint type, load, service condition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** A *discontinuity* is any interruption in the normal structure of the weld (natural or man-made), while a *defect* is a discontinuity that fails to meet the acceptance criteria of the applicable code. All defects are discontinuities, but **NOT** all discontinuities are defects.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Glass Bottle" Mental Model

Imagine you are gluing two halves of a **sealed glass bottle** back together using molten plastic as an adhesive:

- If the molten plastic **doesn't fill** the entire gap → **Lack of Penetration**
- If tiny **air bubbles** get trapped inside → **Porosity**
- If the two halves don't **melt and mix properly** at the boundary → **Lack of Fusion**
- If the plastic pulls apart as it cools → **Crack**
- If the plastic melts through to the outside → **Burn-Through**
- If the bottle **warps and tilts** because of uneven heat → **Distortion**

Welding is essentially this exact process — except at temperatures of **1500°C–2500°C** with metals instead of plastic. Any small mistake in heat input, filler metal, shielding gas, or travel speed produces these "imperfections" called **defects**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Welding Defects (Master Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 demands mastery of **three classification axes**: source, location, and morphology.

| Classification Axis | Categories | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **By Location** | (a) External / Surface, (b) Internal / Subsurface, (c) Through-thickness | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **By Origin** | (a) Process-related, (b) Material-related, (c) Operator/Skill-related | ⭐⭐ |
| **By Morphology** | (a) Volumetric (porosity, slag), (b) Planar (cracks, lack of fusion), (c) Dimensional (undercut, distortion) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 1.4 Standardized Reference Standards (KTU-Highlighted)

The following standards govern weld defect acceptance levels — frequently cited in KTU theory questions:

- **IS 9595** — Indian Standard: Recommendations for metal arc welding of carbon and carbon-manganese steels
- **AWS D1.1** — American Welding Society Structural Welding Code (Steel)
- **ASME Section IX** — Welding and Brazing Qualifications
- **ISO 6520** — Classification of imperfections in metallic fusion welds
- **BS 4870** — British Standard for approval testing of welders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Symbol Note (ISO 6520):** Each defect carries a numerical code (e.g., 100 series = cracks, 200 series = cavities, 300 series = solid inclusions, 400 series = lack of fusion/penetration). This is a high-yield KTU exam fact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Systematic Catalog of Welding Defects

### 2.1.1 Cracks (ISO 6520 Code: 100 series) — **Most Critical Defect**

Cracks are **planar, sharp-edged discontinuities** that propagate under stress and are universally **REJECTED** regardless of size. They form when localized stress exceeds local fracture strength during the thermal cycle of welding.

#### (A) Hot Cracks (Solidification Cracks) — Code 104
- **When:** Form during solidification of the weld pool, at temperatures between **solidus and liquidus** (the "Brittle Temperature Range," BTR).
- **Why:** Liquid films exist along grain boundaries; shrinkage strains pull grains apart.
- **Appearance:** Longitudinal, transverse, or crater centerline cracks; visible under microscope as dendritic separation.
- **Causes:** High sulfur/phosphorus content, high depth-to-width ratio, excessive restraint, high travel speed.
- **Mathematical Criterion (Schaeffler-Campbell Progenitor Condition):**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{shrink}} + \varepsilon_{\text{restraint}} > \delta_{\text{min}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HOT CRACK}$$

where $\varepsilon_{\text{shrink}}$ = thermal contraction strain, $\varepsilon_{\text{restraint}}$ = external restraint strain, $\delta_{\text{min}}$ = minimum ductility in BTR.

#### (B) Cold Cracks (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking / HIC) — Code 102
- **When:** Form **hours or days** after welding completes, typically at temperatures **below 200°C**.
- **Why:** Hydrogen (from moisture, electrodes, oil, rust) diffuses into the HAZ, accumulates at tri-axial stress zones (martensite-austenite boundaries), and causes delayed brittle fracture.
- **Critical Susceptibility Index:**

$$\text{CSI} = \%C + \frac{\%Mn}{6} + \frac{\%Cr + \%Mo + \%V}{5} + \frac{\%Ni + \%Cu}{15}$$

- If $\text{CSI} > 0.45$, preheating to **150°C–250°C** is **MANDATORY** to slow hydrogen diffusion.

#### (C) Crater Cracks — Code 105
- **When:** Form at the termination point of a weld bead when arc is extinguished without proper crater filling.
- **Why:** Rapid solidification shrinkage at low weld pool volume; high shrinkage stress concentration.
- **Prevention:** Use **run-off tabs** or reverse-crater-filling technique (reverse travel 25 mm before arc off).

---

### 2.1.2 Cavities (Porosity) — Code 200 series

Gas pockets trapped in solidifying weld metal.

| Type | Morphology | ISO Code |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform porosity** | Evenly distributed spherical voids | 201 |
| **Cluster porosity** | Localized grouping (4–10 pores) | 202 |
| **Wormhole / Elongated** | Elongated tunnel-like cavities | 203 |
| **Surface porosity** | Open to surface (visible) | 204 |
| **Subsurface porosity** | Hidden, RT/UT detectable only | 205 |

**Formation Mechanism (Sievert's Law Governing Gas Solubility):**

$$S = K\sqrt{P}$$

where $S$ = gas solubility in molten metal, $P$ = partial pressure of gas above weld pool, $K$ = temperature-dependent equilibrium constant.

As the weld solidifies, $S$ drops sharply → excess gas is rejected → **bubbles form**.

**Critical Cause Matrix:**
- High sulfur content in steel → **CO** and **SO₂** porosity
- Damp electrodes → **H₂** porosity
- Inadequate shielding → **N₂** porosity
- Contaminated base metal (paint, oil) → **Hydrocarbon** porosity

---

### 2.1.3 Solid Inclusions — Code 300 series

Foreign material entrapped inside weld metal.

#### (A) Slag Inclusion (Code 301–304)
- Non-metallic solid material (oxides, flux residues) trapped between weld passes or at weld-parent interface.
- **Severe form:** Tungsten inclusion (GTAW without gas pre-flow / proper amperage) — codes as **410**.
- **KTU Pearl:** Slag in multi-pass welds indicates **inadequate inter-pass cleaning** between passes.

#### (B) Oxide Inclusion (Code 303)
- Aluminum, titanium, or silicon oxides trapped due to inadequate deoxidation in weld pool.

---

### 2.1.4 Lack of Fusion & Lack of Penetration — Code 400 series

The two **most exam-frequent** defects in KTU papers.

#### (A) Lack of Fusion (Code 401)
- Filler metal does **not fuse** with parent metal or previous bead along the sidewall.
- **Cause:** Insufficient heat input, dirty surfaces, incorrect torch angle.

$$\text{Heat Input} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

where $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min).

If $\text{Heat Input} < 0.6 \text{ kJ/mm}$ in carbon steel → high probability of **lack of fusion**.

#### (B) Lack of Root Penetration (Code 402)
- Root of the joint is **not melted through** the full thickness.
- **Cause:** Root gap too small, root face too large, low current, wrong polarity.

**Penetration Geometry Relationship:**

$$P_{\text{depth}} = 0.1 \cdot I \quad \text{(approximate, for SMAW on mild steel)}$$

where $I$ = welding current in amperes, $P_{\text{depth}}$ in mm.

---

### 2.1.5 Surface Defects — Code 500 series

#### (A) Undercut (Code 501–504)
- Groove melted into the parent metal at the **toe** of the weld, not filled by filler.
- **Cause:** Excessive current, long arc length, incorrect torch angle, high travel speed.
- **Acceptance Limit (IS 9595):** $\text{Undercut depth} \leq 0.5 \text{ mm}$ for butt welds in non-critical service; **zero tolerance** in fatigue-loaded structures.

#### (B) Spatter (Code 602)
- Droplets of molten metal ejected from arc, adhering to parent metal surface.
- **Cause:** Long arc, high current, magnetic arc blow, damp electrodes.
- **Severity:** Cosmetic in most cases, but signifies unstable arc → possible subsurface defects.

#### (C) Overlap (Code 505)
- Filler metal flows **over** the parent surface without proper fusion at the toe.
- **Acceptance:** Generally **rejectable** because overlap creates a stress riser at the toe.

---

### 2.1.6 Distortion & Warpage (Code 500/600 dimensional series)

Residual stress-induced geometric deviation from original shape. Governed by **Transient Thermal Stress Analysis.**

$$\sigma_{\text{residual}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f(\text{restraint})$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of the base metal
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion
- $\Delta T$ = temperature gradient across the joint
- $f(\text{restraint})$ = function of joint restraint (0 = free, 1 = fully restrained)

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Distortion Modes:** (1) Longitudinal shrinkage, (2) Transverse shrinkage, (3) Angular distortion, (4) Buckling distortion.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Criterion | Variables / Units | Defect Predicted |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta_{\text{defect}} = \vert X_a - X_s \vert > \epsilon_p$ | Dimensions (mm) | General definition |
| 2 | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000}$ | $\text{kJ/mm}$ | Insufficient HI → LF/LoP |
| 3 | $\text{CSI} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Dimensionless | Cold cracking susceptibility |
| 4 | $S = K\sqrt{P}$ | Sievert's Law | Porosity formation |
| 5 | $P_d \approx 0.1 \cdot I$ | mm (penetration depth) | Root penetration |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_r > \delta_{\min}$ | Strain | Hot cracking |
| 7 | $\sigma_r = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f(\text{restraint})$ | MPa | Distortion |
| 8 | $v_{\text{critical}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{A_{\text{bead}} \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}$ | mm/min | Undercut / HAZ width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Equation 8:** $v_{\text{critical}}$ is the *maximum safe travel speed* above which undercut occurs. $\eta$ = arc efficiency (SMAW ≈ 0.80, GTAW ≈ 0.60, GMAW ≈ 0.85), $\rho c$ = volumetric heat capacity of steel $\approx 5 \times 10^6 \text{ J/(m}^3 \cdot \text{K)}$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Critical Defect Concern | Inspection Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessels / Boilers** | Cracks, LoP, LF (catastrophic failure risk) | 100% Radiographic Testing (RT) |
| **Offshore / Subsea Pipelines** | Hydrogen cracking, corrosion-assisted cracking | Automated UT, Phased Array |
| **Aerospace (Ti alloys)** | Lack of fusion in thin sections | Eddy current + RT |
| **Automotive (Resistance Spot)** | Expulsion, weld nugget porosity | Cross-section metallography + Peel test |
| **Civil / Bridges** | Undercut, slag inclusion (fatigue initiation) | Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) |
| **Nuclear** | Zero-defect acceptance; HAC tracking | RT + UT + Helium leak test |

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Input Equation — From Physics to Practical Use

### Starting Principle
The **first law of thermodynamics** applied to the welding arc states that the energy delivered by the arc per unit length of weld is the heat input.

**Step 1 — Electrical power delivered to the arc:**

$$P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{Volts} \cdot \text{Amperes}]$$

**Step 2 — Not all electrical power reaches the weld pool.** Some is lost as radiant heat, spatter kinetic energy, and convection. The fraction retained is the **arc efficiency**, $\eta$.

$$P_{\text{absorbed}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

**Step 3 — Energy deposited per unit length of weld** (divide by travel speed $v$ in mm/s and convert to kJ):

$$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert J to kJ and seconds to minutes** (multiply by 60/1000):

$$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

> For **SMAW**, $\eta \approx 0.80$ is built into empirical charts, so the field form omits $\eta$:

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000}} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

### Worked Numerical Example
A welder uses SMAW at $V = 25 \text{ V}$, $I = 200 \text{ A}$, $v = 5 \text{ mm/s}$. Determine the heat input and identify whether it is suitable for a 10 mm thick low-carbon steel plate.

**Solution:**

$$\text{HI} = \frac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{300000}{5000} = 60 \text{ J/mm} = 1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Interpretation:** For 10 mm mild steel, the recommended heat input range is **1.0–1.8 kJ/mm**. The value 1.5 kJ/mm lies **within the acceptable band** → suitable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cold Cracking Susceptibility Index (CSI)

### Origin — Ito-Bessyo Carbon Equivalent Framework

The **critical cooling time** $t_{8/5}$ from 800°C to 500°C determines martensite formation. If this cooling time is too short, hard, brittle martensite forms in the HAZ, providing nucleation sites for hydrogen-induced cracks.

**Step 1 — Establish the base equation** relating alloy content to hardenability:

$$\text{CE}_{\text{IW}} = \%C + A \cdot \%Mn + B \cdot (\%Cr + \%Mo + \%V) + C \cdot (\%Ni + \%Cu)$$

**Step 2 — Empirical calibration** against actual cracking data from Japanese welding research (Ito & Bessyo, 1968) yielded:

$$A = \frac{1}{6}, \quad B = \frac{1}{5}, \quad C = \frac{1}{15}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting** the constants:

$$\boxed{\text{CSI} = \%C + \frac{\%Mn}{6} + \frac{\%Cr + \%Mo + \%V}{5} + \frac{\%Ni + \%Cu}{15}}$$

### Decision Criterion Table

| CSI Value | Preheating Required? | Typical Preheat Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| $< 0.30$ | Optional | None |
| $0.30 \leq \text{CSI} < 0.45$ | Recommended | 100°C–150°C |
| $0.45 \leq \text{CSI} < 0.60$ | Mandatory | 150°C–250°C |
| $\geq 0.60$ | Mandatory + PWHT | 250°C+ and Post-Weld Heat Treatment |

### Worked Numerical Example
A pressure-vessel steel contains: C = 0.20%, Mn = 1.2%, Cr = 0.5%, Mo = 0.3%, Ni = 0.4%, Cu = 0.3%, V = 0.05%. Determine if preheating is required.

**Solution:**

$$\text{CSI} = 0.20 + \frac{1.2}{6} + \frac{0.5 + 0.3 + 0.05}{5} + \frac{0.4 + 0.3}{15}$$

$$\text{CSI} = 0.20 + 0.200 + \frac{0.85}{5} + \frac{0.70}{15}$$

$$\text{CSI} = 0.20 + 0.200 + 0.170 + 0.0467$$

$$\boxed{\text{CSI} = 0.617}$$

**Decision:** CSI $> 0.60$ → **Mandatory preheat to ≥ 250°C + Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT).**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Porosity — Sievert's Law Application

### Background
Gas solubility in molten metal follows **square-root dependence** on partial pressure (for diatomic gases like H₂, N₂).

**Step 1 — Sievert's Law statement:**

$$S = K \sqrt{P}$$

At equilibrium, gas dissolved $\propto \sqrt{\text{partial pressure}}$.

**Step 2 — During cooling from liquidus to solidus:**

At temperature $T_1$ (molten): $S_1 = K(T_1) \sqrt{P_{\text{ambient}}}$
At temperature $T_2$ (solidifying): $S_2 = K(T_2) \sqrt{P_{\text{ambient}}}$

**Step 3 — The discontinuity at solidification:**

As $T$ drops below solidus, $K(T)$ drops **by a factor of ~10**, while the gas molecules remain. Therefore:

$$S_{\text{dissolved}} \gg S_{\text{equilibrium, solid}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Excess gas is rejected as bubbles}$$

**Step 4 — Bubble formation condition** (classical nucleation requires the dissolved gas to overcome surface tension):

$$\Delta P \geq \frac{2 \gamma}{r_{\text{critical}}} \quad \text{where} \quad r_{\text{critical}} = \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{RT \ln(S/S_0)}$$

If dissolved gas concentration $S$ exceeds equilibrium $S_0$ enough, bubbles nucleate and become **trapped porosity** if the solidification front moves faster than the bubble can float out (solidification velocity $v_s > v_{\text{Stokes}}$):

$$v_{\text{Stokes}} = \frac{2 (\rho_{\text{metal}} - \rho_{\text{bubble}}) g r^2}{9 \mu}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect-Prevention Reference Grid

| Defect | ISO Code | Primary Cause | Effect on Joint | Prevention Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot crack | 104 | High S/P, deep pool, restraint | Brittle fracture | Lower S/P, preheat, reduce depth/width |
| Cold crack (HIC) | 102 | Hydrogen + martensite + stress | Delayed brittle failure | Preheat, low-H electrodes, PWHT |
| Crater crack | 105 | Improper arc termination | Stress concentration | Run-off tab, crater fill technique |
| Porosity | 201–205 | Gas entrapment | Reduced load area | Clean base metal, dry electrodes, proper gas |
| Slag inclusion | 301–304 | Insufficient inter-pass cleaning | Stress riser | Wire brush, grind between passes |
| Lack of fusion | 401 | Low HI, dirty surface | Discontinuity plane | Increase HI, clean, correct angle |
| Lack of penetration | 402 | Insufficient root gap/heat | Reduced throat thickness | Increase current, root gap |
| Undercut | 501–504 | High speed, long arc | Stress concentration | Reduce speed, short arc |
| Distortion | 500/600 | Non-uniform heating | Geometric deviation | Pre-set, balanced welding, back-step sequence |
| Spatter | 602 | Long arc, magnetic blow | Cosmetic / unstable arc | Correct settings, anti-spatter spray |
| Overlap | 505 | Low travel speed | Toe stress concentration | Increase speed, reduce current |
| Burn-through | 506 | Excessive HI on thin plate | Hole through joint | Lower current, faster travel, backing |

---

## 3.5 Stress-Riser Severity Ranking (Critical for KTU Design Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *Which defect is most harmful?* The answer is always based on **stress concentration factor $K_t$**, not visual size.

| Defect Type | Approximate $K_t$ | Severity Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Through-thickness crack | $K_t \to \infty$ | 1 (highest) |
| Lack of fusion (planar) | $\approx 5$–$8$ | 2 |
| Sharp undercut | $\approx 3$–$4$ | 3 |
| Slag inclusion (elongated) | $\approx 2$–$3$ | 4 |
| Cluster porosity | $\approx 1.5$–$2$ | 5 |
| Spherical porosity | $\approx 1.2$–$1.5$ | 6 |

This ranking is why **cracks and lack of fusion** are *always rejectable*, while **porosity** may be acceptable up to a code-defined limit.
