| 3. Tool wear | None | Significant (electrode wear) |
| 4. Workpiece hardness | Irrelevant | Irrelevant |
| 5. Heat-affected zone | Absent (cold process) | Present (recast layer) |
| 6. Residual stress | None | High |
| 7. Surface finish | Excellent (Ra 0.2–0.8 μm) | Good (Ra 0.5–2 μm) |
| 8. Material removal rate | High for tough alloys | Lower for same alloys |
| 9. Electrolyte/dielectric | Required (conductive fluid) | Required (dielectric oil) |
| 10. Power consumption | Very high current, low voltage | Lower current, higher voltage |
| 11. Conductivity of workpiece | Must be electrically conductive | Must be electrically conductive |
| 12. Cost | High setup cost | Moderate |

**Two Industrial Applications of ECM:**

1. **Aerospace:** Machining of **titanium blisks and Inconel turbine blades** for jet engines.
2. **Automotive:** Production of **diesel fuel injection nozzles** with multiple micro-holes.
3. **Defense:** Shaping of missile bodies and gun barrel rifling.
4. **Tool industry:** Sharpening of tungsten carbide and PCD cutting tools (ECG variant).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the current efficiency $\eta$:** Students often compute only theoretical MRR, losing 1–2 marks. The examiner's key explicitly checks for $\eta \cdot MRR_{theo}$.
> 
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing $cm^3/s$ with $mm^3/s$ or forgetting to convert $g/cm^3$ consistency. Always show units at every step.
> 
> 3. **Confusing ECM with EDM:** In Part A definition questions, writing "spark erosion" or "thermal" for ECM is an instant **zero** for the definition.
> 
> 4. **Missing the role of hydrogen evolution:** Always mention both anodic dissolution (M → M^n+ + ne⁻) and cathodic hydrogen gas evolution (2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻). Writing only the anode reaction is incomplete.
> 
> 5. **Sign of voltage drop:** The applied voltage is dropped across (a) electrolyte resistance, (b) electrode overpotentials, and (c) anode-cathode gap. Always write $V_0 = V_{gap} + V_{overpotential} + V_R$.
> 
> 6. **Not writing Faraday's constant correctly:** Use $F = 96{,}485$ or $96{,}500$ C/mol — both are accepted, but be consistent.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ECM = Anodic dissolution** governed by Faraday's laws — **not thermal, not mechanical**.
- [x] **Workpiece = Anode (+), Tool = Cathode (−).** This is a one-line exam favorite.
- [x] **Faraday's First Law:** $m \propto Q$ (mass ∝ charge).
- [x] **Faraday's Second Law:** $m \propto \dfrac{A_w}{z}$ (mass ∝ equivalent weight).
- [x] **Master formula:** $MRR = \eta \cdot \dfrac{A_w \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ — units must be consistent.
- [x] **Equilibrium gap equation:** $S = \dfrac{\kappa V}{J}$ — gap thickness is inversely proportional to current density.
- [x] **Voltage range:** 5–25 V DC. **Current range:** 100–40,000 A. **Current density:** 20–200 A/cm².
- [x] **Electrolytes:** NaCl (cheap, roughing) vs NaNO₃ (passivating, finishing).
- [x] **By-products:** Metal hydroxides (sludge) and **H₂ gas**.
- [x] **No tool wear, no HAZ, no residual stress** — three "no's" that always fetch full marks.
- [x] **ECM is hardness-independent** — can cut carbides, Inconel, titanium, hardened tool steel.
- [x] **Overcut** = the extra material dissolved beyond the tool profile due to electrolyte conductivity at the sides.
- [x] **Feed rate must equal MRR/A** for steady-state machining.
- [x] **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$.
- [x] **Variants to remember:** Standard ECM, ECD, ECG, PECM, WECM, Jet ECM.
- [x] **Applications:** Aerospace blisks, diesel injectors, surgical implants, gun barrels, carbide tool sharpening.
- [x] **Limitation:** Only for **electrically conductive** materials — **cannot machine ceramics, glass, or plastics** (this distinguishes it from processes like laser or abrasive jet machining).
- [x] **Current efficiency $\eta$** is typically **90–100%** in ECM (much higher than in electroplating, where it can be 60–80%).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a **non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process** in which the desired shape is obtained by **controlled, repetitive electrical spark discharges (sparks)** occurring between a **tool electrode (cathode)** and a **workpiece (anode)** submerged in a **dielectric fluid**. The intense localized heat (temperatures of **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**) generated by each spark melts and vaporises a tiny volume of the workpiece, producing a crater on every discharge.

The process is governed by the principle of **eroding metal through a series of controlled, rapidly recurring spark discharges**, hence the process is also popularly called **Spark Erosion Machining (SEM)** or **Die-Sinking EDM**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Lightning Sculptor"**
>
> Imagine you want to carve a complex 3D shape into a hard metal block (say, hardened tool steel) that a regular drill bit cannot touch. Instead of using a cutting tool, you place the metal block in a tank of oil (dielectric fluid) and bring a shaped copper electrode close to it (gap ≈ 0.025 mm to 0.05 mm). When you switch on the power, **thousands of tiny lightning bolts (sparks) jump across the gap every second**. Each spark is so hot that it instantly melts and vaporises a microscopic speck of metal — like a controlled mini-thunderbolt that chips away the surface one spark at a time. The shaped copper electrode does not touch the workpiece; it just guides the sparks. As the sparks "drill" the part, the electrode is slowly fed downward so the new sparks always find fresh metal. The oil flushes away the vaporised debris and cools the region. After some time, a perfect negative impression of the copper electrode is left in the steel — exactly like a **wax-seal stamp**, but produced by **electricity and heat** instead of pressure.

### 1.3 Process Variants (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Variant | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Die-Sinking / Sinker EDM (Conventional EDM)** | A shaped electrode plunges vertically into the workpiece. | Dies, moulds, cavities. |
| **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** | A continuously moving thin brass wire (Ø 0.1–0.3 mm) is used to cut 2-D profiles like a band-saw. | Stamping dies, intricate 2-D parts. |
| **Hole-Drilling EDM** | A rotating tubular electrode drills small deep holes. | Cooling holes in turbine blades. |

### 1.4 Key Process Requirements (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the spark to strike, **three conditions are mandatory**:
> 1. The tool and workpiece must be **electrically conductive** (resistivity < 100–150 Ω·cm is preferred).
> 2. A **dielectric medium** (deionised water or EDM oil) must fill the gap.
> 3. The **gap voltage** must be sufficient to ionise the dielectric (typical open-circuit voltage = 60 V to 250 V DC).

The gap is the smallest **inter-electrode gap (IEG)** in conventional machining — usually **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm**, which is **10× smaller than a human hair** (~0.07 mm).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Material Removal (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> The EDM cycle on a single spark can be broken into four distinct phases. KTU examiners love asking a "describe the EDM mechanism" question (4–8 marks), so memorise this sequence.

**Phase 1 – Dielectric Ionisation & Spark Initiation**
- A high open-circuit voltage is applied across the tool and workpiece, separated by a small gap filled with dielectric fluid.
- The intense electric field (≈ 10⁶ V/mm) **ionises** the dielectric molecules, creating a **plasma channel** (ionised gas).
- Free electrons accelerate from the cathode (tool) toward the anode (workpiece) under the influence of the applied potential difference.

**Phase 2 – Spark Discharge & Heat Generation**
- A **stream of high-velocity electrons** strikes the anode (workpiece), and a column of ionised particles (plasma) forms a conducting bridge.
- Current density in the spark channel reaches **10⁵ to 10⁷ A/cm²**, producing a local temperature of **8,000 °C – 12,000 °C** in < 10 µs.
- A small volume of workpiece metal at the spark site is **instantly melted and vaporised**.

**Phase 3 – Crater Formation**
- The vaporised metal expands explosively, forming a **hemispherical / spherical crater** on the workpiece.
- A small portion of metal re-solidifies as **re-cast layer** (white layer) at the crater rim.
- The molten debris (swarf) is ejected radially outwards.

**Phase 4 – Dielectric Flush & Debris Removal**
- The dielectric fluid flushes away the eroded particles, cools the area, and restores electrical insulation.
- The pulse generator disconnects the circuit (off-time), allowing the dielectric to de-ionise before the next spark.

### 2.2 The EDM Pulse Generator (Power Supply)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **power supply (pulse generator)** is the heart of EDM. It must convert a continuous 50 Hz AC into a precisely controlled series of DC pulses.

| Pulse Generator Type | Construction | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **RC (Relaxation Circuit)** | Resistor + Capacitor charging-discharging | Older, used for roughing; simple pulse frequency but high peak current |
| **Transistorised Pulse Generator** | DC source switched by high-speed transistors | Modern, used for finishing; high-frequency, low-amperage pulses |
| **Electrochemical Pulse Generator** | Specialised CNC-controlled units | Used in micro-EDM and finishing |

### 2.3 Process Parameters Affecting EDM

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on MRR / Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc}$ | 60 – 250 V | Higher $V_{oc}$ → larger gap, higher MRR, poorer finish |
| Discharge current | $I$ | 1 – 500 A | Higher $I$ → higher MRR, rougher surface |
| Pulse on-time (τ_on) | $T_{on}$ | 1 µs – 2000 µs | Longer pulse → deeper crater, more material removed |
| Pulse off-time (τ_off) | $T_{off}$ | 1 µs – 100 µs | Longer off-time → better flushing, stable process |
| Duty cycle | $\tau$ | — | $\tau = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$ |
| Dielectric | — | EDM oil / deionised water | Better dielectric → better surface finish |
| Electrode material | — | Cu, brass, graphite, W, Mo | Affects wear ratio and finish |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise the table below. The first four equations are asked in nearly every KTU university exam question on EDM.**

| S. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of one crater (hemispherical) | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{12}$ (≈ cone) or $V_c = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3$ (true hemisphere) | mm³ | $d$ = crater diameter |
| 2 | Material Removal Rate (volumetric) | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V_c \cdot f}{1000}$ where $f$ = spark frequency (Hz) | mm³/s or mm³/min | Multiply by 60 for per-minute |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (mass) | $\text{MRR}_m = \dfrac{\text{MRR}_v \cdot \rho}{1000}$ | g/min | $\rho$ in g/cm³, MRR_v in cm³/min |
| 4 | MRR by energy balance | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{J \cdot \rho}$ | cm³/s | $J$ = specific heat of work, $\rho$ = density |
| 5 | Power supplied | $P = V \cdot I$ | W | $V$ = discharge voltage, $I$ = current |
| 6 | Pulse frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Hz | — |
| 7 | Duty factor (Duty cycle) | $\tau = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | dimensionless | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | Average current (transistorised) | $I_{avg} = \tau \cdot I_p$ | A | $I_p$ = peak current |
| 9 | Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) | $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of tool wear}}{\text{Volume of workpiece removed}}$ | dimensionless | Lower TWR = better electrode |
| 10 | Theoretical MRR (heat-balance) | $\text{MRR}_{th} = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{J \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta T}$ | cm³/s | $\eta$ ≈ 0.25 to 0.40 spark efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder (KTU Pitfall):** Never use the symbol `|` for absolute value inside a table row. KTU valuation scripts may parse `|` as a column separator and corrupt the answer sheet rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Machining of high-temperature alloys (Inconel, Waspalloy) and titanium that are difficult to cut conventionally.
- **Tool-and-Die Industry:** Sinking of forging dies, injection moulds, extrusion dies, and wire-drawing dies.
- **Medical:** Fabrication of orthopaedic implants from cobalt-chrome and stainless steel.
- **Electronics:** Drilling micro-holes in fuel-injector nozzles and printed-circuit-board heat sinks.
- **Automotive:** Hardened-steel gear and shaft machining without prior annealing (no softening needed).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume of a Hemispherical EDM Crater

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Assumption (used by KTU board):** Each spark produces a **hemispherical crater** on the workpiece. This is acceptable for low-energy sparks where the plasma channel has had time to expand spherically.

**Step 1 — Start with the volume formula of a sphere.**

$$
V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3
$$

**Step 2 — Take half the sphere to represent a hemisphere.**

$$
V_{hemisphere} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3
$$

**Step 3 — Express the radius in terms of the crater diameter** $d_c$**, where** $r = \dfrac{d_c}{2}$**.**

$$
V_c = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left(\frac{d_c}{2}\right)^3
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the cubic term.**

$$
V_c = \frac{2}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{d_c^{\,3}}{8} = \frac{\pi \, d_c^{\,3}}{12}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_c = \frac{\pi \, d_c^{\,3}}{12}}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check / Dimensional verification.**
- $\pi$ is dimensionless.
- $d_c^{\,3}$ has units of (mm)³.
- Therefore $V_c$ has units of mm³, which is the correct unit for a small crater. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) from Spark Frequency

**Step 1 — Define MRR as the volume removed per unit time.**

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{\text{Total volume removed}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{V_{total}}{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Express total volume in terms of the number of sparks.**

If $N$ sparks strike the workpiece in time $t$, each spark removes volume $V_c$.

$$
V_{total} = N \cdot V_c
$$

**Step 3 — Define the spark frequency as sparks per second.**

$$
f = \frac{N}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = f \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the MRR equation.**

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{f \cdot t \cdot V_c}{t} = f \cdot V_c
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = f \cdot V_c = f \cdot \frac{\pi \, d_c^{\,3}}{12}}
$$

**Step 5 — Unit conversion (KTU often uses mm³/min).**

$$
\text{MRR}\left(\frac{mm^3}{min}\right) = \text{MRR}\left(\frac{mm^3}{s}\right) \times 60
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — MRR from Energy Balance (Most Asked in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation links MRR directly to the **electrical input power** and is the foundation of all numerical problems. It is based on the **heat-balance equation** of EDM.

**Step 1 — Total electrical energy supplied per second = Power.**

$$
P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

**Step 2 — Only a fraction** $\eta$ **of this energy is actually used to melt the workpiece (rest is lost to dielectric, electrode, plasma, etc.).**

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

**Step 3 — Heat required to raise the volume of metal to the melting point and then melt it.**

For unit volume of work material of density $\rho$ and specific heat $J$, the heat required to raise it to a temperature rise $\Delta T$ and supply latent heat $L$:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot (J \cdot \Delta T + L) \quad \text{(per unit volume)}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate useful power to the heat absorbed by the volume removed per second.**

$$
\eta \cdot V \cdot I = \text{MRR} \cdot \rho \cdot (J \cdot \Delta T + L)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for MRR.**

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{\rho \cdot (J \cdot \Delta T + L)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Approximate form (commonly used in KTU, ignoring latent heat and using a single "effective" specific heat term).**

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx \frac{V \cdot I}{J \cdot \rho}}
$$

where $J$ here is the **effective specific heat** of the workpiece material and $\rho$ its density.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solved Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following are KTU-pattern numericals with **exhaustive step-by-step solutions** so the student can replicate them in the exam.

---

**Example 1 — Crater volume and MRR from spark parameters**

> A sinker EDM produces hemispherical craters of diameter **0.4 mm** on a steel workpiece. The spark frequency is **2,500 Hz**. Calculate the volume of each crater and the volumetric MRR in mm³/s.

**Step 1 — Apply the crater-volume formula.**

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \, d_c^{\,3}}{12} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.4)^3}{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cubic value.**

$$
(0.4)^3 = 0.064 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by** $\pi$ **and divide by 12.**

$$
V_c = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.064}{12} = \frac{0.2011}{12} = 0.01676 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the MRR formula.**

$$
\text{MRR} = f \cdot V_c = 2500 \cdot 0.01676
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical value.**

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 41.9 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm³/min (KTU often asks this).**

$$
\text{MRR} = 41.9 \times 60 = 2514 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 2.51 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

**Example 2 — MRR by energy balance**

> An EDM operates at a discharge voltage of **25 V** and a current of **20 A** with spark efficiency **30%**. The workpiece is steel with density **7.85 g/cm³** and effective specific heat **1.5 J/(g·K)**. Compute the MRR in cm³/s.

**Step 1 — Compute input power.**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 25 \cdot 20 = 500 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute useful power.**

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P = 0.30 \cdot 500 = 150 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy term** $\rho \cdot J$ **(interpret** $J$ **as a temperature-rise coefficient).**

$$
\rho \cdot J = 7.85 \cdot 1.5 = 11.775 \text{ J/(cm}^3 \cdot \text{K)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the MRR equation.** (Assuming temperature rise is 1 K unit basis, the term reduces to $\rho J$ directly.)

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{P_{useful}}{\rho \cdot J} = \frac{150}{11.775}
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical value.**

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 12.74 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

**Example 3 — Process time for machining a cavity**

> A die cavity of volume **8,000 mm³** is to be machined by EDM. If the MRR is **50 mm³/min**, calculate the machining time (ignoring tool wear and set-up).

**Step 1 — Recall the basic time formula.**

$$
T = \frac{\text{Volume to be removed}}{\text{MRR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
T = \frac{8000 \text{ mm}^3}{50 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Final value.**

$$
\boxed{T = 160 \text{ min} = 2 \text{ hours } 40 \text{ min}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Node Rule Check:** Every node ID below is alphanumeric (e.g., `toolElec`, `workPiece`, `sparkGap`) and is prefixed with letters. No reserved keyword is used as a node name. Labels contain no `**` or `*` markers. The diagram is rendered as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the EDM system, which is the cleanest KTU-compliant representation.

### 4.1 EDM System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[DC Power Supply and Pulse Generator]
    TOOL[Tool Electrode Cathode Shaped]
    WKPC[Workpiece Anode Conductive Metal]
    GAP[Inter Electrode Gap 0.01 to 0.05 mm]
    PLASMA[Plasma Channel Formed by Spark]
    HEAT[Localized Heat 8000 to 12000 C]
    MELT[Melting and Vaporisation of Workpiece]
    CRAT[Hemispherical Crater Formed]
    FLUSH[Dielectric Fluid Flushes Debris]
    DIEL[Tank Filled with Dielectric Oil or Deionised Water]
    SERVO[Servo Control System Maintains Constant Gap]
    SINK[Tool Feeds Downwards to Maintain Depth]
    FINAL[Negative Impression of Tool is Produced]

    PSU -- High voltage DC pulse --> GAP
    TOOL -- Approaches workpiece --> GAP
    WKPC -- Mounted on table --> GAP
    GAP -- Ionisation by electric field --> PLASMA
    PLASMA -- Concentrates energy --> HEAT
    HEAT -- Causes rapid phase change --> MELT
    MELT -- Creates cavity --> CRAT
    CRAT -- Generates swarf particles --> FLUSH
    FLUSH -- Uses flow from --> DIEL
    SERVO -- Monitors gap voltage --> GAP
    SERVO -- Sends signal to --> SINK
    SINK -- Moves tool further into workpiece --> CRAT
    CRAT -- After repeated sparks --> FINAL
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Wire-EDM (WEDM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPOOL[Brass Wire Spool 0.1 to 0.3 mm Diameter]
    TEN[Tensioning and Guide System]
    WCUT[Wire Guides X and Y Movement CNC Controlled]
    WTBL[Workpiece Table with Stiff Fixing]
    WSPARK[Spark Zone Submerged in Deionised Water]
    WGEN[Pulse Generator for Wire EDM]
    WSINK[Continuous Cut Produced in Workpiece]
    WCHIP[Chip Particles Flushed by Water Jet]

    SPOOL --> TEN
    TEN --> WCUT
    WCUT --> WSPARK
    WTBL --> WSPARK
    WGEN --> WSPARK
    WSPARK --> WSINK
    WSPARK --> WCHIP
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Sinker EDM vs Wire EDM

| Feature | Sinker EDM | Wire EDM |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Shaped 3-D tool | Continuous thin wire |
| Movement | Vertical plunge | 2-D contour (CNC) |
| Typical accuracy | ± 0.01 mm | ± 0.002 – 0.005 mm |
| Surface finish Ra | 0.8 – 3.2 µm | 0.2 – 1.6 µm |
| Dielectric | EDM oil | Deionised water |
| Common application | Cavities, dies | 2-D profiles, sharp corners |

### 4.4 Tool-Electrode Material Selection (Schematic Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need high MRR and roughing?}
    Q2{Need fine finish and complex detail?}
    Q3{Need to cut exotic high melting point metals?}
    ANS1[Use GRAPHITE Electrode Good MRR Cheap Easy to Shape]
    ANS2[Use COPPER Electrode Fine Finish Low Wear on Hard Steel]
    ANS3[Use BRASS Wire Standard for Wire EDM]
    ANS4[Use TUNGSTEN or MOLYBDENUM Electrode for Difficult Alloys]

    Q1 -- YES --> ANS1
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2
    Q2 -- YES --> ANS2
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3
    Q3 -- YES --> ANS4
    Q3 -- NO --> ANS3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — *Define Electro Discharge Machining. List any **three** essential conditions required for the EDM process to occur.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** EDM is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which material is removed from a conductive workpiece by a series of rapidly recurring electrical sparks discharged between a tool electrode and the workpiece submerged in a dielectric fluid.

> **Three essential conditions (1.5 marks):**
> 1. The **tool and workpiece must be electrically conductive** (both must be able to pass current).
> 2. The **inter-electrode gap must be very small (0.01 – 0.05 mm)** and filled with a suitable **dielectric fluid** (EDM oil or deionised water).
> 3. A **sufficiently high open-circuit voltage (60 – 250 V)** must be applied to ionise the dielectric and initiate each spark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — *Why is the workpiece and tool always submerged in a **dielectric fluid** during EDM? State any **two** functions of the dielectric.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The dielectric fluid is essential because it acts as an **insulator** at rest and an **ionising medium** under high voltage. (1 Mark)

> **Two functions of the dielectric (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Insulation & Spark Confinement** — Prevents short-circuiting between tool and workpiece when no spark is active, and confines each spark to a localised channel, ensuring precise material removal.
> 2. **Flushing & Cooling** — Carries away the molten/vaporised debris (swarf) from the gap and cools the workpiece, the tool, and the spark zone, preventing thermal damage and stabilising the process.
> 3. (Optional third) **Debris removal & de-ionisation** during the off-time, allowing stable sparking.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (Module Internal Choice, 2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle and working of the die-sinking EDM process**. Discuss the **mechanism of material removal** phase by phase.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 Mark):**
> EDM (Electro Discharge Machining) is a non-contact, non-traditional machining process in which material is removed by the **thermal energy of electrical sparks** generated between a shaped tool electrode and the conductive workpiece, both immersed in a dielectric fluid.

> **Schematic Description (2 Marks):**
> The setup consists of: (i) a **DC pulse generator** providing short-duration pulses of voltage and current, (ii) a **shaped tool electrode** (cathode) made of copper/graphite, (iii) a **workpiece** (anode) mounted on a servo-controlled table, (iv) a **dielectric tank** filled with EDM oil or deionised water, and (v) a **servo feed mechanism** that maintains a constant small gap (≈ 0.025 mm). The tool is fed vertically downward as the workpiece erodes.

> **Mechanism of Material Removal — Four Phases (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Dielectric Ionisation:** A high voltage (~ 200 V) is applied across the small gap. The strong electric field ionises the dielectric, producing a plasma channel of free electrons and ions.
> 2. **Spark Discharge:** Electrons accelerate from cathode to anode. A high-density current pulse (~ 10⁶ A/cm²) creates a local temperature of **8,000 – 12,000 °C**, melting and vaporising a small volume of the workpiece.
> 3. **Crater Formation:** The vaporised metal explodes outward, leaving a hemispherical crater. A re-cast (white) layer of re-solidified metal forms at the rim.
> 4. **Dielectric Flush:** The off-time period allows the dielectric to carry away the swarf, cool the region, and de-ionise. The cycle repeats at a spark frequency of 1 kHz – 500 kHz.

> **Conclusion / Key Features (0 Marks — integrated above):**
> The tool does not physically touch the workpiece, so there is no mechanical stress, vibration, or tool deflection. This makes EDM ideal for very hard, brittle, or intricate parts.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A die-sinking EDM operation produces **hemispherical craters of diameter 0.3 mm** at a spark frequency of **3,000 Hz**. The workpiece is tool steel (ρ = 7.85 g/cm³). Calculate:
  (i) The volume of each crater.
  (ii) The volumetric MRR in mm³/s.
  (iii) The mass MRR in g/min.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **(i) Volume of one crater (2 Marks):**
> Apply the hemispherical crater formula:
> $$ V_c = \frac{\pi \, d_c^{\,3}}{12} = \frac{3.1416 \cdot (0.3)^3}{12} $$
> Compute the cube:
> $$ (0.3)^3 = 0.027 \text{ mm}^3 $$
> Substitute:
> $$ V_c = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.027}{12} = \frac{0.08482}{12} $$
> $$ \boxed{V_c = 7.068 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^3 } $$

> **(ii) Volumetric MRR (3 Marks):**
> $$ \text{MRR} = f \cdot V_c = 3000 \cdot 7.068 \times 10^{-3} $$
> $$ \boxed{\text{MRR} = 21.20 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} } $$
> Convert to mm³/min:
> $$ \text{MRR} = 21.20 \cdot 60 = 1272 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 1.272 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} $$

> **(iii) Mass MRR (2 Marks):**
> Convert volumetric MRR to cm³/min and multiply by density:
> $$ \text{MRR}_{mass} = 1.272 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \cdot 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$
> $$ \boxed{\text{MRR}_{mass} = 9.99 \text{ g/min} \approx 10 \text{ g/min}} $$

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **RC pulse generator circuit** used in EDM with a neat sketch. Compare it briefly with the **transistorised pulse generator**.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **RC (Relaxation) Pulse Generator — Working (4 Marks):**
> The circuit consists of a **DC source**, a **charging resistor R**, a **capacitor C**, and a small gap between the tool and workpiece that acts as the **switch**. The cycle is:
> 1. **Charging Phase:** When the supply is switched on, the capacitor C charges through resistor R from the DC source. The voltage across the gap rises exponentially.
> 2. **Breakdown Phase:** When the gap voltage exceeds the **dielectric breakdown strength** of the fluid (~ 100 V/mm), the dielectric ionises and the gap becomes conductive. The capacitor suddenly discharges through the gap, producing the spark.
> 3. **De-ionisation Phase:** After discharge, the gap voltage drops to zero, the spark extinguishes, the dielectric de-ionises, and the capacitor begins charging again.
> 4. The cycle repeats at a frequency that depends on the **R and C values** and the gap distance. Higher R or C → lower frequency → longer spark → rougher cut.

> **Transistorised Pulse Generator — Comparison (3 Marks):**
> - Uses **high-speed transistors** as on/off switches to produce square-wave pulses of independently controlled amplitude, frequency, and duty cycle.
> - Provides **precise control** over pulse on-time and off-time, allowing fine finishing.
> - Produces **lower peak currents and shorter pulses** → better surface finish and lower tool wear.
> - The RC circuit, by contrast, has **fixed discharge characteristics** — best suited for **roughing**; the transistorised type is preferred for **finishing and micro-EDM**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EDM is used to machine a **die cavity of 50 cm³** from a hardened-steel block. The machine operates at a discharge voltage of **30 V**, current of **15 A**, with **spark efficiency of 25%**. Given density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³** and effective specific heat = **1.5 J/(g·K)**, calculate:
  (i) The MRR in cm³/s using the heat-balance equation.
  (ii) The total machining time.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **(i) MRR by heat balance (4 Marks):**
> Compute input power:
> $$ P = V \cdot I = 30 \cdot 15 = 450 \text{ W} $$
> Compute useful power:
> $$ P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P = 0.25 \cdot 450 = 112.5 \text{ W} $$
> Compute $\rho \cdot J$:
> $$ \rho \cdot J = 7.85 \cdot 1.5 = 11.775 \text{ J/(cm}^3 \cdot \text{K)} $$
> Apply MRR equation:
> $$ \text{MRR} = \frac{P_{useful}}{\rho \cdot J} = \frac{112.5}{11.775} = 9.55 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} $$

> **(ii) Machining time (3 Marks):**
> Convert volume to cm³ (already given): 50 cm³.
> $$ T = \frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{50 \text{ cm}^3}{9.55 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}} = 5.24 \text{ s} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the spark efficiency factor (η):** Many students write MRR = (V·I)/(ρ·J) directly. KTU board specifically tests whether you remember to **multiply the input power by η** before using it as useful heat. Losing this costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in ρ·J:** Always confirm that ρ is in g/cm³ (not kg/m³) and J is in J/(g·K). Mixing SI and CGS is the **#1 reason for wrong numerical answers** in KTU EDM papers.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert volume:** If the cavity is given in cm³, MRR must also be in cm³/s. If given in mm³, use mm³/min.
> 4. **Hemisphere vs cone confusion:** KTU uses the hemisphere model ($V_c = \pi d^3/12$). Some textbooks use a cone model ($V_c = \pi d^2 h/12$). Always state your assumption in the answer.
> 5. **Duty factor ignored in average current:** For transistorised circuits, average current is $\tau \cdot I_p$, **not** $I_p$ directly. This appears in numericals on transistor pulse generators.
> 6. **Confusing TWR with MRR:** Tool Wear Ratio is a **dimensionless** ratio of wear volumes; do not compute it using rates.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read These Bullets 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- EDM = **Spark Erosion Machining**; non-contact, non-traditional, **thermo-electric** process.
- Three essentials: **conductive tool, conductive workpiece, dielectric medium**.
- **Open-circuit voltage = 60–250 V DC**; **gap = 0.01–0.05 mm**; **spark temperature = 8,000–12,000 °C**.
- Four phases of material removal: **Ionisation → Spark Discharge → Crater Formation → Dielectric Flush**.
- Crater volume (hemispherical) — $\boxed{V_c = \dfrac{\pi d_c^{\,3}}{12}}$.
- Volumetric MRR — $\boxed{\text{MRR} = f \cdot V_c}$.
- Energy-balance MRR — $\boxed{\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{\rho (J \Delta T + L)}}$.
- Spark frequency — $f = 1/(T_{on} + T_{off})$; **Duty factor** $\tau = T_{on}/(T_{on}+T_{off})$.
- Average current in transistorised EDM = $\tau \cdot I_p$.
- **Pulse generators:** RC (relaxation, roughing) vs Transistorised (finishing, micro-EDM).
- **Dielectric functions:** insulate, ionise, flush debris, cool, de-ionise.
- **Tool electrode materials:** Cu (low wear, fine finish), **graphite** (high MRR, roughing), brass (WEDM wire), W/Mo (exotic alloys).
- **Sinker EDM** = shaped 3-D electrode plunging vertically; **Wire EDM** = travelling wire cuts 2-D profiles.
- **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** = (Tool volume worn) / (Workpiece volume removed); lower TWR = better electrode.
- **Recast layer (white layer)** — thin re-solidified metal at the crater rim, may affect fatigue life.
- **MRR depends on:** discharge current, pulse on-time, open-circuit voltage, density, specific heat.
- **Surface finish depends on:** pulse energy (low energy = smoother Ra), dielectric, flushing.
- **Limitations:** slow process, only conductive materials, high specific energy consumption, re-cast layer.
- **Major applications:** forging/injection moulds, extrusion dies, aerospace alloys, micro-holes in turbine blades.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Machining (EBM) and Electron Beam Welding (EBW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Machining (EBM) and Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Electron Beam Machining (EBM) is a non-conventional, thermal material removal process in which a high-velocity, focused beam of **electrons** is used to remove material by **melting and vaporization**. The kinetic energy of the accelerated electrons is converted into intense heat upon striking the workpiece, achieving extremely high power densities in the order of **10⁹ W/mm²**, enabling precise micro-machining of hard, brittle, and refractory materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** EBM is classified under **Thermal Energy Based Non-Traditional Machining Processes**. The process MUST operate under high vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) to prevent electron scattering by gas molecules.

### 1.2 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

**Formal Definition:**
Electron Beam Welding (EBW) is a fusion welding process in which a concentrated beam of high-velocity electrons, generated in a vacuum environment, is focused on the joint interface. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into thermal energy, producing a deep, narrow weld pool with an exceptionally **high depth-to-width ratio** (typically 20:1 to 50:1), enabling joining of thick sections in a single pass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Distinction:** EBM = material **REMOVAL** process. EBW = material **JOINING** process. Both share identical physical principles of energy conversion (kinetic → thermal).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: The "Sunlight Through a Magnifying Glass" Effect**
Imagine focusing sunlight through a magnifying glass onto a single spot on a piece of paper — the paper ignites because the same energy is concentrated into a tiny area. Now imagine magnifying that effect by a factor of **one million** by replacing sunlight with a beam of electrons accelerated to nearly **half the speed of light**. That is exactly what EBM/EBW does:

- The **electron gun** acts like the "sun" (source of energy).
- The **magnetic lens** acts like the "magnifying glass" (focusing).
- The **workpiece** is the "paper" that melts/vaporizes.
- The **vacuum chamber** is needed because electrons are extremely light and would be knocked off-course by air molecules, just like wind scattering sunlight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** Charge of an electron, **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (Coulombs)**. Mass of an electron, **mₑ = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**. These constants govern every energy calculation in EBM/EBW.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Electron Beam Processes

| Process Type | Primary Purpose | Typical Application |
|--------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **EBM (Machining)** | Material removal | Micro-drilling, slotting, engraving |
| **EBW (Welding)** | Material joining | Aerospace, nuclear, automotive |
| **EB Surfacing** | Surface modification | Hardfacing, cladding |
| **EB Cutting** | Profile cutting | Thin sheet metal profiling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Energy Conversion

The working principle of EBM and EBW rests on **three sequential energy conversion stages**:

1. **Electrical Energy → Kinetic Energy**: Electrons emitted from a cathode (tungsten filament) are accelerated by a high potential difference (50–200 kV).
2. **Kinetic Energy → Thermal Energy**: Upon impact with the workpiece, electrons decelerate rapidly. Their kinetic energy is absorbed by the lattice, generating intense localized heat.
3. **Thermal Energy → Phase Change**: The localized heat raises the temperature beyond the material's melting/boiling point, producing material removal (EBM) or fusion (EBW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vacuum is Mandatory:**
> - At atmospheric pressure, electrons collide with air molecules within millimeters of travel.
> - In vacuum, electrons travel in **straight ballistic paths** to the workpiece.
> - Vacuum also prevents oxidation of the molten metal.

### 2.2 Main Components of an EBM/EBW System

**1. Electron Gun (Cathode Assembly)**
- **Material:** Tungsten (W) filament, or Lanthanum Hexaboride (LaB₆) for higher emission.
- **Mechanism:** **Thermionic emission** — filament heated to ~2500°C releases electrons.
- **Function:** Generates the electron beam.

**2. Accelerating Anode**
- High negative potential (50–200 kV DC) applied to cathode.
- Creates a strong electric field that accelerates electrons to ~0.3c to 0.7c (where c = speed of light).

**3. Electromagnetic Focusing Lens**
- Acts like an optical lens but for electrons.
- Concentrates the diverging electron beam to a focal spot of diameter **0.1–1 mm**.

**4. Electromagnetic Deflection Coils**
- Steer the focused beam across the workpiece (for machining patterns) or hold it stationary (for welding/drilling).

**5. Vacuum System**
- Maintains chamber pressure of **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr**.
- Uses diffusion pumps + mechanical pumps in cascade.

**6. Work Chamber**
- Houses the workpiece, XYZ table, and viewing system.

### 2.3 Process Parameters

| Parameter | EBM Range | EBW Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Accelerating Voltage | 50–200 kV | 30–200 kV | Determines electron velocity & penetration |
| Beam Current | 1 μA – 10 mA | 10–500 mA | Determines heat input |
| Beam Diameter (focal spot) | 0.025–1 mm | 0.5–2 mm | Determines precision |
| Power Density | 10⁸ – 10⁹ W/mm² | 10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm² | Determines material removal/welding capability |
| Vacuum Pressure | 10⁻⁵ torr | 10⁻⁴ torr | Prevents electron scattering |
| Depth-to-Width Ratio (Weld) | N/A | 20:1 to 50:1 | Signature feature of EBW |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|--------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m_e v^2$ | Kinetic energy of an electron | Energy calculation |
| 2 | $KE = eV$ | Energy gained through accelerating voltage V (e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C) | Standard KTU 3-mark derivation |
| 3 | $P_{beam} = V \cdot I$ | Beam power (W) | Heat input calculation |
| 4 | $PD = \dfrac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}}$ | Power Density (W/mm²) | Most frequently asked formula |
| 5 | $A_{spot} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Focal spot area for diameter d | Power density substitution |
| 6 | $Q = P \cdot t$ | Total heat energy delivered (Joules) | Welding heat input |
| 7 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length (J/mm) for welding | EBW parameter calculation |
| 8 | $v_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}$ | Final electron velocity | Velocity derivation |
| 9 | $MRR = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V_{removed}}{t}$ | Material removal rate | EBM performance |

**Where:**
- $m_e$ = electron mass = **9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- $e$ = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = beam current (Amperes)
- $d$ = focal spot diameter (mm)
- $\eta$ = process efficiency (typically 0.65–0.85 for EBW)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)
- $\rho$ = density of workpiece

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** The vertical bar symbols inside formulas (e.g., absolute values) are intentionally replaced with LaTeX-safe delimiters to preserve KTU markdown table integrity. Use $\vert x \vert$ format in derivations, never raw `|x|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** EBW welds titanium fuselage frames and rocket nozzle assemblies.
- **Nuclear:** Welding zirconium alloy fuel rod end-plugs where contamination is unacceptable.
- **Electronics:** EBM drills micro-holes (Ø 50 μm) in printed circuit boards.
- **Tooling:** EBM textures carbide cutting tool inserts.
- **Medical:** EBM produces micro-features on cardiovascular stents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Kinetic Energy of an Accelerated Electron

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy gained by an electron when accelerated through a potential difference of V volts.

**Step 1: Identify the work done on the electron.**
The work done by the electric field on the electron as it travels from cathode to anode is equal to the product of charge and potential difference.

$$W = e \cdot V$$

**Step 2: Apply the Work–Energy Theorem.**
According to the Work–Energy Theorem, the net work done on a particle equals its change in kinetic energy. The electron starts from rest, so initial kinetic energy = 0.

$$W = \Delta KE = KE_{final} - KE_{initial} = KE_{final} - 0$$

**Step 3: Equate and obtain the kinetic energy expression.**

$$KE = eV$$

**Step 4: Express in standard units.**
Substituting $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ and V in volts:

$$KE = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot V \text{ Joules}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU standard).**
For V = 100 kV = 10⁵ V:

$$KE = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(10^5) = 1.602 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6: Convert to electron-volts (eV) for KTU standard notation.**

$$KE = 100{,}000 \text{ eV} = 100 \text{ keV}$$

> This is the **founding equation** of every EBM and EBW numerical — commit it to memory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity of an Accelerated Electron

**Problem Statement:** Determine the final velocity of an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 150 kV.

**Step 1: Start from kinetic energy expression.**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$$

**Step 2: Solve algebraically for velocity v.**

$$v^2 = \frac{2eV}{m_e}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**
Given: V = 150 kV = $1.5 \times 10^5$ V; $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1.5 \times 10^5)}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the numerator.**

$$2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.5 \times 10^5 = 4.806 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5: Divide by electron mass.**

$$\frac{4.806 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = 5.275 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 6: Take the square root.**

$$v = \sqrt{5.275 \times 10^{16}} = 2.297 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7: Express as a fraction of speed of light (c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s).**

$$\frac{v}{c} = \frac{2.297 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.766$$

**Result:** The electron travels at **76.6% of the speed of light**. At this speed, relativistic corrections become significant — KTU examiners often award bonus marks for mentioning this.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Density at the Workpiece

**Problem Statement:** An EBM system operates at 100 kV accelerating voltage with a beam current of 5 mA. The magnetic lens focuses the beam to a spot diameter of 0.5 mm. Calculate the power density at the workpiece.

**Step 1: Compute the beam power.**

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I$$

$$P_{beam} = (100 \times 10^3 \text{ V}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}) = 500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Compute the focal spot area.**

$$A_{spot} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the power density.**

$$PD = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{500}{0.1963} = 2547 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard KTU notation (in MW/mm²).**

$$PD = 2.547 \times 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2 = 2.55 \text{ kW/mm}^2 \approx 2.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MW/mm}^2$$

**Result:** Even with modest parameters, the power density is **~2.5 kW/mm²** — easily enough to melt tungsten, titanium, and ceramics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The same calculation at 150 kV and 10 mA on a 0.1 mm spot yields **~1.9 × 10⁵ W/mm² = 190 MW/mm²**, approaching the order of magnitude cited in textbooks (10⁹ W/mm² for highly focused micro-EBM).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Heat Delivered in EBW

**Problem Statement:** An EBW operation uses 120 kV accelerating voltage and 50 mA beam current for 2 seconds. Calculate the total heat energy delivered, assuming 70% process efficiency.

**Step 1: Compute the input beam power.**

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I = (120 \times 10^3) \times (50 \times 10^{-3}) = 6000 \text{ W} = 6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Compute the useful heat delivered to the workpiece.**

$$Q = \eta \cdot P_{beam} \cdot t$$

$$Q = 0.70 \times 6000 \times 2 = 8400 \text{ J}$$

**Result:** **Q = 8.4 kJ** of useful heat delivered — sufficient to fully penetrate a 25 mm thick stainless steel joint in a single pass.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EBM vs. EBW

| Attribute | EBM | EBW |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Primary Purpose** | Material removal | Material joining |
| **Beam Power** | Lower (W range) | Higher (kW range) |
| **Spot Size** | Very small (≤ 0.1 mm) | Larger (0.5–2 mm) |
| **Travel Mechanism** | Beam deflection (no tool) | Joint held stationary or table moved |
| **Tolerance Achievable** | ± 0.025 mm | ± 0.1 mm (joint alignment) |
| **Hazard** | X-ray generation in vacuum | X-ray + spatter |
| **Typical Industries** | Electronics, micromachining | Aerospace, nuclear, defense |
| **Weld Depth/Width** | Not applicable | 20:1 to 50:1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: EBM/EBW System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[High Voltage DC Power Supply 50 to 200 kV] --> CTRL[Control Console and Timer]
    CTRL --> EMIT[Electron Gun Tungsten Cathode]
    EMIT -->|Thermionic Emission| BEAM[Focused Electron Beam]
    BEAM --> FL[Electromagnetic Focusing Lens]
    FL --> DF[Electromagnetic Deflection Coils X Y]
    DF -->|Vacuum 10 to the power minus 5 torr| WC[Work Chamber with XYZ Table]
    WC --> WP[Workpiece]
    WP -->|Kinetic to Thermal Energy| MELT[Phase Change Melting Vaporization or Fusion]
    MELT --> OUT1[Material Removal EBM]
    MELT --> OUT2[Welded Joint EBW]
    PWR -.->|High Vacuum Interlock| VP[Vacuum Pump System Diffusion plus Rotary]
    VP --> WC
    CTRL -.->|Beam Current Control| EMIT
    CTRL -.->|Deflection Signals| DF
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree: Types of EBW

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EBW[Electron Beam Welding EBW] --> VAC[Vacuum EBW]
    EBW --> NVAC[Non Vacuum EBW]
    VAC --> HV[High Vacuum EBW less than 10 to the power minus 4 torr]
    VAC --> LV[Soft Vacuum EBW 10 to the power minus 1 to 10 to the power minus 3 torr]
    HV --> APP1[Aerospace and Nuclear Applications]
    LV --> APP2[Thin Sheet Metal and Automotive]
    NVAC --> APP3[Large Structures Shipbuilding and Heavy Fabrication]
    EBW --> PULSE[Pulsed Mode EBW]
    EBW --> CONT[Continuous Wave CW EBW]
    PULSE --> PUSE1[Micro Welding and Heat Sensitive Parts]
    CONT --> CONT1[Deep Penetration Single Pass Welding]
    EBW --> KEY[Keyhole Welding Deep Narrow Weld]
    EBW --> CON[Conduction Mode Shallow Wide Weld]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Keyhole vs. Conduction Mode Welding (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KMW[Keyhole Welding Mode]
        K1[High Power Density above 10 to the power 5 W per mm squared] --> K2[Beam Vaporizes Metal forming a Vapor Capillary]
        K2 --> K3[Capillary Penetrates Through Full Thickness]
        K3 --> K4[Depth to Width Ratio 20 to 50 is 1]
    end
    subgraph CMW[Conduction Welding Mode]
        C1[Lower Power Density below 10 to the power 5 W per mm squared] --> C2[Heat Conducts Through Solid Metal]
        C2 --> C3[Shallow Weld Pool forms on Surface]
        C3 --> C4[Depth to Width Ratio close to 1 is 1]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Most industrial EBW applications use the **Keyhole Mode** because it produces deep welds in a single pass without multiple thermal cycles — critical for preventing distortion in thick aerospace components.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Electron Beam Machining (EBM). State any two advantages and one limitation of EBM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
EBM is a non-traditional, thermal material removal process in which a high-velocity focused electron beam (accelerated at 50–200 kV) is used to remove material by melting and vaporization under high vacuum. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into intense localized heat, producing power densities of the order of **10⁹ W/mm²**.

**Advantages (1.5 Marks — any two):**
1. Capable of machining **hard, brittle, and refractory materials** (tungsten, ceramics, carbides) that are difficult to machine conventionally.
2. **No mechanical tool** is used, hence **no tool wear** and **no contact force** on the workpiece.
3. Achieves **very high precision** with tolerances of ± 0.025 mm and surface finish up to 0.2 μm Ra.
4. Can drill **micro-holes** as small as Ø 0.05 mm with high aspect ratios.

**Limitation (0.5 Mark — any one):**
1. The process requires **high vacuum**, making the equipment extremely expensive and limiting workpiece size.
2. **X-ray generation** during electron deceleration requires special shielding for operator safety.
3. Low **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** for bulk material removal; suitable only for micro-machining.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an Electron Beam Welding (EBW) system. Mention any two advantages of EBW over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
In EBW, a focused beam of high-velocity electrons, generated by a tungsten filament cathode and accelerated by a high voltage (50–200 kV) anode, is directed at the joint interface inside a vacuum chamber. Upon impact, the kinetic energy of the electrons is instantly converted into thermal energy, melting and fusing the parent metal across the joint. The molten metal solidifies to form a deep, narrow weld. Two main modes exist:

- **Keyhole mode** (deep penetration) — used for thick sections.
- **Conduction mode** (shallow weld) — used for thin sheets.

**Advantages over arc welding (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **Higher depth-to-width ratio (20:1 to 50:1)** allows welding of thick sections in a single pass without beveling.
2. **Minimal Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** due to concentrated energy input → reduced distortion.
3. Can join **dissimilar metals** (e.g., copper to steel, titanium to stainless steel) that are difficult to weld conventionally.
4. Welding occurs in vacuum → **no atmospheric contamination** (no oxides, no nitrides in weld pool).

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Answer **ONE** full question (14 marks). Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying 7 marks each. Both sub-parts are compulsory.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a) With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of an Electron Beam Machining (EBM) system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for architecture.)*

**Construction — Main Components (3 Marks):**

1. **Electron Gun:** A tungsten filament cathode (heated to ~2500°C) that emits electrons by thermionic emission. Sometimes LaB₆ cathodes are used for higher emission density.
2. **Accelerating Anode:** Maintained at a high positive potential (50–200 kV) relative to the cathode. Establishes a strong electric field that accelerates emitted electrons.
3. **Electromagnetic Focusing Lens:** A magnetic lens that converges the diverging electron beam to a fine focal point (Ø 0.025–1 mm) on the workpiece. It performs the same function as a glass lens does for light.
4. **Deflection Coils:** Electromagnetic coils that steer the focused beam across the workpiece to produce the desired machining pattern (slots, holes, contours) without moving the workpiece.
5. **Vacuum Chamber:** Houses the entire system at 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr to prevent electron scattering. Equipped with a high-vacuum pump train (rotary + diffusion).
6. **Workpiece Manipulator (X-Y-Z Table):** Holds and positions the workpiece with high precision.
7. **Observation System:** Optical telescope or CCD camera for monitoring the process.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- The tungsten filament is heated → thermionic emission produces a cloud of electrons.
- The high-voltage anode accelerates these electrons to velocities approaching 0.7c.
- The electromagnetic lens focuses the beam to a microscopic spot on the workpiece.
- Upon impact, kinetic energy → thermal energy → material at the focal spot **melts and vaporizes**.
- Deflection coils raster the beam to machine the required geometry.
- The vaporized material is removed by the vacuum pump; the molten material is blown away as spatter.

---

**Q: (b) An EBM system operates at an accelerating voltage of 150 kV and a beam current of 8 mA. The electromagnetic lens focuses the beam to a circular spot of diameter 0.4 mm. Calculate: (i) Beam Power, (ii) Power Density at the workpiece, and (iii) the velocity of the electrons. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- V = 150 kV = 1.5 × 10⁵ V
- I = 8 mA = 8 × 10⁻³ A
- d = 0.4 mm
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**(i) Beam Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_{beam} = V \times I$$

$$P_{beam} = (1.5 \times 10^5) \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) = 1200 \text{ W} = 1.2 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Power Density (3 Marks):**

$$A_{spot} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.16}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$PD = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{1200}{0.1257} = 9546 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

$$PD \approx 9.55 \times 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 9.55 \text{ kW/mm}^2$$

**(iii) Electron Velocity (2 Marks):**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.5 \times 10^5}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{4.806 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{5.275 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$v = 2.297 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.766c$$

**Result:** Beam power = **1.2 kW**, Power density = **9.55 kW/mm²**, Electron velocity = **2.297 × 10⁸ m/s**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a) Compare Electron Beam Welding (EBW) with Laser Beam Welding (LBW) in terms of principle, equipment, weld characteristics, and applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **EBW (Electron Beam Welding)** | **LBW (Laser Beam Welding)** |
|-----------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Kinetic energy of accelerated electrons → heat | Photon energy of coherent light → heat |
| **Energy Source** | High-voltage electron gun (50–200 kV) | CO₂ / Nd:YAG / Fiber laser |
| **Environment** | High vacuum mandatory (10⁻⁴ torr) | Atmospheric or shielding gas |
| **Beam Generation** | Thermionic emission from cathode | Stimulated emission in lasing medium |
| **Weld Depth/Width Ratio** | 20:1 to 50:1 (very high) | 5:1 to 10:1 (moderate) |
| **Power Density** | 10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm² | 10⁵ – 10⁶ W/mm² |
| **Weldable Materials** | All metals including refractory | Metals + non-metals (plastics, ceramics) |
| **Weld Quality** | Extremely clean (no atmosphere) | Clean (with shielding gas) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Very high (vacuum system) | High (but no vacuum) |
| **Joint Preparation** | Minimal | Requires precise gap control |
| **Reflectivity Issue** | None — electrons absorbed by all materials | High — polished metals reflect laser |
| **Applications** | Aerospace, nuclear, defense, critical welds | Automotive, shipbuilding, electronics |
| **Operator Hazard** | X-ray generation (shielding required) | Laser eye safety |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
EBW is preferred for **critical, deep-penetration welds in reactive/refractory metals** in vacuum, while LBW is preferred for **flexible, atmospheric, high-speed production welding** of a wider range of materials including non-metals.

---

**Q: (b) An EBW operation is performed on a 20 mm thick stainless steel plate using 100 kV accelerating voltage and 100 mA beam current. The welding travel speed is 5 mm/s, and the process efficiency is 75%. Calculate: (i) Beam Power, (ii) Heat Input per unit length, and (iii) the time required to weld a 500 mm long joint. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- V = 100 kV = 10⁵ V
- I = 100 mA = 0.1 A
- Travel speed, v = 5 mm/s
- Process efficiency, η = 0.75 = 75%
- Joint length, L = 500 mm

**(i) Beam Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_{beam} = V \times I = (10^5) \times (0.1) = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Heat Input per unit length (3 Marks):**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{beam}}{v} = \frac{0.75 \times 10{,}000}{5}$$

$$H = \frac{7500}{5} = 1500 \text{ J/mm}$$

**(iii) Time to weld 500 mm joint (2 Marks):**

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{500}{5} = 100 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ min } 40 \text{ s}$$

**Result:** Beam power = **10 kW**, Heat input per unit length = **1500 J/mm**, Total welding time = **100 s**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in EBM/EBW Numerical Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Failing to convert kV to V and mA to A before substituting into $P = V \times I$. A student who writes $P = 100 \times 0.1$ instead of $10^5 \times 0.1$ loses **full marks (2 M)** on the power step.
> 2. **Spot Area Formula Error:** Using $A = \pi d$ (circumference) instead of $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ (area). The examiner deducts 1 mark immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting Efficiency (η):** In EBW heat-input calculations, students often compute $H = P/v$ instead of $H = \eta P/v$. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing KE and eV units:** Not converting Joules to keV (divide by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$) loses the conceptual mark.
> 5. **Skipping Vacuum Justification:** Any 7-mark question on "Explain EBM" that omits **why vacuum is required** is automatically marked down by 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Conflating EBM and EBW:** Mixing the purpose of the two processes in a comparison answer leads to partial loss. Always state: EBM = **removal**, EBW = **joining**.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Principle:** KE of electrons → heat on impact → melting/vaporization.
- **Core Equations:** $KE = eV$; $P = VI$; $PD = P/A$; $v = \sqrt{2eV/m_e}$.
- **EBM purpose:** Material **removal** (drilling, slotting, micro-machining).
- **EBW purpose:** Material **joining** (deep, narrow welds with high depth-to-width ratio).
- **Vacuum level:** 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr — mandatory for both processes.
- **Cathode:** Tungsten filament (thermionic emission) — temperature ~2500°C.
- **Accelerating voltage range:** 50–200 kV.
- **Electron velocity at 150 kV:** ~2.3 × 10⁸ m/s (~0.77c) — relativistic regime.
- **Power density:** EBM = 10⁹ W/mm², EBW = 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm².
- **Key feature of EBW:** Depth-to-width ratio of 20:1 to 50:1 — single-pass deep welding.
- **Two welding modes:** **Keyhole** (deep) and **Conduction** (shallow).
- **Key components to draw in diagram:** Filament, anode, magnetic lens, deflection coils, vacuum chamber, workpiece.
- **Major advantages:** No tool wear, no mechanical force, works on refractory metals, minimal HAZ.
- **Major limitations:** Very expensive equipment, X-ray hazard, vacuum limits workpiece size.
- **EBW vs LBW:** EBW needs vacuum but is better for reflective/refractory metals; LBW is atmospheric but struggles with highly reflective surfaces.
- **Applications to memorize:** Aerospace (titanium), nuclear (zirconium alloys), electronics (PCB micro-drilling), automotive, defense.
- **Most-tested KTU numerical:** Beam power, power density, and electron velocity — practice at least 3 variations before the exam.
- **Examiner's bias:** Always quote numerical answers with **units** and to **3 significant figures**; never leave them bare.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Machining (LBM) and Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Machining (LBM) and Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔦 LASER BEAM MACHINING (LBM) – CORE TECHNICAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LASER BEAM MACHINING (LBM)** is a **non-contact, thermal-type, non-traditional material removal process** in which material is removed by the **thermal energy of a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of photons** generated by stimulated emission of radiation. The beam is focused to a very small spot (typically **10 µm – 1 mm**) on the workpiece, producing a very high power density (typically **10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm²**), which causes **melting and vaporization** of the workpiece material.

The acronym **LASER** stands for:
**L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation.

LBM belongs to the family of **Energy Beam / Thermal NTM Processes** along with:
- Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
- Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)
- Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Burning Glass" 🔍☀️

Imagine holding a magnifying glass on a bright sunny day. When the sun's rays (parallel light) pass through the lens, they converge to a tiny, dazzling point. If you hold a piece of paper at that point, the paper catches fire — even though the sun is far away.

**LBM works on exactly the same principle**, but with four upgrades:
1. The "sunlight" is replaced by a **laser** — a special, super-organized, single-colour, perfectly parallel beam of light.
2. The magnifying glass is replaced by an **optical lens** (focal length 25–250 mm).
3. The "paper" is replaced by **metal/ceramic/plastic** that must be cut/drilled.
4. The heat is so intense (**>10,000 °C in microseconds**) that the material **boils away** as vapour and plasma.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-life example:** Eye surgeons use pulsed laser beams to reshape the cornea (LASIK). In industry, lasers cut intricate patterns in **stents, mobile-phone glass, jet-engine turbine blades, and silicon wafers**.

## 1.3 The Four Magic Properties of a Laser Beam

A normal light bulb emits light in all directions, in all colours, in a scattered, random manner. A laser is engineered to do the **opposite** in every respect.

| # | Property | What it Means | Engineering Benefit |
|---|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Monochromaticity** | All photons have the **same wavelength (λ)** | Selective absorption by material; predictable energy transfer |
| 2 | **Coherence** | All photons are in **phase** (in time) | Extreme peak intensity; very high power density |
| 3 | **Directionality (Collimation)** | Beam is **parallel**, very low divergence | Long working distance without loss of intensity |
| 4 | **High Brightness (Intensity)** | Photon flux per unit area per unit solid angle is huge | Tiniest spot = highest power density |

## 1.4 Stimulated Emission – The Heart of a Laser

A photon is a discrete packet of electromagnetic energy. Its energy is given by the **Planck-Einstein relation**:

$$
E \;=\; h\nu \;=\; \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $\nu$ = frequency of the radiation (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the laser (m)

When an excited atom is struck by an incoming photon of exactly the right energy, it emits **two photons** — the original one plus a duplicate, in phase and in the same direction. This cascade multiplies photons exponentially → **Light Amplification**.

> [!NOTE]
> For this to dominate, the lasing medium must be in a state of **Population Inversion** (more atoms in the excited state than in the ground state) — a non-equilibrium condition maintained by a process called **Pumping**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a LBM System – Component-Level Breakdown

A complete industrial LBM installation consists of **four** major subsystems:

1. **Laser Source (Resonator)** – Produces the coherent beam.
2. **Beam Delivery System** – Mirrors, optical fibres, beam expanders, focusing lens.
3. **CNC Workstation / Motion System** – Moves the workpiece or the beam.
4. **Auxiliary Systems** – Gas-assist nozzle, fume extraction, chiller, fume filter.

### 2.1.1 Components of a Laser Resonator

A laser resonator is an **optical cavity** in which light bounces back and forth between two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective → output coupler) to build up intensity by repeated stimulated emission.

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Active (lasing) medium** | Provides atoms/molecules that undergo stimulated emission (e.g., CO₂ gas, Nd:YAG crystal) |
| **Pumping source** | Supplies energy to achieve population inversion (e.g., flash lamp, electrical discharge, diode laser) |
| **Optical cavity (resonator)** | Two parallel mirrors that reflect photons back and forth |
| **Output coupler** | Partially reflective mirror that allows a fraction of the beam to escape as the useful laser |

## 2.2 The LBM Material-Removal Mechanism — Step-by-Step

When the focused laser beam strikes the workpiece, the following **thermal chain reaction** occurs within microseconds:

- **Step 1 — Absorption:** Photons strike the workpiece surface. Electrons in the material absorb the photon energy and jump to a higher energy state.
- **Step 2 — Heating:** The absorbed energy converts into heat. The lattice temperature rises rapidly.
- **Step 3 — Melting:** When the surface temperature reaches the **melting point**, the material starts to liquefy.
- **Step 4 — Vaporization / Ablation:** With continued energy input, the temperature exceeds the **boiling point** and the material vaporizes.
- **Step 5 — Plasma formation:** At very high power density, the vapor becomes ionized, forming a **plasma plume** above the cut.
- **Step 6 — Melt ejection:** An assist gas (O₂, N₂, Ar) blows the molten and vaporized material out of the cut zone, producing a clean kerf.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The energy required to remove a unit volume of material is called the **Specific Energy (SE)**. For LBM, SE ranges from **10⁴ – 10⁶ J/mm³** depending on the material.

## 2.3 Classification of Industrial Lasers Used in Manufacturing

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Wavelength (λ) | Mode | Best For |
|------------|---------------|----------------|------|----------|
| **CO₂ Laser** | CO₂ + N₂ + He gas | **10.6 µm** (far IR) | Continuous / Pulsed | Cutting non-metals, thick metals, woodworking, acrylic |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | Neodymium-doped Yttrium Aluminium Garnet crystal | **1.064 µm** (near IR) | Continuous / Pulsed | Cutting/drilling metals, welding |
| **Excimer Laser** | Inert gas + halogen (e.g., ArF, KrF) | **0.193 – 0.351 µm** (UV) | Pulsed only | Micro-machining, semiconductor lithography |
| **Fiber Laser** | Doped optical fibre (Yb) | **1.07 µm** | Continuous | Sheet-metal cutting, high-speed marking |
| **Ruby Laser** | Chromium-doped Al₂O₃ | **0.694 µm** | Pulsed | Drilling, tattoo removal (historical) |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📐

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CARD — Memorise this table for KTU exams. Every 14-mark question is solved using one or more of these.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Photon Energy** | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $E$ in Joules, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\lambda$ in m |
| 2 | **Beam Divergence** | $\theta = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$ | $\theta$ in rad, $D$ = beam diameter (m). Smaller θ → better collimation |
| 3 | **Spot Diameter at Focus** | $d = \dfrac{f \, \theta}{1}$ ≈ $1.22 \, \dfrac{f \, \lambda}{D}$ | $f$ = focal length of lens, $D$ = input beam diameter |
| 4 | **Focused Spot Area** | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | $A$ in m² or mm² |
| 5 | **Power Density (Intensity)** | $I = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ = laser power (W), $A$ = spot area; units W/mm² |
| 6 | **Energy per Pulse** | $E_{p} = P \, t_{p}$ | $t_{p}$ = pulse duration (s) |
| 7 | **Pulse Repetition Rate Energy** | $E_{total} = E_{p} \times f_{p} \times t$ | $f_{p}$ = pulses/s, $t$ = machining time (s) |
| 8 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \dfrac{P}{SE \times \rho}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $SE$ = Specific Energy (J/kg) |
| 9 | **Depth of Cut per Pulse** | $h = \dfrac{E_{p}}{A \, \rho \, [L + c \, (T_{m} - T_{0})]}$ | $L$ = latent heat of fusion, $c$ = specific heat, $T_m$ = melting point |
| 10 | **Specific Energy** | $SE = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ — measure of process efficiency |
| 11 | **Cutting Speed (Kerf, simplified)** | $v = \dfrac{P}{w \times t \times SE}$ | $w$ = kerf width, $t$ = thickness |
| 12 | **Number of Photons per second** | $N = \dfrac{P \lambda}{h c}$ | Photon flux (photons/s) |

### 2.5 Key Process Parameters Influencing LBM

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Quality |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **↑ Laser Power (P)** | ↑ MRR linearly | ↑ HAZ (heat-affected zone), more dross |
| **↓ Pulse Duration (tₚ)** | ↑ Peak power density, less HAZ | Sharper edges, micro-machining |
| **↓ Spot Diameter (d)** | ↑ Power density, ↑ MRR | Narrower kerf, better precision |
| **↑ Pulse Frequency** | ↑ Overall productivity | May cause overlap, recast layer |
| **Assist Gas Type** | Reactive (O₂) → exothermic boost | Inert (N₂, Ar) → clean, oxide-free cut |
| **Focal Point Position** | Above / on / below surface | Affects kerf taper and depth |

## 2.6 Advantages, Limitations & Applications

### ✅ Advantages
- No contact → **no tool wear, no mechanical force, no vibrations**.
- Can machine **any material** — metals, ceramics, glass, polymers, composites, diamonds.
- Excellent for **micro-features** (µm-level), narrow kerf (< 0.1 mm).
- High flexibility — easily programmed via CNC / galvanometer scanners.
- Suitable for **heat-sensitive or brittle** materials when pulsed.

### ❌ Limitations
- **High capital cost** (CO₂ and fibre lasers are expensive).
- Low **energy efficiency** (wall-plug efficiency often < 10% for CO₂, ~30% for fibre).
- **Concave / tapered kerf** due to beam divergence along depth.
- **Hazardous** — reflected laser radiation can damage eyes (Class IV laser) → needs enclosures, goggles.
- Limited to **shallow depths** compared to plasma/oxy-fuel cutting for thick plates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **exam-grade** — every algebraic step is shown, explained, and unit-checked. KTU examiners award full marks only when each transition is clearly justified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Focused Spot Diameter (Airy Disc)

The smallest spot to which a lens can focus a parallel beam of diameter $D$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is given by the **Airy diffraction limit**.

### Given Data (Standard symbols)
- $D$ = diameter of the collimated beam entering the lens
- $f$ = focal length of the focusing lens
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the laser

### Step 1 — Start with the half-angle of diffraction
For a circular aperture, the first minimum of the diffraction pattern occurs at a half-angle $\theta$ given by the Rayleigh criterion.

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert angular spread to linear spot size
The spot diameter at the focal plane is the chord length subtended by angle $2\theta$ at distance $f$. For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$.

$$
d \;=\; 2 \, f \, \theta
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\theta$
$$
d \;=\; 2 \, f \times \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \, f \, \lambda}{D}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation
- **Shorter wavelength (UV excimer) → smaller spot → finer machining.**
- **Larger input beam diameter → smaller focused spot** (beam expanders are used to enlarge $D$).
- **Smaller focal length lens → smaller spot** (but also shallower depth of focus — focus is hard to hold on a wavy surface).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density (Beam Intensity) at the Workpiece

Power density is the **single most important quantity** in any thermal NTM process. It determines whether the material will only heat, melt, or vaporize.

### Step 1 — Spot area from the focused spot diameter
The focused spot is approximately circular.

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d^{2}}{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Intensity definition
Intensity = power delivered per unit area.

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the spot diameter $d$
Using $d = 2.44 \, f \, \lambda / D$:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( \dfrac{2.44 \, f \, \lambda}{D} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 5.95 \, f^{2} \, \lambda^{2}}{D^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final expression for power density

$$
\boxed{\,I \;=\; \dfrac{4 P D^{2}}{\pi \left( 2.44 \, f \, \lambda \right)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.273 \, P D^{2}}{f^{2} \, \lambda^{2}}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example
A pulsed Nd:YAG laser with $\lambda = 1.064 \, \mu m$, $P = 2 \, kW$, $D = 10 \, mm$, and $f = 50 \, mm$ is used.

**Compute the spot diameter first:**

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.064 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \times 50 \times 1.064}{10} \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{129.808}{10} \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
d \;\approx\; 12.98 \, \mu m
$$

**Now compute the spot area:**

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (12.98 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 1.685 \times 10^{-10}}{4}
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; 1.323 \times 10^{-10} \, m^{2} \;=\; 1.323 \times 10^{-4} \, mm^{2}
$$

**Now compute the power density:**

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{2000 \, W}{1.323 \times 10^{-4} \, mm^{2}} \;\approx\; 1.51 \times 10^{7} \, \dfrac{W}{mm^{2}}
$$

This is more than enough to vaporize steel (which only needs ~$10^{6} \, W/mm^{2}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** Industrial LBM machines routinely deliver $10^{6} - 10^{9} \, W/mm^{2}$. Our computed value of $1.51 \times 10^{7} \, W/mm^{2}$ lies comfortably in this range ✔.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Drilling

Consider a single laser pulse of energy $E_{p}$ incident on a hole of diameter $d$. Suppose the pulse removes material of depth $h$.

### Step 1 — Mass removed per pulse
If $\rho$ is the density of the workpiece material:

$$
m \;=\; \rho \times V \;=\; \rho \times \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times h
$$

### Step 2 — Energy balance
The pulse energy goes into:
1. Raising the temperature from ambient $T_0$ to melting point $T_m$ — sensible heat.
2. Latent heat of fusion $L$.
3. (If vaporization regime) latent heat of vaporization $L_{v}$.

For the **melting + ejection regime**, the energy balance is:

$$
E_{p} \;=\; m \left[ c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \right] \;+\; E_{loss}
$$

If we assume **no losses** (ideal case):

$$
E_{p} \;=\; \rho \times \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times h \times \left[ c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for depth of penetration per pulse $h$

$$
h \;=\; \dfrac{4 E_{p}}{\pi d^{2} \, \rho \left[ c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \right]}
$$

### Step 4 — Material Removal Rate (volume)
If the pulse repetition rate is $f_p$ pulses/second:

$$
\boxed{\,MRR \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times h \times f_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{p} \, f_{p}}{\rho \left[ c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \right]}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- MRR is **directly proportional to pulse energy and pulse rate**.
- MRR is **inversely proportional** to density and the latent-heat/sensible-heat factor.
- Materials with high $T_m$ and high $L$ (e.g., tungsten, ceramics) require more energy per unit MRR.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Energy (Process Efficiency Metric)

Specific Energy is a universal benchmark used to compare **all** material-removal processes.

### Step 1 — Definition
By convention, the energy required to remove a unit volume of material is the **Specific Energy**:

$$
SE \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Energy input per unit time}}{\text{Volume removed per unit time}} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{MRR}
$$

### Step 2 — Units
$$
\left[ SE \right] \;=\; \dfrac{W}{mm^{3}/s} \;=\; \dfrac{J/s}{mm^{3}/s} \;=\; \dfrac{J}{mm^{3}}
$$

### Step 3 — Typical Specific Energy Values (KTU memory card)

| Process | SE Range (J/mm³) | Energy Efficiency |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Conventional turning** | 1 – 5 | High |
| **EDM** | 10 – 30 | Medium |
| **LBM** | **10⁴ – 10⁶** | **Low** |
| **EBM** | 10⁴ – 10⁵ | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LBM has **very high specific energy** because most of the beam energy is lost as heat to the surrounding material, radiation, and plasma, instead of being used purely for material removal. The process is still preferred because of its **non-contact nature, versatility and precision**, not its thermal efficiency.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Which Parameter Changes What?"

| Change Made | Effect on Power Density $I$ | Effect on MRR | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Kerf |
