|-------------|----------------------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| ↑ Laser power $P$ | ↑ proportional | ↑ | ↑ | — |
| ↓ Wavelength $\lambda$ | ↑ ($\propto 1/\lambda^2$) | ↑ | ↓ (sharper) | Narrower |
| ↑ Beam diameter $D$ | ↑ ($\propto D^{2}$) | ↑ | ↑ | — |
| ↑ Focal length $f$ | ↓ ($\propto 1/f^{2}$) | ↓ | — | Wider |
| Shorter pulse $t_p$ | ↑ peak power | ↑ per pulse | ↓ (minimal HAZ) | Cleaner |
| Use O₂ assist gas | — | ↑ (exothermic) | ↑ (oxidation) | Slightly wider |
| Use inert N₂/Ar assist | — | ↓ | ↓ (clean cut) | Narrow, no dross |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an LBM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[High Voltage DC Power Supply] --> PUMP[Pumping Source Flash Lamp or Diode]
    PUMP --> RESON[Optical Resonator Two Mirrors]
    RESON --> OUT[Output Coupler Partial Mirror]
    OUT --> EXPND[Beam Expander Telescope]
    EXPND --> MIRR1[Deflection Mirror 1]
    MIRR1 --> MIRR2[Deflection Mirror 2 Galvanometer]
    MIRR2 --> LENS[Focusing Lens f 25 to 250 mm]
    LENS --> GAS[Coaxial Assist Gas Nozzle O2 N2 Ar]
    GAS --> WORK[Workpiece on CNC XY Table]
    WORK --> EXHAUST[Fume Extraction and Filter]
    CTRL[Computer with CNC and Process Controller] -.-> PWR
    CTRL -.-> MIRR2
    CTRL -.-> WORK
    CHILL[Industrial Chiller] -.-> RESON
    style PWR fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style LENS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0044aa
    style WORK fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa0000
    style CTRL fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Material Removal in LBM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Beam Generation by Stimulated Emission] --> B[Beam Collimation by Output Mirror]
    B --> C[Beam Expansion and Shaping]
    C --> D[Beam Steering by Mirrors or Galvo]
    D --> E[Focusing by Lens to Micron Spot]
    E --> F[Photon Absorption by Workpiece]
    F --> G[Solid to Liquid Phase Melting]
    G --> H[Liquid to Vapour Phase Vaporization]
    H --> I[Plasma Plume Formation]
    I --> J[Assist Gas Ejects Melt and Vapour]
    J --> K[Clean Kerf or Hole Formed]
    style A fill:#fce4ec
    style K fill:#c8e6c9
```

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix – LBM vs Other Thermal NTMs

| Feature | LBM | EBM | PAM | EDM |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Energy Beam | Photons | Electrons | Plasma arc | Sparks |
| Atmosphere | Air / Gas | Vacuum required | Water / Air | Dielectric fluid |
| Power Density | $10^{6}$ – $10^{9}$ W/mm² | $10^{9}$ W/mm² | $10^{5}$ – $10^{6}$ W/mm² | $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ W/mm² |
| MRR | Low – Medium | Low | High | Low |
| HAZ | Moderate | Low (vacuum) | High | Small |
| Tool Wear | None | None | Electrode wears | Electrode wears |
| Cost | High | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| Best Application | Micro-drilling, sheet cutting, profile cutting | Fine hole drilling, micro-machining | Thick plate cutting, coating | Hardened steel, intricate cavities |

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Laser Beam Machining. List any four characteristics of a laser beam that make it suitable for material removal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** LBM is a non-contact, thermal-type non-traditional machining process in which material is removed by the **thermal energy of a focused, coherent laser beam** that raises the workpiece temperature above its vaporization point, ejecting material as vapour and molten droplets assisted by a gas jet.

**Four characteristics (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Monochromaticity** – single wavelength, ensuring predictable absorption.
2. **Coherence** – all waves in phase, producing very high peak intensity.
3. **Directionality (collimation)** – nearly parallel beam with very small divergence, enabling long focal distances.
4. **High brightness** – enormous power density at the focused spot (10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the terms: Population Inversion and Stimulated Emission. Why are both necessary for laser action?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Stimulated Emission (1 mark):** When an incident photon of energy $E = h\nu$ strikes an atom already in an excited state, it forces the atom to drop to a lower energy state, emitting a **second photon identical** to the incident one in phase, frequency, direction, and polarisation. This is the basic amplification step.
- **Population Inversion (1 mark):** Normally, more atoms exist in the lower (ground) energy state. To make stimulated emission dominate over absorption, we must artificially create a non-equilibrium state in which **more atoms occupy the higher energy state** than the lower one. This is called population inversion.
- **Necessity (1 mark):** Without population inversion, absorption would dominate and the beam would be attenuated instead of amplified. Without stimulated emission, there would be no coherent amplification — only random spontaneous photons, as in an ordinary bulb. Both are therefore essential for sustained, coherent laser output.

---

## 📕 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE, students must answer **one full 14-mark question** from the module. Below are **TWO complete alternative questions** (OR-style choice) — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **📌 QUESTION A (14 MARKS)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **principal components of a Laser Beam Machining (LBM) system** and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **power density of a focused laser beam**. A 500 W pulsed Nd:YAG laser is focused through a 75 mm focal-length lens. The beam diameter at the lens is 8 mm and the wavelength is 1.06 µm. Calculate the **spot diameter and the power density** at the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a) — Components of LBM System (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks)** — Already shown in Section 4.1 (Functional Block Diagram of LBM). Student should draw and label.

**Description (5 × 1 = 5 marks):**

1. **Laser Resonator (Source):** Houses the active medium (CO₂ gas / Nd:YAG rod) and the two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective output coupler). Provides the coherent beam.

2. **Pumping Source:** Flash lamp, electrical discharge, or diode laser. Supplies energy to the active medium to achieve population inversion.

3. **Beam Delivery System:** Consists of beam expanders, deflection mirrors, optical fibres, and focusing lens. Carries the beam to the workpiece and concentrates it to a tiny spot.

4. **CNC Work Table (X-Y-Z positioning):** Holds and moves the workpiece as per the programmed path, enabling complex 2D/3D profiles.

5. **Auxiliary Systems:** Assist gas nozzle (O₂/N₂/Ar for ejection), fume extractor, chiller for cooling the laser tube, and a control computer with operator interface.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b) — Power Density Derivation & Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Focused spot diameter from Rayleigh criterion:

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \, f \, \lambda}{D}
$$

**Step 2:** Spot area (circular):

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Power density definition:

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $d$:

$$
\boxed{\,I \;=\; \dfrac{0.273 \, P D^{2}}{f^{2} \, \lambda^{2}}\,}
$$

**Numerical Substitution (3 marks):**

Given: $P = 500 \, W$, $f = 75 \, mm = 75 \times 10^{-3} \, m$, $D = 8 \, mm = 8 \times 10^{-3} \, m$, $\lambda = 1.06 \, \mu m = 1.06 \times 10^{-6} \, m$.

**Compute spot diameter $d$:**

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \times 75 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.06 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{2.44 \times 75 \times 1.06}{8} \times 10^{-6}
$$

Numerator: $2.44 \times 75 = 183.0$; $183.0 \times 1.06 = 193.98$

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{193.98}{8} \times 10^{-6} \;\approx\; 24.25 \, \mu m
$$

**Compute spot area $A$:**

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (24.25 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 588.06 \times 10^{-12}}{4}
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; 4.618 \times 10^{-10} \, m^{2} \;\approx\; 4.618 \times 10^{-4} \, mm^{2}
$$

**Compute power density $I$:**

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{500 \, W}{4.618 \times 10^{-4} \, mm^{2}} \;\approx\; 1.083 \times 10^{6} \, \dfrac{W}{mm^{2}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Q.A (b):**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $f$, $D$, $\lambda$ all to the **same unit (m)** before substituting. Many students mix mm and µm.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Writing the spot diameter formula as $1.22 \, f \, \lambda / D$ (missing the factor 2) — always use the **2.44** form because $d = 2 f \theta$ and $\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not stating **units of power density** in the final answer. Always write $W/mm^2$ or $W/m^2$.

---

### **📌 QUESTION B (14 MARKS)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Compare **CO₂ laser, Nd:YAG laser, and Excimer laser** on the basis of active medium, wavelength, mode of operation (CW/Pulsed), and typical industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laser drilling process uses pulses of 5 J energy, pulse duration 0.5 ms, on an aluminium plate of 5 mm thickness. Density of Al = 2700 kg/m³, melting point = 660 °C, ambient = 30 °C, specific heat $c = 900 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$, latent heat of fusion $L = 397 \, kJ/kg$. Assuming no heat loss, calculate the **depth of penetration per pulse** if the focused spot diameter is 0.6 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a) — Comparison of Lasers (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (7 × 1 = 7 marks, with one mark per cell):**

| Feature | **CO₂ Laser** | **Nd:YAG Laser** | **Excimer Laser** |
|---------|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Active Medium** | Gas mixture of CO₂, N₂, He | Nd-doped Yttrium Aluminium Garnet crystal | Inert gas + halogen (e.g., ArF, KrF) |
| **Wavelength** | 10.6 µm (far IR) | 1.064 µm (near IR) | 0.193 – 0.351 µm (UV) |
| **Mode of Operation** | CW and Pulsed | CW and Pulsed | Pulsed only (cannot run CW) |
| **Power Output** | Up to tens of kW | Up to ~6 kW (CW), higher pulsed | Typically < 100 W average |
| **Beam Delivery** | Mirrors only (fibre not feasible at 10.6 µm) | Optical fibre compatible | Mirrors |
| **Absorption** | High in non-metals, low in metals (use high power) | High in metals | Excellent in almost all materials (UV) |
| **Typical Applications** | Sheet-metal cutting, woodworking, acrylic, thick plate cutting | Drilling, welding, cutting stainless steel, marking | Micro-machining, semiconductor lithography, eye surgery (LASIK) |

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b) — Depth of Penetration per Pulse (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $E_{p} = 5 \, J$
- $t_{p} = 0.5 \, ms$ (not directly needed for depth, but given for context)
- $\rho = 2700 \, kg/m^{3}$
- $T_{m} = 660 \, °C$, $T_{0} = 30 \, °C$
- $c = 900 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$
- $L = 397 \, kJ/kg = 397{,}000 \, J/kg$
- $d = 0.6 \, mm = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \, m$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Energy balance equation (no loss):**

$$
E_{p} \;=\; m \left[ c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \right]
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Express mass $m$ in terms of depth $h$ and spot area:**

Spot area:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (0.6 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4}
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \, m^{2}
$$

Mass melted:

$$
m \;=\; \rho \, A \, h \;=\; 2700 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \times h \;=\; 7.633 \times 10^{-4} \, h \quad [kg]
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute the specific energy per kg:**

$$
c (T_{m} - T_{0}) \;=\; 900 \times (660 - 30) \;=\; 900 \times 630 \;=\; 567{,}000 \, J/kg
$$

Total energy per kg:

$$
c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L \;=\; 567{,}000 + 397{,}000 \;=\; 964{,}000 \, J/kg
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Solve for $h$:**

$$
E_{p} \;=\; m \times 964{,}000
$$

$$
5 \;=\; (7.633 \times 10^{-4} \, h) \times 964{,}000
$$

$$
5 \;=\; 735.82 \, h
$$

$$
h \;=\; \dfrac{5}{735.82} \;\approx\; 6.795 \times 10^{-3} \, m
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h \;\approx\; 6.80 \, mm\,}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Sanity check & conclusion:**

The computed depth per pulse (~6.8 mm) is **slightly greater than the plate thickness (5 mm)**, which means **a single pulse is enough to drill through the 5 mm aluminium plate**. This is consistent with industrial practice, where aluminium — being highly reflective of IR — is normally drilled with a **short, high-energy pulse** to reach the vaporization regime.

**Distribution of remaining 2 marks:**
- 1 mark for proper unit consistency throughout.
- 1 mark for the final boxed answer with units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Q.B (b):**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using density in $g/cm^{3}$ without conversion to $kg/m^{3}$ (or vice versa for latent heat). Energy will be off by orders of magnitude.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the **sensible heat term** $c(T_m - T_0)$. Some students use only the latent heat $L$, losing one full mark.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing the final answer without units, or as $6.8 \times 10^{-3}$ (omitting the conversion to mm). Always state "$h \approx 6.8 \, mm$".

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Night-Before-Exam" 60-Second Revision Card for LBM**

- ⭐ **LBM = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation → used for material removal.**
- ⭐ **LASER** acronym = **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**.
- ⭐ **Two conditions for lasing:** (1) Population inversion, (2) Optical resonator (feedback).
- ⭐ **Photon energy:** $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ — memorize $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$.
- ⭐ **Spot diameter:** $d = 2.44 f \lambda / D$ — Rayleigh criterion.
- ⭐ **Power density:** $I = 4P/(\pi d^{2}) = 0.273 \, P D^{2}/(f^{2} \lambda^{2})$.
- ⭐ **MRR (drilling):** $MRR = E_{p} f_{p} / [\rho \{c(T_m - T_0) + L\}]$.
- ⭐ **Specific Energy:** $SE = P/MRR$, units $J/mm^{3}$. LBM has very high SE ($10^{4}$–$10^{6}$ J/mm³).
- ⭐ **CO₂ laser:** $\lambda = 10.6 \, \mu m$, best for non-metals and thick metal sheets.
- ⭐ **Nd:YAG laser:** $\lambda = 1.064 \, \mu m$, best for metals; can be fibre-coupled.
- ⭐ **Excimer laser:** UV, pulsed only, used for micro-machining and semiconductor lithography.
- ⭐ **Material removal mechanism:** Absorption → Heating → Melting → Vaporization → Plasma → Assist-gas ejection.
- ⭐ **Process parameters (↑ effect on MRR):** ↑Power, ↑Pulse frequency, ↓Spot size, ↓Wavelength.
- ⭐ **Advantages:** No tool wear, no force, works on any material, micro-machining, narrow kerf.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** High cost, low efficiency, tapered kerf, eye-hazard (Class IV).
- ⭐ **Applications:** Drilling micro-holes (fuel injectors, turbine blades), sheet-metal profile cutting, engraving, scribing, surgical tools, PCB via drilling.
- ⭐ **Compare confidently** with EDM, EBM, PAM — exam favourite!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Welding (LBW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** is a non-contact fusion welding process that joins materials by melting them with a highly concentrated, coherent, and monochromatic beam of light generated by a laser source. The localized energy input creates a narrow, deep weld pool with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ), making it suitable for high-precision and high-speed joining of both similar and dissimilar metals, as well as non-metals.

The term **LASER** itself is an acronym for:
**L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, students must understand the principles of laser generation, types of industrial lasers (CO₂, Nd:YAG, Fiber, Disc lasers), focusing optics, process parameters, the two operating modes (conduction and keyhole), and the engineering applications of LBW in modern manufacturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are outdoors on a sunny day, and you use a **magnifying glass** to focus sunlight onto a single dry leaf. The concentrated light beam instantly burns a tiny, precise spot on the leaf, while the rest of the surroundings remain unaffected. This is exactly how a laser works in welding — but with energy millions of times more intense.

Think of LBW as **"welding with a controlled beam of light bullets"**:
- The laser is the "gun" producing perfectly parallel, in-phase light particles (photons).
- The focusing lens is the "magnifying glass."
- The workpiece is the "target."
- The narrow, intense energy point melts and fuses the metal without physically touching it.

> [!TIP]
> A standard **CO₂ laser** with **10.6 µm** wavelength and **2 kW** power can achieve a power density of approximately **10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²**, enough to vaporize steel.

## 1.3 Fundamental Properties of Laser Light

| Property | Description | Engineering Importance |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Monochromaticity** | Single wavelength output | Predictable material interaction |
| **Coherence** | Photons in phase (temporal + spatial) | Allows tight focusing |
| **Directionality** | Low beam divergence (~ mrad) | Energy delivered to a small spot |
| **High Brightness** | Power per unit area per unit solid angle | Sufficient to melt any engineering material |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental constants that govern LBW are:
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - **Photon energy:** $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$
> - **Wavelengths of common industrial lasers:** CO₂ = **10.6 µm**, Nd:YAG = **1.06 µm**, Fiber = **1.07 µm**, Disc = **1.03 µm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Laser Generation — Stimulated Emission

The laser is generated through a three-stage process inside a resonant optical cavity:

1. **Pumping (Excitation):** External energy (electrical, optical) excites atoms in the gain medium (e.g., CO₂ gas, YAG crystal, fiber) to a higher energy level — creating a **population inversion**.
2. **Stimulated Emission:** An incident photon of the correct wavelength strikes an excited atom, causing it to drop to a lower energy state and emit an **identical photon** (same phase, direction, frequency, and polarization).
3. **Optical Amplification:** The emitted photons travel between two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective) at the ends of the cavity, triggering a chain reaction of stimulated emission. The partially reflective mirror emits the amplified beam as the laser output.

## 2.2 Types of Industrial Lasers Used in LBW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask the comparison between **CO₂** and **Nd:YAG / Fiber lasers**. Memorize the differences in wavelength, efficiency, and beam delivery method.

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Wavelength | Beam Delivery | Typical Power | Key Feature |
|------------|---------------|------------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **CO₂ Laser** | CO₂/N₂/He gas | **10.6 µm** | Mirror-based (external) | 1 – 25 kW | High power, requires He gas mixture |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | Yttrium Aluminum Garnet crystal doped with Nd³⁺ | **1.06 µm** | Fiber-optic | 0.1 – 6 kW | Can weld reflective metals (Al, Cu) |
| **Fiber Laser** | Yb-doped optical fiber | **1.07 µm** | Direct fiber | 0.5 – 50 kW | High efficiency, compact, maintenance-free |
| **Disk Laser** | Yb:YAG thin disk | **1.03 µm** | Fiber | 1 – 16 kW | Excellent beam quality at high power |

## 2.3 Mechanism of Laser Beam Welding

The laser beam is focused through a lens (typically ZnSe for CO₂, fused silica for fiber) onto the workpiece. The interaction proceeds in **three sequential stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Absorption:** The workpiece surface absorbs the laser energy. Absorptivity depends on **wavelength, surface condition, and material type**. Lower wavelength (fiber) gives higher absorption in metals.
- **Stage 2 — Melting & Vaporization:** Sufficient energy causes localized melting, and at higher power densities, vaporization occurs forming a vapor cavity (keyhole).
- **Stage 3 — Solidification:** The heat source moves (or workpiece moves) and the molten pool solidifies, forming the weld bead.

## 2.4 The Two Operating Modes

> [!NOTE]
> The mode of LBW is governed by **Power Density** ($W/mm^2$). This is a frequent KTU question.

### (a) Conduction Mode (Low Power Density)
- Power density: **$10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm²**
- Heat is transferred into the material primarily by **thermal conduction**.
- Weld pool is **wide, shallow, hemispherical** in cross-section.
- Depth-to-width ratio ≈ **0.5**
- Used for **shallow welds, lap joints, and thin sheets**.

### (b) Keyhole Mode (High Power Density)
- Power density: **$10^6$ – $10^7$ W/mm²**
- The intense beam **vaporizes** the metal, forming a deep, narrow vapor cavity called the **keyhole**.
- The keyhole acts as a black-body absorber, trapping laser energy and allowing **deep penetration** welds.
- Depth-to-width ratio can exceed **10:1**.
- Used for **thick plates, deep penetration, and high-speed welding**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Every KTU 2024 numerical on LBW tests one of the formulas below. Memorize them with their units and typical ranges.

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | $PD = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $PD$ = Power Density, $P$ = Laser Power, $A$ = Spot Area | $\text{W/mm}^2$ | Determine operating mode |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $d$ = Focused beam diameter | $\text{mm}^2$ | Spot area calculation |
| 3 | $d = f \cdot \theta$ | $f$ = Focal length of lens, $\theta$ = Beam divergence | mm | Minimum spot size |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | $\lambda$ = Wavelength, $D$ = Beam diameter at lens | rad | Diffraction-limited spot |
| 5 | $HI = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | $HI$ = Heat Input, $v$ = Travel speed | $\text{J/mm}$ | Weld energy input |
| 6 | $BPP = \dfrac{d \cdot \theta}{4}$ | $BPP$ = Beam Parameter Product | $\text{mm·mrad}$ | Beam quality indicator |
| 7 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h$ = Planck, $c$ = light, $\lambda$ = wavelength | J or eV | Energy of a single photon |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{d}{2f \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}$ | Focused spot radius $R$ | mm | Focused spot geometry |

## 2.6 Critical Process Parameters

The four primary parameters that control weld quality:

- **Laser Power (P):** Higher power → deeper penetration.
- **Travel Speed (v):** Higher speed → narrower, shallower weld; lower speed → wider, deeper weld.
- **Focal Point Position (FPP):** Position of the focal point relative to the workpiece surface. **Negative FPP** (below surface) → deeper penetration; **Positive FPP** (above surface) → wider weld.
- **Shielding Gas (typically Argon or Helium):** Protects weld pool from oxidation, suppresses plasma formation above the workpiece.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

LBW is critical in industries demanding **high precision, low distortion, and high speed**:
- **Automotive:** Tailor-welded blanks, transmission gears, battery cells for EVs.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blades, fuel injectors, airframe structures (Ti alloys).
- **Electronics:** Battery tab welding, hermetic sealing of micro-components.
- **Medical:** Stent manufacturing, surgical instruments.
- **Shipbuilding & Heavy Engineering:** Thick plate joining with high-power fiber lasers (>15 kW).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Focused Spot Diameter from Beam Divergence

The minimum achievable focused spot diameter is determined by the **diffraction limit** of the optical system.

**Given:**
- Beam divergence: $\theta$
- Focal length of focusing lens: $f$

**Step 1 — Geometric Relationship:**
The focused spot diameter is the product of the focal length and the divergence angle of the beam at the lens.

$$d = f \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Express Divergence in Terms of Wavelength:**
For a diffraction-limited Gaussian beam, the full-angle divergence is given by:

$$\theta = \frac{4 \lambda}{\pi D}$$

where $D$ is the unfocused beam diameter at the lens and $\lambda$ is the laser wavelength.

**Step 3 — Substitute into Spot Diameter Equation:**

$$d = f \cdot \frac{4\lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{4 f \lambda}{\pi D}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify Using the Constant:**
The factor $\dfrac{4}{\pi}$ can be approximated numerically:

$$d \approx 1.27 \cdot \frac{f \lambda}{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **shorter wavelength** (fiber laser at 1.07 µm vs. CO₂ at 10.6 µm) and a **longer focal length** can produce a smaller spot — but the smallest spot also requires a **larger initial beam diameter** $D$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density at the Workpiece

**Given:**
- Laser power: $P$ (W)
- Focused spot diameter: $d$ (mm)

**Step 1 — Spot Area (Circular Cross-Section):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Power Density Definition:**

$$PD = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$PD = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Units Check:**

$$PD = \frac{\text{W}}{\text{mm}^2} = \text{W/mm}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A **2 kW CO₂ laser** ($\lambda = 10.6 \, \mu m$) is focused using a lens of focal length $f = 100$ mm. The unfocused beam diameter at the lens is $D = 20$ mm. Calculate: (a) the minimum focused spot diameter, (b) the power density, and (c) the predicted mode of operation.

**Part (a) — Focused Spot Diameter:**

$$d = \frac{4 f \lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{4 \times 100 \times 10.6 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$d = \frac{4.24}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{4.24}{62.83} = 0.0675 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) — Power Density:**

$$PD = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} = \frac{4 \times 2000}{\pi \times (0.0675)^2}$$

$$PD = \frac{8000}{\pi \times 0.004556} = \frac{8000}{0.01431} \approx 5.59 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Part (c) — Mode Identification:**

Since $PD = 5.59 \times 10^5 \text{ W/mm}^2$, this lies just within the **conduction mode** range ($10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm²). To achieve keyhole mode, the power must be increased or the spot diameter reduced (shorter focal length lens).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** For steel, a keyhole mode LBW typically requires **$PD > 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Effects on Weld Geometry

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Weld Width | Increase Effect on Penetration Depth | Increase Effect on HAZ |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|
| Laser Power ($P$) | Slight increase | **Strong increase** | Slight increase |
| Travel Speed ($v$) | **Decrease** | **Decrease** | Decrease |
| Focal Position (above surface) | **Increase** | Decrease | Increase |
| Beam Diameter ($d$) | **Increase** | Decrease | Increase |
| Absorptivity (coating) | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight increase |

## 3.5 Heat Input Calculation

The heat input per unit length of weld is given by:

$$HI = \frac{P}{v}$$

**Worked Example:** A 3 kW laser at 1 m/min travel speed:

$$v = 1 \text{ m/min} = 16.67 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$HI = \frac{3000}{16.67} = 180 \text{ J/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For comparison, conventional arc welding (SMAW) typically delivers **1,000 – 2,000 J/mm**, making LBW a **low heat input** process.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Complete LBW System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply Unit] --> B[Laser Resonator Cavity]
    B --> C[Beam Shaping Optics]
    C --> D[Fiber Optic / Mirror Delivery]
    D --> E[Focusing Lens Assembly]
    E --> F[Workpiece Surface]
    F --> G[Weld Pool and Keyhole]
    G --> H[Solidified Weld Bead]
    I[Shielding Gas Supply] --> F
    J[Cooling System] --> B
    K[CNC Motion Controller] --> F
    L[Process Monitor Sensors] --> K
    G --> L
    H --> M[Quality Inspection Station]
    M --> N[Accept or Reject Decision]
```

**Block Description (top to bottom):**
- **Power Supply:** Provides high-voltage DC excitation to the laser source.
- **Laser Resonator Cavity:** Houses the gain medium and the two mirrors that amplify light via stimulated emission.
- **Beam Shaping Optics:** Collimates and shapes the raw beam into a usable profile.
- **Beam Delivery:** Mirrors (CO₂) or optical fiber (Nd:YAG/Fiber) transport the beam to the workpiece.
- **Focusing Lens:** ZnSe lens for CO₂; fused silica for fiber/Nd:YAG. Reduces spot to ~0.1–0.5 mm.
- **Workpiece:** The metal/component to be welded.
- **Shielding Gas:** Argon/Helium flow protects the molten pool from atmospheric contamination.
- **Cooling System:** Water or air cooling of laser source and optics.
- **CNC Controller:** Coordinates motion of workpiece and laser head.
- **Process Sensors:** Monitor temperature, plasma, seam tracking.
- **Quality Inspection:** NDT such as ultrasonic, X-ray, or visual inspection.

---

## 4.2 Conduction Mode vs. Keyhole Mode — Process Physics Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Beam Strikes Surface] --> B{Power Density Check}
    B -->|PD less than 10^6 W/mm2| C[Conduction Mode]
    B -->|PD greater than 10^6 W/mm2| D[Keyhole Mode]
    C --> E[Surface Melting Only]
    E --> F[Heat Spreads by Conduction]
    F --> G[Shallow Hemispherical Pool]
    G --> H[Wide Shallow Weld]
    D --> I[Surface Vaporization]
    I --> J[Deep Vapor Cavity Forms]
    J --> K[Keyhole Acts as Black Body Absorber]
    K --> L[Multiple Internal Reflections]
    L --> M[Deep Narrow Penetration Weld]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Keyhole Formation Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Beam Impact and Surface Heating] --> B[Stage 2: Localized Melting Begins]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Vaporization Reaches Threshold]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Vapor Pressure Pushes Molten Metal Aside]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Vapor Cavity Keyhole Forms]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Beam Enters Keyhole and Reflects Multiple Times]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Energy Coupled Deep into Material]
    G --> H[Stage 8: High Depth to Width Ratio Weld]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: Selection of LBW Mode

| Criterion | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Power Density | $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm² | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |
| Depth / Width Ratio | < 0.5 | > 5 (up to 12) |
| Weld Appearance | Smooth, cosmetic | Deep, narrow, may have root porosity |
| Typical Thickness | < 2 mm | 2 – 25 mm |
| Speed | Moderate | High |
| Plasma Risk | Low | Moderate to high |
| Common Application | Hermetic seals, lap joints | Butt welds, deep structural joints |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1. Define Laser Beam Welding. List any four advantages of LBW over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**
Laser Beam Welding (LBW) is a fusion welding process in which a highly focused, coherent, monochromatic beam of light is used to melt and join the workpiece materials. The beam is generated by a laser source and focused through lenses to achieve the high power density required for welding.

**Advantages over conventional arc welding:**
1. **Low heat input** → minimal distortion and narrow HAZ.
2. **High welding speed** → improved productivity.
3. **Non-contact process** → no tool wear, no electrode consumption.
4. **Weldable dissimilar metals** (e.g., Cu–Al, Ti–steel) and non-metals.
5. **Deep penetration** in thick sections with single-pass welding.
6. Easily automated with CNC and robotic systems.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Conduction Mode and Keyhole Mode of LBW. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|-------|-----------------|---------------|
| Power Density | $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm² | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |
| Mechanism | Melting only (no vaporization) | Melting + vaporization |
| Pool Shape | Hemispherical / wide | Deep, narrow |
| Depth / Width Ratio | < 0.5 | > 5 |
| Typical Application | Thin sheets, lap joints | Thick plates, deep butt welds |
| Plasma Formation | Negligible | Significant (needs shielding gas) |

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
>
> **Q. A (a)** Explain the principle of Laser Beam Welding with a neat block diagram of the system. Discuss the different types of industrial lasers used in LBW. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. A (b)** A **4 kW fiber laser** ($\lambda = 1.07 \, \mu m$) is focused through a lens of focal length **150 mm**. The unfocused beam diameter at the lens is **25 mm**. Calculate (i) the minimum focused spot diameter, (ii) the power density, and (iii) the expected mode of welding. Given: the workpiece is **mild steel** with absorptivity ≈ 35% at this wavelength. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Principle of LBW:**
LBW is based on **stimulated emission of radiation**. When an external energy source (pumping) excites atoms in a gain medium to a higher energy state, they emit photons of identical phase, wavelength, and direction. These photons are amplified within an optical cavity (between two mirrors) and emerge as a coherent, high-intensity laser beam. This beam, when focused on the workpiece, melts and fuses the material.

**Block Diagram of LBW System:**
*(Refer to the Functional Architecture Flow diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — it depicts Power Supply → Resonator Cavity → Beam Shaping → Delivery → Focusing Lens → Workpiece, with auxiliary blocks for Shielding Gas, Cooling, and CNC Control.)*

**Types of Industrial Lasers (4 types):**

1. **CO₂ Laser** — Gas laser using CO₂/N₂/He. Wavelength = 10.6 µm (far infrared). Power range 1–25 kW. Beam delivered by mirrors. Most common in heavy industry.
2. **Nd:YAG Laser** — Solid-state laser using Neodymium-doped Yttrium Aluminum Garnet. Wavelength = 1.06 µm (near IR). Delivered by optical fiber. Good for reflective metals.
3. **Fiber Laser** — Uses Yb-doped optical fiber as gain medium. Wavelength = 1.07 µm. Highest wall-plug efficiency (~30–40%). Delivered by fiber. Maintenance-free.
4. **Disk Laser** — Uses Yb:YAG thin disk. Wavelength = 1.03 µm. Excellent beam quality (low BPP). Suitable for high-power precision welding.

---

### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $P = 4 \text{ kW} = 4000 \text{ W}$
- $\lambda = 1.07 \, \mu m = 1.07 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$
- $f = 150 \text{ mm}$
- $D = 25 \text{ mm}$

**Step (i) — Minimum Focused Spot Diameter:**

$$d = \frac{4 f \lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{4 \times 150 \times 1.07 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 25}$$

$$d = \frac{0.642}{78.54} = 8.17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx 8.2 \, \mu m$$

**Step (ii) — Power Density:**

$$PD = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} = \frac{4 \times 4000}{\pi \times (8.17 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$PD = \frac{16000}{\pi \times 6.67 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{16000}{2.095 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$PD \approx 7.64 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Accounting for 35% absorptivity:**

$$PD_{abs} = 0.35 \times 7.64 \times 10^{7} \approx 2.67 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step (iii) — Mode of Welding:**

Since $PD_{abs} = 2.67 \times 10^7 \text{ W/mm}^2 \gg 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$, the process operates in **Keyhole Mode**. Deep penetration with high depth-to-width ratio is expected.

---

## 5.3 PART B: Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
>
> **Q. B (a)** What are the principal process parameters of LBW? Explain the effect of laser power, travel speed, and focal point position on weld bead geometry. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. B (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the formation of a **keyhole** during LBW. What is the role of shielding gas in LBW? **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Principal Process Parameters of LBW:**
The four major parameters are:
1. **Laser Power (P)** — measured in watts/kW.
2. **Travel Speed (v)** — measured in mm/s or m/min.
3. **Focal Point Position (FPP)** — distance of focal point from workpiece surface.
4. **Shielding Gas Type and Flow Rate.**

**Effect of Process Parameters on Weld Geometry:**

- **Laser Power (↑):** Penetration depth **increases significantly**; bead width increases slightly. Excessive power causes undercut, spatter, and burn-through.
- **Travel Speed (↑):** Both penetration depth and bead width **decrease**. Very high speed → lack of fusion. Very low speed → wide HAZ, excessive heat input.
- **Focal Point Position:**
  - **At surface (0):** Balanced geometry.
  - **Above surface (+):** Wider, shallower weld (similar to conduction mode).
  - **Below surface (–):** Deeper, narrower weld (keyhole mode enhanced).

> **Formula linkage:** $HI = \dfrac{P}{v}$ — heat input controls the HAZ size.

---

### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Keyhole Formation Stages (refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.3):**

1. The laser beam strikes the workpiece surface; energy is absorbed.
2. Surface temperature rises to vaporization point of the material.
3. The recoil pressure of vaporization displaces molten metal, creating a **vapor cavity** (keyhole).
4. The laser beam enters the cavity and undergoes **multiple internal reflections** along the cavity walls.
5. Each reflection deposits more energy, increasing absorption efficiency (the keyhole acts as a near-black-body absorber).
6. The cavity stabilizes at a depth depending on power and travel speed; molten metal flows around the keyhole and solidifies behind it.

**Role of Shielding Gas:**

- **Protects the molten pool** from atmospheric contamination (oxidation, nitridation).
- **Suppresses plasma formation** above the workpiece. Plasma (ionized metal vapor) can refract and defocus the beam, reducing penetration.
- **Common gases:** Argon (Ar), Helium (He), or Ar-He mixtures. Helium has higher ionization energy → better plasma suppression.
- **Typical flow rate:** 10–30 L/min, delivered through a coaxial nozzle.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in power density** — Always write $\text{W/mm}^2$. Students often write $W$ or omit units → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $PD$ with $HI$** — Power density ($W/mm^2$) determines the **mode**; Heat Input ($J/mm$) determines **HAZ size**. KTU examiners check this distinction strictly.
> 3. **Using the wrong wavelength** — Remember CO₂ = 10.6 µm, Fiber = 1.07 µm. Mixing them in numericals = full marks lost.
> 4. **Not mentioning absorptivity** — When given a metal like aluminum or copper, students must state that low absorptivity at 10.6 µm makes CO₂ laser unsuitable without surface treatment.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** — A block diagram of the LBW system is **mandatory** for 7-mark questions. A text-only answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to state "non-contact process"** — This is the single most important distinguishing feature of LBW. Examiners explicitly test it.
> 7. **Mixing up conduction vs. keyhole** — Conduction = melting only; Keyhole = melting + vaporization. State the depth-to-width ratio to make the answer complete.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam!**

- **LASER** = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Three stages: **Pumping → Stimulated Emission → Amplification**.
- **Key properties:** Monochromaticity, Coherence, Directionality, High Brightness.
- **Industrial lasers (4):** CO₂ (10.6 µm), Nd:YAG (1.06 µm), Fiber (1.07 µm), Disk (1.03 µm). Fiber laser has highest efficiency.
- **Critical formulas to memorize:**
  - $PD = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}$
  - $d = \dfrac{4 f \lambda}{\pi D}$
  - $HI = \dfrac{P}{v}$
  - $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$
- **Conduction Mode:** $PD = 10^5$–$10^6$ W/mm², shallow, wide weld, depth/width < 0.5.
- **Keyhole Mode:** $PD > 10^6$ W/mm², deep narrow weld, depth/width > 5, vapor cavity forms.
- **Process parameters (4):** Power, Travel Speed, Focal Point Position, Shielding Gas.
- **Shielding gas:** Argon or Helium; coaxial nozzle; suppresses plasma + protects weld pool.
- **Key advantages:** Low HAZ, high speed, non-contact, dissimilar metal joining, automation-friendly.
- **Key limitations:** High capital cost, safety hazards (Class IV laser), low absorptivity in some metals (Al, Cu at 10.6 µm), needs precise joint fit-up.
- **Typical applications:** Automotive tailor-welded blanks, aerospace turbine blades, EV battery welding, stent manufacturing, electronics hermetic sealing.
- **Numerical solving order:** (1) Compute spot diameter $d$, (2) Compute $PD$, (3) Compare to $10^6$ W/mm² threshold, (4) Decide mode, (5) Comment on weld geometry.
- **BPP (Beam Parameter Product):** Lower BPP = better beam quality. Fiber lasers have BPP < 2 mm·mrad.
- **Examiner trap:** Always include **units, sketch, and the word "non-contact"** in LBW answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Welding (USW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET402 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Welding (USW) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** is a *solid-state welding* process in which two workpieces are joined by the application of **high-frequency mechanical vibrations (typically 20–40 kHz)** under a static clamping force (called the *weld pressure* or *clamp force*). The localized frictional heat generated at the interface — without any external heat, filler metal, or flux — causes the atoms of the two surfaces to interdiffuse across the joint line, producing a metallurgical (or polymeric) bond. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** > "Ultrasonic welding is a solid-state joining process in which coalescence is produced at the interface of two parts by the local application of high-frequency vibratory energy while the parts are held together under a static clamping load." According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET402 — Manufacturing Processes, Module 4)**, USW is classified under the family of **Non-Conventional / Modern Welding Processes**, specifically grouped with other solid-state techniques such as **Friction Welding, Friction Stir Welding, and Diffusion Bonding**, but distinguished by the use of **acoustic (sonic) vibration energy** rather than rotational or bulk thermal energy. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine rubbing your palms together **very, very fast** (about 20,000–40,000 rubs per second — which is faster than a hummingbird's wing beat) while gently pressing them against each other. Two things happen: 1. **Friction generates heat right at the skin-to-skin interface** — but the inside of your hand stays cool. 2. The combination of **heat + pressure + micro-deformation** causes the two surfaces to "merge" — they no longer come apart cleanly along the original line. Ultrasonic Welding does exactly this, but with **metal or plastic parts**. A vibrating tool (called a *sonotrode* or *horn*) replaces your hand, and an ultrasonic transducer (a device that converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration) replaces your muscles. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway for Students:** > No spark. No flame. No filler rod. No melting pool. Just **vibration + pressure = weld**. > That's why it is called a **solid-state** process — the base metal never actually crosses its melting point. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Range | KTU-Specified Value | |---|---|---| | Ultrasonic Frequency | **20 kHz – 40 kHz** | **20 kHz** (most common) | | Vibration Amplitude | 10 – 50 µm | **20–35 µm** | | Clamp / Weld Force | 50 N – 2500 N | **200–1500 N** | | Weld Time | 0.1 – 1.5 s | **0.3–0.8 s** | | Power Consumption | 50 W – 3000 W | **500–2000 W** | | Weldable Metals | Al, Cu, Ni, Au, Ag, Sn | **Aluminium alloys (primary)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Industrial Frequency = 20 kHz** — this is the value most KTU board questions expect students to recall by default. Higher frequencies (35 kHz, 40 kHz) are used for micro-welding of thin foils and electronic components. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics Behind Ultrasonic Welding — Step-by-Step Breakdown The USW process can be decomposed into **four sequential physical phenomena** that occur within milliseconds: ### Step 1 — Transduction (Electrical → Mechanical) A high-frequency electrical signal (typically 20 kHz sinusoidal, generated by an ultrasonic generator / power supply) is fed into a **piezoelectric transducer**. - The transducer contains **piezoelectric crystals** (commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate — *PZT*). - When an alternating voltage is applied, the crystals **expand and contract** at the same frequency as the input signal. - This produces a mechanical vibration of the **same 20 kHz frequency** but with very small amplitude (a few micrometres). ### Step 2 — Amplification (Booster Stage) The amplitude from the transducer is too small (~5–10 µm) to do useful welding work. A **booster** (also called a *velocity transformer* or *amplitude transformer*) amplifies the vibration amplitude. - The booster is a tuned metal bar shaped so that one end is **half-wavelength** long and tapered. - It works on the principle of **mechanical resonance** — like pushing a child on a swing at just the right moment. ### Step 3 — Coupling (Horn / Sonotrode) The amplified vibration is delivered to the workpiece through the **sonotrode** (also called *horn* or *welding tool*). - The horn is precisely tuned to the operating frequency (one full wavelength long). - It transmits the vibration to the **upper workpiece** while the lower workpiece is held against a stationary anvil. - Frictional shear stress at the interface — produced by the relative oscillatory motion under clamp force — generates **localized frictional heat**. ### Step 4 — Bond Formation Once the interface temperature reaches a critical value (but still **below the melting point**), the following mechanisms occur simultaneously: - **Plastic deformation** of surface asperities (microscopic peaks). - **Disruption and dispersion of oxide films** and surface contaminants. - **Atomic interdiffusion** across the joint line (micro-scale). - **Mechanical interlocking** of surface roughness features. The result is a **solid-state weld** — the joint is metallurgically continuous without ever crossing the solidus temperature. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU-board-tested. Memorize them using the units given.** | # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f = \dfrac{v}{2 L}$ | $f$ = resonant frequency (Hz), $v$ = speed of sound in horn material (m/s), $L$ = horn length (m) | Defines horn resonance condition | | 2 | $A_{out} = A_{in} \times M$ | $A_{out}$ = output amplitude (µm), $A_{in}$ = input amplitude (µm), $M$ = mechanical gain of horn | Booster/horn amplification ratio | | 3 | $P_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot v_{rel}$ | $P_{fric}$ = frictional power (W), $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $F_c$ = clamping force (N), $v_{rel}$ = relative velocity (m/s) | Power dissipated as heat at interface | | 4 | $v_{rel} = 2 \pi f A$ | $v_{rel}$ = peak velocity (m/s), $f$ = frequency (Hz), $A$ = amplitude (m) | Peak sliding velocity at joint | | 5 | $Q = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot \int v_{rel}\,dt$ | $Q$ = total heat generated (J), $t$ = weld time (s) | Cumulative heat input to joint | | 6 | $T_{interface} \approx T_0 + \dfrac{\mu F_c v_{rel}}{2 k A_{contact}}$ | $T_0$ = ambient (K), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A_{contact}$ = contact area (m²) | Localized interfacial temperature rise | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{weld}}{P_{input}}$ | $\eta$ = process efficiency (~30–60 %), $P_{weld}$ = useful weld power, $P_{input}$ = electrical input | Overall energy conversion efficiency | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall Avoided:** Formula (3) and (4) — students frequently confuse $v_{rel}$ (velocity) with $A$ (amplitude). Remember: **velocity = frequency × amplitude × 2π**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Ultrasonic welding is the **dominant joining process** in the following industries: | Industry | Application | |---|---| | **Automotive** | Wiring harness joints, aluminium heat-exchanger tubes, airbag igniters, EV battery tabs | | **Electronics** | PCB-to-component soldering without flux, lithium-ion battery tab welding (Cu-Al), semiconductor lead bonding | | **Medical** | Sealing of IV filter housings, dialysis membrane assemblies, sterile packaging | | **Aerospace** | Honeycomb core joining, lightweight aluminium panel assembly, sensor housings | | **Packaging** | Thermoplastic film sealing, blister packs, food-grade pouches | | **Solar** | Copper ribbon-to-cell tabbing in photovoltaic modules | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Context:** With Kerala's growing EV manufacturing and solar panel assembly sector (Tata, Vikram Solar, etc.), USW knowledge is increasingly relevant to campus placements in production engineering roles. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Resonance Condition of the Sonotrode (Horn) The sonotrode behaves as a **half-wavelength acoustic resonator**. We will derive its fundamental frequency equation from first principles. ### Given The horn is a uniform bar of length $L$, made of material with longitudinal speed of sound $v$. We treat the bar as a 1-D longitudinal vibrator with both ends free (since the tip touches a soft interface, not rigidly clamped acoustically). ### Step 1 — Wave Equation in a Bar The longitudinal displacement $\xi(x,t)$ of a particle in the bar satisfies the 1-D wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Separation of Variables We assume a solution of the form: $$\xi(x,t) = X(x) \cdot T(t)$$ Substituting into the wave equation and dividing by $X(x) T(t)$: $$\frac{1}{T} \frac{d^2 T}{d t^2} = v^2 \cdot \frac{1}{X} \frac{d^2 X}{d x^2} = -\omega^2$$ This separation yields two ODEs. The spatial solution takes the standard sinusoidal form: $$X(x) = A \cos\left(\frac{\omega x}{v}\right) + B \sin\left(\frac{\omega x}{v}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Free-Free Boundary Conditions For a free-free bar, the **stress is zero at both ends**, i.e., $\frac{dX}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$. Differentiating $X(x)$: $$\frac{dX}{dx} = -\frac{A \omega}{v} \sin\left(\frac{\omega x}{v}\right) + \frac{B \omega}{v} \cos\left(\frac{\omega x}{v}\right)$$ **At $x = 0$:** $0 = B \cdot \frac{\omega}{v} \implies B = 0$ **At $x = L$:** $0 = -\frac{A \omega}{v} \sin\left(\frac{\omega L}{v}\right)$ For non-trivial solution $A \neq 0$: $$\sin\left(\frac{\omega L}{v}\right) = 0 \implies \frac{\omega L}{v} = n \pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ ### Step 4 — Fundamental Frequency For the **fundamental mode** ($n = 1$): $$\omega = \frac{\pi v}{L}$$ Converting $\omega = 2 \pi f$: $$2 \pi f = \frac{\pi v}{L}$$ $$\boxed{f = \frac{v}{2 L}}$$ This is the **fundamental resonance frequency** of a half-wavelength free-free horn. For typical values: - Titanium horn: $v \approx 5100$ m/s, $L = 0.1275$ m $\implies f \approx 20$ kHz ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Heat Generation at the Weld Interface ### Step 1 — Sliding Velocity from Oscillatory Motion The horn tip moves according to: $$\xi(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f t)$$ The instantaneous velocity is: $$v_{inst}(t) = \frac{d\xi}{dt} = 2 \pi f A \cos(2 \pi f t)$$ The **peak velocity** is: $$v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A$$ The **RMS velocity** is: $$v_{rms} = \frac{v_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\, \pi f A$$ ### Step 2 — Instantaneous Frictional Power At any instant, the frictional shear force is $F_{fric} = \mu F_c$, and the frictional power dissipated is: $$P_{fric}(t) = F_{fric} \cdot v_{inst}(t) = \mu F_c \cdot 2 \pi f A \cos(2 \pi f t)$$ ### Step 3 — Average Power Over One Cycle $$\langle P_{fric} \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T P_{fric}(t)\, dt$$ $$\langle P_{fric} \rangle = \frac{2 \pi f \mu F_c A}{T} \int_0^T \cos(2 \pi f t)\, dt$$ Since $\int_0^T \cos(2 \pi f t)\, dt = 0$ over a full cycle, the time-average vanishes. **This is because the upper workpiece slides back and forth.** The correct averaging is therefore over a half-cycle (one direction of slip), or we use the **RMS** velocity: $$P_{avg} = \mu F_c \cdot v_{rms} = \sqrt{2}\, \pi \mu F_c f A$$ ### Step 4 — Total Heat Input Over Weld Time $$Q = P_{avg} \cdot t_{weld} = \sqrt{2}\, \pi \mu F_c f A \, t_{weld}$$ ### Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Style Problem (Expected in Examinations):** **Given:** $\mu = 0.3$, $F_c = 500$ N, $f = 20{,}000$ Hz, $A = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $t_{weld} = 0.5$ s. **Find:** Total heat input $Q$ at the weld interface. **Solution:** $$v_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (20{,}000) \cdot (30 \times 10^{-6})$$ $$v_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot 0.6 \approx 2.665 \text{ m/s}$$ $$P_{avg} = 0.3 \times 500 \times 2.665 = 399.8 \text{ W}$$ $$Q = 399.8 \times 0.5 \approx 199.9 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 200 \text{ J}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Assessment with Other Welding Processes > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is **extremely high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE — it has been asked in Part B for the past three consecutive exam cycles. | Parameter | USW | Resistance Welding | Friction Welding | Laser Welding | |---|---|---|---|---| | Heat Source | Ultrasonic friction | Electrical $I^2R$ | Rotational friction | Focused light | | Frequency | 20–40 kHz | DC / 50 Hz AC | 0.5–3 Hz rotation | Continuous / pulsed | | State During Weld | Solid state | Partial melting | Solid state | Melting pool | | Typical Weld Time | 0.3–1 s | 0.05–0.5 s | 1–30 s | 0.001–1 s | | Filler Required | **No** | No | No | Sometimes | | Joint Strength | 80–95 % of parent | 85–100 % | 90–100 % | 90–100 % | | Energy Efficiency | **30–60 %** | 80–90 % | 70–85 % | 30–40 % | | Best For | Dissimilar metals (Cu-Al), thin foils | Sheet steel, projection welds | Round bars, shafts | Precision, deep penetration | | Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) | **Negligible** | Small | Small | Moderate | | Operator Skill | Low | Low | Medium | High | # 4. The grey block ("HEAT GENERATION ZONE") is where the actual welding happens — everything to the left is energy conversion, and the static clamp force closes the loop from below. --- ## 4.2 Process Sequence — Step-by-Step Operational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD ST1["STAGE 1: LOADING

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define ultrasonic welding. State any two advantages of the process.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Ultrasonic welding is a solid-state welding process in which two parts are joined by the application of high-frequency vibratory energy (typically 20 kHz) under a static clamping force, producing coalescence through frictional heat generated at the interface without melting of the base material. **Advantages (any two):** 1. Suitable for **dissimilar metal joining** (e.g., copper to aluminium) without brittle intermetallics, because the process remains in the solid state. 2. **Negligible heat-affected zone (HAZ)** and minimal distortion — ideal for thin foils and precision assemblies. 3. **No filler metal, flux, or shielding gas** required, making it clean and environmentally friendly. 4. **Fast cycle time** (typically under one second per weld) — suitable for high-volume automated production. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the function of the sonotrode in ultrasonic welding?** **Model Answer:** The **sonotrode** (also called the *horn*) is the final vibrating component of an ultrasonic welding system. Its primary functions are: 1. **Mechanical coupling** — it transmits the amplified high-frequency vibration from the booster to the upper workpiece. 2. **Amplitude tuning** — its length and shape (taper, stepped, or catenoidal profile) determine the **final amplitude** delivered to the joint. 3. **Mechanical impedance matching** — it matches the acoustic impedance of the transducer to that of the workpiece for maximum energy transfer. 4. **Geometric shaping** — its tip profile determines the **weld spot geometry** (circular, rectangular, line weld, etc.). It is dimensioned as a **half-wavelength resonator** at the operating frequency (20 kHz), typically machined from **titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)** or **aluminium** for low acoustic loss and high fatigue strength. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 4 long-answer questions carry **14 marks** and may include **internal choice** (a student answers either Q-A or Q-B, not both). The split is typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** --- ### Question A — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of ultrasonic welding. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the process parameters affecting weld quality in ultrasonic welding. How is the process advantageous for joining dissimilar metals? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Working Principle:** Ultrasonic welding uses high-frequency mechanical vibration combined with static clamping force to produce a solid-state bond. The principle is based on **acoustic energy conversion** in four stages: 1. **Electrical Conversion:** A 20 kHz high-frequency electrical signal is generated by an ultrasonic generator. 2. **Electromechanical Conversion:** The piezoelectric transducer (PZT crystals) converts this electrical signal into mechanical vibration of the same frequency. 3. **Amplification:** The booster amplifies the amplitude (typically from 8 µm to 25 µm). 4. **Frictional Bonding:** The horn delivers this amplified vibration to the upper workpiece while the lower one is held stationary under clamp force. The relative sliding at the interface generates frictional heat, which — combined with pressure — causes plastic deformation, oxide disruption, and atomic interdiffusion across the joint line. **Main Components and Functions:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Ultrasonic Generator** | Converts 50 Hz mains to 20 kHz high-frequency AC signal | | **Piezoelectric Transducer** | Converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration | | **Booster** | Amplifies vibration amplitude | | **Sonotrode / Horn** | Couples vibration to workpiece, shapes weld geometry | | **Anvil** | Stationary support for lower workpiece | | **Pneumatic Clamp** | Provides static weld force | | **Controller / Timer** | Sets weld time, energy, or distance mode | > *[A neat block diagram should be drawn showing: Generator → Transducer → Booster → Horn → Workpiece → Anvil, with the clamp force applied vertically. KTU expects a labelled diagram.]* --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Process Parameters Affecting Weld Quality:** 1. **Weld pressure (clamp force):** Too low → insufficient heating, weak bond. Too high → excessive deformation, thinning, tool wear. Optimum range: 200–1500 N. 2. **Vibration amplitude:** Controls the sliding velocity at the interface. Typical: 20–35 µm. Higher amplitude → faster heating but risks over-welding. 3. **Weld time / weld energy:** Too short → incomplete bond. Too long → grain growth and reduced strength. Modern controllers use **energy mode** (cut-off when preset energy is delivered) or **distance mode** (cut-off after preset collapse). 4. **Frequency:** Mostly fixed at 20 kHz by transducer design. 5. **Surface condition:** Clean surfaces (degreased, lightly abraded) yield best results. Oxide films are dispersed during welding but should not be excessive. 6. **Material properties:** Hardness, ductility, and thermal conductivity of both parts govern the optimum parameter window. **Dissimilar Metal Joining — Advantages:** 1. **No melting → no brittle intermetallic phases** (the main killer of Cu-Al, Al-Mg joints in fusion welding). 2. Bond forms by **atomic interdiffusion at sub-melting temperatures**, preserving the original microstructure of both materials. 3. Successful industrial examples include **copper-aluminium joints in EV battery tabs**, **aluminium-brass refrigeration tubes**, and **gold-aluminium wire bonds in microelectronics**. --- ### Question B — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** Describe the phenomenon of ultrasonic welding with reference to the role of the piezoelectric transducer and the booster. Derive the relationship between the resonant frequency and the horn length. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare ultrasonic welding with resistance welding and friction welding on the basis of heat source, joint strength, weld time, HAZ, and applications. State the limitations of ultrasonic welding. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Role of the Piezoelectric Transducer:** The piezoelectric transducer is the heart of the USW system. It contains **piezoelectric crystals** (commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT) arranged in a *Langevin sandwich* configuration. When the high-frequency AC signal (20 kHz) from the generator is applied across the electrodes of these crystals, the **inverse piezoelectric effect** causes them to expand and contract synchronously with the signal, producing mechanical vibration. The amplitude of this vibration is small (typically 5–10 µm), which is why amplification is needed. **Role of the Booster:** The booster is a tuned metal element placed between the transducer and the horn. It is shaped (typically tapered or stepped) to provide **mechanical amplitude amplification** through the principle of **acoustic impedance transformation**. If its input cross-section is $A_1$ and output cross-section is $A_2$, the amplitude gain is: $$M = \sqrt{\frac{A_1}{A_2}}$$ A typical booster gives $M = 2$ to $3$, taking the input amplitude from 8 µm to 20–25 µm. **Derivation of Resonance Condition:** As derived in Section 3.1 of these notes, treating the horn as a free-free longitudinal bar with wave speed $v$: $$\frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2}$$ Separating variables and applying free-free boundary conditions ($\frac{dX}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$): $$\sin\left(\frac{\omega L}{v}\right) = 0 \implies \omega = \frac{n \pi v}{L}$$ For the **fundamental mode** ($n = 1$): $$\omega = \frac{\pi v}{L} \implies 2 \pi f = \frac{\pi v}{L}$$ $$\boxed{f = \frac{v}{2 L}}$$ **Numerical Verification:** For a titanium horn: $v \approx 5100$ m/s. For $f = 20$ kHz: $$L = \frac{v}{2f} = \frac{5100}{2 \times 20{,}000} = 0.1275 \text{ m} = 127.5 \text{ mm}$$ --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | USW | Resistance Welding | Friction Welding | |---|---|---|---| | **Heat Source** | Ultrasonic friction (20 kHz) | Electrical $I^2R$ heating | Rotational friction (1–3 Hz) | | **Joint Strength** | 80–95 % of parent | 85–100 % of parent | 90–100 % of parent | | **Weld Time** | 0.3–1 s (very fast) | 0.05–0.5 s (very fast) | 1–30 s (slower) | | **HAZ** | Negligible | Small | Small | | **Applications** | Dissimilar metals, thin foils, plastic welding | Sheet steel, projection welds, automotive | Round shafts, tubes, drill bits | **Limitations of USW:** 1. **Limited to thin sections** — typically < 3 mm thickness for metals; cannot replace arc welding for thick plates. 2. **Material restrictions** — only suitable for **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Ni, Au, Ag) and **thermoplastics**. Ferrous metals (steel) require too much power due to high yield strength. 3. **Joint geometry restriction** — works best with **lap or spot configurations**; butt welding is difficult. 4. **High initial capital cost** of ultrasonic generators and precision tooling. 5. **Tool wear** — sonotrodes require periodic replacement (typically every 50,000–100,000 cycles) due to fatigue. 6. **Sensitive to surface contamination** — heavy oils or thick oxide layers reduce weld quality. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Most Frequently Lose Marks on USW Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing the roles of the transducer, booster, and horn.** Many students describe all three as "amplifiers." Only the **booster and horn** amplify; the **transducer only converts** electrical to mechanical energy. (Loss: **2 marks**) > > 2. **Writing "ultrasonic" without specifying "20 kHz."** Examiners explicitly want the frequency value. Always pair the term "ultrasonic" with the frequency. (Loss: **1 mark**) > > 3. **Classifying USW as a fusion welding process.** It is a **solid-state process**. Marking this wrong costs 3 marks on classification-based questions. > > 4. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram in 14-mark questions.** Even a brief, neat block diagram is worth 3–4 marks in Part B answers. A text-only answer is heavily penalized. > > 5. **Not stating "no filler metal, no flux, no shielding gas."** This is the single most important advantage examiners test for. > > 6. **Mixing up resonance formulas.** The half-wavelength formula $f = v/(2L)$ applies to the horn; do not confuse with quarter-wave ($f = v/(4L)$) which is for a clamped-free horn (rarely used in USW). --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall** - ✅ USW = **Solid-state**, **20 kHz**, **No melting**, **No filler**, **No flux**, **No shielding gas**. - ✅ Frequency = **20 kHz** (industrial standard); range **20–40 kHz** for micro-welding. - ✅ Amplitude at horn tip = **20–35 µm**; clamped force = **200–1500 N**; weld time = **0.3–0.8 s**. - ✅ Four energy conversions: **Electrical (50 Hz) → Electrical (20 kHz) → Mechanical (low amplitude) → Mechanical (high amplitude) → Acoustic → Thermal (friction) → Mechanical (solid-state bond)**. - ✅ Five components in series: **Generator → Transducer → Booster → Horn/Sonotrode → Workpiece**, with **Anvil** supporting from below and **Pneumatic Clamp** applying force. - ✅ Resonance formula: $\boxed{f = \dfrac{v}{2 L}}$ for half-wavelength free-free horn. - ✅ Heat generation: $Q = \sqrt{2}\, \pi\, \mu\, F_c\, f\, A\, t_{weld}$. - ✅ Best for: **Dissimilar metals (Cu-Al, Al-Ni, Au-Al)**, **thin foils**, **battery tabs**, **plastics**. - ✅ NOT suitable for: **Steel (ferrous)**, **thick plates (> 3 mm)**, **butt joints**. - ✅ Key advantages: **Negligible HAZ**, **fast cycle time**, **no consumables**, **clean process**. - ✅ Key limitations: **Material restrictions**, **thickness restrictions**, **high tool cost**. - ✅ Exam buzzwords to use: *piezoelectric effect, sonotrode, booster, half-wavelength resonance, frictional heat, solid-state coalescence, atomic interdiffusion, energy mode, distance mode.* - ✅ Remember the **comparative table** — at least one USW-vs-other-process comparison is asked almost every exam cycle.
